title: Toe Tap Foundations
objective: Develop basic touch and feel for the ball using rapid alternating toe taps.
learningOutcomes: Players can alternate feet on top of the ball in a controlled rhythm
Players develop touch sensitivity between foot and ball
Players build coordination and balance on one foot while tapping with the other
Players gain confidence handling the ball at their feet
equipment: Flat open area or cones to mark personal space
pitchSize: Each player needs a 2x2m personal space
organisation: Each player stands with a ball at their feet in their own marked space. Players are spread out across the area so they cannot collide with each other. Coach stands where all players can see and hear clearly.
instructions: Place the ball on the ground directly in front of you.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, weight slightly forward on the balls of your feet.
Gently place the sole of your right foot on top of the ball.
Lift your right foot off the ball and immediately place the sole of your left foot on top.
Continue alternating feet in a steady rhythm - right, left, right, left.
Keep the ball completely still. Only the feet move up and down on top of it.
Count the taps aloud with the players to build rhythm: 1, 2, 3, 4...
After 2 minutes of static toe taps, progress to light movement: allow the ball to roll slightly forward with each tap so the player follows it.
After a further 2 minutes, challenge players to go as fast as they can for 10 seconds then slow down, developing tap speed and control.
Final 2 minutes: players move in any direction they choose while toe-tapping, developing spatial awareness.
coachingPoints: Stay on the balls of your feet at all times - never flat-footed. Imagine you are bouncing very gently.
Keep your head up and eyes looking forward, not down at the ball. Feel the ball with your feet.
Both knees should be slightly bent throughout. Stiff legs make the movement jerky and unbalanced.
The tapping motion comes from the ankle and knee, not the whole leg. Small, quick movements are better.
Keep your arms slightly out to the sides for balance - like a plane with wings.
The sound of the taps should be even - tap, tap, tap, tap - not uneven or rushed.
Relax your shoulders. Tension in the upper body makes the movement difficult.
The contact point is the front of the sole, just below the toes - not the toe itself.
Progress at each players own pace. Some will be fast immediately, others need more time on the basics.
Celebrate the rhythm, not just the speed. A slow correct rhythm is better than a fast incorrect one.
commonMistakes: Players look down at the ball constantly, losing awareness of space around them
Using the toe to tap rather than the sole of the foot
Standing flat-footed, making the movement slow and uncoordinated
Tapping in an uneven rhythm with one foot faster than the other
Tensing the upper body and losing balance during faster taps
Allowing the ball to roll away because the tap is too forceful
Kicking the ball sideways instead of tapping directly on top
corrections: For looking down: place a coloured cone at eye height 3m away and tell them to keep that cone in view at all times
For toe-tapping: ask the player to show you the sole of their boot, then use that part on the ball
For flat feet: place a piece of paper under their heel and tell them to not let it touch the ground
For uneven rhythm: clap or use a metronome app to give a consistent beat for them to follow
For upper body tension: ask the player to shake their arms loose before starting
For too much force: ask them to tap as if the ball is a sleeping baby they do not want to wake up
For kicking sideways: draw a cross on the top of the ball with chalk and ask them to tap the centre of the cross
progressions: Speed bursts: alternate between maximum speed (10 seconds) and controlled slow rhythm (10 seconds) continuously
Moving toe taps: allow the ball to travel as the player toe-taps, dribbling in a straight line then turning
Add commands: coach calls LEFT or RIGHT and the player must have that foot on the ball within 2 taps
Partner challenge: two players side by side count their taps - who reaches 50 first without losing the ball?
regressions: Allow the player to use only one foot (sole of foot on top, lift off, touch ground, back on ball)
Use a larger ball (size 5 instead of size 3) which is easier to make contact with
Reduce the rhythm speed to a walking pace tempo where the player counts to 3 between each tap
Allow the player to hold onto a wall or cone while learning the balance aspect
matchApplication: Toe taps build the foundation of all ball mastery. The soft touch developed here directly translates to first-touch control under pressure, shielding the ball from opponents in tight spaces, and the quick feet needed when a player receives the ball with a defender closing in. Players with strong toe-tap habits develop more natural comfort with the ball at their feet.
coachTips: Make it a game: count to 20 as a group together. The shared counting builds focus and rhythm simultaneously.
Use music with a clear beat - the rhythm helps U8 players find their natural tapping pace.
Do not correct every mistake at once. Pick one coaching point per player and focus on that.
Demonstrate the drill yourself before starting - children learn through imitation at this age.
Praise effort and rhythm, not just performance. Say great rhythm or lovely soft touch rather than just good.
Walk around and give individual encouragement. U8 players respond more to personal attention than group instruction.
diagramDescription: Each player stands in a marked 2x2m square (cone corners) with one ball at their feet. Players are arranged in a grid formation with 3m between each square. Coach stands at the front of the grid. Arrows show feet alternating on top of ball in place.

title: Roll and Stop
objective: Teach players to use the sole of the foot to roll the ball in different directions and stop it under control.
learningOutcomes: Players can roll the ball sideways with the sole in both directions
Players can stop a rolling ball using the sole of the foot
Players develop soft, sensitive touch using the underside of the boot
Players learn to move the ball without looking at their feet
organisation: Each player in their own 3x3m cone-marked square with a ball. Coach in a position to be seen and heard by all. Players face the same direction initially.
instructions: Place the ball at your feet in the centre of your square.
Put the sole (flat underside) of your right foot on top of the ball.
Gently push the ball to the left using the sole - the ball rolls sideways.
Follow the ball, bring it back to centre using the sole of the left foot.
Now roll it right with the left foot, then back with the right foot.
Practice this roll-and-return movement for 2 minutes until it feels natural.
Now progress: roll the ball forward using the sole of one foot, let it travel 1m, then stop it with the sole of the other foot.
The stop is the key skill: place the sole ON TOP of the moving ball to trap it completely.
After 3 minutes: roll in all directions - forward, backward, left, right - stopping at each cone boundary.
Final 3 minutes: free movement in the square using only the sole to move and stop the ball.
coachingPoints: The sole of the foot is the flat rubber bottom of the boot - use this surface, not the toe or the side.
When rolling, the foot pushes through the top of the ball, not into the side of it.
The stop requires placing the sole ON the ball, not behind it - the ball must be trapped under the foot.
Keep the ball in your square. If it goes to the edge, use the sole to drag it back.
Eyes up as much as possible. Feel the ball with your foot - do not always look at it.
The rolling movement is slow and controlled at this stage. Speed comes much later.
Keep your weight on the standing leg, not on the rolling foot.
When the ball is moving, move your feet to stay close to it. Do not reach for it.
Use both feet equally. Spend the same amount of time on left and right.
commonMistakes: Using the toe or inside of foot instead of the sole
Pressing down too hard and causing the ball to squirt forward uncontrolled
Stopping the ball with one foot but not transferring to the next movement
Standing still and reaching for the ball rather than moving to it
Using only the dominant foot and neglecting the weaker foot
corrections: Show the player the bottom of your own boot and say: use this exact surface
Reduce the pressure: ask them to roll the ball as if they might break it
Use a larger ball (size 5) which is harder to squirt away
Draw a footprint on the ground and ask them to step to it to stay close to the ball
After each roll with the right foot, count to 3 before using the right foot again to force left-foot use
progressions: Move while rolling: players roll the ball around the inside of their square in a pattern (square, circle, triangle)
Add a tempo: roll right for 5 seconds, stop, roll left for 5 seconds, creating a continuous flow
Partner mirror: two players face each other and mirror each other - one rolls right, the other rolls left simultaneously
Escape the zone: player dribbles using sole rolls to all 4 corners of their square and back to centre as fast as possible
regressions: Use a larger ball to make contact easier
Reduce to just rolling one direction and stopping before introducing opposite direction
Allow two hands on knees (bent posture) to help players feel the movement without worrying about balance
matchApplication: The sole-of-foot roll is one of the most useful close-control tools in professional football. It is used to change direction in tight spaces, drag the ball away from a pressing defender, and feign in one direction before going another. Mastery of this technique at U8 builds the foundation for the drag-back, roulette, and body feint moves used at senior level.
coachTips: This is a feel drill. Resist the urge to talk too much - give players time to experience the sensation.
Walk among the players and physically guide their foot to the correct position if needed.
Frame everything positively: say try placing your sole a little further on top of the ball not you are doing it wrong.
The stopping skill takes longer than the rolling skill. Spend more time on stops in the first session.
Play soft background music to relax the players. Young players tense up when they feel observed.
After 5 minutes, let players free-play using sole rolls with no instruction. Observe what they do naturally.
diagramDescription: Grid of 3x3m squares marked by corner cones, one per player. A ball sits in the centre of each square. Arrows show the ball rolling left (right foot pushing) then right (left foot pushing) within the square boundaries. Player stands at centre.

title: Inside Outside Roll
objective: Develop the ability to move the ball using the inside and outside of both feet in a flowing sequence.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the inside of the foot to push the ball across the body
Players can use the outside of the foot to push the ball away from the body
Players develop rhythmic ball manipulation with both feet
Players become comfortable with the ball in tight personal space
pitchSize: 3x3m per player, arranged in a grid
organisation: Players in individual squares. Coach demonstrates the pattern before players attempt it. All players face the same direction. Pattern is slow and deliberate in the first phase.
instructions: Stand with the ball between your feet, feet slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Use the INSIDE of your right foot to push the ball to the left. The ball crosses in front of your left foot.
Use the INSIDE of your left foot to push the ball back to the right. The ball crosses in front of your right foot.
Repeat this inside-inside movement for 90 seconds until it is comfortable.
Now learn the outside: use the OUTSIDE of your right foot to push the ball to the right, away from your body.
Step your right foot over to meet it, then use the OUTSIDE of your left foot to push the ball to the left.
Practice outside-outside for 90 seconds.
Now combine: inside right, inside left, outside right, outside left in a sequence.
The ball should barely move in space - all movement is between your feet, not across the area.
Final 3 minutes: players move around their square while continuing the pattern, keeping the ball close throughout.
coachingPoints: The inside surface is the large flat area on the inside of the boot, between the toe and the heel.
The outside surface is the smaller area on the outside of the boot, roughly in line with the little toe.
The ball should never travel more than 1 boot-length in any direction. Keep it very tight.
Stay on the balls of your feet throughout. This allows quick lateral movement.
The movement is in the hips and ankles, not the knees. Try to keep your knees soft and slightly bent.
Both feet must be used equally. Deliberately count to ensure even left-right usage.
The sequence is rhythmic - like a dance step. Find the beat and maintain it.
Keep your eyes up after the first few minutes. The ball should be sensed with the feet, not watched.
Arms out slightly for balance. The movement is lateral and requires upper-body counterbalance.
commonMistakes: Ball travels too far on each touch, causing the player to chase it
Using only the dominant foot for the majority of touches
Looking down at the ball throughout the entire drill
Tapping the ball with the toe instead of the inside or outside surface
Standing upright with straight legs, limiting lateral mobility
Moving too fast before the pattern is established at slow pace
corrections: Place 2 cones 50cm apart on each side of the player - the ball must stay between the cones
Ask the player to narrate what they are doing: inside right... inside left... to build self-awareness
Put a sticker on the inside and outside of each boot so the player can see the contact surfaces
For leg stiffness: ask the player to do 5 jumps in place then resume to reset their natural knee bend
For too fast: count aloud with the player - slow count of 1, 2, 3, 4 for each touch sequence
progressions: Increase speed: full speed inside-outside for 10 seconds, then controlled for 10 seconds, repeating
Add a dribble: after 4 touches in place, take 3 dribbling touches forward before resuming the pattern
Defender pressure: a passive partner stands 1m away and the player must maintain the pattern while the partner reaches toward the ball (no tackle - just pressure)
Pattern competition: who can complete the most inside-outside sequences in 30 seconds without losing control?
regressions: Separate the skills: practice inside only for 3 minutes before introducing outside
Slow the pace to one touch every 3 seconds to build muscle memory without pressure
Use a larger ball to make the contact surface easier to find
Allow the player to watch the ball for the entire drill before adding the eyes-up challenge
matchApplication: The inside-outside foot roll is the foundation of close ball control in match situations. When a player receives the ball with limited space and a defender close, they use inside-outside touches to manipulate the ball without giving the defender a clear chance to tackle. This drill directly builds the touch quality seen in professional players when they receive in tight central areas and quickly shift the ball to create a passing or dribbling angle.
coachTips: This drill looks simple but teaches very sophisticated motor patterns. Allow a full 3-4 sessions before expecting fluency.
Film the players on a phone and show them their own technique. Young players respond very positively to seeing themselves.
Use a counting rhythm - one, two, three, four - that all players follow together for the first 3 minutes.
Identify one player doing it very well and ask them to demonstrate to the group. Peer learning is highly effective.
Do not add the moving component until the static pattern is clearly established in all players.
Connect to a real match moment: tell players about a specific professional player known for close control and ball mastery.
diagramDescription: Grid of 3x3m squares with corner cones. Ball in the centre of each square. Player stands over the ball. Arrows show inside-right push (ball goes left), inside-left push (ball returns right), outside-right push (ball goes right), outside-left push (ball returns left). Sequence is circular.

title: V-Pull Ball Mastery
objective: Master the V-pull move: dragging the ball across the body and pushing out in the opposite direction to deceive a defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the V-pull with both feet
Players understand when to use the V-pull in a match situation
Players develop the coordination to combine a drag-back with a directional change
Players build deception and disguise into their ball-carrying
equipment: 4 cones per player to mark the V-pull grid
organisation: Place 3 cones in a triangle per player: cone A at top (2m ahead), cone B at right (2m right), cone C at left (2m left). Player starts at centre with ball. The V-pull pattern uses all 3 cones.
instructions: Start at the centre with the ball. Face cone A (the top cone).
Dribble the ball toward cone A using the inside of the right foot.
At the moment you reach cone A, place the sole of your right foot on the ball.
Drag the ball backward toward your body (the drag-back).
As the ball rolls back, push it with the INSIDE of your LEFT foot toward cone C (to the left).
This creates a V shape: forward to cone A, drag back to centre, push left to cone C.
From cone C, push the ball back to centre with the inside of the right foot.
Now repeat: go to cone A, drag back, this time push RIGHT to cone B.
Practice the V-shape 10 times in each direction before combining freely.
After 6 minutes: the player decides which direction to go at cone A, creating a genuine deceptive movement.
coachingPoints: The fake is in the approach: approach cone A as if you are going PAST it. This is what sells the deception.
The drag-back starts with the sole firmly on top of the ball, not the toe or inside - the sole.
The drag-back pulls the ball toward the standing foot - keep it close, do not pull it too far back.
The push-out immediately follows the drag. There should be no pause between drag and push.
The push-out goes to the side, not forward yet. Get the ball to the side foot first.
After the push-out, accelerate. The move only works if you go quickly after it.
Eyes up through the move: you are watching the imaginary defender, not the ball.
The move must be performed in one fluid motion - drag-push-go is one continuous action.
Plant the standing foot wide of the ball on the drag to give room for the push-out.
At match pace the drag-back and push-out should take less than 0.5 seconds combined.
commonMistakes: Pausing between the drag-back and the push-out, giving a real defender time to recover
Using the toe instead of the sole on the drag-back, causing the ball to fly backward
Not accelerating after the push-out, meaning the move gains no ground
Performing the move at a static position rather than at dribbling pace
Only practicing to one side, creating a predictable and one-dimensional move
Looking at the ball throughout, losing the deceptive element of the move
corrections: Count: 1 (drag), 2 (push), 3 (go). They must be in a quick 1-2-3 sequence with no extra counts
Film the move and show the player: point out exactly where the pause occurs
For toe drag: put a sticker on the sole and tell them only the sticker should touch the ball
For no acceleration: place a cone 3m from the push-out target. They must reach that cone in 2 steps
For one-sided: alternate cones B and C on every rep - they never know which side until the drag-back starts
For ball-watching: ask them to describe what colour shirt you (the coach) are wearing while doing the move
progressions: V-pull with passive defender: a partner stands at cone A and the player must go past them using the V-pull
V-pull then dribble: after the push-out, dribble to a gate 5m away before resetting
Double V-pull: perform the move twice in succession before taking 3 dribbling touches
Reaction V-pull: player jogs on the spot until coach points to cone B or C - player immediately performs V-pull to that cone
regressions: Separate the skills: practice the drag-back alone (no push) for 2 minutes first
Use a marker line instead of a cone so there is no obstacle to worry about
Reduce to one direction only (always push left) until confident before adding right
Walk through the V-pull in slow motion - drag, pause, push, pause, go - to establish the muscle memory
matchApplication: The V-pull is one of the most effective 1v1 moves in senior football. It is used by advanced players when they receive the ball with a defender cutting off their forward route. The drag-back fakes the defender into going one way while the push-out goes the opposite direction, creating immediate space. The move is particularly effective in central midfield when a player wants to escape a pressing player and switch direction of attack.
coachTips: Demonstrate the move yourself from both directions before any player attempts it.
Show a video clip of a professional player using the V-pull (Xavi, Iniesta, and De Bruyne all use it regularly).
The deception element cannot be drilled without some form of pressure. Add a passive defender as soon as possible.
Do not rush the transition to live defenders - the technical pattern must be automatic first.
U10 players are ready for moves with real deceptive intent. Frame this as learning a professional move.
Keep a record of which players have mastered both directions and which need more work on the weaker side.
diagramDescription: 3 cones per player in a triangle: cone A 2m ahead (top), cone B 2m to the right, cone C 2m to the left. Player at centre. Arrow goes forward to cone A, curves back (drag), then splits to either cone B or cone C (push). Second arrow shows acceleration away from the selected cone.

title: Ball Mastery Pressure Circuit
objective: Develop high-speed ball mastery across 5 techniques under increasing time pressure.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute 5 ball mastery moves at competitive speed
Players develop automaticity - performing moves without thinking
Players build confidence handling the ball in tight spaces
Players learn to switch between different techniques fluidly
players: 1 per ball, groups of 4-6 per station
organisation: 4 personal squares arranged in a 2x2 grid with 3m between squares. Each square has a player with a ball. Coach stands at the side where all 4 squares are visible. Each round is 45 seconds of work followed by 15 seconds rest. Coach calls the move before each round.
instructions: ROUND 1 - Sole Rolls (45 sec): Use only the sole of alternating feet to roll the ball side to side, keeping it within the square.
REST 15 seconds - shake out the legs.
ROUND 2 - Inside Chops (45 sec): Use the inside of one foot to chop the ball to one side, then use the inside of the other foot to chop back. Rapid, close touches.
REST 15 seconds.
ROUND 3 - Outside Pushes (45 sec): Push the ball with the outside of the right foot to the right, then outside of left foot to the left. Small rapid touches.
REST 15 seconds.
ROUND 4 - Drag and Push (45 sec): Sole drag backward with right foot, push out with left inside. Repeat alternating drag feet.
REST 15 seconds.
ROUND 5 - Free Combination (45 sec): Players combine any moves they choose in any order, maintaining maximum control.
FINAL MINUTE - Competition: Who can perform 20 consecutive touches (any technique) without the ball leaving their square?
coachingPoints: Maximum speed is NOT the goal in rounds 1-4. Clean, controlled technique at 80 percent pace is the standard.
Each touch should be deliberate - know which surface you are using before your foot meets the ball.
Stay in your square throughout. Going out means the touch was too heavy and the control is not there yet.
Keep your weight on the balls of your feet throughout all rounds. Flat-footed players are always one step behind.
During rest periods, shake out your ankles and take 3 deep breaths. Recovery between rounds matters.
In the free combination round, vary your moves. Doing the same move repeatedly is not ball mastery - it is repetition.
Keep your eyes up for at least 50 percent of each round. Feel the ball with your feet.
The transition between different moves is the hardest part. Work on smooth transitions, not just individual moves.
Breathing: do not hold your breath. Players who hold their breath tire faster and tense up.
The competition final minute is timed but control is still the priority. A competition touch that leaves the square is a failure.
commonMistakes: Going maximum speed immediately and losing control in round 1
Defaulting to the dominant foot in all rounds
Stopping completely between moves rather than transitioning fluidly
Ball constantly leaving the square due to heavy touches
Standing upright with stiff legs throughout all rounds
Losing focus during rest periods and not recovering properly
corrections: Reduce square size by half for players whose ball leaves the zone: the smaller space forces softer touches
For dominant foot: tie a small ribbon to the weak ankle as a reminder to use it equally
For poor transitions: walk through the transition in slow motion between rounds 1 and 2
For stiff legs: start each round with 5 bounces in place to reset the athletic posture
For loss of focus in rest: give a specific task during rest (count your squares, check your laces) to maintain engagement
progressions: Reduce square size from 4x4m to 3x3m after each full circuit
Add a passive partner who reaches toward the ball during rounds (no tackle): player must maintain control while shielding
Extend work period to 60 seconds and reduce rest to 10 seconds after the first full circuit
In the combination round: name a move just before players start it so they must switch moves mid-sequence
regressions: Reduce to 30 seconds work and 30 seconds rest per round
Allow players to use larger squares (5x5m) if the 4x4m is too restrictive
Run only 3 rounds (sole, inside, combination) before building back to 5 in a later session
matchApplication: Ball mastery under pressure is the foundation of technical performance in matches. A player who can execute these techniques automatically, without thinking, can receive the ball in tight areas, evade a pressing opponent, and maintain possession in congested spaces. Midfielders who do this circuit consistently develop the first-touch and quick-feet qualities that professional clubs specifically look for in 10-13 year old players.
coachTips: Play music during the work periods and turn it off for rest. The music-rest structure helps players regulate their effort.
Do not interrupt rounds 1-4 with coaching. Let players work through the entire round then give feedback in the rest.
Walk around all 4 squares during work periods and give one specific piece of feedback per player per round.
Celebrate the weakest player succeeding just as loudly as the most talented. Ball mastery confidence is fragile at U11.
Record each players completion count in the competition minute. Show improvement over 4 sessions builds motivation.
This circuit can be used as a pre-session warm-up once players know all 5 moves.
diagramDescription: 2x2 grid of 4x4m squares with 3m between each. One player per square with a ball. Coach position marked at the corner of the grid. Each square shows inward arrows (ball controlled within square boundaries). Rounds are indicated by a sequence list beside the diagram.

title: Street Football Moves 1v1
objective: Apply 5 advanced ball mastery moves against a live defender in a 1v1 environment.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the scissors, step-over, Cruyff turn, elastico, and shoulder drop vs a defender
Players understand which move to use based on the defenders body position
Players develop the deception and timing needed to execute moves under real pressure
Players gain confidence and enjoyment from the 1v1 competitive format
players: Pairs (8-16 players total across multiple zones)
pitchSize: 10x8m per 1v1 zone. Multiple zones run simultaneously.
organisation: Set up pairs in 10x8m rectangular zones. Each pair has 1 ball and 1 bib (the defender wears the bib). A mini-goal (or 2-cone goal, 2m wide) sits on each short end of the zone. Players swap attacker and defender roles after every 2 minutes.
instructions: PHASE 1 (6 minutes): Attacker practices moves in isolation. Defender stands static at the midpoint. Attacker dribbles and performs the named move within 2m of the defender.
Coach names one move per 90-second period: scissors, step-over, Cruyff turn, shoulder drop, elastico.
Attacker performs the move and dribbles past the defender to the far mini-goal. Reset and repeat.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): Defender becomes semi-active (moves side to side but does not tackle). Attacker uses any move to get past and score.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): Full live 1v1. Defender can tackle. Attacker earns 1 point per move successfully completed AND 1 additional point for a goal. Defender earns 2 points per clean tackle.
Rotate roles every 2 minutes throughout all phases. Count points across the whole session.
coachingPoints: SCISSORS: swing one foot around the ball from inside to outside without touching it. The second foot goes the other way and takes the ball. Sell it with your body and eyes.
STEP-OVER: swing your foot over the top of the ball (outside to inside) without touching it. Plant that foot, push ball other way with outside of the foot that went over.
CRUYFF TURN: shape to pass or shoot, drag ball behind standing leg with inside of kicking foot, accelerate the other way. Speed before and after is critical.
ELASTICO: push ball one way with outside of foot, immediately flick it back the other way with the inside in one snapping motion. Requires flexible ankle.
SHOULDER DROP: dip one shoulder hard in the direction you are NOT going, shift weight that way, then push ball the other way and accelerate.
The move only works if it is unexpected. Approach the defender at dribbling speed, not slow speed.
Watch the defenders feet: if their weight goes to one side, push ball to the other immediately.
After the move, the first 2 steps away from the defender are the most important. Accelerate hard.
Do not repeat the same move twice in a row. A predictable move is an ineffective move.
The best 1v1 players use the defenders momentum against them. Wait for them to commit before moving.
commonMistakes: Performing the move too far from the defender - the move needs to be within 1-1.5m to work
Slowing down before the move, which signals to the defender that something is coming
Not accelerating immediately after the move, allowing the defender to recover
Using the same move regardless of the defenders body position
Over-complicating the move with too many steps - most effective 1v1 moves are very simple
Losing the ball during the move because the touch was too heavy or the wrong surface was used
corrections: Place a cone 1.5m in front of the defender: attacker must reach the cone before starting any move
For slowing down before the move: count aloud - 1 (normal dribble speed), 2 (move), 3 (go). 1 and 3 must sound the same
For no acceleration: place a cone 3m past the defender. Attacker must reach it in 2 touches after the move
For wrong move choice: freeze play and ask the attacker which way the defenders weight was on. Then ask which move exploits that
For heavy touch: reduce the speed of the drill to 50 percent until the touch stays close
progressions: 2v1 defending: defender gets a partner who can step in if the first defender is beaten (tests ability to beat one player and immediately handle another)
Points system for variety: a different move earns a different point value. Players are incentivised to use all 5 moves
Target gate: after beating the defender, must pass through a 1m gate 5m away. Tests quality of touch after the move
Time limit: attacker has 8 seconds to score from receiving the ball. Creates match-realistic urgency
regressions: Phase 1 only (static defender) for the full session if players cannot manage semi-active defending
Reduce to 2 moves (scissors and step-over) rather than 5
Increase zone to 15x10m to give more time and space for less confident players
matchApplication: 1v1 dribbling is one of the most game-changing skills in football. A player who can consistently beat a defender 1v1 creates numerical advantages, draws other defenders out of position, and generates the highest-quality chances. Wide players who beat their full-back regularly are among the most dangerous attackers in senior football. This drill builds exactly those skills in a competitive, enjoyable environment that U12 players connect with strongly.
coachTips: Keep the competitive element alive throughout. Post the points score where players can see it.
Do not over-coach during phase 3. Let them problem-solve. Observe and coach only in the transitions between phases.
Connect each move to a professional player famous for it: scissors - Ronaldo, Cruyff turn - Johan Cruyff, elastico - Ronaldinho.
Some players will have a natural preferred move. Allow them to develop that while encouraging them to practice all 5.
The session should feel like street football: free, competitive, and fun. Avoid too much structure.
End the session with a league: everyone plays everyone for 2 minutes. Most points after all matchups wins.
diagramDescription: Multiple 10x8m zones arranged side by side. Each zone has 2 mini-goals (2 cones each) on the short ends. Attacker (circle) starts at one end, defender (triangle) starts in the middle. Arrow shows attacker dribbling toward defender, performing a move (spiral), and continuing to score at the far end. Multiple zones run simultaneously.

title: Inside Foot Pass Foundations
objective: Teach the correct technique for the inside-of-foot pass along the ground.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the inside of the foot to pass the ball accurately
Players understand the correct body position for passing
Players can pass to a stationary target at 6-10m
Players develop the follow-through movement needed for accuracy
equipment: 4 cones per pair to mark passing distance
organisation: Players in pairs standing 6m apart facing each other. A cone marks each players position. Two side cones create a 3m wide corridor so players can see if passes are accurate (within the corridor). All pairs run simultaneously. Coach positioned at the side.
instructions: Player A stands at their cone with the ball at their feet.
Place the non-kicking foot 10cm beside the ball, pointing at the target (partner).
Turn the kicking foot outward so the inside (flat area on the inside of boot) faces the target.
Swing the kicking leg back from the knee, keeping the inside of the foot turned toward the target.
Contact the CENTRE of the ball with the INSIDE of the foot.
Follow through in a straight line toward the target. The kicking foot should finish pointing at your partner.
Player B receives the ball, stops it with the sole or inside of their foot, and passes back.
After 5 passes each: move cones to 8m apart. After 5 more: move to 10m.
Coach emphasises one technical point per 2-minute period so players are not overwhelmed.
Final 2 minutes: pass AND move after each pass (step 2m to the side before returning to the cone).
coachingPoints: PLANT FOOT: non-kicking foot beside the ball, pointing at your target. If the foot points left, the ball goes left.
BODY POSITION: slightly bent over the ball, not upright. Leaning back sends the ball into the air.
CONTACT SURFACE: the large flat area between the heel and the big toe on the inside of the boot. This is the most accurate passing surface.
ANKLE: lock the ankle of the kicking foot before contact. A floppy ankle means an inaccurate pass.
CONTACT POINT on the ball: the horizontal centre of the ball. Too low and it lifts, too high and it bounces awkwardly.
FOLLOW-THROUGH: the kicking foot continues toward the target after striking the ball. Short follow-through = short pass.
WEIGHT: pass with enough pace that the ball reaches your partner without stopping. U8 target is smooth rolling pass.
RECEPTION: receiver should prepare the next pass while the ball is travelling to them. No watching the ball arrive.
STANDING POSITION: feet shoulder-width apart, weight on the balls of feet. Ready to receive and send.
COMMUNICATION: look at your partner before passing. Do not pass without checking they are ready.
commonMistakes: Using the toe to kick the ball (toe-punching) which causes inaccuracy and inconsistency
Plant foot too far behind the ball, causing the pass to lift off the ground
Plant foot pointing in the wrong direction, causing the ball to go sideways
No follow-through, resulting in weak and inaccurate passes
Swinging the whole leg back from the hip rather than just from the knee
Receiver watching the ball arrive instead of preparing the return pass
Rushing the pass before setting up the correct body position
corrections: Toe-punching: tape a sticker on the inside of the boot and tell them only the sticker can touch the ball
Plant foot position: place a cone beside the ball where the plant foot should go and ask them to step on it
Plant foot direction: draw an arrow on the floor toward the partner showing where the foot should point
No follow-through: place a cone on the target line 50cm past the ball. After passing, the foot must touch past that cone
Leg swing: put a hand on the players knee and ask them to swing only below your hand
Receiver not preparing: count 3 seconds after receiving before they can pass back, forcing composure
progressions: Moving pass: both players jog toward each other, pass when 6m apart, jog back, repeat
Gate pass: place 2 cones 1m wide at the midpoint between players. Ball must go through the gate
1-touch return: player B receives and must pass back with 1 touch (no controlling touch first)
Target pass: a third cone replaces the partner and players must pass to hit the cone (accuracy challenge)
regressions: Shorten distance to 4m for players struggling with accuracy at 6m
Use a larger ball (size 5) to make the contact surface easier
Allow the player to place their foot slowly beside the ball and check the position before kicking
Start with a stationary ball rolled by the coach rather than one they have to control first
matchApplication: The inside-foot pass is the most commonly used technique in football at every level. Over 70 percent of all passes in a senior match are made with the inside of the foot because it provides the highest accuracy. Getting this technique correct at U8 is the single most important technical skill a young player can develop. Every combination play, every switch, every short link pass uses this technique.
coachTips: Demonstrate each technical point slowly and clearly from the same angle that the players use.
Give the technique a simple name they remember: flat face of the boot, not the toe or outside.
Pair players of similar height and strength where possible at U8. Pass weight across a big size mismatch is challenging.
Check plant foot direction before anything else. This is the root cause of most passing inaccuracy.
After 5 minutes of technique, give them 2 minutes of free practice with no instructions. Observe who retains what.
The gate (cone at midpoint) provides immediate feedback on accuracy without the coach having to say anything.
diagramDescription: Pairs of players facing each other 6m apart, marked by a cone at each end. Side cones create a 3m wide corridor. Ball shown passing from Player A to Player B along the corridor with a straight arrow. A small diagram shows the plant foot position beside the ball with the kicking foot contact point marked.

title: Pass and Support Triangle
objective: Develop passing accuracy, support movement, and the habit of moving after every pass in a 3-player triangle.
learningOutcomes: Players can pass accurately over 8-15m with the inside of the foot
Players understand the principle of following the pass (move after every pass)
Players develop the habit of offering support to the ball-carrier
Players build the basic concept of keeping the ball moving in a triangle shape
players: Groups of 3 (9-15 players total across multiple triangles)
pitchSize: Triangle with 10m sides per group. Multiple triangles set up across a 40x30m area.
organisation: 3 cones form a triangle with 10m sides. Each cone is labelled A, B, C. One player starts at each cone. One ball starts at cone A. All triangles run simultaneously. Coach positioned to see all groups.
instructions: Player A passes to Player B using the inside of the foot.
Immediately after passing, Player A moves to the next cone clockwise (A moves to where C is standing).
Player B controls the ball (1 touch to control, 1 touch to pass) and passes to Player C.
Immediately after passing, Player B moves clockwise to where A was.
Player C receives, controls, and passes to where Player A originally was (which is now a new player).
The ball goes around the triangle while players rotate clockwise after every pass.
After 4 minutes: reverse the rotation (anticlockwise). Change the passing direction too.
After 4 minutes: introduce a 2-touch maximum rule (1 control, 1 pass - no extra touches).
After 4 minutes: 1-touch only (no controlling touch - receive and immediately pass).
Final 3 minutes: players choose the pace and direction freely, continuing to move after every pass.
coachingPoints: MOVE IMMEDIATELY after passing. Do not watch to see if the pass was good. Pass and go.
The movement after the pass must be toward the next position, not sideways or backward.
Show for the ball: as the pass is being made to your cone, take a step toward the ball. Do not wait at the cone.
Body position when receiving: open your hips so you can see both the ball coming and where you want to pass next.
First touch direction: control the ball with a touch that sets up your next pass. Do not trap it under your foot.
Pass quality: the pass must arrive at a comfortable height (ankle to knee) and pace for your teammate.
Communicate: call the name of the player you are passing to just before you play the ball.
Eye contact: look at where you are passing BEFORE the ball arrives at you. Already know the next pass.
Distance between cones: 10m means a firm pass with laces or inside foot. Not a soft roll.
Support angle: the player waiting at the cone should not stand directly behind or in front - stay at the triangle angle.
commonMistakes: Not moving after the pass - standing and watching the ball instead
Moving only after seeing if the pass was accurate rather than immediately upon release
First touch not setting up the next pass - trapping the ball under the foot instead
Pass arriving too hard or too soft for the receiver
Players drifting off the triangle shape, creating angles that are too narrow or too wide
No communication - silent triangle with no calling of names or positions
corrections: Pass and chase: physically follow the ball immediately after every pass for the first 2 minutes. Running directly to where the ball went reinforces the movement habit
First touch: draw a direction arrow at each cone showing the recommended first touch direction
Pass weight: mark the passers feet with a cone and ask them to stay at it until the ball reaches the partner - watching teaches the correct pace
Triangles drifting: use fixed cones that cannot be moved so the shape is maintained
Communication: make it a rule that passes without calling the name do not count
progressions: Defender in the middle: 1 passive defender inside the triangle tries to intercept passes (3v1 rondo format)
Two-triangle combination: two triangles are placed 5m apart. Ball must be transferred between triangles before completing a rotation
Direction calls: coach calls REVERSE randomly and the triangle immediately switches rotation direction
1-touch pressure: 1-touch only, no exceptions. If a player takes 2 touches the ball resets to the start of that rotation
regressions: Reduce to just passing (no movement) for 2 minutes until the pass technique is consistent
Increase triangle sides to 15m to give more time on the ball
Start with 3 touches (control, look, pass) before reducing to 2
Allow players to call HELP if they want 5 extra seconds before passing
matchApplication: The pass-and-move principle is the foundation of all team combination play. A team where every player moves after passing creates constant new passing options and prevents static defending. This triangle drill directly replicates the movement patterns seen in professional midfield play: receive, set your first touch to face forward, and pass with a teammate already moving to receive. The 1-touch progression replicates modern high-tempo play where holding the ball in tight areas risks immediate pressure.
coachTips: Walk between the triangles during the drill, not around the outside. Presence within the group gives better feedback.
The key habit to build is the IMMEDIATE movement. Clap each time a player moves before the pass arrives at the next cone.
If a group is consistently losing shape, pause the group (not all groups) and reset their triangle with the cones.
The 1-touch phase will feel chaotic at first. Let it breathe for 90 seconds before providing feedback.
Count the number of consecutive successful passes in each triangle and celebrate improvements.
Link this to match situations: tell players this is exactly what happens when a winger receives, controls, and passes to the overlapping full-back.
diagramDescription: Equilateral triangle with 10m sides, cone at each corner (labelled A, B, C). Player at each cone. Ball shown passing from A to B (straight arrow). Curved arrow shows A moving clockwise toward C position. B then passes to C while B moves clockwise. Multiple triangles across the area.

title: Rondo 4v1 Square
objective: Develop quick decision-making, accurate short passing, and support angles in a confined possession exercise.
learningOutcomes: Players can keep possession of the ball under defensive pressure with 2 touches
Players understand how to create passing angles by moving constantly
Players develop the communication skills needed in possession situations
Players build composure and decision-making speed under pressure
players: 5 per group (4 attackers, 1 defender). Run multiple groups simultaneously.
organisation: 4 cones form a 10x10m square. 4 attackers, one on each corner outside the square, plus 1 defender inside the square. All groups run simultaneously. Coach moves between groups. Defender wears a bib. Swap defender when: defender touches the ball, ball goes out, or after 2 minutes.
instructions: 4 attackers start outside the square, one at each cone. 1 defender starts inside.
Attackers keep the ball from the defender using passes along the outside edges of the square.
Maximum 2 touches per attacker: 1 to control, 1 to pass.
Passes can go directly to adjacent corners or diagonally across the square.
The defender tries to intercept passes or touch the ball.
If the defender intercepts: the player who played the losing pass becomes the new defender.
If ball goes out of play: the player who played it out becomes the defender.
After 2 minutes regardless of outcome: rotate defender clockwise.
Attackers may not stand still. They must always be showing for the ball and adjusting position.
After 8 minutes progress to 1-touch only for the outer attackers.
coachingPoints: SUPPORT ANGLES: never stand directly in line with the ball and another attacker. Create a triangle so the ball-carrier always has 2 options.
MOVEMENT: if you pass, you should move slightly along the edge to create a new angle. Never stand still after passing.
BODY POSITION: hips open so you can see both where the ball is coming from and where you can pass to. Do not face the ball with your chest.
FIRST TOUCH: control the ball with a touch that keeps it moving forward. A touch that stops the ball dead kills the tempo.
DECISION BEFORE RECEIVING: as the pass is coming to you, already know your next pass. Do not decide after you receive.
DIAGONAL PASS: using the diagonal (across the square) is the highest-value pass as it skips a section and makes the defender travel furthest.
SPEAK EARLY: call the name of who you want to receive before the ball leaves your foot. Give your teammate time to prepare.
DEFENDER AWARENESS: know where the defender is before the ball arrives. If defender is on your side, pass away. If far, you have a touch more time.
TEMPO: the tempo must be high enough that the defender cannot get set. Slow play makes the exercise easy for the defender.
MISTAKES: a mistake (ball lost or out) is not failure. Diagnose WHY it was lost and fix it in the next sequence.
commonMistakes: Attackers standing still at their cone and waiting for the ball rather than moving
Playing the safest pass rather than the best pass (always going adjacent instead of diagonal)
2-touch becoming 3-touch: receiving, stopping the ball, then passing
Players facing the incoming ball chest-on rather than hips-open
Passing without checking where the defender is first
Defender ball-watching rather than positioning to cover multiple options
corrections: Static attackers: add a rule that an attacker must step at least 1m away from their cone before receiving
Always safe pass: reward diagonal passes with 2 points instead of 1 to incentivise them
3-touch: add a strict count rule - a 3rd touch means the player immediately goes in as defender
Chest-on receiving: place a cone behind each attacker. They must keep the cone visible when receiving (forces hips open)
Not checking defender: pause the group when a player looks only at the ball and ask them to describe where the defender is
Defender ball-watching: give the defender a task (cover the most dangerous angle) to make them think positionally
progressions: Expand to 4v2: two defenders in the square. Attackers need to find the safest pass more quickly.
1-touch rule: all passes must be first-time. Forces decision-making before receiving.
Score with through pass: attackers earn a point by playing a successful ground pass diagonally through the square to the far corner.
Direction call: coach calls SWITCH and attackers must immediately change the direction of circulation.
regressions: Increase square to 12x12m to give more space and time on the ball
Allow 3 touches (control, set, pass) before reducing to 2
Passive defender: defender can only walk at half pace
No diagonal passes allowed: only adjacent corner passes until the group is comfortable with 2-touch
matchApplication: The rondo (possession circle) is the single most widely used passing drill in world football. Every professional club from grassroots to elite uses it daily. The rondo develops the technical and cognitive skills needed to keep possession under pressure: soft first touch, quick decision-making, supporting angles, and communication. The 4v1 format directly replicates central midfield play where a player receives under pressure and must quickly find a free teammate. The defenders urgency replicates the closing pressure faced in every match.
coachTips: The rondo can be used as a warm-up, a main session drill, or a pre-match activation - it is completely versatile.
Watch the attackers movement when they do NOT have the ball. This is the most important coaching moment - are they creating angles?
The best rondo players are those who decide what to do BEFORE the ball arrives. Coach this specific moment.
Use the rondo to identify your quickest thinkers. They are not always the most technically gifted players.
Let the groups run independently and move between them giving 1 coaching point per group per rotation.
Connect to match: show a video of Barcelona or Man City using the rondo shape in a game. U11s love professional connections.
diagramDescription: 10x10m square with 4 corner cones. 4 attackers (circles) positioned at each corner outside the square. 1 defender (triangle) inside the square. Arrows show passing options: adjacent corners (2 options) and diagonal pass (1 option across the square). Defender shown in centre with coverage arc indicating the angle they can cover.

title: Breaking Lines with a Forward Pass
objective: Develop the ability to identify and execute forward passes that break through defensive lines.
learningOutcomes: Players understand what a line-breaking pass is and why it is valuable
Players can identify the correct moment to play a vertical pass vs holding possession
Players develop the technique to play firm accurate passes in behind a defensive line
Players build the decision-making habit of looking forward first before passing sideways
players: 11 per session (or half a team split)
pitchSize: Half pitch (50x35m). Three horizontal zones: defensive (15m), middle (20m), attacking (15m).
organisation: Lay out 3 horizontal zones with cone lines. A back 4 defends in the defensive zone and may not enter the middle zone. 4 midfielders in the middle zone may not enter the attacking or defensive zones. 2 attackers in the attacking zone. 1 goalkeeper defends the full-size goal at the back of the defensive zone. Attacking team starts in their zones. Coach observes from the side.
instructions: Set up: 4 midfielders in the central zone (20m wide), 4 defenders restricted to the defensive zone, 2 strikers restricted to the attacking zone. GK in goal.
Ball starts with the midfielders in the central zone.
Midfielders keep possession within their zone. Defenders press from inside their zone only.
PHASE 1: A line-breaking pass is played when a midfielder finds a striker in the attacking zone BETWEEN or BEHIND the defender line.
When a line-breaking pass reaches a striker: the restriction lifts. 1 midfielder may enter the attacking zone to support the 2v4.
If the pass is completed: strikers and supporting midfielder try to score.
If a defender intercepts: they play out to a wide target player (coach or neutral) and the sequence resets.
PHASE 2 (after 10 min): restrictions loosened. Defenders can press to the edge of the central zone. Midfielders can now enter the attacking zone after the line-breaking pass.
Award 1 point for a line-breaking pass completed to a striker. 3 points for a goal from a line-breaking pass. 0 points for goals from possessional play without a line break.
Run for 20 minutes. First team to 10 points wins.
coachingPoints: A LINE-BREAKING PASS goes THROUGH or IN BETWEEN the defensive line - not around the outside of it.
Timing is critical: the pass must be played the moment the striker creates space between defenders, not before (offside risk) and not after (defender closes).
Weight of pass: firm enough to beat the defender but not so hard the striker cannot control it at pace.
Where to play it: aim for the space between where the striker IS and where they are RUNNING. Lead them into the space.
Communication: the striker must signal (verbal or non-verbal) that they are making the run before the midfielder passes.
Deception before the pass: look the other way, fake a square pass, then play the vertical. Deception creates the split second needed.
Midfielder body position: open hips to see all options including the vertical. A midfielder facing sideways cannot see forward.
The decision rule: look forward first. Only play sideways if the vertical option genuinely does not exist.
After the line-breaking pass: the midfielder who played it must IMMEDIATELY support - do not admire the pass.
The striker must time their run: start too early = offside trap spring. Start too late = pass cannot be played. The run triggers the pass.
commonMistakes: Midfielders playing sideways when a clear vertical option exists (looking for safety instead of penetration)
Pass played too early before the striker has beaten the offside line
Pass played too late when the striker has already beaten the defenders and the pass is now easy to intercept
Pass weight too light: arrives at the striker at a comfortable height for the defender to intercept
Striker not timing the run to the pass: either starting too early (offside) or waiting until the pass is already being played
Midfielder facing the wrong way and not seeing the vertical option at all
corrections: Freeze game when a sideways pass is played and a vertical was available: show the group the option that was missed
Offside timing: use a freeze-and-show approach. Blow whistle when the striker makes the run and ask: is this the right moment?
Pass weight: set up a target 15m ahead and demand the pass reaches it in 2 seconds or less (lightweight passes fail this test)
Midfielder orientation: before the drill starts, all midfielders must set their feet to face the forward zone (side-on at minimum)
Striker timing: walk through the striker run and the midfielder pass timing in slow motion before live reps
progressions: Add a pressing trigger: when a defender steps into the central zone (pressing), the midfielder must immediately play the vertical
Remove the zone restriction entirely and let the full game flow: but award 3 points only for line-breaking goals
2-touch central zone: midfielders have maximum 2 touches which increases passing speed and urgency
Add a second line: put a 3rd horizontal line so there are 2 lines to break through, testing the ability to connect multiple line-breaking passes
regressions: Allow midfielders to enter the attacking zone freely: removes the need for the precision vertical pass
Reduce to 2 defenders (back 2 instead of back 4) to create more space for the vertical pass
Start with a passive defending back 4 (they hold position but do not press actively)
matchApplication: Line-breaking passes are the single most important progressive actions in modern football. Every elite pressing team (Man City, Liverpool, Barcelona) specifically trains their players to identify and execute vertical passes that break the opponents defensive structure. A single line-breaking pass can eliminate 4-6 opponents from the game in one action. The ability to see and play the vertical pass before the horizontal one is the primary difference between a possession player and a penetrating player at senior level.
coachTips: The scoring system is the most important part of this drill. If you remove line-breaking goals scoring 3x, players will naturally default to safer sideways passes.
Pause the game regularly (every 3-4 minutes) for a 30-second team debrief. Show the group the available options on the freeze frame.
Film the drill from above if possible. The overhead view shows the gaps in the defensive line that players on the ground cannot see.
Connect to video: show footage of De Bruyne or Trent Alexander-Arnold playing vertical passes through defensive lines.
This drill exposes the thinking speed of each midfielder. Fast thinkers see the vertical immediately. Work specifically with slower thinkers.
At U14, this is an advanced concept. Introduce it gradually: start with the restriction game before moving to full freedom.
diagramDescription: Half pitch divided into 3 horizontal zones with cone lines: defensive zone (15m, bottom), middle zone (20m, centre), attacking zone (15m, top). 4 defenders (triangles) in defensive zone. 4 midfielders (circles) in middle zone. 2 strikers (stars) in attacking zone. GK in goal at back. Arrow shows a line-breaking pass from midfielder in central zone, through the gap between defenders (red line), to striker in attacking zone. Striker makes an angled run shown by a dotted arrow.

title: Long Switch of Play
objective: Develop the technique and decision-making for accurate long-range switching passes across the full width of the pitch.
learningOutcomes: Players can play an accurate driven or lofted long pass across 35-50m
Players understand when to switch play (ball overloaded, weak side open)
Players develop the technique: laces drive for flat switches, inside foot lofted for aerial switches
Players can execute the switch under pressure and immediately support after playing it
pitchSize: Full pitch width (60-68m wide) x 50m long
organisation: Full pitch width, 50m long. Wide target zones (8m x 15m) on each flank. A central zone (20m wide) where the switching player receives. Defend: 2 wide defending players, 2 central defenders. Attack: 2 central midfielders, 2 wingers in the wide zones. Coach observes from halfway.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Technical (8 minutes): Pairs at 35m, 45m, 55m apart practice the driven inside-foot long pass. 5 passes each distance. Focus: accuracy (ball must land within 2m of the stationary partner) and height (below head height on arrival).

PHASE 2 - Technique switch (4 minutes): Same distances but using a lofted ball (chip technique): contact under the ball, slight lean back, follow-through up. Ball should arrive above knee height but below head for a half-volley control.

PHASE 3 - Conditioned game (13 minutes): 4v4 in a 40x30m central area. Wide target players in the 8x15m zones on each flank. The ball can only be played to the wide targets via a long switch pass (minimum 30m). No short passes out wide allowed.
When a switch reaches a wide target: the wide player has 3 touches maximum before crossing or cutting inside.
The team that switched wins 1 point for a completed switch, 3 points for a goal following a switch.
Teams must switch at least twice before attempting a goal.
Central players cannot enter the wide zones. Wide targets cannot enter the central area.
coachingPoints: DRIVEN LONG PASS TECHNIQUE: approach at a slight angle. Plant foot 10-15cm beside the ball. Ankle locked, toes slightly down. Contact through the centre-centre of the ball with the laces. Follow through high. This keeps the ball flat and fast.

LOFTED PASS TECHNIQUE: plant foot beside ball. Contact UNDER the ball at the bottom third. Lean slightly back on contact. Follow through upward. Ball will arc. Use this for passes over 45m or when there is pressure that means a flat pass would be intercepted.
WHEN TO SWITCH: look for the weak side (more space, fewer defenders, winger in a 1v1 advantage). Do not switch just to switch - switch because it creates a better situation.
BODY POSITION BEFORE SWITCHING: before the switch pass arrives at the central player, they should already know they are going to switch. Open hips to see both sides.
DISGUISE: look toward the ball side before playing the switch. The switch works better if defenders are not anticipating it.
WEIGHT: the pass must arrive at a weight the winger can control first-time or with one touch. Too hard and it runs out of play. Too soft and it is intercepted before it arrives.
COMMUNICATION: the winger on the far side should make an early calling movement (a wave or a shout) to indicate they are in a better position.
AFTER THE SWITCH: the player who switched must immediately become a support option. Do not watch the ball - move.
RECEIVING THE SWITCH: the winger must communicate and move to a position where they can control the ball at pace - not static at the touchline.
TIMING: the switch is most effective immediately after the ball has been moved toward one side, when defenders have committed. The far side then has maximum space.
commonMistakes: Switch pass too light: ball arrives at the winger at walking pace and is easily intercepted
Switching at the wrong moment: switching when the ball side is not genuinely overloaded
Lofted switch too high: ball arcs down steeply and is difficult for the winger to control
No disguise before the switch: the long pass is telegraphed by the body position, allowing defenders to intercept
Winger not ready to receive: static position means the ball goes past them or they need multiple touches to bring it under control
Central player facing the wrong way before receiving, meaning they cannot see the weak side
corrections: Light pass: place a target (cone or person) 35m away. Pass must reach it in under 2.5 seconds (times with phone stopwatch)
Wrong timing: freeze game and ask the group: which side has more space right now? Confirm the correct switch moment
High loft: videoing the pass and showing the player the contact point (too far under the ball = too high)
Telegraphing: instruct player to look left before playing the switch right. Forces the look-away disguise
Winger not ready: give the winger a 3-step movement rule - they must take 3 steps toward the ball as the switch is played
Wrong orientation: place a cone behind the central player showing the weak side direction. They must be able to see it before receiving
progressions: Add defenders in the wide zones (1v1 for the winger): tests whether the switch creates a genuine 1v1 advantage
One-touch switch: central player must switch first time (no control touch)
No target zones: wingers move freely, central player must identify the right winger from both options
Full 8v8 game with switch reward: 3-point goals from switches vs 1-point goals from build-up
regressions: Reduce distance to 25m first to establish technique before building to 45m
Use a larger central zone to give more time before playing the switch
Slow the game to 70 percent pace in phase 3 before full intensity
matchApplication: Switching play is one of the highest-value actions in senior football. When a team compresses the play to one side and then quickly switches to the other side with an accurate long pass, the opposition is completely disorganised. The wide player on the far side typically has 3-5 seconds of space before the defence can recover. This creates the best-quality crossing and 1v1 situations in the game. Every top team from Pep Guardiolas City to Ancelottis Real Madrid trains the switch as a primary attacking weapon.
coachTips: U16 players are physically capable of the full 40-50m switch pass. Do not reduce the distance to make it easier - teach the technique at full range.
The lofted switch is harder to intercept than the driven switch. Coach both techniques explicitly.
The timing of the switch (not just the technique) is the most important learning point at this age.
Film the conditioned game from above if possible. The wide spaces are only visible from an aerial view.
Celebrate the quality switch even if it does not lead to a goal. The decision is more important than the outcome in the training environment.
Connect to professional footage: show Trent Alexander-Arnold switching play from right to left with lofted precision passes.
diagramDescription: Full-width pitch (60m wide) x 50m long. Central 20m zone in the middle. Wide zones (8m x 15m) on each flank. Ball shown in central zone (central player receives). Long curved arrow across full width shows the switch pass to the opposite wide zone winger. Winger shown in the wide zone ready to receive. Defenders shown concentrated on the ball side, creating a gap on the switch side. Dotted arrows show central players moving to support after the switch.

title: Cushion and Control
objective: Teach players to use a cushioned touch to bring the ball under instant control with the inside of the foot.
learningOutcomes: Players can use a withdrawing cushion touch to control a moving ball
Players understand the difference between a hard stop and a soft cushion
Players can control passes along the ground at different speeds
Players position their body correctly to receive the ball
organisation: Pairs 12m apart. Player A is the server (controls quality and pace). Player B is the receiver. Cones mark the receiving position. After 5 receives each, swap roles. Run multiple pairs simultaneously.
instructions: Player A rolls a pass to Player B at walking pace first.
As the ball approaches, Player B steps forward to meet it (do not wait for it to come to you).
Place the inside of the receiving foot out toward the ball.
At the moment of contact: WITHDRAW the foot slightly backward in the direction the ball is travelling. This cushions the impact.
The ball should slow to a stop within 1 step of the controlling foot.
After controlling: pass immediately back to Player A.
After 5 reps at walking pace: Player A passes at jogging pace.
After 5 reps: Player A passes at running pace (firm pass).
After 5 reps: Player A passes varying between low, medium, and high pace randomly. Player B must adapt.
Final 3 minutes: move the receiving cone so Player B receives on their weaker foot instead.
coachingPoints: MOVE TO MEET THE BALL: take 1-2 steps forward as the ball arrives. Receiving on your back foot is the most common first-touch error.
CUSHION vs STOP: a cushioned touch absorbs the balls momentum. A stopped touch kills the ball dead. A cushion keeps the ball alive and in front of you.
THE WITHDRAWAL: as the inside of the foot meets the ball, pull the foot back in the same direction the ball is travelling. This absorbs the pace.
BODY POSITION: slight bend in the knee of the standing leg. Body leans slightly forward. Do not lean back - this sends the ball up in the air.
FOOT HEIGHT: the inside of the foot should be at the same height as the centre of the ball - roughly ankle height for a ground pass.
EYES ON THE BALL: track the ball all the way into contact. Looking away causes miscontrol.
FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION: the cushion should place the ball slightly to one side of centre, ready for the return pass. Not directly under the foot.
WEIGHT TRANSFER: as the ball is cushioned, weight shifts to the back foot slightly to allow the foot to withdraw comfortably.
SOFT ANKLE: a tense ankle blocks the cushion. Relax the ankle before contact to allow the withdrawing movement.
commonMistakes: Waiting for the ball to come to them instead of stepping forward to meet it
Letting the ball hit the foot without withdrawing: the ball bounces away uncontrolled
Tensing the ankle at the moment of contact which blocks the cushion
Leaning backward at contact which causes the ball to bounce up into the air
Controlling the ball directly under the foot making the next pass awkward
Looking at where they want to pass BEFORE the ball reaches them causing a miscontrol
corrections: Step forward: place a cone 2m in front of the receiving position. Player must step past the cone to receive (forces forward movement)
Bouncing ball: reduce the pass speed. At slower pace the withdrawal habit has time to form before increasing intensity
Tense ankle: ask the player to shake out their foot before each receive to reset ankle relaxation
Leaning backward: place a cone just behind the players heel so they feel the consequence of leaning back
Ball under foot: draw a small circle 30cm to the side of the foot position where the ball should rest after control
progressions: Control and turn: after the cushion touch, immediately turn 90 degrees and pass to a third player positioned at the side
Reduce the distance to 8m: gives less time for the receive, replicating match pressure
Server varies the height: sometimes rolls, sometimes lofts to knee height, testing different control surfaces
1-touch: challenge of receiving and immediately passing with 1 touch (advanced)
regressions: Use a larger ball at slower speed to make the cushion easier to feel
Start with a static ball that the coach sets rolling slowly from very short range (3m)
Allow the player to use two hands on the ball first to understand the withdrawal concept before using feet
matchApplication: First-touch control is one of the most important technical skills in football because every time a player receives the ball in a match, the quality of the first touch determines everything that follows. A player who cushions the ball under control immediately has time to look up and make a decision. A player who miscontrols has to chase the ball while opponents close in. Professional players receive thousands of passes per training session specifically to automate the cushion touch so it becomes completely unconscious.
coachTips: The cushion concept is easier to understand with hands first. Demonstrate catching a ball and letting your hands absorb the impact before introducing the foot version.
Stand beside the receiver and hold your hand lightly at their ankle height as the ball arrives. Their foot should meet your hand at contact and withdraw away from it.
Film the miscontrol moments and show the player where the foot was (stiff and forward rather than soft and withdrawing).
Use a gentle tone when correcting: the natural instinct is to let the ball come to you. Rewiring this takes 3-4 sessions.
The first 5 reps at walking pace are the most important. Do not rush to faster speeds before the cushion habit is forming.
diagramDescription: Pair of players 12m apart. Cones mark receiving position for Player B. Ball (with speed arrow) travels from Player A to Player B. At receiving position: diagram of Player Bs foot showing the inside surface meeting the ball, with a small backward arrow showing the withdrawal direction. Ball shown settled 30cm to the side of the standing foot in a controlled position.

title: Turn on First Touch
objective: Develop the ability to receive a pass with the back to goal and use the first touch to turn and face forward.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the inside-foot turn to change direction on the first touch
Players can use the outside-foot hook to turn on the first touch
Players can assess when it is safe to turn vs when they must lay off
Players develop the body position needed to receive with the back to goal
equipment: Cones to mark receiving position and target areas
organisation: Player A (server) stands 10m behind Player B. Player B stands with back to Player A, facing Player C who stands 10m in front. B receives from A, turns, and passes to C. C then becomes the server, passing to B from the front as B turns to face A... sequence continues. Passive defender added in progressions.
instructions: Player B stands with back to Player A (back to goal in the match context).
B calls for the ball: takes a step forward (away from A) then comes short to receive.
A passes to Bs feet. As the ball arrives, B selects a turn technique:
INSIDE FOOT TURN: let the ball travel across the body, use the inside of the FAR foot to redirect the ball 90 degrees and accelerate through the turn.
OUTSIDE HOOK: as ball arrives, use the outside of the NEAR foot to hook the ball around and behind the body, turning 180 degrees to face forward.
After the turn: Player B passes to Player C (who is now in front).
Player C becomes the new A (server from behind). Player B turns again, now receiving from C and turning to find A.
PHASE 2 (after 8 minutes): Passive defender added 1m behind B. B must assess: if defender is close, lay the ball back to A. If space, turn.
PHASE 3: Semi-active defender. B makes the turn-or-lay-off decision for real.
Count successful turns (points for B) and successful defender intercepts (points for defender). Swap every 3 minutes.
coachingPoints: THE SETUP MOVEMENT: take a step AWAY from the server (backward step) before coming short to receive. This creates space between you and the defender.
BODY SHAPE ON RECEIVE: receive on the half-turn (hips at 45 degrees to the ball) so you can already see both sides before it arrives.

INSIDE FOOT TURN: as the ball arrives, angle the body sideways. Let the ball travel slightly past you. Place the inside of the far foot (the one further from the ball direction) on the ball and redirect it around your body.
OUTSIDE HOOK: use the outside of the near foot to flick the ball behind the standing leg in one movement. This requires flexibility in the ankle and a low centre of gravity.
THE DECISION (turn or lay-off): feel for the defender with your back and arms before receiving. If the defender is within arms-reach: lay off. If there is a body-length of space: turn.
FIRST TOUCH AWAY FROM PRESSURE: regardless of the turn type, the first touch must move the ball away from where the defender is.
LOOK BEFORE TURNING: quick glance over the shoulder before the pass arrives. This tells you where the defender is WITHOUT giving them information about your intent.
PROTECT WHILE TURNING: use the standing body to shield the ball during the turn. The body is always between the ball and the defender.
AFTER THE TURN: immediately look up. The turn created the advantage. Now use it by playing forward quickly.
DECEPTION: let the defender think you are going one way before hooking or turning the other. Sell the fake direction.
commonMistakes: Receiving the ball directly in front of the body (chest-on) instead of on the half-turn
Not creating space before receiving (standing still at the receiving position rather than moving to create room)
Turning regardless of the defenders position (turning when a lay-off was the correct decision)
First touch going directly toward the defender on the turn
Over-ambitious turn attempt that results in miscontrol and possession loss
Not using the body to shield during the turn
corrections: Half-turn receiving: place a cone to the side of B at 45 degrees and ask them to always be able to see that cone when receiving
Not creating space: add a rule - B must take 1 step away before every receive. If they do not, the pass does not count
Turn-or-lay-off: freeze the moment before the ball arrives and ask B where the imaginary defender is - can you turn?
Turn direction toward defender: after each turn, ask B to point to where the defender was. Was the first touch away from them?
Miscontrol on ambitious turn: regress to inside-foot turn only (simpler) before reintroducing outside hook
progressions: Live 1v1: real defender who can tackle. B earns points for successful turns and passes to C. Defender earns points for intercepts.
Immediately shoot after turning: instead of passing to C, B turns and shoots at a mini-goal beyond C (replicates the striker receive-turn-shoot movement)
3-touch maximum: B has maximum 3 touches from receive to pass. Increases urgency and quality of first touch.
Server from different angles: A serves from the right of B, then from left, testing turn technique on different approach angles
regressions: Static ball on the ground: B places their foot on the ball and practices the turn movement without a pass coming in
Walk-through pace: serve the ball very slowly at walking pace to allow more time for the turn technique
No defender at all in phase 1: allow B to choose any turn freely before adding the defender decision
matchApplication: Receiving with the back to goal and turning is the primary skill of a central striker or number 10. But it is equally important for central defenders receiving pressure, midfielders receiving in tight areas, and any player who receives on a transition facing the wrong direction. The turn-or-lay-off decision is what separates the best technical players at youth level: they see the defender before the ball arrives, make the decision before the ball arrives, and execute the first touch in the right direction. The great number 9s in world football (Firmino, Lewandowski, Kane) are all exceptional at this exact movement.
coachTips: Do not teach both turns in the same session. Spend a full session on the inside turn before introducing the outside hook.
The half-turn body shape is the most important coaching point in the whole drill. If they receive chest-on, the turn is impossible at match pace.
Film the sequence from behind the server so you can see the body angle, the turn, and the defender position all in one shot.
Make the decision-making phase (turn vs lay-off) the explicit focus in phase 2 and 3. Ask the group to narrate their decisions aloud.
Strikers especially love this drill because it is directly relevant to their role. Show Firmino or Harry Kane highlights of this exact technique.
