diagramDescription: 15x10m area. Player B in the centre. Player A stands 10m behind B (server from the back). Player C stands 10m in front of B (target). Arrow from A to B shows the incoming pass. B shown in half-turn body position. Curved arrow shows the inside foot turn around the body. Second arrow from B to C shows the pass out after the turn. Passive defender (D) shown 1m behind B in phase 2. Small inset diagram shows the half-turn body angle (hips at 45 degrees).

title: The Clinical Finish - Inside the Box
objective: Develop confident, accurate finishing inside the penalty area from various angles and distances.
learningOutcomes: Players can finish with the inside of the foot to the corner of the goal
Players can finish with the laces (driven shot) from central positions
Players develop composure in front of goal under pressure
Players learn to pick their spot before shooting and commit to it
organisation: 5 shooting positions set up around the penalty area: P1 = right side, 14m, 45-degree angle. P2 = right side, 10m, narrow angle. P3 = centre, 16m (penalty spot area). P4 = left side, 10m, narrow angle. P5 = left side, 14m, 45-degree angle. A server stands 5m outside each position with a supply of balls. GK in goal. Shooters rotate through all 5 positions.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Unopposed (8 minutes): Server plays ball to feet of shooter at each position. Shooter takes 1 controlling touch then finishes. GK is live but does not receive coaching on saves during this phase.
At P1 and P5 (wide angles): aim for the FAR bottom corner. Near-post finishes from wide angles are the most common goalkeeper-saved attempts.
At P2 and P4 (narrow angles): aim for the near post bottom corner. Tight angle means the far post is too long a distance to find reliably.
At P3 (centre): either corner low. Decision made BEFORE receiving the ball based on the GKs position.
PHASE 2 - With pressure (8 minutes): A passive defender stands 2m behind the shooter as they receive. Defender cannot tackle but creates awareness of pressure.
Shooter must still pick a corner and commit to it. Do not change the decision mid-shot.
PHASE 3 - From crosses (4 minutes): Server delivers low crosses from P1 and P5. Shooters make near-post and far-post runs and finish from the delivery.
coachingPoints: PICK YOUR SPOT BEFORE YOU SHOOT: as the ball is being played to you, look at the GK and decide which corner to aim for. NEVER decide after you receive.
COMMIT TO YOUR DECISION: once you pick a corner, do not change it when the GK moves. Changing mid-shot is the most common cause of misses at U13.
WIDE ANGLES (P1, P5): always aim for the far bottom corner. The GK covers the near post naturally by standing in the correct position. The far bottom corner is always less covered.
NARROW ANGLES (P2, P4): bend the ball toward the near post. The goal is narrower from here - the far post is almost unreachable.
INSIDE FOOT FOR PLACEMENT: use the inside of the foot for all non-power shots. It provides the most accuracy.
LACES FOR POWER: if the GK is well set and both corners are covered, a shot with the laces (driven through the centre of the ball) is harder to save than a placed shot.
BODY POSITION: slight lean forward over the ball prevents the shot rising. Leaning back means it goes into the crowd.
LOW SHOTS: shots below the GKs knee are statistically the most likely to score. Train to finish low by default.
CONTROLLING TOUCH: the first touch must put the ball slightly forward and away from the body. Not directly under the foot.
COMPOSURE: slow down the approach into the shot. Many missed chances come from rushing. Take one extra touch if needed.
THE MOMENT AFTER A MISS: reset mentally immediately. A good finisher gives every attempt the same confidence regardless of the last shot.
commonMistakes: Changing the decision mid-shot when the goalkeeper moves
Aiming for the near post from wide angles (the lowest-percentage finish)
Ball rising consistently because the player is leaning back
Control touch going too far, meaning the shot comes from a worse position
Looking at the ball when shooting rather than at the corner they have selected
No mental routine before finishing - just reacting randomly
corrections: Changing decisions: ask the player to point to the corner they are aiming for before receiving. Then commit to that corner only
Wide angle near post: explain with cones where the goalkeeper naturally stands at a wide angle and why the near post is always covered
Shot rising: place a 1m high string between 2 poles 2m in front of goal. Every shot must go under it
Heavy control touch: mark a 1m x 1m shooting box with cones. The first touch AND the shot must both come from inside the box
Rushing the shot: add a 1-count after receiving (player must count 1 before shooting) to force composure
progressions: Live defender: full active defender who can block or tackle before the shot is taken
Goalkeeper-guided decision: coach calls which corner as the ball is served. Player must change their plan in real time
Moving into positions: instead of a static receive, server plays the ball into space and shooter runs onto it to finish first time
Combination and finish: add a simple 1-2 combination before the finish (striker combines with a CM then finishes)
regressions: No goalkeeper in phase 1: removes the decision element and allows pure technique practice
Increase distance to 20m: the extra distance makes the angles wider and finishes simpler
Allow 2 controlling touches before shooting
matchApplication: Finishing is the highest-value technical skill in football because it directly determines whether a team scores. A team can dominate possession but if their strikers cannot convert clear chances they will not win matches. The 5-position system in this drill replicates exactly the types of chances created in youth football: cut-in from wide, through on goal centrally, and crosses from both sides. The key learning point - decide before you receive - is something that separates elite finishers from average ones and is directly transferable to match performance.
coachTips: Keep the session high-repetition. 6 shooters rotating through 5 positions means each player shoots 8-10 times in 20 minutes. Volume is key for finishing confidence.
Never dwell on missed shots at U13. Acknowledge, correct technically, and immediately move to the next attempt.
Track conversion rate: shots taken vs goals scored. Challenge each player to hit a personal best each session.
Ask the goalkeeper for their observations after each phase. Keepers often notice things about shooters that coaches miss from the side.
The mental routine (pick your spot before you shoot) should be repeated as a mantra every time a player steps into a shooting position.
Watch where shots go: if most shots go right from a left-footed player, the plant foot is pointing right. Fix the plant foot first.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Penalty area marked. 5 shooting positions shown as labeled circles: P1 (right, 14m, wide), P2 (right, 10m, narrow), P3 (centre, 16m), P4 (left, 10m, narrow), P5 (left, 14m, wide). Server shown 5m outside each position (small square). Arrows from each position show the ideal shot target in the goal: P1 and P5 arrows aim for far bottom corner. P2 and P4 arrows aim for near post low. P3 arrow shows two options (either corner low). GK shown in goal.

title: Playing Out from the Back
objective: Develop the ability to build from the goalkeeper through the defence and into midfield under pressing pressure.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the positional shape required to play out from the back
Players can identify and use the correct passing option under a press
GK develops footwork and distribution to build from the back
Players build confidence under pressure rather than kicking long immediately
organisation: Defending team: GK, 4 defenders in a back 4 shape, 2 central midfielders. Pressing team: 2 forwards (high press) and 2 midfielders (pressing support). Neutral players: 2 wide targets (cannot be pressed - they receive the ball if the build-up reaches them). Coach observes from the side.
instructions: GK starts with the ball. Build-up team must play out from the back to reach one of the 2 wide neutral players (positioned at the halfway line, wide left and wide right).
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): 2 passive pressing forwards only. Build-up team practices the positional shape: GK plays to CB, CB switches to other CB or plays to FB who plays to CM.
Coach walks through the structure: which player should be in each position when the GK has the ball?
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): Add 2 pressing midfielders. Press becomes active (4v6 but with pressing urgency). Build-up team must find a way through.
If build-up team reaches a wide neutral target: 2 points. If they play a long ball (before being genuinely pressured): 0 points.
If pressing team wins the ball: 3 points.
PHASE 3 (9 minutes): Full 6v4 press with all pressing rules removed. Build-up plays against real pressure and must stay composed.
coachingPoints: GK STARTING POSITION: when the team plays out from the back, the GK should stand high (at the top of the 6-yard box) and create a short passing option for every CB.
SPLITTING THE CBs: both CBs should be positioned at the edges of the 18-yard box (wide). This stretches the pressing forwards and creates more space.
MIDFIELDERS SHOWING: a CM must show between the defensive and midfield pressing lines to offer a short option. They should be visible at all times.
FULL-BACKS HIGH: the FBs push to the edge of the penalty area when the GK has the ball. They are the primary wide escape route.
PLAYING THROUGH, NOT OVER: the principle is to find a free player with a ground pass. Only play long if genuinely no option exists.
THE FREE PLAYER: under any press, one player is always free. The job is to find them with the next pass. Every player reads the press to identify who is free.
PASS WEIGHT: passes in build-up must be firm enough to travel quickly but accurate enough to land comfortably. Slow build-up passes give pressers time to intercept.
TRIGGERING THE PRESS: identify what triggers the press (GK in hands). Once triggered, move quickly before the press is set.
COMPOSURE: the word NO (do not pass) is as important as the word YES (make the pass). If no option is safe, recycle.
1-TOUCH BUILD-UP: under a high press, 1-touch or 2-touch passing is essential. Every extra touch gives the presser more time.
COMMUNICATION: the GK should be the loudest voice. They see the whole picture and should direct the build-up: left CB, switch, CM, etc.
commonMistakes: CBs staying narrow when the GK has the ball (not splitting to create width)
CMs hiding behind the pressing forwards instead of finding pockets between the lines
Players panicking and playing long before the genuine press arrives
GK staying on the goal line instead of stepping up to create short options
Passing to a player who is being pressed immediately rather than finding the free player
Build-up too slow: 3+ touches per player giving the press time to set and win the ball
corrections: CBs narrow: place cones at the edge of the 18-yard box and make it a rule: CBs must stand outside these cones when GK has the ball
CMs hiding: the CM must call the GKs name and physically show their body between the press lines before the GK passes
Early long ball: only allow long kicks if the coach holds up a red card (show it when the press genuinely prevents build-up). All other long kicks are discouraged
GK on line: place a cone at the top of the 6-yard box. GK must stand in front of it when a CB has the ball
Panic passing: freeze the game when a player panics and ask: who was free at that moment?
progressions: Add a 3rd pressing player: 4v6 becomes 5v6, increasing the difficulty of finding the free player
GK footkick only (no hands for distribution): all build-up starts with GK using feet from a goal kick
Win the ball and break: pressing team scores by building their own 3-touch sequence after winning the ball
Full 11v11 game with the rule: goals only count if the attack started with a GK or CB distribution (no clearances)
regressions: Walk-through: coach leads the team through the correct shape at walking pace before any pressure is added
Passive press only (phase 1 extended for the full session)
Allow 3-4 touches per player before pressure is introduced
matchApplication: Playing out from the back has become a fundamental team tactical skill in modern football at all levels from U12 to senior. Teams that can play out under a press gain territory and maintain possession. Teams that kick long under pressure give the ball back to the opposition. The positional structure in this drill (GK high, CBs wide, CMs between lines, FBs up) is the exact shape used by elite clubs including Man City, Liverpool, Arsenal, and Barcelona. Teaching it at U14 gives players 4+ years to master the concept before they reach senior football.
coachTips: Walk through the ideal positions with no ball before adding any pressure. The structure must be understood before the pressure is applied.
Give the GK a specific script to say during the drill: use their name to show who to play to. This develops GK communication.
The most important learning point: ALWAYS find the free player. Stop the game and ask regularly: who was free there?
Praise composure explicitly: say that was a great decision to recycle rather than criticising the player for not finding the forward pass.
At U14, the concept of building from the back is relatively new. Expect many mistakes in sessions 1-3. Improvement comes in session 4+.
Film the drill from behind the goal. The shape of the defenders spreading wide is only clearly visible from that angle.
diagramDescription: Half pitch. Full-size goal (bottom). GK at top of 6-yard box. 2 CBs at edges of 18-yard box (wide). 2 FBs at edge of penalty area (wider). 2 CMs shown in the midfield line between the pressing shape and the defensive line. 2 pressing forwards (triangles) shown 10m from the CBs. Arrows show build-up passing options: GK to left CB, CB to FB, FB to CM in pocket. 2 wide neutrals shown at halfway line with target icons. Pressing midfielders shown behind the forward press line.

title: Jockeying and Delay
objective: Teach the correct jockeying technique: delaying an attacker while staying on their feet and waiting for the right moment to tackle.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the defensive stance and jockeying posture
Players can delay an attacker for a minimum of 5 seconds without committing to a tackle
Players learn when the right moment to tackle is (when the ball moves away from the attacker)
Players develop patience in 1v1 defending - not diving in immediately
organisation: 20x5m narrow channel per pair (width restriction forces the 1v1 situation). Attacker starts at one end with the ball. Defender starts 3m away. A mini-goal (2m wide) at the defenders end. Pairs work simultaneously. Swap roles every 3 reps.
instructions: Defender sets their stance: side-on body (one foot ahead of the other), weight on the balls of their feet, knees slightly bent.
The near foot (leading foot) points toward the attacker at a 45-degree angle.
The body is side-on: showing the attacker one side (usually showing them toward the weaker foot or toward the touchline).
Attacker drives at the defender and tries to reach the mini-goal.
Defender job: maintain the distance (1.5m from the ball), move backward and sideward to stay goal-side, NOT diving in.
The tackle comes ONLY when the attacker takes a heavy touch and the ball moves away from their feet by more than 1m.
Until that moment: pure delay. No lunge, no dive, no reaching in.
After 5 minutes: add a rule that if the defender delays for 5 seconds without being beaten, they win the rep.
After 5 more minutes: add a recovering teammate who arrives from behind after 5 seconds (defender delays until support arrives).
coachingPoints: DEFENSIVE STANCE: side-on body. If you face the attacker square on, they can go either side equally easily. Side-on forces them to one side.
SHOW ONE WAY: position your body to guide the attacker toward the touchline (less dangerous) or toward their weaker foot. DO NOT show them inside toward goal.
DISTANCE: stay 1.5m from the ball. Too close = easy to be turned. Too far = attacker has no pressure and can shoot.
WEIGHT ON BALLS OF FEET: never flat-footed. A flat-footed defender cannot react to a direction change. Stay bouncy and reactive.
THE BACKWARD MOVEMENT: move backward at the same speed the attacker is moving forward. Do not allow the attacker to run past you because you are moving too slowly.
EYES ON THE BALL, NOT THE FEET: the ball tells you where to go. Attackers can fake with their body but the ball must go somewhere real.
PATIENCE: the best defending is waiting. Every second you delay gives a teammate time to recover. You do not need to win the ball immediately.
THE TACKLE MOMENT: the only time to tackle is when the ball is 1m+ from the attacker and they are off balance. This happens naturally if you wait.
DO NOT DIVE IN: a defender on the floor is no defender at all. Stay on your feet at all costs.
RECOVERY: if beaten, spin quickly and sprint to recover. Do not stand and watch.
commonMistakes: Immediately lunging at the ball when the attacker comes close (diving in)
Standing square-on instead of side-on, allowing the attacker to go either side
Incorrect distance: either too far away (no pressure) or too close (easy to get around)
Flat-footed stance: cannot react to direction changes
Watching the attackers body and feet instead of the ball
Giving up and stopping when beaten - not sprinting to recover
corrections: Diving in: put a £5 note (fake) under the defenders foot. They must not let it out from under their foot. This keeps them grounded.
Square-on: place a cone behind the defenders near heel showing the correct 45-degree angle. Foot must always point at that cone.
Distance: place 2 cones 1.5m apart. Defender and ball must be on opposite sides of the gap at all times.
Flat feet: ask the player to get into a sprint starting position and observe their weight distribution (forward onto balls of feet) then transfer this to the defensive stance.
Watching body: put a sticker on the ball and tell the defender to always be able to see the sticker.
progressions: 1v1 with no channel (open space): defender must manage the space without the channel restriction
Attacker with specific moves: attacker must try to use step-overs and scissors to beat the defender
1v1 then 2v2: after the first defender delays for 3 seconds, a second attacker joins (tests if the delay has given a second defender time to arrive)
Channel becomes a full pitch: wider space means defender must cover more ground and manage the wider 1v1 scenario
regressions: Slow dribble only: attacker walks at 50 percent pace, giving more time for the defender to set their stance
No tackle phase: defender only delays (no tackle allowed) for the full drill to establish patience
Wider channel (8m): more space gives the defender more time to adjust position
matchApplication: Jockeying is the primary individual defending skill that all youth players must master. The reason most 1v1 defending in youth football fails is that defenders dive in too early, get beaten, and create numerical disadvantages. A defender who can jockey and delay keeps the game even (1v1 remains 1v1) while a teammate recovers. The patience to NOT tackle until the right moment is the most sophisticated defensive skill to teach, and it must be established at U12 before players are facing genuinely skilled attackers at U14+.
coachTips: Frame this as the hardest skill in defending: it takes more courage to NOT tackle than to dive in. Restraint requires confidence.
Walk through the defensive stance physically with each player in the first session: stand beside them and adjust foot angle, weight, and body position manually.
Ask attackers to deliberately take a heavy touch at some point in every rep. This gives defenders the practice of recognising and acting on the tackle moment.
Celebrate delays as loud as goals. Clap every time a defender successfully delays for 5 seconds without being beaten.
The single most important coaching point: EYES ON THE BALL. Demonstrate how attackers fake with body but ball must go somewhere real.
diagramDescription: 20x5m narrow channel. Attacker (circle with ball) at one end. Defender (triangle) 3m from attacker, side-on body position shown with a small figure diagram. Mini-goal (2 cones, 2m wide) at the defenders end. Arrow shows attacker driving forward. Defender shown in correct side-on stance with the near foot angle labeled at 45 degrees. Distance bracket shows 1.5m gap between defender and ball.

title: Set Position and Ready Stance
objective: Establish the correct GK set position, ready stance, and basic movement across the goal.
learningOutcomes: GK can adopt the correct set position in front of the goal
GK understands angle play and how to reduce the shooting angle
GK can side-step across the goal without crossing their feet
GK builds the habit of being set before every shot
organisation: GK in goal. Server at the penalty spot. 3 cones placed at specific angles: one on the goal line centre, one at the 6-yard box centre, one at the penalty spot. These are the depth positions the GK uses based on where the ball is.
instructions: READY STANCE: GK stands feet shoulder-width apart, weight on the balls of feet (slight forward lean). Knees slightly bent. Hands at waist height, palms facing outward slightly. Eyes on the ball.
POSITIONING ON THE LINE: GK stands on the goal line cone. Coach points to where the ball is. GK adjusts position to be on the line between the ball and the centre of the goal.

STEP OFF THE LINE: server moves to the penalty spot. GK must step forward (off the goal line to the 6-yard box cone) to reduce the angle. Ask: why do we come off the line? Less of the goal is visible to the shooter from the GKs angle.
ANGLE DEMONSTRATION: use string from the cone (ball position) to the two posts. The GK stands where the two strings diverge - this is the correct angle position.
SIDE-STEP MOVEMENT: server moves the ball left and right slowly. GK side-steps across the goal, staying on the correct angle line, never crossing feet.
PHASE 2 (after 8 minutes): Server makes actual shots (gentle, along the ground). GK must be in set position BEFORE the shot is taken.
If GK is still moving when the shot is taken: the shot does not count (teaches the habit of being set before every shot).
coachingPoints: THE READY STANCE is not static: the GK should be constantly making small bouncing movements (like a spring) to stay reactive. A flat-footed GK cannot dive.
ANGLE PLAY: always stand on the imaginary line between the ball and the centre of the goal. As the ball moves, the GK moves.

HOW FAR TO COME OUT: when the ball is inside the penalty area, step to the 6-yard box line. When the ball is at the edge of the area, step to the penalty spot line. When the ball is outside the area, stay on or near the goal line.
SIDE-STEP: move laterally without crossing the feet. If the right foot goes right, the left foot follows. Never cross the feet: it takes longer to set and creates a stumble risk.
THE SET MOMENT: before every shot, the GK MUST have both feet on the ground with weight centred. This is the position from which the fastest dive can happen.
HANDS POSITION: hands at waist height, palms out. Fingers spread slightly. This is the fastest position to catch from.
EYE CONTACT: maintain eye contact with the ball at all times during movement.
COMMUNICATION: the GK should call KEEPERS BALL for any high ball or cross they are coming for.
commonMistakes: Flat-footed ready stance: cannot dive quickly from a flat position
Standing on the goal line when the ball is inside the penalty area (angle not reduced)
Crossing the feet when side-stepping which causes stumbling and incorrect positioning
Being in motion when the shot is taken (not set before the shot)
Hands too low or too high (not in the optimal ready position)
Looking at the shooter rather than the ball
corrections: Flat feet: bounce a ball on the floor beside the GK and ask them to spring up and touch it with both hands. This resets the athletic posture
Angle play: use the string demonstration (string from ball to each post) so GK can physically see where they should stand
Crossing feet: place 2 cones the width of the goalpost apart. GK must complete the side-step without the feet going outside the cones
Not set before shot: introduce a freeze signal: GK must be completely still when they hear the whistle (before the shot)
Hands too low: tape a cone at waist height as a reminder of where hands should sit
progressions: Shots from different angles: server moves to 5 different positions and shoots from each. GK must adjust angle before each shot.
Cross movement: server serves balls that the GK must come off the line to claim (combines angle play with coming for high balls)
Distribution after save: after each save, GK must throw accurately to a target player within 3 seconds
regressions: Use a junior goal (5m wide) which is more appropriate for U10 physical size
Server only rolls gentle shots along the ground in phase 2
Use only the set position (no angle work) in the first session
matchApplication: The set position and angle play are the foundation of all goalkeeping. A GK who is always in the correct position saves more shots not through reactions but through positioning: less of the goal is exposed so the attacker must beat both the GK and the smaller visible target. Professional goalkeepers talk about saving shots before they are taken by their positional intelligence. Teaching angle play at U10 builds this intelligence across a 6-8 year period before senior football.
coachTips: The string demonstration of angle play is the most effective teaching tool available. Use it every time with a young GK.
Goalkeepers are often forgotten in general training sessions. Give them specific GK coaching time every session.
Remind the GK constantly that they are the most important player on the pitch: every other player sees their GK as the last line. Build the confidence.
Film the GK from behind the goal and show them their own angle positioning. The angle from behind shows clearly whether they are on the correct line.
GK coaching should always be positive first. Identify what is correct before pointing out errors.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal (or junior goal). 3 cones shown inside the penalty area: one on goal line centre, one at 6-yard box centre, one at penalty spot. GK shown in correct set position (hands at waist, feet shoulder-width, slight forward lean). String lines shown from the penalty spot position to both goal posts with the GK standing at the intersection point. Side arrows show side-step movement across goal without feet crossing.

title: 1v1 Shot-Stopping
objective: Develop confident, technically correct shot-stopping across all zones of the goal from realistic shooting positions.
learningOutcomes: GK can save driven shots to low left, low right, mid left, mid right, and high corners
GK develops the collapse dive technique for shots to the sides
GK can parry wide and recover to a second save position
GK builds confidence and rhythm in a high-repetition shot-stopping session
players: 1 GK plus a server (or 2 GKs alternating in goal)
organisation: 6 shooting positions set up: P1 (centre, 14m), P2 (left centre, 14m), P3 (right centre, 14m), P4 (left 45 degrees, 12m), P5 (right 45 degrees, 12m), P6 (close range, 10m centre). Server fires from each position in rotation. All shots are along the ground first (first 10 minutes) then mixed heights.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Low shots only (8 minutes): Server fires from each position in rotation. All shots along the ground. GK must use correct technique.
Low central shots: body position central, drop to knees slightly and collect with both hands behind the ball.
Low side shots: step toward the ball, then collapse down on the same side as the ball. Lead with the lower hand, top hand supports. Land on the side of the body, not the stomach.
PHASE 2 - Mixed heights (8 minutes): Server fires low, mid, and high shots from each position. GK must adjust technique for each height.
Mid-height shots (waist-chest): step toward the ball, W-grip catch (thumbs pointing toward each other, palms facing ball). Pull ball into chest.
High shots (near crossbar): push off both feet, extend arms fully, tip or catch at highest point possible.
PHASE 3 - Quick fire (4 minutes): Server fires 2 shots in quick succession (5 seconds between shots). GK saves the first then must recover position for the second.
coachingPoints: SET POSITION BEFORE EVERY SHOT: GK must be completely still with weight centred before the shot is struck. A moving GK cannot dive effectively.
COLLAPSE DIVE: for shots to the side, step with the near foot (the foot closer to the ball) and FALL to the side. Do not dive over the ball. The body goes TO where the ball is.
LOWER HAND FIRST: the bottom hand catches the ball. The top hand supports from behind. Never catch with only the top hand for low shots.
LAND ON THE SIDE: GK should land on the outside of the lower hip, never on the stomach. Stomach landing makes getting up slower.
W-GRIP: for mid and high shots, thumbs should point toward each other (not downward). This creates a basket for the ball and is the strongest catching position.
PARRY WIDE: if the shot cannot be caught cleanly, parry it WIDE of the post. Never parry it back toward the centre. A central parry creates a rebound chance.
AFTER THE SAVE: immediately look up to see if a rebound player is waiting. Do not stay on the ground.
FOOTWORK: small shuffle steps before shots give the GK the athletic position needed to dive. A static flat-footed GK reacts half a second slower.
HANDS EARLY: move the hands toward the ball as early as possible. Late hands mean the ball is caught at the last moment (highest risk).
MENTALLY RESET: between every shot, the GK says next one to themselves and resets their focus.
commonMistakes: Diving OVER the ball (the body and ball end up on different sides)
Catching with the top hand only for low shots (the ball goes through the hands)
Landing on the stomach instead of the side of the body
Incorrect W-grip (thumbs pointing down or outward) for mid and high balls
Parrying the ball back toward the centre rather than wide
Not recovering quickly enough after the save for a second shot
corrections: Diving over the ball: put a cone next to the ball. GK must touch the cone with their lower hand when saving low shots
Top hand only: the coach presses one hand down and removes it. GK uses only their lower hand for 5 reps to build the habit
Landing on stomach: place a crash mat beside the goal. Practice the fall technique on the mat with no ball first
W-grip: trace the W shape on the GKs gloves with a marker so they can see the correct hand orientation
Central parry: place a cone 1m wide of each post. Every parry must go past that cone (not toward the centre)
progressions: Shots with a screen: attacker stands between server and GK blocking the GKs sight line until the last moment (reaction save)
Double save: two servers fire simultaneously from different positions (tests angle management and decision on which shot to save)
Combination save and 1v1: save the shot, get up, immediately face an attacker who drives at goal from the penalty spot
Shots under fatigue: 5 squat jumps, immediately face 3 quick shots. Tests maintaining technique under physical stress
regressions: Use softer balls (foam or size 3) for the first session with a GK learning dive technique
Slow down the shooting pace: server lobs ball at reduced pace to allow more time for technique
Shots to one side only until the collapse dive is correct before adding the other side
matchApplication: Shot-stopping is the primary technical skill in goalkeeping. A GK who can consistently save shots at all heights and angles from realistic positions gives their team a significant advantage. The collapse dive technique enables saves to the low corners which are the statistically most common goal-scoring shot target at all levels. The recovery habit after a save prevents rebound goals which account for approximately 20 percent of all goals in youth football. Developing these techniques systematically from U10-U14 creates the foundation for senior-level keeping.
coachTips: GK-specific coaching requires specialist knowledge. If you are not confident in GK technique, consider inviting a GK coach for specific sessions.
Keep repetitions high: a GK should face minimum 60-80 shots in a full session. Low repetition sessions do not build the habit.
Alternate rest: if you have 2 GKs, they alternate in goal after every 5 shots. This keeps both fresh and doubles the repetition.
The positive relationship between coach and GK is especially important: GKs face constant scrutiny and need confident, supportive coaching.
Film the dive technique in slow motion. The collapse dive is hard to teach with words alone - seeing it clearly is transformative.
Every session should end with a save competition: who can save more from 10 shots. Competitive elements build sharpness.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Penalty area shown. 6 shooting positions labeled P1-P6: P1 centre 14m, P2 left centre 14m, P3 right centre 14m, P4 left 45-degree angle 12m, P5 right 45-degree angle 12m, P6 close range centre 10m. Server shown at each position with a small figure. Goal corners labeled with shot targets. Small diagrams show: collapse dive technique (step, fall to side, lower hand first), W-grip hand position, and parry direction (wide of post).

title: Speed and Agility with the Ball
objective: Develop sprinting speed and agility while maintaining ball control, replicating match-speed movement with a ball.
learningOutcomes: Players can dribble at maximum speed through directional changes
Players develop explosive first-step acceleration with a ball
Players build agility through multi-directional ball dribbling
Players learn to maintain control at sprint speed
organisation: Set up 4 agility circuits simultaneously, 3-4 players per circuit. Each circuit: straight sprint gate (15m, cone gates at 5m and 10m), diagonal cone pattern (5 cones in a W shape), tight cone slalom (6 cones 1m apart), and return sprint with the ball. Players complete the circuit continuously. Coach times each lap.
instructions: STATION 1 - Sprint gates (15m): Dribble at maximum pace through 2 cone gates (each gate 1m wide). The gate constraints force the player to keep the ball forward and controlled at speed.

STATION 2 - W pattern: 5 cones arranged in a W shape, each cone 3m apart. Player dribbles to the first cone, cuts hard left, dribbles to the next, cuts hard right. Focus: sharp directional changes at speed with the outside of the foot.
STATION 3 - Tight slalom: 6 cones 1m apart in a straight line. Player dribbles using alternate feet at each cone. Challenge: complete 2 passes through the slalom per minute.
STATION 4 - Sprint return: from the last slalom cone, sprint with the ball back to the start as fast as possible. Ball control secondary - pace primary.
Players complete the full circuit continuously for 8 minutes then rest 2 minutes.
TIMED ROUND: after rest, 1 timed circuit each. Record time. Challenge: beat their own time in a subsequent session.
coachingPoints: ACCELERATION: the first 3 steps are the most important in any sprint. Drive the knees forward and pump the arms. Ball must stay within 2 steps of the feet during acceleration.
BALL TOUCH AT SPEED: when dribbling at full speed, use the outside of the boot for most touches. Inside foot touches slow you down.
DIRECTION CHANGE: to change direction sharply, plant the outside foot firmly, drop the shoulder and hips in the new direction, and drive off the planted foot.
BODY ANGLE: when accelerating, lean forward. When decelerating to cut, lower the centre of gravity by bending the knees more.
TIGHT SPACES (slalom): use the inside and outside of the foot alternately. Small rapid touches are faster than big heavy touches in a slalom.
HEAD UP: even at maximum speed, take a 0.5 second look up every 2-3 touches. In a match, the space ahead matters.
RECOVERY BETWEEN REPS: walk during the recovery. Slow jogging is neither a rest nor a sprint - it is the worst of both. Walk slowly, then sprint. No half-efforts.
BREATHING: exhale sharply on every direction change. This engages the core and stabilises balance in the cut.
commonMistakes: Using the inside of the foot for all dribbling touches at speed (inside is slower than outside at pace)
Taking too many touches in the straight sprint section (2-3 touches per 5m is optimal)
Upright posture during acceleration (should lean forward at 45 degrees in the first 3 steps)
Jogging between stations instead of using the recovery walk properly
Plant foot not driving off firmly in direction changes (passive push rather than explosive drive)
corrections: Inside foot at speed: mark alternate cones as outside-of-foot only. Players must touch every marked cone with the outside of the boot
Too many touches: place 2 cones in the 15m sprint gate. Player may only take 3 touches from start to first cone (forces longer touches at speed)
Upright acceleration: crouch start (hands on knees) before each sprint forces the forward lean position
Passive direction change: place a resistance band around the waist (held by a partner) for the W pattern. Band creates resistance that demands explosive plant and push
Jogging recovery: use a clear visual signal (whistle = sprint, hand up = stop and walk). No jogging allowed
progressions: Defender added: a defender chases from 3m behind in the straight sprint gate. This adds genuine urgency and game-realism
Ball skill at turn point: at the end of the W pattern, a skill move must be performed before the return sprint
Race format: two players start simultaneously. Creates competitive urgency and natural pace increase
Fatigue load: add 5 press-ups or 5 squat jumps before each circuit entry
regressions: Reduce to just the straight sprint gate and return sprint (2 stations instead of 4) for younger or less experienced players
Remove the ball in phase 1 (pure agility) before adding the ball in phase 2
Wider slalom cones (1.5m apart instead of 1m) to reduce difficulty
matchApplication: Speed with the ball is one of the most match-decisive physical attributes in football. A player who can maintain close control while accelerating creates goal-scoring chances that slower players cannot. At U11, the physical attributes being developed (explosive first step, sharp direction changes) are at a critical period of motor development. The cognitive and physical patterns built in this speed circuit will transfer directly to match performance - particularly in 1v1 situations where the ability to accelerate away from a beaten defender determines whether a chance is created.
coachTips: Speed training should come early in the session when players are fresh. Do not put this drill at the end of a long session.
Maximum efforts require maximum rest: minimum 1:3 work-rest ratio for genuine speed work. Do not rush the recovery.
U11 players are in a sensitive period for speed development. The correct movement patterns learned now will be maintained for life.
Record times for every player and share improvement data. Seeing concrete speed improvement is a massive motivator.
Variety is key: change the circuit layout every 3-4 sessions to prevent boredom and challenge different movement patterns.
diagramDescription: 25x20m area showing 4 circuit stations arranged end to end. Station 1: 15m sprint with 2 cone gates at 5m and 10m (straight arrow through gates). Station 2: W-pattern of 5 cones (zigzag arrows showing direction changes). Station 3: 6-cone slalom with alternating left-right arrows showing tight weaving. Station 4: return arrow (full speed dribble back to start). Circuit direction shown with large curved arrow. Timing positions marked.

title: Corner Kick - Near Post and Far Post Delivery
objective: Develop a reliable corner kick attacking routine with 2 delivery options and 3 attacking movement patterns.
learningOutcomes: Corner taker can deliver consistently to the near post and far post
Attacking players know their designated runs for each delivery type
The team understands the structure of an attacking corner in terms of positions
Players can execute the routine at match speed with live defending
organisation: Corner kick position (right and left). Full-size goal with GK. 4-5 attackers in position. 4-5 defenders in defensive positions. Corner taker at the flag. Attackers and defenders are designated (not rotating) in phase 1.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Walk-through (6 minutes): Corner taker delivers from the right. No pressure. Attackers walk through their runs.

NEAR POST DELIVERY: corner taker signals 1 finger. Ball delivered with pace to the NEAR post area (6m from goal, near side). Runner A attacks the near post. Runner B pulls to far post. Runner C delays at the edge of the area.

FAR POST DELIVERY: corner taker signals 2 fingers. Ball delivered with a curling arc to the FAR post area. Runner B attacks the far post. Runner A makes a near-post decoy run. Runner C waits at penalty spot for a flick-on or clearance.
EDGE RUNNERS: Runner C and D hold the edge of the area for second-ball opportunities. D is specifically positioned to shoot from any clearance.
PHASE 2 - With passive defenders (7 minutes): defenders hold positions but do not jump or tackle. Attackers execute both routines against the passive shape.
PHASE 3 - Live (7 minutes): full live corner with defenders free to mark and GK to claim. Corner taker chooses near or far delivery based on defensive shape.
coachingPoints: CORNER TAKER TECHNIQUE - NEAR POST: aim to deliver the ball to the NEAR POST at a pace and height the runner must attack (slightly above head height). The ball curves INTO the goal not away from it.
CORNER TAKER TECHNIQUE - FAR POST: aim higher (more loft) and target the far post area. The ball should arc and land 2m from the far post. Use the side of the foot for better accuracy than laces.

RUNNER TIMING - NEAR POST: arrive at the near post as the ball does. Start your run LATE (from outside the area). Early runs allow defenders to track you. Late runs mean defenders are already committed.

RUNNER TIMING - FAR POST: the far post runner must start from the opposite side of the goal area. Run toward the ball. Arrive at the far post when the ball has passed the near post (so you are behind the defenders who followed the near post movement).
EDGE PLAYERS: edge players must be ready for a first-time shot. Stand in a shooting position (weight on balls of feet, ready to react). Do not stand upright waiting.
GK COMMUNICATION: attackers must listen for KEEPERS from the GK. If the GK shouts, attackers must not challenge (avoid collision and the GK has committed).
SCREENING: attackers who are NOT the primary target should still make a run. This pulls defenders and creates space for the target runner.
SECOND BALL: after every delivery, one attacker is designated to attack a second ball. They should position 10m from goal rather than in the immediate penalty area.
commonMistakes: Corner taker delivering too short or too long consistently - no reliable target zone
Attackers starting their runs too early (before the ball is in the air) allowing defenders to track
Primary runners all going to the same zone regardless of the signal
Edge players standing too far from the goal or not in a shooting position
Attacking players not reacting to KEEPER call and colliding with the GK
corrections: Corner delivery: place 2 cones at the near and far post areas at head height (on poles or held by assistants). Corner taker must hit their target zone in 3 of 5 attempts before progressing
Early runs: mark a starting cone 5m inside the area. Attackers cannot leave the cone until the ball is in the air
Wrong zone: assign numbers to runs (Run 1 = near post, Run 2 = far post). Corner taker holds up fingers and runners must respond to the number
Edge players: mark X positions on the floor where edge players stand. They must be on their X before the corner is taken
GK call: run a specific exercise: GK shouts KEEPER before the delivery. All attackers stop immediately. Practice until automatic
progressions: Add variation: a short corner option (corner taker plays to a wide support player instead of delivering in). Forces defenders to press out and creates a different angle
Post-corner counter: after every defending clearance, a neutral plays a quick counter and the defending team attacks 3v2
Live 11v11 corner practice: the corner routine in the context of a full game
regressions: Only near post delivery until it is mastered, before introducing the far post option
Walk-through pace with passive defenders for 2 full sessions before adding any live defending
matchApplication: Set pieces account for approximately 30-35 percent of all goals in professional football and a higher percentage in youth football (because defensive organisation is weaker). A team with a reliable corner routine creates clear scoring opportunities every time they win a corner. The near post and far post system in this drill is the foundation of almost every attacking corner routine used from U13 through to professional football. The timing of runs (late departure) is the most important learning point as it directly determines whether the attacker loses their marker.
coachTips: Practice the corner delivery separately (corner taker alone for 5 minutes) before integrating the team.
The finger signal system (1 = near post, 2 = far post) is simple and reliable. Keep it simple - youth players forget complex signals under match pressure.
Designate roles clearly before every rep: Runner A, B, C have specific jobs. Random movement in corners creates chaos.
Celebrate successful executions loudly. A well-delivered corner with a clean header goal is one of the most exciting moments in youth football.
Both right and left corner delivery must be practiced equally.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Corner flag shown bottom right. Corner taker at flag. 3 attacker runs shown with numbered dotted arrows: Run 1 (near post, forward from outside area to near post), Run 2 (far post, from left side of goal area to far post), Run 3 (edge player at penalty spot). 4 defenders shown in defensive positions near goal. GK shown in goal. Two delivery arcs shown: near post delivery (flatter, fast curve to near post) and far post delivery (higher arc, landing 2m from far post).

title: Maradona Turn
objective: Master the Maradona 360-degree turn to escape pressure and change direction in tight spaces.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the Maradona turn at match speed
Players understand when to use the turn based on the defenders position
Players develop the coordination to complete a 360 spin while retaining possession
Players build confidence using advanced skill moves under pressure
organisation: Each player in a 5x5m square with a ball. Coach demonstrates the full turn slowly before players attempt. Phase 1 is static practice. Phase 2 adds movement. Phase 3 adds a passive defender.
instructions: Start with the ball rolling slowly toward you from a light tap.
Step your right foot OVER the ball and place it on the far side of the ball (trapping it between your right foot and left foot).
Using the sole of your right foot, drag the ball backward behind you.
As the ball rolls back, pivot on your left foot and spin your body 180 degrees to the right.
Your right foot continues to drag the ball as your body completes the spin.
After 180 degrees, your left foot steps over the ball and drags it the remaining 180 degrees.
You have now completed a full 360 degree turn and are facing the original direction with the ball at your feet.
Practice slowly for 5 minutes counting each drag: drag right, pivot, drag left, go.
After 5 minutes increase to 75 percent pace moving around the square.
Final 5 minutes: passive defender stands at one side of the square and the player uses the Maradona turn to spin away from them.
coachingPoints: FIRST DRAG: the right foot steps OVER the top of the ball and the sole pulls it backward. The foot does not kick it — it drags smoothly.
PIVOT FOOT: the left foot is the pivot point for the first 180 degrees. Stay on the ball of the left foot — never the heel.
BODY ROTATION: the shoulders lead the spin. Look over your shoulder in the direction you are spinning before your feet move.
SECOND DRAG: the left foot takes over the drag for the second 180 degrees. This is where most players lose the ball — the handover between feet must be seamless.
BALL CONTROL: the ball should travel in a tight circle no bigger than 1m diameter. If it travels wider the turn is too loose.
SPEED: the turn must be performed quickly — under 1.5 seconds at match pace. A slow Maradona turn gives the defender time to recover.
USE THE BODY AS A SHIELD: during the turn your body is always between the ball and the defender. Use this — lean into the defender slightly.
EXIT SPEED: after the turn is complete take 2-3 rapid acceleration steps immediately. The turn only creates advantage if you move after it.
DISGUISE: approach the ball at normal dribbling pace. Do not slow down before the turn — that signals something is coming.
BOTH DIRECTIONS: practice the turn spinning both clockwise and anticlockwise. A one-direction turn is predictable.
commonMistakes: Losing the ball on the handover between right and left foot during the spin
The ball travelling too far from the body making it loose and easy to intercept
Slowing down before initiating the turn which signals the move to the defender
Not accelerating away after completing the turn
Pivoting on the heel instead of the ball of the foot causing a loss of balance
Only practicing in one direction making the player one-dimensional
corrections: Ball handover: slow the turn to walking pace and focus specifically on the moment the second foot takes over from the first
Ball too loose: place a small cone circle 1m diameter and the ball must stay inside it during the full turn
Slowing before turn: coach claps to set a dribbling rhythm and the player must maintain that rhythm into the turn
No acceleration after: place a cone 3m behind the player in the exit direction — they must reach it within 2 steps of completing the turn
Heel pivot: mark an X on the ball of the foot with tape as a reminder of the pivot contact point
One direction only: alternate cones left and right in the square forcing turns in both directions
progressions: Semi-active defender: defender can shadow but not tackle, creating real pressure during the turn
Live 1v1: full defending. Player earns a point for each successful Maradona turn that retains possession
Maradona then pass: after the turn the player must find a target with an accurate pass within 2 touches
Speed competition: who can complete 5 Maradona turns fastest while keeping the ball in their square
regressions: Static ball: practice the turn from a completely still ball before introducing a moving one
Two-stage: practice the first 180 degrees only until comfortable before adding the second 180
Walk-through pace: complete the full turn at walking speed for 3 minutes before adding any pace
matchApplication: The Maradona turn is one of the most effective pressure-escaping moves in football. When a player is closed down from behind or from the side and has no forward passing option, the 360 turn allows them to spin out of pressure and face a completely different direction. It is particularly effective for midfielders receiving on the back foot and strikers shielding the ball with a defender pressing from behind. Diego Maradona, Zinedine Zidane, and more recently Luka Modric all use this exact technique as a primary pressure-escape tool.
coachTips: Show a slow-motion video of the Maradona turn before the session. Visual learning accelerates understanding of the coordination required.
Break the turn into two halves in the first session: right drag and pivot (first 180) then left drag and exit (second 180). Only combine when both halves are clean.
The most common failure point is the handover between feet. Spend extra time walking through this specific moment.
U13 players respond very well to being told this is a professional-level move. The aspiration drives the effort.
Do not add the defender until the turn is clean at 75 percent pace. Adding pressure too early creates panic and reinforces bad habits.
diagramDescription: 5x5m square with corner cones. Player in the centre with ball. Circular arrow shows the 360 degree spin path (clockwise shown as primary). Numbered positions: 1 (start, right foot steps over ball), 2 (first drag, 90 degrees), 3 (handover, 180 degrees), 4 (second drag, 270 degrees), 5 (exit, 360 degrees complete). Passive defender shown at one corner of the square with arrow showing their approach line.

title: Advanced Ball Mastery Combination
objective: Execute a continuous 8-move ball mastery combination at match speed demonstrating full technical fluency.
learningOutcomes: Players can link 8 ball mastery moves in a continuous flowing sequence
Players develop automaticity — executing moves without conscious thought
Players build the close control foundation needed for match-intensity dribbling
Players develop the physical coordination and foot speed of an elite technical player
organisation: Individual squares 5x5m. The 8-move sequence is: toe taps, inside chops, outside rolls, V-pull left, V-pull right, Cruyff turn left, Cruyff turn right, drag and accelerate. Coach teaches each move separately before combining.
instructions: MOVE 1 - Toe taps (8 reps): Alternate sole of foot on top of ball rapidly. Stay on balls of feet.
MOVE 2 - Inside chops (4 each side): Inside of right foot chops ball left, inside of left chops right. Rapid.
MOVE 3 - Outside rolls (4 each side): Outside of right pushes ball right, outside of left pushes left.
MOVE 4 - V-pull left: Sole drag backward with right foot, push out left with inside of left foot.
MOVE 5 - V-pull right: Sole drag backward with left foot, push out right with inside of right foot.
MOVE 6 - Cruyff turn left: Shape to go right, drag ball behind standing leg with inside of right foot, go left.
MOVE 7 - Cruyff turn right: Shape to go left, drag ball behind standing leg with inside of left foot, go right.
MOVE 8 - Drag and accelerate: Sole drag backward 1m, then 3 explosive dribbling touches forward at maximum pace.
Practice each move separately for 2 minutes before linking them.
Final 8 minutes: full combination continuously for 45 seconds, rest 15 seconds, repeat.
coachingPoints: FLOW: the combination must be one continuous movement with no pause between moves. Each move transitions directly into the next.
FOOT SPEED: the goal is maximum foot speed on moves 1-3 and maximum deception on moves 4-7. Different energy for different moves.
EYES UP: after the first 3 minutes of learning, eyes must be up for 80 percent of the combination. The feet must feel the ball.
WEIGHT ON BALLS OF FEET: throughout the entire combination. If the heels touch the floor the speed drops significantly.
CRUYFF EXECUTION: the shape before the Cruyff must be convincing. Lean the body one way before dragging the other. No deception means no value.
DRAG AND ACCELERATE: the final move must be explosive. The drag creates space, the acceleration exploits it. Half-effort on the acceleration wastes the whole sequence.
RHYTHM: find a rhythm in the early moves (toe taps, inside chops) then use that rhythm as the engine for the whole sequence.
BOTH FEET EQUALLY: moves 4-7 must be performed equally on both sides. Track which side is weaker and spend extra time on it.
BREATHING: exhale on each direction change. Holding breath causes muscle tension and reduces coordination.
PRESSURE MINDSET: imagine a defender 1m away throughout the sequence. Every touch is keeping the ball away from them.
commonMistakes: Pausing between moves breaking the flow of the combination
Eyes down throughout the entire combination
Weak execution of the Cruyff turns with no convincing body shape before the drag
Half-speed acceleration on the final move wasting the advantage created by the drag
Neglecting the weaker foot on moves 4-7
Flat-footed posture slowing foot speed throughout
corrections: Breaking flow: count aloud through the sequence — 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8 — the counting forces a rhythm that prevents pausing
Eyes down: place a coloured cone at eye height 4m away. Player must be able to name its colour at any point in the sequence
Weak Cruyff shape: ask the player to exaggerate the body lean before the drag. Make it theatrical — the exaggeration builds the habit
Weak acceleration: place a cone 3m ahead. The final acceleration must reach the cone in 2 touches maximum
Weak foot neglect: do the sequence in reverse (start with Cruyff right then left) forcing the weaker foot into the harder moves first
Flat feet: bounce test — player bounces 5 times before starting each 45-second set to reset the athletic posture
progressions: Timed competition: record time to complete one full sequence. Challenge personal best each session.
Passive defender: defender stands 1m away throughout the sequence. Player must complete the sequence without the ball touching the defender.
Random stop: coach calls STOP at any moment in the sequence. Player must freeze with the ball under control.
Add a 5m sprint after the drag and accelerate: the combination ends with a genuine match-speed sprint.
regressions: Reduce to a 4-move sequence (toe taps, inside chops, V-pull left, drag and accelerate) before building to 8
Allow a 1-second pause between each move while learning
Walk through the full 8-move sequence at 30 percent speed for 5 minutes before adding pace
matchApplication: Elite technical players at senior level have automatic ball mastery — their close control is not something they think about, it is something that happens while they are thinking about the next pass, the run ahead of them, or the position of the defender. This combination drill builds exactly that automaticity. The 8-move sequence covers every close-control scenario a player encounters in a match: pressure from behind (toe taps, shielding), pressure from the side (V-pull, chops), closing down (Cruyff turn), and space to exploit (drag and accelerate). Players who complete this drill daily for 4-6 weeks show measurable improvements in close-control under pressure.
coachTips: Film each player performing the combination at the start and end of the month. The improvement in fluency is one of the most motivating things a U15 player can see.
This drill works excellently as a pre-training warm-up once players know the sequence. 3 sets of 45 seconds activates the feet and mind before any session.
Identify the weakest move for each player individually and set them a personal target: improve that one move this week.
Do not run this drill when players are physically fatigued. Ball mastery at high speed requires fresh legs and a fresh mind.
Challenge the group: who can complete the sequence without losing the ball 10 times in a row? Creates focus without pressure.
diagramDescription: 5x5m square per player with corner cones. Ball in centre. Numbered arrows showing the 8-move sequence path within the square: 1 (toe taps, in place), 2 (inside chops, lateral), 3 (outside rolls, lateral), 4 (V-pull left, diagonal backward then left), 5 (V-pull right, diagonal backward then right), 6 (Cruyff turn left, forward shape then left exit), 7 (Cruyff turn right, forward shape then right exit), 8 (drag back then 3 forward explosive arrows exiting the square). Full circuit shown as a continuous flowing path.

title: Elite 1v1 Street Ball
objective: Apply elite-level ball mastery and deception in competitive 1v1 situations at match intensity.
learningOutcomes: Players can select and execute the correct skill move based on the defenders body position and weight distribution
Players develop the deception and timing that separates good dribblers from elite ones
Players build resilience and confidence through competitive 1v1 repetition
Players understand the risk-reward calculation in 1v1 situations in different areas of the pitch
pitchSize: 12x8m per 1v1 zone. Multiple zones running simultaneously.
organisation: Multiple 12x8m zones. Each zone has a cone goal at each end (2m wide). Pairs compete 1v1 within the zone — attacker tries to score at either goal, defender tries to win the ball and score. No goalkeepers. Roles are not fixed — whoever wins the ball becomes the attacker. Continuous play. Points scored over 3-minute rounds.
instructions: Both players start at the centre of the zone. Coach rolls ball in to start.
Whoever wins the first touch is the attacker. They try to score at either goal.
Defender immediately tries to win the ball using correct jockeying technique.
No restarts — if the ball goes out, the player who did NOT put it out restarts from the nearest cone.
Play continuously for 3 minutes. Count goals. Highest scorer wins the round.
After each 3-minute round: 90 seconds rest. Coach gives one specific coaching point to both players.
Rotate opponents after every 2 rounds so players face different defensive styles.
PHASE 2 (after 12 minutes): attacker must attempt a named skill move before shooting. Coach calls the move at the start of each possession.
PHASE 3 (final 8 minutes): free play — any move, any time, full intensity.
coachingPoints: READ THE DEFENDER: before attempting any move, read their body weight. If weight is on the right foot — go left. If feet are together and balanced — use a speed move (step-over or shoulder drop). If they are lunging — let them commit and go past.
TIMING: the move must happen at the moment the defender commits or shifts their weight. Too early and they recover. Too late and the space is gone.
DISTANCE FROM DEFENDER: initiate the move 1-1.5m from the defender. Too far and there is no deception. Too close and there is no time to execute.
DISGUISE: every move needs a convincing setup. The shoulder drop, the eye movement, the body lean — these are what create the advantage, not the technical move itself.
FIRST TOUCH AWAY: after the move, the first touch must be into space and away from the defender. A touch back toward the defender wastes the advantage.
TWO GOALS: use the two-goal format intelligently. If the defender overcommits one way, go to the other goal. Do not always go to the same goal.
RISK MANAGEMENT: in the final third, take risks with skill moves. In defensive areas, simpler is better. The zone teaches this naturally.
RESILIENCE: losing the ball is part of 1v1 play. Elite dribblers lose the ball regularly — they also create more chances than any other player. Do not stop trying.
ACCELERATION AFTER THE MOVE: the 2 steps after the move are as important as the move itself. Explosive acceleration makes the move unstoppable.
ENJOY IT: 1v1 play is the most enjoyable aspect of football. Play with confidence and freedom.
commonMistakes: Attempting the same move repeatedly regardless of the defenders position
Initiating the move too far from the defender removing the deceptive element
Poor or absent disguise before the move — purely mechanical execution with no deception
Hesitating after the move instead of accelerating immediately
Going to the same goal every time making the attacker predictable
Losing confidence after losing the ball and becoming passive
corrections: Same move every time: in phase 2 the coach names a different move each possession to force variety
Too far from defender: place a cone 1.5m in front of the defender. Attacker must reach the cone before starting the move
No disguise: freeze play when a move is attempted with no setup and ask: what did you do to make them think you were going the other way?
No acceleration: place a cone 4m past the defender. After the move the attacker must reach it within 3 touches
Same goal every time: make one goal worth 2 points and the other worth 1 and alternate which is which every minute
Passive after losing ball: reframe losing the ball as neutral — celebrate the attempt as loudly as the success
progressions: 2v1: second defender joins after 5 seconds. Attacker must use a move to beat the first defender before the second arrives.
Pressure scoring: attacker earns 2 points for a goal scored within 8 seconds of winning the ball (rewards directness)
Move requirement: attacker must complete a minimum of 2 skill moves before shooting or the goal does not count
Tiny goals (1m): reduces the target and demands a move to create a genuine shooting angle
regressions: Slow down to 70 percent intensity for 1 round before building to full pace
Passive defender for the first round — they move but do not tackle
Wider zone (15x10m) giving the attacker more space and time
matchApplication: 1v1 ball mastery is the highest-value individual skill in attacking football. A player who can consistently beat their direct opponent creates a numerical advantage (the beaten defender is effectively removed from the game), draws covering defenders (creating space for teammates), and generates the highest-quality chances. Wide forwards who win 1v1s consistently are among the most valued players in the world. This drill replicates the exact competitive, pressured environment of a real 1v1 in a match — the two-goal format means the attacker must read space as well as beat the defender, exactly as in a real match.
coachTips: Keep the energy high throughout. 1v1 drills feed on competitive atmosphere — music, encouragement, and a points leaderboard all help.
Move between zones constantly during play. Give each pair one specific coaching point per round rather than stopping everyone.
Watch for players who stop trying after losing the ball. Address this privately and positively — resilience in 1v1 is a mindset, not a skill.
The coach-named move phase (phase 2) is the most important coaching moment. Watch whether players can execute a named move under real pressure.
End the session with a tournament format: winners play winners, losers play losers. Creates maximum competitive intensity for the final 5 minutes.
diagramDescription: 12x8m zone with 4 corner cones. Cone goal (2m wide) at each short end. Two players shown at centre facing each other. Ball shown between them. Multiple zones arranged side by side across a 40x30m area. Arrows from each player show movement toward both goals. Small inset shows the two-goal format: attacker can score at either end forcing the defender to cover both directions.

title: Senior Ball Mastery Under Match Pressure
objective: Maintain elite close control and ball mastery under maximum physical and competitive pressure in a match-realistic format.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute ball mastery skills at full match intensity with defensive pressure
Players develop the mental resilience to attempt skill moves under high-stakes competitive pressure
Players build the habit of using close control to escape pressure rather than playing long
Players develop the complete 1v1 dribbling toolkit appropriate for senior competitive football
organisation: 4 zones running simultaneously. Each zone is a 1v1 competition (or 2v2 if player numbers allow). Mini-goals at each end. Play is continuous. 4 minutes per round, 1 minute rotation. Players rotate opponents after each round. Coach circulates between zones.
instructions: Set up 4 zones of 15x12m with mini-goals at each end.
Assign pairs to zones. Both players are attackers and defenders simultaneously — whoever has the ball attacks.
Ball starts with a coin toss. Play begins immediately at full intensity.
RULE: a goal only counts if the scorer has attempted at least one skill move in that possession before scoring.
If a player scores without attempting a move — goal does not count and possession switches.
This rule forces skill moves even under competitive pressure — exactly replicating the match environment.
4-minute rounds. Count goals. After each round players rotate to the next zone (new opponent).
PHASE 2 (after 16 minutes): groups of 3 (2v1) in each zone. Overload creates more realistic match pressure.
Final round: no skill move rule — pure competition. Observe which players still attempt moves by habit.
coachingPoints: BALL MASTERY IS NOT SHOWING OFF: at senior level, close control serves a purpose — escaping pressure, creating space, buying time for support. Every move must have a clear reason.
READ AND REACT: the best senior dribblers do not plan their moves. They read the defender in real time and react. Train the reaction, not the choreography.
BODY STRENGTH: at senior level, combine skill moves with physical strength. Use the shoulder and hip to shield while executing the move.
COMPOSURE UNDER PRESSURE: senior football is faster and more physical than youth football. The composure to execute close control under that intensity is the difference between good and elite.
KNOW YOUR MOVE: each player should have 2-3 moves they can execute reliably at 100 percent intensity. These are their weapons. Know them, trust them, use them.
QUICK RELEASE: after the move, release the ball quickly — pass, shoot, or drive. Holding too long at senior level allows defensive recovery.
PROTECT WITH THE BODY: use the non-dominant side of the body as a shield throughout any close-control situation. The arm creates space legally.
FITNESS IN CLOSE CONTROL: senior 1v1 play is physically demanding. Maintain quality of touch even in the 20th minute of intense play.
RISK AWARENESS: close control in your own defensive third is high risk. In the attacking third it is high reward. Adjust accordingly.
MENTAL STRENGTH: attempting a skill move and losing the ball is not failure. Not attempting the move and wasting the possession is failure.
commonMistakes: Abandoning skill moves after the first failed attempt and reverting to simple play
Attempting moves in the wrong areas of the pitch (own defensive third)
Executing moves at reduced intensity — going through the motions rather than committing
Not using the body to complement the technical skill move
Holding the ball too long after the move allowing defensive recovery
corrections: Abandoning moves: the skill move rule (goal does not count without a move) forces continued attempts regardless of previous failures
Wrong area: add a coloured zone in the defensive third where skill moves are banned — forces positional awareness
Reduced intensity: add fitness consequence for failed moves in wrong areas (5 press-ups) — raises the stakes
Not using body: specific coaching on the shoulder shield — practice shielding drill before starting the zone game
Holding too long: 3-touch maximum after completing a skill move — must release within 3 touches
progressions: 2v2 in zones: adds supporting play and creates decision between using a move or playing a combination
Transition game: after scoring, team immediately defends the counter. Tests close control in transition.
Points for moves: award 1 extra point for a completed skill move regardless of whether a goal follows. Values the attempt.
No-rule final: remove all restrictions in the final 5 minutes. Observe what habits have been built.
regressions: Reduce to passive defender for one round to build confidence before full intensity
Increase zone to 20x15m giving more time and space
Remove the skill move goal requirement for the first round to establish the competitive format before adding the rule
matchApplication: Senior ball mastery under pressure is what separates technically gifted players from truly effective senior players. In adult football the pace, physicality, and intelligence of defending means that close-control skills must be executed faster, under more physical pressure, and with immediate decisive action after the move. The drill format here — continuous competitive play with the skill move requirement — forces exactly the match-realistic application of close control that training drills often fail to replicate. Players who complete this drill consistently develop the confidence and resilience to attempt skill moves in high-stakes match moments.
coachTips: At senior level you can be more direct with coaching feedback. Name mistakes clearly and give the correction immediately.
The skill move goal requirement is the key mechanism of this drill. Enforce it consistently — any wavering removes the learning value.
Watch the final no-rule round carefully. The players who still attempt moves by habit have internalised the lesson. Those who revert to simple play need more repetition.
Senior players respond to competitive formats. Keep the points score visible and the competition real.
This drill works well as a pre-match activation — 10 minutes at 70 percent intensity wakes up the close-control instincts before a game.
diagramDescription: 40x30m area divided into four 15x12m zones by cone lines. Each zone has a mini-goal (2 cones, 2m wide) at each short end. Players shown in 1v1 format within each zone. Ball shown between the two players at zone centre. Coach position shown circulating between zones. Phase 2 diagram shows 3-player (2v1) format in each zone with the single defender shown in bib.

title: Cone Dribble and Freeze
objective: Develop basic dribbling control using small touches to navigate through cones and stop the ball on command.
learningOutcomes: Players can dribble through a cone course using small controlled touches
Players can stop the ball instantly on a command signal
Players develop spatial awareness while moving with the ball
Players build confidence carrying the ball in different directions
pitchSize: 20x10m area with cone courses set up
organisation: Set up 4 parallel cone courses side by side. Each course: 8 cones in a straight line 1.5m apart. Players line up at the start of each course. When the player ahead reaches the 4th cone the next player goes. Freeze game: when coach blows whistle all players stop ball immediately.
instructions: Stand behind the first cone with the ball at your feet.
Dribble to the right of cone 1, left of cone 2, right of cone 3 — weaving through all 8 cones.
Use small touches — the ball should never travel more than 1 step ahead of you.
When you reach the end, dribble back through the cones from the other side.
FREEZE GAME: at any moment the coach blows the whistle. Every player must stop their ball instantly using the sole of their foot.
Any player whose ball rolls more than 1m after the whistle does 3 star jumps and rejoins.
After 5 minutes change to free dribbling in the whole area — no cones, just dribble anywhere.
Freeze game continues in the free dribbling phase.
Final 2 minutes: players try to knock others balls away while keeping their own — last player with their ball wins.
coachingPoints: SMALL TOUCHES: the ball should always be close to your feet. Big heavy touches lose the ball around cones.
USE BOTH FEET: try to use your right foot on the right side of each cone and left foot on the left side.
LOOK UP: after every 2-3 touches look up to see where you are going and where other players are.
SOFT TOUCH: the ball does not need to be kicked hard. A gentle nudge keeps it close and under control.
FREEZE RESPONSE: when you hear the whistle, your foot goes straight on top of the ball immediately.
BODY LEAN: lean forward slightly over the ball when dribbling. Standing upright makes it harder to control.
ENJOY IT: dribbling is one of the most fun parts of football. Smile and enjoy having the ball at your feet.
CHANGE DIRECTION: in the free dribbling phase, change direction often. Do not always go in a straight line.
commonMistakes: Heavy touches that send the ball too far ahead and around the outside of cones
Using only the dominant foot for all touches
Looking at the ball the entire time and bumping into other players
Kicking the ball and running after it rather than dribbling with small touches
Not stopping the ball quickly enough on the freeze whistle
corrections: Heavy touches: move cones closer together (1m apart) so heavy touches automatically miss the gate
One foot: every time the ball goes past a cone with the right foot, the next cone must use the left
Not looking up: place a coloured cone at the end of the course and ask players what colour it is (forces eyes up)
Running after ball: slow down to walking pace — dribble at walking speed only for 2 minutes
Slow freeze: practice freeze separately — walk around freely then freeze on whistle before adding the ball
progressions: Reduce cone spacing to 1m to demand tighter, more precise touches
Add a second whistle signal meaning reverse — players must turn and dribble the other way immediately
Knock-out game: players dribble freely and try to kick others balls away while protecting their own
Race format: first player back through the cone course and stopped behind the start line wins
regressions: Increase cone spacing to 2m to allow more time between touches
Allow players to use hands to stop the ball on the freeze whistle before progressing to feet only
Walk through the course at slow pace before adding any speed
matchApplication: The ability to dribble with small controlled touches in tight spaces is the foundation of all close control in football. A player who can keep the ball close while moving in different directions will not lose it in tight areas under pressure. The freeze game directly replicates the moment in a match when a player must stop the ball and change direction quickly when the route forward is closed. Ball control at U8 is entirely about developing touch sensitivity and spatial awareness — both of which this drill targets directly.
coachTips: The freeze game is the most engaging element for U8 players. Use it frequently — the element of surprise keeps attention high.
The knock-out game at the end should feel like a game, not a drill. Let it run freely with minimal coaching interruption.
Praise specific touches: great little touch there rather than generic well done. Specific praise builds specific habits.
Walk through the course yourself first showing exaggerated small touches so players know exactly what is expected.
U8 players need variety every 3-4 minutes. Shift between the cone course and free dribbling regularly.
diagramDescription: 20x10m area with 4 parallel slalom courses each using 8 cones 1.5m apart. Players shown at the start of each course. Weaving arrows show the path through cones (right of 1, left of 2 etc). Whistle icon shown with FREEZE label. Free dribbling area shown with random arrows in the space beside the cone courses. Knock-out game shown with scattered players and arrows showing ball-kicking directions.

title: Speed Dribble Gates
objective: Develop dribbling at pace through directional gates using the outside of the foot.
learningOutcomes: Players can dribble at 80 percent speed while maintaining close control
Players use the outside of the foot as the primary surface for pace dribbling
Players can change direction rapidly while carrying the ball at speed
Players develop the explosive first step acceleration needed to drive at defenders
pitchSize: 30x20m area with 8 two-cone gates positioned randomly
