organisation: Scatter 8 two-cone gates (each gate 1.5m wide) randomly across the 30x20m area. Players dribble through as many gates as possible in 45 seconds. Count the gates. Rest 15 seconds. Repeat. Coach can reposition gates between rounds to create new patterns.
instructions: Gates are scattered randomly across the area — each gate is 2 cones 1.5m apart.
On go: dribble through as many gates as possible in 45 seconds.
A gate only counts if the ball passes through the gap between both cones — not around the outside.
Use the outside of the dominant foot for straight-line dribbling between gates.
When turning to reach the next gate, use the inside of the foot to redirect.
After passing through a gate you cannot immediately re-enter the same gate — must find a different gate.
Count your own gates. Remember your score.
After the 45 seconds: 15 second rest. Compare scores. Next round: beat your own score.
After 3 rounds: challenge a partner. Stand back to back at a central cone. On go — whoever passes through more gates in 45 seconds wins.
Final round: coach removes 2 gates every 15 seconds creating scarcity and forcing faster decision-making.
coachingPoints: OUTSIDE OF FOOT: for straight-line dribbling between gates the outside of the foot is fastest. Inside foot turns are slower. Train the outside touch deliberately.
LOOK AHEAD: as you pass through one gate, your eyes should already be identifying the next gate. Planning ahead eliminates hesitation.
ACCELERATION: after each gate, accelerate hard for 3-4 touches before slowing to find the next gate. Burst-coast rhythm is faster than constant medium pace.
SHARP TURNS: to redirect to a new gate, plant the outside foot firmly and push hard in the new direction. Gentle turns lose time.
TOUCH WEIGHT: between gates use longer touches (1.5-2m). Near a gate use shorter touches (30-40cm). Match the touch length to the situation.
BODY POSITION: lean forward when accelerating between gates. Body angle drives the pace.
WIDE SEARCH: scan the full area not just the gates directly ahead. The furthest available gate might be the best choice if other players are congesting the near gates.
EFFICIENCY: a direct route to a gate is always better than a curved one. Plan straight lines between gates.
commonMistakes: Using the inside of the foot for all dribbling including straight-line sections (slower than outside foot)
Looking at the ball rather than scanning for the next gate
Constant medium pace rather than accelerate-coast rhythm between gates
Turning too wide when redirecting to a new gate, losing metres of ground
Missing gates by going around the outside of the cones rather than through the gap
corrections: Inside foot habit: mark the outside of the boot with tape and tell players only the tape can touch the ball on straight sections
Ball-watching: announce random gate colours or numbers each round and ask players to call them out as they pass through
No acceleration: add a 3-step sprint marker between 2 specific gates — all players must sprint between those 2 gates
Wide turns: place an extra cone inside the turning radius. Players cannot go outside it when redirecting
Missing gates: reduce gate width to 1m so accuracy through the gate is non-negotiable
progressions: Defender added: one player is designated defender and tries to knock other players balls away. Adds real pressure.
Points system: near gates (within 5m) worth 1 point, far gates (over 10m) worth 2 points. Rewards ambition.
Reduce time to 30 seconds and increase intensity expectation
Blind gates: coach calls a gate number and players must find and pass through that specific gate only
regressions: Increase gate width to 2m for players struggling to pass through 1.5m gates at pace
Reduce to 5 gates in a smaller 20x15m area
Slow down to jogging pace for the first round before building to 80 percent
matchApplication: Speed dribbling through gates directly replicates the match skill of driving at defenders and creating space in open areas. A player who can dribble at pace while scanning for the next option — the next gate in this drill, the next pass or run in a match — is a genuinely threatening attacking player. The acceleration-coast pattern between gates mirrors the burst-of-pace followed by reassessment that advanced wingers and midfielders use when driving forward. The scanning habit built here is one of the most important cognitive skills in football.
coachTips: Reposition the gates between every 2-3 rounds. New patterns prevent players memorising a route and force genuine real-time decision-making.
Keep score publicly and celebrate improvement. The personal-best format is highly motivating for U10 players.
Walk through the area before starting and show players what going through a gate looks like vs going around it.
The final round with gates being removed creates genuine urgency. Players will naturally increase their pace and decision speed.
This drill doubles as an excellent warm-up for any session at U10 and above.
diagramDescription: 30x20m area with 8 two-cone gates (labeled G1-G8) scattered randomly at various angles and positions across the space. Multiple players shown dribbling with arrows showing their individual paths through different gates. Arrows show straight-line acceleration between gates and sharp turns to redirect. Outside-of-foot contact shown in small detail diagram. Timer icon shows 45-second work period.

title: Winger 1v1 Crossing Combination
objective: Develop the winger skill set: beat the full-back 1v1 and deliver a quality cross into the box.
learningOutcomes: Players can use a skill move to beat a defending full-back in a realistic wide position
Players understand when to cut inside and when to go to the byline
Players can deliver accurate low and lofted crosses after beating a defender
Players develop the decision-making between cutting inside for a shot or crossing for a teammate
players: Groups of 4 (winger, full-back, 2 strikers). Multiple groups run simultaneously.
equipment: Cones to mark the wide channel
pitchSize: Wide channel: 15m wide x 30m long on each flank. Full penalty area at the end.
organisation: Wide channel marked on the right flank. Winger starts at the halfway line with the ball. Full-back starts 10m inside the channel. Two strikers positioned in the penalty area (one near post, one far post). Roles rotate every 3 minutes.
instructions: Winger receives a pass from the coach at the halfway line and drives into the wide channel.
Full-back comes to meet them and jockeys at the edge of the channel.
Winger must use a skill move to beat the full-back — step-over, scissors, or cut-back.
OPTION A: beat the full-back and get to the byline, then deliver a low cross to the near-post striker.
OPTION B: cut inside past the full-back onto the strong foot and shoot or play to the far-post striker.
The choice between A and B is based on the full-backs body position: if they show inside, go to byline. If they show the byline, cut inside.
After 8 minutes: full-back becomes active and can recover after being beaten.
Strikers must make movement to receive: near-post run for option A, far-post run for option B.
Rotate: winger becomes striker, striker becomes full-back, full-back rests.
Final 5 minutes: live — no pre-set options. Winger decides in real time.
coachingPoints: APPROACH SPEED: drive at the full-back at pace. A slow approach allows them to set their defensive position. Speed creates uncertainty.
READ THE FULL-BACK: as you approach, read their body position. Which foot is forward? Which way is their weight? This tells you which side is faster to go.
THE MOVE TIMING: initiate the move 1-1.5m from the full-back. Too early and they recover. Too close and there is no space to execute.
GOING TO BYLINE: the low cross from the byline is the most dangerous delivery. Keep the ball close after beating the defender so you can deliver quickly before cover arrives.
CUTTING INSIDE: the cut inside onto the strong foot is most effective when the shot angle is good. If the angle is too narrow, play to the far-post striker instead.
CROSS QUALITY: a low driven cross along the ground is harder to defend and easier to finish than a lofted cross. Aim for the 6-yard box or the penalty spot.
LOFTED CROSS: use a lofted cross when the near-post is covered and the far-post runner is in space. The cross must arrive at head height in the far-post area.
STRIKER MOVEMENT: strikers must move to the ball — not wait for it to come to them. Near-post timing is especially critical.
FIRST TOUCH AFTER MOVE: after beating the full-back, the first touch must be into the space behind them — not sideways. Forward momentum is everything.
DECISION SPEED: in a match the decision between byline and cut-inside must be made in under 0.5 seconds. Train this decision until it is automatic.
commonMistakes: Approaching the full-back too slowly giving them time to set their position and close the space
Attempting the skill move too far from the full-back removing the deceptive effect
Always choosing the same option (always byline or always cut inside) regardless of the defenders position
Poor cross quality after beating the defender — overhitting or underhitting
Strikers standing still in the box rather than making timing runs to meet the cross
corrections: Slow approach: place a cone 10m from the defender and tell the winger they must be at full speed by that cone
Move too far from defender: place a cone 1.5m in front of the full-back — move must start at that cone
Always same option: call out byline or inside just before the full-back is beaten, forcing a real-time decision
Poor cross quality: practice crosses separately without the defender for 5 minutes — full-back added after
Static strikers: give each striker a starting cone and a target cone — they must run from one to the other as the winger beats the defender
progressions: Add a covering centre-back who steps across after the full-back is beaten: winger must cross quickly before cover arrives
2v2 in the box: two defenders join the strikers making finishing harder and demanding higher cross quality
Time pressure: winger must cross within 3 seconds of beating the defender
Two touch byline: after the byline is reached, winger has 2 touches only to deliver the cross
regressions: Passive full-back: defender holds position but does not jockey or tackle — just blocks space
Pre-set decision: tell the winger before each rep whether to go byline or cut inside — removes decision element while technique is built
No defender: winger drives, selects option, and delivers to strikers with no pressure
matchApplication: The winger who can beat a full-back 1v1 and deliver a quality cross is one of the most valuable players in football at every level from U14 to senior. The best wide players in the world (Salah, Mbappe, Saka) all combine a reliable skill move to beat the full-back with a quick decision between cutting inside and delivering from the byline. This drill replicates that exact situation in a complete, realistic format including the cross and the finish. The reading-the-full-back coaching point is the most important transfer to match performance.
coachTips: Let players choose their own skill move. The move they are most confident with is the most effective one for them personally.
The cross quality coaching is easy to neglect when the 1v1 beating is exciting. Give equal time to cross delivery.
Film the session from behind the goal. The quality of crossing angles and striker runs is only clearly visible from that position.
Connect to professional footage: Salah cutting inside from the right, Saka delivering from the byline. U14 players respond strongly to professional examples.
Rotate all players through the winger role. Every player needs to understand wide play regardless of their usual position.
diagramDescription: Right flank wide channel (15x30m) with corner cones. Winger (circle with ball) at top of channel. Full-back (triangle) 10m inside channel. Two strikers (stars) in penalty area: one at near post, one at far post. Arrow A shows winger driving to byline and delivering low cross to near-post striker. Arrow B shows winger cutting inside and shooting or playing to far-post striker. Full-back jockeying position shown between both options. Skill move spiral shown at the decision point.

title: Drive and Commit
objective: Develop the ability to drive at defenders at match speed, commit them, and make the correct decision to pass, shoot, or continue dribbling.
learningOutcomes: Players can drive at defenders at match speed without hesitation
Players understand how to commit a defender before playing the pass or shooting
Players develop the decision-making triangle: dribble, pass, or shoot based on the defenders reaction
Players build the directness and confidence of an elite attacking player
organisation: Three attacking players start at the halfway line. Two defenders are positioned 20m from goal. GK in goal. Coach serves the ball to the central attacker. Play begins immediately. Multiple groups rotate through.
instructions: Coach serves ball to central attacker who drives forward at full pace.
The two defenders must hold their position until the attacker reaches 15m from them.
When the attacker reaches 15m: defenders engage.
OPTION 1 (defender commits centrally): attacker plays square pass to wide attacker who crosses or shoots.
OPTION 2 (defender shows inside): attacker drives outside and either crosses or cuts back to the arriving third attacker.
OPTION 3 (both defenders hold back): attacker shoots from distance.
OPTION 4 (attacker beats one defender): drive past and finish 1v1 with GK.
The decision is made in real time — the attacker reads what the defenders give them.
3v2 + GK runs for 20 minutes. Rotate attackers and defenders every 4 minutes.
Final 5 minutes: 3v3 + GK. Extra defender tests the decision-making further.
coachingPoints: DRIVE WITH PURPOSE: when you receive the ball in space, your first action must be to drive forward at maximum pace. Hesitation allows defenders to set.
COMMIT THE DEFENDER: drive directly at the defender — force them to make a decision. Their decision tells you your option.
READ IN REAL TIME: as the defender commits, the correct option becomes clear. Train your eyes to pick up the cues: shoulders, feet, weight distribution.
PASS INTO SPACE: when playing to a wide attacker, play the pass into the space ahead of them — not to their feet. They should run onto it at pace.
SHOOT EARLY: if both defenders hold deep, shoot from distance. A shot from 20m is harder to defend than a shot from 12m because the GK has less time.
1V1 WITH GK: if you beat a defender and face the GK 1v1, do not slow down. Drive to within 8m then place your shot to the far post or chip if GK comes out early.
WIDE ATTACKER MOVEMENT: as the central attacker drives, wide attackers must make attacking runs immediately — not wait to see what happens.
THIRD MAN: the third attacker holds slightly deeper than the other two. They are the safety option if the first two options are covered.
TEMPO: the whole sequence from receiving the ball to a shot or cross should take under 6 seconds. Longer and the defence recovers.
DIRECTNESS: at U17 level the biggest technical failing is lack of directness. Commit to driving. Commit to the decision. Do not slow down.
commonMistakes: Slowing down when approaching the defenders rather than driving at full pace
Making the pass or shot decision before the defender has committed (passing too early removes the 3v2 advantage)
Wide attackers standing still rather than making forward runs as the central attacker drives
Shooting too close to the goalkeeper (straight at the body) rather than to the corners
Playing back to the coach or a safe square pass when a forward option exists
corrections: Slowing down: place a speed gate 5m from the defenders — central attacker must be at full speed through the gate
Passing too early: defenders are instructed to hold for 2 extra seconds before engaging — forces the attacker to drive closer before deciding
Static wide attackers: give each wide attacker a starting position and an end position — they must be running between them as the ball is served
Weak finishing: spend 5 minutes on 1v1 GK finishing separately before the full 3v2 drill
Safe passes: remove the back-pass option entirely — ball must always go forward once the drive starts
progressions: 3v3 + GK: adds an extra defender making the decision harder and the drive more important
One-touch finish: after the central attacker plays the pass, the receiver must finish first time
Counter press: after the attackers score or lose the ball, the defenders immediately counter-press 2v3 in the opposite direction
Blind start: central attacker starts with their back to goal and must turn before driving — adds a receiving element
regressions: Passive defenders (hold position, do not engage) for the first 2 rounds to establish the decision pattern
Reduce to 2v1 + GK to simplify the decision before building to 3v2
Walk through the decision options at slow pace before adding full intensity
matchApplication: The drive-and-commit principle is the foundation of all effective attacking play in senior football. A player who drives at defenders with pace and purpose forces the defensive structure to react — and that reaction creates the opportunity. The 3v2 format replicates the most common attacking overload in football: the transition moment where the attacking team has a numbers advantage. The ability to drive, read the defenders reaction, and make the correct decision in under 1 second is what separates elite attacking players from average ones at U17 and senior level.
coachTips: The most important coaching point is the drive itself. If the central attacker does not drive with genuine pace the whole drill becomes passive. Be explicit about this.
Stop the drill immediately when a player slows down approaching the defenders. Reset and drive again at full pace. Build the habit.
Film the sequence from the side. The timing of the pass relative to the defenders commitment is only visible from a side-on angle.
Connect to professional examples: Mbappe driving at defenders, Vinicius Junior committing full-backs. U17 players respond strongly to elite examples.
In the final 5-minute free round, do not coach. Observe. The habits built in the previous 20 minutes either appear or they do not. What you see tells you what to work on next session.
diagramDescription: Final third (35x50m). Coach shown at halfway line with ball supply. Central attacker (circle) receives ball and drive arrow shows forward run at pace. Two defenders (triangles) shown 20m from goal with engagement trigger line shown as a dotted line 15m from them. Wide attackers (circles) on each flank with forward run arrows. GK in goal. Four numbered option arrows: 1 (square pass left after defender commits), 2 (drive outside right), 3 (long shot), 4 (1v1 with GK). Third attacker shown slightly deeper as safety option.

title: Three Turns Foundation
objective: Teach three basic turning techniques: inside hook, outside hook, and the drag-back.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the inside of the foot to hook the ball and turn 180 degrees
Players can use the outside of the foot to hook the ball and turn
Players can use the sole to drag the ball back and change direction
Players begin to understand when to turn rather than pass
equipment: 3 cones per player in a triangle (2m sides)
organisation: Each player has 3 cones in a small triangle 2m on each side. They stand at cone A and practice each turn at the cone, moving to the next cone after each successful turn. Coach demonstrates all three turns before players begin.
instructions: INSIDE HOOK (at cone A): dribble toward cone A. As you reach it, use the inside of the right foot to hook the ball around the cone turning 180 degrees to the left.
After the hook: dribble to cone B.
OUTSIDE HOOK (at cone B): dribble toward cone B. Use the outside of the right foot to hook the ball around the cone turning 180 degrees to the right.
After the hook: dribble to cone C.
DRAG-BACK (at cone C): dribble toward cone C. Place sole on ball and drag it backward away from the cone then push forward with the inside of the left foot.
After the drag-back: dribble back to cone A.
Repeat the circuit continuously for 4 minutes.
After 4 minutes: switch to using the LEFT foot for all three turns.
After another 4 minutes: choose any turn at each cone — freedom to pick the technique they prefer.
coachingPoints: INSIDE HOOK: the inside of the foot wraps around the side of the ball and hooks it in the new direction. It is not a kick — it is a smooth redirecting motion.
OUTSIDE HOOK: the outside of the foot contacts the far side of the ball and redirects it. Requires flexibility in the ankle.
DRAG-BACK: the sole of the foot sits on top of the ball and pulls it backward. After the drag, push immediately with the other foot.
BODY FOLLOWS THE TURN: after the hook or drag, your whole body must turn in the same direction as the ball. Ball and body turn together.
CLOSE TO THE CONE: the turn happens right at the cone, not 1m before it. A turn 1m before the cone is not a turn — it is a curve.
SOFT TOUCH: all three turns require a soft, sensitive touch. Heavy contact sends the ball too far.
BOTH FEET: every turn must be practiced on both feet. The weaker foot will feel awkward at first — this is normal.
LOOK AFTER THE TURN: as soon as the turn is complete, look up to see where you are going next.
commonMistakes: Turning too far from the cone rather than right at it
Using the toe instead of the correct foot surface for each turn
Body not following the ball — ball turns but player stays facing the same direction
Drag-back too long — pulling the ball too far back before pushing forward
Only using the dominant foot for all turns
corrections: Turning too early: place a small marker right at the cone. Players must touch the marker with their foot before turning.
Wrong surface: walk through each turn at 10 percent pace physically guiding the players foot to the correct contact point
Body not following: hold the players shoulders gently and guide them through the full body rotation after the hook
Drag-back too long: place a cone 50cm behind the starting position — the drag-back must stop before that cone
Dominant foot only: alternate which foot must be used on each lap — right foot circuit, left foot circuit, alternate
progressions: Increase pace to 80 percent — the circuit should be jogged rather than walked
Add a defender: passive defender stands at cone B and the player must use the correct turn to get around them
Timed circuit: how many full triangle circuits can a player complete in 60 seconds?
Player calls the turn: partner calls inside, outside, or drag-back just before the player reaches each cone
regressions: Practice each turn in isolation (not in the circuit) until the technique is clear before combining
Use a larger triangle (3m sides) to give more time between cones
Allow players to stop the ball completely before attempting the turn
matchApplication: Turning is one of the most essential skills in youth and senior football. A player who can turn quickly under pressure can escape tight situations, change the direction of attack, and create opportunities that would otherwise be closed off. The three turns taught here — inside hook, outside hook, and drag-back — cover 90 percent of all turning situations in a match. Learning them at U9 gives players 8+ years to refine and apply them before senior football.
coachTips: Demonstrate all three turns slowly and clearly before the session starts. Show common mistakes intentionally so players know what to avoid.
The inside hook is the easiest of the three. Start all players on this turn and ensure it is clean before introducing the others.
U9 players learn best by doing rather than watching extended demonstrations. Keep demos brief and get them on the ball quickly.
Walk around the triangles giving individual feedback. One coaching point per player per circuit.
Celebrate turns that are performed right at the cone. This is the hardest habit to build and deserves specific praise.
diagramDescription: Small triangle of 3 cones (A, B, C) with 2m sides per player. Multiple triangles across the area. Player shown at cone A with ball. Arrow shows inside hook turn around cone A, dribble to B. Outside hook shown at cone B, dribble to C. Drag-back shown at cone C, dribble back to A. Small inset diagrams show foot contact point for each turn type.

title: Cruyff Turn Under Pressure
objective: Master the Cruyff turn and apply it effectively against a live defender to escape pressure and create space.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the Cruyff turn at match speed with convincing deception
Players understand the situations where the Cruyff turn is most effective
Players can use the Cruyff turn to receive and turn in one movement
Players develop the disguise and body shape needed to make the turn effective
players: Pairs plus groups for live play (8-16 total)
pitchSize: 15x10m zone per pair for phase 1. 20x20m for live phase.
organisation: Phase 1: pairs 10m apart. Phase 2: groups of 3 (server, receiver with defender behind, target). Phase 3: 3v3 live game where Cruyff turns earn bonus points.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Technique (5 minutes): Player A serves a pass to Player B. B shapes to control or pass forward, then drags the ball behind their standing leg with the inside of the kicking foot and turns 180 degrees.
The key: the SHAPE must look like a pass or shot. The deception is everything.
Practice 8 reps each side. Focus on the convincing shape before the drag.
PHASE 2 - With pressure (8 minutes): Defender stands 1m behind the receiver. Server plays the ball in. Receiver must Cruyff turn away from the defender and pass to a target player 10m away.
Defender can pressure but not tackle in the first 4 minutes. Full defending in the last 4 minutes.
PHASE 3 - Live 3v3 (5 minutes): Small-sided game. Any successful Cruyff turn earns 1 bonus point on top of goals.
coachingPoints: THE SHAPE IS EVERYTHING: before the drag-back the player must genuinely look like they are about to pass or shoot. Drop the shoulder, lean forward, swing the leg back — then drag. The deception sells the turn.
STANDING FOOT POSITION: the standing foot should be positioned so the ball can be dragged BEHIND it naturally. If the standing foot is too close to the ball the drag has no room.
THE DRAG: the inside of the kicking foot wraps around the ball and pulls it backward across and behind the standing leg. The ball ends up on the other side of the body.
BODY ROTATION: as the ball is dragged, the body must rotate 180 degrees to follow. The turn is a full body movement, not just a foot movement.
EXIT ACCELERATION: immediately after the turn, take 2-3 hard acceleration steps in the new direction. The Cruyff only creates an advantage if you move quickly after it.
READ THE DEFENDERS WEIGHT: the Cruyff turn works best when the defender has committed to one side. If they are balanced and set, a different turn may be better.
BOTH FEET: a Cruyff turn on both feet is twice as dangerous as one on only the dominant foot. Train both equally.
USE IN RECEIVING: the Cruyff is most effective when receiving the ball with a defender behind — shape to control or pass forward then turn away from the pressure.
DISGUISE THE TURN: do not slow down before the Cruyff. Approach at normal speed and execute at that speed.
commonMistakes: Obvious intent — the shape before the drag does not look like a real pass or shot
Standing foot positioned too close to the ball preventing a clean drag-behind
Dragging too wide — ball ends up too far to the side rather than behind the standing leg
Not accelerating after the turn — standing still after the drag wastes the advantage
Only practicing on the dominant foot
corrections: Obvious intent: ask a partner to guess which direction they are going. If the partner always guesses correctly the shape is not convincing. Work on the fake swing.
Standing foot too close: place a cone as a reference point for standing foot position — foot must be beside the cone (20cm to the side of the ball)
Drag too wide: place a cone directly behind the standing leg — the ball must end up beside that cone after the drag
No acceleration: cone 3m behind the standing position — player must reach it in 2 steps after the turn
Dominant foot only: even-numbered reps use left foot, odd-numbered use right foot — no choice
progressions: Live 1v1: full defending. Attacker tries to Cruyff turn past the defender and score at a mini-goal
Cruyff then pass: after the turn the player must play an accurate pass to a target within 2 touches
Two defenders: one in front, one behind. Player must Cruyff turn away from the front defender while avoiding the back one
Speed challenge: how quickly can a player execute 5 successful Cruyff turns on each foot?
regressions: Static ball: practice the Cruyff from a still ball before adding a served pass
Walk-through pace: perform the full turn at walking pace for 3 minutes to establish the muscle memory
Remove the defender entirely for phase 2 — technique only before adding pressure
matchApplication: The Cruyff turn (named after Johan Cruyff who popularised it in the 1970s) remains one of the most effective pressure-escaping and direction-changing moves in modern football. It is used by players at every level from youth football to the Champions League. The turn is particularly effective in receiving situations where a player has a defender close behind them — the shape to pass or shoot freezes the defender for the split second needed to execute the drag and turn. Messi, Iniesta, and Xavi all use variations of the Cruyff turn as part of their standard technical vocabulary.
coachTips: The deception element of the Cruyff is what makes it different from other turns. Spend more time on the convincing shape than on the drag itself.
Show a slow-motion video of Johan Cruyff or Messi executing the turn. The visual understanding accelerates technical learning significantly.
In phase 3 the bonus point for Cruyff turns incentivises players to use the move in a competitive context. Enforce the bonus consistently.
Do not rush to the live phase. If the technique is not clean in phases 1 and 2 it will not appear in the live game. Quality before competition.
The Cruyff turn on the weaker foot takes 3-4 sessions to feel natural. Manage expectations and celebrate improvement on the weaker side.
diagramDescription: Phase 1: pair 10m apart. Server (square) passes to receiver (circle). Receiver shown with convincing shape (body leaning forward, leg swinging back), then drag arrow shows ball moving behind standing leg. Exit arrow shows acceleration in new direction. Phase 2: adds defender (triangle) 1m behind receiver and target player 10m away. Phase 3: 20x20m 3v3 grid with mini-goals at each end. Cruyff turn shown in centre of pitch.

title: Cross and Finish
objective: Develop accurate crossing technique from wide positions and connect it with quality finishing movement.
learningOutcomes: Players can deliver accurate low and driven crosses from wide positions
Players understand the target zones for crossing (near post, far post, penalty spot)
Players develop the approach run and body position needed for a quality cross
Strikers develop the movement patterns to meet crosses from both flanks
pitchSize: Full width of the penalty area plus wide channels
organisation: Wide position cones on both flanks at the edge of the penalty area (byline angle) and at 45 degrees (cut-back position). 2-3 crossers on each flank with a ball supply. 2-3 strikers in the box. GK in goal. Crossers serve alternately from left and right.
instructions: Crosser drives from the wide cone toward the byline at pace.
TARGET ZONE 1 - Near post: drive to the byline and whip a low cross to the near post area (6-yard box edge). Striker at near post makes a near-to-far run and strikes first time.
TARGET ZONE 2 - Far post: deliver a lofted cross to the far post area landing 2m from the far post at head height. Far-post striker times a run from the outside to attack the ball.
TARGET ZONE 3 - Cutback: from the 45-degree cone, cut the ball back to the penalty spot. A third striker holds at the penalty spot and strikes first time.
After 8 minutes on crossing technique: run all three zones simultaneously with all strikers moving at once.
Crosser decides which zone to target based on where the strikers have positioned.
GK is live throughout — quality of cross must beat the GK as well as defenders.
Final 4 minutes: add a defender at the near post. Crosser must deliver past the defender.
coachingPoints: APPROACH ANGLE: approach the ball from a slight inside angle. This allows the crosser to open the body and strike across the ball to create curl.
CONTACT POINT FOR LOW CROSS: contact the centre of the ball with the inside of the foot. Follow through toward the target. Keep head down over the ball.
CONTACT POINT FOR LOFTED CROSS: contact the bottom third of the ball with the instep. Lean back slightly to generate height. Follow through upward.
CROSS BEFORE THE DEFENDER: in a match the cross must be delivered before the full-back recovers. Create urgency — drive at the byline and cross immediately.
NEAR POST CROSS: should arrive at pace, below knee height. This type of cross is the hardest to defend and the easiest to redirect into goal.
FAR POST CROSS: must arrive at head height in the far post zone. Too flat and the GK catches it. Too high and it goes over the striker.
CUTBACK: the cutback to the penalty spot is the highest-percentage crossing option statistically. The ball is moving away from goal which makes it hard to defend.
STRIKER MOVEMENT: strikers must begin their run as the crosser reaches the byline — not when the ball is in the air. Timing is everything.
FIRST-TIME FINISH: from a cross, aim to strike first time wherever possible. Taking a controlling touch gives defenders time to recover.
NEAR-POST TO FAR-POST RUN: the most effective striker movement from a near-post cross is running from near post to far post — this takes you away from the defender and creates space.
commonMistakes: Crosser stopping before crossing rather than delivering from a moving position
Cross arriving too high for a near-post delivery or too low for a far-post lofted cross
Strikers starting their run too early (before the ball is at the byline) or too late (when the ball is already in the air)
Crossing from a square-on body position producing an inaccurate cross
Strikers running to where the ball is rather than into the space the ball will arrive in
corrections: Stopping before crossing: place a cone 2m before the crossing position. Crosser must be moving at full pace past the cone when they cross
Wrong cross height: use target zones — hang a string or bib at the correct height at the near post and far post as a visual reference
Striker timing: give each striker a starting cone 5m back from their target position. They must leave the cone exactly when the crosser reaches the byline
Square-on body: mark foot position on the ground showing the angled approach. Crosser must plant their standing foot on the mark
Running to ball not space: use the near-to-far run drill: cones mark start position (near post area) and end position (far post area). Run between them.
progressions: Add two defenders in the box: crossing must now beat both defenders as well as the GK
Combination before cross: winger receives from a midfielder, beats a cone (representing a defender), then crosses
First-time cross: instead of dribbling to the byline, a pass is played into the wide channel and the crosser crosses first time
Heading only: strikers can only head the cross — no feet. Develops aerial attacking movement specifically
regressions: No GK in phase 1 — focus entirely on cross technique and target zones
Static crosser: ball placed at the byline position and crossed from a standing position before adding movement
Target cones: place a cone in each target zone and crosser aims to hit the cones before adding moving strikers
matchApplication: Crossing and finishing from wide areas accounts for a very high percentage of goals at youth and senior level. A team with quality crossers who can find the near post, far post, and cutback zones creates the best chance-creation in the final third. The three target zones in this drill cover every crossing situation a player encounters in a match. The striker movement patterns (near-to-far run, far-post timing, penalty spot hold) directly replicate what professional strikers do when attacking crosses from wide positions.
coachTips: The cutback to the penalty spot is consistently the most underused crossing option in youth football. Emphasise it specifically — coaches who teach it see immediate improvement in chance conversion.
Give crossers 10 practice deliveries with no strikers before adding the movement. The crossing technique must be established before the combination element is added.
Watch striker movement closely — most errors in crossing-and-finishing come from poor runs rather than poor crosses.
Film from behind the goal. The cross quality and striker positions are most clearly visible from directly behind.
Connect to professional examples: Trent Alexander-Arnold cutbacks, Kevin De Bruyne near-post deliveries.
diagramDescription: Full-width penalty area. Wide channels shown on both flanks. Three crossing positions marked: byline position (bottom left and right corners), 45-degree position (wider angle). Three target zones shown in the box: near-post zone (6-yard box edge), far-post zone (2m from far post), penalty spot (cutback target). Strikers shown with movement arrows: near-to-far run, far-post timing run, penalty spot hold position. Curved cross arrows show each delivery type. GK shown in goal.

title: Early Cross Decision
objective: Develop the ability to deliver early crosses before the defender can recover, creating the highest-value crossing opportunities.
learningOutcomes: Players understand when to cross early vs driving to the byline
Players can deliver an accurate driven cross from open play positions (not just the byline)
Players read the position of the strikers before crossing and select the correct delivery
Players build the confidence to cross under defensive pressure without taking extra touches
organisation: Ball starts with a central midfielder at the halfway line. Wide midfielder (winger) makes a run. CM plays into the wide channel. Winger receives and must decide: early cross (before the full-back arrives) or drive to byline. Two strikers in the box, one defender. GK in goal. Full-back starts 5m behind the winger.
instructions: CM plays into the wide channel. Full-back starts 5m behind the winger.
If the winger receives and the full-back is more than 3m away: EARLY CROSS immediately (2 touches maximum — control and cross).
If the full-back is within 3m: drive to the byline and cross from there.
The early cross is driven with pace through the 6-yard box area. Strikers must attack the space the ball will arrive in.
PHASE 1 (6 min): full-back is passive — always more than 3m away. Winger practices early cross only.
PHASE 2 (8 min): full-back is active at half pace. Winger must read the distance and make the correct decision.
PHASE 3 (8 min): full-back at full pace. Real decision — cross early or drive. Wrong decision loses possession.
Score: 2 points for a goal from an early cross. 1 point for a goal from a byline cross. 0 points for any possession lost unnecessarily.
coachingPoints: THE EARLY CROSS PRINCIPLE: when the full-back has not recovered, the cross must be delivered immediately. Every extra touch gives the defender time to close.
2-TOUCH MAXIMUM FOR EARLY CROSS: touch 1 sets the ball for crossing. Touch 2 delivers the cross. No extra touches. This is a discipline.
BODY OPEN ON FIRST TOUCH: the first touch must open the body toward the crossing target, not set the ball for another dribbling touch. This is the key technical point.
CROSS WITH PACE: an early cross must be driven with pace — not a lofted ball. A driven cross through the 6-yard box area at pace is almost impossible to defend.
READ STRIKER POSITIONS BEFORE RECEIVING: as the pass is coming to you, glance at the box. Where are the strikers? This tells you where to deliver before you receive.
DRIVE THE CROSS THROUGH THE DANGER ZONE: aim for the space between the GK and the near post defender. A cross through this zone forces defenders to make a decision.
WHEN TO DRIVE TO BYLINE: if the full-back is within 3m, driving is safer than the early cross because the cross will be blocked. Take the defender on instead.
TRUST THE DECISION: once you decide to cross early, commit fully. A half-committed early cross is worse than no cross at all.
STRIKERS MUST ANTICIPATE: strikers should be moving as the winger controls the ball — not waiting to see the cross delivered.
FIRST-TIME FINISH: from a driven early cross, a first-time finish is almost always the right choice. The ball is moving at pace — redirect it, do not stop it.
commonMistakes: Taking extra touches before the early cross giving the full-back time to recover
First touch closing the body toward the touchline rather than opening toward the box
Early cross too slow — floating cross rather than driven cross allows the defence to recover
Driving to the byline when the full-back is far away — missing the crossing opportunity
Strikers waiting to see the cross rather than anticipating and moving early
corrections: Extra touches: count aloud for the winger — one (control), two (cross). Any third touch is a penalty.
Wrong first touch: place a cone showing the correct first touch direction (angled toward the box). Foot must go past that cone on touch 1.
Slow cross: hold a string across the 6-yard box — the cross must be driven low enough to go under the string and fast enough to reach it in under 1.5 seconds.
Wrong decision: freeze the game when a winger drives with a far full-back — ask: how far was the full-back? Was this the right decision?
Static strikers: give strikers a starting cone and a movement trigger — they must leave the cone the moment the winger receives.
progressions: 2 defenders in the box: increases difficulty of the finish and demands better cross quality
Add a second crossing option: a third wide player on the far side can overlap and receive a switch instead of the cross
Transition: if the defence wins the ball they immediately play out to a neutral and attack 3v2 the other way
Crossing competition: count only early cross goals over a 10-minute period. Tests both the decision and the execution.
regressions: Always cross early in phase 1 (no decision required) until the 2-touch discipline is established
Passive full-back throughout — removes the defensive pressure and allows pure crossing technique
Reduce to 1 striker in the box to simplify the target selection
matchApplication: The early cross is one of the most underused and highest-value actions in senior football. Analysis of professional football consistently shows that crosses delivered before the defence is set (early crosses) have a significantly higher chance of leading to a goal than crosses delivered from the byline against a recovered defence. Teaching wide players to recognise the early cross opportunity and execute it in 2 touches is a major tactical and technical upgrade at U16 and above. This drill makes that decision and execution automatic.
coachTips: The 2-touch discipline is the single most important habit in this drill. Enforce it absolutely — 3 touches means the cross is cancelled regardless of the outcome.
The points system (2 for early cross goal, 1 for byline goal) directly incentivises the correct decision. Do not remove this incentive.
Walk through the full-back distance concept clearly before phase 2. Players must know exactly what 3m looks like in practice.
The first-touch body opening is the most important technical point for coaches to observe and correct individually.
Film from directly behind the goal. Early cross quality and striker positioning are only clearly visible from this angle.
diagramDescription: Full-width pitch x 40m. CM shown at halfway line passing into wide channel. Winger (circle) receives in channel. Full-back (triangle) shown at two positions: FAR (more than 3m away, early cross arrow shown) and CLOSE (within 3m, drive-to-byline arrow shown). Two strikers in box with anticipation movement arrows. GK in goal. Defender in box. Early cross arrow shows driven ball through 6-yard box. Byline cross arrow shows ball from corner area. Points system shown (2pts early, 1pt byline).

title: Heading Fundamentals
objective: Develop correct heading technique including forehead contact, neck firmness, and directional control.
learningOutcomes: Players can head the ball using the correct forehead contact point
Players keep their eyes open through the heading contact
Players can direct headed clearances and passes to a target
Players develop confidence heading a moving ball
organisation: Pairs 8m apart. Player A is the server (throws the ball underarm to a comfortable head height). Player B heads back to Player A. After 10 headers each, swap roles. Multiple pairs run simultaneously. Build from stationary to moving headers.
instructions: Server throws the ball underarm to head height, 30cm in front of the headers forehead.
Header stands with feet shoulder-width apart, one foot slightly forward.
As the ball arrives: eyes open, watch the ball onto the forehead.
Contact with the centre of the forehead — the flat bone between the eyebrows.
At the moment of contact: neck firm, head drives THROUGH the ball not away from it.
Direct the header back to the server by angling the forehead at contact.
After 10 reps each: increase distance to 10m.
After 10 more reps: server delivers from the side (40-degree angle). Header must angle forehead to direct ball to a third target.
Final 5 minutes: walking headers — server walks toward header, header walks backward, heading continuously while both are moving.
Introduce JUMP heading: server lofts slightly higher and header must jump to meet the ball at the peak of their jump.
coachingPoints: FOREHEAD CONTACT: the contact point is the flat bone between the eyebrows. Never the top of the head (causes pain and inaccuracy) and never the temple.
EYES OPEN: this is the hardest habit to build. Eyes must stay open all the way through contact. Closing the eyes at contact causes misses and poor direction.
ATTACK THE BALL: drive the forehead toward the ball — do not wait for the ball to arrive. The aggression in attacking the ball determines the power.
NECK FIRM: a loose, floppy neck absorbs the impact into the head. A firm neck transfers the power to the ball. Tighten the neck muscles just before contact.
USE THE WHOLE BODY: the heading power comes from the whole body — bend the knees and arch the back before contact then drive forward as you head.
DIRECTION CONTROL: the direction of the header is determined by where the forehead is angled at contact. To head right, open the right side of the forehead. To head left, open the left side.
JUMP TIMING: for aerial headers, leave the ground at the right moment to meet the ball at the peak of the jump — not on the way up or down.
SAFETY: heading should only be practiced with size-appropriate balls and at appropriate ages. U12 is the recommended starting age for heading in training.
commonMistakes: Eyes closing at the moment of contact causing poor direction and weak headers
Using the top of the head rather than the forehead causing pain and inaccuracy
Neck too loose absorbing the impact rather than directing the ball
Waiting for the ball rather than attacking it, reducing power significantly
No whole-body involvement — heading only with the neck muscles
corrections: Eyes closing: put a sticker on the ball and ask players to read the sticker after heading. Forces eyes to stay open.
Top of head: mark the correct contact zone on the forehead with a finger before each rep pointing to the flat forehead bone
Loose neck: ask the player to nod strongly and firmly ten times before heading — this activates and firms the neck muscles
Waiting for ball: serve the ball slightly behind the header so they must lean forward to reach it — trains attacking the ball
No body use: ask the player to show a big arch of the back before each header then drive forward as they head
progressions: Competitive headers: count consecutive successful headers between pairs without the ball touching the ground
Directed headers: place 3 target cones and server calls which target the player must direct the header to
Jump headers from crosses: server delivers a lofted ball from wide and header must time a jumping header
Header and volley: after the header, partner volleys back, header heads again — continuous
regressions: Use a soft foam ball for the first 5 minutes to remove fear and build the heading habit
Reduce distance to 4m so the serve is very gentle and predictable
Static header: ball placed on the servers hands at head height and header simply leans forward to contact it
matchApplication: Heading is a critical skill for central defenders (defensive headers), centre-forwards (attacking headers from crosses), and midfielders (aerial battles in midfield). A player who can head correctly — with forehead contact, firm neck, and directional control — wins aerial battles and creates or prevents goals. The habit of eyes open through contact is the single most important heading skill because it enables accurate direction. U12 is the appropriate age to begin developing heading technique which can then be refined over the following 6 years.
coachTips: Safety first: use size 4 balls at U12 and ensure servers deliver the ball gently and accurately. No powerful throws.
The eyes-open habit is the hardest to build. Do not progress to more complex heading until this is consistent.
Some players have a natural fear of heading. Start with very soft serves and foam balls if necessary. Build confidence gradually.
Connect heading to real match situations: explain when each type of header (defensive clearance, attacking header, flick-on) is used.
Monitor players carefully for any discomfort. Heading should never be painful when performed correctly.
diagramDescription: Pairs of players 8m apart. Server (square) shown throwing ball underarm at head height. Header (circle) shown in correct stance: feet shoulder-width apart, one foot forward, slight knee bend. Contact point diagram shows forehead contact zone highlighted. Direction arrows from forehead angle toward target. Jump header shown with trajectory arc from server loft to player peak jump position.

title: Attacking Header Under Pressure
objective: Develop powerful, accurate attacking headers from crosses under the physical pressure of a close defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can attack a crossed ball aggressively and direct it powerfully toward goal
Players develop the timing of the jump to meet the ball at the peak
Players use the body to create space from a tight-marking defender before heading
Players build confidence competing aerially in match-realistic pressure situations
players: Groups of 4 (crosser, header, defender, GK). Multiple groups.
organisation: Crosser at the wide cone (right flank, edge of box). Header and defender in the box. GK in goal. Crosser delivers and header must win the aerial battle against the defender and direct toward goal. Rotate roles every 5 minutes.
instructions: Crosser delivers a lofted cross from the right flank to the near-post or far-post area.
Header starts 3m from the intended landing zone with the defender directly beside them.
As the cross is delivered: header must make a movement to create separation from the defender (step away then attack the space).
NEAR-POST HEADER: near-post cross. Header attacks with a near-to-far-post run, meeting the ball at its earliest point. Aim to glance the ball toward the far post.
FAR-POST HEADER: far-post cross. Header holds their run, attacks the ball late as it arrives in the far-post zone. Power header downward toward goal.
The defender marks tightly and competes for the ball — live aerial battle.
GK makes genuine saves.
Score: 2 points for headed goal, 1 point for header on target, 0 for winning ball but heading wide or over.
After 5 minutes: add a second crosser on the left. Header must now compete from both sides.
coachingPoints: CREATE SEPARATION FIRST: before attacking the cross, step away from the defender (1 step away from your run direction). This creates the space to make an attacking run into.
ATTACK THE BALL AT THE EARLIEST POINT: do not wait for the ball to come to you. Run toward the ball and meet it at the highest point.
JUMP FROM ONE FOOT: the most powerful and agile jump for an aerial header is from one foot (a running jump) not two feet. Practice taking off on the correct foot.
PEAK OF JUMP: the contact must happen at the absolute peak of the jump — not on the way up and not on the way down. Time the jump accordingly.
POWER FROM THE BODY: arch the back before contact and then drive the whole upper body forward as you head. This is where aerial header power comes from.
DOWNWARD DIRECTION: aimed headers on goal should always go downward. A downward header is harder for the GK to save and does not go over the crossbar.
NEAR-POST GLANCE: for near-post headers the touch is a glance — redirect the balls pace rather than generating new power. Angling the forehead at 45 degrees diverts the cross toward the far post.
EYES ON THE BALL: throughout the run, in the jump, and at the point of contact — eyes on the ball. Look at the target only after heading.
PHYSICAL CONTACT: aerial battles involve legal body contact. Use the shoulder and arm (not elbow) to create and hold space in the air.
COMPOSURE: heading under pressure requires courage and composure. Train the confidence to compete aggressively in aerial battles.
commonMistakes: Running directly toward the ball without creating separation from the defender first
Jumping from two feet which produces a lower and less dynamic jump than a single-foot take-off
Eyes closing at contact causing poor direction and weak contact
Header going over the crossbar because the body is leaning back at contact
Waiting for the ball rather than attacking it at the earliest opportunity
corrections: No separation movement: require a mandatory step in the opposite direction before every attacking run
Two-foot jump: mark the take-off foot with a coloured sock. That foot must leave the ground first on every jump
Eyes closing: sticker on the ball. Coach asks what colour was the sticker after every header
Ball going over: place a string 30cm above the crossbar. Every header must go under it. Forces a downward angle.
Waiting for ball: move the delivery point so the cross arrives earlier — header must go toward it or miss it
progressions: Add a second defender: 1v2 aerial battle — must create space from two defenders
Two crossers: one from left, one from right. Header does not know which side the cross will come from until it is delivered.
Compete for heading against a target: highest header of the session (measured by height of contact) wins bonus points
Full corner routine: headers from corners with full defensive and attacking teams
regressions: Passive defender (holds position but does not jump) for the first 3 minutes
No defender for phase 1 — pure heading technique from crosses
Reduce crossing distance to 10m to make the delivery more consistent and predictable
matchApplication: Attacking headers from crosses are responsible for a significant proportion of goals at every level of football. A striker or midfielder who can outjump a defender and direct a cross toward goal is an enormous asset. The technical elements in this drill — creating separation, one-foot take-off, body arch and drive, downward direction — are exactly what professional centre-forwards practice specifically. Players like Olivier Giroud, Harry Kane, and Erling Haaland are exceptional aerial threats because of dedicated technical development in these exact areas.
coachTips: The separation movement before the run is the most important tactical coaching point. Emphasise it specifically and celebrate it when players use it correctly.
The downward header is a habit that takes significant time to build. Use the string above the crossbar as a consistent reminder.
Aerial competition requires physical courage. Create a positive, competitive atmosphere — celebrate committed aerial challenges as much as goals.
Film from behind the goal. The trajectory, power, and direction of headers is most clearly visible from directly behind.
The near-post glance is a sophisticated technique. Introduce it after the power heading is established.
diagramDescription: Penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Wide channel on right flank with crosser cone. Two players shown in box: header (circle) and defender (triangle) standing beside each other. Separation movement arrow shows header stepping left before attacking the cross. Cross delivery arc shown from wide position to near-post and far-post zones. Jump trajectory shows one-foot take-off and peak contact point. Downward header arrow shows ball direction toward goal bottom corner. Points system shown.

title: Weak Foot Foundations
objective: Begin developing the weak foot through high-repetition passing, dribbling, and shooting using only the non-dominant foot.
learningOutcomes: Players can pass accurately with their weak foot over 8m
Players can dribble a simple cone course using only the weak foot
Players build confidence using the foot they naturally avoid
Players understand why developing both feet makes them a better player
organisation: Three stations set up simultaneously: Station 1 (pass against wall with weak foot), Station 2 (dribble cone course using only weak foot), Station 3 (weak-foot shooting at mini-goal). Players rotate every 4 minutes. Dominant foot is not allowed at any station.
instructions: STATION 1 - Wall passing (4 min): Stand 6m from the wall. Pass against the wall with the weak foot only. Control the rebound with the weak foot. Aim to complete 20 successful passes. If no wall: partner passes back while the working player uses only their weak foot.
STATION 2 - Weak foot dribble (4 min): 6-cone slalom course. Dribble through using ONLY the weak foot — no dominant foot touches allowed. Walk pace first then build to jogging pace.
STATION 3 - Weak foot shooting (4 min): Ball placed at 8m from a 3m wide mini-goal. Strike with weak foot only. Use inside of foot for accuracy. Count goals from 10 attempts.
Final 3 minutes: free practice — players choose any of the three skills using only their weak foot.
coachingPoints: THE WEAK FOOT FEELS WRONG AT FIRST: this is completely normal. Every professional player had to develop their weak foot through practice. The awkward feeling goes away with repetition.
SAME TECHNIQUE AS THE STRONG FOOT: everything you learned about passing, dribbling, and shooting applies equally to the weak foot. Plant foot, contact point, follow-through — all the same.
SLOW DOWN: use the weak foot at 50 percent of your normal pace. Speed will come naturally as the foot gets stronger and more coordinated.
SMALL TOUCHES IN DRIBBLING: the weak foot tends to produce heavy touches at first. Be conscious of smaller, softer touches to keep the ball close.
INSIDE OF FOOT FOR PASSING: use the inside of the weak foot for passing — it is the most accurate surface regardless of which foot is used.
CELEBRATE WEAK FOOT SUCCESS: every accurate weak foot pass, every cone navigated, every shot on target with the weak foot is a genuine achievement worth celebrating.
DAILY PRACTICE: the weak foot improves dramatically with daily practice. Even 5 minutes of weak foot work every day produces visible improvement in 4-6 weeks.
A TWO-FOOTED PLAYER IS TWICE AS DANGEROUS: a player who can pass and shoot with both feet is much harder to defend because the defender cannot predict which foot will be used.
commonMistakes: Using the dominant foot when it gets difficult rather than persisting with the weak foot
Going too fast before the weak foot coordination is established
Giving up after the first misplaced pass or miscontrol
Using the toe to kick with the weak foot rather than the correct inside or laces surface
Not practicing the weak foot outside of training sessions
corrections: Using dominant foot: tie a ribbon to the dominant foot ankle — if they use it everyone can see
Too fast: slow down to walking pace only. Speed is the enemy of weak foot development at this stage.
Giving up: reframe every miscontrol as progress — every bad touch with the weak foot is one step closer to a good touch
Toe kicking: same correction as strong foot — sticker on the inside surface, only the sticker touches the ball
Not practicing outside training: set a daily challenge: 50 weak foot touches against a wall every evening
progressions: Increase distance at station 1 to 10m then 12m
Add a gate through the slalom (1m wide) that must be hit at station 2
Increase shooting distance to 10m then 12m at station 3
Combine strong and weak foot: strong foot pass, weak foot control, weak foot pass — 3-touch sequence
regressions: Reduce wall distance to 4m for players really struggling with accuracy
Use a larger mini-goal (4m wide) at station 3 to increase confidence with scoring
Remove the slalom cones and allow free dribbling with weak foot only
matchApplication: A player with a strong weak foot is significantly more difficult to defend. Defenders can no longer show them onto their weaker side because there is no weak side. Every professional player who plays at senior level can use both feet competently. Players who develop their weak foot at U10-U12 have 6+ years to build it to near-equal standard by the time they reach senior football. The daily practice habit built here is more important than any single session.
coachTips: Frame weak foot development as exciting, not remedial. Tell players they are learning something that most players never work on seriously.
Share the story of a professional player famous for their weak foot development (Phil Foden, Pedri, and Iniesta are all examples of technically two-footed players).
The ribbon on the dominant foot makes the dominant-foot rule visual and fun rather than restrictive.
Set a 4-week weak foot challenge: count goals at station 3 at week 1. Repeat the count at week 4. The improvement is always visible and always motivating.
Do not accept dominant foot cheating. The value of this drill exists only if the weak foot is used exclusively.
diagramDescription: 20x15m area with 3 stations. Station 1 (left): wall shown with 6m distance marker. Player shown passing with weak foot (non-dominant foot highlighted). Station 2 (centre): 6-cone slalom with weak-foot-only dribble arrows (single curved arrows, not alternating). Station 3 (right): 3m wide mini-goal with player 8m away shooting with weak foot. Rotation arrows link the three stations. Ribbon icon shown on dominant ankle. Weak foot symbol (smaller foot icon) shown at each station.

title: Weak Foot Pressure Game
objective: Apply the weak foot under competitive pressure in a small-sided game format that forces its use.
learningOutcomes: Players can use the weak foot to pass and shoot under real match pressure
Players develop the automaticity of using the weak foot when it is the most efficient option
Players build competitive confidence using their weaker foot
Players understand positionally when the weak foot is the correct technical choice
organisation: 4v4 or 5v5 in a 30x20m area. 4 mini-goals — one on each side of the rectangle (not just the ends). RULE: any goal scored with the weak foot counts DOUBLE. Any goal scored with the dominant foot counts single. Players must use both feet throughout. Referee (coach) enforces weak foot goals doubling.
instructions: Play 4v4 normal football rules in the 30x20m area.
4 goals — one on each side — means players attack and defend in all directions.
Any goal scored using the weak foot: 2 points. Dominant foot goal: 1 point.
First to 10 points wins each 5-minute round.
Coach announces which foot is the weak foot for each player at the start (they cannot claim their stronger foot is actually their weak foot).

PHASE 2 (after 10 min): introduce WEAK FOOT ZONES — two 5m zones marked on the pitch where any player in possession MUST use their weak foot only. Any dominant foot touch in these zones: possession switches.
PHASE 3 (final 5 min): weak foot only for all touches by all players. Chaos — but enormously beneficial for weak foot development.
coachingPoints: POSITIONAL AWARENESS: as you receive the ball, be aware of which foot is naturally set up for the next pass or shot. If the weak foot is ready — use it. Do not reset to the strong foot.
WEAK FOOT SHOOTING: when in shooting range and the weak foot is closest to the ball — shoot. The instinct to reset to the strong foot costs time and loses the shooting angle.
GAME INTELLIGENCE: the double-points rule changes the game. Recognise shooting opportunities with the weak foot and prioritise them.
WEAK FOOT PASSING: under pressure, a quick weak foot pass is always better than a slow strong foot pass. Speed of release is more important than which foot.
FIRST TOUCH WITH WEAK FOOT: practice consciously setting your first touch so the weak foot is positioned for the next action.
DO NOT TELEGRAPH: when using the weak foot, do not slow down or change body position in a way that signals to the defender what you are doing.
CELEBRATE WEAK FOOT GOALS: this is the most important moment in weak foot development — the feeling of scoring with the weaker foot is extremely motivating.
TURN WITH WEAK FOOT: in the weak foot zones, practice turning using the weak foot as the pivot and driving foot.
commonMistakes: Resetting from weak foot to strong foot in every situation even when the weak foot is the better option
Avoiding the weak foot zones rather than using them as a development opportunity
Using the weak foot only when absolutely forced to — not embracing the development opportunity
Toe-kicking with the weak foot when under pressure rather than using the correct surface
corrections: Resetting to strong foot: whenever coach sees a reset call WEAK FOOT OPPORTUNITY — did you miss it?
Avoiding zones: make the weak foot zones smaller and more central so they cannot be avoided
Not embracing: highlight weak foot goals loudly and celebrate them more than any other goal in the session
Toe-kicking: slow the game for 2 minutes in phase 3 so players can use correct technique without time pressure
progressions: Weak foot only for all outfield shooting (dominant foot only for GK): maximum weak foot shooting pressure
Bonus point for a weak foot assist leading to a goal (regardless of which foot finishes)
Tournament: 3 teams of 4. Rotate opponents every 5 minutes. Weak foot points system throughout.
regressions: Increase goal size to 3m wide to improve conversion and confidence with weak foot shooting
Remove weak foot zones in phase 2 and keep only the points system
Allow more time with the ball in the weak foot zones (no pressure from defender for 3 seconds)
matchApplication: The weak foot pressure game directly replicates the match situations where the weak foot should be used. Many U14 players identify their weak foot correctly in isolation drills but immediately default to their strong foot in a competitive game. The double-points system creates a genuine competitive incentive to use the weaker foot in real pressure situations. The weak foot zones force the skill in specific areas. By U14 a player who cannot use their weak foot competently under pressure is significantly limited — this drill addresses that limitation directly and measurably.
coachTips: Be consistent enforcing the weak foot zone rule. Any leniency removes the learning value immediately.
Track which players score weak foot goals over the session and highlight their improvement publicly.
The double-points system changes the tactical approach to the game. Players start to move into weak-foot scoring positions deliberately. This is the real learning moment.
Phase 3 (weak foot only) will feel chaotic. Let it run — the chaos forces players to work through their weak foot discomfort under real pressure which is exactly what is needed.
Use this game format as a warm-up for any session at U14 and above. 10 minutes of weak foot pressure game before the main drill activates both feet for the session.
diagramDescription: 30x20m rectangle with 4 mini-goals: one on each side of the rectangle (left side, right side, top end, bottom end). 4v4 shown with bibs. Two weak foot zones (shaded rectangles, 5m wide) shown crossing the central portion of the pitch. Players in weak foot zones shown with weak foot icon. Points system: weak foot goal = 2pts shown in large text, dominant foot goal = 1pt. Phase 3 text shows WEAK FOOT ONLY ALL PLAYERS.

title: Advanced Skill Move Sequence
objective: Combine 4 skill moves in a continuous flowing sequence at match speed.
learningOutcomes: Players can link multiple skill moves without pausing
Players develop automaticity in skill execution
Players build confidence using moves against live opposition
Players understand when to use each move based on defender position
equipment: 1 ball per pair, cones for 15x8m channels
organisation: Pairs in 15x8m channels. Attacker at one end with ball, passive then active defender at midpoint, mini-goal at far end. Coach demonstrates the 4-move sequence before players attempt it.
instructions: MOVE 1 - SCISSORS: approach the defender, swing one foot around the ball outside to inside without touching it, plant that foot, push ball with outside of the other foot in the opposite direction.
MOVE 2 - STEP-OVER: swing your foot over the ball outside to inside without touching it, plant, push ball the other way with outside of opposite foot.
MOVE 3 - DOUBLE TOUCH: tap ball inside with right, immediately outside with right, creating a sharp directional change in two touches.
MOVE 4 - SHOULDER DROP: dip shoulder hard one way, shift body weight that direction, push ball hard the other way and accelerate.
PHASE 1 (6 min): walk through each move statically. Coach names each move, players practice it in isolation on the spot.
PHASE 2 (7 min): passive defender at midpoint. Attacker dribbles and selects any move to get past. Defender holds position but does not tackle.
PHASE 3 (7 min): live defender. Attacker must beat defender using one of the 4 moves to score in mini-goal. Defender earns point for clean tackle. Rotate every 3 reps.
coachingPoints: APPROACH SPEED: do not slow down before the move. Approach at dribbling pace. Slowing down signals to the defender something is coming.
SELL THE FAKE: the move only works if the defender believes the initial direction. Use your eyes, shoulders, and hips to sell the fake direction.
BALL DISTANCE: the ball should be within 1 step at all times during the approach. Too far away and the move cannot be executed cleanly.
SCISSORS TIMING: the swinging foot goes AROUND the ball, never through it. The push-away comes from the opposite foot immediately after.
STEP-OVER DIRECTION: the foot goes OVER the top of the ball. The ball goes in the OPPOSITE direction to the foot that went over.
DOUBLE TOUCH SPEED: the inside-outside combination must happen in under 0.5 seconds. Too slow and the defender reads it.
SHOULDER DROP COMMITMENT: the shoulder must drop convincingly. A half-hearted drop fools nobody. Commit fully to the fake direction.
FIRST TOUCH AFTER THE MOVE: the push away from the move must go into space, not back toward the defender. Always push into open space.
ACCELERATION: after any move, the first 3 steps away must be at maximum pace. The move creates a split second of advantage - use it immediately.
REPETITION: each move needs 20+ successful reps before it becomes automatic. A move performed at 90% is a move that will fail under match pressure.
READ THE DEFENDER: watch the defenders weight distribution. If weight is on the right foot, go left. The move exploits which way the defender cannot go quickly.
CONFIDENCE: a move performed with hesitation will fail even if technically correct. Commit fully or do not attempt it.
commonMistakes: Performing the move too far from the defender - needs to be within 1-1.5m to be effective
Slowing down before the move which telegraphs intent to the defender
Not accelerating after the move allowing the defender to recover
Using only one or two moves and becoming predictable
Attempting the move with the ball too far from the feet
Looking at the ball instead of the defender during the approach
corrections: For too far from defender: place a cone 1.5m in front of defender. Move must happen before or at the cone
For slowing down: coach claps a steady rhythm - pace must not change before the move
For no acceleration: place a gate cone 3m beyond the defender. Player must reach it in 2 steps after the move
For predictability: call out which move must be used just before the player reaches the defender
For ball distance: use a smaller playing area to force closer control
For ball-watching: ask player to call out the colour of the defenders bib while performing the move
progressions: Full live 1v1 with no channel restriction - open space forces the player to create the angle themselves
Two defenders - beat the first, immediately face the second 3m beyond
Points system: scissors 1pt, step-over 1pt, double touch 2pt, shoulder drop 2pt - highest scorer wins
Time challenge: how many successful moves can each player complete in 90 seconds against a passive defender
regressions: Reduce to two moves only (scissors and step-over) before building to all four
Walk-through pace with static defender for the full session if players struggle with the live element
Wider channel (12m) gives more space to execute
matchApplication: Skill moves in 1v1 situations are match-decisive. A winger who can consistently beat a full-back 1v1 is among the most dangerous players on the pitch. The moves in this drill directly replicate what elite wingers use in professional football every week. At U14 players are physically ready to execute these at competitive pace - the challenge is building the confidence and automaticity to use them under genuine match pressure rather than freezing and playing safe.
coachTips: Film players attempting each move and show them the footage immediately. Seeing their own technique is transformative at U14.
Connect each move to a professional: scissors to Cristiano Ronaldo, step-over to Arjen Robben, shoulder drop to Thierry Henry.
Do not correct every mistake simultaneously. Focus on one technical element per player per session.
The competition element in Phase 3 is essential. Without it players do not develop the ability to execute under pressure.
Celebrate attempts even when they fail. A player who tries a move and fails is developing. A player who never tries is not.
diagramDescription: 15x8m channel. Attacker (circle) at one end with ball. Defender (triangle) at midpoint. Mini-goal at far end. Arrows show attacker approaching defender at pace. Spiral symbol at defender position shows skill move execution point. Arrow continues past defender to mini-goal. Small inset diagrams show foot path for each of the 4 moves.

title: Senior Ball Mastery Rondo
objective: Execute high-intensity ball mastery in a competitive rondo format with full pressing pressure.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain close control under intense physical and mental pressure
Players develop the automaticity to use ball mastery skills in a possession context
Players build composure when pressed from multiple angles
Players understand how ball mastery enables possession retention
equipment: 1 ball per group, 4 cones per 12x12m square, bibs
organisation: 5 attackers outside a 12x12m square (one on each side and one floater who moves freely). 1 defender inside. Ball circulates among the 5 attackers. Defender tries to intercept. If defender touches ball, the player who played the losing pass becomes defender. Rotate defender after 90 seconds regardless.
instructions: Ball starts with any outside attacker. They have maximum 2 touches.
The floater can move anywhere along the outside of the square to create an angle.
If pressed by the defender approaching the square boundary, the attacker uses a ball mastery move (sole roll, inside-outside, drag-back) to shift the ball and play a different angle.
PHASE 1 (6 min): 2-touch rule. Focus on using ball mastery on the first touch to set up the second (passing) touch.
PHASE 2 (6 min): 1-touch rule. Forces higher tempo and earlier decision-making.
PHASE 3 (6 min): Free touches but defender becomes 2 defenders (5v2). Players must use ball mastery more frequently to find safe angles.
Count consecutive successful passes in each group. Team record is the target to beat each session.
coachingPoints: FIRST TOUCH AS A WEAPON: the first touch is not just to control - it is to move the ball to the optimal position for the next pass. A sole roll sideways, a drag-back, or an inside redirect all create better passing angles than a dead stop.
BODY POSITION: receive on the half-turn always. Hips at 45 degrees so both passing options are visible before the ball arrives.
FEEL THE PRESSURE: sense where the defender is without looking directly at them. Use peripheral vision and body awareness.
WHEN TO USE BALL MASTERY: when the defender is within 2m and cutting off the obvious pass. Ball mastery buys the split second needed to find the alternative.
TEMPO: the pace of passing must be fast enough that the defender cannot get set. Slow rondos are easy for defenders.
THE FLOATER: the floating player is the most valuable position. They must constantly move to always offer an angle that bypasses the defender.
COMMUNICATION: call the name of the player you are passing to before the ball leaves your foot.
WEIGHT OF PASS: firm enough to travel quickly, accurate enough to arrive comfortably. A weak pass gives the defender time to intercept.
AFTER THE PASS: move immediately to create a new angle. The player who passes and stands still is the player who makes the next pass impossible.
2-TOUCH DISCIPLINE: the first touch sets the ball, the second touch passes it. Never take a third touch without a very good reason.
UNDER PRESSURE COMPOSURE: when the defender is close, slow your thoughts down. The correct pass is always available if you look for it.
MISTAKES ARE DATA: when the ball is lost, analyse why immediately. Wrong first touch direction, heavy touch, poor weight? Fix it next rep.
commonMistakes: Standing still after passing instead of moving to create a new angle
Receiving chest-on instead of on the half-turn
Using ball mastery moves when a simple pass was available (overcomplicating)
Pass weight too light allowing the defender to intercept easily
Floater standing in a straight line with the ball and another attacker (no angle created)
Players panicking and playing the first available pass rather than the best pass
corrections: Static receivers: add a rule that every player must take at least one step before receiving the ball
Chest-on receiving: place a cone behind each corner player. They must be able to see it when receiving (forces half-turn)
Overcomplication: remind players - ball mastery is for when you need it, not every touch
Light passes: the pass must reach the partner in under 2 seconds. If not, it does not count
