Floater alignment: the floater must always be visible to the ball-carrier without the defender being in the way
Panic passing: introduce a 1-second rule - player must pause for 1 count before passing to force composure
progressions: 5v2 rondo: two defenders inside creates significantly more pressure and forces faster decisions
1-touch everywhere: no control touch allowed for any player
Direction calls: coach shouts SWITCH and circulation direction reverses immediately
Scoring rondo: attackers score a point by playing a diagonal pass that travels through the square to the far corner
regressions: Increase square to 15x15m to give more time and space
Allow 3 touches in the first phase before reducing to 2
Use a passive defender (can only walk) for the first 4 minutes
matchApplication: Ball mastery in a rondo context directly replicates the demands of central midfield play at senior level. Every professional club uses the rondo as a daily training tool precisely because it develops the skills most needed in match situations: composure under pressure, quick decision-making, first touch quality, and the ability to use technical skills to create passing angles in tight spaces. U16 players who master this rondo format are developing the exact attributes needed for senior football.
coachTips: The rondo is the most efficient training drill in football. It can be used as a warm-up, a main session activity, or a pre-match activation.
Walk between groups and give one specific coaching point per group per rotation. Do not stop the drill to address the whole group.
The competitive element (consecutive pass count) keeps intensity high without the coach needing to push.
Identify which players are the weak link in each group and work with them individually during the rest period.
Connect to professional football: show video of Barcelona or Man City using the rondo in their training sessions.
diagramDescription: 12x12m square with 4 corner cones. 4 attackers (circles) on each side outside the square. 1 floater (diamond) shown moving along the outside. 1 defender (triangle) inside. Arrows show passing options around the square. Dotted arrow shows diagonal pass option through the square. Ball mastery symbol shown at one corner where the defender approaches.

title: Senior Passing Combinations
objective: Execute complex multi-player passing combinations at full match intensity with positional awareness.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute 3 and 4-player passing combinations automatically
Players develop the positional intelligence to create and fill spaces after passing
Players build the match-speed combination play needed for senior competitive football
Players can transition from combination play to a shooting opportunity in one sequence
equipment: 3 balls, 20 cones, bibs (2 colours), full-size or mini goals
organisation: A 40x30m area divided into 3 horizontal zones. 4 central midfielders in the middle zone. 2 wide players in each wide channel. 2 forwards in the attacking zone. Ball starts with midfielders. Neutral GK at each end. Multiple balls in play simultaneously to maintain intensity.
instructions: COMBINATION 1 - THIRD MAN: Player A passes to B. B passes to C. A makes a run to receive from C in a new position. The third man (A) receives the ball having made a run that the defender cannot track.

COMBINATION 2 - OVERLAP: wide player receives from midfielder and plays inside to forward. Midfielder who played the first pass overlaps on the outside. Forward plays into the overlapping midfielders run.
COMBINATION 3 - DOUBLE SWITCH: ball played from left to right through 3 passes across the full width. Each player plays 1-touch. Ball arrives to the right wide player in space.
PHASE 1 (8 min): combinations without defenders. Walk through each combination pattern twice then execute at speed.
PHASE 2 (10 min): 2 central defenders added. Combinations must find a way through or around the central defending block.
PHASE 3 (7 min): full game. 6v6 in 40x30m. Goals only count from a combination (minimum 3 passes in a sequence ending with a shot).
Count combinations completed successfully per 5-minute period. Target: 15+ for senior players.
coachingPoints: MOVEMENT BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: the run that creates the combination must start before the pass is played. Reacting after the pass is always too late.
1-TOUCH MINIMUM: senior combinations must be predominantly 1-touch. 2-touch is acceptable in tight spaces. 3-touch kills the combination.
BODY SHAPE: every player must be on the half-turn before receiving. This allows the next pass or run to begin the moment the ball arrives.
THIRD MAN TIMING: the third mans run must be timed so they arrive at the receiving position exactly when the ball does. Too early = defender tracks. Too late = ball has already been played elsewhere.
WEIGHT OF PASS: in combinations, passes must be firm enough to travel quickly but not so hard the receiving player cannot control and play in one movement.
COMMUNICATION: combinations require constant verbal communication. Call OVERLAP, THIRD MAN, SWITCH before the action, not during it.
DISGUISE: look one direction before playing another. Every pass in a combination should have some element of misdirection.
SPACING: players in combinations must maintain optimal distance. Too close and they are easy to press. Too far and passes are interceptable.
SWITCH OF PLAY TIMING: the double switch works best when defenders have shifted to one side. Wait for the shift before initiating.
FORWARD RUNS: after every combination, at least one player must make a forward run into the attacking zone. Combinations that circulate laterally without penetrating are useless.
INTENSITY: senior combinations must be executed at match intensity. Training pace combinations do not transfer to match performance.
DECISION HIERARCHY: 1) Can I play forward? 2) Can I combine and create forward? 3) Can I switch? 4) Can I recycle? Always look forward first.
commonMistakes: Combinations breaking down because of heavy first touches under pressure
Players not making the third-man run until after the pass has been played
1-touch passes going sideways rather than forward or penetrating
Combinations becoming predictable because of insufficient disguise
No forward run after the combination is completed
Players stopping to admire a good combination rather than pressing the advantage immediately
corrections: Heavy first touch: reduce the space to 25x20m which forces quicker, softer touches
Late third-man run: freeze the combination at the moment of the second pass and show where the third man should already be
Sideways combinations: reward only combinations that end in a forward pass or shot. Lateral combinations score 0 points
Predictability: rotate which player makes the third-man run each rep so all players practice all roles
No forward run: make it a rule that a combination is only complete when a player has run into the attacking zone after it
Admiring the pass: immediately after any combination, blow the whistle - this signals that the next action must be immediate
progressions: Combinations against a high press: 2 extra defenders specifically press the ball immediately
Time limit: full combination must be completed within 8 seconds of the first pass
Combination and finish: all combinations must end with a shot within 2 touches of the combination ending
Full 11v11 match with combination bonus: goals from combinations score 3 points, all other goals score 1
regressions: Remove defenders entirely for the first 10 minutes to establish the movement patterns
Allow 2 touches throughout before introducing the 1-touch rule
Reduce to 2-player combinations (wall pass only) before building to 3 and 4-player
matchApplication: Complex passing combinations are what separate technically excellent teams from merely capable ones at senior level. The third-man run, the overlap, and the double switch are patterns used by every elite club from Manchester City to Real Madrid in every match. The reason they are effective is that they move the ball faster than defenders can track. Systematic practice of these combinations at training is the only way they become automatic in matches. Senior players who can execute combinations under pressure without thinking about them are among the most effective in the game.
coachTips: Senior players respond better to explanation of WHY than instruction of WHAT. Always connect the combination to a match context.
The competition element in Phase 3 is essential. Senior players need the competitive stimulus to execute at match intensity.
Film the combinations from above if possible. The patterns and spaces are only clearly visible from an aerial perspective.
Rotate positions regularly. Every player should experience every role in each combination.
Keep the session high intensity throughout. Senior players who are not challenged physically and mentally will not engage fully.
diagramDescription: 40x30m area divided into 3 zones. Central midfielders shown in middle zone. Wide channels with wide players. Forward zone with 2 strikers. Ball shown in central zone. Arrows illustrate each combination: Combination 1 (third man) shows A-to-B-to-C with A making a curved run. Combination 2 (overlap) shows inside pass then wide run. Combination 3 (double switch) shows 3 passes across the width. Defenders shown in Phase 2 positions.

title: First Touch Under Pressure
objective: Develop the ability to take a quality first touch while a defender closes from behind.
learningOutcomes: Players can control a pass with a positive first touch while aware of pressure
Players use the first touch to move the ball into space rather than killing it dead
Players develop the body position needed to sense pressure without seeing it
Players build composure when receiving with a defender close
equipment: 1 ball per group, cones for 12x8m zone
organisation: Player A (server) stands 12m from Player B (receiver). Player C (defender) stands 2m behind Player B. A serves to B. B must control and pass back to A before C can reach the ball. Rotate roles every 4 minutes.
instructions: Player A passes firmly to Player B at a comfortable height.
Before the ball arrives, Player B checks over their shoulder to sense where Player C is.
Player B steps forward to meet the ball (do not wait for it).
First touch: Player B controls the ball with a touch that moves it AWAY from Player C.
Second touch: Player B immediately passes back to Player A.
Player C can move toward the ball the moment Player A passes. They cannot tackle before Player B touches it.
PHASE 1 (5 min): C walks toward B after the pass. B has plenty of time. Focus on touch direction.
PHASE 2 (5 min): C jogs after the pass. B must be quicker with touch and pass.
PHASE 3 (5 min): C sprints after the pass. B must have the ball away in 2 touches before C arrives.
Count successful completions (B passes back to A before C touches ball): target 8 from 10.
coachingPoints: MOVE TO MEET THE BALL: always step forward 1-2 steps to receive. Never let the ball come to you when a defender is behind.
CHECK BEFORE IT ARRIVES: a quick shoulder check as the ball is in the air tells you where the defender is. This informs your first touch direction.
FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION: the touch must go AWAY from the defender. If C is behind you, the touch goes forward or to the side. Never directly backward.
BODY AS A SHIELD: use your body naturally between the ball and the defender. The ball is in front, the defender is behind. Do not turn into the defender.
CUSHION TOUCH: a soft cushioned first touch that keeps the ball moving is better than a hard stop. The ball stays alive and playable.
POSITIVE TOUCH: the first touch should be forward or sideways into space. A touch that stays under your foot is a touch that gets tackled.
SPEED OF THOUGHT: decide what the first touch will do BEFORE the ball arrives. Deciding after is too slow when a defender is closing.
RELAX UNDER PRESSURE: tension makes touches heavier and less accurate. Soft hands and soft feet make better touches.
QUICK SECOND TOUCH: the second touch (the pass) must follow immediately after the first. No pause. Touch, pass, done.
COMMUNICATE: call for the ball from A so they know you are ready. Do not receive passively.
commonMistakes: Waiting for the ball to come to them instead of stepping forward to meet it
First touch going back toward the defender instead of away
Two separate actions (stop then pass) instead of one fluid receive-and-pass movement
No shoulder check before the ball arrives so the touch direction is random
Tensing up when the defender is close causing a heavy miscontrol
Ball going too far on the first touch making the second touch difficult
corrections: Not stepping forward: place a cone 2m in front of B. They must step past the cone to receive
Touch toward defender: mark the ground with arrows showing where the first touch must go (forward or sideways, never back)
Two separate actions: count 1-2 aloud with each player. Touch on 1, pass on 2. No gap between
No shoulder check: make it a rule - no shoulder check means the rep starts again
Tension: have players shake their arms before each rep to reset relaxation
Heavy first touch: reduce the speed of As serve so there is more time to develop the soft-touch habit
progressions: Reduce to 1 touch: receive and pass in one motion with no controlling touch at all
C is now an active defender who can tackle immediately (no grace period)
Server varies height: sometimes ground pass, sometimes knee-height, sometimes chest-high
Receive and turn: instead of passing back to A, B must turn and pass to a third player 8m to the side (adds a turning element)
regressions: C walks throughout the full session (no jogging or sprinting phase)
Serve the ball more slowly so B has more time
Remove C entirely for the first session to establish the touch direction habit without pressure
matchApplication: First touch under pressure is the most important technical skill for any player who receives in congested areas. Central midfielders, strikers, and full-backs constantly receive passes with defenders immediately closing. The quality of the first touch determines everything: a positive touch into space creates a playing opportunity, a poor touch means a tackle, a lost ball, and a potential chance for the opposition. Professional players receive 50-100 passes per training session specifically because the first touch must be completely automatic to be effective under match pressure.
coachTips: Walk beside each B player and observe the shoulder check habit. This is the most important technical point in the drill.
The transition from Phase 1 to Phase 3 (walking to sprinting defender) should not be rushed. Each phase needs at least 5 minutes.
Count successful completions out loud for the group. Building toward the 8 from 10 target creates positive pressure.
Connect to a match situation: describe receiving a pass with a pressing midfielder 2m behind you. What do you do? This drill is the answer.
At U11 this concept may take 2-3 sessions to establish. First touch direction is a sophisticated idea. Be patient.
diagramDescription: 12x8m zone. Player A (server circle) at one end, 12m from Player B (receiver square) who stands in the middle. Player C (defender triangle) stands 2m directly behind Player B. Arrow shows pass from A to B. Shoulder-check symbol shown at B (small arc arrow). First touch arrow shows ball moving forward/sideways (away from C). Second touch arrow shows immediate pass back to A. C shown with movement arrow toward B starting from the moment A passes.

title: First Touch and Turn - Advanced
objective: Develop elite-level first-touch technique for receiving under tight marking and immediately turning to face forward.
learningOutcomes: Players can receive under close marking and immediately turn to face forward in one movement
Players can select the correct first touch based on the defenders position
Players develop the composure to receive in the most pressured areas of the pitch
Players build the technical foundation for playing as a number 10 or advanced midfielder
equipment: 2 balls per group, cones for 20x15m zone, bibs
organisation: Player A servers from 15m. Player B receives with back to A, facing Player C (target) 10m ahead. Player D is an active marker standing 1m behind B. B must receive from A, turn past D, and pass to C. Rotate every 4 minutes.
instructions: Player B stands with back to A, hips at 45 degrees so they can see D and sense where C is.
Player B makes a setup movement: one step toward A (away from D), then checks back sharply to receive.
As A plays the ball, B checks over their opposite shoulder to confirm Ds position.
OPTION 1 - INSIDE HOOK TURN: if D is behind and to the right, B lets the ball run across the body and hooks it with the inside of the left foot, turning left away from D.
OPTION 2 - OUTSIDE CHOP TURN: if D is directly behind, B chops the ball with the outside of the right foot, spinning right and accelerating away.
OPTION 3 - LAY-OFF: if D is too tight (within 0.5m), B plays the ball back to A first-time and immediately moves to receive again from a different angle.
PHASE 1 (6 min): D is passive (holds position). B chooses any turn freely.
PHASE 2 (8 min): D is semi-active (can step to one side to cut off a turn). B must read Ds position and choose the correct turn.
PHASE 3 (6 min): D is live. Can tackle after B touches the ball. B earns a point for each successful turn and pass to C.
Count: B earns 1 point per turn, D earns 2 points per tackle. First to 10 wins the rotation.
coachingPoints: SETUP MOVEMENT: the step away before receiving is critical. It creates the half-metre of space between B and D that makes the turn possible. Without it D is too tight to turn past.
HALF-TURN BODY SHAPE: hips at 45 degrees allows B to see the incoming ball AND sense Ds position. Chest-on receiving eliminates all tactical awareness.
SHOULDER CHECK TIMING: check over the shoulder as the ball is in the air, not after it arrives. The information must be processed before the touch.
THE DECISION IS BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: B must decide which turn to use the moment the shoulder check is complete. Deciding after the ball arrives is too slow at this level.
INSIDE HOOK TECHNIQUE: let the ball travel slightly across the body. The inside of the far foot redirects it around the body. This is a flowing, curved movement.
OUTSIDE CHOP TECHNIQUE: contact the ball with the outside of the foot, pulling it behind the standing leg in one sharp movement. Requires ankle flexibility.
ACCELERATION AFTER THE TURN: the first 2-3 steps after the turn must be at sprint pace. The turn created the advantage - use it immediately.
SHIELDING DURING THE TURN: use the body to maintain a barrier between the ball and D during the turning movement. The body is always between ball and defender.
THE LAY-OFF DECISION: if D is within 0.5m, the turn is impossible at this level. Lay the ball off and immediately reposition. This is a sign of maturity not weakness.
FIRST TOUCH QUALITY: the turn is only possible if the first touch puts the ball in exactly the right position. A poor first touch makes any turn impossible.
DISGUISE: look one way before turning the other. B can look right and turn left. This buys the half-second needed.
COMPOSURE: receiving under close marking is the most pressured technical situation in football. Slow the mind, speed up the feet.
commonMistakes: Receiving chest-on which eliminates the ability to see the defender and limits turn options
Not making the setup movement before receiving, allowing the defender to be too tight
Deciding which turn to use after the ball arrives rather than from the shoulder check
First touch putting the ball in the wrong position making the turn impossible
Attempting to turn when the defender is too tight (within 0.5m) rather than laying off
Not accelerating after the turn allowing the defender to recover
corrections: Chest-on receiving: place a cone to the side of B at 45 degrees. B must always be able to see it when receiving
No setup movement: D starts 0.5m from B. B must create space with the step before receiving
Late decision: instruct B to point to which direction they will turn before A passes
Poor first touch: reduce As serve speed so B has more time to develop the correct touch position
Turning when too tight: add a contact rule - if D is within touching distance, B must lay off
No acceleration: place a gate cone 4m past the turn position. B must reach it before D
progressions: Blind turn: B does not check their shoulder before receiving and must read Ds position from feel alone (highly advanced)
Two defenders: D is joined by a second defender 3m ahead. B must turn past D and immediately face E
Receive, turn, and shoot: instead of passing to C, B turns and shoots at a mini-goal beyond C
Combination: after B turns and passes to C, C plays back to B who has continued the run (third-man combination)
regressions: D is completely passive for the first 6 minutes (just stands there) before introducing movement
Reduce to one turn type only (inside hook) until it is established before adding the outside chop
A serves from 8m instead of 15m giving B more time to see the ball and prepare
matchApplication: Receiving under close marking and immediately turning is the primary technical skill of a number 10 or advanced midfielder. Players like Kevin De Bruyne, Martin Odegaard, and Jude Bellingham make this movement 20-30 times per match. The ability to receive in tight spaces and instantly face forward transforms a player from a possession-keeper into a penetrating threat. At U16, players are ready for the full complexity of this technique including the decision-making element between turns and lay-offs. Mastery of this drill separates technically excellent players from elite ones.
coachTips: This drill requires pre-teaching of the individual turn techniques (inside hook and outside chop) before attempting the full decision-making sequence.
The shoulder check habit is the most important element. It cannot be shortcut. Every rep must include the shoulder check.
Film B from behind the server. This angle shows the body shape, shoulder check, and turn direction most clearly.
Connect this specifically to the players position on the pitch. For a striker, receiving back to goal and turning is the defining skill of their role.
At U16, players can discuss tactics intelligently. Explain the tactical purpose of each turn option and when each is appropriate.
diagramDescription: 20x15m zone. Player A (server) at far end 15m from B. Player B (receiver) in centre facing away from A. Player C (target) 10m ahead of B. Player D (defender) 1m behind B. Three turn diagrams shown: Option 1 (inside hook - curved arrow around left), Option 2 (outside chop - sharp turn right), Option 3 (lay-off arrow back to A with B repositioning). D shown with coverage arc showing which turns are available based on their position.

title: Dribble and Feint Foundations
objective: Teach basic dribbling with direction changes and a simple body feint to change direction.
learningOutcomes: Players can dribble with the ball under control at walking and jogging pace
Players can change direction using the inside and outside of the foot
Players can use a basic shoulder dip to change direction
Players develop comfort carrying the ball across an open area
equipment: 1 ball per player, 12 cones for the dribbling area
organisation: Scatter 12 cones randomly across a 20x20m area. Each player has a ball. Players dribble freely around the area, using the cones as obstacles to dribble around. Coach stands at the edge.
instructions: Players dribble freely around the area at their own pace. No collisions with other players or cones.
DIRECTION CHANGE PRACTICE: when approaching a cone, players use the INSIDE of the foot to tap the ball in a new direction.
After 2 minutes: when approaching a cone, players use the OUTSIDE of the foot to push the ball in a new direction.
BODY FEINT: when approaching a cone as if going left, dip the left shoulder hard left, shift body weight left, then push ball right with the outside of the right foot and go right.
Practice the body feint at walking pace first: step 1 - dip shoulder left. Step 2 - push ball right. Step 3 - accelerate right.
After 3 minutes of practice: players dribble around the area and use the body feint at EVERY cone they approach.
CHALLENGE: can you reach all 12 cones in 2 minutes using a direction change or body feint at each one?
Final 2 minutes: free dribbling with no instructions. Players use whatever they have learned naturally.
Coach calls FREEZE randomly and every player must stop the ball immediately under the sole of their foot.
coachingPoints: KEEP THE BALL CLOSE: take small touches so the ball stays within 1-2 steps at all times. Big touches lose the ball.
USE BOTH FEET: try to use the left foot as much as the right. Most players naturally use only their stronger foot.
HEAD UP: look where you are going, not at the ball. Feel the ball with your feet.
SMALL TOUCHES IN TIGHT SPACES: when there are other players nearby, take smaller touches. When there is space, take bigger touches.
BODY FEINT COMMITMENT: the shoulder dip must be big and convincing. A small dip does not fool anyone.
AFTER THE FEINT GO FAST: after the shoulder dip and push, take 2 quick steps away. Do not do the feint and then walk.
INSIDE OF FOOT FOR DIRECTION: when turning around a cone, the inside of the foot gives the most control.
OUTSIDE OF FOOT FOR SPEED: when pushing the ball into open space, the outside of the foot is quicker.
ENJOY IT: dribbling is the most fun part of football. Be creative and try things.
FREEZE CHALLENGE: when the coach calls freeze, get the ball stopped instantly. This builds ball control awareness.
commonMistakes: Big heavy touches that send the ball too far away
Using only the dominant foot and neglecting the weaker foot
Looking at the ball instead of where they are going
Body feint with no commitment - too small a shoulder dip
Not accelerating after the body feint
Colliding with other players because head is down
corrections: Heavy touches: play in a smaller space (15x15m) which forces softer touches
One foot only: put a ribbon on the weak ankle as a reminder to use it
Looking down: call out the colour of a cone 5m ahead. Player must answer without looking down at the ball
Weak feint: exaggerate the demonstration - dip the shoulder so far it is almost theatrical. U9 players need an obvious model
No acceleration: place a cone 3m after each feint point. Player must reach it in 2 steps
Collisions: reduce the number of players in the space at one time. Start with 8 players then increase
progressions: Add a chasing game: one player without a ball tries to kick other players balls out. Everyone with a ball protects it while dribbling
Colour cones: each colour requires a different direction change (red = inside foot, blue = outside foot, yellow = body feint)
Partner mirror: two players mirror each other - one leads with dribbling moves, the other copies
Dribbling race: straight 15m dribble. Who can dribble the fastest while keeping the ball under control
regressions: Walk pace only for the first 3 minutes before adding jogging pace
Remove the body feint and focus only on inside and outside direction changes
Use a larger ball (size 4) which is easier to control for smaller players
matchApplication: Dribbling is the first individual skill in football. Every player needs to be comfortable carrying the ball and changing direction under control. The body feint introduced in this drill is the foundation of all 1v1 dribbling moves used at senior level. Building comfort and confidence with the ball at U9 is the most important thing a coach can do for long-term player development. Players who are comfortable dribbling at U9 are more willing to take risks and be creative at U14 and above.
coachTips: Keep this drill fun and free. U9 players learn best through play and exploration rather than rigid instruction.
The FREEZE command keeps players alert and practicing ball control without realising it.
Walk among the players and give individual encouragement. Spot one good thing each player is doing and tell them specifically.
Do not correct too many things at once. Pick the most important issue (usually big heavy touches or looking down) and focus on that.
Let players be creative. If a player invents their own move at this age, celebrate it rather than correcting it.
diagramDescription: 20x20m area with 12 cones scattered randomly. Players (circles with balls) shown moving between cones with curved dribbling arrows. Body feint diagram shown separately: shoulder dip arrow going left, ball push arrow going right, acceleration arrow going right. FREEZE symbol shown with all players stopped with sole on ball.

title: Turning and Dribbling Combinations
objective: Develop the ability to combine a receiving turn with immediate dribbling to beat a defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can receive, turn, and immediately dribble at pace
Players can combine the Cruyff turn with a dribbling run
Players understand how turning creates space for a dribbling attack
Players develop the physical coordination for quick footwork sequences
equipment: 1 ball per group, cones for 20x10m channel
organisation: 20x10m channel per group of 3. Player A servers from one end. Player B receives in the middle. Player C is a passive defender 2m behind B. A mini-goal at the far end from A. B receives, turns past C, and dribbles to finish.
instructions: Player A plays a pass to Player B in the middle of the channel.
Player B receives with back to the mini-goal, facing A.
Player C stands 2m behind B (between B and the mini-goal).
B uses a CRUYFF TURN: shape to pass back to A, drag the ball behind the standing leg with the inside of the kicking foot, spin and face the mini-goal.
After the Cruyff turn B has turned 180 degrees and now faces the mini-goal with C behind them.
B takes 3-4 dribbling touches at pace and finishes into the mini-goal.
C can begin moving toward the goal the moment B touches the ball to turn.
PHASE 1 (5 min): walk through the Cruyff turn without a defender. Just the movement.
PHASE 2 (5 min): passive C (holds position). B practices the turn and dribbling run.
PHASE 3 (5 min): C moves toward goal after B touches. B must dribble quickly after the turn.
Count goals scored from successful Cruyff turns: target 6 per player per rotation.
coachingPoints: CRUYFF TURN SETUP: shape the body as if about to pass back to A. This is what fools the defender.
THE DRAG: the inside of the kicking foot contacts the ball and DRAGS it behind the standing leg. Not a push - a drag.
SPIN: as the ball goes behind the standing leg, the whole body spins 180 degrees to face the new direction.
ACCELERATE IMMEDIATELY: the moment the ball is dragged behind the standing leg, the body must already be accelerating in the new direction.
CLOSE CONTROL AFTER THE TURN: the first 2 dribbling touches after the turn must be small and controlled. The turn happened in a tight space.
BUILD TO SPEED: touch 1 and 2 after the turn are small. Touch 3 onward can be bigger as space opens up.
USE THE BODY AS A SHIELD: during the dribbling phase, keep the body between the ball and the recovering defender.
FINISHING TOUCH: the final touch before finishing should position the ball for a clean strike. Not too close and not too far.
SELL THE FAKE: the shape-to-pass movement must be convincing. A half-hearted fake is easy to read.
EYES UP AFTER THE TURN: once turned, look up immediately to see the goal and any remaining defenders.
commonMistakes: Turning without the setup fake (no shape-to-pass movement)
Pushing the ball sideways instead of dragging it behind the standing leg
Not accelerating immediately after the turn
First touch after the turn too heavy, sending the ball away
Not shielding the ball during the dribbling phase when the defender recovers
Finishing too early (too far out) or too late (too close to goal)
corrections: No fake: physically show B how to shape the body toward A before the drag. Practice the shape without the ball first
Pushing not dragging: put the coaching emphasis on pulling the ankle ACROSS the body. The motion is inward not outward
No acceleration: after the turn B must touch a cone 2m ahead within 2 steps
Heavy touch: play in a narrower channel (6m wide) which forces tighter control after the turn
No shielding: remove the ball and practice the shielding body position (arm out, low centre of gravity) separately
Finishing: mark a shooting zone 6-8m from goal. The finish must come from inside the zone
progressions: C actively tries to tackle from the moment B receives (not just from when B touches to turn)
Add a second defender 5m ahead of the mini-goal: B must beat 2 defenders with the turn and dribble
Different turn: alternate between Cruyff turn and inside hook turn on alternate reps
Combination: B receives, turns, dribbles, then plays to a wide player (A who has moved wide) for a cross and finish
regressions: Remove C entirely in phase 1 and 2. Just practice the turn and dribble pattern
Slow pace throughout with a static ball for the first 5 minutes
Wider channel (15m) gives more space to dribble after the turn
matchApplication: Receiving and turning is one of the most match-decisive combinations in football. When a striker or number 10 receives a pass with their back to goal and immediately turns past the defender, they create a goal-scoring opportunity from what initially looked like a defensive situation. This combination is used by elite number 9s and 10s in every match. The Cruyff turn in particular is one of the most effective and famous moves in football history precisely because it combines deception with immediate acceleration in a very small space.
coachTips: Teach the Cruyff turn technique in isolation (no pass, no defender) at the beginning of the session before integrating it.
Show a video of Johan Cruyff performing the original turn, or a modern player like De Bruyne using it.
The quality of the setup fake is the most important coaching point. Spend extra time on this.
Walk with the players through the turn in slow motion to build the muscle memory before adding speed.
The finishing element adds excitement and makes the drill feel like a match situation. Keep the mini-goals small to make finishing challenging.
diagramDescription: 20x10m channel. Player A (server) at far end. Player B (receiver) in centre facing A. Player C (defender) 2m behind B. Mini-goal at the end near C. Arrow from A to B shows the serve. Cruyff turn diagram shows: fake pass shape (arrow toward A), drag behind standing leg (curved inward arrow), spin and face goal (180-degree rotation arrow). Dribbling arrows show B accelerating toward mini-goal. C shown with chase arrow behind B.

title: Explosive 1v1 Dribbling
objective: Develop the ability to dribble at maximum speed in a 1v1 situation using body position and pace to beat a defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can dribble at sprint speed while maintaining close control
Players understand how to use body position to protect the ball in a 1v1
Players develop the explosive acceleration needed to go past a defender
Players build confidence in 1v1 dribbling situations
equipment: 1 ball per pair, cones for 20x8m channels, bibs
organisation: 20x8m channels, one per pair. Attacker starts at one end with ball. Defender starts 5m ahead (not at the midpoint). Mini-goal (2m wide) at the far end. The 5m head-start gives the attacker an initial advantage that the drill is built around exploiting.
instructions: Attacker starts dribbling as soon as coach blows the whistle.
Defender can start moving toward the attacker the moment the attacker moves.
Attacker must use the initial 5m advantage to set pace and body position before the defender closes.
KEY CONCEPT: the attacker should already be at dribbling pace when they reach the defender. Not accelerating into the defender.
When the defender is within 2m: attacker drops their shoulder hard in one direction and pushes ball the other way with the outside of the foot.
After the push: 3 maximum-pace steps away to create separation before the defender recovers.
Attacker scores by touching the ball over the far end line. Defender scores by kicking the ball out of the channel.
PHASE 1 (6 min): defender can only side-step (no sprinting). Attacker practices the approach and shoulder drop.
PHASE 2 (6 min): defender is fully active. Full 1v1.
PHASE 3 (6 min): attacker gets only 2m head start instead of 5m. Much harder.
Track wins and losses. Every player needs to win AND lose. Both develop better players.
coachingPoints: APPROACH SPEED: reach the defender at pace. Slowing down before the move signals intent and gives the defender time to set.
BODY POSITION: low centre of gravity (slightly bent knees) makes you harder to knock off the ball and quicker to change direction.
THE SHOULDER DROP: must be big and convincing. Dip the shoulder hard enough that the defender shifts their weight.
PUSH DIRECTION: push the ball with the OUTSIDE of the foot away from the dropped shoulder. The ball goes the OPPOSITE way to the shoulder.
FIRST STEP: the first step after the push must be explosive. Think of it as a sprint start from a standing position.
PROTECT THE BALL: after going past, get the body between the ball and the recovering defender. Do not let them get goalside.
USE THE SPACE: after beating the defender in the channel, drive hard toward the mini-goal. Do not slow down.
VARIATION: if the shoulder drop does not work, try the opposite shoulder drop on the next rep. Build a repertoire.
RECOVERY: if the ball is lost, immediately sprint back to defend. The transition from attack to defence must be instant.
1V1 MINDSET: back yourself. Players who hesitate in 1v1 always lose. Commit to the move and go.
commonMistakes: Slowing down before the move which allows the defender to get set
Shoulder drop too small to convince the defender to shift weight
Push with the inside of the foot (slower) rather than the outside
No explosive first step after the push - walking pace rather than sprinting
Not protecting the ball after going past the defender
Stopping to celebrate after beating the defender instead of finishing
corrections: Slowing down: mark a speed cone 3m before the defender. Attacker must be at full pace when they reach the speed cone
Small drop: exaggerate the demonstration. Show a drop so big it almost looks comical. U13 players respond to this
Inside foot push: mark the outside of the boot with a sticker - only the sticker can contact the ball on the push
No explosive step: after the push the attacker must tag a cone 3m away before the defender recovers
Not protecting: immediately after going past, coach calls PROTECT and attacker must get body between ball and defender
Not finishing: make the rule that unless the ball crosses the far line the rep is not complete
progressions: Remove the channel width restriction: open space 1v1 across half the pitch (much harder)
Add a second chance: if attacker beats defender, a second defender starts from the far end line
Dribble then cross: instead of a mini-goal, attacker must dribble past and then cross to a finishing player
Points system: beating the defender 1pt, scoring 2pt, defender tackle 2pt. Track across the full session
regressions: Increase head-start to 8m to give attacker more time and confidence
Defender is passive (side-step only) for the full session
Wider channel (12m) gives more room to manoeuvre
matchApplication: 1v1 dribbling is the highest-impact individual skill in football. A player who can consistently beat a defender creates goal-scoring opportunities that cannot be created any other way. Wingers who beat full-backs, strikers who beat centre-backs - these moments change matches. At U13 players are entering the physical and cognitive phase where 1v1 skills can be developed to a high level. The explosive pace and body position elements in this drill are the same techniques used by professional wingers and will transfer directly to match performance.
coachTips: Keep the pairs well-matched physically where possible. A big size mismatch makes the drill one-sided and less developmental for both players.
Both attacker and defender roles are important. Spend equal time coaching defensive technique (jockeying) alongside attacking technique.
The competition element is essential at U13. Track results and create a leaderboard. Competitive players respond to rankings.
Film the shoulder drop moment in slow motion and show players. The technique is hard to self-correct without visual feedback.
After Phase 2, ask the attackers what worked and what did not. Encouraging self-analysis develops tactical intelligence.
diagramDescription: 20x8m channel. Attacker (circle with ball) at one end. Defender (triangle) 5m ahead. Mini-goal at far end. Arrow shows attacker dribbling toward defender at pace. At the defender position: shoulder drop diagram (body angled one way, ball pushed other way with curved outside-foot arrow). Arrow continues past defender to mini-goal. Phase 3 shows reduced 2m head-start.

title: Senior Dribbling Under High Press
objective: Develop the ability to maintain ball control and dribble through a high press in realistic match conditions.
learningOutcomes: Players can retain possession when pressed aggressively from multiple angles
Players develop the footwork and body positioning needed to escape pressing traps
Players can identify and exploit space created by the press
Players build the physical and mental resilience to perform under maximum pressure
organisation: 30x25m area. 4 ball-carriers (attackers) start at corners. 4 pressers (defenders) start at midpoints of each side. 2 neutral players roam freely and offer support. Ball-carriers must maintain possession for 30 seconds against the press. Coach observes from outside.
instructions: Ball-carriers start with a ball each at the 4 corners.
Pressers start on the side midpoints and press as soon as the whistle blows.
Ball-carriers must: maintain possession, find a neutral support player, or combine with another ball-carrier to escape the press.
PRESSING TRAP: two pressers work together to funnel a ball-carrier into a corner. Ball-carrier must escape using dribbling, a drag-back, or a quick combination.
When a presser wins the ball they become a ball-carrier. The player who lost the ball becomes a presser.
PHASE 1 (8 min): 4v2 (4 ball-carriers, 2 pressers, 2 neutrals). Easier press.
PHASE 2 (10 min): 4v4 (4 ball-carriers, 4 pressers, 2 neutrals). Full press.
PHASE 3 (7 min): 4v6 (4 ball-carriers vs 6 pressers, no neutrals). Extreme press. Tests maximum dribbling skill.
Count: how long can each ball-carrier retain possession? Target 20 seconds in Phase 2.
coachingPoints: SCANNING: scan the area every 2 touches. Know where the pressers are before they arrive. Do not wait for the press to materialise.
LOW CENTRE OF GRAVITY: bend the knees more under pressure. Lower centre of gravity makes you harder to knock off the ball and quicker to change direction.
SMALL TOUCHES WHEN PRESSED: when a presser is within 2m, take very small touches to maintain control. Big touches are easy to intercept.
SHIELDING: use the body to keep the presser on one side. Arm slightly out, body between ball and presser at all times.
DRAG-BACK TO ESCAPE: when pressed from the front and side simultaneously, a drag-back creates immediate space behind you.
FIND THE NEUTRAL: the neutral players are always free. Get the ball to them before the press is complete.
EXPLOIT OVER-COMMITMENT: when a presser commits (lunges), push the ball past them immediately and accelerate.
COMBINATION TO ESCAPE: when a fellow ball-carrier is nearby and free, a quick 1-2 combination immediately beats the press.
STAY CALM: the most common error under a high press is rushing. Take one extra touch if needed. Panic leads to poor decisions.
BODY FEINT BEFORE PASSING: feinting before playing the ball to a neutral draws the presser the wrong way and creates more time.
TURN TO FACE FORWARD: whenever possible after escaping the press, turn to face forward. Facing backward under pressure eventually leads to a mistake.
PRESS AWARENESS: understand HOW the press is organised. If 2 pressers are funnelling you left, go right. If they are cutting off the short pass, play long.
commonMistakes: Panicking when pressed and playing the ball to a presser in a moment of stress
Standing upright with legs straight making them easy to knock off the ball
Taking big touches under pressure giving the presser easy interceptions
Not scanning before the press arrives meaning they are always reacting rather than anticipating
Not using the neutral players who are always available
Not accelerating away after escaping the press - slowing down allows re-pressing immediately
corrections: Panicking: count 1-2 aloud when any ball-carrier receives. They must not play the ball before the count of 1
Standing upright: show the athletic position (bent knees, low hips, weight forward) and ask players to hold it throughout
Big touches: reduce the area to 20x20m which forces smaller touches
No scanning: every ball-carrier must call out how many pressers are within 5m of them every 5 seconds
Not finding neutral: give neutrals a visual signal (arm raised) to make them more obvious
No acceleration: place gate cones 5m from the press area. Ball-carriers must dribble through the gate after escaping
progressions: No neutrals: pure 4v4 press. All escape must come from dribbling and combination play alone
Random ball introduction: coach throws a new ball into the area randomly. Ball-carriers must win it before pressers
High-press with counter: when the press wins the ball they immediately attack a mini-goal 15m away (simulates the transition)
Full 11v11 press game: one team presses from the front, other team must play out under the press from their own half
regressions: Reduce to 4v1 press (only one presser) to build confidence before adding more
Give ball-carriers 3 touch maximum (can dribble freely within the 3 touches)
Reduce area to 20x15m with only 2 pressers to allow more time and space
matchApplication: Playing through or against a high press is the defining tactical challenge in modern football. Every elite team presses and every elite team must be able to play through pressure. At senior level, the ability to maintain possession under a coordinated press requires both the individual technical skills (dribbling, shielding, drag-backs) and the collective intelligence to find free players and combination solutions. Players who can perform under maximum pressing pressure are the most valuable members of any senior squad. This drill develops exactly those skills in a realistic, high-intensity environment.
coachTips: Senior players need maximum intensity. Do not allow jogging defenders or half-hearted pressing. The drill is useless at low intensity.
The neutral players are a critical element. Brief them separately before the drill to understand their role: always show, always be available, always play first-time.
Phase 3 (4v6) is deliberately unfair. The purpose is to push ball-carriers beyond their comfort zone technically.
Debrief after each phase: what worked? What got the ball lost most often? Let players identify solutions before you provide them.
Connect to the matchday context: which teams will they face that press heavily? How does this drill prepare them specifically?
diagramDescription: 30x25m area. 4 ball-carriers (circles) at corners with balls. 4 pressers (triangles) at side midpoints. 2 neutrals (diamonds) shown in open spaces. Arrows show pressers closing toward nearest ball-carrier. Shielding diagram shows low body position with arm out. Escape arrows show drag-back away from double press. Neutral position shown with available passing arrow. Phase progression shown: P1 (4v2), P2 (4v4), P3 (4v6).

title: Third Man Combination
objective: Develop the third man run concept where a third player receives after a two-player combination frees them from a defender.
learningOutcomes: Players understand how a two-player combination creates space for a third player
Players can time a third man run to arrive as the second pass is played
Players develop the habit of looking for the third man option before passing
Players build combination play instincts in groups of three
players: Groups of 3 plus 1 passive defender (9-16 total)
organisation: Three players (A, B, C) in a 20x15m zone. One passive defender marks Player C. Player A starts with the ball. The sequence: A plays to B, B lays off back to A, A plays in behind the defender to C who has timed a run. Rotate roles every 5 minutes.
instructions: Player A stands at one end with the ball. Player B stands 8m ahead. Player C stands 15m ahead with a passive defender marking them.
Player A passes to Player B.
Player B takes one touch to control and immediately lays the ball back to Player A.
As soon as Player B receives the ball, Player C begins their run away from the defender.
Player A, receiving the lay-off from B, immediately plays the ball into the space behind the defender for Player C.
Player C receives on the run and controls the ball at the far end of the zone.
The key timing: C starts the run when B touches the ball, not before.
After 5 minutes rotate: B becomes the third man runner, C becomes the lay-off player, A remains the initiator.
After 10 minutes add a semi-active defender on C who can now track the run.
Final phase: all three roles rotate freely and players decide when to trigger the combination.
coachingPoints: THIRD MAN TIMING: the run must start when the lay-off pass is being made, not before. Too early and the defender tracks you. Too late and the pass cannot be played.
LAY-OFF QUALITY: the lay-off from B must be firm, accurate, and into the path of A who is already moving forward. A soft lay-off kills the tempo.
A MUST MOVE: Player A should take one step forward as they play to B, so they are already in motion to receive the lay-off and play forward.
C RUNS BEHIND: the third man run goes BEHIND the defender, into the space on the far side. Not beside the defender.
THE PASS TO C: played into the space ahead of C, not to their feet. Lead them into the run.
BODY SHAPE OF B: when B receives from A, their first touch should open their hips to see both A (behind) and C (ahead) simultaneously.
COMMUNICATION: C calls GO or NOW at the moment they begin their run so B and A know the combination is triggered.
DECEPTION: C should make a small movement toward the ball before making the run away. This freezes the defender for a split second.
SPEED OF COMBINATION: the A-to-B-to-A sequence must be completed in under 3 seconds. Slow combinations give the defender time to recover.
EYES BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: A must see where C is running before the lay-off arrives at their feet.
commonMistakes: C starting the run too early, allowing the defender to track them easily
Lay-off from B going sideways instead of back to A who is moving forward
A stopping to wait for the lay-off instead of moving into the path of it
Pass to C going to their feet instead of into the space ahead of their run
The combination being too slow, allowing the defender to reset and cover C
No communication between players about when the run is triggered
corrections: C early run: place a cone at Cs starting position. C cannot leave the cone until B makes contact with the ball
Sideways lay-off: draw an arrow on the ground showing the lay-off direction back toward A
A stopping: place a cone 3m ahead of A. They must be past that cone when the lay-off arrives
Pass to feet: place a target cone 3m behind the defender. Pass must reach or pass that cone
Slow combination: count aloud: 1 (A to B), 2 (lay-off), 3 (A to C). Must be done in 3 counts
No communication: make the call mandatory - no call means the combination does not start
progressions: Active defender: defender on C can now track and attempt to intercept the final pass
Add a finishing element: C receives and must finish into a mini-goal at the far end
Double third man: after the first combination, C lays off to a fourth player who makes a second third man run
Compressed space: reduce the zone to 15x10m, forcing faster decision-making and tighter passing
regressions: No defender at all: pure technique practice with no pressure
Slow the combination to walking pace and narrate each step before adding pace
Reduce to static C: C does not run, just receives to establish the passing pattern first
matchApplication: The third man combination is one of the most effective ways to break through a compact defensive block. When two players combine quickly, defenders are drawn to the ball. This creates the moment when a third player, arriving late into space, receives in a dangerous position with time and room. This exact pattern is used repeatedly by elite clubs in the final third: a winger and striker combine, drawing the full-back, and the overlapping midfielder arrives as the third man. Teaching it at U12 builds the instinctive understanding needed at senior level.
coachTips: The concept of the third man is abstract for U12 players. Walk through it at slow motion before any live practice.
Use the phrase: two players combine to FREE the third. This framing helps players understand why they are doing it.
The most common error is C running too early. Focus all early coaching on C timing rather than the passing quality.
Once the pattern is understood, reduce your coaching and let the group find the rhythm themselves.
Connect to a match situation: show a video clip of a professional third man goal if possible.
diagramDescription: 20x15m zone. Player A at one end with ball. Player B 8m ahead in the middle. Player C 15m ahead with a passive defender (triangle) marking them. Arrow 1: A passes to B. Arrow 2: B lays off back to A. Arrow 3: C makes a run behind the defender (curved dotted arrow). Arrow 4: A plays the ball into space ahead of C. Defender shown tracking but arriving too late.

title: Driven Pass Under Pressure
objective: Develop the ability to play accurate driven passes under defensive pressure at match speed.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a firm driven pass accurately over 20-30m under pressure
Players develop composure when receiving with a defender closing from behind
Players learn to use body position to shield and create a passing angle simultaneously
Players build the habit of playing forward first under pressure before going sideways
organisation: 30x20m zone per group of 4. Player A starts at one end with ball. Player B receives in the centre. A passive then active defender starts 2m behind B. Player C and D are target players at the far end, positioned wide left and wide right. B must receive, deal with the defender, and drive a pass to either C or D.
instructions: Player A plays a firm pass into Player B who is positioned in the centre of the zone.
As the pass travels to B, the defender (starting 2m behind B) begins closing at jogging pace.
B receives the ball and must use their body to feel where the defender is.
B takes a touch to set the ball and immediately plays a driven pass to either C or D.
The driven pass must be along the ground, arrive at C or D within 2 steps of their position.
After the pass: B sprints to a wide position. C or D plays a new ball back to A who plays in again.
Phase 2 after 8 minutes: defender is fully active and can intercept or tackle before B passes.
Phase 3: B has a maximum of 2 touches. Receive and drive in 2 touches.
Rotate roles every 5 minutes.
coachingPoints: BODY POSITION ON RECEIVE: receive the ball slightly side-on so you can feel the defender with your back and see the target players in the same glance.
FEEL THE DEFENDER: before the ball arrives, reach a hand back slightly to sense where the defender is. This tells you how much time you have.
FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION: take the first touch away from the defender and toward your preferred passing foot. Do not take it directly under your body.
DRIVEN PASS TECHNIQUE: plant foot beside the ball, ankle locked, contact through the centre of the ball with the laces. Follow through toward the target.
DECISION BEFORE RECEIVING: as the pass is coming to you, already know which side (C or D) you are passing to. The defender position will tell you which side has more space.
WEIGHT OF DRIVEN PASS: firm enough to beat any interception attempt but not so hard that C or D cannot control it. Aim for the ball to arrive at chest pace.
PROTECT THE BALL: if the defender arrives very quickly, use the body to shield and buy half a second before the pass.
EYES UP: after the first touch, eyes go immediately to C and D to confirm which one is better positioned.
DO NOT HOLD THE BALL: a midfielder who holds the ball under pressure invites the tackle. Receive and release within 2 seconds.
COMMUNICATION FROM C AND D: they must call their name or signal their position as the ball travels to B.
commonMistakes: Taking the first touch directly under the body, making the driven pass awkward
Looking at the ball when driving the pass instead of at the target
Pass too light: arrives at C or D at walking pace and is easily intercepted
Holding the ball too long under pressure before passing
Not using the body to shield when the defender arrives very quickly
Choosing the wrong target: passing to the more defended side instead of the open side
corrections: First touch under body: mark a cone 50cm to the side of B. First touch must go past the cone
Looking at ball: ask B to call out which colour bib C or D is wearing as they strike the pass
Light pass: set a 3-second rule: pass must reach C or D in under 3 seconds from release
Holding ball: add a 2-touch maximum rule. Third touch = defender wins the rep
Wrong target: freeze after wrong decision and ask B to show where the defenders weight was
progressions: Two defenders: one closes from behind, one drifts across the passing lane, B must find the gap
1-touch drive: B must drive the pass first time with no controlling touch
Add a finish: C or D receives the driven pass and immediately shoots at a mini-goal
Blind receive: B faces away from A until the pass is played, then turns to receive (replicates receiving without knowing where the ball is coming from)
regressions: Passive defender only for the first 5 minutes
Reduce distance to 20m and allow 3 touches
Remove the defender completely and focus on driven pass technique only
matchApplication: Driving accurate passes under pressure is a fundamental midfield skill at U15 and above. In modern football, midfielders receive the ball with an opponent closing within 1-2 seconds on almost every touch. The ability to receive, assess, and drive a precise pass to a teammate under that pressure is what separates ball-playing midfielders from those who simply circulate the ball sideways. This drill directly replicates the central midfield scenario where a player receives with pressure from behind and must find a forward or wide player quickly.
coachTips: Stand at the defender position for the first few reps to show exactly what closing pressure feels like from Bs perspective.
The body position coaching point (side-on receive) is the most impactful technical correction in this drill. Spend extra time on it.
Time the A-to-B pass to B-to-C/D sequence. The best midfielders complete this in under 2 seconds total.
Use this drill as a warm-up for tactical sessions involving central midfield play.
At U15 players are ready to handle real pressure. Do not stay in the passive defender phase too long.
diagramDescription: 30x20m zone. Player A at bottom with ball. Player B in the centre. Defender (triangle) 2m behind B. Player C top left, Player D top right. Arrow 1: A drives pass to B. Arrow 2: Defender closes from behind (red arrow). Arrow 3: B takes one touch sideways (small arrow). Arrow 4: B drives pass to C or D (long arrow). Two target positions shown at top with cones.

title: Senior Positional Rondo 5v2
objective: Develop elite-level passing speed, positional sense, and decision-making under intense pressure in a 5v2 possession format.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain possession with 1-touch passing under sustained pressure
Players develop elite positional awareness and support angles
Players build the mental resilience to stay composed under defensive aggression
Players develop communication habits that sustain high-tempo possession
players: 7 per group (5 attackers, 2 defenders). Run multiple groups.
organisation: 8x8m square. 5 attackers positioned around and inside the square. 2 defenders inside. 1-touch rule throughout. Defenders swap when: they touch the ball, or 10 consecutive passes are completed by the attackers. If defenders win the ball they try to play 3 consecutive passes themselves before resetting.
instructions: 5 attackers start in and around the 8x8m square. 2 defenders inside.
Attackers keep the ball with 1-touch passing only. No second touches.
Defenders press both the ball and the next likely receiver simultaneously.
Count consecutive successful passes aloud. Reset count on any error.
Target: reach 10 consecutive passes. If reached, defenders swap.
If defenders win the ball: they attempt 3 consecutive passes. If successful they earn a point.
Phase 2 after 10 minutes: reduce square to 7x7m.
Phase 3: reduce to 6x6m. The tightest space demands the highest quality.
Run for 20 minutes total. Track which pair of defenders concedes the most 10-pass sequences.
coachingPoints: BODY SHAPE: always receive on the half-turn. If you receive chest-on to the ball in this space you have nowhere to go.
DECISION BEFORE RECEIVE: you must know your next pass before the ball arrives at you. At elite level this is non-negotiable.
SUPPORT ANGLES: never stand in a straight line with two other attackers. Always create a triangle. Straight lines are easy to press.
MOVEMENT: after every pass, move immediately to a new position. A player who stands still after passing offers no support angle.

THE SAFE PASS vs THE BRAVE PASS: the safe pass goes to the player with most space. The brave pass goes to the player in the tightest space but creates the best angle for the next pass. Elite players choose the brave pass more often.
PRESSURE MANAGEMENT: when a defender is very close to you, one touch is your only option. Do not try to control under that pressure.
VERBAL AND VISUAL COMMUNICATION: every player without the ball should be showing and calling simultaneously.
WEIGHT AND DIRECTION: every pass must arrive at a comfortable height and to the correct foot of the receiver. A bad pass in tight space is instantly lost.
TEMPO: the tempo must be high enough that the defenders cannot get set. The moment tempo drops, the defenders can organise.
COMPOSURE UNDER AGGRESSION: senior defenders press hard and physically. Stay technically composed regardless of the physical pressure.
commonMistakes: Receiving chest-on to the ball, making the next pass impossible in tight space
Standing still after passing instead of immediately creating a new angle
Playing the safe pass every time instead of the pass that creates the best position for the next action
Panicking under pressure and playing the ball into a defender
Poor pass weight: too hard or too soft, both cause problems in tight space
Not moving before the ball arrives to create a better receiving angle
corrections: Chest-on receive: place a cone behind each attacker. They must be able to see the cone when receiving (forces half-turn)
Static after pass: clap every time a player moves immediately after passing. Make movement the celebrated action
Always safe pass: award 2 points for a 10-pass sequence that includes at least 3 central passes (most dangerous)
Panic pass: slow the drill to 2-touch for 2 minutes to restore composure before returning to 1-touch
Poor weight: the player who plays the bad pass goes into the defending pair next
Not moving before receive: add a rule that attackers must take 1 step before every receive
progressions: 4v2: remove one attacker, making it harder to find the free player
Directional rondo: one side of the square is the target side. Attackers earn a bonus point for completing a pass along that side
Defenders can now use their body (shoulder): adds physicality to the defensive pressure
Time limit: attackers must complete 10 passes in under 30 seconds
regressions: Allow 2 touches for 5 minutes before returning to 1-touch
Increase square to 10x10m
Reduce to 5v1 to rebuild confidence before adding the second defender
matchApplication: The 5v2 rondo at senior level is the most widely used possession drill in professional football for a reason: it perfectly replicates the conditions of match possession. The decision-making speed, the positional support habits, and the composure under pressure developed here transfer directly into match performance. Teams that rondo daily develop a shared language of possession: they know each others movement patterns, they anticipate passes before they are played, and they maintain composure under the highest press. Every elite club from Barcelona to Liverpool uses this drill as a daily non-negotiable.
coachTips: The 5v2 rondo is best used as a pre-session warm-up at senior level. 15 minutes of rondo raises the technical standard of everything that follows.
Do not over-coach during the rondo. Observe, identify the key error, stop the group briefly, give one point, restart.
The competitive element (counting consecutive passes) is essential. Remove it and the tempo drops immediately.
Watch the moment BEFORE each pass: is the player already in motion to a new position? This is the most important movement habit to build.
Identify the two slowest decision-makers and pair them as defenders to give them maximum time under pressure.
diagramDescription: 8x8m square with 4 corner cones. 5 attackers (circles) positioned around the square with 2 inside and 3 on the edges. 2 defenders (triangles) inside pressing. Arrows show multiple passing options from the ball-carrier. Small curved arrows show attacker movement after passing. Defenders shown with pressure arrows toward the ball and the next likely receiver simultaneously.

title: Pressing Triggers and Press Traps
objective: Teach players to recognise pressing triggers and execute a coordinated press as a unit.
learningOutcomes: Players understand what a pressing trigger is and can identify 4 common triggers
Players can execute a coordinated press within 1 second of a trigger occurring
Players develop compactness during the press to prevent easy escape passes
Players learn what to do when the press is beaten
organisation: 40x30m zone. Ball-playing team of 5-6 try to keep possession. Pressing team of 5-6 try to win the ball using coordinated pressing. Coach stands at the side and calls TRIGGER at specific moments to initiate a press. Four triggers are established before the drill starts.
instructions: ESTABLISH THE 4 TRIGGERS before starting: (1) A back pass to the goalkeeper. (2) A heavy touch by any player. (3) A pass to a wide player facing their own goal. (4) The ball going into a specific zone (the trap zone, marked by cones).
Ball-playing team starts with possession and moves the ball freely in the zone.
Pressing team holds a mid-block shape (compact, organised) when no trigger has occurred.

When ANY trigger occurs: the nearest presser immediately closes the ball at maximum speed. The two nearest pressers cover the most likely escape passes. All other pressers compact inward to reduce space.
The press lasts maximum 5 seconds. If the ball is not won in 5 seconds: drop back to mid-block shape.
If the press wins the ball: pressing team plays 3 passes to earn a point before resetting.
Phase 2 after 10 minutes: ball-playing team is told the 4 triggers and tries to avoid them.
Phase 3: the trap zone trigger is used deliberately. Pressing team lures the ball into the trap zone then presses at maximum intensity.
coachingPoints: PRESSING TRIGGER DEFINITION: a trigger is a moment when the opponent is vulnerable. Vulnerability means: less time, less space, back to goal, or in a pre-set trap zone.
IMMEDIATE REACTION: when a trigger occurs, the nearest presser must move within 1 second. A 2-second delay means the trigger moment has passed and the press is pointless.
THE PRESS ANGLE: the first presser closes at an angle that cuts off the most dangerous pass. Do not run directly at the ball. Run to the side that blocks the forward pass.
COVER SHADOWS: the two covering pressers position themselves in the cover shadow of the ball-carrier. This means standing in the passing lane so that pass is blocked without moving.
COMPACTNESS: when the press is triggered, all players not involved in the immediate press must move 5m closer to the ball. The space around the ball must shrink.
PRESS COORDINATION: the press only works if all pressing players move simultaneously. One player pressing alone is easily bypassed.
INTENSITY: the press must be at maximum speed. A slow press gives the opponent time to play out and creates gaps behind the pressers.
WHEN THE PRESS IS BEATEN: if the ball escapes the press, all pressers immediately drop to their defensive shape. Do not continue pressing when beaten.
COMMUNICATION: the player initiating the press calls PRESS loudly so teammates know to react. This is the most important communication moment.
THE TRAP ZONE: the most sophisticated pressing is when a team lures the opponent into a specific zone where the press is pre-set. This requires patience before the trigger.
commonMistakes: Pressing too slowly after the trigger: the moment has passed before the press is set
Running directly at the ball instead of at an angle that cuts off the forward pass
Only one player pressing while others watch rather than compact
Continuing to press after the ball has escaped the press (leaving gaps behind)
No communication: silent pressing means players react individually not collectively
Pressing at the wrong moment: pressing when no trigger has occurred leaves gaps
corrections: Slow press: use a stopwatch. From trigger to press arrival must be under 2 seconds. Time every rep.
Direct run: place a cone between the presser and the ball showing the angled approach direction
Only one presser: add a rule that when the press is called, the coach counts how many players moved. Minimum 3 must move.
Press after beaten: blow whistle the moment the ball escapes. All players must freeze then reset to shape
No communication: the press does not count unless PRESS is called aloud first
Wrong moment pressing: add a penalty point for pressing when no trigger has occurred
progressions: Ball-playing team told the triggers and actively tries to avoid them: tests the pressing teams ability to force triggers
Add a counter-attack: if the pressing team wins the ball they immediately attack a mini-goal 20m away
Full 8v8 game with pressing rules: goals from a press-won ball earn 3 points
Reduce the zone: 30x20m forces more pressure and more trigger moments
regressions: Reduce to 2 triggers instead of 4 for the first session
Walk through the press shape at slow motion before any live reps
Start with a passive ball-playing team who do not try to avoid the press
matchApplication: Coordinated pressing is one of the defining tactical features of modern football at all levels. The highest-performing teams do not press randomly - they press to a plan, using pre-set triggers and trap zones to force opponents into making mistakes. Klopp at Liverpool, Guardiola at City, and Tuchel at Chelsea all built their defensive systems on this exact concept. Teaching pressing triggers at U14 gives players 4+ years to develop the coordinated instincts needed to execute a high press at senior level. The most important concept is that pressing without triggers is just running - pressing with triggers is a tactical weapon.
coachTips: Spend 5 minutes before the drill explaining and demonstrating all 4 triggers with no ball. Understanding comes before execution.
The trap zone trigger is the most sophisticated concept. Introduce it in session 2 after the other 3 are established.
Film the pressing patterns from above if possible. The shape of the press is only visible from an elevated angle.
The communication habit (calling PRESS) is the most important habit to build. Celebrate it every single time.
At U14 this concept will take 3-4 sessions to become instinctive. Be patient in the first session.
diagramDescription: 40x30m zone with a marked trap zone in one corner (8x8m cones). Ball-playing team (circles) spread across the zone in possession. Pressing team (triangles) in compact mid-block shape. Trigger moment shown: ball passed to wide player facing own goal (trigger 3). First presser shown with fast arrow closing at an angle to cut off the forward pass. Two covering pressers shown in cover shadow positions (standing in passing lanes). All other pressers shown with inward compact arrows.

title: Defensive Cover and Balance
objective: Develop the defensive principles of cover (second defender support) and balance (third defender preventing switches) in a back three scenario.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the roles of first, second, and third defenders
Players can provide cover support 2m behind and to the side of the first defender
Players develop the balance position that prevents a switch of play
Players communicate defensive roles during live play
organisation: 35x25m zone. A back 3 defends against 3 attackers. 3 wide neutral target players positioned outside the zone on each side (one left, one right, one top). Attackers earn a point by playing to a wide neutral target. Defenders earn a point by winning the ball and completing 3 consecutive passes.
instructions: Walk through the three defensive roles before starting:
FIRST DEFENDER (pressure): nearest defender to the ball. Job: close the ball-carrier immediately and apply pressure.
SECOND DEFENDER (cover): the defender closest to the first defender. Job: position 2m behind and to the ball-side of the first defender. Cover the most dangerous space behind the press.
THIRD DEFENDER (balance): the defender furthest from the ball. Job: position centrally to prevent a switch of play. Must be able to recover to either side.
3 attackers start with the ball and try to play to a wide target.
As the ball moves, all 3 defensive roles shift. The nearest defender becomes first, the next becomes cover, the furthest becomes balance.
The roles change on every pass. Players must communicate their role aloud.
Phase 2 after 10 minutes: attackers told they can use the switch pass. Third defender must now shift quickly to cover the switch.
Phase 3: live game. Defenders can win the ball and counter.
coachingPoints: FIRST DEFENDER ANGLE: close at an angle that shows the attacker toward the less dangerous side. Usually toward the touchline, away from central areas.
COVER POSITION: second defender is never directly behind the first. Always 2m to the ball side and 2m behind. This covers the space if the first defender is beaten.
BALANCE POSITION: third defender is on the same side as the ball but positioned centrally enough to recover to the far side if a switch is played.
SHIFTING TOGETHER: all three defenders must shift simultaneously as the ball moves. If one is slow the defensive shape has a gap.
COMMUNICATION: every defender calls their role. The first defender says PRESSING. The cover says COVERING. The balance says BALANCE. This prevents two players doing the same job.
COVER SHADOW: the second defender positions to cover the space behind the first AND to block the most dangerous through pass simultaneously.
COMPACTNESS: the three defenders should always be within 8-10m of each other. If they spread wider than this the gaps become undefendable.
REACTION TO THE SWITCH: when the ball switches to the far side, all three roles immediately flip. The balance becomes the new first defender. Speed of recognition is critical.
PATIENCE: defenders should not all press together. First defender pressures, the other two hold their shape until the first is beaten.
DEPTH: the cover and balance defenders should always be slightly deeper (closer to their own goal) than the first defender. Depth prevents being caught in behind.
commonMistakes: Two defenders pressing the ball simultaneously, leaving the cover space empty
Cover defender too far behind (more than 3m) making them too deep to be effective
Balance defender drifting too wide, leaving the central switch lane open
Defenders not shifting simultaneously when the ball moves
No communication: players guess their role instead of calling it
corrections: Two defenders pressing: stop the game and physically place the second defender in the correct cover position. Show them where to stand.
Cover too deep: use a rope or stick to show the maximum 2m distance behind the first defender
Balance too wide: place a central cone that the balance defender must stay within 3m of at all times
Not shifting together: freeze the game on every pass and check all three positions before resuming
No communication: make it a rule that the drill pauses until all three roles are called aloud
progressions: Add a fourth attacker: defenders must maintain shape against a 4v3 overload
Remove the role labels: players must identify and assume the correct role without being told
Full 6v6: two sets of three defenders work as a back 6 unit
Switch pass activated: attackers specifically target the switch to test the balance defender
regressions: Static ball: no movement, just position all three defenders correctly against a stationary attacker
Walk through at slow motion with coach directing each movement
2v2 cover only: just first and second defender before adding the balance role
matchApplication: Cover and balance are the two most important defensive principles beyond individual defending. Every professional defensive unit operates on these principles: the first defender pressures, the second covers the space behind, and the third provides balance against the switch. When all three work together in real time the defensive unit is extremely difficult to break down. Teaching these roles at U13 gives players the conceptual framework that all senior defensive coaching builds on. A centre-back who understands cover and balance instinctively is immediately more valuable to any team.
coachTips: The three role names (pressure, cover, balance) must become part of the team vocabulary. Use them constantly in match situations too.
Stand behind the defensive line during the drill so you can see the shape clearly. The cover and balance positions are invisible from the side.
The most common mistake (two pressing) is also the most damaging. Celebrate the correct cover position as loudly as a goal.
Connect to the match: show a video of a professional defensive unit shifting together as the ball moves.
At U13 the concept is new. Expect 2-3 sessions before it becomes instinctive.
diagramDescription: 35x25m zone. Three defenders (triangles) shown in cover and balance shape. Ball with attacker (circle) on the left side. First defender shown closing at an angle (arrow toward ball). Second defender shown 2m behind and to the ball side (cover position labeled). Third defender shown centrally positioned (balance labeled). Wide neutral targets shown outside the zone on three sides. Arrows show how all three defenders shift when ball moves to the right (all arrows pointing right simultaneously).

title: Defending Crosses
