objective: Develop the defensive unit skills needed to deal with crosses: marking, communication, and clearing effectively.
learningOutcomes: Defenders understand zonal and man-marking principles for defending crosses
Players can clear crossed balls confidently with power and direction
Players develop communication between the goalkeeper and defenders during crosses
Players learn the recovery run position when a cross is delivered
organisation: Full penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Two wide servers positioned in wide channels (left and right, 5m from touchline, level with the penalty area). 3-4 defenders in the box. 2-3 attackers in the box. Servers alternate delivering crosses from left and right.
instructions: PHASE 1 - No attackers (5 minutes): Defenders practice clearing positions only. Server delivers crosses from left and right. GK and defenders communicate and clear every ball. Focus on clearing technique and communication.
PHASE 2 - With passive attackers (8 minutes): 2-3 attackers stand in the box but do not jump or challenge. Defenders must organise and mark their assigned attackers while also tracking the ball.
Defensive positions before the cross: one defender on the near post (1m from the post), one defender on the far post (1m from the post), one or two defenders marking the central attackers.
GK positions centrally between the posts and takes responsibility for anything in the central zone above head height.
On delivery: GK calls KEEPER for balls they are claiming. Defenders clear anything the GK does not claim.
Clearing technique: meet the ball, do not wait for it. Head the ball with power away from goal and toward the touchline.
PHASE 3 - Live (9 minutes): Attackers can now move, jump, and attempt to score from crosses. Defenders must mark, communicate, and clear under pressure.
coachingPoints: NEAR POST DEFENDER: position on the near post, one step off the line. You are responsible for any ball played to the near post area and any ball the GK cannot reach.
FAR POST DEFENDER: position on the far post, one step off the line. You are responsible for any ball that drops beyond the GK on the far side.
CENTRAL DEFENDERS: mark the most dangerous attackers. Stay goal-side and ball-side simultaneously. Never let an attacker get in front of you on a cross.
GK DOMINANCE: the GK must be the dominant voice. KEEPER means everyone gets out of the way. Defenders must trust the GK and not challenge for balls the GK has called.
CLEARING DIRECTION: always clear away from goal. Clear toward the touchline or back the way the cross came. Never head across the face of your own goal.
MEET THE BALL: defenders who wait for the ball to come to them are beaten by attackers. Take one aggressive step toward the ball when it arrives in your zone.
SECOND BALL: after every clear, one defender must immediately move to press the second ball. Clearances often fall to an opponent 15-20m from goal.
SHAPE AFTER CLEAR: after clearing, defenders must immediately get back to their defensive shape. Do not watch the clearance go.
COMMUNICATION CONSTANT: defenders must call their name when they are claiming a ball so teammates do not challenge for the same ball.
HIGH LINE: defenders should not drop too deep in the box before the cross. A high defensive line makes it harder for attackers to get a run at the cross.
commonMistakes: GK staying on the goal line instead of coming to claim crosses in their area
Defenders not calling when they are claiming a cross, causing confusion and collision
Clearing the ball across the face of own goal (the most dangerous clearance in football)
Near post defender being too far from the post, allowing a near post flick-on
Defenders dropping too deep before the cross, giving attackers a running start
Clearing with the wrong part of the head (forehead must be used, not the top or side)
corrections: GK on line: place a cone at the edge of the 6-yard box. GK must step past it for every cross in the central zone
No communication: make a rule that uncalled claims do not count. If no name is called, the ball is contested
Clearing across goal: place a cone at the near post. Any clearance that goes between the two goals (across) is a failure. Clearances must go past the near post cone
Near post too wide: tie a 1m string from the near post. Defender must stay within the string length
Dropping too deep: place a cone at the 6-yard box line. Defenders must stay in front of this cone before the cross
Wrong heading technique: mark a cross on the forehead of a training mannequin to show the contact point
progressions: Add a recovery runner: one attacker starts outside the box and makes a late run as the cross is delivered, testing the defenders ability to track a late arrival
Second ball pressure: after every clear, a second server immediately plays the clearance back in as a second cross
Add a central striker who drops short to distract defenders before spinning to attack the cross
11v11 corner and cross game: goals only from crosses or corners
regressions: Phase 1 extended: spend the full session on positions and GK-defender communication before adding attackers
Slow delivery: servers loft the ball gently at reduced pace to allow defenders to find the correct position
One side only: only crosses from the right for the first session before adding the left
matchApplication: Defending crosses is one of the most game-critical defensive skills because crosses and set pieces from wide areas account for a significant proportion of goals at all levels of football. A defensive unit that is organised, communicates clearly, and clears with conviction concedes far fewer goals from wide deliveries. The near post, far post, and central marking system in this drill is the exact defensive shape used by professional clubs defending set pieces and open play crosses. Building this organisation at U15 gives defenders 3+ years to automate the shape before senior football.
coachTips: The GK is the most important player in this drill. Give them specific coaching time on when to come for crosses and when to stay.
Spend the full Phase 1 (no attackers) before adding any attacking pressure. The defensive positions must be established first.
The clearing direction coaching point is the single most impactful correction in youth football. Across-the-goal clearances cause goals. Hammer this point every session.
Film the defensive shape from behind the goal. The near post and far post positions are impossible to evaluate from the side.
Connect to match situations: show a professional defensive unit dealing with crosses and identify the near post, far post, and central defenders in the footage.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with full-size goal. GK shown in central position. Near post defender 1m from near post (labeled NP). Far post defender 1m from far post (labeled FP). 2 central defenders shown marking 2 attackers goal-side and ball-side. Wide server shown in left channel with cross delivery arc into the penalty area. Clearing direction shown with arrow going away from goal toward the touchline. GK claim zone shown as semicircle in the central area above head height.

title: Distribution — Throwing and Rolling
objective: Develop accurate GK distribution using the roll, javelin throw, and overarm throw to restart play quickly.
learningOutcomes: GK can roll the ball accurately to a target up to 12m
GK can use the javelin throw to reach targets up to 20m
GK can use the overarm throw for longer distances accurately
GK understands when to use each type of distribution
organisation: GK in goal. 4 target players positioned at different distances: T1 at 8m (short roll), T2 at 15m (javelin), T3 at 22m (overarm), T4 at 30m (overarm maximum). Each target player has a small cone target area (2m wide). GK distributes to each target in rotation, working on accuracy before adding movement.
instructions: ROLL (to T1, 8m): GK bends low, swings the arm like a bowling action, releases the ball along the ground. Ball should reach T1 without bouncing, at comfortable pace.

JAVELIN THROW (to T2, 15m): GK holds the ball at ear height in the throwing hand. Steps forward with the opposite foot. Drives the throwing arm forward in a javelin action. Ball should arc slightly and land at T2 feet.
OVERARM THROW (to T3, 22m): GK holds ball in throwing hand, takes a step, swings arm in a full overarm arc. Contact through the back of the ball. Follow through fully.
MAXIMUM THROW (to T4, 30m): same as overarm but with full body rotation and a longer approach step.
Phase 1 (5 minutes): static targets, accuracy focus. 3 attempts to each target.
Phase 2 (5 minutes): targets move laterally 3m before the throw. GK must lead the throw.
Phase 3 (5 minutes): GK receives a shot, saves it, and immediately distributes to the correct target called by the coach.
coachingPoints: ROLL TECHNIQUE: stay low throughout the roll. A standing roll bounces. The arm swings through like a bowling ball. Release point is close to the ground.
ROLL TARGET: aim for the players preferred foot, not the centre of the body. If they are right-footed, aim for the right foot.
JAVELIN STEP: the step forward with the opposite foot transfers body weight into the throw. Without this step the throw loses 30 percent of its distance.
JAVELIN FOLLOW-THROUGH: the throwing arm must follow through fully, finishing pointing at the target. A stopped follow-through causes the ball to go sideways.
OVERARM RELEASE POINT: release the ball when the throwing arm is at 1 oclock position (slightly past vertical). Too early and it goes high. Too late and it goes into the ground.
ACCURACY OVER DISTANCE: a throw that reaches the target but is inaccurate is worse than a slightly shorter accurate throw. Build accuracy before distance.
WHEN TO USE EACH: roll for players close and in space. Javelin for medium distances to a player facing toward you. Overarm for longer distances or to a player making a run.
QUICK DISTRIBUTION: the best GK distribution happens within 3 seconds of claiming the ball. Fast distribution starts counter-attacks before the opposition can organise.
LOOK BEFORE THROWING: scan the field before distributing. Identify the free player and the best type of throw for the distance and position.
commonMistakes: Rolling the ball while standing upright, causing the ball to bounce
No step in the javelin throw, reducing power and accuracy significantly
Releasing the overarm throw too late, causing the ball to go into the ground 10m short
Always distributing to the nearest player rather than the best-positioned player
Throwing directly to a player who is tightly marked instead of into space ahead of them
corrections: Standing roll: ask the GK to touch the ground with their throwing hand at the release point
No javelin step: mark the step position on the ground with a cone. GK must step onto it before releasing
Late overarm release: ask the GK to release when their arm is pointing at the crossbar (1 oclock position)
Always nearest player: remove T1 and make the GK choose between T2, T3, and T4 on every distribution
Throwing to marked player: add a passive defender beside each target. GK must throw to the unmarked target
progressions: Moving targets: all target players jog continuously. GK must lead the throw to where they will be, not where they are
Opposition pressure: a pressing player runs at the GK after the save. GK must distribute quickly before the presser arrives
Full distribution game: after every save, GK distributes to start a counter-attack. Team scores from the counter within 5 touches
regressions: Reduce target distances: T1 at 5m, T2 at 10m, T3 at 15m for younger or less experienced GKs
Static targets only until accuracy is established at each technique
Practice each throw type separately for a full session before combining all three
matchApplication: GK distribution is the starting point of every team attack from a saved shot, caught cross, or back pass. A GK who can distribute quickly and accurately to different distances has the ability to instantly transition a defensive action into an attacking opportunity. The teams with the best GK distribution (Liverpool under Alisson, City under Ederson) consistently start counter-attacks that create goal-scoring chances within 5 seconds of winning the ball. Teaching distribution at U11 builds the habit of looking to attack immediately after saving rather than simply clearing the ball long.
coachTips: GK distribution is often neglected in coaching sessions. Give it dedicated time every week.
The javelin throw is the most versatile and underused distribution technique in youth football. Most GKs only roll or kick. Build the javelin as their primary medium-range option.
Quick distribution is a mentality as much as a technique. Reward GKs who distribute within 3 seconds with a positive shout.
The look-before-throwing habit must be built from U11. A GK who always distributes to the nearest player will still be doing it at senior level.
Work with the outfield players during this drill: they must show for the ball and call for it. Distribution is a two-way skill.
diagramDescription: Goal with GK. Four target players shown at increasing distances: T1 at 8m (straight ahead), T2 at 15m (slight left), T3 at 22m (slight right), T4 at 30m (wide right). Rolling action shown with low arm swing arrow to T1. Javelin throw arc shown to T2. Overarm throw arc shown to T3 and T4. Release point for overarm shown as a small dot at 1 oclock position on the throwing arm arc. Each target player shown with a 2m wide cone target zone.

title: GK Footwork and Agility
objective: Develop fast, precise GK footwork for lateral movement, coming off the line, and recovery after diving.
learningOutcomes: GK can side-step across the full goal width without crossing feet
GK can come off the line and set position within 2 steps
GK develops explosive lateral movement from the set position
GK builds the habit of quick recovery footwork after diving
organisation: Full-size goal. 4 cones placed: one at each post (ground level), one at the left 6-yard box corner, one at the right 6-yard box corner. Server stands at the penalty spot. GK works through 4 footwork patterns before facing live shots in the final phase.
instructions: PATTERN 1 - Side-step across (3 minutes): GK starts at the left post cone. Side-steps to the right post cone without crossing feet. Touches right post, side-steps back. Repeat for 3 minutes at maximum speed.

PATTERN 2 - Come off line (3 minutes): GK starts on goal line centre. Server calls LEFT or RIGHT. GK steps forward 2 steps and sets at the correct angle (toward the called side). Server immediately fires a shot to the opposite side. GK must react and save.

PATTERN 3 - T-movement (3 minutes): GK starts centre goal. Steps forward 3 steps (toward penalty spot), side-steps left to the 6-yard corner cone, back to centre, side-steps right to the right 6-yard corner cone. Repeat continuously.

PATTERN 4 - Dive and recover (4 minutes): Server fires a shot to the left. GK dives and saves. Immediately gets up and side-steps back to centre. Server fires to the right. GK dives right. Recover to centre. Repeat.
LIVE PHASE (5 minutes): No patterns. Server fires rapidly from the penalty spot. GK uses all footwork learned to reach every shot.
coachingPoints: SIDE-STEP FUNDAMENTALS: feet never cross. Right foot steps right, left foot follows. If the feet cross the GK is momentarily off-balance and cannot dive.
WEIGHT ON BALLS OF FEET: throughout every pattern, the GK must be on the balls of their feet. Flat-footed GKs are 0.3 seconds slower in every direction.
BOUNCE: between every movement, a small two-foot bounce resets the weight and maintains the athletic position. Stop the bounce and the reaction time increases.
COMING OFF THE LINE: the step forward must be at the correct angle. Two steps forward on the angle of the ball position reduces the angle. Coming straight forward does not.
RECOVERY AFTER DIVE: the get-up after a dive must be explosive. Push off the ground with both hands, bring the knees under the body, and be upright within 2 seconds.
SET POSITION TIMING: the GK must be completely still in the set position at the moment the shot is struck. A moving GK cannot dive effectively.
HANDS READY: throughout all footwork patterns, hands remain at waist height, palms facing outward. Never let the hands drop to the sides during movement.
EYES ON THE BALL: maintain eye contact with the ball during all movement. Looking at the feet causes the GK to lose the ball flight.
SHORT STEPS: footwork is done with short, quick steps not long strides. Long strides create momentum that is difficult to stop and redirect.
commonMistakes: Crossing the feet during side-stepping which creates a stumble risk
Flat-footed stance throughout the patterns reducing reaction speed
Slow recovery after diving: staying on the ground for more than 3 seconds
Coming straight forward off the line instead of at the correct angle
Hands dropping to the sides during movement patterns
Long strides in the side-step making it impossible to stop and redirect quickly
corrections: Crossing feet: place a hurdle or stick between the feet and ask the GK to side-step without touching it
Flat feet: place a piece of paper under each heel. GK must not let it touch the ground during patterns
Slow recovery: time the recovery with a stopwatch. Target: upright within 2 seconds of the save
Straight off-line: place a cone at the correct angle position. GK must step to the cone not straight forward
Hands dropping: tie a resistance band at waist height across the goal. GK must keep hands above it during movement
Long strides: place 6 cones 50cm apart across the goal. GK must step in each gap (forces short quick steps)
progressions: Add a second server: two servers alternate firing from left and right. GK must recover between every shot
Reaction ball: server bounces a reaction ball unpredictably. GK must move to save from an unknown direction
Timed circuit: complete all 4 patterns as a circuit. Time the whole circuit and challenge the GK to beat their time
Fatigue element: 10 squat jumps before the live phase to test whether footwork quality is maintained under fatigue
regressions: Reduce goal to a junior goal (5m) for U12 players who are not yet physically large enough for full-size
Practice each pattern separately for one session before combining all four
Slow the side-step to 50 percent pace until the no-crossing-feet habit is established
matchApplication: GK footwork is the foundation of every other goalkeeping skill. A GK who moves precisely and explosively with correct footwork will always get to shots that a flat-footed or slow-moving GK cannot reach. The side-step is used on every shot that is not directly at the GK. The recovery after diving is used multiple times per match when a rebound situation develops. The coming-off-line movement happens on every through ball and 1v1 situation. Goalkeepers who work specifically on footwork consistently outperform more naturally talented GKs who neglect it because footwork is the base that all other GK technique sits on.
coachTips: Footwork sessions are best done at the start of a session when the GK is fresh. Tired footwork reinforces bad habits.
Use a metronome app to set the pace for side-step patterns. External rhythm helps GKs find their natural quick-step tempo.
Film the side-step from in front of the goal so the foot crossing error is visible. Most GKs do not know they are crossing their feet until they see it.
The bounce habit between movements is very easy to develop and has a significant impact on reaction speed. Make it a non-negotiable habit from the first session.
GK footwork is often neglected by coaches who are not GK specialists. A simple 15-minute footwork circuit before every session transforms GK performance over a season.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. 4 cones shown: at left post (ground level), at right post, at left 6-yard corner, at right 6-yard corner. Pattern 1 shown as a horizontal arrow between the two post cones (side-step). Pattern 2 shown as a forward arrow (2 steps) then an angle arrow left or right (angle off line). Pattern 3 shown as a T-shape: forward arrow to centre then horizontal arrows to each 6-yard corner cone. Pattern 4 shown as dive-left arrow, recovery arrow to centre, dive-right arrow. Server shown at penalty spot.

title: Acceleration from Rest
objective: Develop explosive first-step acceleration from a stationary position with and without the ball.
learningOutcomes: Players can accelerate from 0 to maximum speed within 3 steps
Players develop the correct body position for explosive acceleration
Players build the habit of explosive starting movement in match situations
Players develop acceleration both with and without the ball
organisation: Multiple 30m sprint corridors side by side, one per player. Start line marked at one end. Timing gates (or manual timing) at 5m, 10m, and 30m. Players complete reps individually then recover while others go. Minimum 30 seconds rest between each rep.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Without ball (6 minutes): Pure acceleration work. Players start in a ready position: feet slightly wider than shoulders, knees bent, weight forward on the balls of the feet, hands ready.
On the whistle: explosive first step (always lead with the dominant foot), lean forward aggressively for the first 3 steps, pump the arms hard.
Sprint through to 30m. Walk back. Minimum 30 seconds rest. 6 total reps.
PHASE 2 - With ball (6 minutes): Same setup but with a ball at the players feet. On the whistle: push the ball forward 2m with the outside of the foot on the first step, then sprint after it.
The first touch sends the ball forward, the body follows immediately. Do not touch then stop before accelerating.
6 reps with the ball.

PHASE 3 - Reaction starts (6 minutes): Players start in different positions (sitting, lying face down, lying face up, back to the direction of sprint). On the whistle they get up and sprint 20m. Tests reaction and explosive start from different positions.
4 reps from each starting position.
coachingPoints: FORWARD LEAN: in the first 3 steps the body should be at a 45-degree angle to the ground. Upright posture in the early steps means the player is decelerating before they have accelerated.
FIRST STEP: the first step is short and powerful, driving directly backward into the ground. A long first step reaches forward and kills the acceleration.
ARM DRIVE: arms drive hard front to back (not across the body) in the first 5 steps. The arm drive directly powers the leg drive in acceleration.
KNEE DRIVE: the knees drive forward and up in each stride. A GK who shuffles rather than drives their knees will never accelerate effectively.
GRADUAL UPRIGHT: the body gradually becomes upright over the first 10m. Players who try to run upright from step 1 lose the acceleration phase completely.
EYES FORWARD: look ahead during the sprint, not at the ball or at the ground. Eyes forward keeps the head in the correct position for optimal stride.
BALL TOUCH AT PACE: when accelerating with the ball, the first touch must send the ball far enough that the player runs onto it (about 2m), not so far that it runs away.
MAXIMUM EFFORT: acceleration training only works at 100 percent effort. 90 percent effort does not develop the neuromuscular patterns needed for explosive starts.
RECOVERY: full recovery between reps is non-negotiable for speed work. 30 seconds minimum. If players are tired, the sprints become endurance training, not speed training.
commonMistakes: Standing upright for the first 3 steps instead of maintaining a forward lean
First step reaching forward (too long) instead of driving backward into the ground
Arms crossing the body instead of driving front to back
Not giving 100 percent effort on every rep
Ball touch in phase 2 going too far ahead, causing the player to sprint past it
Insufficient rest between reps, turning the session into endurance training
corrections: Upright start: place a low hurdle 1m ahead of the start. GK must lean under it for the first 3 steps
Long first step: draw a short step mark 40cm ahead of the start. First step must land on it
Arms crossing: hold a ball in each hand during the first few reps to prevent arm crossing (arms cannot cross when holding balls)
Not 100 percent: use a partner race. The competitive element brings out maximum effort automatically
Ball too far: place a cone 2m ahead of the start. First touch must land before the cone
Insufficient rest: use a visible timer or count rest periods aloud so players know when to go
progressions: Gate sprints: add 2 directional gates at 10m. Coach calls LEFT or RIGHT just before the sprint. Player must go through the correct gate (adds decision-making to acceleration)
Acceleration into 1v1: after the 20m sprint a passive defender steps in. Player must use their acceleration to beat the defender
Resisted acceleration: light resistance band around the waist held by a partner for the first 5m, then released
Competition: pairs race over 10m from different starting positions. Most wins after 6 reps earns a point
regressions: Reduce sprint distance to 15m for players who struggle with maintaining form over 30m
Remove the ball for the full session and focus on pure acceleration mechanics
Reduce to 4 reps per phase with longer rest periods
matchApplication: Explosive acceleration is one of the most valuable physical attributes in football. The vast majority of decisive actions in a match (getting to a through ball, closing down an opponent, making a run in behind, recovering to make a tackle) are decided in the first 5-10m of movement. A player who can cover 5m in 1 second vs a player who takes 1.3 seconds will consistently arrive first in every contested situation. At U13, players are entering a critical period for developing fast-twitch muscle fibre and explosive movement patterns. Speed work done now will have a greater impact than the same work done at U17.
coachTips: Speed training must happen at the start of a session before any fatigue. Post-session speed work is wasted.
Explain the reason for full rest periods: partial rest means the reps are no longer speed training. Players who understand the science rest properly.
The forward lean concept is counterintuitive. Most players think running upright is faster. Demonstrate by running upright for 10m then with the lean for 10m and show the difference.
Film the first step from the side. The short driving step vs the reaching step is easy to see on film but hard to feel.
Use this session 2-3 times per month. More frequent speed work without adequate recovery causes fatigue rather than adaptation.
diagramDescription: Multiple 30m sprint corridors side by side (3m wide each). Start line at one end. Timing markers shown at 5m, 10m, and 30m. Player shown in ready position (45-degree lean, knees bent, weight forward). Three consecutive footprint diagrams show: short first step, slightly longer second step, full stride third step — illustrating the gradual stride extension during acceleration. Ball shown 2m ahead of the start for phase 2 (first touch position).

title: Agility and Coordination Ladder
objective: Develop foot coordination, agility, and rhythm through progressive agility ladder patterns.
learningOutcomes: Players can complete 3 agility ladder patterns with correct footwork
Players develop foot-eye coordination and body rhythm
Players build fast feet habits that transfer to ball control and movement
Players enjoy physical training through the variety and challenge of ladder work
equipment: 1 agility ladder per 2-3 players (or tape on the ground as a substitute)
organisation: 4-6 agility ladders set up in parallel. 2-3 players per ladder. Players complete the pattern, jog back to the start, and repeat. Coach demonstrates each new pattern from the side before players attempt it. All patterns run simultaneously.
instructions: PATTERN 1 - One foot per box (3 minutes): Run through the ladder placing one foot in each box. Arms drive front to back. Stay on the balls of the feet throughout. Progress to maximum speed after 2 slow reps.
PATTERN 2 - Two feet per box (3 minutes): Place both feet in each box (left then right, left then right). Stay low. Quick, light contacts with the ground. Do not flat-foot.

PATTERN 3 - In-out lateral (3 minutes): Stand to the side of the ladder. Step both feet into the first box, then both feet out to the side, then both feet into the second box. Move along the ladder laterally. Both directions.

PATTERN 4 - Icky shuffle (3 minutes): Step right foot in, left foot in, right foot out to the right side of the ladder, left foot in next box, right foot in, left foot out to the left side. This is the most complex pattern - walk through it first.
FINAL MINUTE: players choose their favourite pattern and go at maximum speed. Competition: who completes the most full ladder lengths in 60 seconds.
coachingPoints: BALLS OF FEET: all ladder work is done on the balls of the feet, never flat-footed. The heel never touches inside the ladder box.
QUICK CONTACTS: the foot should touch the ground and immediately come back up. The less time the foot spends on the ground the faster the overall speed.
ARM DRIVE: arms drive front to back in all patterns. The arm rhythm drives the foot rhythm. Slow arms means slow feet.
EYES FORWARD: after the first 2 slow reps, players should know the pattern well enough to keep eyes forward rather than looking at the ladder.
RHYTHM FIRST, SPEED SECOND: establish the correct footwork rhythm at slow pace before increasing to maximum speed. Fast but incorrect footwork reinforces bad movement patterns.
POSTURE: slight forward lean throughout all patterns. Upright posture creates heel-striking which is slow.
BREATHING: exhale on every step contact. This keeps the core engaged and the breathing relaxed.
MISTAKES ARE NORMAL: at U10 the patterns are complex and mistakes are expected. Encourage persistence and celebrate improvement.
JOYFUL MOVEMENT: ladder work should feel like a game. Keep the energy high, celebrate good patterns, make it fun.
commonMistakes: Flat-footed contacts inside the ladder boxes reducing speed and agility
Looking at the ladder throughout instead of ahead after the first few reps
Arms swinging across the body instead of front to back
Rushing the pattern before it is established at slow pace
Stepping on the ladder rungs instead of in the boxes
corrections: Flat feet: ask players to imagine the floor is hot inside the ladder boxes. Quick contacts only.
Looking at ladder: place a cone at eye height 5m beyond the ladder end. Keep that cone in sight during the pattern.
Arms across: hold a water bottle in each hand during the first rep. Bottles going across the body is immediately visible.
Rushing: clap a slow beat for the first 2 reps of each new pattern. Players match their footwork to the clap.
Stepping on rungs: colour the rungs with chalk so steps on the rung leave a mark (feedback without coaching)
progressions: Ball in hands: complete the pattern while holding a ball at chest height. Tests whether coordination is maintained with an additional element.
Ball at feet after ladder: dribble through a cone gate immediately after completing the ladder pattern
Eyes closed (pattern 1 only): complete the one-foot pattern with eyes closed after 3 successful sighted reps
Sequence challenge: complete patterns 1, 2, and 3 back to back without stopping at the start
regressions: Walk through every new pattern 3 times before jogging it
Reduce to patterns 1 and 2 only for players who find patterns 3 and 4 too complex
Use wider tape marks on the ground instead of a ladder if the ladder boxes are too small for younger players
matchApplication: Agility ladder work directly develops the fast feet and coordination that underpin all football movement. The quick contact time, the directional change ability, and the foot-eye coordination built in ladder sessions transfer directly to ball control, sharp turning, and quick directional changes in match situations. At U10 the nervous system is in a critical period of development for coordination. Movement patterns learned well at this age are maintained for life. U10 players who do regular agility work consistently outperform those who do not in speed, touch, and general athletic ability throughout their development years.
coachTips: Agility ladders are an investment that lasts years. If your club does not have them, tape on the ground works just as well.
Keep the energy and the music high during ladder work. U10 players need the environment to feel exciting.
Demonstrate every pattern slowly yourself before players attempt it. Children learn best through imitation.
The Icky Shuffle (pattern 4) is genuinely complex. Do not move on from the walk-through until the majority of players have the footwork correct.
Run ladder sessions 2-3 times per week at U10. The frequency builds the motor patterns faster than once per week.
diagramDescription: 4-6 agility ladders set up in parallel with 3m between each. Players shown at the start of each ladder. Pattern 1 shown with single footprint in each box progressing forward. Pattern 2 shown with two footprints per box. Pattern 3 shown laterally with feet going in-out-in-out along the side. Pattern 4 (Icky Shuffle) shown with numbered footprints: 1 (right in), 2 (left in), 3 (right out right side), 4 (left in next box), 5 (right in), 6 (left out left side). Jog-back arrow shows return path beside the ladder.

title: Direct Free Kick Delivery
objective: Develop the technique for accurate direct free kick delivery over and around the wall, covering both driven and dipping shots.
learningOutcomes: Players can strike a driven free kick accurately toward the corners of the goal
Players can apply curve and dip to a free kick to go around or over the wall
Players understand the relationship between distance, angle, and technique selection
Players develop a consistent free kick routine that replicates match conditions
players: 2-4 free kick takers, GK, 3-4 defenders (wall), target players
equipment: 4 cones to mark the wall position
pitchSize: Full pitch, using the shooting area between 18m and 28m from goal
organisation: Free kick positions set up at 5 locations: 22m central, 22m left of centre, 22m right of centre, 25m left angle, 25m right angle. 3-4 defenders form a wall at the correct distance (9.15m from the ball). GK in goal. Free kick takers rotate through all 5 positions. 3 attempts per position per player.
instructions: WALL SETUP: 3-4 defenders stand in a line at 9.15m from the ball (approximately 10 paces). The end of the wall should cover the near post. GK positions to cover the far post and central area.

TECHNIQUE 1 - DRIVEN FREE KICK (central and near-angle positions): approach at a slight angle, plant foot 15cm beside the ball pointing at the target, strike through the centre of the ball with the laces, follow through toward the target. Ball should travel fast and flat, dipping late.

TECHNIQUE 2 - CURVED FREE KICK (wide angle positions): approach at a steeper angle from the opposite side to the intended curve direction, strike the inside of the ball with the inside of the foot, follow through across the body. Ball curves from outside the wall around and toward the far post.
ROUTINE: every free kick must begin with the same routine - place the ball, take 5 steps back, 2 steps to the side, deep breath, then the approach.
Phase 1 (10 minutes): no wall, pure technique at each position.
Phase 2 (10 minutes): wall added (passive - does not jump). GK live.
Phase 3 (5 minutes): wall jumps. GK live. Match conditions.
coachingPoints: DRIVEN TECHNIQUE - CONTACT POINT: strike through the horizontal centre of the ball with the laces. Slightly below centre gives lift, exactly centre gives a flat driven shot.
DRIVEN TECHNIQUE - PLANT FOOT: 15cm beside the ball pointing at the target. If the plant foot points right the ball goes right. This is the primary accuracy control.
CURVED TECHNIQUE - APPROACH ANGLE: approach from the opposite side to the intended curve (if curving left, approach from the right side). Steeper approach angle creates more curve.
CURVED TECHNIQUE - CONTACT POINT: strike the inside half of the ball with the inside of the foot. The more inside the contact, the more curve but less power. Balance the two.
OVER OR AROUND THE WALL: the decision is based on the angle. Central positions: drive over the wall (lower trajectory clears the wall and dips). Wide positions: curve around the end of the wall.
THE ROUTINE: a consistent pre-kick routine builds muscle memory and removes thinking during the kick. Every great free kick taker has the same routine every time.
WEIGHT TRANSFER: transfer the body weight from back foot to plant foot in a smooth flowing motion. Stopping or pausing in the run-up kills the power.
FOLLOW-THROUGH: the kicking foot should finish high and across the body on the curved kick, or pointing directly at the target on the driven kick.
GK PERSPECTIVE: aim for the corners of the goal that are hardest for the GK to reach given their wall-side starting position. Usually the far bottom or far top corner.
COMPOSURE: the routine and the breathing (deep breath before the approach) create the composure needed to execute under pressure.
commonMistakes: Plant foot pointing in the wrong direction causing the ball to miss the target
Approaching the ball too directly (no angle) making the curved kick impossible
Striking too far underneath the ball causing it to go over the bar
No consistent routine: different approach every time creates inconsistent results
Ball going into the wall because the intended curve is not enough to clear the end of the wall
Losing composure during the run-up causing a rushed, mistimed strike
corrections: Plant foot direction: draw an arrow on the ground showing where the plant foot should point for each position
No angle on approach: place a cone at the correct approach angle. Player must walk around the cone before the run-up
Over the bar: place a target (marker) at the top corner of the goal at head height. Aim for the target to regulate trajectory
No routine: assign a specific routine (5 back, 2 side, breath) and enforce it on every attempt
Ball into wall: move the ball 1m further from centre and re-approach until the curve clears the wall end
Rushed run-up: count the steps aloud. A rushed run-up has fewer steps than the routine specifies
progressions: Wall jumps on a signal: GK chooses when to call JUMP. Free kick taker must read this and decide whether to go over or under the jump
Two-player routine: add a second player who makes a decoy run over the ball before the taker strikes (deception element)
Combination free kick: one player passes to a second player who takes a first-time strike from 2m further from goal
Under pressure: defenders allowed to charge down after the whistle (replicates the urgency of a match free kick)
regressions: No wall and no GK for the first 10 minutes: pure technique practice at each position
Reduce to 2 positions only (central and one wide) before expanding to all 5
Allow a full run-up (from 10m back) before progressing to a match-realistic run-up length
matchApplication: Direct free kicks are one of the highest-value set piece situations in football because they can create direct goal-scoring opportunities from anywhere within 25m of goal. A team with 2-3 reliable free kick takers who can consistently threaten from different positions puts enormous pressure on opponents. The technique developed in this drill (the driven shot over the wall and the curved shot around it) covers the two most commonly used free kick techniques at all levels from grassroots to elite. The pre-kick routine is the element that most separates good from great free kick takers: it creates the composure and consistency that turn technique into results.
coachTips: Identify the best free kick takers in your squad early and give them extra practice time. 1 reliable free kick taker is more valuable than 4 inconsistent ones.
The routine is the most important habit to build. Monitor it closely and insist on it in training so it is automatic in matches.
Film free kicks from behind the goal. The curl and dip of the ball are only fully visible from directly behind.
Connect to professional examples: show Ronaldo driven free kicks (the knuckleball technique), Beckham curved free kicks, and Pirlo chip free kicks to illustrate different techniques.
Do not rush from phase 1 to phase 2. Players need to establish consistency without a wall before adding the wall complication.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. 5 free kick positions shown: P1 (central, 22m), P2 (22m left of centre), P3 (22m right of centre), P4 (25m left angle), P5 (25m right angle). Wall of 4 defenders shown at 9.15m from each position. GK shown behind the wall toward the far post. Driven free kick shown from P1 with a flat trajectory arc over the wall and dipping into the far top corner. Curved free kick shown from P4 with a bending arc around the right end of the wall into the far bottom corner. Approach angles shown for both techniques with small approach arrows.

title: Goal Kick Build-Up Routines
objective: Develop organised, reliable goal kick routines that allow the team to maintain possession and build from the back.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the positional shape required for goal kick possession
GK and defenders can execute short and long goal kick options reliably
Players learn to trigger movement at the right moment during a goal kick
Teams develop 2 set goal kick routines that can be executed under pressure
organisation: Half pitch. GK takes goal kicks from the 6-yard box. Back 4 in defensive shape. 2-3 midfielders positioned to receive. Optional press team of 2-3 forwards. Two routines established before the drill starts: ROUTINE A (short build-up) and ROUTINE B (long switch).
instructions: ROUTINE A - SHORT BUILD-UP: GK plays short to one CB who has stepped to the side of the 18-yard box. CB plays to the other CB who has stepped to the opposite edge of the box. Full-backs push into wide midfield positions. CMs drop between the lines. The ball builds through the defenders to a CM.

ROUTINE B - LONG SWITCH: GK drives the ball long to a wide target (full-back or winger) who has made a run to the halfway line on the far side. The opposite side is deliberately overloaded with players to draw the press and open the long side.
Walk through both routines at slow motion with no pressure (5 minutes).
Phase 2 (8 minutes): 2 passive pressing forwards. Team executes both routines against the press.
Phase 3 (7 minutes): Active pressing forwards who can intercept. Team must read the press and choose the correct routine.
Signal system: GK holds up 1 finger (Routine A) or 2 fingers (Routine B) before every goal kick.
Score: 1 point for completing 5 passes from a Routine A goal kick. 1 point for the long switch reaching the target.
coachingPoints: GK COMMUNICATION: the GK decides the routine and signals it clearly BEFORE placing the ball. Every player must see the signal.
MOVEMENT TIMING: outfield players must not start their movement until the GK places the ball. Early movement alerts the press.
ROUTINE A - CB WIDTH: both CBs must be at the edge of the 18-yard box (wide). Narrow CBs make the routine too easy for the press to cover.
ROUTINE A - CM SHOWING: CMs must drop below the halfway line and show between the lines (between the pressing forwards and the pressing midfielders). If they are not visible the CB has no pass.
ROUTINE B - FAR SIDE OVERLOAD: before the kick, 3-4 players must show on one side. This draws the press and opens the far side for the long kick.

ROUTINE B - GK TECHNIQUE: the long goal kick must be driven and accurate. A short or inaccurate long kick is easily intercepted. The GK drives through the ball with the laces from the side of the foot.
READING THE PRESS: if the opposition press high and man-mark (no space for Routine A): use Routine B. If the opposition sit in a mid-block (space between the lines): use Routine A.
CONFIDENCE: players must believe in the routine. Hesitation or changing the routine mid-execution causes turnovers. Commit to the signal.
SECOND BALL: on Routine B, every player not receiving the long kick must immediately sprint to contest the second ball if the header is lost.
ADAPTABILITY: if the routine breaks down early (pass is intercepted), the team must immediately transition to defensive shape.
commonMistakes: Players starting their movement before the GK places the ball, alerting the press
CBs staying narrow, making the short build-up too easy for the press to cover
CMs not showing between the lines, leaving the CB with no safe passing option
GK choosing Routine A when the press is too high and too narrow (wrong reading of the press)
Long kick inaccurate: landing in a contested area rather than on the target player
Players not reacting to the second ball after a Routine B kick that is headed away
corrections: Early movement: add a rule that movement cannot start before the GK says NOW (verbal trigger replaces ball placement trigger)
Narrow CBs: place wide cones at the edge of the 18-yard box. CBs must stand outside these cones
CMs not showing: make the CM movement mandatory. If the CM is not between the lines the ball cannot be kicked
Wrong routine selection: freeze the game after a wrong choice and ask the group: was the press high or mid? Which routine does that mean?
Inaccurate long kick: GK takes 5 practice long kicks to the target before the phase begins
No second ball reaction: designate one player as the second ball chaser on Routine B. Their only job is to sprint to contest it
progressions: Add a third routine: ROUTINE C where the GK plays to a full-back who has dropped very deep to receive
Opposition presses actively and communicates: pressing team shouts their press strategy so both teams must adapt
Time pressure: goal kick must be taken within 5 seconds of the signal (replicates the match rule)
Full 9v9 game where goals from build-up play starting from a goal kick earn double points
regressions: No press for the full session: establish both routines without any defensive pressure first
Reduce to Routine A only for one full session before introducing Routine B
Slow motion walk-through at the start of every session until the routine is automatic
matchApplication: Goal kicks are one of the most common restarts in football (typically 10-15 per team per match) and represent significant possession opportunities that most teams waste by simply kicking long randomly. A team with 2 organised goal kick routines that all players understand can turn every goal kick into either a ball-possession moment or a direct chance creation moment. The decision-making element (reading the press to choose the right routine) develops game intelligence that transfers to all other possession and build-up situations in the match.
coachTips: Introduce goal kick routines in pre-season when players have time to learn them without match pressure.
The signal system (1 or 2 fingers) must be visible to everyone. Practice the signal communication before anything else.
The GK is the decision-maker. Empower them to make the right choice and back it up in every training session.
Connect to professional matches: show footage of a team using a short goal kick build-up successfully and identify each players position in the routine.
Routines need 4-6 training sessions before they are reliable in a match. Be patient.
diagramDescription: Half pitch with full-size goal at bottom. GK shown in the 6-yard box. ROUTINE A shown: CB left (wide left of 18-yard box), CB right (wide right), GK arrow to CB left, CB left arrow to CB right, two CMs shown between the lines with receiving arrows, full-backs shown in wide midfield positions. ROUTINE B shown on a separate small diagram: GK long kick arc to far side winger at halfway line, overloaded near side shown with 3-4 players clustered to draw the press, far side wide and open. Press forwards (triangles) shown for both routines.

title: First Time Finishing
objective: Develop the ability to strike a moving ball first time toward goal with accuracy and confidence.
learningOutcomes: Players can strike a rolled ball first time with the inside of the foot
Players develop the body position needed for a first-time finish
Players build confidence shooting at goal without taking a controlling touch
Players learn to pick a target before the ball arrives
organisation: Server stands at the edge of the penalty area with 3 balls. Shooter starts 12m from goal. Server rolls the ball across the shooter at a comfortable pace. GK in goal. After 3 attempts rotate server, shooter, and any waiting players.
instructions: Server rolls the ball across the penalty area from right to left at jogging pace.
Shooter approaches from a slight angle, timing their run to meet the ball.
As the ball rolls across, the shooter plants the non-kicking foot beside the path of the ball.
Strike through the centre of the ball with the inside of the foot in one smooth action.
Follow through toward the target. The finish goes to the far bottom corner.
After 5 minutes: server rolls from left to right so shooter uses the other foot.
After 10 minutes: server varies the pace (slow, medium, fast) and the shooter must adjust their timing.
GK is live throughout and tries to save every shot.
Count goals scored per player across 9 attempts (3 from each direction plus varied pace).
coachingPoints: TIMING THE RUN: do not stand still and wait for the ball. Walk or jog into the finish so your body is already moving when you strike.
PLANT FOOT: the non-kicking foot plants beside the rolling ball, not behind it. Too far behind causes the shot to lift.
BODY OVER THE BALL: lean slightly forward over the ball at contact. Leaning back sends it over the crossbar.
INSIDE FOOT CONTACT: use the large flat inside surface, not the toe. The inside foot gives the most accuracy for first-time finishes.
PICK YOUR CORNER FIRST: decide which corner you are aiming for before the ball arrives. Do not decide at the moment of contact.
FOLLOW THROUGH: the kicking foot continues toward the target after contact. A short follow-through kills the power and accuracy.
KEEP IT LOW: aim for the bottom half of the goal. Low shots are harder to save than high ones.
APPROACH ANGLE: approach at a 30-45 degree angle to the direction of the rolling ball. A straight approach makes the contact awkward.
CONFIDENCE: commit to the shot. Half-hearted first-time finishes are always poor. Strike with intention.
commonMistakes: Waiting for the ball to stop before shooting instead of striking it first time
Plant foot too far behind the ball causing the shot to lift over the bar
Using the toe instead of the inside of the foot causing wild inaccuracy
No approach angle — standing directly in line with the rolling ball
Looking at the ball during the finish instead of at the target corner
Leaning back at the moment of contact sending the ball high
corrections: Waiting: add a rule that the ball must be struck before it reaches a cone 2m past the shooter
Plant foot behind: draw a line on the ground level with the ball path. Plant foot must land on this line
Toe finish: tape a sticker on the inside of the boot — only the sticker can contact the ball
No approach angle: place a cone at 45 degrees from the ball path — shooter must walk around it before finishing
Looking at ball: ask the shooter to call out which corner they aimed for after every attempt
Leaning back: place a cone 50cm in front of the shooter. Their head must be over that cone at contact
progressions: Lofted serve: server chips the ball through the air instead of rolling — shooter volleys or half-volleys
Two touch then finish: control with one touch, finish with the second
Defender added: a passive defender stands between the server and the goal — shooter must get the shot away before the defender blocks
Competition: pairs take turns finishing. First to 5 goals from 9 attempts wins
regressions: Use a stationary ball first to establish body position before adding movement
Reduce to a junior goal to make scoring easier and build confidence
Server rolls the ball directly to the shooters preferred foot before adding the across-body finish
matchApplication: First-time finishing is one of the most valuable skills in football because the best chances in a match are often half-chances that must be taken quickly before a defender can block. A player who can reliably strike a moving ball first time with accuracy will score more goals than one who always needs an extra touch. This drill directly replicates the most common youth football goal-scoring scenario: a ball rolled or passed across the face of the goal that must be finished first time.
coachTips: Keep the session high-energy and competitive. Finishing sessions are among the most enjoyable for U11 players.
Count every goal loudly and celebrate them. Goal-scoring confidence is built through positive experiences.
Do not over-coach during the finishing reps. Let players attempt, then give one correction per player.
Ensure both feet are practiced equally. Many players will try to avoid their weaker foot.
End every finishing session with a competition format. The pressure of competition replicates match conditions.
diagramDescription: Penalty area with junior or full-size goal and GK. Server (square) positioned at the edge of the penalty area on the right side. Rolling ball shown with a horizontal arrow moving left across the penalty area. Shooter (circle) shown approaching from the right at a 45-degree angle to meet the ball. Finish arrow goes to the far bottom left corner of the goal. Second setup shown with server on the left and shooter approaching from the left (weak foot finish).

title: Shooting Under Pressure
objective: Develop the composure and technique to finish accurately with a defender closing from behind at match pace.
learningOutcomes: Players can shoot accurately with a defender within 2m of them
Players develop the body shielding needed to create a shooting opportunity under pressure
Players learn to disguise the shot direction until the last moment
Players build the physical and mental composure for high-pressure finishing
organisation: 5 shooting positions around the penalty area. At each position a server stands 5m outside the area. Shooter starts beside the server. A defender starts 3m behind the shooter. On the servers pass the defender immediately closes. Shooter has 2 touches to finish before the defender arrives. GK live throughout.
instructions: Shooter and defender start level at the edge of the area. Server is 5m further out.
Server plays a firm pass into the area toward the shooting position.
The moment the ball is played: defender sprints to close the shooter.
Shooter takes a controlling first touch away from the closing defender.
Second touch is the finish — must be within 2 touches of receiving.
GK tries to save every shot. Defender tries to block.
After every attempt rotate shooter, defender, and server.
Phase 2 after 10 minutes: reduce to 1 touch finish only — receive and shoot in one action.
Phase 3: defender starts only 1m behind — maximum pressure, minimum time.
coachingPoints: FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION: the first touch must go away from the closing defender and toward the shooting angle. A touch directly under the body gives the defender time to block.
BODY SHIELD: use the body between the ball and the defender on the first touch. Drop the shoulder slightly to create separation.
PICK YOUR CORNER EARLY: as the pass is coming to you, decide which corner you are aiming for. The defender closing does not change your corner choice.
SHOOT EARLY: do not wait for the perfect moment. The best finishing under pressure happens quickly. Two touches maximum.
DISGUISE: look toward one corner before shooting the other. Even under pressure, a small disguise gives the GK less time to react.
LOW AND ACCURATE: under pressure players tend to blast the ball high. Focus on a low, accurate finish rather than maximum power.
WEIGHT TRANSFER: transfer body weight onto the standing foot quickly after the first touch so the shooting foot is free immediately.
COMPOSURE: slow your breathing before receiving. The physical sensation of composure reduces panic under pressure.
AFTER THE SHOT: follow up the finish immediately in case the GK parries. Second-chance goals come from players who keep moving after shooting.
commonMistakes: First touch going directly toward the defender rather than away from them
Taking more than 2 touches and allowing the defender to make a block
Blasting the shot without accuracy because of the pressure from behind
Stopping and looking up when the defender closes rather than shooting immediately
First touch too heavy sending the ball out of the shooting range
corrections: Touch toward defender: place a cone 1m to the side of the receiving position — first touch must go toward the cone not toward the defender
Too many touches: strict 2-touch rule enforced — third touch means the rep does not count
Blasting: place a string 1m high across the goal 2m from the line — shots must go under it (forces accuracy over power)
Stopping: count aloud after the pass — 1 (first touch), 2 (shoot). Both must happen within 2 counts
Heavy first touch: reduce the size of the shooting area so heavy touches immediately go out of range
progressions: Two defenders: one closes from behind, one steps across the shooting lane — shooter must find the gap
GK rushes: GK comes off the line as the shot is taken — shooter must choose to shoot early or chip
No first touch: ball must be struck first time from the pass regardless of pace or direction
Rebound finish: after every main shot a second ball is immediately rolled in for a rebound finish
regressions: Passive defender only — closes but cannot block — removes the physical pressure while maintaining the mental pressure
Increase to 3 touches before the 2-touch rule is enforced
Reduce the defender start distance to 5m giving more time on the first touch
matchApplication: Finishing under defensive pressure is the defining skill of a senior striker. In match conditions almost every shooting opportunity has a defender within 2-3m. The ability to take a controlled first touch away from pressure and shoot accurately in the next action separates strikers who score from those who create chances but cannot convert them. At U16 players are approaching the physical and cognitive level where this pressure is match-realistic. The habits built now — first touch away from pressure, early shoot decision, composure — carry directly into senior performance.
coachTips: The defender must close at genuine match pace. A slow defender makes the drill too easy and does not build the correct habits.
Watch the first touch direction on every rep. This is the single most important coaching moment in the drill.
Use this drill at the end of a session when players are slightly fatigued — finishing under physical tiredness is the most match-realistic condition.
Track each players conversion rate. Goals from 10 attempts under pressure is a meaningful performance metric.
Film from behind the goal. The shot placement relative to the GK is only clearly visible from directly behind.
diagramDescription: Penalty area with full-size goal and GK. 5 shooting positions shown around the area (P1-P5). At each position: server (square) 5m outside the area, shooter (circle) at the area edge, defender (triangle) 3m behind the shooter. Arrow 1: server plays pass into the area. Arrow 2: defender sprints to close (red arrow). Arrow 3: shooter takes first touch sideways away from defender (small arrow). Arrow 4: shooter finishes to far corner (long arrow to goal). Defender shown arriving just after the shot.

title: Striker Movement and Finish
objective: Develop the full striker movement package — near post run, far post run, drop and turn — combining with a quality finish.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute three distinct striker movement patterns at match pace
Players develop the timing of runs to arrive as the ball does
Players finish from different angles and positions created by their movement
Players build the intelligent movement habits of a senior striker
pitchSize: Full penalty area plus wide channels
organisation: Two servers in wide channels (left and right), each with 5 balls. GK in goal. Strikers work in pairs, one striker active one resting. Three movement patterns are established before the drill starts. Strikers rotate between patterns every 5 minutes.
instructions: MOVEMENT 1 - NEAR POST RUN: Striker starts at the far post. Server (right channel) shapes to cross. Striker makes a sharp diagonal run to the near post, arriving as the ball is delivered. Finish: near post redirect or flick past the GK.

MOVEMENT 2 - FAR POST RUN: Striker starts at the near post. Server (left channel) shapes to cross. Striker holds position until server looks up, then makes a curved run to the far post arriving late. Finish: volley or half-volley at the far post.

MOVEMENT 3 - DROP AND TURN: Server plays a pass to the strikers feet from the edge of the area. Striker drops short to receive with back to goal, takes one touch to turn, and finishes in the second touch.
Each movement repeated 5 times before rotating.
After all 3 movements: free movement — server decides which delivery, striker must read it and make the correct run.
GK is live throughout. Count goals from each movement pattern.
coachingPoints: NEAR POST TIMING: the near post run must start LATE. If you run too early the defender tracks you and the space closes. Wait until the server has their head down over the ball.
FAR POST CURVE: the far post run curves around the back of the defensive line. A straight run is easy to track. Curve around and arrive late from the far side.
DROP AND TURN: when dropping to receive, open the hips at 45 degrees so you can see both the server and the goal before the ball arrives. Do not receive chest-on.
FINISH AT NEAR POST: redirect the pace of the cross rather than striking through it. The near post finish uses the balls momentum — a slight touch is often enough.
FINISH AT FAR POST: position the body to volley across the goal (toward the far bottom corner). Do not hit straight back the way the ball came.
READING THE SERVER: in the free phase, read the servers body position, the angle of approach, and the height of the delivery to predict which finish is needed.
ARRIVE LATE, ARRIVE RUNNING: the best striker finishes come from players arriving at pace. A static striker is easy for the GK to cover.
SECOND BALL INSTINCT: after every finish stay alert for a rebound. Follow every shot immediately.
COMMUNICATION: call for the ball only when you are in the correct position. Calling too early signals your run to the defender.
commonMistakes: Starting the near post run too early allowing the defender to track the movement easily
Far post run going in a straight line rather than a curve making it predictable
Receiving the drop pass chest-on making the turn slow and telegraphed
Arriving at the near post stationary rather than at running pace
Finishing straight back at the GK from the far post rather than across goal
Not following up the finish for a rebound
corrections: Early near post run: place a cone at the penalty spot — striker cannot leave it until the server looks down at the ball
Straight far post run: place 2 cones creating a curved gate the striker must run through on the way to the far post
Chest-on receive: place a cone to the side showing the 45-degree receiving angle — striker must face that cone on receive
Stationary at near post: use a gate 2m from the near post — striker must be running through the gate when the ball arrives
Finishing back at GK: place a target cone at the far bottom corner — every far post finish must aim for that cone
No follow-up: place a second ball ready to roll in immediately after every primary shot
progressions: Add a central defender who must be beaten by the movement: tests whether the run is good enough to lose a marker
Two strikers simultaneously: both make runs from different positions — server picks one to cross to
Combination before finish: striker combines with a central midfielder before making the run
Live crossing game: full 6v4 in the penalty area with crosses from both sides
regressions: Walk through each movement pattern at slow pace before adding pace and a live cross
Passive GK — holds position but does not dive — removes the saving element while establishing the movement patterns
One movement only per session until all three are established individually
matchApplication: Striker movement is what creates finishing opportunities. A striker who makes intelligent runs — arriving late, curving around defenders, reading the server — will consistently find themselves in positions to score. The three movements in this drill cover the majority of finishing situations in senior football: near post crosses, far post crosses, and the drop-and-turn from a central pass. Elite strikers like Lewandowski, Kane, and Firmino are exceptional not because of their technical finishing alone but because their movement creates the space and time to finish well. Teaching movement patterns at U17 gives players the foundation for senior striker play.
coachTips: Demonstrate each movement yourself or use a senior player to show the correct run shape before the drill starts.
The timing of the near post run is the most complex concept. Spend extra time on this before the far post and drop-and-turn.
Video the movements from the side. Run shape and timing are most clearly visible from a side-on angle.
U17 strikers respond very well to professional footage. Show Lewandowski near post runs and Kane drop-and-turn movements.
The free phase at the end is the most valuable. This is where the learning transfers — reading the server and making the right decision unprompted.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Two servers shown in wide channels (left and right). Movement 1: striker arrow from far post diagonally to near post (near post run). Movement 2: striker arrow curving from near post around and to far post (curved far post run). Movement 3: striker drops short from central position (backward arrow), receives, turns (curved arrow), and finishes. All three movement arrows shown in different colours. GK shown in goal.

title: Possession Rondo with Transition
objective: Develop high-tempo possession with an immediate transition to attack when the ball is won.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain possession under intense pressure in a 6v3 format
Players develop the instant transition mindset when possession is won or lost
Players build the ability to switch from defensive to attacking mode in one action
Players develop composure in possession and urgency in transition
equipment: Cones for the possession zone and target goals
pitchSize: 20x20m possession zone with two 3m mini-goals 15m beyond each end
organisation: 20x20m square. 6 possession players keep the ball from 3 defenders inside the square. Two mini-goals are placed 15m outside the square on opposite ends. When defenders win the ball they immediately counter-attack toward either mini-goal. Possession team transitions to defend the counter. Swap defenders every 3 minutes or when 3 goals are conceded.
instructions: 6 possession players start with the ball inside the 20x20m zone.
3 defenders press inside the zone trying to win the ball.
Possession team: maximum 2 touches. Keep the ball moving.
Count consecutive passes. Target: 10 consecutive passes earns 1 point.
When a defender wins the ball: immediate transition. All 3 defenders attack toward either mini-goal outside the zone.
The 6 possession players must immediately defend the counter — they have numerical advantage (6v3) but are caught out of shape.
If the defenders score in a mini-goal: 3 points for the defending team.
If the possession team recovers the ball before a goal: they return to the possession phase.
Run for 20 minutes. Track points for both teams.
coachingPoints: POSSESSION MENTALITY: every player without the ball must be moving to create a passing angle. Static players make possession impossible under a 3-player press.
2-TOUCH DISCIPLINE: receive and release within 2 touches. Every extra touch gives a defender time to close and intercept.
TRANSITION TRIGGER: the moment the ball is lost every possession player must immediately sprint to a defensive position. Do not watch the ball being lost — react instantly.
DEFENSIVE TRANSITION SHAPE: when the defenders break out, the 6 possession players must not all chase the ball. Two players press the ball, four hold a defensive shape to cover the mini-goals.
COUNTER-ATTACK SPEED: when the defenders win the ball they must attack at maximum speed before the possession team organises. The first 3 seconds after winning the ball are the most dangerous.
PASSING ANGLES IN POSSESSION: always offer the ball-carrier 3 passing options. One short, one medium, one long. Never leave them with only one option.
COMMUNICATION IN BOTH PHASES: possession phase requires calling for the ball. Defensive transition requires calling positions and cover.
COMPOSURE UNDER PRESS: the 3-player press will feel intense. Slow down your decision-making, not your movement. Think before receiving, move quickly after.
WINNING THE SECOND BALL: after a counter-attack is stopped, the ball is contested. Both teams must immediately compete for the second ball with equal intensity.
commonMistakes: Possession players standing still and waiting for the ball rather than creating passing angles
Taking more than 2 touches under pressure giving the defenders time to intercept
Slow defensive transition — watching the ball being lost instead of immediately sprinting to defend
All 6 possession players chasing the counter-attack ball rather than holding a defensive shape
Counter-attack played too slowly giving the possession team time to reorganise
corrections: Static in possession: add a rule that possession players must take at least one step before every receive
Too many touches: strict 2-touch rule — third touch means the possession team loses 1 point
Slow transition: freeze the game at the moment of ball loss and ask: who moved immediately and who watched?
All chasing counter: designate 2 players as immediate pressers and 4 as shape-holders before the drill starts
Slow counter: add a 5-second rule — if the counter does not reach the mini-goal zone within 5 seconds it is called back
progressions: Reduce to 6v4: one more defender makes the possession phase harder and the counter-attack more dangerous
1-touch possession: all passes must be first-time, creating the highest-tempo version of the drill
Directional counter: defenders can only attack one specific mini-goal, creating a predictable threat the possession team must organise against
Full pitch version: possession in the centre circle, counter-attack toward full-size goals
regressions: Allow 3 touches in the possession phase before reducing to 2
Remove the counter-attack for the first 5 minutes — pure possession only until the possession phase is working
Increase the zone to 25x25m to give more time and space in possession
matchApplication: Possession with transition is the tactical core of modern football. The best teams in the world are dangerous in two opposite moments: when they have the ball (possession) and the instant they win it (transition). The 6v3 rondo with counter-attack directly replicates the central midfield battle in a match — keeping the ball under pressure and instantly attacking when possession is regained. The transition element is what makes this drill match-realistic rather than simply a possession exercise. Teams that practice this specific combination develop the dual mentality of composure in possession and aggression in transition.
coachTips: The transition moment is the most important coaching opportunity in the drill. Pause the game immediately after a transition and debrief both teams.
The points system must be maintained throughout. Without scoring, the intensity drops and the drill becomes a casual possession exercise.
Watch the possession team shape during the counter-attack. Do they hold a defensive structure or all chase? This is the most common organisational error.
At U15 this drill will feel chaotic in the first session. Let it breathe before coaching. Observe first, then give specific feedback.
Connect to a professional match: show footage of a team winning the ball and immediately counter-attacking before the opposition can organise.
diagramDescription: 20x20m square (cone corners). 6 possession players (circles) spread across the zone. 3 defenders (triangles) pressing inside. Ball shown in possession with passing angles to 3 nearby players. Outside the zone: two mini-goals (2m wide) shown 15m beyond each end of the square. Counter-attack arrows show defenders breaking out of the zone toward a mini-goal with possession players transitioning to defend. Points system shown beside the diagram.

title: High Press Defensive Shape
objective: Develop a coordinated high press from a 4-4-2 shape that wins the ball in the opponents half.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the 4-4-2 high press shape and their individual role within it
Players can execute a coordinated press within 2 seconds of a trigger
Players develop compactness and cover during the press
Players learn what to do when the press is beaten and how to recover
pitchSize: Full pitch, focusing on the opponents half
organisation: Ball-playing team starts with the GK or a CB in their own half. Pressing team sets up in a 4-4-2 shape in the opponents half, positioned at the halfway line. Coach establishes the pressing triggers before the drill starts. The ball-playing team tries to advance into the pressing teams half and score in mini-goals. The pressing team tries to win the ball high and score in the ball-playing teams mini-goal.
instructions: ESTABLISH THE SHAPE: pressing team lines up in 4-4-2. Two forwards at the top, four midfielders in a line behind, four defenders behind them. All positioned at the halfway line.
Ball-playing team starts with their GK or CB. They try to play out from the back.
PRESS TRIGGER: the moment the ball goes to a CB or is played backward to the GK, the nearest forward presses immediately.
When the first forward presses: the second forward covers the other CB. The two wide midfielders push up to press the full-backs. The two central midfielders hold their line.
The press is a 4-player action: 2 forwards and 2 wide midfielders. The back 4 hold shape and do not press.
If the press wins the ball in the opponents half: immediate attack toward the ball-playing teams mini-goal.
If the ball escapes the press: all 4 press players immediately drop back to their defensive shape.
Phase 2: ball-playing team is coached to play through the press deliberately — tests whether the press can adapt.
Phase 3: full 9v9 game with the high press as the defensive strategy.
coachingPoints: PRESS TRIGGER RECOGNITION: every player must recognise the trigger simultaneously. If one player presses and others do not react, the press is beaten easily.
FORWARD PRESS ANGLE: the first forward closes the CB at an angle that cuts off the pass back to the GK. Do not run directly at the CB.
WIDE MIDFIELDER ROLE: when the press is triggered the wide midfielder on the side of the ball must sprint to press the near full-back. This covers the most common press escape route.
COMPACTNESS: the 4 pressing players must stay within 10m of each other. If the shape spreads wider than 10m the gaps between pressers are exploitable.
THE BACK 4 HOLD: the back 4 must resist the urge to press forward. Their role is to hold the defensive line and cover the space behind the press.
PRESS DURATION: the press lasts a maximum of 5 seconds. If the ball is not won, all 4 press players drop immediately to their shape.
TRANSITION AFTER WINNING: when the press wins the ball the 2 forwards immediately attack. The 2 wide midfielders support wide. Speed of transition is critical.
COMMUNICATION: the first forward to press shouts PRESS. This triggers all other press players to move simultaneously.
PRESS OR HOLD DECISION: not every situation warrants a press. If the ball-playing team has already escaped the press trigger zone, the pressing team holds shape rather than pressing randomly.
commonMistakes: Only one player pressing while others watch rather than reacting simultaneously
First forward running directly at the CB rather than at an angle to cut off the GK pass
Back 4 pressing forward to join the press, leaving space in behind
Press continuing for more than 5 seconds after failing to win the ball
No communication — silent press means the reactions are individual not collective
Wide midfielders not pressing the full-backs, leaving the most common escape route open
corrections: Only one pressing: freeze the game and show the group which 4 players should be pressing and where each should be
Direct run at CB: place a cone between the presser and the CB showing the angled approach direction
Back 4 pressing: place a cone line at the halfway line — back 4 cannot cross it during the press
Long press: use a stopwatch. Press must be completed or abandoned within 5 seconds
No communication: the press does not start until PRESS is called aloud
Wide midfielder not pressing: assign the wide midfielder a specific target (the full-back) and make it their only job during the press
progressions: Ball-playing team coached to play through the press — tests the pressing teams ability to adapt
Add a trigger call from the coach: coach calls PRESS at random moments forcing the team to react to an external trigger
Full 11v11 match with the high press as the mandatory defensive strategy
Press from different starting positions: pressing team starts in a mid-block and transitions to a high press on a trigger
regressions: Walk through the press shape at slow motion with no ball before any live reps
Passive ball-playing team — they do not try to play through the press — allows the shape to be established
Reduce to 2 forwards only pressing before adding the wide midfielders
matchApplication: The coordinated high press is one of the most powerful defensive tactics in modern football. When executed correctly it forces opponents into mistakes in dangerous areas, winning the ball close to the opponents goal where counter-attacks are immediately threatening. Teams like Liverpool under Klopp, Dortmund under Klopp, and Bayer Leverkusen under Alonso built their entire identity around the coordinated high press. The 4-4-2 pressing shape is the most commonly used in professional football because it creates natural pressing lanes that are difficult for ball-playing teams to escape. Teaching it at U16 gives players 2+ years to master the concept before senior football.
coachTips: Do not start with the live press. Spend 10 minutes walking through the shape and the trigger reaction with no ball.
The communication habit (calling PRESS) is the most important thing to establish in session 1. Without it the press is a collection of individuals not a coordinated unit.
Film from above if possible. The shape of the press and the gaps between pressers are only clearly visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional footage: show a Liverpool or Dortmund high press in slow motion identifying each players role.
