At U16 this concept takes 4-6 sessions to become automatic. Be patient and consistent in the first sessions.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Ball-playing team in their own half with GK and back 4. Pressing team shown in 4-4-2 shape at the halfway line: 2 forwards at top, 4 midfielders in a horizontal line, 4 defenders in a line behind. Press trigger shown: ball goes to CB (ball-playing team). First forward shown closing at angle (arrow cutting off GK pass). Second forward shown covering other CB. Two wide midfielders shown sprinting to press full-backs. Two central midfielders shown holding position. Back 4 shown holding the halfway line. Compact 10m press width labeled.

title: Counter Attack 3v2
objective: Develop the speed, decision-making, and finishing quality needed to convert a 3v2 counter-attack into a goal.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the 3v2 counter-attack structure and their role within it
Players can maintain width and depth during a counter-attack
Players develop the decision to shoot or pass based on the defenders positions
Players build the urgency and directness needed in counter-attacking play
players: Groups of 5 (3 attackers, 2 defenders) plus GK. Multiple groups.
organisation: 3 attackers start at one end. 2 defenders start 10m ahead of them. GK in the full-size goal at the far end. On the coaches signal the 3 attackers advance and try to score. The 2 defenders retreat and try to prevent a goal. After every attempt all 5 players return to start and reset.
instructions: SETUP: ball-carrier in the centre with one attacker wide left and one wide right. Both defenders starting 10m ahead.
On GO: central attacker drives forward with the ball. Wide attackers sprint forward maintaining width (stay near the touchlines).
Defenders retreat backward, staying goal-side. They cannot tackle until the ball enters the final 20m.
Ball-carrier assesses: if a wide attacker is free — pass and support. If both defenders commit centrally — shoot.
Rule: at least one pass must be made before shooting in phase 1.
Phase 2 after 8 minutes: defenders can press immediately from the start. No delayed press.
Phase 3: the ball-carrier can shoot from anywhere if the angle is on. Free decision-making.
Count goals. Award 2 points for a goal from a pass and 1 point for a solo goal. This encourages the team play.
coachingPoints: WIDTH IS EVERYTHING: wide attackers must stay wide — within 3m of the touchline. Narrow attackers make the defenders job easy because they only need to cover a small area.
CENTRAL ATTACKER DRIVES FORWARD: the ball-carrier must drive at pace toward goal. A slow counter-attack allows the defenders to organise and the GK to set.
OUTNUMBER THE DEFENDERS: in a 3v2, one attacker is always free. The job is to find them quickly. If the defenders split to cover wide, shoot. If they stay narrow, pass wide.
PASS OR SHOOT DECISION: look at the defenders hips. If both defenders are facing the ball-carrier: pass wide. If one has moved to cover wide: shoot.
TIMING OF THE WIDE PASS: the pass to a wide attacker should be played when they are in a shooting position, not when they are still 20m from goal.
SUPPORT AFTER PASSING: when the ball-carrier passes wide they must immediately support — move to a position to receive a return pass or attack the far post.
DIRECTNESS: counter-attacks must be direct. No sideways passes, no slowing down, no waiting. Every touch must advance toward goal.
FINISHING: the quality of the final finish must be as high as in any other finishing drill. The counter-attack creates the chance but the finish wins the game.
GK PRESSURE: the GK will rush out on a 3v2. The wide attacker receiving must be aware of this and choose to shoot early or pass if the GK commits.
commonMistakes: Wide attackers drifting narrow toward the ball, reducing the width and making the 3v2 a 3v2 in a small area
Ball-carrier slowing down or dribbling sideways rather than driving directly forward
Passing too early when the wide attacker is not yet in a shooting position
Taking too many touches and allowing the defenders to recover their shape
Finishing poorly under the pressure of the counter-attack situation
corrections: Narrow wide attackers: place touchline cones that wide attackers must stay outside of throughout the counter-attack
Slow ball-carrier: add a 10-second rule — the counter must result in a shot within 10 seconds of GO
Early pass: place a shooting zone cone 20m from goal — wide attacker must be inside it before receiving the final pass
Too many touches: add a 3-touch maximum for the ball-carrier in phase 1
Poor finish: add a dedicated 5-minute finishing session before the counter-attack drill to warm up the finishing quality
progressions: 3v3: a third defender joins after the attackers cross the halfway line — tests whether the 3v2 advantage can be used before it becomes 3v3
Counter from a defensive action: the drill starts with the GK saving a shot and distributing quickly — replicates the match trigger for a counter
Full pitch counter: attackers start from their own half and must cover the full pitch before finishing
Time challenge: 3v2 must result in a goal attempt within 8 seconds of GO
regressions: 2 defenders passive — they hold position but do not tackle — allows attackers to establish the movement pattern
Reduce to 2v1 first to establish the simpler overload decision before adding the third attacker
No GK in phase 1 — open goal finishing to build confidence before adding GK pressure
matchApplication: The 3v2 counter-attack is the highest-value attacking situation in football after a 2v1. When a team wins the ball and has three attackers against two defenders, with space behind the defensive line, the probability of scoring is extremely high — if the counter-attack is executed correctly. Width, directness, and the correct pass-or-shoot decision are the three elements that determine whether the chance is converted. Every elite team trains counter-attacks specifically because the transition from defence to attack is where the most dangerous chances are created. At U14 the decision-making speed needed for counter-attacks is beginning to develop. This drill builds the spatial awareness and urgency that make counter-attacks effective.
coachTips: The width coaching point is the most important. Stop every rep where the wide attackers drift narrow and physically reset them to the correct position.
The pass-or-shoot decision is the highest-level cognitive element. Ask the ball-carrier to narrate their decision: I passed because both defenders covered wide. I shot because they stayed narrow.
Keep the reps high — every group should get 8-10 attempts in 20 minutes. Counter-attack quality improves rapidly with repetition.
The points system (2 for a pass-then-goal, 1 for a solo goal) is important. Without it players will always shoot selfishly regardless of the defensive shape.
Connect to match footage: show a professional 3v2 counter-attack and pause at the decision moment — identify which attacker was free and whether the correct decision was made.
diagramDescription: 60x40m area with full-size goal at one end and GK. 3 attackers shown at the start: central ball-carrier (circle with ball) and two wide attackers (circles) near the touchlines. 2 defenders (triangles) shown 10m ahead retreating goal-side. Arrows show: central attacker driving forward (straight arrow), wide attackers sprinting wide and forward (diagonal arrows). Decision moment shown: if defenders split to cover wide — shoot arrow from central. If defenders stay central — pass arrow to wide attacker with finish arrow to goal.

title: Movement off the Ball in the Final Third
objective: Develop intelligent off-ball movement in the final third to create and exploit space for teammates.
learningOutcomes: Players understand how their movement creates space for teammates
Players can execute 3 specific off-ball movements: the diagonal run, the overlap, and the underlap
Players time their runs to arrive in space as the ball arrives
Players develop the habit of constantly moving when they do not have the ball
organisation: Final third setup. A wide player (winger) starts with the ball wide right. A central striker is in the central area. A central midfielder is at the top of the area. A full-back overlaps from behind. Two passive defenders hold positions in the area. GK in goal.
instructions: MOVEMENT 1 - DIAGONAL RUN: Winger (right) has the ball. Striker makes a diagonal run from central area toward the near post, dragging the centre-back. This creates space in behind for the overlapping full-back.
Winger plays the ball to the overlapping full-back who crosses into the space vacated by the striker.
MOVEMENT 2 - OVERLAP: Winger cuts inside with the ball. Full-back makes an overlapping run around the outside of the winger. Winger plays to the overlapping full-back who crosses.

MOVEMENT 3 - UNDERLAP: Winger is on the ball wide right. Central midfielder makes a run between the winger and the central striker (underlapping run into the box). Winger plays the ball into the underlapping midfielders run.
Practice each movement 5 times then combine freely.
Phase 2: add active defenders who can press. Players must read the defensive shape and choose the correct movement.
Phase 3: full small-sided game (5v5) in the final third where all three movements are encouraged.
coachingPoints: MOVEMENT CREATES SPACE: every run you make either creates space for yourself or for a teammate. A diagonal run by the striker drags a defender and opens space for the overlap.

DIAGONAL RUN TIMING: the striker makes the diagonal run at the moment the winger receives the ball — not before, not after. Too early and the defender recovers. Too late and the space is already open but the pass is already played.
OVERLAP TRIGGER: the full-back starts the overlap run when the winger cuts inside. The cutting inside movement is the trigger for the overlap.

UNDERLAP ANGLE: the underlapping midfielders run goes between the winger and the penalty area, through the channel between the full-back and the centre-back. The angle is inside and forward — not wide.
COMMUNICATION: the player making the run must signal (verbal or visual) to the ball-carrier so the ball-carrier knows the option is available.
THIRD MAN AWARENESS: always be aware of who the third man is. The winger passes to the full-back (movement 1). Who is the third man? The striker arriving at the far post from the diagonal run.
CONSTANT MOVEMENT: players without the ball must never stand still in the final third. Defenders cannot track a player who is constantly moving.
QUALITY OF THE FINAL BALL: movement creates the opportunity but the final ball must be quality. An overlap with a poor cross wastes all the work done by the movement.
commonMistakes: Making runs too early before the trigger movement has happened
Straight runs instead of diagonal runs — straight runs are easy for defenders to track
Not communicating the run to the ball-carrier so the opportunity is missed
Ball-carrier not seeing or using the movement made by teammates
Overlap running too wide — running down the touchline rather than at an angle toward the goal
corrections: Early runs: establish a clear visual trigger for each movement — winger receives the ball (movement 1 trigger), winger cuts inside (movement 2 trigger)
Straight runs: place a cone at the end of the diagonal showing the correct angle — run must finish at the cone
No communication: make communication mandatory — run cannot start without a verbal call
Ball-carrier not seeing movement: add a rule that the ball-carrier must look over both shoulders before playing the ball
Overlap too wide: place an inner cone channel showing the angle of the overlap toward goal rather than toward the touchline
progressions: Active defenders: defenders can now press and track runs — tests whether the movement is quick and disguised enough
Combination before movement: add a one-two or third-man combination before the final movement
Full 6v5 in the final third: free movement, players choose which of the three movements to make based on the situation
Shooting from all movements: every movement must end with a shot on goal
regressions: Walk through each movement with no defenders before adding any pressure
One movement only per session until that movement is automatic
Passive defenders who hold position but do not track runs
matchApplication: Off-ball movement is what separates good attacking teams from great ones. The team that moves constantly creates constant problems for defenders who cannot track multiple runs simultaneously. Every goal in professional football is preceded by off-ball movement that created the space. The diagonal run, overlap, and underlap are the three most commonly used movements in the final third at elite level. Teaching players to recognise the trigger for each movement and time it correctly develops the attacking intelligence that is the hallmark of technically sophisticated teams.
coachTips: Spend time before the drill explaining what each movement is trying to achieve — what space it creates and who benefits from it.
The ball-carrier coaching is as important as the runner coaching. If the ball-carrier does not see or use the run it does not matter how good the run is.
Film from above for this drill. The space created by diagonal runs is only clearly visible from above.
Connect to professional footage: show a winger cutting inside to create an overlap, or a striker making a diagonal run to create space for a midfielder.
The free phase (phase 3) is where genuine learning happens. Let it run without too much interruption — observe and give specific feedback at the end.
diagramDescription: Final third (35x40m) with full-size goal. Winger shown wide right with ball. Movement 1: diagonal run arrow from striker (central) toward near post. Space created shown (dotted box). Overlapping full-back arrow around the outside running into the space. Movement 2: winger cut-inside arrow, full-back overlap arrow around the outside. Movement 3: underlapping midfielder arrow between the winger and the box, through the channel. Each movement shown in a different colour with the ball path shown as a dashed arrow.

title: Strength and Balance for Football
objective: Develop functional football strength and balance through bodyweight exercises specific to football movement patterns.
learningOutcomes: Players develop single-leg strength and balance needed for kicking and shielding
Players build core stability that supports all football movement
Players develop the physical resilience to withstand physical challenges in matches
Players understand the connection between physical strength and football performance
organisation: 5 stations set up in a circuit. 2-3 players per station. 40 seconds work, 20 seconds rest and rotate. 3 full circuits. Coach demonstrates each exercise before starting.
instructions: STATION 1 - SINGLE LEG SQUAT: Stand on one leg, arms out for balance. Slowly lower the body by bending the standing knee to 90 degrees then return. 10 reps each leg. Builds single-leg strength for kicking and changing direction.

STATION 2 - NORDIC HAMSTRING CURL: Kneel on the ground, partner holds ankles. Slowly lower the upper body toward the ground controlling the descent with the hamstrings. Push back up with hands. 6-8 reps. Builds hamstring strength critical for sprint injury prevention.

STATION 3 - LATERAL BAND WALK: Place resistance band around ankles. Walk sideways 10 steps right, 10 steps left in a semi-squat position. Builds hip abductor strength for lateral defending and directional change.

STATION 4 - PLANK WITH HIP DIP: Hold a plank position. Slowly rotate the hips to touch the right hip to the floor then left. 10 each side. Builds rotational core stability used in every kicking and turning action.

STATION 5 - SINGLE LEG BALANCE WITH BALL TOSS: Stand on one leg. Partner throws a ball at different heights and angles. Catch and throw back while maintaining single-leg balance. 20 catches each leg. Builds balance and coordination under distraction.
After 3 circuits: 5 minutes of football activity (passing or dribbling) to apply the strength in a football context.
coachingPoints: SINGLE LEG SQUAT FORM: keep the knee of the standing leg in line with the toes. Do not let the knee cave inward. Control the descent slowly — 3 seconds down, 1 second up.
NORDIC CURL CONTROL: the descent must be controlled by the hamstrings, not gravity. A slow controlled lowering is the exercise. Dropping fast is not.
LATERAL WALK POSTURE: stay in a semi-squat throughout the lateral walk. Standing upright removes the exercise from the hip abductors. Stay low.
PLANK STABILITY: the hips should only dip 5-10cm. Do not let them drop to the floor. Core tension must be maintained throughout.
BALANCE AND DISTRACTION: in station 5, the balance leg must not move when catching and throwing. Any wobble means the core and ankle stability need more work.
BREATHING: exhale on the effort phase of every exercise. Inhale on the recovery phase. Holding the breath increases tension and reduces performance.
QUALITY OVER QUANTITY: 6 perfect Nordic curls are worth more than 10 sloppy ones. Stop any player whose form breaks down and correct before continuing.

CONNECTION TO FOOTBALL: remind players after each station what football action the exercise directly supports — single leg squat supports shooting and shielding, Nordic curl prevents hamstring injury in sprinting.
commonMistakes: Knee caving inward on single-leg squat reducing effectiveness and increasing injury risk
Dropping fast on the Nordic curl instead of controlling the descent slowly
Standing upright during the lateral band walk removing the hip abductor challenge
Hips dropping too low in the plank hip dip losing core tension
Moving the balance foot during station 5 instead of absorbing through the ankle and core
corrections: Knee caving: place a resistance band just above the knee. The band provides feedback when the knee caves inward
Fast Nordic drop: use a count — 3 seconds down minimum. Coach counts aloud for the group
Upright lateral walk: place a cone at waist height beside the player — they must stay below the cone height throughout
Plank hips too low: place a foam roller under the hips — hips should only just touch it on the dip
Moving balance foot: draw a circle around the balance foot with chalk — foot cannot leave the circle during the ball toss
progressions: Add weight: hold a ball or light medicine ball during single-leg squats and plank exercises
Increase Nordic rep count to 10 after 4 sessions of 6-8
Single leg squat with eyes closed: removes visual balance feedback forcing greater proprioceptive challenge
Add a football element to each station: control a ball at the top of the single-leg squat, head a ball during the balance station
regressions: Single-leg squat reduced to 45 degrees only for players who cannot yet reach 90 degrees
Nordic curl with hands immediately on ground if unable to control the full descent
Remove the resistance band from station 3 for younger players
matchApplication: Football-specific strength is the foundation of physical performance in matches. Single-leg strength determines the power of a shot, the ability to shield the ball under physical pressure, and the stability needed when changing direction at speed. Hamstring strength from Nordic curls is the single most evidence-based injury prevention exercise in football — teams that perform Nordic curls regularly have significantly fewer hamstring injuries. Core stability from the plank supports every passing, shooting, and heading action. Teaching these exercises at U14, when players are beginning their physical development period, establishes the strength foundation that will support their football performance for their entire career.
coachTips: Nordic hamstring curls are uncomfortable. Warn players before they start and encourage them through it. The discomfort is normal and the exercise is extremely valuable.
Demonstrate every exercise before players attempt it. Poor form on strength exercises can cause injury.
Build this circuit into the warm-down or as a standalone session 2-3 times per week during pre-season.
The connection to football must be made explicit at U14. Tell players: this exercise makes your shot harder and this exercise means you will not pull a hamstring chasing a through ball.
Track improvement: record how many Nordic curls each player can do in 40 seconds in session 1. Show the improvement after 6 sessions.
diagramDescription: 20x10m circuit area with 5 stations marked by cones. Station 1 (single-leg squat): figure shown on one leg bending to 90 degrees with arrows showing descent and return. Station 2 (Nordic curl): figure shown kneeling with partner holding ankles, lowering forward with hamstring tension arrow. Station 3 (lateral band walk): figure shown in semi-squat with band at ankles, lateral arrows. Station 4 (plank hip dip): figure in plank with hip rotation arrows left and right. Station 5 (balance with ball toss): figure on one leg with partner throwing ball at different heights. Circuit rotation arrow shown.

title: Penalty Technique and Routine
objective: Develop a consistent penalty technique and pre-kick routine that produces accurate, confident penalty kicks.
learningOutcomes: Players can strike a penalty accurately to any designated corner
Players develop a consistent pre-kick routine that replicates match conditions
Players understand the psychological elements of penalty taking
Players build the composure to execute under the pressure of a live GK
organisation: One player at the penalty spot at a time. GK in goal. Other players wait behind the penalty spot in a queue. Each player takes 3 penalties per rotation, targeting different corners. After each rotation players discuss and evaluate their routine.
instructions: ESTABLISH THE ROUTINE before any kicking: every player chooses and commits to their own routine. Suggested routine: place the ball, take 4 steps back and 2 to the side, look at the target corner, deep breath, short run-up, strike.
PHASE 1 - Unopposed corner practice (5 minutes): No GK. Player calls their target corner aloud before kicking. Ball must hit or enter that corner. Focus is purely on technique and accuracy.
PHASE 2 - GK but routine focus (8 minutes): GK is live. Player must complete their full routine before every kick — no skipping steps. GK can move after the player begins their run-up but not before.
PHASE 3 - Pressure kicks (7 minutes): after every miss the player does 10 press-ups before their next attempt. After every goal the player earns 2 points. Creates consequence and simulates pressure.
Between phases: group discussion — what worked in the routine, what was rushed, how did the GK affect the decision?
coachingPoints: DECIDE BEFORE STEPPING BACK: the corner target must be chosen before the routine begins. Never change the decision once the routine starts — changing decision mid-kick is the most common cause of missed penalties.
THE ROUTINE IS THE ANCHOR: the routine creates the mental and physical state needed to kick accurately. Every step of the routine must be the same every time. Rushing any step is a warning sign.

PLACEMENT vs POWER: 80 percent of penalties are saved because the ball goes centrally or the routine breaks down — not because the GK guessed correctly. A well-placed kick to the corner does not need to be powerful.
GK MOVEMENT: do not look at the GK during the run-up. Focus on the chosen corner. GK movement is designed to distract — eyes on the corner not the GK.
BREATHING: take a deep breath at the start of the run-up. This slows the heart rate and reduces muscle tension. A tense body produces a poor kick.
CONTACT POINT: for a placed penalty, use the inside of the foot to the opposite corner. For a driven penalty, use the laces through the centre of the ball with the ankle locked.
STANDING FOOT BESIDE THE BALL: plant foot beside the ball pointing at the target corner. This is the primary accuracy control for penalty kicks.
CONFIDENCE POSTURE: approach the ball with an upright, confident posture. Hunched shoulders and a slow approach signal nerves to the GK. Walk confidently.
AFTER THE KICK: commit to following up the kick immediately regardless of whether it looks like a goal or a miss. Second-chance penalty rebound goals exist.
commonMistakes: Changing the target corner during the run-up after seeing the GK move
Rushing the pre-kick routine, skipping steps under pressure
Looking at the GK during the run-up instead of the target corner
Trying to kick too hard causing loss of accuracy and control
Plant foot pointing in the wrong direction sending the ball wide
corrections: Changing decision: ask the player to point to their target corner before beginning the routine. Once pointed, it cannot change.
Rushing routine: count the routine steps aloud as a group — 1 (place ball), 2 (step back), 3 (look at corner), 4 (breath), 5 (run), 6 (kick). All 6 must be heard.
Looking at GK: put a sticker on the target corner. Players must focus on the sticker throughout the approach.
Kicking too hard: remove power as a goal — accuracy only. Ball must end up within 50cm of the post to count.
Plant foot wrong direction: draw an arrow on the ground pointing at the target corner where the plant foot goes
progressions: Sudden death shootout: two players alternate penalties. First to miss loses. Winner stays on.
Blind GK: GK does not watch the approach and only reacts to the kick — tests accuracy without the distraction element
Team shootout: 5 players each take 1 penalty. Best team score from 5 wins.
Pressure simulation: other players stand in a line watching and must be silent — replicates the match environment
regressions: No GK in phase 1 — corner target only — removes the GK distraction until the technique is established
Reduce to one target corner only (always bottom right) until accuracy is consistent before adding choice
Allow a longer run-up for players who struggle with the standard 4-step approach
matchApplication: Penalties decide approximately 30 percent of knockout matches in professional and youth football. A team with 4-5 reliable penalty takers has a significant advantage in cup competitions and championship shootouts. The most important element is not the technique but the routine — the pre-kick process that creates the mental state needed to kick accurately under extreme pressure. The greatest penalty takers (Ronaldo, Messi, Ramos) all have completely consistent routines that never change regardless of the match situation. Teaching a routine at U13 gives players years to automate it before the highest-pressure situations.
coachTips: The psychological element of penalties is as important as the technique. Discuss the mental side with U13 players — they are ready for this conversation.
Never mock or criticise a missed penalty in training. The fear of failure in a real shootout comes from negative experiences in training.
Have every player develop and write down their own routine in session 1. Seeing it written reinforces it.
The group discussion between phases is valuable. Players learn from each other about what worked and what did not.
End every penalty session with a successful kick for every player. Never let a player finish on a miss.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Penalty spot marked. Player shown at the start of the routine: ball placed on the spot, player 4 steps back and 2 to the side (position shown with small X). Target corners shown in the goal (bottom left, bottom right, top left, top right) with letters A, B, C, D. Arrow from player position to ball showing the run-up direction. Arrow from ball to bottom right corner (B) showing the penalty direction. GK shown in diving position on the opposite side.

title: Throw-In Routines
objective: Develop correct throw-in technique and two simple throw-in routines to maintain possession from restarts.
learningOutcomes: Players can perform a legal throw-in with correct foot position and release
Players understand two throw-in routines for keeping possession
Players develop the habit of creating a target before taking the throw
Players learn when to use a short throw versus a long throw
equipment: Cones to mark the touchline and target zones
organisation: Groups of 4 along the touchline. One player is the thrower. Two players are receivers in front of the thrower. One player is a passive defender. Thrower stands on or behind the touchline. Rotate roles every 3 minutes.
instructions: TECHNIQUE (3 minutes): Thrower practices the basic throw-in. Both feet on or behind the line. Ball held with both hands behind the head. Full arc from behind the head, release above the head. Ball must travel in a straight line from behind the head.

ROUTINE 1 - DROP AND RETURN: Receiver A stands 3m ahead. Receiver A takes one step toward the thrower (short movement), receives the throw, and immediately plays a return pass to a third player who runs past. The drop movement freezes the passive defender for 1 second.

ROUTINE 2 - OVERLAP: Receiver A stands 5m ahead. Receiver B stands behind Receiver A. Receiver A makes a run toward the thrower (drawing the defender). Thrower plays to Receiver B who has run into the space left by A. B plays forward.
Practice each routine 5 times before rotating the thrower.
Phase 2 after 8 minutes: add an active defender who tries to intercept the throw.
Final 3 minutes: free throw-in — thrower chooses routine 1 or 2 based on the defenders position.
coachingPoints: LEGAL THROW-IN: both feet must be on or behind the touchline at the moment of release. Stepping over the line is a foul throw. Check feet position before every throw.
RELEASE POINT: the ball must be released from above and behind the head in a continuous arc. Releasing from the side or stopping the arc mid-throw is illegal.
BOTH HANDS EQUAL: the throw must have equal force from both hands. A dominant-hand throw that curves sideways is illegal and inaccurate.
TARGET BEFORE THROW: look at your target receiver before picking up the ball. Know where you are throwing before you start the routine.
ROUTINE 1 TIMING: Receiver A must make the drop movement at the moment the thrower picks up the ball — this is what draws the defender. Too early and the defender recovers.
ROUTINE 2 OVERLAP: Receiver B must time the run into space to arrive as the throw is made. Starting the run too early allows the defender to track both players.
SHORT vs LONG: use the short routine (Routine 1) when closely defended. Use the longer routine (Routine 2) when there is space ahead.
QUICK RESTART: throw-ins should be taken quickly to catch the opposition out of position. Pick the ball up and throw within 5 seconds when a simple option is available.
commonMistakes: One or both feet over the touchline at the moment of release (foul throw)
Releasing the ball from the side rather than from above the head
Not looking at the target before beginning the throw
Receiver moving too early before the thrower has picked up the ball
Throwing to a heavily marked player instead of using the routine to create space
corrections: Foul throw feet: place a tape line on the ground. Both feet must be behind it — pause before throwing and check feet
Side release: ask the thrower to touch both hands together above and behind the head before beginning the arc — this resets the starting position
Not looking at target: add a rule that the thrower must call the name of the receiver before picking up the ball
Receiver early movement: add a verbal trigger — receiver only moves when they hear the thrower say GO
Throwing to marked player: freeze the game when this happens and ask: which receiver was free and which routine would have found them?
progressions: Long throw: introduce the long throw technique — step back from the line, run up, and throw with maximum distance
Active defenders: both defenders are now active and can press the throw
Combination after throw: the receiver must complete a 2-touch combination with a third player before the team advances
Throw-in into the box: practice throw-ins in the final third near the penalty area with attacking runners
regressions: No defender for the first 5 minutes — pure technique practice with no pressure
Reduce to 1 routine only (Routine 1) before introducing Routine 2
Thrower can use both a throw and a kick-in (remove the legal throw pressure) to establish the routine before adding the technique focus
matchApplication: Throw-ins are one of the most common restarts in football and one of the most wasted. Most youth teams simply throw to the nearest player without a plan, often giving possession back to the opposition. A team with two reliable throw-in routines can consistently retain possession from restarts and create attacking opportunities from areas of the pitch where the opposition does not expect danger. At U12 the throw-in routine is an achievable and immediately match-applicable learning outcome that every player can contribute to regardless of their technical level.
coachTips: Foul throws are extremely common at U12. Spend the first 3 minutes purely on legal throw-in technique before introducing the routines.
The quick restart element (5-second rule) should be introduced from the start. Slow throw-ins give the opposition time to organise.
Both routines should feel natural and automatic by session 3. Use them in practice matches immediately to reinforce the learning.
Celebrate successful routines in matches. When a throw-in routine results in a goal or a dangerous attack, make the connection explicit.
Check feet position on every throw in the first session. Foul throws are a habit that is hard to break once established.
diagramDescription: 20x15m area along a touchline. Touchline shown as a solid vertical line. Thrower (square) behind the line. Routine 1: Receiver A shown 3m ahead making a drop step toward the thrower (short arrow back), receiving throw (arc arrow), playing to Receiver C who runs past (forward arrow). Passive defender shown frozen by the drop movement. Routine 2: Receiver A shown making a run toward the thrower (drawing the defender). Receiver B shown running into the space left by A. Throw arc shown to B. B plays forward. Both routines shown in separate small diagrams.

title: Cruyff Turn Foundations
objective: Teach the Cruyff turn as a fundamental skill for changing direction away from a defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the Cruyff turn with both feet
Players understand when to use the Cruyff turn in a match
Players develop deceptive body shape before the turn
Players can accelerate away after the turn
equipment: Cones to mark personal space (5x5m each)
organisation: Players in individual 5x5m squares. Coach demonstrates the full sequence slowly before players attempt. All practice simultaneously. A passive partner stands at one cone to represent a defender in the final phase.
instructions: Start with the ball at your feet, facing one end of your square.
Shape as if you are going to pass or shoot forward with your right foot. Plant the right foot beside the ball as normal.
Instead of kicking through the ball, drag it behind your standing leg using the inside of your right foot.
The ball travels behind your left leg and ends up on the other side of your body.
Turn your body 180 degrees to follow the ball and accelerate in the opposite direction.
Practice this right-foot Cruyff turn for 3 minutes: shape to pass, drag behind, turn, go.
After 3 minutes switch to the left foot: shape with left, drag behind with inside of left, turn right.
After 3 minutes: add movement. Dribble slowly toward one cone, execute the Cruyff turn at the cone, dribble to the opposite cone, turn again. Continuous.
Final 4 minutes: a passive partner stands at one cone. Player dribbles toward them, executes the Cruyff turn just before reaching them, and dribbles away.
Swap partner and player roles every 2 minutes.
coachingPoints: THE FAKE: the fake pass or shot must be convincing. If the body shape does not look like a real pass, the defender will not react and the turn loses effectiveness.
PLANT FOOT POSITION: the plant foot goes beside the ball exactly as it would for a real pass. This is what sells the deception.
THE DRAG: use the INSIDE of the kicking foot to drag the ball. The foot hooks around the ball and pulls it behind the standing leg. It is not a toe-drag.
BEHIND THE STANDING LEG: the ball must go behind the standing leg, not beside it. Behind means the defender cannot see or reach the ball during the turn.
180 DEGREE TURN: the body turns completely to face the new direction. A half-turn leaves the player side-on and slow.
ACCELERATION AFTER: the first two steps after the turn must be explosive. The whole point of the turn is to create space. Use that space immediately.
CLOSE CONTROL: the drag must keep the ball within one step. If the ball goes too far behind, the player over-runs it during the turn.
EYES UP: during the turn, the player should glance up to see where to go next. Do not look at the ball through the entire movement.
TIMING: perform the turn just before reaching the defender, not while still 3-4m away. Too early and the defender has time to recover.
commonMistakes: Kicking through the ball instead of dragging it behind the standing leg
Ball going beside the standing leg instead of behind it, making it accessible to the defender
Half-turning rather than completing the full 180 degrees
No acceleration after the turn, losing the advantage created
Performing the turn too far from the defender where it has no deceptive value
Using the toe rather than the inside of the foot on the drag
corrections: Kicking through: slow the motion to walking pace and physically guide the foot into the drag movement
Ball beside not behind: place a cone directly behind the standing leg. Ball must go past the cone.
Half turn: place a cone pointing in the new direction. Player must face it after the turn.
No acceleration: place a cone 3m in the new direction. Player must reach it in 2 steps after the turn.
Too far from defender: place a cone marking the turn point 1m from the passive defender
Using the toe: stick a sticker on the inside of the boot and ask them to use only the sticker on the drag
progressions: Semi-active defender: the partner can now shuffle sideways to block the turn but cannot tackle
Cruyff then pass: after the turn, immediately play a pass to a target player before resetting
Cruyff in a channel: narrow 3x15m channel. Player dribbles, turns at each end using Cruyff, continuous for 60 seconds
1v1 with the turn: live defender, player must use the Cruyff turn to beat them and reach the far end
regressions: Practice the drag motion without the turn first: just shape, drag, stop
Use a stationary ball placed directly beside the standing leg to practice the drag angle
Walk through the full sequence at slow motion counting each step aloud
matchApplication: The Cruyff turn is one of the most widely used skill moves in professional football because it is both simple and extremely effective. It is used by attacking midfielders and wingers when they receive with a defender close behind, by strikers who receive with their back to goal, and by any player who wants to quickly change direction away from pressure. The fake pass that sets it up is what makes it work. Johan Cruyff made it famous but it is used by elite players at every level today.
coachTips: Name the move after Cruyff and tell the players who he was. U10 players love learning that a move is named after a real legend.
Demonstrate the move yourself at full speed first, then break it down slowly.
The inside-of-foot drag is the hardest part to get right. Spend most coaching time on this single technical point.
Do not rush the progression to a live defender. The static and passive phases build the muscle memory needed.
Celebrate every successful turn loudly. U10 players need positive reinforcement to persist with a challenging new skill.
diagramDescription: 5x5m square per player with a cone at each corner. Player shown with ball. Stage 1: forward arrow (fake), Stage 2: curved arrow behind standing leg (drag), Stage 3: 180-degree rotation arrow, Stage 4: acceleration arrow opposite direction. Passive defender shown at one cone end.

title: Turn and Face 360 Degree Awareness
objective: Develop the ability to receive the ball and turn to face any direction based on where pressure is coming from.
learningOutcomes: Players can select the correct turn based on the defenders position
Players develop 360 degree awareness before receiving
Players can execute the inside turn, outside hook, and Cruyff turn under pressure
Players build the habit of scanning before the ball arrives
organisation: 8x8m square. Player A in the centre. Players B, C, D on three sides of the square (one side empty). B has the ball. A passive defender stands 1m behind A. B passes to A. A must turn away from the defender and pass to C or D. Rotate roles every 3 minutes.
instructions: Player A stands in the centre, back to the empty side of the square.
Before B plays the pass, A takes a quick look over each shoulder to see where the defender is.
B plays a firm pass to A feet.
A uses the correct turn to get away from the defender and face forward:
If defender is on the left: use the outside hook with the left foot (push ball to the right, turn right).
If defender is on the right: use the outside hook with the right foot (push ball to the left, turn left).
If defender is directly behind and close: use the Cruyff turn to go either side.
If defender is far enough away: use a simple inside-foot open turn to face the best option.
After turning, A passes to either C or D (the free player) and moves to a new position.
Phase 2 after 10 minutes: defender becomes semi-active. A must read the defender position and select the turn in real time.
coachingPoints: SCAN BEFORE RECEIVING: the scan over the shoulder happens BEFORE the ball arrives, not after. A player who scans after receiving has no time to use the information.
READ THE DEFENDER: the scan tells you where the defender is. Use that information to choose the turn direction. Always turn away from the defender.
BODY SHAPE ON RECEIVE: receive the ball on the half-turn whenever possible so you can see both the incoming ball and the defender simultaneously.
INSIDE OPEN TURN: when the defender is far away, the simplest turn is best. Control the ball with the inside of the foot, redirect it 90 degrees, face forward.
OUTSIDE HOOK: when the defender is close on one side, use the outside of the other foot to hook the ball around and behind, turning away quickly.
CRUYFF TURN: when the defender is directly behind and close, the Cruyff creates the most space because it goes the opposite direction to the fake.
FIRST TOUCH AWAY FROM DEFENDER: regardless of which turn, the first touch must move the ball away from where the defender is. Never touch it toward them.
QUICK DECISION: the turn decision must be made before the ball arrives. Players who decide after receiving are always a half-second too slow.
PASS IMMEDIATELY AFTER TURNING: turning creates an advantage. Use it immediately by playing forward. Do not hold the ball after the turn.
COMMUNICATION: C and D should signal where they are available as A receives. A verbal or visual signal tells A where to turn to.
commonMistakes: Not scanning before the ball arrives, receiving blind and having no information about the defender
Using the same turn regardless of where the defender is
First touch going toward the defender rather than away
Holding the ball too long after the turn, allowing the defender to recover
Receiving chest-on to the ball, making any turn slow and awkward
Choosing the turn after receiving rather than before
corrections: No scan: make the scan mandatory. A must call out the defender position (LEFT, RIGHT, BEHIND) before receiving. If no call, the rep does not count.
Same turn always: freeze the game and ask A which way the defender was. Then ask which turn that means.
Touch toward defender: after each rep ask A to point to where the defender was and where the first touch went. They should be opposite.
Holding ball: add a 2-touch maximum after the turn.
Chest-on receive: place a cone to the side of A showing the half-turn angle they should receive on.
Late decision: add a colour call. Coach calls RED (defender close) or GREEN (defender far) just before the pass. A must react to the colour.
progressions: Live defender: defender can now tackle after the turn.
Blind receive: A faces away from B until the pass is played, then turns to receive.
Multiple defenders: two defenders at different positions. A must scan both and choose the safest turn.
1-touch after turn: A must pass with 1 touch after the turn.
regressions: No defender at all: A practices all three turns freely against no pressure
Static defender: stands completely still so A can take time to identify the correct turn
Coach calls the turn: coach shouts CRUYFF, OUTSIDE, or OPEN to tell A which turn to use
matchApplication: Receiving and turning under pressure is one of the most important skills for midfielders and strikers. In every match a central player receives the ball with defenders close behind multiple times. The players who turn successfully create goal-scoring opportunities. The players who cannot turn are forced to lay the ball back every time, making them predictable and easy to defend against.
coachTips: The scanning habit is the most important thing to build here, not the turn technique. Make it the focus of every rep in the first 5 minutes.
Film a few reps and show the player the moment they scanned (or did not scan). Seeing themselves receive blind is very effective.
Connect to professional players known for excellent receiving and turning -- Firmino, Ozil, Bergkamp. Show clips if possible.
At U13 the scanning habit can be established if coached consistently. Do not skip this drill.
The verbal call (LEFT, RIGHT, BEHIND) trains the brain to process and categorise information quickly under pressure.
diagramDescription: 8x8m square. Player A in centre. Player B on bottom side with ball. Players C and D on left and right sides. Passive defender 1m behind A. Three turn options shown: outside hook right, outside hook left, Cruyff turn. Final pass arrow from A to C or D.

title: Speed Turn Receive and Go
objective: Develop the ability to receive at pace, turn at full speed, and accelerate immediately in the new direction.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a speed turn at 80 percent pace without losing control
Players develop the body mechanics for turning at high speed
Players build the habit of taking the first touch in the direction of travel
Players can turn and accelerate within 1.5 seconds
organisation: 25m corridor per pair. Player A at one end with ball. Player B starts at the 12m mark facing away from A. A rolls the ball to B. B must receive on the move, turn at the 12m mark, and sprint to the far end (25m). A times the turn-to-sprint sequence if a stopwatch is available.
instructions: Player B jogs toward the 12m cone (away from A).
Player A rolls the ball to B feet as B approaches the cone.
B takes a first touch that redirects the ball forward in the direction of the sprint (past the 12m cone toward the 25m end).
The first touch IS the turn -- it sends the ball in the new direction and B body follows immediately.
B sprints after the ball and controls it before the 25m cone.
Key principle: the turn is one touch, not receive-then-turn. The receive and the turn are the same action.
Phase 1 (5 minutes): rolling ball at jogging pace. B approaches at jog, touches and turns.
Phase 2 (6 minutes): A plays a firmer pass. B approaches at 70 percent pace, touches and turns at full speed.
Phase 3 (7 minutes): B approaches at full speed. The first touch must control and redirect simultaneously. Sprint to the end.
Swap roles after every 5 reps.
coachingPoints: THE FIRST TOUCH IS THE TURN: this is the central concept. The receive and the turn happen in one touch. B does not stop the ball then turn.
TOUCH DIRECTION: the first touch sends the ball 2-3m forward in the sprint direction. Not too far and not too close.
BODY LEAN: as the ball is being received, the body is already leaning in the new direction. Body and ball go together.
OUTSIDE OF FOOT: at speed, the outside of the foot is the most natural surface for the redirecting touch.
SOFT TOUCH: a heavy touch sends the ball too far ahead. A soft touch keeps the ball within 2-3 steps.
HEAD UP: after the touch, immediately look up to see the space ahead.
ARMS DRIVE: immediately after the touch, the arms pump hard to initiate the sprint.
ACCELERATION SHAPE: the first 3 steps after the touch should be low and forward-leaning.
TIMING OF APPROACH: B should be decelerating slightly as they reach the turn point so the first touch is controlled.
commonMistakes: Stopping the ball completely before turning, losing all momentum
First touch too heavy, ball runs too far ahead
First touch too soft, ball stays under feet and causes a stumble
Body upright after the touch, losing the acceleration advantage
Approaching the turn point at full speed with no slight deceleration, causing miscontrol
Using the inside of the foot for the touch at pace, which is slower than the outside
corrections: Stopping before turning: remove the turning point cone. B must keep moving throughout.
Heavy touch: place a cone 3m beyond the turn point. The first touch must not send the ball past it.
Soft touch: insist B is running after the touch within 1 second.
Upright after touch: place a low cone 1m ahead of the turn point. B must lean under it in the first step.
Full pace approach: place a deceleration marker 3m before the turn point. B must take 3 shorter steps before the turn.
Inside foot: call OUTSIDE before each rep to remind B which foot surface to use.
progressions: Timed reps: time from A pass to B reaching the 25m cone. Target under 4 seconds.
Defender chasing: A plays the pass then immediately sprints to try to tag B before 25m.
Direction unknown: B does not know which way to turn until A plays the ball to the left or right side.
Two-touch limit: B must reach the 25m cone in maximum 2 touches after the turn.
regressions: Walking pace through the full sequence to establish the one-touch redirect concept
Static ball: B places foot on a stationary ball and practices the redirect touch direction
Reduce distance to 15m
matchApplication: The speed turn is used constantly in senior football by forwards and midfielders who receive the ball on the move and need to immediately accelerate into space. Players who do this well (Mbappe, Salah, Vardy) are almost impossible to defend because they gain 2-3 steps on any defender the moment they touch the ball.
coachTips: The concept of one touch being both the receive and the turn is difficult initially. Walk through it in slow motion first.
Show a video of Mbappe or Salah receiving on the move and accelerating.
Phase 1 at jogging pace is essential. Do not rush to full pace.
Time the reps if you have a stopwatch. Concrete improvement data is hugely motivating for U16 players.
This drill is excellent as a pre-session warm-up activity once the technique is established.
diagramDescription: 25m corridor per pair. Player A at 0m with ball. Player B jogging from 0m toward the 12m turn-point cone. Pass arrow from A. At 12m: first touch arrow showing ball redirected forward toward 25m end. Sprint arrow from B following ball to 25m cone.

title: The Matthews Feint
objective: Develop the Matthews feint (shoulder drop) to beat a defender with a change of direction using body deception.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the Matthews feint with the right and left shoulder
Players understand the timing required for the feint to work against a defender
Players develop explosive acceleration immediately after the feint
Players can combine the feint with a dribbling run in a 1v1 situation
organisation: 20x5m narrow channel. Attacker at one end with ball. Passive then active defender at the midpoint. Mini-goal (2 cones) at the far end. Pairs work simultaneously. Swap roles every 3 minutes.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Static technique (5 minutes): No defender. Player practices the feint movement against a cone.
Approach the cone at dribbling pace.
Drop the right shoulder hard to the right as if driving that way. Shift body weight right.
Immediately push the ball to the LEFT with the outside of the right foot.
Accelerate left past the cone.
This is the Matthews feint: show right, go left. Show left, go right.
Practice 5 reps going right, 5 reps going left.
PHASE 2 - Passive defender (7 minutes): Real partner stands as defender but cannot move feet. Attacker uses the feint to go past them.
PHASE 3 - Active defender (6 minutes): Defender can move but cannot tackle. Attacker must make the defender commit before executing the feint.
Competition: attacker earns 1 point for reaching the far mini-goal. Defender earns 1 point for keeping the attacker in the channel.
coachingPoints: THE SHOULDER DROP: the shoulder drops hard and low in the fake direction. Half-hearted drops do not fool defenders.
WEIGHT SHIFT: body weight actually shifts to the fake side for a split second. This is what causes the defender to react.
WAIT FOR THE DEFENDER TO REACT: the feint only works when the defender reacts to it. If the defender does not move, approach closer and try again.
TIMING: the push-off happens the instant the defender shifts their weight to the fake side. Half a second after they commit.
PUSH-OFF SURFACE: the outside of the foot pushes the ball in the real direction. Quick, firm, one touch.
ACCELERATION: the first two steps after the push-off must be explosive.
APPROACH SPEED: approach the defender at controlled dribbling pace, not sprint pace.
DIRECTION CHOICE: go to the defenders weaker side if possible. Most defenders recover faster to their strong side.
EYES ON THE DEFENDER: watch the defenders feet and weight distribution, not the ball. Their feet tell you when to execute the feint.
commonMistakes: Shoulder drop not convincing enough -- defender does not react
Executing the feint before the defender has reacted to it
Not accelerating after the feint, allowing the defender to recover
Approaching the defender at sprint pace, making the feint uncontrolled
Always feinting to the same side, becoming predictable
Looking at the ball instead of watching the defenders feet
corrections: Not convincing: exaggerate the shoulder drop in slow motion. Drop right shoulder all the way to knee height if needed.
Feinting too early: place a cone 1.5m from the defender. Feint must happen within the cone distance.
No acceleration: place a cone 3m past the defender in the go direction. Player must reach it in 2 steps.
Sprint approach: mark a deceleration zone 3m before the defender.
Always same side: call LEFT or RIGHT before each rep. Player must feint to the called side.
Watching ball: ask the player to describe the defenders shirt colour before each rep.
progressions: Live 1v1: defender can now tackle. Full competitive 1v1 in the channel using any skill move.
Double feint: two shoulder drops before the push-off to increase deception.
Feint then cross: after beating the defender with the feint, player delivers a cross to a target player.
Feint in a wide channel: widen to 10m. Attacker can now use the feint in both directions.
regressions: Practice against a cone only (no person) until the movement is fluid
Slow motion walk-through with a partner who tells the attacker when they have successfully fooled them
Remove the acceleration requirement -- just feint and stop. Add acceleration in the next session.
matchApplication: The Matthews feint (named after Sir Stanley Matthews) is one of the simplest and most effective 1v1 moves in football. It works because it exploits the defenders natural instinct to respond to body movement. Wide players use it constantly to beat full-backs.
coachTips: Name the move after Matthews and show a clip if possible. U14 players respond well to historical context.
The waiting for the defender to react element is the hardest coaching point. Most players execute too early. Insist on patience.
The competitive phase (points system) is essential. The feint only develops under real match pressure.
Some players will naturally feint better to one side. Allow them to develop their stronger side first.
Connect to modern players: Robben always cut inside from the left but still beat defenders every time.
diagramDescription: 20x5m narrow channel. Attacker at one end with ball. Defender at midpoint. Mini-goal at far end. Three-stage diagram: attacker approaching, right shoulder drop and weight shift right, outside-of-foot push left and acceleration past defender.

title: Low Driven Cross
objective: Develop accurate low driven crosses from wide positions into the danger zone in front of goal.
learningOutcomes: Players can deliver a low driven cross accurately across the face of goal
Players understand the target zones for different types of crosses
Players develop the approach and body position for crossing from wide
Players learn when a low driven cross is more effective than a lofted cross
players: 8-12 (4-6 wide players, 2-3 attackers in the box, GK)
equipment: Cones to mark crossing positions and target zones
pitchSize: Full width of penalty area plus wide channels
organisation: Two crossing positions: left channel and right channel (wide, level with penalty area). 3 target zones in the penalty area: Zone A (near post, 5m from post), Zone B (penalty spot), Zone C (far post, 5m from far post). 2-3 attackers positioned in zones. GK in goal.
instructions: TECHNIQUE: approach the ball from a slight angle. Plant foot 15cm beside and behind the ball. Strike through the bottom half of the ball with the laces. Ankle locked and foot pointed down. Follow through low toward the target zone.
The low driven cross travels fast along the ground or at shin-to-knee height. It is not lofted.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): No attackers or GK. Wide player delivers 5 crosses from each side aiming at the three target zones.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): Attackers in the box. Wide player chooses which zone to cross to. GK in goal.
Attackers must communicate: call NEAR, SPOT, or FAR to indicate which zone they want the cross.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): Add a defender per attacker. Wide players must find an attacker unmarked or in front of their defender.
Score 1 point for a cross reaching its target zone. Score 3 points for a goal from a low driven cross.
coachingPoints: LOW DRIVEN vs LOFTED: a low driven cross is more dangerous because it is harder for the GK to claim, travels faster, and attackers can deflect it without a clean header.
APPROACH ANGLE: approach from a slight angle (30-45 degrees) rather than straight at the ball.
CONTACT POINT ON THE BALL: strike the bottom half of the ball. Contact through the centre keeps it low.
ANKLE LOCKED: the ankle must be locked and the foot pointed down at contact.
FOLLOW THROUGH: follow through low, keeping the kicking foot below knee height after contact.
TARGET ZONE SELECTION: near post cross if the attacker is making a near post run. Penalty spot cross if an attacker is arriving late. Far post cross if the near and central zones are covered.
TIMING OF CROSS: deliver when the attacker is in motion, not static.
COMMUNICATION: look up before crossing to see which zone has an attacker in position.
FIRST TOUCH BEFORE CROSSING: if dribbling at pace, take a controlled touch to set the ball for the cross before striking.
commonMistakes: Cross too lofted: contact too far under the ball causing it to go high
Cross too close to the GK: aiming too central
Cross going out of play at the far post: too much pace
Crossing too early before the attacker has made their run
No communication with attackers -- crossing blind to an empty zone
Approaching the ball straight-on rather than at an angle
corrections: Too lofted: place a 1m high string between poles at the penalty spot. Every cross must go under it.
Too central: place a cone at the near post position. Cross must pass inside the cone.
Too much pace: ask the player to aim for a specific attacker rather than a zone.
Crossing too early: add a rule that the cross cannot be played until the attacker raises their hand.
No communication: make the call mandatory. No call means no rep counts.
Straight approach: place a cone at 45 degrees from the ball. Player must touch the cone during approach.
progressions: Crossing after beating a defender: add a wide defender who can block.
First time cross: ball played to the wide player who must cross first time.
Combination then cross: wide player receives from a CM, dribbles to the byline, then crosses.
Two attackers making runs: both near and far post attacker run simultaneously.
regressions: No GK and no defenders: pure technique practice with clear targets
Stationary ball: practice the crossing technique from a stationary ball
Reduce to one target zone only until accuracy is established
matchApplication: The low driven cross is statistically more likely to result in a goal than a lofted cross because it is harder to defend and easier for attackers to deflect. Professional clubs specifically train wide players to deliver low crosses as a priority.
coachTips: The low driven cross is often neglected in youth football where lofted crosses are more common. Specifically teach and reward low crosses.
Stand at the far post during the drill to evaluate the height and pace of each cross from the receiving perspective.
The communication habit (NEAR, SPOT, FAR calls) must be built in training before it transfers to matches.
Wide players need volume of crossing repetitions. Each player should complete minimum 15-20 crosses per session.
Connect to match situations: show footage of a low driven cross from a wide position leading to a goal.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Two wide crossing positions (left and right channels). Three target zones marked: Zone A (near post), Zone B (penalty spot), Zone C (far post). Low cross trajectory shown as fast arrow from crossing position, below knee height, into Zone B. Lofted cross shown for contrast as high arc (marked as X - avoid).

title: Crossing from Deep Full-Back Overlap
objective: Develop the full-back overlap run and cross, combining movement, timing, and delivery in a realistic match scenario.
learningOutcomes: Full-backs can time an overlap run to arrive at the crossing position at the right moment
Players can deliver crosses from deep wide positions at pace
Players understand the relationship between the overlap run and the attackers movement in the box
Teams develop the coordination between the full-back, winger, and strikers
pitchSize: Full pitch width from halfway line to goal
organisation: Winger positioned wide on the halfway line. Full-back starts 10m behind and inside the winger. CM holds possession centrally. Two strikers in the penalty area. GK in goal.
instructions: CM starts with the ball centrally. Winger calls for the ball and receives wide on the halfway line.
As the winger receives, the full-back immediately starts the overlap run: sprinting from behind and outside the winger.
The winger takes 1-2 touches to hold the ball while the full-back makes the run past them.
When the full-back reaches the crossing position (wide, level with the penalty area), the winger plays the ball into their path.
The full-back arrives at the ball at pace and crosses first time or with one controlling touch.
Crossing options: low driven to near post, whipped ball to far post, or cut-back to the edge of the penalty area.
Two strikers make coordinated runs: one to the near post, one to the far post or penalty spot.
Phase 1 (8 minutes): passive defenders. Full sequence focus.
Phase 2 (8 minutes): active wide defender tries to block the cross.
Phase 3 (6 minutes): defenders in the box too. Full realistic scenario.
coachingPoints: FULL-BACK RUN TIMING: the full-back must start their run the moment the winger receives the ball.
OVERLAP PATH: the full-back runs outside the winger (on the touchline side), not through them.
WINGER ROLE: the winger holds the ball long enough for the full-back to get level, then releases early enough that the full-back receives at pace.
CROSSING AT PACE: the full-back receives and crosses without breaking stride if possible.
CROSS SELECTION: near post if one striker has made a near post run. Far post whipped ball if the striker is arriving late. Cut-back if both post options are covered.
STRIKER COORDINATION: near post striker times a late run from outside the area.
COMMUNICATION: full-back calls OVERLAP as they start the run.
DEPTH OF CROSS: cross should be delivered from as close to the byline as possible.
FIRST TIME CROSS: whenever possible the full-back crosses first time.
commonMistakes: Full-back starting the overlap run too late
Winger releasing the ball too early before the full-back is in the crossing position
Full-back stopping to control before crossing instead of crossing at pace
Strikers both going to the same zone
Cross delivered too close to the GK
No communication between full-back and winger
corrections: Late overlap: place a timing cone 5m ahead of the winger. Full-back must pass this cone before the winger plays the ball.
Early winger release: add a rule that the winger must take minimum 2 touches before playing to the full-back.
Stopping to control: full-back is restricted to 1 touch only on the cross.
Both strikers same zone: assign zones before the drill.
Cross too central: mark a no-cross zone with a cone 6m from the near post.
No communication: make the OVERLAP call mandatory.
progressions: Underlap variation: instead of the overlap, the full-back runs inside the winger into the central area.
Counter-attack version: ball starts with the GK who distributes to the winger on the break.
Two-sided: run the drill from both left and right simultaneously.
Live 3v3 in the box: three defenders vs three attackers plus the crossing full-back.
regressions: Walk through the entire sequence at slow motion before any live reps
Remove defenders entirely until the timing between full-back and winger is established
Start with a stationary cross to establish the crossing technique before adding the overlap run
matchApplication: The full-back overlap is one of the most potent attacking movements in modern football. When a full-back overlaps their winger it creates a 2v1 overload on the wide defender. Full-backs like Trent Alexander-Arnold, Andrew Robertson, and Joao Cancelo built entire careers on this movement.
coachTips: The timing relationship between full-back and winger is the key coaching point. It takes 3-4 sessions to get right.
Stand at the crossing position during the drill to evaluate the cross delivery.
Connect to professional football: show Trent Alexander-Arnold overlap and cross highlights.
At U15 players have the physical capacity to execute this movement at match pace. Push for full pace in Phase 2.
The OVERLAP call must be built in training before it works in matches.
diagramDescription: Full pitch from halfway to goal. Winger shown wide left on halfway line. Full-back starting position 10m behind and inside winger. Overlap run arrow shows full-back sprinting wide past winger to left channel crossing position. Pass arrow from winger into full-back running path. Three cross options shown: low near post, whipped far post, cut-back.

title: Body Position Shielding
objective: Teach the correct body position for shielding the ball from a defender while maintaining control.
learningOutcomes: Players can adopt the correct low, wide body position for shielding
Players can maintain shielding position while a defender applies light pressure
Players understand when to shield the ball in a match situation
Players can shield and release the ball at the right moment
organisation: Pairs in 3x3m squares. One player has the ball and must shield it. The other applies gentle pressure trying to reach around and touch the ball. Roles swap every 60 seconds.
instructions: SHIELDING POSITION: stand with the ball at your feet. Step between the ball and the defender. Body faces sideways (not chest-on to the defender).
Bend your knees and lower your centre of gravity. Wide stance -- feet wider than shoulder-width.
Use the arm closest to the defender as a light barrier (not a push -- just awareness).
Keep the ball on the far foot (away from the defender).
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): static shielding. Defender applies light hand pressure on the shielders back. Shielder maintains position for 10 seconds. Swap.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): moving shield. Shielder moves around the 3x3m square while shielding. Defender follows and tries to reach the ball.
PHASE 3 (3 minutes): shield then pass. Coach calls RELEASE at random moments. Shielder must immediately play a pass to a target before the defender can intercept.
PHASE 4 (3 minutes): free 1v1 in the square. Player can shield OR dribble. Most time in possession after 60 seconds wins.
coachingPoints: BODY BETWEEN BALL AND DEFENDER: your body is the barrier, not your arms. Always position your body so the defender cannot reach the ball without going through you.
LOW CENTRE OF GRAVITY: bend the knees and get low. A low centre of gravity is much harder to push off the ball.
WIDE STANCE: feet wider than shoulder-width gives a more stable base.
BALL ON FAR FOOT: the ball sits on the sole or inside of the foot furthest from the defender.
SIDEWAYS BODY: face sideways to the defender. A chest-on body position gives the defender access to the ball on both sides.
FEEL THE DEFENDER: use your back and arm to feel where the defender is.
WHEN TO SHIELD: shield when you are waiting for support, buying time, or need a second to play out.
RELEASE TIMING: shield until a passing option opens up, then release immediately.
LEGAL USE OF ARM: the arm can be extended slightly as a sensing tool but cannot push the defender.
commonMistakes: Ball too close to the defender -- not keeping it on the far foot
Upright stance -- easily knocked off the ball
Narrow feet -- unstable base
Chest-on to defender -- giving them access on both sides
Holding or pushing the defender with the arm -- illegal
Not releasing the ball when a passing option appears
corrections: Ball close to defender: mark the far foot position with a cone. Ball must stay above that cone.
Upright stance: ask the player to touch their own knees with both hands while shielding.
Narrow feet: draw two foot outlines wider than shoulder-width. Player must stand on the outlines.
Chest-on: place a cone beside the player showing the sideways body direction.
Illegal arm use: no arm contact allowed at all in Phase 1.
Shielding too long: add a 5-second maximum shield time.
progressions: Shielding in a corridor: 3x10m corridor. Shielder must shield the ball from one end to the other.
3v1 shielding: 3 players in a small square. One defender tries to win the ball.
Shield and turn: after 3 seconds of shielding, player must turn away from the defender.
Match application: small-sided game where the team can earn bonus points for successful shields followed by a pass.
regressions: Defender uses one arm only (other arm behind back) to reduce the pressure
Increase the square size to 5x5m
Defender stands completely static for Phase 1
matchApplication: Shielding is a critical skill for any player who receives the ball under pressure. It is used by strikers holding the ball up for support, by midfielders buying time when pressed, and by defenders protecting the ball near their own corner flag. A player who can shield effectively under pressure keeps possession for their team in moments when a less technically able player would lose the ball.
coachTips: Demonstrate the shielding position yourself from the correct angle so players can see the sideways body, wide stance, and ball position all at once.
The key teaching point is the low centre of gravity. Make it feel like a game: can you be knocked off the ball?
The arm contact rule is important. Explain clearly what is legal and what is not.
Match the pairs by size and strength where possible.
Connect to match situations: show a video of a striker holding up play by shielding the ball until a teammate arrives.
diagramDescription: 3x3m square per pair. Player A (shielder) in the centre with ball. Body shown sideways to Player B (defender). Ball on far foot (away from defender). Wide stance shown. Arm lightly extended as sensor. Defender behind shielder trying to reach around.

title: Counter Attack 2v1 and 3v2
objective: Develop the decision-making and execution skills for creating and converting counter-attack overloads.
learningOutcomes: Players can exploit a 2v1 overload with a simple pass-and-shoot combination
Players understand when to pass and when to shoot in a 2v1 or 3v2
Players develop the habit of running at the spare space in an overload
Players build composure when a goal-scoring chance is created on the break
organisation: 40x25m area with a mini-goal at each end. 2 attackers and 1 defender at one end (2v1). 3 attackers and 2 defenders at the other end (3v2). Groups rotate between scenarios every 5 minutes.
instructions: 2V1 SCENARIO: 2 attackers vs 1 defender approaching a mini-goal. Attacker A has ball, Attacker B runs wide.
Defender must choose: press the ball or cover the run.
If defender presses ball: A passes to unmarked B who shoots.
If defender covers run: A drives forward and shoots.
The correct decision: attack the space the defender vacates.
3V2 SCENARIO: 3 attackers vs 2 defenders approaching the goal.
Attacker in possession drives forward. Pass to the attacker furthest from both defenders.
Continue until a shot is taken. Reset and repeat.
Each scenario runs for 5 minutes before rotating.
coachingPoints: THE 2V1 PRINCIPLE: one player draws the defender, the other goes free. Always attack the vacated space.
READ THE DEFENDER: in the 2v1, watch the defenders hips. If they point toward you, pass. If toward your partner, drive and shoot.
DO NOT PASS TOO EARLY: passing before the defender commits allows them to recover.
DRIBBLE AT THE DEFENDER: in a 2v1, drive directly at the defender to force them to commit.
THE 3V2 PRINCIPLE: always find the free player. With 3v2, one attacker will always be completely free.
WIDE SPACING: in 3v2, attackers must stay wide apart to stretch the 2 defenders.
TEMPO: counter-attack overloads must be executed at speed.
COMPOSURE IN FRONT OF GOAL: the finish must be calm and placed.
COMMUNICATION: the player without the ball calls the direction they are running.
SUPPORT RUNS: a third attacker in a 2v1 should make a run even if unlikely to receive.
commonMistakes: Passing too early before the defender has committed
Ball-carrier drifting wide instead of at the defender
3v2 attackers bunching in the centre instead of spreading wide
Shooting when the pass is the better option (and vice versa)
Slowing down when the overload is created
Losing composure in front of goal
corrections: Early pass: ball-carrier must get within 5m of the defender before passing
Drifting wide: place a central cone line. Ball-carrier must stay within 2m of the centre line
Bunching: mark 3 lanes in the 3v2 zone. One attacker in each lane
Wrong decision: freeze after each rep and ask what the defender chose
Slowing down: from crossing halfway to a shot must be under 5 seconds
Composure: practice finishing only (no defenders) for 3 minutes to rebuild calm
progressions: Passive defender turns active: the 2v1 defender can now tackle
Add a recovery runner: a 4th defender starts at the far goal and sprints after 3 seconds
2v1 then 3v2 in sequence: complete a 2v1 then immediately face a 3v2 at the other end
Full-side counter-attack game: 7v7 with overloads rewarded with 3 points
regressions: Static drill first: defender stands still while attackers practice the pass-and-shoot combination
Reduce to 2v1 only for the whole session before introducing 3v2
Use a larger goal to build confidence
matchApplication: Counter-attack overloads are among the best goal-scoring opportunities in football. Every time a team transitions with more attackers than defenders, they have a high-percentage chance of scoring. The decision-making skills developed here directly translate to match goal-scoring.
coachTips: The freeze-and-ask technique is the most effective coaching tool in this drill. Use it every 2-3 reps.
Do not let players overthink the decision. The 2v1 choice should be made in under 0.5 seconds.
Energy and excitement are important. Counter-attacks are exciting.
Rotate groups so every player gets time in both the 2v1 and 3v2 scenarios.
Connect to match situations: show a professional counter-attack goal from an overload.
diagramDescription: 40x25m area with mini-goals. 2V1 SCENARIO: 2 attackers dribbling toward 1 defender. Two branching arrows: pass if defender covers inside, shoot if defender covers wide run. 3V2 SCENARIO: 3 attackers spread wide driving at 2 defenders. Free attacker highlighted. Pass arrow to the free attacker.

title: High Press Structure and Triggers
objective: Develop a coordinated high press as a team defensive tactic using defined triggers and press structures.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the high press shape and their individual role
Players can execute a high press within 1 second of a trigger
Players learn what to do when the press is beaten
Players develop the fitness and mentality to press intensely
organisation: Full pitch. Defending team uses a high press from the front. Attacking team plays out from the back. Coach stops the game to correct press shape and trigger recognition.
instructions: ESTABLISH THE TRIGGERS: (1) GK has the ball. (2) A CB receives under pressure. (3) A full-back faces their own goal.
PRESSING SHAPE: front 2 press the CBs. Wide midfielders press the full-backs. Central midfielders cover the opposition CMs.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): walk-through. Coach directs every movement. No live pressure from the attacking team.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): attacking team plays out at 50 percent pace. Pressing team executes at match pace.
PHASE 3 (9 minutes): full intensity. Pressing team must recognise triggers, communicate, and press together.
Score: 3 points for winning the ball within 5 seconds. 1 point for forcing a long kick. 0 points for being bypassed.
Attacking team score: 3 points for playing through the press to a midfielder.
coachingPoints: PRESS SHAPE NOT INDIVIDUAL: the press must happen as a unit.
COVER SHADOW: every pressing player positions in the cover shadow of a potential receiver, blocking the easy pass.
THE PRESS TRAP: the press is most effective when it traps the ball in a wide area near the touchline.
PRESS INTENSITY: the press must be at maximum speed. A slow press is worse than no press.
PRESS COMMUNICATION: the pressing striker calls PRESS loudly. Every other player reacts immediately.
WHEN NOT TO PRESS: if the trigger has not occurred, hold the mid-block shape.
RECOVERY WHEN BEATEN: if the press is bypassed, every player immediately drops to compact mid-block.
PRESSING FITNESS: the high press requires short explosive bursts of maximum intensity.
PRESS DISTANCE: triggered from within 30-35m of the opposition goal.
PATIENCE: wait for the right trigger.
commonMistakes: Pressing individually without a coordinated team response
Pressing too slowly after the trigger
No cover shadows: running directly at the ball rather than cutting off passing lanes
Continuing to press after being bypassed
Random pressing without waiting for a trigger
