Front players pressing while midfielders hold position
corrections: Individual pressing: show which player is pressing and which 2 players should cover the most likely escape passes
Slow press: 2-second timer from trigger to first pressing player at the ball
No cover shadows: draw the cover shadow positions on the ground with chalk
No recovery: blow whistle when the press is beaten. All players must be in recovery positions within 3 seconds
Random pressing: give the pressing trigger call to one specific player only
Midfield staying: when press is triggered, the nearest CM must also press the most likely CM escape pass
progressions: Press with overload: give the pressing team an extra player (5v4)
Extended high block: goals from press-won balls in the final third earn 2 points
Press game: full 11v11. Pressing team earns points for winning the ball high
Fatigue press: 30 seconds of high intensity running before executing 2 minutes of pressing
regressions: Passive attacking team to allow the pressing team to practice without movement complexity
Reduce to 5v5 in a 30x20m area before expanding to full pitch
Remove the trigger system and just practice pressing the GK every time
matchApplication: The high press has become one of the most influential tactical concepts in modern football. Jurgen Klopp popularised it at Liverpool, Guardiola uses it at Man City. Teams that press well win the ball higher up the pitch and prevent opponents from building from the back.
coachTips: The walk-through phase is non-negotiable before any live pressing.
The trigger system is the most important concept. Without triggers, pressing is just running.
Film from above or behind the pressing team.
Connect to professional pressing: show Klopp Liverpool in full flight.
The scoring system that rewards both teams creates genuine competitive pressing intensity.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Pressing team in 4-4-2 high press shape: front 2 pressing CBs, wide midfielders pressing full-backs, CMs covering opposition CMs. Attacking team in possession with GK. Trigger shown. Coordinated press arrows show all pressing players moving simultaneously. Touchline shown as extra defender.

title: Switch and Build GK to Winger
objective: Develop the ability to quickly switch play from GK possession through the spine of the team to a winger in space.
learningOutcomes: Players execute a clean switch from GK to the far winger within 4 passes
Players understand the positional requirements for switching play from the back
GK develops the confidence and technique to play out under pressure
Players build the ability to exploit the weak side after switching play
organisation: Full team shape. Pressing team of 3-4 players. The building team executes the switch pattern: GK to near CB, CB to CM, CM to far CM or full-back, full-back or CM to far winger.
instructions: GK starts with the ball (from a goal kick or save scenario).
NEAR CB moves wide to the edge of the 18-yard box. GK plays to the near CB.
NEAR CB turns and plays to the near CM who has dropped between the lines.
NEAR CM plays quickly across to the FAR CM or FAR FB who has pushed wide.
FAR CM or FB plays the switch pass to the FAR WINGER who has held their wide position.
FAR WINGER receives and has 3 touches to create a chance.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): no pressure. Shadow play through the switch pattern at full pace.
PHASE 2 (9 minutes): 3-player press from the opponent. Execute the switch while being pressed.
PHASE 3 (8 minutes): full 11v11. 2 points for completing the switch to the winger. 3 points for a shot after the switch.
coachingPoints: GK STARTING POSITION: GK must be at the top of the 6-yard box when possession is regained.
CB POSITIONING: near CB immediately moves to the edge of the 18-yard box. Width is the key.
CM SHOWING: near CM must drop into a clear passing lane between the opposing midfielders and forwards.
SPEED OF PLAY: every pass in the switch must be played quickly (maximum 2 touches for the CB and near CM).
FAR SIDE TIMING: far winger must hold their wide position and NOT come inside before the switch arrives.
SWITCH PASS QUALITY: pass to the far winger must be driven and accurate.
AFTER THE SWITCH: the rest of the team must immediately advance to support.
PRESS READING: the CM must look up and identify whether the switch to the far side is available before receiving.
COMMUNICATION: far winger must signal to confirm they are in position before the switch is made.
TRANSITION: if the switch is intercepted, immediately press the player who won the ball.
commonMistakes: GK staying on the goal line instead of stepping up
CB staying too narrow and receiving in the pressing zone
Near CM not dropping between the lines
Switch pass going too high or too soft
Far winger coming inside before the switch is made
Building team not pressing immediately when the switch is intercepted
corrections: GK on line: cone at top of 6-yard box. GK must step past it before receiving
CB too narrow: wide marker cone at edge of 18-yard box. CB must stand outside it
CM not dropping: CB cannot pass to CM until CM is visibly between the lines
Poor switch pass: switch must arrive below knee height and within 2 steps of the winger
Winger coming inside: cone showing the holding position. Must stay outside until switch arrives
No press after interception: if building team does not press within 3 seconds, opponents earn 2 points
progressions: Full press added: 6-7 player press instead of 3-4
Switch to score: winger must cross for a striker to score within 10 seconds
Two switches in sequence before a shot is attempted
Free game with switch reward: 3 points for goals preceded by a completed switch
regressions: Walk through with no press until every player can execute their role from memory
Use a passive press before adding active pressing
Reduce the pattern to GK-CB-CM-winger (4 passes) before adding far-side complexity
matchApplication: The ability to switch play quickly through the spine of the team is one of the most sophisticated team tactical skills. Man City, Liverpool, and Bayern Munich all train this pattern daily. The far winger receiving the switch ball has 3-5 seconds of space to create a chance.
coachTips: Every player must know their role before the drill starts. Spend 5 minutes on roles and positions before touching a ball.
The near CM position is the most important and most difficult.
Film from above or behind the goal.
Connect to professional examples: show Trent Alexander-Arnold switching play.
At U16 players are ready for this complexity. Be patient through the first 2 sessions.
diagramDescription: Full pitch with full team shape. GK at top of 6-yard box. Near CB at edge of 18-yard box. Near CM between lines. Far CM or FB wide. Far winger at right touchline. Numbered pass arrows: 1 (GK to near CB), 2 (near CB to near CM), 3 (near CM to far CM or FB), 4 (switch pass to far winger). Pressing team on near side. Far side shown open where winger receives.

title: Reaction Saves and Double Save
objective: Develop fast reaction saves and the ability to recover quickly for a second shot after the initial save.
learningOutcomes: GK can make fast reaction saves from close range
GK develops the explosive recovery movement after making a save
GK can set position for a second shot within 2 seconds of the first
GK builds the mental focus to stay alert for rebounds
organisation: Full-size goal. GK in goal. Server A stands 10m from goal centrally. Server B stands 12m from goal, 3m to the right. Servers alternate firing shots. After each save, GK must immediately recover before Server B fires.
instructions: Server A fires a shot at the GK (low, driven, to either side).
GK makes the save.
IMMEDIATELY after the save, GK recovers: stands up, side-steps back to centre, sets position.
Server B fires a shot within 3 seconds of Server A shooting.
GK makes the reaction save from Server B.
Both servers reset and repeat.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): Server A fires low shots only. Server B fires central shots.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): Server A fires to any height. Server B fires to the opposite side from Server A.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): Server B fires within 1.5 seconds. Maximum intensity.
After each round of 6 saves, GK rests 60 seconds. Minimum 4 complete rounds.
coachingPoints: SAVE AND RECOVER: saving the first shot is only half the job. The recovery for the second is equally important.
RECOVERY SPEED: after a save, GK must be upright and set within 2 seconds.
SET POSITION: the set position for the second shot must be established BEFORE Server B fires.
PARRY DIRECTION: if the first shot cannot be caught cleanly, parry it WIDE of the post, not toward the centre.
EYES ON SERVER: after saving the first shot, GK must immediately find Server B with their eyes.
BODY POSITION FOR SECOND SAVE: recovery must get the GK into the correct weight-forward set position.
ANTICIPATION: watch Server B for body shape cues before the shot.
MENTAL RESET: after a missed save, mentally reset immediately.
COMMUNICATION: after each double save sequence, GK calls READY to signal they are set for the next round.
commonMistakes: Staying on the ground too long after the first save
Parrying the first shot toward the centre creating a rebound for Server B
Not setting the correct weight-forward position before the second shot
Looking at the first saved ball instead of immediately finding Server B
Losing mental focus after a missed save
Flat-footed set position
corrections: Staying down: GK must be upright within 2 seconds. Stopwatch every rep.
Central parry: cone 1m wide of each post. Any parry must go past the cone.
Wrong weight distribution: paper under each heel. If it touches the floor the GK is flat-footed.
Looking at first ball: Server B calls READY immediately. GK must look at Server B when they hear it.
Mental focus: after each save, GK calls the colour of the next server shirt.
Flat-footed: mandatory bounce between every save to reset weight-forward position.
progressions: Reduce recovery time to 1 second between shots
Add a third server for a triple save sequence
Screens: a player stands between Server A and GK blocking sight until 1m away
Fatigue saves: 10 burpees then immediately face a double save sequence
regressions: Extend recovery time to 5 seconds
Start with Server A only until consistency is established
Use softer balls at reduced pace
matchApplication: The double save and reaction save are critical because rebounds and second chances are extremely common in matches. A GK who saves the first shot but cannot recover for the rebound concedes preventable goals.
coachTips: Keep repetitions high. A GK should face minimum 60-80 shots in this session.
Film the recovery movement from the side.
Praise the recovery speed as enthusiastically as the save itself.
Use a stopwatch for recovery timing.
GK coaching must be positive. Build confidence alongside technique.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Server A centrally 10m from goal with shot arrow. GK diving to save. Recovery sequence shown: push off floor, knees under body, upright, side-step to centre, set position. Server B shown 12m from goal 3m to the right. Reaction save arrow to opposite side. Rebound cone shown 1m wide of each post.

title: Cross Claiming and Commanding the Box
objective: Develop the GKs ability to claim crosses confidently, communicate with defenders, and command the penalty area.
learningOutcomes: GK can claim crosses in a crowded penalty area with conviction
GK develops the correct take-off, catch position, and landing technique
GK uses clear communication to organise defenders
GK can decide quickly between claiming the cross and staying
organisation: Full-size goal. GK in goal. Wide server on each flank. 2-3 attackers and 2-3 defenders in the box. Wide servers alternate delivering varied crosses. GK must organise defenders and claim or stay.
instructions: PHASE 1 (6 minutes): No attackers. 2 defenders only. GK practices claiming and organising. Focus on technique and communication.
KEEPER call: when GK is coming for the cross, call KEEPER loudly (minimum 2 times). Defenders must stop jumping and get out of the way.
AWAY call: when GK is NOT coming, call AWAY. Defenders must attack the ball and clear.
Take-off: jump from one foot. The near foot is the take-off foot. Opposite knee drives upward.
Catch position: hands form a W-grip behind and around the ball at the highest point.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): 2 attackers added. GK must claim under light challenge.
PHASE 3 (8 minutes): live crossing situation. Attackers compete fully.
Award: GK earns 1 point per clean catch, -1 point per dropped catch.
coachingPoints: DECISION -- CLAIM OR STAY: the GK claims if they can reach the ball at its highest point before any attacker.
CALL EARLY: KEEPER must be called the moment the GK decides to come.
CALL VOLUME: the call must be loud enough to be heard over match noise.
COME WITH CONVICTION: if the GK decides to claim, they go 100 percent.
TAKE-OFF TIMING: jump to reach the ball at the peak of the jump.
W-GRIP: thumbs pointing toward each other forming a W shape behind the ball.
PULL INTO CHEST: after catching, immediately pull the ball into the chest.
BODY PROTECTION: the raised knee protects the GK from aerial challenges.
LANDING: land on both feet in a stable balanced position.
COMMUNICATION AFTER THE CATCH: after claiming, immediately call OUT.
commonMistakes: Not calling KEEPER until the last moment
Going for a cross they cannot reach
Staying when the GK should have come
W-grip breaking down under challenge
Landing on one foot and falling after claiming
Not calling AWAY on crosses they are not claiming
corrections: Late call: GK cannot move until they have called KEEPER. If they move without calling, the drill resets.
Poor decision: pause after every decision and ask the GK to justify it.
Staying wrongly: mark a catching zone (4m wide, from 6-yard box to penalty spot). Any cross in this zone must be claimed.
W-grip breakdown: practice the W-grip catch separately with a partner throwing from 2m.
One-foot landing: practice landing drills without a ball. 10 reps before the cross session.
No AWAY call: defenders cannot go for the ball until they hear AWAY.
progressions: High press on the catch: attacker is allowed to shoulder challenge as GK catches
Crosses from behind: server crosses from 30m from goal
Distribution after claim: GK must distribute to a specific target within 3 seconds
Crowded box: increase to 4 attackers and 2 defenders
regressions: Phase 1 extended for the full session
Slow delivery for technique focus
One side of deliveries only before adding crosses from both sides
matchApplication: Claiming crosses is one of the most difficult and high-impact skills in goalkeeping. A GK who commands their box and claims confidently removes significant danger from wide deliveries. The communication system (KEEPER and AWAY) prevents the dangerous confusion that causes dropped crosses and conceded goals.
coachTips: The call volume requirement is non-negotiable.
Stand inside the penalty area during the drill.
Film the claiming sequence from behind the goal.
Build a GK who makes correct decisions more than one who claims everything regardless of risk.
After a dropped catch, immediately give a positive coaching point and move to the next rep.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Wide server on left flank delivering cross. 2-3 attackers and 2-3 defenders in box. GK shown leaving line with KEEPER call. Jump sequence shown. AWAY call shown on a cross GK is not claiming. Claiming zone shown from 6-yard box to penalty spot.

title: Football Endurance Interval Running
objective: Develop match-specific aerobic and anaerobic endurance through high-intensity interval running that replicates a 90-minute match.
learningOutcomes: Players build the aerobic base needed to maintain intensity for 90 minutes
Players develop anaerobic fitness for repeated short sprints
Players understand aerobic and anaerobic energy systems in football
Players complete interval work at the correct intensities
organisation: Full pitch used as distance reference. Players work in 4 phases of interval running replicating football energy demand.
instructions: PHASE 1 - AEROBIC BASE (6 minutes): continuous run at 65 percent effort (comfortable conversation pace). Players run around the perimeter.
PHASE 2 - THRESHOLD RUNS (8 minutes): 4 reps of 2-minute threshold runs at 80-85 percent effort. 90 second rest between reps. Run lengths of the pitch.
PHASE 3 - SHORT SPRINT INTERVALS (7 minutes): 8 reps of 30-second sprints at 95-100 percent effort. 60 seconds rest between reps. Sprint width of the pitch.
PHASE 4 - RECOVERY RUN (4 minutes): return to 50-55 percent effort. Perimeter of the pitch. Active recovery, not a rest.
Throughout: no stopping between phases. Reduce pace, do not stop.
coachingPoints: AEROBIC vs ANAEROBIC: aerobic energy (Phases 1 and 2) uses oxygen and is sustainable. Anaerobic energy (Phase 3) produces lactate and can only be sustained for short bursts.
65 PERCENT EFFORT: this is NOT a jog. It is a sustained run that would be uncomfortable to maintain for more than 20-30 minutes.
THRESHOLD PACE: the 80-85 percent effort builds the lactate threshold. A higher threshold means sustaining higher intensity longer.
SPRINT QUALITY: the 30-second sprints must be at genuine maximum effort.
RECOVERY BETWEEN SPRINTS: 60 seconds is intentionally short, replicating match conditions.
ACTIVE RECOVERY: the Phase 4 jog removes lactate from the muscles faster than standing still.
INDIVIDUAL PACING: effort percentage should be relative to THEIR maximum, not someone elses.
HYDRATION: drink water during every rest period.
CONSISTENT TRAINING: endurance improvements require 8-12 weeks of consistent interval training.
commonMistakes: Running at the wrong intensity (too fast in Phase 1, too slow in Phase 3)
Stopping during rest instead of actively recovering
Sprint effort dropping below 90 percent in later reps
No hydration during rest periods
Players comparing pace with others rather than using individual effort percentages
Stopping completely at the end of a phase
corrections: Wrong intensity: use the talk test. Phase 1: full conversation. Phase 2: short sentences. Phase 3: cannot speak. Phase 4: full conversation.
Stopping during rest: any player who stands still adds 5 seconds to their sprint time.
Sprint effort dropping: pair players of similar fitness for competitive element.
No hydration: water bottles at the start line. Players must drink during every rest.
Comparing with others: effort percentage is individual. A slower player at 95 percent is working harder than a faster player at 80 percent.
Stopping between phases: music with timing built in. Music continues means keep running.
progressions: Add a ball: Phase 3 sprints become dribbling sprints
Increase sprint distance to 60m for players with excellent base fitness
Reduce rest to 45 seconds between Phase 3 sprints
Add a 5th phase: 2v2 high intensity small-sided game for 3 minutes
regressions: Reduce Phase 3 to 6 reps of 20-second sprints
Increase Phase 3 rest to 90 seconds
Remove Phase 2 and replace with a 3-minute jog
matchApplication: Football analysis shows players cover 10-13km per game, with 1-3km at high intensity and 200-400m at maximum sprint intensity. Building both aerobic base and anaerobic capacity at U16 is critical for players who want to compete effectively at senior level.
coachTips: Do this session at the start of the season and repeat once per week.
Lead from the front in Phase 1 and 2. Your pace sets the correct effort level.
The talk test is the most accessible intensity guide for youth players.
Connect to match performance: a player who does interval training consistently will perform better in the 80th minute.
This session is hard. Celebrate players who push through the discomfort.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Phase 1: perimeter loop arrows (continuous oval). Phase 2: 4 lengths with rest bars between. Phase 3: 8 short sprint arrows (width) with walk-back arrows. Phase 4: perimeter loop (slower arrows). Effort labels: 65%, 80-85%, 95-100%, 50-55%.

title: Recovery Run and Last Ditch Tackle
objective: Develop the ability to recover defensively at full sprint and execute a last-ditch sliding tackle from behind.
learningOutcomes: Players can recover at full sprint to get goal-side of an attacker
Players develop the correct timing and technique for the sliding tackle
Players understand when a sliding tackle is correct vs jockeying
Players build the confidence to make a last-ditch tackle
organisation: 30x8m narrow channel per pair. Attacker starts at one end with ball. Defender starts level with the attacker but 3m to the side (not goal-side). Defender must sprint to get goal-side and either jockey or tackle before the attacker reaches the far end. Mini-goal at the far end.
instructions: Attacker and defender start level at one end. Attacker has ball, defender is 3m to the side.
On GO: attacker drives forward toward the far mini-goal. Defender sprints to get goal-side.
RECOVERY SPRINT: the defender sprints at maximum pace, taking the shortest route to get between the attacker and the goal.
Once goal-side: defender slows to jockeying pace and stays between ball and goal.
If attacker accelerates past: defender executes the sliding tackle (Phase 2 and 3 only).
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): recovery sprint only. Defender earns 1 point for being goal-side before the attacker reaches 20m.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): sliding tackle technique. Coach demonstrates. Defender can slide when ball is 1m ahead of the attacker.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): live 1v1. Defender can jockey or slide. Full competition.
coachingPoints: RECOVERY SPRINT PATH: the shortest route is not necessarily directly behind the attacker. Cut across to intercept the path.
ACCELERATION: the recovery sprint must be at absolute maximum pace.
TRANSITION TO JOCKEY: once goal-side, immediately slow down and adopt the jockeying stance.
WHEN TO SLIDE: only when the attacker takes a heavy touch (ball goes more than 1m ahead). Never slide when the ball is under close control.
SLIDING TACKLE TECHNIQUE: the sliding leg extends toward the ball, contact made with the instep or side of the foot to redirect the ball away.
DIRECTION OF SLIDE: slide to redirect the ball out of play, toward the touchline, not toward the centre.
STAY ON YOUR FEET: jockeying is always better than a slide if the attacker still has close control.
HEAD POSITION IN SLIDE: head stays up and eyes on the ball.
RECOVERY AFTER SLIDE: get up immediately whether the tackle was successful or not.
commonMistakes: Recovery sprint not at maximum pace
Stopping the sprint too early before being genuinely goal-side
Sliding when the attacker still has close control
Sliding toward the centre instead of the touchline
Not getting up immediately after the sliding tackle
Jockeying too far away from the attacker
corrections: Not at maximum pace: pair players in a race for the goal-side position
Stopping too early: mark the goal-side position with a cone. Defender must reach it before slowing down.
Sliding too early: freeze every time a slide is attempted when ball is under control. Ask: was ball more than 1m ahead?
Central redirect: draw a touchline cone 2m from channel edge. Ball must go past it after tackle.
Staying on floor: if defender is still on floor after 2 seconds, attacker earns a bonus point.
Too far in jockey: mark 1.5m distance with cone. Defender must stay within that distance of the ball.
progressions: Recovering from greater deficit: attacker starts 5m ahead
Recovering against pace: use the fastest attackers
Recover then defend a cross: attacker delivers a cross instead of shooting
Full pitch recovery: defender starts in the centre circle and must recover against a 10m head start
regressions: Reduce channel to 20m
Phase 1 only (no tackle) for full session to establish the recovery sprint habit
Passive attacker: attacker jogs slowly in Phase 1
matchApplication: The recovery run is one of the most important physical and tactical defensive skills. Every time a defender is beaten, their only option is to sprint to recover goal-side. The sliding tackle from behind is the last resort when recovery is achieved but the attacker has the ball at pace. Building both at U14 gives defenders the confidence to recover from difficult situations.
coachTips: The recovery sprint is purely physical. There is no technique to coach beyond go as fast as you possibly can.
The sliding tackle technique must be demonstrated clearly before any live attempts.
In Phase 3 connect the two elements: recovery sprint, jockey, then slide only if the heavy touch comes.
Celebrate successful recovery runs even when the attacker still scores.
At U14 the competitive element is essential. Defenders only develop genuine recovery pace when there is something real to defend.
diagramDescription: 30x8m narrow channel. Attacker (circle) and defender (triangle) level at start, defender 3m to the side. RECOVERY arrow shows defender sprinting diagonally to get goal-side. Goal-side position shown with star. Jockey position with correct side-on stance. Heavy touch with ball 1m ahead. SLIDE TACKLE with sliding leg extending toward ball, redirect arrow going toward touchline. Mini-goal at far end.

title: Pressing as a Forward High Intensity
objective: Develop the pressing technique and intensity required from forwards when the team loses possession high up the pitch.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the pressing angle that cuts off the most dangerous pass
Players can press at match intensity for repeated 5-8 second bursts
Players develop the cover shadow technique to block passing lanes
Players build the habit of pressing immediately after losing possession
organisation: 12x12m square. Player A (presser) starts in the centre. Players B, C, D start at three corners. B passes to C. A must immediately sprint to press C using the correct pressing angle.
instructions: Player B passes to Player C at a corner of the square.
The moment B plays the pass, Player A (presser) sprints to press C.
PRESSING ANGLE: A does not run directly at C. A runs to a position that cuts off Cs most dangerous passing option (usually the pass to D).
By blocking the pass to D with their body position, A forces C to either hold the ball or play backward.
C can pass to D or hold the ball. A tries to win the ball or force an error.
If C passes to D, A immediately shifts to press D using the same pressing angle technique.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): B and D are passive. A presses C who can only hold or pass back to B.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): D can now receive and turn. A must press C then shift to press D if needed.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): all players active. Count how many times A wins or forces an error in 3 minutes.
coachingPoints: PRESSING ANGLE (most important): the press goes to the position that cuts off the most dangerous pass, not directly to the ball.
COVER SHADOW: A positions their body so that D is behind A from Cs perspective, making the pass to D very difficult.
PRESS SPEED: the press must be at maximum intensity for the 5-8 seconds it takes to reach C.
FORCE THE DIRECTION: by blocking the D-pass, A forces C to go backward or hold. This is the press working correctly.
COMPACT WITH COVER: when A presses, the rest of the team must compact toward the ball to cover the space A vacated.
PRESS TRIGGERS: A should press the moment the pass from B is made, not when C receives it.
ENERGY MANAGEMENT: maximum intensity for 5-8 seconds. Then the presser recovers.
COMMUNICATION: A must call PRESS to alert the rest of the team.
READ THE BODY SHAPE: if C receives with back to goal, press very aggressively. Facing forward, more cautious press.
commonMistakes: Running directly at the ball instead of to the pressing angle
Pressing at 70-80 percent effort rather than 100 percent
No cover shadow: A arrives in a direct line with C but not blocking any passing lane
Not pressing immediately when the pass is played
Continuing to press for more than 8-10 seconds causing fatigue
No communication: silent pressing with no team coordination
corrections: Direct run: place a cone showing the pressing angle position. A must run to the cone, not directly to the ball.
Low effort: use a race element. A must arrive at C before C can play their next pass.
No cover shadow: A must call out which passing lane they are blocking before pressing.
Late press: A must be within 2m of C before C controls the ball.
Pressing too long: 8-second press clock. After 8 seconds, A must reset to a mid-block position.
No communication: PRESS call is mandatory. No call means no press points even if ball is won.
progressions: Active square: B, C, D can all move freely. A presses any player who receives.
4v1 pressing: 4 players keep possession in 12x12m. 1 presser tries to win. Count wins in 3 minutes.
Combination pressing: 2 pressers in the square (2v4). They must coordinate.
Game with press reward: goals scored within 5 seconds of winning ball from a press earn 3 points.
regressions: Reduce square to 8x8m
B and D completely static so A only needs to press C
Walk through the pressing angle at slow motion before any live pressing
matchApplication: Pressing from the forward position is one of the most important defensive contributions attacking players can make. The cover shadow technique is the difference between a press that wins the ball and one that just creates noise. Klopp Liverpool forwards were famous for their pressing intelligence.
coachTips: The pressing angle is the most important concept and the hardest to teach. Spend majority of Phase 1 time here.
Demonstrate the cover shadow by standing in the correct position and asking the group where the D-pass goes.
The trigger (pressing when the pass is played) takes 3-4 sessions to establish.
Connect to professional pressing: show Firmino pressing a CB at Liverpool.
At U13 this concept is new. Use lots of freeze-and-show moments.
diagramDescription: 12x12m square. Player A (presser) in centre. Player B at one corner with ball. Player C at adjacent corner. Player D at opposite corner. Pass arrow from B to C. A pressing arrow going to position that cuts off the D-pass. Cover shadow shown as a shaded zone blocking the passing lane to D. C shown with limited options.

title: Volley Technique Foundations
objective: Teach the correct technique for striking a volley cleanly with accuracy and control.
learningOutcomes: Players can strike a dropping ball cleanly with the laces
Players develop the correct body position for a side-on volley
Players understand the relationship between contact point and ball trajectory
Players build confidence striking the ball without it touching the ground first
organisation: Pairs 8m apart. Player A throws the ball gently underarm so it drops in front of Player B at knee to waist height. B volleys back to A. Swap roles every 6 reps. Build from stationary to moving.
instructions: Player A throws the ball underarm in a gentle arc so it drops just in front of Player B.
Player B stands side-on to the direction of the throw, weight on the standing leg.
As the ball drops to knee-to-waist height, B swings the striking leg through the ball.
Contact point: the laces meet the CENTRE of the ball at the moment it is at the ideal height.
Follow through low and toward the target. The volley should travel flat, not high.
After 6 reps each at gentle pace: increase the throw height slightly.
After 6 more reps: introduce a side-on volley where the ball is thrown from an angle rather than straight on.
Final phase: server tosses the ball with more pace, simulating a half-volley off a bouncing ball.
coachingPoints: BODY SIDE-ON: turn the body side-on to the ball before it arrives. A square-on body produces an unpredictable strike.
WATCH THE BALL ONTO THE FOOT: eyes track the ball all the way to contact. Looking up early is the most common cause of a miscued volley.
CONTACT THE CENTRE: striking the centre of the ball with the laces keeps the volley flat and driven. Striking underneath sends it high.
KNEE OVER THE BALL: at the moment of contact, the striking knee should be positioned over the ball, not behind it. This keeps the shot down.
SHORT BACKSWING: a volley needs a shorter, sharper backswing than a normal shot because the ball is moving and timing is tighter.
BALANCE ON THE STANDING LEG: keep the standing leg slightly bent and balanced. A stiff standing leg causes the body to fall away from the strike.
TIMING THE STRIKE: the contact must happen as the ball reaches the ideal height, neither too early (ball still too high) nor too late (ball has dropped too low).
FOLLOW THROUGH TOWARD TARGET: the kicking leg continues toward the target after contact, which controls direction and adds power.
ARMS FOR BALANCE: use the arms actively for balance during the volley motion, especially on the follow-through.
commonMistakes: Striking underneath the ball causing it to balloon high over the target
Body facing square-on to the ball producing an inconsistent strike
Looking up before contact causing a mishit
Standing leg too stiff causing loss of balance during the strike
Backswing too long causing mistimed contact with a moving ball
Contact too early or too late relative to the ideal striking height
corrections: Ballooning: place a target zone (cone marker) at knee height and ask the player to keep volleys under that height
Square body: place a cone to the side showing the side-on body angle the player should adopt before striking
Looking up early: ask the player to call out the colour of the ball at the moment of contact (forces eyes down)
Stiff standing leg: ask the player to bend the standing knee more before each volley to build a soft, balanced base
Long backswing: reduce the backswing deliberately - tell the player to take the foot back only to knee height before striking
Wrong timing: server calls NOW just before the ideal striking moment so the player learns the correct timing window
progressions: Increase throw pace and height to simulate a more difficult volley
Add a target: 2 cones forming a 3m gate that the volley must pass through
Half-volley: server bounces the ball once before the player strikes it just after the bounce
Moving volley: player jogs toward the server and must time the run and strike in one movement
regressions: Reduce throw height so the ball drops from a much shorter distance, making timing easier
Allow the ball to bounce once before volleying (a half-volley is easier to time than a clean volley)
Use a softer ball to reduce any fear of the strike
matchApplication: The volley is a technically demanding skill that appears regularly in matches: clearing crosses, finishing chances that arrive in the air, and dealing with long passes under pressure. The side-on body position and centre-of-ball contact taught here form the technical foundation for both defensive clearances and attacking volleyed finishes. Players who master the volley early develop a significant technical advantage because most youth players avoid striking the ball before it touches the ground.
coachTips: Demonstrate the volley yourself first - the side-on body position is much easier to understand visually than verbally.
Start with very gentle, predictable throws. Confidence with the technique must come before pace.
Many young players are naturally hesitant to volley. Build trust gradually with soft balls and gentle serves.
Praise correct technique even when the strike is not powerful. Technique first, power later.
Connect to match situations: explain that this is exactly the technique used to clear a cross or finish a half-chance.
diagramDescription: Pairs 8m apart. Server (square) shown throwing the ball in a gentle underarm arc. Receiver (circle) shown in side-on body position with knee over the ball at the moment of contact. Trajectory arrow shows the ball dropping to knee-waist height before the strike. Follow-through arrow shows the kicking leg continuing toward the target after contact.

title: Side Volley and Half Volley
objective: Develop the side volley and half volley techniques used to strike crosses and bouncing balls accurately at goal.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a side volley from a crossed ball at goal
Players can execute a half volley immediately after the ball bounces
Players understand when to use each technique based on ball height and trajectory
Players develop the explosive hip rotation needed for power in both techniques
organisation: Server positioned wide (crossing position) delivers balls at varying heights into the penalty area. Shooter positioned 12-14m from goal. GK in goal. Two techniques are practiced: side volley (ball arrives at hip-chest height) and half volley (ball bounces once before the strike).
instructions: SIDE VOLLEY SETUP: server delivers a cross arriving at hip to chest height. Shooter must be side-on to the flight of the ball, not facing the server directly.
As the ball arrives, the shooter rotates the hips explosively, swinging the striking leg across the body to meet the ball.
Contact point: the side of the laces meets the side of the ball, sending it across goal.
HALF VOLLEY SETUP: server delivers a firm, dropping ball that bounces once just in front of the shooter.
As the ball bounces, the shooter strikes it the instant it leaves the ground - the moment of minimum bounce height.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): side volley technique only, no GK. Focus on hip rotation and contact.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): half volley technique only, no GK. Focus on timing the strike to the bounce.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): both techniques mixed randomly with GK live. Player must read the delivery and select the correct technique.
coachingPoints: SIDE VOLLEY HIP ROTATION: the power comes from the hip rotation, not the leg alone. Rotate the hips explosively through the ball.
BODY ANGLE FOR SIDE VOLLEY: the body should be almost side-on to goal at the moment of contact, allowing the leg to swing across the body.
EYE ON THE BALL THROUGHOUT: for both techniques, watching the ball all the way to contact is the single most important factor for a clean strike.
HALF VOLLEY TIMING: the half volley must be struck the instant the ball leaves the ground after the bounce. Too early and the bounce has not happened. Too late and the ball has risen too high.
KNEE OVER THE BALL (half volley): just as with the standard volley, keeping the striking knee over the ball at contact keeps the shot from rising.
SHORT, SHARP STRIKE: both techniques require a compact, controlled swing rather than a big wind-up. Power comes from technique, not from a long backswing.
WEIGHT TRANSFER: shift weight from the back foot to the front foot through the strike for both techniques, adding power and stability.
READ THE DELIVERY EARLY: as the cross or pass is travelling, identify whether it will arrive at side-volley height or bounce for a half volley, and prepare the correct technique in advance.
FOLLOW THROUGH: the kicking leg continues through the ball toward the target after contact in both techniques.
commonMistakes: Body facing the ball directly rather than side-on for the side volley, reducing power and accuracy
Striking too early or too late relative to the half volley bounce
Looking up before contact on either technique
Big looping backswing rather than a short, sharp strike
Not preparing the correct technique in advance based on how the ball is arriving
Losing balance during the hip rotation on the side volley
corrections: Square body: place a cone to the side showing the body angle required before the cross arrives. Player must be turned toward it.
Half volley timing: server calls BOUNCE the moment the ball hits the ground, training the player to recognise the cue.
Looking up: ask the player to call the colour of the ball just before striking on every rep.
Long backswing: limit the backswing to a maximum of knee height before the strike - exaggerated swings are not permitted.
Wrong technique selection: pause before each serve and ask the player to predict whether this will be a side volley or half volley based on the delivery.
Losing balance: practice the hip rotation separately with no ball first, focusing purely on the rotational movement and balance.
progressions: Live defender applies light pressure during the strike, replicating match conditions
Combine both techniques in a single sequence: side volley control followed immediately by a half volley finish
Reduce the distance to goal to 10m increasing the urgency and difficulty of clean technique
Competition: count successful strikes on target out of 10 attempts for each technique
regressions: Reduce ball pace and height for both techniques to make timing easier
Practice each technique separately for a full session before combining them
Allow the ball to bounce twice for the half volley if timing is too difficult initially
matchApplication: Side volleys and half volleys are advanced finishing techniques that appear in some of the most spectacular and effective goals in football. More importantly at a practical level, they are essential skills for dealing with crosses and bouncing balls in genuine match situations - many scoring opportunities arrive at exactly these heights and a player who can only finish with the ball on the ground will miss a significant proportion of their chances. Strikers and attacking midfielders who develop these techniques convert a higher percentage of the chances created for them.
coachTips: These are genuinely advanced techniques. Expect inconsistency in the early sessions - this is completely normal and improves with repeated practice.
The hip rotation for the side volley is best demonstrated by the coach performing the movement slowly without a ball first.
Use a stopwatch or simple counting to help players understand the timing window for the half volley - it happens in a fraction of a second.
Film a few attempts and show players their own technique. The side-on body position is much easier to correct when seen on film.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of side volleys and half volleys scored in matches to illustrate both the technique and the value of mastering it.
diagramDescription: Penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Server shown wide delivering crosses at two heights: hip-chest height (side volley) and a bouncing trajectory (half volley). Shooter positioned 12-14m from goal. Side volley diagram shows side-on body position with hip rotation arrow and leg swinging across the body. Half volley diagram shows the ball bouncing once with a strike arrow at the moment the ball leaves the ground.

title: 1v1 Attacking Fundamentals
objective: Teach the basic principles of attacking a defender 1v1: acceleration, change of direction, and confidence on the ball.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the basic approach to beating a defender in a 1v1 situation
Players can use a simple change of pace to create separation from a defender
Players develop confidence taking on a defender rather than always passing
Players learn to accelerate immediately after beating a defender
organisation: 15x5m narrow channel. Attacker starts at one end with the ball. Defender starts in the middle of the channel, completely passive (cannot move their feet). Mini-goal at the far end. Pairs work simultaneously. Swap roles every 4 reps.
instructions: Attacker dribbles toward the passive defender at a controlled pace.
As the attacker approaches, they choose a side to attack - left or right of the defender.
The key technique: a sudden change of pace. Approach at medium speed, then explode past the defender with 2-3 fast touches.
After passing the defender, continue at full speed to the mini-goal and finish.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): defender is completely static (feet planted). Attacker focuses purely on the acceleration past them.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): defender can reach with their arms and lean but cannot move their feet. Attacker must choose the side with more space.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): defender can shuffle sideways slowly. Attacker must read the defenders movement and accelerate at the right moment.
coachingPoints: CHANGE OF PACE: the most basic and effective 1v1 weapon is a change of speed. Approach at a controlled pace, then suddenly accelerate past the defender. The change itself creates the separation.
CLOSE TOUCHES NEAR THE DEFENDER: take small, controlled touches as you approach the defender to maintain control, then use bigger touches to accelerate away once past them.
PICK YOUR SIDE EARLY: decide which side you are attacking before you reach the defender. Hesitating at the last moment slows you down.
KEEP THE BALL CLOSE WHEN CLOSE TO THE DEFENDER: the touch that takes you past the defender should be close enough that they cannot get a foot to it.
ACCELERATE AFTER, NOT JUST AT: many young players accelerate INTO the defender then slow down. The acceleration must continue AFTER you have gone past them.
CONFIDENCE: believe you can beat the defender before you try. Hesitant attempts rarely succeed even with good technique.
USE YOUR BODY: lean slightly toward the side you are NOT going as you approach - this is a simple early form of deception.
EYES UP: look at the defender and the space beyond them, not down at the ball, as you make your decision.
commonMistakes: Slowing down or stopping right at the defender instead of accelerating past
Touches too heavy near the defender, allowing them to intercept even when static
Not accelerating after getting past the defender
Always attacking the same side, becoming predictable
Hesitating and not committing to a direction
Looking down at the ball instead of at the defender and the space
corrections: Slowing at defender: place a cone 2m past the defender that the attacker must reach within 2 touches of passing them
Heavy touches: reduce the space between attacker and defender to 1m forcing very small, controlled touches
No acceleration after: time the attacker from passing the defender to reaching the mini-goal, challenge them to beat their own time
Always same side: alternate which side is open by moving the defender slightly left or right before each rep
Hesitating: count down from 3 before each rep forces the attacker to commit to a decision by the time they reach 0
Looking down: ask the attacker to say which hand the coach is holding up as they approach the defender
progressions: Defender becomes fully live and can tackle, but must give the attacker first move advantage by starting 1m further away
Add a second defender who can recover after the first is beaten, testing whether the attacker continues accelerating
Time trial: how quickly can the attacker beat the defender and reach the goal across 5 attempts
Two-goal option: mini-goals at both corners of the far end so the attacker must also make a finishing decision
regressions: Defender stands even further back (5m into the channel) giving the attacker more time and space to build confidence
Widen the channel to 8m to give more room for the change of direction
Allow the attacker unlimited touches near the defender rather than demanding tight control immediately
matchApplication: The ability to beat a defender 1v1 is one of the most valuable individual skills in football because it directly creates goal-scoring opportunities, draws defenders out of position, and gives a team an attacking outlet when other options are closed. At U10, building the confidence and the simple technical foundation - change of pace, close control, acceleration after the move - sets up a player to develop more sophisticated skill moves in later years. The mental component (believing you can beat a defender) is just as important as the technical component at this age.
coachTips: Confidence is the primary outcome at this age, not technical perfection. Celebrate attempts as much as successes.
Keep the passive defender phase long enough that every player experiences success before adding any real pressure.
Use enthusiastic encouragement specifically when a player chooses to attack a defender rather than pass - this reinforces the right mentality.
Vary which players are paired together so less confident players are not always facing the strongest defenders.
Frame this drill as fun and exciting rather than a technical examination - the joy of beating a defender 1v1 is one of the great pleasures of football.
diagramDescription: 15x5m narrow channel with corner cones. Attacker (circle) at one end with ball. Defender (triangle) static in the middle. Mini-goal at the far end. Arrow shows the attacker approaching at controlled pace, then a burst arrow showing acceleration past the defender on the chosen side, continuing to an acceleration arrow toward the mini-goal.

title: Reading the Defender 1v1
objective: Develop the ability to read a defenders body position and select the correct attacking approach to beat them.
learningOutcomes: Players can identify which side of a defender is open based on their body shape
Players understand how to use the defenders own momentum against them
Players develop a decision-making process for selecting the right 1v1 approach
Players build a small toolkit of 1v1 techniques appropriate to different defensive situations
organisation: 12x8m zone with a mini-goal at each end. Attacker and defender face off 1v1. Defender starts in a jockeying stance (side-on, showing the attacker one direction). Multiple zones run simultaneously. Swap roles every 3 reps.
instructions: Defender adopts a jockeying stance, deliberately showing the attacker toward one side (their stronger covering side).
Attacker must read the defenders body shape before deciding which way to attack.
KEY PRINCIPLE: if the defender shows you inside, attack outside. If they show you outside, attack inside. Go where there is more space, not where the defender expects you to go.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): defender holds a clearly exaggerated stance so the open side is obvious. Attacker practices reading and reacting correctly.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): defender holds a more subtle stance. Attacker must look more carefully at small cues - foot position, hip angle, weight distribution.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live 1v1. Defender can move and tackle. Attacker must read in real time and execute the correct attacking option.
Score 1 point for the attacker reaching the goal, 1 point for the defender winning the ball or forcing the attacker backward.
coachingPoints: READ THE FEET FIRST: the defenders front foot tells you which way they are showing you. If their right foot is forward, they are usually directing you to their left (your right).
WEIGHT DISTRIBUTION: a defender whose weight is loaded onto one foot is slower to react to a move on the opposite side. Notice where their weight sits.
DO NOT ATTACK THE STRONG SIDE: the side the defender is showing you toward is their strong covering side - they have already set up to deal with an attack there.
USE THE DEFENDERS MOMENTUM: if the defender steps toward you to press, use that forward momentum against them with a sharp change of direction.
COMMIT ONCE DECIDED: once you have read the defender and chosen your side, commit fully and quickly. Hesitating after deciding gives the defender time to adjust.
VARY YOUR APPROACH: do not always make the same read in the same way - a good defender will learn your patterns. Mix your decision-making.
SPEED OF DECISION: the read must happen as you approach, not when you are already at the defender. Identify the cues from 3-4m away.
BODY FEINTS SUPPORT THE READ: once you have decided which way to go, a small body feint toward the opposite side increases your chance of success.
commonMistakes: Attacking the side the defender is showing rather than the open side
Reading the defender too late, after already committing to an approach
Hesitating after making the read, allowing the defender to adjust
Always making the same decision regardless of what the defender shows, becoming predictable
Not using any deception to support the chosen direction
Focusing only on the ball and missing the defenders body cues entirely
corrections: Attacking wrong side: pause the game immediately after a wrong-side attempt and ask the attacker to describe which way the defender was showing them
Reading too late: place a marker cone 4m from the defender - the attacker must verbally call which side they are attacking by the time they reach the cone
Hesitating after deciding: count the time from the cone to the move attempt - it should be under 1 second
Predictable decisions: alternate the defenders stance unpredictably between reps so the attacker cannot fall into a pattern
No deception: require a small body lean to the closed side before every move toward the open side
Missing body cues: cover the ball with a cone for 2 seconds before each rep and ask the attacker only to look at the defenders stance during that time
progressions: Defender disguises their stance more effectively, making the read genuinely difficult
Add a recovering second defender who can cover if the first is beaten
Time pressure: attacker has 4 seconds to make contact with the defender and execute their move
Full competitive league: round-robin 1v1 contests with cumulative points across multiple opponents
regressions: Defender holds an extremely exaggerated and obvious stance throughout for players still learning to read body position
Slow the defenders movement to half pace during live phases
Allow the attacker extra time (5+ seconds) to study the defenders stance before starting their run
matchApplication: Reading a defenders body position before deciding how to attack them is one of the more sophisticated 1v1 skills and is what separates intelligent attacking players from those who rely purely on pace or technique. Defenders deliberately position themselves to show an attacker toward a particular side, expecting the attacker to either fall for it or to overcome it with pure speed. A player who reads the cues correctly and attacks the genuinely open side wins many more 1v1 duels with less physical effort, because they are working with the geometry of the situation rather than against it.
coachTips: The body-reading skill is genuinely difficult and takes many repetitions to develop. Be patient through the early exaggerated-stance phase.
Stand directly behind the defender during phase 1 so you can see exactly what the attacker should be reading and point it out clearly.
Use freeze-frame moments liberally - stop the game, ask the attacker what they saw, and confirm or correct their read.
This is an excellent skill to connect to match footage - show clips of professional 1v1 situations and ask players to identify which side was open before the attacker moved.
Defenders benefit from this drill too - understanding how to disguise their stance more effectively makes them better defenders.
diagramDescription: 12x8m zone with mini-goals at each end. Defender (triangle) shown in jockeying stance, front foot and hip angle indicating the side they are showing the attacker. Attacker (circle) shown reading the cues with an arrow indicating the genuinely open side (opposite to where the defender is showing). Move arrow shows the attacker accelerating into the open space past the defender.

title: Elite 1v1 Combinations
objective: Develop a complete personal 1v1 toolkit by combining skill moves, change of pace, and decision-making in realistic competitive situations.
learningOutcomes: Players develop 2-3 reliable, match-ready 1v1 moves they can execute under pressure
Players combine technical skill with tactical reading of the defender
Players understand how field position affects the risk and reward of attempting a 1v1
Players build the resilience and confidence to keep attempting 1v1s after failures
equipment: Mini-goals or full-size goal with GK
pitchSize: 15x10m zone per pair, with a finishing zone
organisation: 15x10m zone leading into a finishing area (mini-goal or full-size goal with GK). Attacker and defender compete live 1v1. The zone is positioned to represent either a wide attacking area or a central area, with the coach varying the context to teach risk-reward decision-making.
instructions: PHASE 1 - Personal toolkit (6 minutes): each player identifies and practices their 2 preferred 1v1 moves (e.g. step-over and shoulder drop, or scissors and change of pace) against a passive defender.

PHASE 2 - Contextual practice (8 minutes): the zone is set up to represent a WIDE area near the opposition byline. Attacker practices 1v1s here with high risk tolerance encouraged - if it fails, the consequence is minimal.

The zone then shifts to represent a CENTRAL area near the attackers own half. Same 1v1 practice but with the coaching emphasis on risk management - only attempt the 1v1 if confident, otherwise recycle possession.

PHASE 3 - Live competitive 1v1 league (8 minutes): players rotate through opponents in a round-robin format. Each win is recorded. Players must use their established toolkit rather than improvising new moves under pressure.
Throughout: defenders are fully live and competitive. No restrictions on tackling within the laws of the game.
coachingPoints: DEVELOP A PERSONAL TOOLKIT: elite 1v1 players do not have ten different moves - they have two or three that they execute extremely well and use repeatedly with subtle variation.
KNOW YOUR GO-TO MOVE: every player should identify the one move they trust most under maximum pressure and default to it when the situation is genuinely high-stakes.

FIELD POSITION CHANGES THE RISK CALCULATION: a failed 1v1 near the opposition goal costs little - the team simply resets in an advanced position. A failed 1v1 in your own defensive third can directly lead to a goal against. Adjust your willingness to attempt the 1v1 accordingly.
COMBINE PACE WITH SKILL: the most effective 1v1 players blend a technical move with an explosive change of pace immediately afterward - the skill move alone is rarely enough.
STUDY THE DEFENDERS PATTERN: in a live competitive situation, notice how the defender has reacted to previous attempts and adjust your approach for the next one.
MENTAL RESILIENCE: losing a 1v1 duel is normal even for elite players. The willingness to attempt the next one with full confidence, regardless of recent failures, is itself a skill.
DISGUISE AND TIMING REMAIN CRITICAL: even with a well-practiced move, the disguise before it and the precise timing of execution determine whether it succeeds against a quality defender.
COMMIT FULLY: a half-committed attempt at a 1v1 move against a good defender almost always fails. Full physical and mental commitment is required.
USE THE WHOLE BODY: shoulders, hips, and eyes should all support the deception, not just the feet.
commonMistakes: Attempting unfamiliar or rarely-practiced moves under genuine pressure rather than trusting the established toolkit
Attempting high-risk 1v1s in dangerous defensive areas without appropriate risk awareness
Half-committed execution of moves against a live, competitive defender
Becoming discouraged and passive after losing several consecutive 1v1 duels
Not adjusting approach after a defender has successfully read a previous attempt
Relying purely on the skill move without combining it with genuine pace
corrections: Unfamiliar moves under pressure: in phase 3, remind each player of their identified go-to moves before they enter a live duel
Poor risk awareness: explicitly label the zone WIDE/ATTACKING or CENTRAL/DEFENSIVE before each phase 2 segment so the context is unmistakable
Half-committed execution: any move attempted at less than full pace and commitment does not count as a genuine attempt - repeat it
Becoming passive after losses: track and celebrate every genuine attempt, not just successes, to maintain attacking mentality
Not adjusting to the defender: pause after a repeated failure and ask the attacker what the defender has done differently each time
No combination with pace: add a mandatory acceleration requirement - the attacker must be at full speed within 2 touches of completing any move
progressions: Defender given specific instructions to deny the attackers known go-to move, forcing adaptation
Add a finishing requirement under a strict time limit after winning the 1v1 duel
Full small-sided game where 1v1 wins in advanced positions are tracked and rewarded with bonus points
Tournament format across an entire training block, building a cumulative leaderboard
regressions: Reduce to a single identified go-to move per player until that move is genuinely reliable before adding a second
Defender starts in a clearly disadvantaged position (further away or off-balance) to rebuild confidence after a difficult phase
Remove the live competitive league for players who need more development time in the contextual phase
matchApplication: At U16 and beyond, 1v1 ability becomes a defining attribute that separates players in terms of recruitment, playing time, and match impact. Wingers and attacking players who can reliably win 1v1 duels create chances that simply do not exist for teams without that individual quality. Equally important at this level is the tactical intelligence of knowing when to attempt a 1v1 and when to play safely - a quality that distinguishes mature, complete players from talented but reckless ones. This drill develops both the technical execution and the game-understanding required for genuinely effective 1v1 attacking play at a senior-adjacent level.
coachTips: Have an honest conversation with each player about what their go-to move actually is - many players have not consciously identified this and benefit enormously from doing so.
The risk-reward coaching in phase 2 is one of the most valuable tactical lessons in this entire drill set - do not rush past it.
Track wins and losses visibly during the competitive league phase - the data itself motivates continued effort and genuine competition.
Some of the best 1v1 players in the world have remarkably few distinct moves - emphasise quality and reliability of execution over quantity of moves known.
Connect explicitly to real match situations the players have experienced - ask them to recall a recent match moment where a 1v1 decision (good or bad) affected the outcome.
diagramDescription: 15x10m zone leading to a finishing area with mini-goal or full-size goal and GK. Two contextual zone labels shown: WIDE/ATTACKING (near opposition byline, high risk tolerance) and CENTRAL/DEFENSIVE (near own half, risk-managed approach). Attacker and defender shown in live competitive posture. Personal toolkit icon shows 2-3 move options available to the attacker. Acceleration arrow shows the mandatory pace combination after any successful move.

title: Striking Technique Foundations
objective: Develop the fundamental technique for striking a stationary ball cleanly and powerfully with the laces.
learningOutcomes: Players can strike a stationary ball with consistent technique using the laces
Players develop the correct approach angle and plant foot position for a powerful strike
Players understand how the contact point affects the trajectory of the ball
Players build power through correct technique rather than excessive effort
equipment: Cones to mark a target area or wall
pitchSize: 15m striking distance to a wall or target
organisation: Players positioned 12-15m from a wall or large target area, each with a ball. If no wall is available, players work in pairs striking to each other with a defined target zone marked by cones.
instructions: Approach the ball from a slight angle (not directly behind it) - roughly 30-45 degrees.
The approach should be a confident, purposeful run of 3-5 steps, building speed toward the ball.
Plant the non-kicking foot beside the ball, approximately 10-15cm to the side, pointing toward the target.
The kicking leg swings back from the hip, then whips forward from the knee.
Contact point: the laces (the hard, flat part of the boot where the laces are) meet the CENTRE of the ball.
The ankle must be locked firm at the moment of contact - a loose ankle reduces both power and accuracy.
Follow through fully - the kicking leg continues upward and forward after striking the ball.
Practice 10 strikes focusing purely on technique at moderate pace before adding any power emphasis.
After technique is consistent, gradually increase the pace of the approach and the power of the strike.
coachingPoints: APPROACH ANGLE: striking the ball from a slight angle rather than straight behind it allows for a fuller swing of the leg and generates more power.
PLANT FOOT POSITION AND DIRECTION: the plant foot is the single biggest factor in accuracy. Wherever it points, the ball generally follows.
LOCKED ANKLE: lock the ankle firmly just before contact. This transfers maximum power into the ball and prevents the foot collapsing on impact.
CONTACT THE CENTRE FOR A FLAT STRIKE: striking through the centre of the ball with the laces produces a flat, driven trajectory. Striking lower lifts the ball.
HEAD STEADY AND DOWN: keep the head down and eyes on the ball through the contact. Lifting the head early is the most common cause of a mishit.
HIPS DRIVE THE POWER: power comes from hip rotation and the whipping action of the lower leg, not from how hard the player tries to kick.
FULL FOLLOW-THROUGH: a complete follow-through, with the kicking leg continuing well past the point of contact, is essential for both power and control.
BALANCE THROUGHOUT: keep the arms out for balance during the approach and the strike - good balance allows for a cleaner, more controlled contact.
RELAXED BUT FIRM: the body should be relaxed during the approach but firm and committed at the moment of contact - tension throughout the whole body reduces fluency.
commonMistakes: Approaching the ball straight on rather than at an angle, limiting the swing of the leg
Plant foot positioned too far from the ball or pointing in the wrong direction
Ankle loose at the moment of contact, reducing power and causing inconsistent strikes
Lifting the head to look at the target before striking the ball
Trying to generate power through effort and tension rather than correct technique
Short or incomplete follow-through
corrections: Straight approach: place a cone at a 30-45 degree angle from the ball and ask the player to start their run from that cone
Plant foot issues: draw a marker on the ground exactly where the plant foot should land, pointing toward the target
Loose ankle: ask the player to point their toes down and hold the ankle firm before each strike as a conscious check
Lifting the head early: place a sticker on the ball and ask the player to call out the sticker pattern immediately after the strike
Tension and effort: slow the whole movement down by 50 percent and ask the player to focus purely on smooth, relaxed technique
Short follow-through: place a cone 1m past the ball in the direction of the kicking leg swing - the foot must reach past it
progressions: Increase the distance to the target gradually as technique becomes consistent, building power naturally
Add a moving ball - the coach rolls the ball gently and the player must strike it on the move
Introduce a target zone within the larger target area, rewarding accuracy as well as power
Time-based challenge: how many accurate, well-struck balls can be hit in 60 seconds
regressions: Reduce the approach to a single step rather than a full run-up while technique is established
Reduce the distance significantly to build confidence with clean contact before adding power
Allow a slower, more deliberate strike without emphasis on power until the technique is consistent
matchApplication: Clean, powerful striking technique is the foundation for shooting, long passing, clearances, and goal kicks throughout a players entire career. Many of the technical errors seen in older players - inconsistent shots, lack of power, poor accuracy - trace back to fundamentals that were never properly established at a young age. Teaching the correct approach, plant foot position, contact point, and follow-through at U11 builds a technical base that pays dividends in every striking action a player performs from this point forward, whether shooting, passing, or clearing the ball under pressure.
coachTips: This is one of the most important foundational technical sessions in a young players development - take the time to get it right rather than rushing through it.
Demonstrate the technique yourself in slow motion so players can clearly see each component before attempting it themselves.
Resist the temptation to emphasise power too early - power that comes from poor technique reinforces bad habits that are very difficult to correct later.
Use a wall if available - the immediate visual and audible feedback (where the ball goes, how it sounds) helps players self-correct quickly.
Film a few players and show them their own technique alongside the key coaching points - seeing themselves accelerates understanding.
diagramDescription: Player shown 12-15m from a wall or target. Approach angle shown at 30-45 degrees with a 3-5 step run-up. Plant foot position marked beside the ball pointing toward the target. Contact point diagram shows the laces meeting the centre of the ball. Follow-through arrow shows the kicking leg continuing upward and forward after the strike.

title: Driven Strike Power Development
objective: Develop power and accuracy in the driven strike technique used for shooting, long passing, and clearances.
learningOutcomes: Players can generate significant power while maintaining accuracy in their strike
Players understand the technical relationship between approach speed and strike power
Players develop consistency in their striking technique under increasing physical demand
Players can apply driven strike technique to both shooting and long passing situations
equipment: Target wall, mini-goals, or full-size goal
organisation: Players positioned 20-25m from a target (wall with marked zones, mini-goals, or full-size goal). Sufficient ball supply to allow continuous striking with minimal interruption. Multiple stations if group size requires it.
instructions: Begin with a full review of the foundational technique: angled approach, plant foot position, locked ankle, centre contact, full follow-through.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): strike at 70 percent effort, prioritising clean technique and consistent contact over power. 10 strikes minimum.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): increase to 90 percent effort while maintaining the same technical principles. Focus specifically on whether technique holds up as power increases.

PHASE 3 (6 minutes): full power strikes (100 percent effort) with a target requirement - the ball must hit a marked zone, not just travel with power. This combines maximum power with maintained accuracy.
Throughout: emphasise that power comes from a longer, faster approach combined with correct hip rotation and full follow-through, not from tensing the body and swinging harder.
Track distance and accuracy across the three phases to demonstrate the relationship between technique and outcome.
coachingPoints: POWER FROM APPROACH SPEED: a faster, more committed approach run generates significantly more strike power than a slow approach with maximum leg effort.
HIP ROTATION IS THE ENGINE: the rotation of the hips through the strike, not the strength of the leg muscles alone, is the primary source of power in a driven strike.
TECHNIQUE UNDER INCREASING DEMAND: as effort and power increase, technique often deteriorates first at the plant foot and the locked ankle - monitor these specifically.
MAINTAIN THE LOCKED ANKLE AT MAXIMUM EFFORT: this is the technical element most likely to break down under full power and is the primary cause of mishits at high intensity.
FULL FOLLOW-THROUGH AT POWER: the temptation at maximum effort is to stop the leg shortly after contact - resist this and continue the full follow-through every time.
BALANCE UNDER POWER: a powerful strike still requires balance throughout the body - players who lean back excessively to generate power lose both accuracy and consistency.
ACCURACY DOES NOT DISAPPEAR AT POWER: the goal is power AND accuracy together, not power instead of accuracy. A powerful strike that misses the target entirely has limited value.
BREATHE OUT THROUGH THE STRIKE: exhaling through the contact point helps engage the core and supports a powerful, controlled strike.
CONSISTENCY ACROSS REPETITIONS: the aim is reliable power and accuracy across many strikes, not a single exceptional strike followed by inconsistent ones.
commonMistakes: Technique breaking down (particularly the locked ankle and plant foot) as power effort increases
Trying to generate power through upper body tension rather than hip rotation and approach speed
Leaning back excessively at the moment of contact in an attempt to generate more power
Shortening the follow-through at maximum effort
Sacrificing all accuracy in pursuit of pure power, missing the target entirely
Inconsistent results across repeated strikes rather than reliable repeatable technique
corrections: Technique breakdown under power: reduce the effort level back to 80 percent until the technique holds, then gradually rebuild to 100 percent
Upper body tension: ask the player to consciously relax their shoulders and arms before each strike, channelling effort through the hips and legs instead
Leaning back: place a cone slightly in front of the striking position - the players head should stay forward of the cone at the moment of contact
Short follow-through: maintain the cone marker 1m past the ball requiring the kicking leg to reach beyond it even at full power
Sacrificing accuracy: introduce a minimum accuracy requirement (the ball must hit a defined zone) at every power level, including maximum effort
Inconsistent results: track results over 10 consecutive strikes and identify patterns - is the inconsistency in direction, height, or power
progressions: Add a moving ball requiring the player to time their powerful strike on a ball that is rolling or bouncing
Introduce a defender applying light pressure during the approach, replicating match conditions for a shot or clearance
Combine with a passing target at distance - the driven strike technique applied to a 30m+ pass to a specific target zone
Competition format: track maximum recorded ball speed or distance combined with accuracy across a session
regressions: Reduce the target distance and the power demand, rebuilding from a strong technical base before progressing
Allow more repetitions at 70 percent effort if technique consistently breaks down at higher intensities
Return to the foundational technique drill for additional sessions if the basic mechanics are not yet reliable
matchApplication: Power in the driven strike technique has direct application across multiple match situations: shooting from distance, clearing the ball under defensive pressure, and delivering accurate long passes that switch play or find a forward outlet. The specific coaching emphasis in this drill - that power comes from technique (approach speed and hip rotation) rather than effort and tension - is one of the most important lessons in a players technical development, because it explains why some players generate far more power than their physical strength alone would suggest. Players who master this relationship between technique and power develop a genuine and lasting advantage in any striking action.
coachTips: The progression from 70 to 90 to 100 percent effort is deliberately structured to reveal exactly where technique breaks down for each player - use this information to focus future sessions.

Some players will generate impressive power immediately due to natural athleticism, while others will need more repetitions to find it through technique - both outcomes are valuable and should be coached accordingly.
A radar gun or simple stopwatch-based distance measurement, if available, provides excellent motivating feedback for players at this age.
The locked ankle coaching point deserves repeated emphasis throughout the session, as it is consistently the technical element most likely to fail under increased effort.
Connect this technique explicitly to specific match situations the players will recognise: a driven shot from outside the box, a long clearance under pressure, a raking pass to switch play.
diagramDescription: Player shown 20-25m from a target wall or goal with marked zones. Three effort-level phases shown progressively: 70 percent (technique focus), 90 percent (technique under demand), 100 percent (maximum power with accuracy requirement). Approach run shown at increasing speed across the phases. Hip rotation diagram emphasised as the power source. Target zones marked on the wall or goal showing the accuracy requirement at each power level.

title: Advanced Shielding and Spin Away
objective: Develop the ability to shield the ball under intense pressure and spin away into space when the opportunity arises.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain shielding position against a fully active, physical defender
Players develop the technique to spin away from a shielding position into open space
Players understand the timing of when to release from a shield versus when to continue holding
Players build physical and mental resilience under sustained defensive pressure
organisation: 4x4m zone per pair. Attacker receives the ball with their back to the defender (replicating a striker holding up play) and must shield it under full physical pressure before either releasing to a support player or spinning away.
instructions: Attacker receives a pass with their back to the defender, who is positioned close behind, ready to apply full physical pressure.
On receiving, the attacker immediately adopts the shielding position: body between ball and defender, low centre of gravity, wide stable stance.
The defender applies genuine physical pressure - leaning in, trying to reach around for the ball, using legal contact to unbalance the attacker.
The attacker must hold the shield for a minimum of 3 seconds while reading the situation.
DECISION POINT: if a support player becomes available, release the ball to them with an accurate pass. If no support is available and the defenders pressure creates an opening, execute a spin away.

SPIN AWAY TECHNIQUE: using the shielding body position, pivot sharply on the standing foot, dragging the ball with the other foot in the new direction, accelerating immediately away from the defender.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): focus purely on holding the shield under pressure for the full 3 seconds without losing the ball.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): add a support player 8m away who becomes available at random moments - attacker must recognise and release.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live decision-making - attacker chooses between releasing to support or spinning away based on what the defender gives them.
coachingPoints: MAINTAIN THE SHIELDING FUNDAMENTALS UNDER PRESSURE: low centre of gravity, wide stance, body between ball and defender - these do not change just because the pressure has increased.
USE LEGAL CONTACT TO YOUR ADVANTAGE: the shoulder and upper arm can be used to feel and resist the defenders pressure within the laws of the game - use this contact actively rather than passively.
READ THE DEFENDERS PRESSURE PATTERN: a defender who is pushing hard from one side creates an opportunity to spin away on the other side, using their own momentum against them.
THE SPIN IS A SINGLE FLUID MOTION: the pivot and the ball-drag happen together, not as two separate actions - any hesitation between them gives the defender time to recover.
