ACCELERATE IMMEDIATELY AFTER THE SPIN: just as with any other 1v1 technique, the spin only creates value if followed immediately by acceleration into the new space.
KNOW WHEN TO RELEASE RATHER THAN SPIN: if a simple release to a support player is available and safe, this is often the lower-risk and more effective option than attempting a spin away.
STAY CALM UNDER PHYSICAL PRESSURE: the mental composure to continue making good decisions while being physically challenged is as important as the technical shielding ability.
PROTECT THE BALL WITH BOTH SHOULDER AND BODY: use the whole upper body, not just the shoulder, to create a barrier between the ball and the defender.
commonMistakes: Shielding position collapsing under sustained physical pressure due to an unstable base
Holding the ball too long without making a decision, eventually losing it to the defender
Spin away attempted too slowly, allowing the defender to recover and intercept
Always choosing to spin rather than recognising when a simple release is the better option
Using the arm to push the defender illegally rather than legal shoulder-to-shoulder contact
Losing composure and panicking under physical pressure, leading to a rushed and poor decision
corrections: Position collapsing: practice the shielding stance separately against resistance (a coach or teammate pushing gently) before adding the full live scenario
Holding too long: introduce a maximum 5-second shield time after which a decision must be made, win or lose the ball
Slow spin: break the spin into its two components (pivot, then drag) and practice each slowly before combining them at speed
Always spinning: specifically reward successful releases to a support player with equal value to a successful spin, removing any bias toward one option
Illegal arm use: any clear push with the arm immediately results in the ball being awarded to the defender, creating a clear consequence
Losing composure: practice the shielding technique with verbal coaching cues (stay calm, find your balance) repeated during the physical pressure phase
progressions: Two defenders applying pressure from different angles, significantly increasing the difficulty of maintaining the shield
Add a time pressure element - a third defender recovers from distance and the attacker must decide and act before they arrive
Combine with a finishing element - after a successful spin away, the attacker has a clear run at a mini-goal
Full small-sided game where successful holds-and-releases or holds-and-spins under pressure earn bonus points
regressions: Reduce the defenders pressure to moderate rather than maximum physical intensity while the technique is being established
Increase the zone size slightly to give the attacker more room to manoeuvre during the spin
Remove the time pressure (3-second minimum hold) and allow the attacker to decide whenever they are ready
matchApplication: Advanced shielding combined with the ability to spin away is one of the defining qualities of an effective target forward or a midfielder who needs to retain possession in tight, physically demanding areas. The capacity to hold off a defender, read the situation, and either bring others into play or escape with a sudden change of direction creates value in exactly the moments when a team most needs an outlet under pressure. This is a genuinely difficult skill that combines physical resilience, technical control, and tactical decision-making, and players who develop it become extremely valuable in build-up and transition play.
coachTips: The physical element of this drill is significant - ensure players understand what legal contact looks like before introducing full pressure.
Some players will naturally favour releasing the ball while others will favour spinning - both are valid options and the best players develop genuine competence in both.
The decision-making element (release versus spin) is as important as the technical execution and deserves equal coaching attention.
Connect this skill explicitly to the role of a target forward in match play - players who play that position will find this drill particularly relevant to their game.
Film the spin technique from multiple angles if possible - the pivot-and-drag combination is genuinely difficult to coach through verbal instruction alone.
diagramDescription: 4x4m zone per pair. Attacker (circle) shown receiving with back to defender (triangle), adopting a low, wide shielding stance. Defender shown applying physical pressure from behind with a contact arrow. Two decision-outcome arrows shown: release to support player 8m away, or spin away using a pivot-and-drag motion shown as a curved arrow with an acceleration arrow continuing into open space.

title: Weak Foot Match Application
objective: Develop the confidence and reliability to use the weak foot as a genuine match-ready option in passing, shooting, and 1v1 situations.
learningOutcomes: Players can use their weak foot reliably under live competitive pressure
Players develop the decision-making to choose the weak foot when it is genuinely the better technical option
Players reduce their predictability to opponents by demonstrating genuine two-footedness
Players build long-term confidence in their weak foot through realistic match-context repetition
organisation: 40x30m small-sided game area incorporating passing, dribbling, and finishing actions. The key constraint throughout: any action performed when the ball is on the weak-foot side of the body must be completed with the weak foot, not switched to the strong foot.
instructions: Explain the central rule clearly before starting: if the ball arrives or sits on your weak-foot side, you must use your weak foot for that action - no switching the ball across your body to use the strong foot.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): unopposed passing circuit. Players pass in a square formation, with every pass and control performed according to the weak-foot rule based on which side the ball arrives.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): small-sided possession game (4v4) with the weak-foot rule in effect. Coach monitors and stops play immediately if the rule is broken, resetting possession.
PHASE 3 (8 minutes): small-sided game with goals (4v4 plus GK, or two mini-goals). The weak-foot rule remains in effect, including for shots at goal.
Throughout: emphasise that this is not an artificial restriction but training for what should happen naturally in match play - using whichever foot the situation calls for.
coachingPoints: THE RULE REFLECTS GENUINE MATCH LOGIC: in a real match, the ball does not always arrive on your strong-foot side. A player who only uses their strong foot is forced into extra touches, slower play, and predictability.
TRUST THE TECHNIQUE YOU HAVE BUILT: by this stage of development the weak foot technique should be technically sound - the barrier remaining is confidence and habit, not raw ability.
DO NOT SWITCH THE BALL ACROSS YOUR BODY: the habit of taking an extra touch to move the ball to the strong foot is exactly the inefficiency this drill is designed to eliminate.
FIRST TOUCH PREPARATION: anticipate which foot a pass will arrive on and prepare mentally and physically for that foot before the ball arrives.

WEAK FOOT UNDER PRESSURE IS DIFFERENT FROM WEAK FOOT IN ISOLATION: performing technique calmly in an unopposed drill is different from doing so with a defender closing down - this live practice bridges that gap.
CONFIDENCE COMPOUNDS: every successful weak-foot action under pressure builds confidence for the next one - the improvement in this area is often more psychological than technical at this stage.
ACCEPT SOME ERRORS AS PART OF THE PROCESS: weak-foot actions under live pressure will be less polished than strong-foot actions initially - this is expected and is how the gap closes over time.
APPLY TO SHOOTING SPECIFICALLY: a player who can finish confidently with either foot when the ball arrives on that side dramatically increases their goal-scoring efficiency.
commonMistakes: Switching the ball across the body to use the strong foot despite the rule, reverting under pressure
Hesitating significantly when the ball arrives on the weak-foot side, slowing down the whole sequence
Avoiding positions or movements that would result in receiving on the weak-foot side
Technical quality dropping significantly compared to strong-foot actions in the same session
Frustration or discouragement when weak-foot actions are less successful than strong-foot ones
Reverting to safe, simple options exclusively when using the weak foot rather than playing normally
corrections: Switching across body: any clear switch results in an immediate turnover of possession, creating a real consequence
Hesitating: count the time taken to control and release the ball - challenge players to reduce this time across the session
Avoiding weak-foot positions: the coach actively identifies and notes which players are positioning themselves to avoid weak-foot situations, providing direct feedback
Quality drop: reduce the speed of play slightly if quality drops significantly, rebuilding composure before returning to full pace
Frustration: provide specific positive reinforcement after every committed weak-foot attempt regardless of outcome
Reverting to safe options: encourage and reward more ambitious weak-foot actions (a forward pass rather than a backward one, a shot rather than a square ball)
progressions: Remove the explicit rule and instead simply track and reward weak-foot usage statistics across a normal small-sided game
Add a specific weak-foot bonus scoring system - goals or key passes with the weak foot earn extra points
Increase the intensity and game speed to further close the gap between training and match conditions
Apply the same principle in a full training match across an entire session, with the coach tracking weak-foot usage informally
regressions: Reduce the rule to apply only to passing initially, before extending to dribbling and shooting
Reduce game intensity to allow more thinking time for weak-foot decisions while the habit is being built
Apply the rule for shorter periods (5 minutes at a time) within a normal game rather than for the entire session
matchApplication: By U16, technical ability with the strong foot is well established for most committed players, and genuine two-footedness becomes one of the clearest differentiators between good and excellent players. A player who is only comfortable on one foot is fundamentally more predictable and less efficient, regardless of how skilled they are on their preferred side. This drill specifically targets the gap between technical capability (which may already exist from years of isolated weak-foot practice) and match-ready confidence and habit (which only develops through realistic, pressured, competitive repetition). The investment in weak-foot development through the teenage years pays significant dividends as players progress toward senior and representative football.
coachTips: By this age, frame weak-foot development explicitly in terms of competitive advantage and potential pathway opportunities - older players respond well to this framing.

Be prepared for some frustration in phase 2 and 3 as players are confronted with the gap between their weak-foot ability in isolation versus under pressure - this is valuable information, not a failure.
Track specific data if possible: successful weak-foot actions as a percentage of total weak-foot opportunities, and discuss the trend over several sessions.
This drill works very well as a recurring monthly session rather than a single one-off - genuine match-ready weak-foot confidence develops over months, not single sessions.

Identify and have honest conversations with players who are clearly avoiding weak-foot situations tactically (subtly positioning to always receive on their strong side) - this avoidance pattern needs to be addressed directly.
diagramDescription: 40x30m small-sided game area with mini-goals or full-size goal and GK. Players shown in a 4v4 format. Weak-foot zone indicators shown on each player illustrating which side requires weak-foot use based on ball position. Pass and control arrows shown specifically on the weak-foot side. Shooting action highlighted in the finishing zone showing weak-foot finish requirement when the ball arrives on that side.

title: Switching Play to Exploit Space
objective: Develop the recognition and execution of switching the point of attack from one side of the pitch to the other.
learningOutcomes: Players recognise when one side of the pitch is overloaded and the other is open
Players can execute a switch pass across the width of the pitch with accuracy
Players understand the supporting movement required after a switch is played
Players develop patience to build toward a switch rather than forcing it immediately
pitchSize: 40x30m area divided into thirds (left, centre, right)
organisation: 40x30m area split into 3 vertical thirds. Two teams of 5 play possession. A mini-goal sits in each outer third. Goals can only be scored after the ball has been played from one outer third to the other (a switch) within the previous 3 passes.
instructions: Teams play normal possession football within the area.
To score in either mini-goal, the team must have played a successful switch pass (left third directly to right third or vice versa) within the last 3 passes before the shot.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): walk through the concept with no opposition. One team practices building possession on one side then switching to the other before scoring.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): live opposition. Teams must read when the ball side is congested and identify the moment to switch.

PHASE 3 (6 minutes): remove the thirds restriction and allow free play, but the switch-pass goal bonus remains in place (3 points for a goal scored within 3 passes of a switch, 1 point for any other goal).
coachingPoints: RECOGNISE THE OVERLOAD: before attempting a switch, identify that the ball side genuinely has more defenders than passing options. A switch played into an equally crowded area achieves nothing.
BUILD PATIENCE BEFORE SWITCHING: do not rush the switch. Circulate the ball on the congested side first to draw defenders across, which creates more space on the far side.
THE SWITCH PASS ITSELF: must be firm, accurate, and travel quickly enough that the defence cannot recover before the receiving player can use the space.
BODY SHAPE BEFORE RECEIVING THE SWITCH: the central player who plays the switch should be on the half-turn, able to see both sides of the pitch before the ball arrives to them.
SUPPORT THE SWITCH IMMEDIATELY: as soon as the switch is played, at least one extra player should move to support the receiving side, since defenders will recover quickly.
DISGUISE WHERE POSSIBLE: look toward the congested side before playing the switch pass - this delays the defence reading the switch and gains extra time for the receiver.
DO NOT SWITCH FOR ITS OWN SAKE: a switch that moves the ball to a side with no better opportunity is not a good decision - the principle is to find genuine space, not simply to vary the play.
RECEIVING PLAYER MUST BE READY: the player on the far side should be alert and showing for the switch the moment the ball begins to circulate, not standing passively.
commonMistakes: Attempting to switch when the far side is equally as congested as the near side
Rushing the switch before the ball side has genuinely drawn defenders across
Switch pass too slow or inaccurate, allowing the defence to recover before it arrives
No support movement after the switch is played, isolating the receiving player
Players on the far side standing passively rather than preparing to receive
Telegraphing the switch so clearly that the defence intercepts it
corrections: Switching to equally congested side: pause play and ask both teams to describe where the space genuinely is before continuing
Rushing the switch: introduce a minimum of 4 passes on the ball side before any switch is permitted to count for bonus points
Slow or inaccurate switch: introduce a 2-second maximum time limit from the decision to switch to the ball arriving at the far side
No support after switch: award an additional bonus point if a second player arrives to support within 3 seconds of the switch
Passive far-side players: specifically coach the far-side players to make a visible movement (a check-away and back) as the switch is building
Telegraphing: ask the switching player to look toward the congested side immediately before playing the switch pass
progressions: Reduce the time allowed to build before switching, increasing the tempo and difficulty of the read
Add a defender specifically tasked with covering the switch pass lane, forcing more sophisticated disguise
Full-side game with the switch-goal bonus system retained as the primary scoring incentive
Introduce a central zone where the ball cannot be played, forcing the switch to go directly between outer thirds
regressions: Remove the live opposition for longer, allowing the switching pattern to be rehearsed without pressure
Widen the thirds slightly to make the switch pass distance shorter and more achievable
Allow more passes (5 or 6) before the switch is required to count, reducing the pressure to act quickly
matchApplication: Switching the point of attack is one of the most effective ways to break down a well-organised defence, because most defensive systems are built to compress space around the ball and therefore concede space on the far side. Teams that recognise this and switch play decisively create regular opportunities in space that would not otherwise exist. This is a foundational tactical principle used at every level of football from youth to professional, and introducing the concept and the supporting habits (patience, disguise, support movement) at U13 builds the tactical intelligence that becomes increasingly valuable as players progress.
coachTips: The concept of why a switch works (defenders compress toward the ball, leaving space on the far side) is worth explaining explicitly with a diagram or walkthrough before live play begins.
Watch specifically for the patience element - many young players will try to switch immediately rather than building the overload first, and this is the habit most worth correcting.
The bonus scoring system is what makes this drill effective - without an incentive, teams will often default to their habitual style of play rather than practising the switch.
Connect to professional examples: show a clip of a team building on one side before switching play to exploit space on the other.
This is a good drill to revisit across several sessions, as the tactical recognition takes time to become instinctive rather than something players need to be reminded to look for.
diagramDescription: 40x30m area divided into 3 vertical thirds (left, centre, right) by cone lines. Mini-goal in each outer third. Team in possession shown building play in the left third with multiple players and passing triangles. Switch pass arrow shown travelling directly from the left third to the right third, bypassing the centre. Supporting player arrow shows a second player moving to support the right side immediately after the switch.

title: Transition to Attack
objective: Develop the speed and decision-making required to transition effectively from defending to attacking the moment possession is won.
learningOutcomes: Players recognise the moment of transition and react with immediate purpose
Players understand the priority order of options when winning the ball: forward first, then sideways, then backward
Players develop the physical and mental readiness to attack at speed immediately after a defensive action
Teams build a coordinated pattern of movement in the seconds after winning possession
organisation: Two teams play a possession-based game. Whenever Team A loses the ball, Team B has a clear transition opportunity and must react immediately according to the transition principles. The coach calls TRANSITION loudly at the moment possession changes to draw attention to the moment.
instructions: Game is played with normal rules - both teams attempt to keep possession and progress the ball.
The moment possession changes (interception, tackle, loose ball won), the coach calls TRANSITION.
The team that has just won the ball must immediately look forward first - is there a forward pass or run available before the opposition can organise defensively?
If no forward option exists immediately, the next priority is a sideways pass to maintain possession and create a new angle.
Backward passes are the lowest priority and should only be used if no forward or sideways option is safe.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): the coach pauses play briefly at each transition moment to ask the team in possession what their priority option is, reinforcing the decision-making framework.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): live, continuous play with the TRANSITION call but no pause - players must apply the framework in real time.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): add a bonus scoring rule - any goal scored within 8 seconds of a transition moment earns 3 points instead of 1, incentivising quick, purposeful transitions.
coachingPoints: THE FIRST 2-3 SECONDS ARE CRITICAL: the moment after winning the ball, the opposition is still in their attacking shape and disorganised defensively. This window closes quickly.
LOOK FORWARD FIRST: the instinctive reaction for many players is to play safe and sideways. Train the habit of scanning forward immediately upon winning the ball.
PLAYERS WITHOUT THE BALL MUST ALSO TRANSITION: it is not only the ball-winner who must react instantly - every nearby teammate should immediately look for a forward run or supporting position.
DO NOT SLOW DOWN TO ASSESS: elite transitional play happens at speed - players read and react simultaneously rather than stopping to think before moving.
WIDE PLAYERS SPRINT IMMEDIATELY: if your team wins the ball, wide players should be sprinting into space the instant possession changes, not waiting to see what happens.
COMPOSURE WITHIN SPEED: fast does not mean reckless - the forward pass must still be a good decision and well-executed, not just quick for its own sake.
DEFENSIVE TRANSITION IS THE MIRROR IMAGE: the team that has just lost the ball must also react instantly, sprinting to recover position and prevent exactly the attacking transition described above.
COMMUNICATION DURING TRANSITION: the ball-winner benefits enormously from teammates calling for the ball or directing them toward the best option in the chaotic moment after winning possession.
commonMistakes: Pausing to assess after winning the ball rather than reacting with immediate purpose
Defaulting to a safe sideways or backward pass when a forward option genuinely existed
Players without the ball failing to make supporting or forward runs in the transition moment
Treating the transition the same as settled possession play, missing the brief window of opportunity
Rushing a poor decision purely for the sake of speed, losing the ball needlessly
Defending team failing to recognise their own need to transition instantly when possession is lost
corrections: Pausing to assess: use the coach call and immediate freeze technique in phase 1 specifically to highlight and correct this pattern
Defaulting to safe options: after any sideways pass immediately following a transition, ask the player whether a forward option existed
No supporting runs: specifically coach the 2 nearest players to the ball-winner to make an immediate forward or wide run on every transition
Treating transition as settled play: use the TRANSITION call consistently and loudly enough that players cannot ignore the moment
Rushing poor decisions: balance the speed emphasis with a clear standard - quick AND correct, not quick instead of correct
Defending team slow to react: award the attacking team an additional bonus point if the defending team has not recovered to a compact shape within 5 seconds
progressions: Remove the coach call entirely and require players to recognise transition moments independently
Add specific zones from which a transition goal scores extra points, encouraging direct forward play through the centre
Full match-realistic game with no restrictions other than the bonus scoring system for quick transitions
Introduce a numerical disadvantage for the team in transition to increase the difficulty of the decision-making
regressions: Slow the game down initially and use the pause-and-ask method throughout, building the decision framework before adding speed
Reduce the playing area to make forward options more immediately visible and accessible
Remove the bonus scoring system temporarily and focus purely on the correct decision-making pattern without the pressure of reward
matchApplication: The speed and quality of a teams transition from defending to attacking is one of the most significant factors in modern football, responsible for a large proportion of goals scored at every level. Teams like Liverpool under Klopp built much of their attacking identity around exploiting the immediate moments after winning the ball, when the opposition is at their most vulnerable. Training players to recognise this moment, prioritise forward options, and react with both speed and good decision-making creates a genuine attacking weapon and develops the tactical awareness that becomes increasingly important as players progress to higher levels of the game.
coachTips: The TRANSITION call is a simple but highly effective coaching tool - it draws conscious attention to a moment that often passes unnoticed in normal play.
Be specific in your feedback about WHY a particular option was the right or wrong choice in each transition moment, building genuine understanding rather than just compliance.
This drill rewards repeated exposure - the recognition of transition moments and the instinct to react quickly develops over many sessions, not a single one.

Connect explicitly to professional examples: show footage of a quick defensive-to-attacking transition leading directly to a goal, and discuss what each player did in the moments after winning the ball.
Both sides of the transition matter equally - do not let the attacking transition coaching overshadow the equally important defensive transition.
diagramDescription: Full pitch or large area. Two teams shown in a possession game. TRANSITION moment highlighted with a star at the point where possession changes from Team A to Team B. Forward-first priority arrow shown as the primary option, with sideways and backward options shown as lower-priority alternatives. Supporting player movement arrows shown from 2 nearby teammates making immediate forward or wide runs. Defending team shown with recovery arrows sprinting back to compact shape.

title: Transition to Defence
objective: Develop the immediate defensive reaction and organisation required the moment a team loses possession.
learningOutcomes: Players react instantly to losing possession rather than dwelling on the lost ball
Players understand the priority actions in defensive transition: delay, recover shape, and prevent the counter-attack
Teams develop a coordinated counter-press or recovery pattern depending on the situation
Players build the mental discipline to switch immediately from an attacking to a defensive mindset
organisation: Two teams play a possession-based game. Whenever Team A loses the ball, the coach immediately assesses Team As reaction. Two distinct defensive transition options are taught and practised: the immediate counter-press and the organised recovery.
instructions: Game is played with normal rules. The moment a team loses possession, the coach calls LOST BALL loudly.
OPTION 1 - COUNTER-PRESS: if the team has at least 2 players within 5m of the ball when possession is lost, they immediately and aggressively press to win the ball back within 5 seconds.

OPTION 2 - ORGANISED RECOVERY: if the team does not have players close enough to counter-press effectively, all players immediately sprint to recover into a compact defensive shape rather than chasing the ball individually.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): the coach pauses play at each lost-ball moment and asks the team which option applies and why, reinforcing the decision framework.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): live play with the LOST BALL call but no pause - the team must recognise and execute the correct option in real time.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): full live game with both transition principles in effect simultaneously, replicating genuine match conditions.
coachingPoints: THE FIRST REACTION MATTERS MOST: the immediate response in the first 1-2 seconds after losing the ball determines whether a counter-press or recovery is even possible.
COUNT THE NUMBERS NEAR THE BALL: if 2 or more teammates are genuinely close to the ball when possession is lost, an immediate aggressive counter-press has a realistic chance of success.

COUNTER-PRESS WITH FULL COMMITMENT: a half-hearted counter-press achieves nothing and can leave a team exposed - if the decision is to press, it must be immediate and aggressive from all nearby players.

THE 5-SECOND RULE: if the ball is not won back within approximately 5 seconds of a counter-press, the team should abandon the press and recover into shape rather than continuing to chase individually.

SPRINT, DO NOT JOG, TO RECOVER: when the organised recovery option applies, every player must sprint to their defensive position - a jogged recovery gives the opposition time to exploit the disorganisation.
COMMUNICATION DURING TRANSITION: the player closest to the ball should call PRESS or DROP loudly to inform teammates which option the team is executing.
PROTECT THE MOST DANGEROUS SPACE FIRST: when recovering, prioritise covering the central, most dangerous areas before worrying about wide areas.

MENTAL SWITCH IS INSTANT: there should be no pause or moment of frustration after losing the ball - elite players switch their mindset from attacking to defending in the same instant possession changes.
commonMistakes: Hesitating or showing frustration after losing the ball rather than reacting instantly
Attempting to counter-press without sufficient numbers nearby, leaving gaps for the opposition to exploit
Half-hearted counter-pressing that neither wins the ball back nor recovers shape effectively
Jogging rather than sprinting during the organised recovery, allowing the opposition time and space
No communication about which option the team is executing, causing confusion among teammates
Continuing to chase the ball individually well past the 5-second window rather than recovering into shape
corrections: Hesitating after losing ball: use the LOST BALL call consistently and look specifically for the immediate reaction in the first second
Counter-pressing without numbers: in phase 1, explicitly count the players within 5m before confirming whether a counter-press is the correct option
Half-hearted pressing: any counter-press attempt must be at full intensity - a low-effort press is treated as a non-attempt and the team must immediately switch to recovery instead
Jogging recovery: time the recovery from the LOST BALL call to the team reaching a compact shape, challenging the team to reduce this time across the session
No communication: make the PRESS or DROP call mandatory from the player closest to the ball on every transition
Chasing past 5 seconds: use an audible signal at 5 seconds during phase 1 and 2 as a clear cue to switch from pressing to recovering
progressions: Remove the coach call and require the team to recognise and react to lost-ball moments entirely independently
Add a numerical disadvantage for the defending team in certain phases, testing decision-making under additional pressure
Full match-realistic game with the bonus scoring system from the transition-to-attack drill also applied
Introduce specific positional roles in the defensive transition for a more sophisticated team structure
regressions: Slow the game down and use the pause-and-confirm method throughout while the two transition options are being learned
Simplify to a single option (organised recovery only) before introducing the more complex counter-press decision
Reduce the playing area to make recovery distances shorter and more immediately achievable
matchApplication: Defensive transition is widely regarded by elite coaches as one of the most important moments in a match, because a disorganised reaction to losing the ball is one of the most common causes of conceding goals at every level of football. Teams that react instantly and correctly prevent the exact kind of attacking transition opportunities that the corresponding attacking drill is designed to exploit. Developing this instinctive, correct reaction at U15 builds defensive resilience that becomes increasingly valuable as players face faster, more clinical opposition at higher levels.
coachTips: This drill works best when taught alongside or shortly after the transition-to-attack drill, since the two are mirror images of the same tactical moment.
The decision framework is genuinely sophisticated - expect this to take several sessions before it becomes instinctive.
Watch specifically for the emotional reaction to losing the ball - frustration or dwelling on the mistake is the single biggest obstacle to an instant, effective defensive transition.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of an elite teams immediate reaction to losing possession.
Praise instant, correct reactions explicitly and consistently - this is a habit that responds very well to positive reinforcement in training.
diagramDescription: Full pitch or large area. Two teams shown in a possession game. LOST BALL moment highlighted with a star where Team A loses possession. Two branching options shown: OPTION 1 (counter-press, shown with 2+ nearby players aggressively closing down the ball within a 5m radius) and OPTION 2 (organised recovery, shown with all players sprinting back to a compact defensive shape covering the central dangerous area first). 5-second timer icon shown indicating the threshold for abandoning the press.

title: Mid Block Defensive Organisation
objective: Develop the team shape, compactness, and pressing triggers required to defend effectively in a mid-block defensive structure.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the purpose and structure of a mid-block defensive shape
Players maintain compactness both horizontally and vertically as a defending unit
Players recognise and react to pressing triggers within the mid-block system
Teams develop the patience to allow the opposition possession in deeper areas while remaining organised
organisation: Full pitch, two full teams. The defending team is instructed to set up in a mid-block: pressing begins only once the opposition crosses the halfway line, with the team holding a compact shape between their own penalty area and the halfway line.
instructions: The defending team sets a starting reference: the front line of pressure begins at the halfway line, not in the opposition half.
As the opposition builds possession in their own half, the defending team holds their compact mid-block shape, allowing possession but not space.

PRESSING TRIGGERS within the mid-block: a heavy touch by an opponent, a sideways or backward pass, or the ball entering a wide area are all signals for the nearest defender to press while others hold the shape.
VERTICAL COMPACTNESS: the distance between the front line of the block and the back line should not exceed approximately 25-30m at any time.
HORIZONTAL COMPACTNESS: as the ball moves side to side, the entire block shifts together, maintaining narrow spacing between players on the same line.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): walkthrough with the opposition team static, allowing the defending team to physically rehearse the shifting and compacting movements.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): live opposition building from the back. Defending team must hold the mid-block shape and react to triggers as they occur.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full game conditions. Defending team is scored on their ability to maintain the mid-block and to win the ball in the middle third.
coachingPoints: PATIENCE IS THE FOUNDATION: a mid-block requires the discipline to allow the opposition possession in deeper areas without pressing prematurely - this patience is often the hardest element to coach.
THE BLOCK MOVES AS A UNIT: no individual player presses independently of the team shape unless a clear trigger has occurred.
VERTICAL DISTANCE DISCIPLINE: if the gap between the front and back of the block becomes too large, the opposition can play through the gaps.
SHIFT TOGETHER, NOT INDIVIDUALLY: when the ball moves to one side, every player in the relevant line moves together to maintain the same spacing.
RECOGNISE THE TRIGGERS CLEARLY: a heavy touch, a backward pass, or the ball going wide are all moments when pressing intensifies.
DO NOT GET DRAWN OUT OF SHAPE: a common error is one player pressing aggressively and pulling the rest of the team out of their organised shape.
COMMUNICATION MAINTAINS THE SHAPE: players should constantly communicate about spacing and shifting.
WIN THE BALL IN THE MIDDLE THIRD: the purpose of the mid-block is to force the opposition into turnovers in the middle third, ideally setting up a quick transition to attack.
commonMistakes: Pressing too early or individually before a genuine trigger has occurred, breaking the compact shape
Vertical distance between the front and back of the block becoming too large, creating gaps to play through
Players failing to shift together as a unit when the ball changes side
Getting drawn out of position by an individual aggressive press, leaving gaps for the opposition
Lack of communication leading to confusion about spacing and shifting responsibilities
Block becoming too passive and allowing the opposition too much time and comfort in possession
corrections: Pressing too early: pause play immediately when an individual presses without a clear trigger and ask the team to identify what trigger was present
Vertical gap too large: use cone markers at the front and back of the intended block depth as a visual reference
Not shifting together: freeze play when the ball moves and check whether the entire relevant line has shifted
Drawn out of position: specifically coach the player pressing to recognise they must be supported by teammates compacting in to cover
Lack of communication: make it a rule that the furthest player from the ball must call instructions about spacing
Block too passive: introduce a minimum trigger response time - once a trigger occurs, the nearest player must press within 2 seconds
progressions: Reduce the time the opposition has to build before triggers must be acted upon, increasing the intensity
Introduce specific player roles within the mid-block for greater tactical sophistication
Full 11v11 match with the mid-block as the explicit defensive instruction throughout
Combine with the high-press drill, allowing the team to practise transitioning between a mid-block and a high press
regressions: Walk through the shape and shifting patterns extensively with no opposition before introducing any live pressure
Reduce the pitch width temporarily to make the compactness easier to achieve and understand
Simplify the trigger system to a single, very obvious trigger before introducing the full range of triggers
matchApplication: The mid-block is one of the most commonly used defensive structures in football at every level above grassroots, because it balances defensive solidity with the energy efficiency of not pressing high for the entire match. Teams that organise an effective mid-block force the opposition into errors in the middle third, which is often the ideal area to win the ball and launch a quick, dangerous transition to attack. The patience, compactness, and collective discipline required are genuinely sophisticated tactical qualities, and introducing them at U15 prepares players for the defensive structures they will encounter throughout senior football.
coachTips: This is a genuinely advanced tactical concept - expect the early sessions to feel disorganised as players learn to move as a collective unit rather than individually.
Describing the shape from the sideline in real time helps players understand the collective shape that is difficult to perceive from within the team.
The patience element is often the most difficult to coach because young players naturally want to press and win the ball back immediately.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of a team holding a disciplined mid-block shape.
This drill benefits from being revisited across multiple sessions over an extended period.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Defending team shown in a compact mid-block shape positioned between the halfway line and the edge of their own penalty area, with vertical distance between front and back lines marked at approximately 25-30m. Opposition shown building possession in their own half. Trigger moment highlighted with the nearest defender pressing while the rest of the block holds shape and shifts to compact toward the ball side.

title: Low Block Defending the Box
objective: Develop the discipline, organisation, and communication required to defend effectively in a deep, compact low block.
learningOutcomes: Players understand when and why a team adopts a low block defensive strategy
Players maintain extreme compactness in and around the penalty area
Players communicate effectively to track runners and cover dangerous spaces
Teams develop the patience and discipline to absorb sustained pressure without becoming disorganised
pitchSize: Full pitch with emphasis on the defensive third
organisation: Full pitch. The defending team sets up in a low block: the entire team retreats to within approximately 25-30m of their own goal once the opposition has possession in the attacking half, prioritising compactness and central protection over pressing the ball.
instructions: The defending team is instructed that once the opposition has settled possession in the attacking half, the priority is to retreat into a deep, narrow defensive shape rather than pressing.
TWO BANKS STRUCTURE: typically a back line and a midfield line positioned close together, both within 25-30m of the defending teams own goal.
CENTRAL PROTECTION FIRST: the priority is to deny space and passing lanes through the centre of the defence - width can be conceded more readily than central penetration.
TRACKING RUNNERS: as opposition players make runs into the box or between the lines, defenders must communicate and track these runners rather than ball-watching.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): walkthrough with the opposition static, allowing the defending team to physically rehearse the deep, narrow shape and communication patterns.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): live opposition attacking with possession in the final third. Defending team must hold the low block, defend crosses, and clear danger.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full game conditions with the defending team specifically instructed to maintain the low block for sustained periods, including dealing with set pieces and rebounds.
coachingPoints: COMPACTNESS IS EVERYTHING: in a low block, the distance between players must be even smaller than in a mid-block.
DO NOT CHASE THE BALL: the temptation to press the ball aggressively in a low block must be resisted - the priority is maintaining the defensive shape.
PROTECT THE CENTRE ABOVE ALL: opposition players in central areas close to goal are the most dangerous - prioritise denying this space.
COMMUNICATE CONSTANTLY: a low block requires near-constant verbal communication between defenders about tracking runners, covering gaps, and picking up loose attackers.
WIN THE HEADER, CLEAR THE DANGER: when crosses or long balls come into the box, the first priority is always to win the physical contest and clear the ball decisively.
PATIENCE UNDER SUSTAINED PRESSURE: maintaining discipline and shape throughout sustained pressure, rather than becoming disorganised through frustration, is essential.
GOALKEEPER AS AN ORGANISER: the goalkeeper has the best view of the defensive shape from a low block and should be communicating constantly.
RECOVERY AFTER CLEARING: after a defensive clearance, the team should not simply relax - second balls and rebounds are extremely common from a low block.
commonMistakes: Individual players pressing the ball aggressively and breaking the compact low block shape
Allowing space through the centre of the defence while focusing too much on wide areas
Ball-watching rather than tracking opposition runners making movements into dangerous areas
Lack of communication leading to defenders leaving gaps or being unsure who is responsible for which player or space
Losing discipline and shape after a long period of sustained opposition pressure
Relaxing immediately after a clearance rather than remaining alert for the second ball
corrections: Individual pressing: pause play whenever an individual breaks from the block to press and explain why the collective shape should have held
Allowing central space: use a central zone marker and specifically coach defenders to prioritise covering this zone above wide areas
Ball-watching: introduce a rule where every defender must call out which opposition player they are tracking
Lack of communication: specifically require the goalkeeper and central defenders to communicate constantly
Losing discipline under pressure: extend phase 2 and 3 to longer periods to build the physical and mental stamina required
Relaxing after clearance: introduce a rule that after every clearance, at least 2 players must immediately move to cover the most likely second-ball zone
progressions: Add a numerical disadvantage for the defending team to increase the difficulty of maintaining the shape under additional pressure
Introduce set-piece scenarios specifically within the low block context
Full match-realistic game with the defending team required to maintain a low block for an extended period
Combine with a counter-attacking element - reward the defending team for transitioning quickly to attack
regressions: Walk through the shape and the tracking responsibilities extensively with no opposition before introducing any live pressure
Reduce the intensity and pace of the opposition attacks initially, building the shape and communication habits before adding full match pace
Simplify the communication requirements to a single key call before building to the full system
matchApplication: The low block is a defensive strategy used by teams at every level when facing technically or physically superior opposition, or when protecting a lead in the closing stages of a match. While sometimes viewed as a purely defensive approach, executing a low block effectively requires exceptional discipline, communication, and physical resilience. Developing this capability at U16 gives players a genuine tactical tool that can be deployed strategically, and the communication and concentration skills involved transfer to defensive play in any system.
coachTips: This drill works particularly well in the context of a specific game-state scenario - for example, explicitly setting up the practice as the team protecting a 1-0 lead in the final 15 minutes.
The mental and physical demands of a low block over a sustained period are significant.
Goalkeeper communication is a critical and often underused element of low block defending.
Connect to memorable professional examples: many famous defensive performances in cup competitions are built on disciplined low-block defending.
Celebrate disciplined defensive performances as enthusiastically as attacking quality.
diagramDescription: Full pitch with emphasis on the defensive third. Defending team shown in a deep, narrow low block within approximately 25-30m of their own goal, organised in two compact banks. Central protection zone highlighted as the priority area. Opposition shown in sustained possession in the attacking third. Tracking arrows shown from defenders to specific opposition runners. Goalkeeper shown organising the line.

title: Combination Play Third Man Runs
objective: Develop the recognition and execution of third man combination plays to break through organised defences.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the concept of a third man run and how it differs from a simple two-player combination
Players can execute the timing required between the first pass, the lay-off, and the third man run
Players recognise when a third man run is the appropriate option based on defensive positioning
Teams develop fluency in combination play involving multiple players moving in coordinated patterns
pitchSize: 30x25m area, multiple groups if numbers allow
organisation: Groups of 3 attacking players plus 2 defenders in a 30x25m area with a mini-goal as the target. Player A starts with the ball, Player B is positioned to receive a lay-off, and Player C is the third man making the run into space.
instructions: Player A passes to Player B, who has dropped into a position to receive.
As Player B receives, Player C - who has been positioned wide or deep - begins a forward run into the space behind the defensive line.
Player B does not turn or dribble - they immediately lay the ball off, either back to Player A or directly into the path of Player Cs run.
The key principle: the third man (Player C) exploits the space created by the attention the defence gives to the first two players.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): walkthrough at slow pace with no defenders, focusing purely on the timing and movement pattern.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): passive defenders positioned to represent realistic defensive shape.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live defenders. Attacking trio must read the defensive setup and execute the third man combination to break through and score.
coachingPoints: THE THIRD MAN IS UNMARKED BY DESIGN: the entire purpose is that defenders focus their attention on the first two players, leaving the third man in space.
THE LAY-OFF MUST BE IMMEDIATE: Player B receiving the ball should not take an additional touch - an immediate, often one-touch, lay-off is what makes the combination effective.
THE THIRD MANS RUN TIMING IS CRITICAL: starting too early allows defenders to track it; starting too late means the space has closed.
DISGUISE THE INTENTION: the combination is more effective when it does not telegraph itself.
THE FINAL PASS MUST BE WEIGHTED CORRECTLY: whether laid into the path of Player Cs run or back to Player A, the weight and accuracy determines success.
READ THE DEFENSIVE SHAPE FIRST: the third man combination works best against a defence showing pressure on the ball.
PLAYERS MUST KNOW THEIR ROLE WITHOUT VERBAL SIGNALS: relying on shared understanding rather than calling out intentions.
MULTIPLE EXIT OPTIONS: the underlying principle (create space with two, exploit with a third) is more important than memorising a single fixed pattern.
commonMistakes: The third mans run starting too early, allowing defenders to track and mark it
Player B taking an unnecessary extra touch before laying the ball off
Poor weight or accuracy on the lay-off pass, disrupting the timing
Telegraphing the combination so clearly that defenders anticipate and intercept it
Player A or B failing to recognise when the defensive shape makes the combination the correct option
Players standing too close together, reducing the space the combination is designed to create
corrections: Early run: introduce a clear cue that the third man must wait for before beginning their run
Extra touch by Player B: enforce a strict one-touch lay-off rule during phase 1 and 2
Poor lay-off weight: practice the lay-off pass in isolation between Players A and B repeatedly
Telegraphing the move: vary which player becomes the third man randomly across repetitions
Failing to recognise the opportunity: pause play and ask the attacking trio to identify the defensive shape opportunity
Players too close together: use cone markers to establish minimum starting distances
progressions: Add a fourth player as an additional passing option, increasing complexity
Introduce a live, fully competitive defending pair who actively try to anticipate and prevent the combination
Apply within a larger small-sided game, rewarding successful combinations with bonus points
Vary the starting positions of the three attacking players across repetitions
regressions: Slow the entire sequence down significantly and walk through each players role without any defensive pressure
Reduce to a clear, fixed pattern repeated identically before introducing variation
Remove defenders entirely for an extended period, focusing purely on technical execution and timing
matchApplication: Third man combinations are among the most effective patterns for breaking down organised, compact defences, because they exploit the natural tendency of defenders to focus attention on the ball and the most immediate receiving option, leaving a less obvious runner in space. This pattern appears constantly in professional football, particularly against teams that defend in a compact mid or low block.
coachTips: This is a genuinely sophisticated combination pattern - expect the early sessions to require significant walkthrough before the timing becomes natural.
Use a simple diagram or physical walkthrough with players standing in position (no ball) to ensure everyone understands the pattern conceptually.
Rotating which player takes each role ensures all players develop an understanding of every part of the combination.
Connect to professional examples: third man combinations are common in elite football.
Celebrate the recognition of the opportunity as much as the technical execution.
diagramDescription: 30x25m area with a mini-goal as the target. Player A shown with the ball. Player B shown positioned to receive a short pass, with an arrow indicating an immediate one-touch lay-off. Player C shown starting wide or deep with a curved run arrow beginning as the first pass is played, running into space behind the defensive line. Two defenders shown positioned to demonstrate the space the combination is designed to exploit.

title: Creating and Exploiting Overloads
objective: Develop the recognition and tactical use of numerical overloads to create attacking advantages in wide and central areas.
learningOutcomes: Players understand what a numerical overload is and why it creates an attacking advantage
Players recognise how to create an overload through positioning and movement before the ball arrives
Players can exploit an overload efficiently once it has been created, rather than wasting the advantage
Teams develop the patience and movement patterns required to manufacture overloads consistently
pitchSize: 30x20m wide channel zone plus central area
organisation: 30x20m area divided into a wide channel and a central zone. The attacking team is instructed to manufacture a 3v2 or 2v1 situation in the wide channel by moving an extra player across before playing the ball into the overloaded area.
instructions: The attacking team starts with possession centrally. The defending team sets up with players covering both the wide channel and the central area.
To create the overload, the attacking team must move an additional player into the wide channel BEFORE playing the ball there.
Once the overload is established, the attacking team plays the ball into the area and must use the numerical advantage to create a scoring opportunity.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): walkthrough with no defensive pressure, focusing on the movement pattern required to manufacture the overload.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): passive defenders, allowing the attacking team to see and exploit the space created.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live opposition. Attacking team must recognise the opportunity and execute the movement and combination play needed to exploit it.
coachingPoints: CREATE THE OVERLOAD BEFORE PLAYING THE BALL: the extra player must arrive in the wide area before or as the ball is played there.
WHO MAKES THE OVERLOAD RUN: commonly a full-back overlapping, a central midfielder shifting wide, or a winger dropping while another player runs beyond them.
USE THE OVERLOAD QUICKLY: once created, a numerical advantage should be exploited rapidly - hesitating allows the defending team to recover.
COMBINATION PLAY WITHIN THE OVERLOAD: with an extra player, simple combinations become significantly easier against fewer defenders.
DO NOT WASTE THE OVERLOAD ON A LOW-VALUE ACTION: the team should look to progress play meaningfully rather than simply circulating safely.
RECOGNISE WHEN THE OVERLOAD HAS BEEN NEUTRALISED: if the defending team successfully recovers numbers, reset rather than continuing to force the original plan.
BALANCE ACROSS THE TEAM: creating an overload often means a player has vacated their original position - understand the trade-off.
COMMUNICATION TO TRIGGER THE OVERLOAD: a verbal or visual cue from a teammate indicating the right moment helps.
commonMistakes: Playing the ball into the wide area before the overloading player has arrived
Hesitating once the overload is created, allowing the defending team time to recover
Using the overload to simply maintain safe possession rather than progressing play meaningfully
The player creating the overload leaving a significant gap elsewhere
Poor combination play once the overload exists
Continuing to force the original plan even after the defending team has neutralised the overload
corrections: Premature ball into wide area: introduce a clear rule that the ball cannot be played wide until the overloading player has arrived
Hesitating after overload created: introduce a time limit from the overload being established to a shot or cross
Wasting overload on safe play: award bonus points specifically for combination play and progression within the overload zone
Leaving gaps elsewhere: discuss as a team before each phase how the rest of the team will adjust to cover
Poor combination play: practice the specific combination patterns in isolation before adding live opposition
Continuing to force a neutralised overload: coach players to recognise and call out when the defending team has recovered
progressions: Allow the overload to be created in either the wide channel or centrally, increasing decision-making
Add a recovering defender who specifically tracks back to reduce the overload
Apply within a full small-sided game, with the team free to manufacture overloads in any area
Introduce a numerical disadvantage elsewhere as the trade-off for the overload
regressions: Walk through the movement pattern extensively with no ball and no opposition
Reduce to a simple, fixed pattern before introducing variation and decision-making
Remove the live defending element for longer, allowing the attacking pattern to become comfortable
matchApplication: Creating and exploiting numerical overloads is a fundamental attacking principle used at every level of football. Teams that understand how to manufacture an extra player in a specific area create attacking advantages far more consistently than simply hoping for space to appear. This is a genuinely valuable tactical tool that develops a players broader game understanding alongside their technical and physical attributes.
coachTips: The timing principle (creating the overload before playing the ball there) is the single most important coaching point in this drill.
Different teams and formations create overloads in different ways - discuss with players how this applies to your teams usual shape.
Watching professional football specifically for overload creation provides excellent visual examples.
The trade-off element is an important tactical lesson in itself.
This drill works well as a foundation for more specific overload patterns that can be introduced in subsequent sessions.
diagramDescription: 30x20m area showing a wide channel zone and central zone. Attacking team shown initially with possession centrally. Overloading player movement arrow shown crossing into the wide channel BEFORE the ball is played there. Resulting 3v2 numerical advantage shown in the wide channel. Combination play arrows shown within the overloaded zone leading to a cross or shot at the mini-goal target.

title: Creating Width to Stretch the Defence
objective: Develop the positional discipline and movement required to maintain attacking width and stretch the opposition defensive shape.
learningOutcomes: Players understand why maintaining width creates space for the team in central areas
Players develop the discipline to hold wide positions rather than drifting narrow
Players recognise the timing of when to stay wide versus when to come inside
Teams build the habit of using the full width of the pitch in possession
pitchSize: Full width of the pitch x 40m
organisation: Full width playing area by 40m length. Two wide zones are marked at each touchline where wide players must remain positioned during build-up phases, only entering the central area at specific moments.
instructions: The attacking team sets up with 2 wide players positioned in the marked wide zones near each touchline.
During build-up play, wide players must remain in their wide zone, providing a stretching option even if they do not receive the ball immediately.
As the ball progresses into the final third, wide players are released from the zone restriction and can decide whether to stay wide, cut inside, or make runs into the box.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): walkthrough with no opposition, focusing on the wide players maintaining their positions.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): live opposition. The defending team defends compactly, and the attacking team must recognise how holding width stretches this compactness.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): remove the explicit zone restriction and observe whether the team naturally maintains good width based on the habits built.
coachingPoints: WIDTH CREATES CENTRAL SPACE: when wide players hold genuinely wide positions, the opposition defensive shape must stretch, which creates more space in central areas.
DO NOT DRIFT NARROW OUT OF HABIT: many young players naturally drift toward the ball and into central areas - maintaining wide positions requires conscious discipline.
BE AVAILABLE EVEN WITHOUT THE BALL: a wide player who is correctly positioned but not currently receiving the ball is still doing important tactical work.
TIMING OF WHEN TO COME INSIDE: width should be maintained during build-up, but in advanced and dangerous areas, different decisions may be needed.
USE THE FULL WIDTH OF THE PITCH: many youth teams play in a narrow band of the pitch out of habit.
CENTRAL PLAYERS MUST RECOGNISE AND USE THE SPACE CREATED: width is only valuable if central players exploit the space it creates.
COMMUNICATION ABOUT WIDTH: central players can call for or direct wide players to maintain or adjust their width.
PATIENCE TO MAINTAIN THE SHAPE: holding a wide position requires patience and trust that the positioning is contributing to the teams overall attacking shape.
commonMistakes: Wide players drifting toward the ball or into central areas rather than maintaining their stretching position
Central players failing to recognise or use the space that good width creates
Wide players becoming completely disengaged from play because they are not regularly receiving the ball
Width being maintained even in advanced areas where a different decision might be more effective
Lack of communication between central and wide players about the teams overall shape
The team reverting to narrow, congested positioning once the explicit zone restriction is removed
corrections: Wide players drifting narrow: use the visual zone markers prominently and immediately point out any drift toward the centre
Central players not using the space: pause play and specifically ask central players to identify and describe the space created
Wide players disengaging: ensure wide players still receive the ball regularly even in deeper positions
Width maintained inappropriately in advanced areas: clearly define where the zone restriction applies and where it is lifted
Lack of communication: introduce a simple call system where central players can call WIDER or IN
Reverting to narrow positioning without the restriction: specifically praise and reinforce moments where width is maintained naturally
progressions: Remove the zone markers earlier in the session and assess whether the positional habit has transferred
Add a defensive team specifically instructed to compress narrow, testing whether the attacking team exploits the space
Introduce a full-sided game with width-related bonus scoring
Combine with the overloads drill, exploring how width creation and overloads work together
regressions: Reduce the playing area width slightly to make the width-holding habit more immediately achievable
Allow wide players some flexibility to drift narrower temporarily before tightening the discipline
Walk through the concept extensively with cones representing players before physical execution
matchApplication: Maintaining attacking width is one of the most fundamental and consistently effective principles in football at every level, because it directly creates space in the most valuable central areas by forcing the opposition defensive shape to stretch. Teams that play narrow make life significantly easier for defending teams by allowing them to remain compact. Introducing the discipline and understanding of width at U12 builds positional intelligence that supports every other attacking principle a team might use.
coachTips: The zone markers are a simple but highly effective coaching tool - the visual boundary makes the abstract idea of width concrete.
Many young players will find maintaining a wide position without regularly touching the ball genuinely difficult psychologically.
This drill connects naturally to the overloads drill and the switching play drill.
Watching professional football specifically for width provides valuable visual reinforcement.
Be patient with the positional discipline required here - it often takes longer to become habitual than purely technical skills.
diagramDescription: Full width of the pitch by 40m length. Two wide zones marked at each touchline. Wide players shown maintaining position within these zones during build-up play. Central players shown in possession with passing options including the wide players. Defending team shown stretched across the width, with resulting central space highlighted. Arrow shows a central player exploiting the space created.

title: Creating Depth and Playing Beyond the Defence
objective: Develop the movement patterns and passing technique required to create and exploit depth in behind the opposition defensive line.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the value of creating depth as a complement to width in attacking play
Forward players develop the timing of runs in behind the defensive line
Midfield and defensive players develop the technique to play accurate passes into created depth
Teams recognise the relationship between holding width, creating depth, and the space this opens centrally
organisation: A long, narrower playing area with a goal or large target zone at the attacking end. Forward players are coached specifically on movement to create depth, while midfield players practice the timing and technique of passes designed to exploit this depth.
instructions: Forward players begin in a position relatively close to the defensive line, then make a sudden run in behind at a specific moment.
The purpose of this run is twofold: either to receive a pass directly in behind the defence, or simply to push the defensive line backward even without receiving the ball.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): walkthrough with no opposition. Forward players practice the timing of their run relative to when the ball-carrier is ready to play the pass.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): passive defenders holding a line, allowing forward players to time their runs against a realistic offside-style line.

PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live opposition. Forward players must read the defensive line and time their runs to stay onside while beating the defence for pace, and midfield players must play an accurate pass into the created space.
coachingPoints: TIME THE RUN TO THE PASSERS READINESS, NOT YOUR OWN INSTINCT: the most common error is starting the run before the ball-carrier is actually in a position to play the pass.
RUN AT AN ANGLE, NOT IN A STRAIGHT LINE: a diagonal run is generally more effective than a straight run, as it is harder to track and creates a better angle to receive while moving toward goal.
DEPTH RUNS HAVE VALUE EVEN WITHOUT RECEIVING THE BALL: a well-timed run forces the defensive line to retreat, creating space in front of them.
THE PASS MUST LEAD THE RUNNER: the pass into depth should be played into the space ahead of the runner, not to their feet.
WEIGHT OF THE PASS MATTERS SIGNIFICANTLY: too soft and the defence recovers; too hard and the ball runs through to the goalkeeper.
STAY ONSIDE THROUGH TIMING, NOT POSITION: forward players should time their run to begin from an onside position and accelerate beyond the line as the pass is played.
COMMUNICATION BETWEEN THE RUNNER AND THE PASSER: a verbal or visual cue can help synchronise the timing during the learning phase.
VARY THE STARTING POSITION OF THE RUN: forward players who always make the same type of run become predictable.
commonMistakes: Starting the run too early, before the passer is genuinely ready to play the ball
Running in a straight line directly at the defensive line, making the run easy to track
The pass arriving too soft, allowing the defence to recover and intercept
The pass arriving too hard, running through to the goalkeeper
Forward players being caught offside through anticipating the run too early
No communication or shared understanding between the runner and the passer
corrections: Early run: introduce a clear cue that the forward must wait for before beginning the run
Straight line runs: specifically coach and demonstrate the diagonal run angle using cone markers
Pass too soft: introduce a minimum pace requirement for passes into depth
Pass too hard: practice the specific weight of pass required in isolation, with a clear target zone
Forward caught offside: use a visible offside line marker and specifically coach the timing relationship
Lack of communication: introduce a simple visual cue system that the forward must recognise before beginning their run
progressions: Add a live offside trap defended by the back line, significantly increasing the difficulty
Combine depth-creating runs with width, requiring the team to use both principles together
Apply within a full small-sided game, rewarding successful depth-exploiting moves
Introduce a specific 1v1 or 2v1 situation immediately after the forward receives the pass
regressions: Remove any offside consideration initially, allowing forward players to focus purely on the timing and angle of the run
Walk through the timing relationship extensively with no ball, using verbal counting or simple cues
Reduce the distance and pace required initially, building the timing habit before adding speed demands
matchApplication: Creating and exploiting depth in behind the opposition defensive line is one of the most direct and effective ways to create high-quality goal-scoring opportunities, because a player running onto the ball at pace in space behind the defence is extremely difficult to stop. This principle works in combination with width and overloads to stretch the opposition defensive shape in multiple directions simultaneously, making it significantly harder for any defending team to remain compact and organised.
coachTips: The relationship between the runners timing and the passers readiness is the single most important concept and deserves the most repeated coaching emphasis.
Different players will naturally have different preferred angles and timing for their runs.
This drill connects directly to the creating width drill.
Connect to professional examples: forwards renowned for their movement in behind provide excellent illustrative examples.
Be patient with the offside timing element specifically - this is a genuinely difficult skill that even experienced players continue to refine.
diagramDescription: Full pitch length by 30m width playing area. Forward player shown starting in a position close to the defensive line, with a diagonal run arrow beginning at the moment the ball-carrier opens their body to play the pass. Defensive line shown being pushed back by the run. Pass arrow shown leading the runner into the space ahead of them. Goal or target zone shown at the attacking end.

title: Block Tackle Foundations
objective: Teach the correct technique for the block tackle: timing, body position, and contact point.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a correctly timed block tackle using the inside of the foot
Players understand when to attempt a tackle versus when to jockey and delay
Players develop the physical and mental commitment required for an effective tackle
Players learn to stay on their feet after the tackle rather than falling
organisation: Pairs in a 10x5m narrow channel. Player A has the ball and dribbles slowly toward Player B (the tackler). B must time and execute a block tackle. Roles swap every 5 reps.
instructions: Player A dribbles slowly toward Player B at walking pace.
Player B waits in a jockeying stance (side-on, weight on balls of feet, knees slightly bent).
As Player A brings the ball within 1 step of Player B, B commits to the block tackle.
BLOCK TACKLE TECHNIQUE: plant the standing foot beside the attacker. Swing the tackling foot forward. Contact the centre of the ball with the INSIDE of the foot. Drive through the ball.
The tackling foot must be firm at contact - not a stab but a solid, driven block through the ball.
After the tackle, the ball should be won cleanly or deflected away from the attacker.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): attacker is passive (slow dribble, does not try to evade). Focus purely on tackle timing and technique.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): attacker dribbles at jogging pace and can change direction slightly. Defender must adjust.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): competitive 1v1. Attacker tries to score in a mini-goal. Defender tries to win the ball.
coachingPoints: TIMING IS EVERYTHING: the tackle must be attempted when the ball is slightly away from the attacker feet, not when it is under close control. Wait for the heavy touch.
COMMIT FULLY: a half-committed tackle is worse than no tackle at all. It fails to win the ball and leaves the defender off-balance.
INSIDE OF THE FOOT: the block tackle uses the inside of the foot, not the toe. The inside surface provides the largest contact area.
PLANT FOOT CLOSE: the standing foot should be close to the ball providing a stable base for the tackle.
DRIVE THROUGH THE BALL: the tackling motion drives through the centre of the ball, not into the attacker.
STAY ON YOUR FEET: a defender who falls during the tackle cannot continue to defend. Stay balanced throughout.
ANGLE OF APPROACH: approach from a slightly side-on angle rather than directly head-on for a more powerful inside-foot contact.
KEEP EYES ON THE BALL: watch the ball all the way to contact, not the attacker body or feet.
RECOVER POSITION IMMEDIATELY: whether the tackle wins the ball or not, recover to a defensive position immediately after the attempt.
commonMistakes: Stabbing at the ball with the toe rather than blocking with the inside of the foot
Half-committed tackle that fails to win the ball
Attempting the tackle when the ball is under close control rather than waiting for the heavy touch
Falling to the ground during the tackle
Watching the attackers feet rather than the ball
Not recovering position immediately after a failed tackle
corrections: Toe stabbing: place a sticker on the inside of the tackling foot and require it to make contact
Half-committed tackle: any tackle without full commitment must be retried
Wrong timing: call OUT at the moment of a heavy touch so the defender learns to recognise the correct window
Falling to ground: any tackle resulting in the defender falling is immediately corrected
Watching body: ask the defender to call out the colour of the ball at the moment of contact
Not recovering: use a recovery cone 3m behind the defender - must sprint back to it after every tackle attempt
progressions: Increase attacker pace to 80 then 100 percent while maintaining tackle timing
Add a consequence: tackled attacker becomes the defender (winner stays on format)
Introduce a second attacker who receives if the first tackle fails, requiring immediate defensive recovery
Full competitive 1v1 game with goals, multiple tackle opportunities per possession
regressions: Reduce to a completely static ball that the defender simply drives their foot through to feel the correct contact
Walk-through only at slow motion counting steps aloud
Allow the attacker to be fully passive just before the tackle to simplify timing
matchApplication: The block tackle is the most fundamental tackling technique in football and is the basis of all physical defensive play. A defender who can execute a clean, well-timed block tackle wins possession for their team and demonstrates the physical commitment that is an essential part of defensive effectiveness.
coachTips: Physical contact in a tackle is legal and necessary - ensure players are comfortable with the physical element before adding any pace.
The half-hearted tackle problem is the single most important thing to address at U11 - teach the principle of commit or delay early.
Celebrate correct timing (waiting for the heavy touch) as much as the actual tackle success.
Pair players of similar size and strength to make the contact element appropriate.
Demonstrate the block tackle yourself slowly and clearly before players attempt it.
diagramDescription: 10x5m narrow channel. Player A (attacker) dribbling slowly toward Player B (defender) in jockeying stance. Heavy touch moment highlighted with a star. Block tackle shown: standing foot planted close to ball, tackling foot swinging forward with inside of foot contacting ball centre. Drive-through arrow shows force direction through ball.

title: Tackle from the Side and Interception
objective: Develop the side tackle technique and interception positioning used when an attacker is moving across the defender.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a side tackle when an attacker is moving laterally
Players develop the anticipation needed to intercept passes before they reach an attacker
Players understand the body position required for a safe and effective side tackle
Players build defensive awareness of passing lanes and likely ball trajectories
organisation: 20x15m zone. Player A (attacker) moves laterally across the zone with the ball. Player B (defender) must stay goalside and attempt a side tackle when the moment is right. Player C is a second attacker positioned wider. Rotate roles every 4 minutes.
instructions: Player A dribbles laterally across the zone at 70 percent pace, with the ball occasionally running slightly ahead.
Player B tracks the lateral movement, staying goalside and 1-2m from the attacker.
When the ball runs away from A by more than 1m: Player B executes the side tackle.
SIDE TACKLE TECHNIQUE: the tackling leg extends toward the ball from the side, making contact with the inside or instep. Body remains beside the ball rather than in front of it.
INTERCEPTION PHASE: Player C moves to receive a pass from A. Player B must read the intended pass and step into the lane to intercept.
