matchApplication: The 30-in-60 challenge is used in various forms by elite clubs as a pre-session activation that simultaneously tests and develops every aspect of passing performance: technique, decision-making, communication, composure under pressure, and team connection. The timed challenge creates a genuine pressure environment that replicates the urgency of match passing without the defensive element. Groups that can consistently complete 30 accurate passes in 60 seconds have developed a passing speed and accuracy that is directly transferable to match performance. The collective counting, the resets, and the celebration of progress make this one of the most engaging and effective passing drills available.
coachTips: The collective counting is the heartbeat of this drill. Make it loud, energetic, and shared.
Have a visible timer that everyone can see (phone stopwatch in a visible position) so the pressure is concrete and shared.
The post-window discussion (what caused the resets?) is as valuable as the passing itself. Make it brief but specific.
Track the highest consecutive count achieved in each session and set it as the target to beat in the next session.
This drill should eventually become part of every pre-session warm-up. Groups who do it consistently develop a passing quality and connection that is visible in matches.
diagramDescription: 12x12m square with 5-6 players spread around and inside it. Ball shown moving between players with numbered pass arrows (1, 2, 3... 30). Count shown prominently above the square rising toward 30. Timer shown in the corner at 60 seconds and counting down. RESET moment shown with a star where a pass missed its target and the count returned to zero. Communication arrows shown with HERE and BACK labels. Collective energy icons shown as the count rises.

title: Barcelona Colour Rondo
objective: Develop reaction speed, decision-making, and passing accuracy through a rondo where the coach calls a colour to determine who the ball must reach next.
learningOutcomes: Players develop reaction speed to sudden changes in passing direction based on colour calls
Players build the spatial awareness to identify a specific target player under time pressure
Players develop composure in possession when the target can change at any moment
Players experience the cognitive intensity of elite-level passing and decision-making
equipment: Bibs in 3 colours (2 attackers in yellow, 2 in red, 1 in blue)
organisation: Standard 5v2 rondo in an 8x8m square. The 5 attackers wear bibs in 3 colours: 2 yellow, 2 red, 1 blue. During play, the coach randomly calls a colour. The ball MUST reach a player in that colour within the next 3 passes. If it does not, the two defenders earn a point. Normal rondo rules otherwise apply.
instructions: Set up the 5v2 rondo in the 8x8m square.
Bib colours: 2 players in yellow, 2 players in red, 1 player in blue.
The rondo begins as a normal 5v2 possession game. Standard rules apply.
At random moments (every 20-30 seconds) the coach calls a colour: YELLOW, RED, or BLUE.
From the moment the colour is called, the attacking team has 3 passes to get the ball to a player wearing that colour.
If the colour player receives within 3 passes: the attacking team earns 1 point.
If after 3 passes the colour player has not received: the defenders earn 1 point.
Calling BLUE is the hardest (only 1 player in blue). Calling YELLOW is slightly easier (2 players in yellow). The coach varies the difficulty based on the groups performance.
Normal rondo turnover rules still apply: if a defender touches the ball, the player who lost it goes in.
After 10 minutes: rotate bib colours so different players wear each colour.
coachingPoints: THE COLOUR CALL CHANGES EVERYTHING: when the colour is called, the purpose of the possession immediately shifts. The team must now actively work the ball toward the target colour rather than simply keeping it.

IDENTIFY THE TARGET INSTANTLY: the moment the colour is called, every player on the attacking team must immediately locate the target-colour player and assess whether a direct pass to them is available.

DO NOT PANIC: 3 passes is enough time to reach the target if the passing is calm and the movement is good. Panicking leads to rushed, inaccurate passes that use up the 3 passes without reaching the target.
THE TARGET PLAYER MUST MOVE TO HELP: when a colour is called, that player must immediately create a clear passing opportunity by moving to a better receiving position.
USE THE NON-TARGET PASSES WISELY: if a direct pass to the target colour is not immediately available, use 1 of the 3 passes to shift the defenders and create the angle for the target pass.
SPATIAL AWARENESS OF ALL BIB COLOURS: the best players in this drill are continuously aware of where each colour is positioned, not just when a colour is called but throughout the entire possession.
THE BLUE CALL IS THE ELITE CHALLENGE: with only 1 blue player, finding them within 3 passes requires the most specific and precise decision-making. Celebrate every successful blue reach.
COMMUNICATION BETWEEN ATTACKERS: when the colour is called, attackers should communicate: I HAVE YELLOW or GET IT TO RED to focus the collective effort.
THIS IS COGNITIVE PASSING: this drill trains the brain as much as the feet. The reaction to the colour call is a genuine cognitive challenge that develops football intelligence.
commonMistakes: Panicking when the colour is called and playing rushed, inaccurate passes that use up the 3 passes without reaching the target
Not immediately identifying where the target-colour player is when the colour is called
The target-colour player standing still and not creating a receiving opportunity after their colour is called
Using all 3 passes trying to reach the target directly rather than using 1 to create the angle and 2 to execute
No communication between attackers after the colour call, leaving each player making individual decisions
corrections: Panicking: introduce a 1-second pause rule after the colour call during which no one can pass - this forces a moment of assessment before action
Not identifying target: before any colour call can be acted on, one attacking player must verbally confirm where the target-colour player is
Target player standing still: make it a rule that the target-colour player must make a visible movement toward an available receiving position immediately after their colour is called
Using 3 passes directly: if 3 direct attempts fail, coach pauses and shows how 1 positioning pass would have created the angle
No communication: make the communication call (I HAVE YELLOW) mandatory within 1 second of the colour being announced
progressions: Add a fourth bib colour (green) worn by one player, increasing the colour combinations and the cognitive difficulty
Reduce the rondo square to 6x6m to increase the defensive pressure and reduce the available passing angles for the colour passes
Increase defenders to 3 (5v3) making the basic possession harder and therefore the colour challenge more demanding
Remove the 3-pass limit and instead require the ball to reach the target colour by any route but with a time limit of 10 seconds
regressions: Reduce to 2 colours only (yellow and red) until the concept and reaction is established before adding blue
Increase the square to 10x10m giving more space to execute the colour passes
Allow 4 passes instead of 3 to reach the target colour before reducing to 3
matchApplication: The colour rondo combines the possession foundation of the Barcelona rondo with a cognitive challenge that specifically develops the football intelligence component of passing and decision-making. The colour call creates a sudden shift in purpose that replicates the moments in a match when a team must quickly redirect their passing intent to exploit a specific opportunity (a target player making a run, a space opening up, an overlap beginning). The cognitive demand - react, locate, plan, execute - is exactly the sequence that elite midfielders perform hundreds of times in every match, and developing it in a focused training environment accelerates a players football intelligence significantly.
coachTips: The timing of the colour calls is an important coaching skill: too frequent and the drill becomes chaotic; too infrequent and the cognitive element disappears. Aim for a call every 20-30 seconds.
Vary the colour called based on what you observe: if the group is finding it easy, call BLUE more often. If they are struggling, call YELLOW more often.

The post-session debrief on specific colour call moments is very valuable: show the group a moment where the colour call was handled well and one where it was not, and discuss what made the difference.
This drill develops football intelligence faster than almost any other exercise because it forces players to continuously process multiple pieces of information simultaneously.

Connect to professional examples: discuss how elite midfielders like De Bruyne, Modric, and Pedri are constantly scanning and processing multiple passing options simultaneously, which is exactly what this drill trains.
diagramDescription: 8x8m square with 5 attackers (2 in yellow bibs, 2 in red bibs, 1 in blue bib) and 2 defenders. Ball shown in possession with the attacking team. COLOUR CALL shown from the coach: RED. Red-bib players highlighted. Target assessment arrows shown from the ball-carrier to each red player, with the more accessible red player highlighted as the primary option. 3-pass countdown shown. One positioning pass to shift the defenders shown, followed by the pass to the red target. Coach position shown outside the square with colour call icon.

title: Creating Space for Others — Decoy Runs
objective: Develop the understanding and execution of decoy runs that create space for teammates rather than receiving the ball.
learningOutcomes: Players understand that a run without the ball can be as valuable as a run with it
Players develop the timing of a decoy run to draw a defender away from the intended receiver
Players build the selfless attacking mentality of creating for others
Players recognise when a decoy run is the correct option rather than a receiving run
organisation: 30x20m zone. Attacking team of 5-6 tries to create and score in a mini-goal at the far end. 2-3 defenders. The key coaching focus: before every shot or final pass, at least one attacker must have made a decoy run away from the ball in the previous 3 seconds.
instructions: Attacking team builds possession in the zone with 2-3 defenders applying pressure.
Before any shot or through ball can be played, the coach requires evidence that a decoy run has occurred in the previous 3 seconds.
DECOY RUN DEFINITION: a run made without expecting to receive the ball, designed purely to draw a defender away from the intended receiver.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): the coach identifies and calls DECOY every time one is made correctly, pausing to show the group what space it created.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): a bonus point is awarded every time a goal is scored directly from the space created by a correctly timed decoy run.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): free game. No instruction. Observe whether decoy runs happen naturally as a result of phases 1 and 2.
coachingPoints: THE DECOY IS A SACRIFICE: the player making the decoy run gives up their own receiving opportunity to create space for a teammate. This is one of the most selfless and valuable actions in attacking football.

TIMING IS EVERYTHING: a decoy run made too early allows the defender to track and recover. Made at the right moment (just as the ball-carrier is about to play the pass), it draws the defender at exactly the wrong moment.
THE DECOY MUST LOOK REAL: a half-hearted run that the defender can tell is fake does not draw them. Sprint as if you genuinely expect to receive the ball.
READ WHAT YOUR MOVEMENT CREATES: as you make the decoy run, glance back to see whether the defender has followed you. If they have, the space is now open for your teammate.

DECOY RUNS CAN BE IN ANY DIRECTION: toward the ball (to draw a defender tight and create space behind), away from the ball (to stretch the defence), across the face of the defence (to create a gap centrally).
COMMUNICATE WITH THE BALL-CARRIER: a brief eye contact or hand gesture between the decoy runner and the ball-carrier tells the ball-carrier where the space has been created.
NOT EVERY RUN SHOULD BE A DECOY: the skill is knowing when to make a genuine receiving run and when to sacrifice it for a decoy. Both are needed in a complete attacking game.
GREAT TEAMS HAVE MULTIPLE DECOY RUNNERS: when multiple attackers make decoy runs simultaneously, the defence is pulled in multiple directions and the space created is enormous.
THIS IS INTELLIGENT ATTACKING: the players who are most dangerous in attack are not always the fastest or most technically gifted. They are the ones who create space most intelligently.
commonMistakes: Making a decoy run but decelerating or stopping halfway, failing to commit the defender
The decoy run starting too late to draw the defender before the pass is played
Not communicating with the ball-carrier so the created space goes unrecognised and unused
Players always making genuine receiving runs and never making decoy runs for teammates
The decoy run going in an unhelpful direction that draws no defender and creates no space
corrections: Deceleration: require a full sprint for the entire decoy run until the ball has been played
Late start: coach calls DECOY as a signal for the run to begin, training the correct timing relative to the ball-carrier
No communication: require a hand signal from the decoy runner toward the created space immediately after making the run
Never making decoy runs: privately assign one specific player the decoy role for a full 5-minute phase to build the habit
Wrong direction: pause play after an unhelpful decoy and ask the player to identify which direction would have drawn a defender
progressions: Multiple simultaneous decoys: two attackers make coordinated decoy runs in opposite directions simultaneously
Blind decoy: the ball-carrier does not look at the decoy runner at all, forcing the decoy to create space based purely on movement rather than eye contact
Full small-sided game with decoy bonus points tracked cumulatively across an entire session
regressions: Show the concept with walking pace positioning before any live practice
Use a static defender who simply stands in position to demonstrate what happens when they are drawn by a decoy run
Reduce to 3v1 so the decoy concept is simpler and the created space is very obvious
matchApplication: Decoy runs are one of the most intelligent and undervalued attacking movements in football. The greatest attacking teams in history all had players who understood that their movement without the ball was as important as their movement with it. Thierry Henry was famous for making decoy runs that created space for Pires. Roberto Firmino built his entire game around intelligent movement that created space for Salah and Mane. This drill develops exactly that quality: the football intelligence to sacrifice a personal receiving opportunity to create something better for the team.
coachTips: The coach calling DECOY explicitly every time one happens correctly is essential in phase 1 - players cannot develop what they cannot see.
The selfless nature of the decoy run is worth discussing explicitly: explain why sacrificing your own receiving opportunity to help a teammate is one of the highest-value things you can do in attack.
Connect to specific professional examples of decoy runs that directly led to goals - this concept is much easier to understand visually than verbally.
This drill develops football intelligence that transfers to every attacking situation in every match.
Celebrate the player who makes the best decoy run as enthusiastically as the player who scores the resulting goal.
diagramDescription: 30x20m zone with mini-goal at far end. Attacking team (circles) in possession. One attacker shown making a decoy run (dashed curved arrow) toward the right side, drawing a defender (triangle) away from the central area. Space created shown as an open zone (shaded). Ball-carrier shown with a pass arrow into the created central space where another attacker arrives unmarked. Communication arrow shown between the decoy runner and ball-carrier.

title: Third Man Attacking Movement
objective: Develop coordinated three-player attacking movement patterns where the third player arrives into space created by the first two.
learningOutcomes: Players time third-man runs to arrive in space at the exact moment the ball is ready to be played
Players understand the relationship between the first combination and the third man opportunity
Players develop the explosive acceleration needed to exploit the space as it opens
Teams build the coordinated attacking patterns that break through organised defences
players: Groups of 5 (3 attackers plus 2 passive then active defenders)
organisation: Three attackers (A, B, C) work against 2 defenders in a 25x20m zone with a mini-goal as the target. The specific movement pattern: A combines with B (give-and-go or layoff), drawing the defenders attention to the combination, while C times a run to arrive in the space behind just as A or B plays the third ball.
instructions: Players A, B, and C start in specific positions: A has the ball, B is 8m ahead in a central position, C is wide and slightly deeper.
A passes to B. The two defenders immediately press the A-B combination.
As both defenders focus on A and B, C begins a run into the space behind the defenders.
B plays the ball (first or second touch) into the space where C is arriving.
C receives on the run in space and finishes.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): passive defenders (stand in position but do not press). Pure timing practice.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): defenders press actively. A and B must complete the combination quickly enough before both defenders can cover.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live 3v2. All players fully competitive. Defenders can tackle and intercept.
coachingPoints: C STARTS THE RUN AT THE RIGHT MOMENT: C should begin their run when A plays the first pass to B, not before and not after. Starting too early allows the defender to track the run. Starting too late means the space has already closed.
THE COMBINATION DRAWS THE DEFENDERS: the A-B combination only creates the third-man space if it genuinely attracts both defenders. A and B must make the combination look like the main attack.
B MUST SEE BOTH: the player receiving the first pass (B) must simultaneously deal with the ball and be aware of C starting the third-man run. This two-task awareness is the most demanding element.
THE THIRD PASS MUST LEAD C: just like any through ball, the pass to C must go into the space ahead of their run, not to where they currently are.
C MUST ARRIVE AT PACE: the third-man run only works if C arrives at full speed. A slow arrival gives defenders time to recover even after being drawn to the combination.
VARY THE PATTERN: the same principle works from different starting positions and with different initial combinations (overlap, wall pass, layoff). The underlying idea remains: two draw, one exploits.
FINISH CLINICALLY: the whole point of the third-man run is to arrive in space with time. That chance must be finished calmly and accurately rather than rushed.
COMMUNICATION BETWEEN A AND B: A and B need a signal (verbal or visual) to confirm the combination is ready so C knows when to start the run.
commonMistakes: C starting the run too early before the combination has drawn both defenders
B not seeing C beginning the run and playing the ball back to A instead of forward to C
The third pass to C going to their feet rather than into the space ahead of their run
C arriving too slowly, giving the defender time to recover despite being drawn by the combination
The combination between A and B being too slow, giving the defenders time to deal with both the combination and cover C
corrections: C too early: place a cone at C starting position and require C to stay behind it until A has played the first pass to B
B not seeing C: require B to verbally confirm C is running before playing the third ball
Third pass to feet: place a target cone 2m ahead of where C starts their run - the pass must reach or go past it
C too slow: time the run from when C starts to when they receive. Target under 3 seconds for the full run
Combination too slow: enforce a maximum 2-touch rule for both A and B in the combination phase
progressions: Active pressing from both defenders from the start of the drill, with no passive phase
Add a fourth attacker who makes a second run as an alternative option, requiring B to read which third man is better placed
Apply the pattern in a full small-sided game, with bonus points for goals scored from a correctly executed third-man combination
Vary the starting positions and combination types across the session so the pattern becomes adaptable rather than fixed
regressions: Walk through the pattern at slow motion with no defenders, narrating each step before adding any pressure
Reduce to passive defenders only for a full session before introducing active defending
Allow C unlimited time to start their run rather than tying it to a specific trigger moment
matchApplication: Third-man combinations are the most sophisticated and effective attacking patterns in football because they specifically exploit the defenders natural tendency to focus on the ball. When two players combine quickly, both defenders commit to covering the combination, and the third player arrives in the space they have vacated. This pattern is used by every elite attacking team from Barcelona to Liverpool and is responsible for a significant proportion of high-quality goal-scoring opportunities at the top level. Teaching it at U14 gives players 4+ years to develop the timing and awareness before senior football.
coachTips: The pattern must be walked through in complete slow motion before any live attempt - the third-man concept is not immediately intuitive.
The most important coaching point is B seeing C. Film B during the drill and show them moments when they looked and moments when they did not.
Celebrate the perfectly timed third-man run as enthusiastically as the goal - the quality of the movement is the achievement.
Connect to professional examples: show the pattern in a professional match and identify A, B, and C explicitly before the session.
This drill works best when practised regularly - the timing awareness between three specific players develops over many sessions.
diagramDescription: 25x20m zone with mini-goal. Player A shown with ball. Player B shown 8m ahead centrally. Player C shown wide and slightly deeper. Two defenders (triangles) shown focusing on the A-B combination. Pass arrows: A to B (arrow 1), both defenders shown pressing toward the A-B zone. C run arrow shown beginning at the moment A passes to B (timing trigger). B third pass arrow leading into the space ahead of C arriving run. C shown arriving at full pace to receive and finish.

title: Breaking Lines — Passes Through Defensive Blocks
objective: Develop the ability to identify and execute penetrating passes that break through organised defensive lines.
learningOutcomes: Players can identify the passing lane between or through defensive lines
Players develop the weight and timing of penetrating passes
Players understand when to attempt a penetrating pass versus when to recycle
Teams develop the patience to wait for the penetrating opportunity before playing it
pitchSize: 50x35m area with two defined defensive lines
organisation: 50x35m area. Defending team of 5-6 sets up in two clear defensive lines (a midfield line of 3 and a defensive line of 3). Attacking team of 5-6 tries to play the ball through both lines to reach a target zone behind the defensive line.
instructions: The defending team holds two organised lines: midfield line (3 players) across the width at the midfield mark, defensive line (3 players) across the width 20m deeper.
The attacking team starts with possession in front of the midfield line.
FIRST PENETRATION: the attacking team must play a pass through or around the midfield line to reach an attacker positioned between the two defensive lines.
SECOND PENETRATION: the attacker between the lines must now play or dribble through the defensive line to reach the target zone.
Score: 3 points for playing into the target zone behind both lines. 1 point for playing between the two lines. 0 points if possession is lost without breaking either line.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): defending lines are passive (hold position but do not press). Attacking team focuses purely on identifying and executing the penetrating pass.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): defending lines are active. Press the ball and close the gaps. Attacking team must be patient and precise.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): free game. Both teams compete fully.
coachingPoints: IDENTIFY THE GAP BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: the best penetrating passers read the defensive shape while the ball is with someone else and have identified the gap before it is their turn to pass.

WEIGHT IS AS IMPORTANT AS DIRECTION: a penetrating pass with poor weight either arrives too softly (allowing the defender to intercept or recover) or too hard (running away from the intended receiver). The weight must be precise.
THE PASS GOES THROUGH THE GAP, NOT AT THE DEFENDER: aim for the space between defenders, not at a defender hoping they will move. If there is no gap, do not play the penetrating pass.

DRAW THE LINE BEFORE PLAYING THROUGH IT: a penetrating pass is most effective after the defenders have been moved sideways by circulation. Passing side to side before the penetration draws defenders out of position and creates gaps.
THE RECEIVER MUST SHOW: the player in or behind the defensive line must make a movement to show for the penetrating pass at the right moment, not stand stationary.

FIRST TOUCH OF THE RECEIVER FACES FORWARD: the player receiving a penetrating pass must take a first touch that faces them toward goal or toward the next target immediately. A first touch back toward their own goal wastes the penetration.
DO NOT FORCE PENETRATION: if no gap exists, be patient. Recycle the ball and wait for the defensive shape to shift. Forced penetration into a covered lane is worse than no penetration.

COMBINATION BEFORE PENETRATION: a give-and-go or quick one-two that creates a half-step of space for the ball-carrier often creates the penetrating opportunity that did not exist before the combination.
commonMistakes: Attempting penetrating passes when the gap does not yet exist, playing into a defender
Pass weight too light, allowing the defending line to step forward and intercept
The receiver not showing for the ball or showing with poor timing
First touch of the receiver going backward toward their own team, wasting the advantage of the penetration
No patience in build-up, forcing penetration immediately rather than circulating first to create gaps
corrections: Forced penetration: introduce a minimum of 4 passes before any penetration attempt is allowed in the first 5 minutes, building the patience habit
Light pass: set a minimum pace requirement - the penetrating pass must arrive at the receiver before a stepping defender could intercept it
Poor showing: require the receiver to call THROUGH before any penetrating pass to them can be played, confirming they are ready and in position
First touch backward: require the receiver to point toward the target zone immediately after their first touch, reinforcing the forward-facing habit
Impatience: celebrate every completed circulation sequence (4+ passes without attempting penetration) as warmly as the penetrating pass itself
progressions: Add a pressing trigger: when the defensive line steps forward to press the ball, this creates an immediate penetration opportunity behind them which the attacking team must exploit
Full small-sided game with the points system applied, requiring genuine patience and decision-making under competitive pressure
Reduce to one defensive line only, simplifying the concept before reintroducing the two-line challenge
regressions: Use completely passive defenders (static) for the full phase 1 to allow pure identification and execution practice
Walk through the line-breaking concept with the coach physically pointing out each gap before the pass is played
Reduce the number of defenders in each line to 2 rather than 3, creating larger and more obvious gaps to penetrate
matchApplication: Breaking through defensive lines is the central tactical challenge in modern football against organised defences. Every team defending with a compact block is specifically trying to deny penetrating passes. The teams that can consistently break lines (Manchester City, Liverpool, Barcelona) create significantly higher quality goal-scoring opportunities than teams that can only play around defensive shapes. Teaching line-breaking at U14, including the patience to wait for the gap, the technique to play through it, and the first touch to face forward on receiving, builds a quality that is directly transferable to every level of football.
coachTips: Stand level with the defensive lines during the drill to observe the gaps as they appear - you can then point them out to the attacking team in real time.
The patience element is the hardest coaching point at U14 where players naturally want to play forward immediately. Be consistent and specific about rewarding patience.
Use the points system prominently - making the second penetration (3 points) visibly more valuable than the first (1 point) immediately incentivises completing the full line-breaking sequence.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of a team patiently circulating the ball before a penetrating pass breaks through two defensive lines, and identify the moment the gap appeared.
This drill rewards regular revisiting - the line-breaking pattern recognition develops gradually across many sessions.
diagramDescription: 50x35m area. Midfield defensive line (3 triangles) shown across the width at the midfield mark. Defensive line (3 more triangles) shown 20m deeper. Attacking team (circles) shown in possession in front of the midfield line. First penetration arrow shown going through a gap in the midfield line to an attacker positioned between the two lines. Second penetration arrow shown going through or around the defensive line into the target zone (shaded area behind the defensive line). Points values shown: between lines = 1 point, behind both lines = 3 points.

title: 1v1 to Goal — Finishing Under Pressure
objective: Develop the confidence and technique to penetrate a defender in a 1v1 situation and finish clinically.
learningOutcomes: Players can beat a defender 1v1 and finish with composure
Players develop the decision-making between dribbling past the defender and shooting early
Players build the mental composure required when bearing down on goal
Players learn to use the goalkeeper as part of the finishing decision
pitchSize: Full pitch from halfway line to goal
organisation: Players start at the halfway line with a ball. A defender starts 5m ahead of the attacker (giving the attacker a head start). The attacker must beat the defender and finish past the GK. Multiple groups running simultaneously on either side of the pitch.
instructions: Attacker starts at the halfway line with the ball.
Defender starts 5m ahead (between attacker and goal) but facing their own goal (disadvantaged start).
On GO: attacker drives forward with the ball. Defender turns and tries to win the ball before the shot.
Attacker must get a shot on target before the defender catches them or before reaching the penalty area edge.
DECISION POINT at 25m from goal: if the defender is not close, drive into the box and finish low past the GK. If the defender is close, use a skill move to create a shooting angle.
GK sets up at the penalty spot to narrow the angle.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): large head start (8m). Attacker has plenty of time. Focus on composure and finish quality.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): normal head start (5m). Genuine 1v1 challenge.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): small head start (2m). Attacker must use a skill move or shoot early to stay ahead.
coachingPoints: DRIVE AT SPEED EARLY, COMPOSE LATE: the approach to goal should be at full speed to maintain the head start, but in the final 10m, slight control must be regained to allow an accurate finish.
READ THE GK POSITION: as you approach the penalty area, glance at the GK. If they are off their line, a chip or early finish may be available. If they are well-positioned, finish low to the corners.
FINISH LOW: the vast majority of goals in 1v1 situations are scored low. A low finish forces the GK to dive fully to the ground. A high finish gives the GK height and reach to save.
THE NEAR POST IS RARELY THE BEST OPTION: shooting to the near post gives the GK the shortest distance to travel. The far post or the centre of the goal requires a bigger movement from the GK.
DO NOT WAIT TOO LONG: a common error in 1v1 situations is approaching so close to the GK that the shooting angle disappears. Shoot from outside the penalty area if the opportunity is clear.
USE THE DEFENDER TO MAKE THE GK COMMIT: if the defender is close, using a skill move to go past them sometimes also causes the GK to come off their line, creating more space to finish.
COMPOSURE IS A SKILL: composure in front of goal is not a personality trait. It is a trained response. The more 1v1 finishing situations you practice, the more composed you become.
WEAK FOOT IS AN OPTION: if the ball naturally falls to the weak foot in the final approach, use it. A composed weak-foot finish is more valuable than an awkward strong-foot finish.
CELEBRATE EVERY FINISH: any shot on target from a 1v1 situation is a success regardless of whether it goes in. The accuracy is the achievement.
commonMistakes: Losing composure in the final approach and rushing the finish
Waiting too long before shooting, reducing the shooting angle as the GK narrows it
Always shooting to the near post, making it easy for the GK to save
Taking too heavy a touch in the approach, allowing the defender to recover
Not reading the GKs position and shooting directly into where they are standing
corrections: Rushing: add a mandatory 1-second pause at the penalty spot before shooting in phase 1 to build the composure habit
Waiting too long: place a shooting cone at 18m from goal - the shot must be taken before reaching this cone if the defender is within 2m
Near post always: award bonus points for goals scored at the far post only for 5 minutes to break the near post habit
Heavy touch: reduce the head start to 3m and enforce a maximum of 2 touches in the final 15m of approach
Not reading GK: ask the attacker to call OUT (GK off line) or IN (GK on line) as they enter the penalty area before shooting
progressions: Reduced head start to 1m: almost simultaneous start, requiring a skill move or very early shot
Add a second defender who starts at the penalty area edge and can block the shot (but not tackle): tests whether the attacker can adjust their finish under this specific pressure
Competition format: track goals from 1v1 situations across the session and celebrate the highest converter
regressions: Increase head start to 10m to remove all defensive pressure and focus purely on composure and finish quality
Allow the attacker to stop and set up the shot without any defensive pressure at various distances from goal
Use a passive GK (stays on the line) to simplify the finishing decision
matchApplication: The 1v1 with the goalkeeper is one of the highest-quality chances in football and one of the most frequently missed. The composure, decision-making, and technique required to convert these chances consistently separates clinical finishers from average ones. By creating high volumes of 1v1 finishing repetitions in training, players develop the automatic composure responses that replace panic in match situations. The specific technical coaching points (finish low, read the GK, do not wait too long) are the same principles used by elite finishing coaches at professional clubs.
coachTips: Volume of repetitions is the most important variable in this drill - each player should face at least 10-15 1v1 situations per session.
The GK is a coaching participant too. Brief GK on specific things to try (coming off the line, staying compact) to create varied challenges for the attacker.
The composure coaching point is the most important and the hardest to coach. Be patient. It develops over many sessions.
Connect to professional examples: show a famous 1v1 that was missed and one that was converted, identifying the technical difference.
Track conversion rates across sessions. Seeing your own rate improve from 40 percent to 65 percent over a season is enormously motivating.
diagramDescription: Full pitch from halfway line to goal with GK. Attacker (circle) at halfway line with ball. Defender (triangle) shown 5m ahead facing own goal. Distance markers shown at 25m and 18m from goal. GK shown on penalty spot. Decision point shown at 25m: shoot early arrow (if space is clear) vs skill move arrow (if defender is close). Finish arrows shown going low to far post. Head start distances shown for the 3 phases.

title: Wall Pass and Overlap Combinations
objective: Develop the two-player wall pass and overlap as automatic attacking combinations that break through defensive pressure.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a give-and-go (wall pass) at match speed to bypass a defender
Players can time an overlap run to combine with a teammate and create a 2v1
Players understand when to use the wall pass versus the overlap based on the defenders position
Players build the automatic combination habits that characterise fluid attacking play
players: Groups of 3 (attacker, link player, passive defender, plus a finisher for the goal)
organisation: 25x15m zone per group of 3. Player A (attacker) dribbles toward a passive defender. Player B (link player) is positioned wide. The combination is either: A plays to B and overlaps (overlap combination) or A plays to B and B immediately returns it (wall pass). Mini-goal at the far end. Rotate roles every 5 minutes.
instructions: Player A starts with the ball at one end of the zone, dribbling toward the passive defender.
Player B stands 5m wide of the defender.
As A approaches the defender, A plays a pass to B.
OPTION 1 - WALL PASS: B plays the ball immediately back into the space behind the defender on the side A was running. A continues running to collect the return. This is the classic give-and-go.
OPTION 2 - OVERLAP: B holds the ball for 1-2 seconds (enough for A to run past the defender), then plays forward to A who has now overlapped and is in space behind the defender.
In PHASE 1 (6 minutes): the coach calls WALL or OVERLAP before each rep, telling the players which combination to execute.

In PHASE 2 (6 minutes): the passive defender is told to press toward A (triggering the wall pass) or to track B (triggering the overlap). A must read which is happening and execute the correct combination.
In PHASE 3 (6 minutes): the defender is fully active and can tackle. A must read the situation, choose the correct combination, and execute it against real pressure.
coachingPoints: THE WALL PASS TIMING: the pass from A to B must go at the moment A is level with the defender, not 3m before. Too early and the defender can turn and intercept the return. At the right moment, the defender cannot do both.
THE WALL PASS RETURN: B must return the ball first touch into the space behind and beyond the defender, not back to where A was. The return goes into the space A is running into.
THE OVERLAP TRIGGER: A starts the overlap run the moment they play the pass to B. A does not wait to see what B does - they immediately run beyond the defender.

WHICH COMBINATION TO USE: if the defender presses toward A, use the wall pass (the defender is now committed forward and cannot recover). If the defender tracks B (sidestepping), use the overlap (they have moved toward B, leaving space behind for A to run into).
PACE OF EXECUTION: both combinations must be executed at genuine pace. A slow wall pass allows the defender to recover between the first pass and the return.
B MUST ANTICIPATE WHICH IS COMING: in phase 2, B must read the defenders movement as quickly as A does and prepare the correct response.

THESE COMBINATIONS WORK BECAUSE OF DECEPTION: both the wall pass and the overlap appear like simple passes before becoming movement that beats the defender. The defender cannot defend both simultaneously.
FINISH AFTER THE COMBINATION: the combination is only valuable if it creates a genuine chance. Finish with composure after executing the combination successfully.
commonMistakes: Passing from too far away from the defender, giving them time to recover before the return is played
Wall pass return going sideways rather than into the space ahead of A running
A not running after playing the pass in the wall pass option, stopping to watch instead
Not reading the defenders movement to decide which combination is correct
The overlap run starting too late, not creating genuine space behind the defender before B plays the ball
corrections: Passing too far away: place a cone 1-2m from the defender - the first pass must go after A passes this cone
Return going sideways: draw an arrow on the ground showing the return direction - it must go forward, not sideways
A stopping after pass: the wall pass combination does not count unless A is running at the moment B returns the ball
Not reading defender: freeze the drill at the moment of the first pass and ask A to describe what the defender has done and which combination that means
Overlap run too late: A must begin their run before the ball arrives at B, not after - mark the start point for the run with a cone
progressions: Active defender who can challenge for both the wall pass return and the overlap simultaneously
Speed challenge: how quickly can the combination be completed from the first pass to the finish? Time it and challenge the pair to reduce the time
Full small-sided game with bonus points for goals scored directly from a wall pass or overlap combination
regressions: Walk through both combinations at slow motion before any live attempt
Use a completely static defender for the first 10 minutes so players can focus entirely on the technique and timing of both combinations
Reduce to wall pass only for one full session before introducing the overlap choice
matchApplication: The wall pass and overlap are the two most fundamental two-player attacking combinations in football. They work because they force a defender to make an impossible choice: cover the ball or cover the run. Get the timing right and the defender is beaten regardless of what they choose. These combinations are the building blocks of tiki-taka (the wall pass is its most basic expression) and of every fluid attacking system. Players who can execute both automatically and choose between them based on the defenders movement are extremely difficult to defend.
coachTips: The decision between wall pass and overlap should be specifically coached in phase 2 by discussing what the defenders movement means for which option to use.
Both players must understand both roles - rotate positions frequently so B also understands the runners role.

The pace of the combination is the most important quality variable. Use a stopwatch occasionally to show players how their combination time compares to the 2-second standard of elite combination play.
Connect to professional examples: every great attacking team uses both combinations constantly. Show examples from Barcelona (wall pass) and Liverpool (overlap).
Once the combination is established, this drill can be used as a 10-minute warm-up before any attacking session.
diagramDescription: 25x15m zone. Player A shown with ball approaching the passive defender. Player B shown 5m wide. Two combination options shown: WALL PASS (solid arrows) - A passes to B, B immediately returns into space behind defender, A runs to collect; OVERLAP (dashed arrows) - A passes to B, A continues running past defender, B holds then plays forward to A in space. Defender shown being beaten by both combinations. Decision trigger shown: defender pressing A = wall pass; defender tracking B = overlap.

title: One-Two Combinations in Tight Space
objective: Teach the give-and-go (one-two) as a simple two-player combination that bypasses a defender using quick passing and movement.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a give-and-go combination correctly at close range
Players develop the habit of running after a pass to collect the return
Players understand that the one-two is more effective than dribbling in tight space
Players build the quick-passing and movement habits that underpin all combination play
players: Groups of 3 (2 attackers plus 1 passive defender)
equipment: Cones for a simple gate (2 cones, 3m apart)
organisation: Simple 15m channel with a gate (2 cones, 3m apart) at the midpoint representing a defender. Player A starts at one end with ball. Player B stands 3m to the side of the gate. A must use B to play a one-two combination through the gate.
instructions: Player A dribbles toward the gate (the defender).
3m before the gate: A passes the ball to Player B who is standing to the side.
A continues running toward and through the gate without stopping.
B receives and immediately plays the ball back through the gate into the space ahead of A who is now running through it.
A collects the return pass on the other side of the gate and dribbles to the far end.
Swap roles: A becomes B, B goes to the far end, and a new player starts from the beginning.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): walk through at slow pace. Coaching points on the timing and the running after the pass.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): jogging pace. Focus on fluency of the combination.
PHASE 3 (4 minutes): full pace. A must reach full speed through the gate.
coachingPoints: PASS AND RUN, NEVER PASS AND STOP: the most important habit in this drill is that A never stops when they pass to B. Pass and immediately continue running. The combination only works if A is moving.
PASS AT THE RIGHT MOMENT: A should pass to B when they are approximately 3m from the gate, not 8m and not 1m. Too early gives the gate too much time to close. Too late and A is already at the gate.
B MUST PLAY THE RETURN FIRST TOUCH: the return from B should be first touch whenever possible. Any extra touch delays the return and allows the gate (defender) to recover.
THE RETURN GOES THROUGH THE GATE, NOT SIDEWAYS: B must play the return ball through the gate (forward) into the space A is running into. A sideways return does not beat the defender.
A MUST RUN THROUGH THE GATE: A does not slow down or stop at the gate. They run through it trusting the return pass will arrive on the other side.
B MUST BE IN A GOOD POSITION: B cannot be directly behind A or directly behind the gate. A side position (3m to the side) gives B the angle to both receive and return forward.
THE QUALITY OF THE RETURN DETERMINES EVERYTHING: a soft return that does not reach A quickly enough allows the gate to close. A firm, accurate return into the space ahead of A is the goal.
THIS IS THE FIRST BUILDING BLOCK OF ALL COMBINATION PLAY: the one-two is the simplest two-player combination and the foundation of everything more complex that follows.
commonMistakes: A stopping after passing to B instead of continuing to run through the gate
B playing the return too late, after A has already passed through the gate
The return going sideways instead of through the gate into the space ahead of A
A passing to B when still too far from the gate (8m+), giving the defender time to recover
B not being in a position wide enough to receive from A and then return forward
corrections: A stopping: make a rule that A must be past the gate before the combination is considered complete, forcing continuous running
Late return from B: B must return the ball within 1 second of receiving. Use a count-aloud to enforce it.
Return going sideways: draw an arrow through the gate showing the direction the return must travel
Passing too far: place a cone 3m from the gate showing the correct passing point
B too central: place a marker cone 3m to the side showing exactly where B must stand
progressions: Active defender: replace the gate with a passive defender who can lean and reach but cannot move their feet
Two defenders: a second gate appears and A must execute two consecutive one-two combinations to reach the far end
Speed gate: time each complete combination from the first pass to A crossing the far end. Challenge the pair to beat their time
regressions: Reduce to a completely static B (B does not even turn to face A - just returns it off the inside of the foot) to simplify the return to its minimum complexity
Use a very wide gate (5m) to make the combination easier before narrowing it
Allow A to slow down briefly at the gate before picking up pace again if the combination timing is too difficult at full pace
matchApplication: The give-and-go is the most fundamental two-player combination in football and is the direct foundation of every more sophisticated combination that follows it. When players learn the one-two early and correctly (pass and immediately run, trust the return, collect on the other side), they develop a passing-and-movement instinct that carries through their entire career. The greatest technical players in history all had one thing in common: they could execute the one-two at match speed automatically, without thinking. This drill builds exactly that automatic response.
coachTips: Keep the session lighthearted and full of encouragement. The one-two is a new concept for U10 players and it will not be clean immediately.
Demonstrate the combination yourself, including the running-after-passing element, before players attempt it.
The most common error (stopping after passing) requires patient, consistent correction across the whole session.
Once the combination is established, use it as a daily 5-minute warm-up paired with the Brazilian Square from the Streets Meet Tiki-Taka category.
Connect to a simple professional example: show any professional one-two at match speed and ask players to identify which player is A and which is B.
diagramDescription: 15m channel with a 3m-wide gate shown at the 7m mark. Player A shown with ball at one end. Player B shown 3m to the side of the gate. Pass arrow from A to B (just before the gate). A running arrow continuing through the gate. Return arrow from B through the gate into the space ahead. A shown collecting the return on the far side of the gate and continuing to the end of the channel. Timing sequence shown: pass at 3m from gate, return within 1 second, A through gate.

title: Creating Chances in the Final Third
objective: Develop the attacking patterns and individual qualities needed to consistently create goal-scoring opportunities in the final third of the pitch.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the movement, combination, and finishing patterns that create high-quality chances
Players understand the difference between a high-quality chance (close, clear, time) and a low-quality one
Players build the attacking game intelligence to recognise and exploit defensive weaknesses in the final third
Teams develop 2-3 reliable patterns for creating chances from different starting positions
players: 10-12 players (5-6 attackers vs 4-5 defenders plus GK)
pitchSize: Final third of the pitch (35m from goal x full width)
organisation: Final third of the pitch. 5-6 attackers work against 4-5 defenders plus GK. The attacking team starts with possession at the edge of the final third and tries to create and convert high-quality chances. After each sequence (shot on target, possession lost, or ball out of play) the sequence restarts.
instructions: Attackers start with possession at the top of the final third (35m from goal).
Defenders set up in a compact defensive shape (4-3 or 4-4 depending on numbers).
Attackers have 20 seconds maximum to create and attempt a shot. After 20 seconds possession resets.
CHANCE QUALITY SYSTEM: shots are rated by the coach immediately after they are taken:
HIGH QUALITY (3 points): shot from inside the penalty area with time and space. Clear sight of goal.
MEDIUM QUALITY (1 point): shot from outside the box, or inside the box under pressure.
LOW QUALITY (0 points): rushed shot, shot with no sight of goal, shot from very wide angle.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): attackers discuss before each sequence which pattern they will use to create the chance. Coach reviews after each attempt.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): live continuous play. The quality rating system is applied after every shot.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): the attacking team is given a specific starting position (wide left, wide right, or central) before each sequence, replicating the variety of final third entry positions.
coachingPoints: QUALITY OVER QUANTITY: a high-quality chance from inside the box is worth far more than 5 rushed shots from outside it. The 20-second clock creates urgency but should not create panic.
ENTER THE BOX BEFORE SHOOTING: the most consistent pattern for high-quality chances is getting the ball inside the penalty area. Everything before that is preparation for the entry.

COMBINATION BEFORE SHOT: the final pass before a shot is usually more important than the shot itself. A well-timed through ball, overlap, or cutback creates a better finishing opportunity than a dribble directly at the GK.
MOVE DEFENDERS BEFORE ATTACKING THE SPACE: in the final third, one or two quick passes or a dribble that draws a defender out of position creates the gap that the next player can attack.
READ THE GK POSITION: before every shot, the attacker should know where the GK is. Shooting where the GK is not is the simplest finishing principle.

THE CUTBACK IS OFTEN THE BEST CHANCE: a ball cut back from the byline to an arriving attacker at the edge of the penalty area is statistically one of the highest-quality chances in football. Prioritise creating cutback opportunities.
SECOND PHASE ALERTNESS: if the first shot is saved or cleared, every attacking player must immediately react to the second phase opportunity. Many high-quality chances come from rebounds.
PATIENCE IS NOT HESITATION: waiting 3-4 extra seconds for a better chance to develop is patience. Waiting indefinitely while the defence organises is hesitation. Know the difference.
commonMistakes: Rushing shots from distance before genuine final-third entry has been created
Taking too long and using all 20 seconds without creating a clear opportunity
Always attacking the same area of the final third, becoming predictable
No movement from players without the ball, leaving the ball-carrier with only 1-2 options
Not attacking the second phase after the first shot or chance is not converted
corrections: Rushing: apply the chance quality system strictly and award 0 points for low-quality shots, immediately creating an incentive to create better chances
Using all 20 seconds: introduce a sub-target of creating a shot within 12 seconds which earns a bonus point if achieved
Attacking same area: require each sequence to start from a different position (left, centre, right) before returning to free choice
No movement: every attacking player without the ball must make a run at the moment the ball enters the final third
No second phase: place a second attacker specifically as a second-phase runner whose role is to arrive at rebounds and through balls after the first shot
progressions: Reduce defenders to 3 to create more space and allow the attacking patterns to flow more clearly before adding defenders
Remove the time limit and allow free attacking play, then gradually reintroduce the time limit as the patterns become more fluent
Add a specific pattern requirement: all chances must be created from a combination involving at least 2 passes inside the penalty area
regressions: Walk through each of the 2-3 established attacking patterns at slow motion with no defensive pressure before any live attempt
Use passive defenders who hold their shape but do not actively challenge for a full phase before introducing active defending
Reduce to 3v2 to create more immediate space and allow the final-third concepts to be explored without a full defensive block
matchApplication: Creating high-quality chances consistently is the difference between teams that score goals regularly and teams that create opportunities but fail to convert them. The quality rating system in this drill is a direct reflection of professional coaching philosophy: expected goals (xG) measures the quality of chances created, and teams that regularly create high-xG opportunities score more goals than teams that create only low-quality chances. By teaching attackers to prioritise chance quality over chance quantity at U15, this drill develops the attacking intelligence that maximises goal-scoring output at every level.
coachTips: The chance quality system is the most important element of this drill and must be applied consistently and loudly after every shot.
Be specific when rating chance quality and explain why: shot from inside the box with time and space is 3 points because the GK has the least reaction time and the shooter has the most accuracy.
Track cumulative quality points across the session and set a team target to beat in the next session.
Connect to the concept of expected goals (xG) which players may have encountered watching professional football coverage.

The specific patterns the team develops in this drill (their preferred ways of creating chances from different positions) should be practised regularly and become part of the teams attacking identity.
diagramDescription: Final third of the pitch (35m x full width). Full-size goal with GK. 5-6 attackers shown in various positions. 4-5 defenders in compact shape. Starting position shown at the top of the final third. Three annotated chance examples: HIGH QUALITY shown as a ball arriving inside the penalty area with time (3 points label), MEDIUM QUALITY shown as a shot from 25m (1 point label), LOW QUALITY shown as a rushed wide-angle shot (0 points label). Cutback opportunity highlighted from the byline. Second-phase runner shown arriving after the initial shot.

title: Finishing Combinations — Cross and Finish
objective: Develop the cross-and-finish sequence including the movement of strikers and the technique of finishing from crosses.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the timing and technique of finishing from crosses in the penalty area
Wide players develop the technique and decision-making for crossing under pressure
Players build coordinated striker movement patterns (near post, far post, late arrival)
Teams develop reliable crossing and finishing combinations as a consistent attacking weapon
players: 8-10 players (2 wide players, 2-3 strikers, GK, optional defenders)
pitchSize: Full penalty area plus wide channels
organisation: Full penalty area with full-size goal and GK. Wide players in crossing positions (left and right channels). 2-3 strikers in the penalty area. Defenders added progressively. Wide players alternate delivering crosses while strikers practice coordinated movement patterns.
instructions: Establish the three movement patterns before starting:
RUN A (near post): striker drives from inside the penalty area to the near post, timing the run to arrive as the cross is delivered.
RUN B (far post): striker holds a central position and moves late to the far post as the cross is in the air.
RUN C (edge arrival): a third player arrives from the edge of the area to attack any ball that drops short.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): one striker, no defenders. Practice all 3 run types with crosses from both sides. Focus on run timing.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): two strikers simultaneously. Striker 1 makes Run A, Striker 2 makes Run B. Wide player reads which run is being made and crosses to the appropriate zone.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): add defenders (one per striker). Strikers must lose their marker before making the run. Wide players must cross before their own wide defender gets close enough to block.
coachingPoints: TIMING THE RUN TO THE CROSS: the run must start as the wide player is about to cross, not before. A run started too early arrives at the post before the ball and can be easily tracked by a defender.
NEAR POST RUN TECHNIQUE: the near post run is a sharp, definitive movement from central to near post. It must be aggressive and committed. A hesitant near post run is easy for a defender to cover.

FAR POST POSITIONING: the far post runner must hold a central position longer than feels comfortable, waiting until the ball is crossed before committing to the far post. Early movement tips off the defender.
THE CROSS GOES TO THE RUNNER, NOT THE SPACE: the wide player must identify which run is being made and cross to where that runner will be, not to a generic dangerous area.

EDGE OF AREA RUNNER IS THE SAFETY OPTION: the Run C arrival at the edge of the area collects all the balls that fall short of the goalmouth. This player is often the one who scores from rebounds and clearances.
HEADER TECHNIQUE FROM A CROSS: use the across-goal heading principle from the heading drills - head the ball back across goal, toward the far corner from where the cross came.
FINISH WITH THE FIRST OPPORTUNITY: a striker who controls a cross and sets up a finish gives the defence and GK time to recover. Finish at the first opportunity whenever technically possible.
GK COMMUNICATION: the GK must call KEEPER or AWAY on every cross, and defenders must respond immediately. If GK calls KEEPER, all attackers and defenders give way.
commonMistakes: Near post runner arriving too early and being caught offside or tracked too easily
Far post runner moving too early, telegraphing the run and allowing the defender to track it
Wide player crossing to a generic area rather than specifically to the near post run or far post run
Strikers both making the same run, crowding one area and leaving the other empty
Finishing technique breaking down under the specific demands of a crossed ball arriving at pace
corrections: Near post too early: place a cone at the near post - runner cannot start their run until the wide player reaches the crossing position
Far post moving too early: require the far post runner to touch a central cone before making the far post run, ensuring they hold their position
Generic crossing: require the wide player to call NEAR or FAR before crossing, confirming they have identified the run and are crossing to it
Both making same run: assign Run A and Run B to specific players before each phase and enforce the separation
Finishing technique breakdown: practice heading and volleying from crosses in the isolated technique drills before combining with movement
progressions: Add a second wide player on the opposite flank who crosses after the first, creating a second wave of chances if the first cross is cleared
Introduce an additional striker whose specific role is to press the GK if they try to claim the cross
Full game with the crossing and finishing sequence rewarded with double points for goals scored directly from crosses
regressions: Use passive defenders only throughout to allow movement patterns and finishing technique to be established without defensive pressure
Slow the crosses and reduce cross pace so strikers have more time to read, adjust, and finish
Allow a controlling touch before finishing if players are struggling with the direct finishing from crosses
matchApplication: Crossing and finishing is one of the most reliable goal-scoring methods at every level of football, and the specific movement patterns practiced in this drill (near post, far post, edge arrival) are exactly the patterns used by professional attacking teams to exploit wide play. Wide play that creates crosses accounts for a significant percentage of all goals scored at the top level, and the teams with the most coordinated striker movement from those crosses create the best chances. Teaching these specific patterns and the technical finishing quality required at U13 gives players the attacking toolkit they need throughout their career.
coachTips: The coordination between wide players and strikers in terms of the cross going to the specific run is the most important coaching element - celebrate it when it happens correctly.
Phase 2 with two simultaneous strikers is the critical development phase - watch specifically for which runner the wide player is crossing to and whether it matches the actual run being made.
Film Phase 3 from behind the goal to observe the finishing quality, the GK communication, and the defender tracking from the attacking teams perspective.
Connect to professional examples: discuss specific cross-and-finish goals and identify which of the three run types was used.
This drill pairs perfectly with the defending crosses drill from the Defending category - run both in the same session occasionally to develop both sides of the same situation.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Wide player shown in left crossing channel. Three striker movement patterns shown with numbered arrows: Run A (near post run from central, arriving at near post as cross delivered), Run B (far post - striker holds centrally then moves late to far post), Run C (edge of area arrival from 20m). Cross arc shown from wide player. Finish arrows shown from each run to the goal corners. GK shown in central position with KEEPER call label.

title: Playing Out of a High Press Into the Final Third
objective: Develop the composure and technical quality required to play through or around a high press and arrive in the final third with a clear attacking opportunity.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain composure under a high press and play through it cleanly
Players develop the quick combination play needed to bypass a high press with few touches
Players understand the decision between playing through the press and going direct over it
Teams build the confidence to play their way out of pressure rather than conceding possession
organisation: Full pitch. Team A defends deep with the ball. Team B presses high (5-6 players in Team As defensive third). Team A must build through or around Team Bs press and arrive in Team Bs defensive third with a clear opportunity. Team B scores by winning the ball in Team As defensive third.
instructions: Team A has possession in their own defensive third. GK or CB starts with the ball.
Team B applies a high press with 5-6 players pressing immediately when Team A has the ball.
TEAM A OPTIONS for playing through or around the press:
OPTION 1 - QUICK COMBINATION THROUGH: pass quickly between defenders to move the press, find the midfielder between the lines, and play forward.
OPTION 2 - SWITCH PLAY: find the player on the far side of the press where no opposition players have covered.
OPTION 3 - DIRECT BALL OVER THE PRESS: when all close options are covered, a long accurate ball over the press to a striker who has stayed high.
TEAM A SCORES: by arriving in Team Bs defensive third with possession (3 points) or by creating a shot on goal (5 points).
TEAM B SCORES: by winning the ball in Team As defensive third and immediately shooting (3 points).
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): Team B presses at 70 percent intensity. Team A focuses on reading the press and choosing the correct option.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): full intensity press. Team A must execute under genuine pressure.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): free game with both teams able to press and build at will.
coachingPoints: IDENTIFY THE PRESS SHAPE BEFORE IT ARRIVES: as the ball is being passed back to the goalkeeper or a defender, the player receiving should already be reading where the press is coming from and which option will be available.
THE THIRD MAN IS THE KEY: the ball-carrier under a press typically passes to a nearby player who immediately plays to a third player who is free. This two-pass sequence beats most pressing systems.
DO NOT HOLD THE BALL UNDER A PRESS: one touch and release. Any additional touch under a press gives the pressing player time to arrive and takes away the passing option.
SWITCH THE PLAY BEFORE THE PRESS ARRIVES: passing the ball quickly to the far side of the pitch before the press can cover is the simplest and most effective press-avoidance strategy.
THE LONG BALL IS NOT FAILURE: when the close options are genuinely not available, a well-executed long ball to a striker who has stayed high is a completely valid and correct decision.
STAY CALM UNDER PRESSURE: the press is designed to create panic. A team that stays calm, takes one touch and releases, and keeps the ball moving is almost impossible to press effectively.

USE THE GK AS AN OUTFIELD PLAYER: the GK can be the extra player that breaks the press. A GK who is comfortable with the ball at their feet and can switch play is a significant advantage against a pressing team.
ARRIVE IN THE FINAL THIRD WITH PURPOSE: the goal of beating the press is not just to have the ball in the middle third. The goal is to arrive in the final third with a genuine attacking opportunity.
commonMistakes: Holding the ball too long under the press, giving pressing players time to arrive and cut off options
Not reading the press shape early enough to identify the available option before the ball arrives
All players dropping too deep to escape the press, removing any forward options and making the press easier to execute
The long ball option being underhit or inaccurate, falling to the pressing team rather than the intended striker
No movement from the striker who has stayed high, making the long ball option harder to execute
corrections: Holding ball too long: enforce a maximum 2-touch rule for all players in Team As defensive third when under the press
Not reading early: require each player to call THROUGH, SWITCH, or LONG before receiving the ball, confirming they have read the situation
All dropping deep: at least 2 players from Team A must stay in the middle third at all times when Team B is pressing
Long ball underhit: practice the long ball technique specifically (height, distance, placement) in isolation before applying it to the press situation
Striker not moving: assign the striker specific movement instruction before each rep - check away then make a run to receive the long ball
progressions: Remove the safety of the long ball option for 5 minutes, forcing Team A to play through the press by combination only
Add an additional pressing player to Team B, increasing the intensity and creating a genuine overload against Team A
Full match with both teams able to implement pressing and building strategies simultaneously, as in a real match
regressions: Reduce Team B to 3-4 pressing players only to make the press less intense while the build-out patterns are being established
Allow Team A 3 touches per player without any press in Phase 1 before introducing the press
Use the switch and build GK to winger drill from the Tactical category as a foundation before adding the pressing element
matchApplication: Playing through a high press is one of the defining challenges of modern football and a quality that separates teams that can control matches from teams that are reactive and under constant pressure. Teams like Man City, Liverpool, and Barcelona are rarely rattled by opposition pressing because their players have developed the composure, technical quality, and tactical intelligence to find the press-beating pass automatically and quickly. Teaching this quality at U16, where players are approaching the physical and technical maturity to execute it at match speed, gives them one of the most valuable possession skills in modern football.
coachTips: The composure element is as important to coach as the technical element - explicitly praise calm, composed play under the press even when the pass is not perfect.
This session is complex and requires senior players - for U16 it is appropriate but demands focus and concentration from every player.
Walk through all three options (through, switch, long) very clearly before any live play begins.
Connect to specific professional examples: show a team being pressed and successfully playing through it, identifying which option they used and why.
Run this drill regularly as the season progresses - the press-beating composure develops over many repetitions and becomes genuinely automatic by the end of a season of consistent practice.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Team A shown with the ball in their defensive third (GK and defenders). Team B shown pressing with 5-6 players across the width (triangles pressing forward). Three escape options labeled: OPTION 1 (quick combination through - pass arrows between defenders to a midfielder between the lines), OPTION 2 (switch play - long pass to the uncovered far side), OPTION 3 (long ball over the press to a striker who has stayed high). Scoring zones shown: Team A reaching Team Bs defensive third (3 points), Team B winning ball and shooting in Team As defensive third (3 points).

title: Finishing Under Fatigue
objective: Develop the ability to maintain finishing technique and composure when physically fatigued, replicating the demands of the final 20 minutes of a match.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain composure and technique in front of goal when physically tired
Players develop the mental discipline to focus on finishing quality under fatigue
Players build the physical resilience to arrive in the box ready to finish after a run
Players connect finishing quality under fatigue to match performance in the final stages
pitchSize: Full pitch (run from halfway) to goal
organisation: Players sprint from the halfway line to the edge of the penalty area, receive a pass from a server at the edge of the box, and finish. The sprint creates the physical fatigue that replicates the demands of making a late run in a match. GK in goal. Rotate between sprinting and serving every 5 minutes.
instructions: Player A starts at the halfway line.
On GO: A sprints at maximum pace from the halfway line to the edge of the penalty area (approximately 30-35m).
At the edge of the box: a server plays a pass to A. The pass can be:
- A through ball (A runs onto it and finishes first time)
- A lay-off (A receives, takes one touch, finishes)
- A cross from wide (A must head or volley the crossed ball)
A must finish within 2 touches of receiving.
Immediately after the shot: A jogs back to the halfway line and repeats.
Each player completes 6 consecutive sprint-and-finish repetitions before resting.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): through balls only. One direction of run. Straight finish.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): vary the pass type (through ball, lay-off, cross). Player must adjust finishing technique to the delivery.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): add a recovering defender who starts 5m behind A and chases throughout the sprint. Adds pressure to the finish.
coachingPoints: COMPOSURE GROWS WITH PRACTICE: the feeling of being unable to finish cleanly when tired is familiar to every player. The response is not to try to ignore the fatigue but to practice finishing under fatigue until the technique becomes automatic regardless of physical state.

BREATHE BEFORE THE FINISH: the specific moment of finishing requires a brief composure reset. Take one controlled breath as the ball arrives. This physiologically reduces the heart rate for 1-2 seconds and allows a cleaner technical execution.

FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION: when fatigued, the first touch often goes in a poor direction because the brain is managing both the physical demand and the technical decision simultaneously. Focus the first touch on creating a shooting position, not just stopping the ball.

SIMPLE TECHNIQUE WHEN TIRED: complex finishing techniques (volleys, chips, back-heels) require more technical precision than fatigued muscles can reliably provide. Default to the most reliable technique (inside of foot, placed finish) when physically tired.
VISUALISE THE FINISH DURING THE RUN: use the 4-5 seconds of the sprint to mentally prepare for the finish. Decide where you are shooting before the ball arrives.
PRACTICE AT THE CORRECT INTENSITY: if the sprint is done at 70 percent effort, the finishing practice does not replicate match fatigue. Maximum effort sprints only.

MATCH APPLICATION IS EVERYTHING: the goals scored in the 75th-90th minute of a match, when players are physically at their most tired, are often the most decisive. The player who can still finish cleanly at that stage wins matches.
RECOVERY IS SKILL: the jog back between reps is not wasted time. Use it to mentally reset and prepare for the next rep.
commonMistakes: Sprint intensity too low, not creating genuine physical fatigue that replicates match conditions
Finish attempted from too far from goal, reducing the quality of the chance
Too many touches before the finish, using up the brief window before the recovering defender arrives
Not breathing and composing before the finish, rushing the technical execution
Finishing technique changing significantly between rested and fatigued states
corrections: Low sprint intensity: pair players in a race format - the player who arrives first at the edge of the box gets the better delivery, creating an incentive for maximum sprint pace
Finishing too far out: mark the maximum shooting distance (20m) and require A to be inside this mark before shooting
Too many touches: enforce the 2-touch maximum strictly and have A call touch counts aloud
Not composing: add a mandatory 0.5-second wait before the first touch of the finish, creating a small composure window
Technique changing under fatigue: track and compare finishing accuracy between the first rep (fresh) and the sixth rep (fatigued) to make the impact of fatigue visible
progressions: Increase to 8 consecutive reps before rest to deepen the fatigue effect
Add a competitive element: the player with the highest conversion rate across 6 reps wins the round
Combine with the creating chances drill: players must sprint from halfway, arrive at the edge of the final third, and THEN create a chance within the 20-second window
regressions: Reduce to 4 reps before rest and allow 30 seconds of rest between each rep
Reduce the sprint distance to 20m to create a less intense fatigue effect while building the finishing-under-pressure habit
Allow 3 touches before the finish to give more time for composure in the early sessions
matchApplication: Finishing under physical fatigue is a specific and frequently neglected area of technical training. Yet the most decisive goals in football are often scored in the final 20 minutes when players are at their most tired, and the player who can still produce a calm, accurate finish in that state is exceptionally valuable. The breathing and composure technique coached explicitly in this drill, combined with the high volume of fatigued finishing repetitions, directly develops the match-specific capability that determines whether teams score late goals or waste their best late-game chances.
coachTips: The sprint must be genuinely at maximum effort or the drill does not replicate what it is designed to replicate. Be firm about this.
The breathing coaching point is easy to understand but easy to forget under fatigue. Remind players of it specifically before every rep.
