Track conversion rates explicitly and discuss with players after the session whether their technique held up across all 6 reps.
Connect to specific match situations: discuss a time when your team created a good chance late in a match and did not convert it, and connect that to the fatigue element.
This drill is physically demanding. Ensure adequate recovery before and after in the session plan.
diagramDescription: Full pitch from halfway line to goal. Player A shown starting at the halfway line. Sprint arrow shown at maximum pace to the edge of the penalty area (35m). Server shown at the edge of the box with three delivery options labeled (through ball, lay-off, cross). GK shown in full-size goal. 2-touch maximum indicator shown. Recovering defender shown 5m behind A at the start of the sprint in Phase 3. Conversion rate tracker shown with 6 rep slots.

title: 1v1 Spreading and Blocking
objective: Develop the GK technique for closing down a 1v1 attacker — when to come, how to spread, and how to smother the ball.
learningOutcomes: GK can judge the correct moment to come off the line in a 1v1
GK develops the spreading technique to make themselves big at the right moment
GK can smother low balls at the attackers feet safely
GK builds the composure to stay upright and force the attacker to make the first decision
pitchSize: Full pitch penalty area and approach channel
organisation: A channel 10m wide is marked from the penalty spot to 30m from goal. Servers roll balls into the channel from the side at various distances. GK must leave the line, close the angle, and deal with the ball before an attacker running from 25m can reach it.
instructions: GK starts on the goal line, central.
Server plays a ball into the channel at 20m from goal (between the GK and a runner who starts 25m from goal).
GK immediately sprints off the line to close the angle on the ball.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): no attacker. GK practices reading the ball and closing the angle at different distances. Coach rolls balls at various distances — 20m, 15m, 10m from goal.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): attacker added who runs from 25m when the ball is played. GK must arrive before the attacker and deal with the ball.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): attacker starts at 20m (closer). GK must judge whether to come or hold the line based on whether they can arrive before the attacker.
SPREADING TECHNIQUE: as the GK arrives at the ball, get low, spread both arms and body wide, and go feet-first to smother the ball legally.
coachingPoints: COME OR STAY — THE DECISION: come if you can arrive before the attacker and win the ball cleanly. Stay if the attacker will reach the ball first — coming in that situation creates a certain goal.

ANGLE REDUCTION IS THE PRIMARY JOB: as the GK comes off the line, they reduce the shooting angle. Even if they cannot reach the ball, closing to 8-10m from the attacker dramatically reduces the available goal.
STAY BIG AND UPRIGHT AS LONG AS POSSIBLE: do not go to ground too early. An upright GK who forces the attacker to make the first decision is harder to beat than one who dives early.
THE SPREADING MOMENT: only go to ground (spreading) when the attacker is within 2-3m and is about to shoot. Earlier than this and the GK is beaten over the top or around the side.
LEAD WITH THE HANDS, NOT THE HEAD: when spreading, get the hands and arms toward the ball first. This protects the head and gives the best chance of getting a touch on the ball.
BRAVE BUT SAFE: smothering a ball at a players feet requires physical courage. Stay legal (do not endanger the attacker) but commit fully to the spread when the moment is right.
FORCE THE ATTACKER WIDE: position slightly to one side (the dominant shooting side) to show the attacker toward the harder shooting angle.
AFTER THE SPREAD: if the ball is not fully secured, get up immediately and try to clear. Do not stay on the ground watching.
commonMistakes: Coming off the line too late, allowing the attacker to get a clean shot off from close range
Going to ground too early while the attacker still has time and space to go around or chip
Coming off the line when the attacker will clearly arrive first, making a certain goal more likely
Spreading with the head forward rather than the hands, creating a dangerous situation
Not staying big and forcing the attacker to make the decision — committing to a side before the attacker has touched the ball
corrections: Too late off line: place a trigger cone at 20m from goal. GK must move when the ball passes the trigger cone regardless of distance
Going down too early: add a foam noodle at 3m from the GK starting position. GK must not go to ground until they are past this marker
Coming when attacker wins: in Phase 3, the coach calls HOLD or COME before each ball, overriding the GKs decision until the correct habit is established
Head forward in spread: specifically practice the spread motion against a stationary ball, emphasising hands-first approach
Not staying big: add a rule that the GK cannot use their feet until the attacker has made contact with the ball in phase 1
progressions: Reduce the attackers head start to 5m, creating a genuine race that forces the GK to make the decision under real urgency
Add a chip option: the attacker is allowed to chip if the GK comes too far, punishing over-aggressive line departure
Full match 1v1 scenarios: the GK faces multiple 1v1 situations from different angles and distances, applying the decision-making framework throughout
regressions: Use no attacker for the full first session, focusing purely on the GK reading the ball and closing the angle at different distances
Mark the correct spreading position with a cone so the GK has a visual reference for how close they should be before going to ground
Allow the GK to go to ground at any point in Phase 1 to build confidence in the spreading motion before adding timing constraints
matchApplication: The 1v1 situation is one of the most frequent and most important individual GK challenges in football. A GK who handles 1v1 situations confidently — coming at the right moment, staying big, forcing the decision — saves goals that an uncertain GK concedes. Conversely, a GK who comes too soon or too late makes the attackers job dramatically easier. The decision of whether to come or hold is the most important single decision a GK makes in a 1v1, and developing the correct instinct for that decision through repeated practice is the central purpose of this drill.
coachTips: The come or stay coaching point is the most important in this drill and should be discussed explicitly before any live practice begins.
Stand level with the 20m mark during the drill so you can evaluate the GKs decision from the same angle as the attacker.
Celebrate brave, correctly timed spreads explicitly and enthusiastically. Physical courage deserves specific recognition.
Film the drill from the side to show the GK their own angle reduction as they come off the line.
This drill works best at the start of a session when the GK is mentally fresh — the decision-making demands are high.
diagramDescription: Full pitch channel 10m wide from penalty spot to 30m from goal. GK shown on goal line. Ball shown being rolled into the channel at 20m. GK sprint arrow shown leaving the line. Angle reduction shown: two lines from each post to the ball position showing how the shooting angle reduces as the GK advances. Spreading technique shown as GK goes wide and low at 3m from the ball. Attacker running arrow from 25m shown arriving just after the GK. COME vs HOLD decision shown as a branching choice at the trigger cone.

title: 1v1 Set Position and Angle Play
objective: Develop the advanced 1v1 GK skills of precise angle play, set position timing, and decision-making against technically skilled attackers.
learningOutcomes: GK can calculate and hold the correct position for any 1v1 angle around the penalty area
GK develops the set position timing that allows maximum reaction in each direction
GK reads attacking body shape and shooting cues to improve anticipation
GK builds the composure to influence attacker decision-making through positioning alone
pitchSize: Full penalty area and approach zones
organisation: Six shooting positions set around the penalty area at different angles and distances. GK practices the correct set position for each position. Live attackers then approach from each position and the GK must use their positioning and decision-making to deal with the shot or dribble.
instructions: Mark 6 positions around the penalty area: central 12m, central 18m, left angle 14m, right angle 14m, left angle 20m, right angle 20m.

PHASE 1 (6 minutes): coach points to each position in turn. GK walks to the correct set position for that angle and distance. Coach checks whether the position is correct (on the bisector of the angle, at the correct depth off the line).

THE BISECTOR PRINCIPLE: the GK should always be on the line that bisects (cuts in half) the angle between themselves and the two posts. This means if an attacker is directly central, the GK stands centrally. If the attacker is wide left, the GK positions slightly to the left of centre.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): attackers dribble from outside the area and approach each position. GK must hold their set position until the moment of the shot (do not dive before the attacker shoots).

PHASE 3 (8 minutes): attackers can shoot or dribble. GK must read the body shape to decide whether to hold position (if attacker is going to shoot) or come off the line (if attacker is going to dribble).
coachingPoints: THE BISECTOR IS EVERYTHING: the most important technical concept in GK positioning is the bisector. A GK who stands on the bisector for every angle gives themselves equal distance to cover each post. Off the bisector, one post is unprotected.

DEPTH OFF THE LINE: how far off the line the GK stands depends on the angle. For central positions close to goal, stay closer to the line. For wide positions or from longer distances, come further off the line to reduce the shooting angle.
SET POSITION BEFORE THE SHOT: the GK must be completely still in their set position at the moment the shot is struck. A moving GK cannot react as quickly as a set GK.
THE BOUNCE OR READY POSITION: a small two-foot bounce between movements resets the weight onto the balls of the feet and dramatically improves reaction time in any direction.

READ THE BODY SHAPE OF THE ATTACKER: before the shot is struck, the attackers non-kicking foot direction, their body lean, and their eye direction all give clues about where the shot is going. Experienced GKs read these cues unconsciously.
HOLD UNTIL THE LAST MOMENT: the most common GK error in 1v1 situations is diving before the attacker has committed to shooting. Hold the set position and force the attacker to beat you.

INFLUENCE THE ATTACKER THROUGH POSITIONING: a GK who is positioned correctly on the bisector at the right depth has already influenced the attacker. The attacker sees a large GK covering the angle and must attempt a difficult finish.
NARROWING THE ANGLE INCREASES PRESSURE: every step forward the GK takes toward the ball reduces the visible goal for the attacker. Use this tool deliberately.
commonMistakes: Positioning off the bisector, leaving one post consistently more exposed than the other
Standing too close to the goal line, giving attackers too much visible goal to aim at
Diving before the shot is struck (anticipating rather than reacting), making the GK easy to dummy
Not setting the ready position before shots, being caught flat-footed
Not reading the attackers body shape, relying purely on reaction rather than anticipation
corrections: Off bisector: use string from each post meeting at the ball position to physically show the bisector. GK must stand where the string crosses
Too close to line: place a cone showing the minimum depth the GK should stand at for each position
Diving too early: introduce a delay rule — GK cannot move until the ball has left the attackers foot
Not setting position: require the bounce between every movement before the shot is allowed
Not reading body shape: slow attackers to 50 percent and ask the GK to call the shot direction before it is struck, based on reading body cues
progressions: Attackers use feints and dummy shots to test the GKs composure at holding the set position
GK must call the shot direction (LEFT, RIGHT, or CENTRE) before the ball arrives, specifically training the anticipation
Penalty shootout format: 5 penalties using the angle play principles. GK earns a point for every penalty saved using correct positioning rather than guessing
regressions: Reduce to central positions only until the bisector concept and depth off the line are established
Use very slow attacker approaches to give the GK maximum time to find and hold the correct position
Walk through the bisector calculation for each of the 6 positions before any live shots are attempted
matchApplication: Advanced 1v1 GK positioning and angle play is what separates good goalkeepers from great ones. The ability to calculate the correct bisector position for any angle and depth, hold it until the last possible moment, and read the attackers body shape to improve anticipation are skills that develop over years of deliberate practice. Goalkeepers who master angle play make difficult saves look routine because they have already removed the easy options before the shot is struck.
coachTips: The bisector concept needs to be demonstrated with string or cones before any live practice — it is mathematical and precise, not approximate.
This session is GK-specific and intensive. Ensure adequate physical and mental recovery time is built into the session.
Film from behind the goal to show the GK exactly what their positioning looks like from the attackers perspective.
Connect to professional GK examples: discuss how elite GKs like Alisson, Ederson, and De Gea use angle play rather than pure reaction.
This drill rewards regular revisiting. Angle play mastery develops gradually across many sessions.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area. 6 shooting positions marked with numbered cones: 1 (central 12m), 2 (central 18m), 3 (left angle 14m), 4 (right angle 14m), 5 (left angle 20m), 6 (right angle 20m). For each position, the correct GK set position is shown with a cross on the bisector line. Bisector shown as a dashed line from the ball position bisecting the angle between the two posts. Depth indicator shown as a circle around the GK position. HOLD vs COME decision arrow shown at the 3m mark.

title: Organising the Defence — Communication Under Pressure
objective: Develop the GKs habit and technique of communicating constantly and clearly with their defensive unit during live play.
learningOutcomes: GK can give clear, timely instructions to defenders during set pieces and open play
GK develops the vocal authority and confidence to direct older or physically larger teammates
GK builds the specific vocabulary of GK communication (keeper, away, hold, push up, man on)
GK learns when to speak and when silence is also useful
pitchSize: Full penalty area and defensive third
organisation: Full defensive third with goal. 4-5 defenders work with the GK against 4-5 attackers. The primary coaching focus is on the GK — every instruction they give (or fail to give) is evaluated and coached specifically.
instructions: Set up a small-sided game in the defensive third with 4-5 defenders and 4-5 attackers.
The GK starts each sequence from their normal position with attackers pressing in the final third.

VOCABULARY TO BUILD: KEEPER (I am coming for this ball, everyone clear), AWAY (I am not coming, defend and clear), HOLD (defenders hold their line, do not step out), PUSH UP (defensive line move forward), MAN ON (a defender being approached from behind), TIME (a defender has time and space, no immediate pressure).
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): coach pauses play after every GK communication (or lack of it) and evaluates: was the call correct? Was it loud enough? Was it early enough?
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): continuous play with no pauses. Coach notes every missed communication opportunity and reviews after the phase.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): set pieces only — corners and free kicks. GK must organise the defensive shape, call the wall position, and communicate throughout the delivery.
coachingPoints: EVERY BALL IN THE AIR NEEDS A CALL: if a ball is coming into the penalty area, the GK must call KEEPER or AWAY before it arrives. Not after it arrives. Before. Calling after the delivery causes collisions and confusion.
VOLUME MUST CARRY OVER MATCH NOISE: in a training session, a quiet call is sufficient. In a match, it must carry over crowd noise, player noise, and wind. Practice at match volume every time.
EARLY IS ALWAYS BETTER THAN LATE: a call that comes 2 seconds before the ball arrives is enormously more useful than a call that comes 0.5 seconds before. Develop the habit of calling early.
ONE CALL PER SITUATION, REPEATED: do not call ten different things at once. Choose the most important instruction and repeat it twice clearly. KEEPER, KEEPER. HOLD, HOLD.
AUTHORITY AND CONFIDENCE: the GK communicates as the leader of the defensive unit. Their voice must carry authority, not uncertainty. Even when unsure, the call must be confident.
MAN ON IS CRITICAL: telling a ball-carrying defender that an opponent is approaching from behind (MAN ON) prevents turnovers that the defender cannot see coming. This call saves goals.

TIME IS UNDERUSED: telling a defender they have time and space (TIME) allows them to take an extra touch, look up, and play more effectively. Most GKs only call to warn of danger, not to give confidence.
BUILD RELATIONSHIPS WITH DEFENDERS: the GK-defender communication relationship develops over time. Defenders learn to trust specific GK calls. Be consistent in what your calls mean.
AFTER THE BALL IS CLEARED: after a clearance, call AWAY and then immediately reorganise the defensive shape verbally before the next attack begins.
commonMistakes: Calling too late after the ball has already arrived in the dangerous area
Calling too quietly to be heard in a match environment
Calling KEEPER and then hesitating rather than coming, destroying defender trust in the call
Not calling MAN ON when a defender is clearly about to be pressed from behind
No call at all for most situations, leaving defenders uncertain throughout
corrections: Too late: require the GK to call as soon as the ball enters the penalty area, not when it is already at head height — practice with the coach pointing to when the call should have been made
Too quiet: require every call to be audible from the halfway line — check from distance during phase 1
Calling KEEPER then hesitating: if the GK calls KEEPER they must come. No option to stay. Build the commitment into the call from the first session
No MAN ON call: assign a specific coach or assistant to watch the GK and call out every missed MAN ON opportunity during phase 2
Complete silence: count the number of calls the GK makes per 5-minute phase and set an increasing target across sessions
progressions: Add crowd noise (music at high volume) during phase 3 to test whether communication quality holds under noise
Two GKs competing: two GKs rotate after each sequence and are scored on communication quality by the coach and defenders
Full match with the GK wearing a distinctive bib so all communication can be tracked and evaluated post-session
regressions: Reduce to one specific call (KEEPER or AWAY) for the first full session before building the full vocabulary
Use a static defensive shape (defenders in fixed positions) so the GK can focus purely on communication without the added complexity of a moving defence
Start with set pieces only before adding the complexity of open play communication
matchApplication: The GK is the only player who can see the entire game in front of them throughout a match, which makes them potentially the most valuable communicator on the pitch. A GK who communicates constantly, clearly, and authoritatively organises their defensive unit, prevents defensive errors, and saves goals before they are even attempted. Research consistently shows that well-organised defensive units concede fewer goals not primarily because of individual defensive quality but because of the collective organisation that clear communication enables. This drill builds the habits and confidence that make the GK an active defensive leader rather than a passive last line.
coachTips: Model the communication vocabulary explicitly and enthusiastically before the drill begins — players learn the calls faster if they hear them correctly first.
The GKs voice is a coachable skill. Work specifically on volume, timing, and authority across multiple sessions.
Praise early, loud, correct calls immediately and specifically. This is the most important habit to build.
Ask defenders to evaluate the GK communication at the end of each phase — their perspective on which calls were most useful is valuable information.
This drill pairs naturally with the defending corners and defending set pieces drills from the Defending category.
diagramDescription: Full defensive third with goal. GK shown at various positions with communication call labels: KEEPER (arms raised, coming for a cross), AWAY (pointing at a defender, directing them to clear), HOLD (arms out flat, directing defensive line to maintain position), PUSH UP (arms sweeping forward, directing line to step out), MAN ON (pointing at an approaching attacker behind a defender). Volume indicator shown as a sound wave extending to the halfway line. Timing indicator showing early call vs late call comparison.

title: Goalkeeper Communication in Set Pieces
objective: Develop the complete set-piece communication system including wall organisation, corner defending coordination, and post-delivery organisation.
learningOutcomes: GK can organise a defensive wall quickly, accurately, and with authority
GK coordinates the full defensive unit for corner kicks using clear pre-delivery and during-delivery calls
GK develops the post-delivery communication that maintains defensive organisation after the first ball is dealt with
GK builds the leadership confidence to direct experienced senior players
players: GK plus full defensive unit (8-10 players total)
pitchSize: Full pitch (penalty area and approach zones)
organisation: Full set-piece practice session using the GK as the primary organiser. Three set-piece scenarios covered in sequence: direct free kicks, indirect free kicks, and corner kicks. GK leads the organisation of each scenario.
instructions: SCENARIO 1 — DIRECT FREE KICK (8 minutes):
Free kick awarded 20m from goal, slightly left of centre.
GK immediately sprints to the near post, measures the wall angle (wall must cover the near post from GK perspective).
GK calls the number of players needed in the wall: THREE WALL, TWO WALL etc.
GK directs the wall LEFT or RIGHT until the near post is covered.
GK calls WALL SET when satisfied, then moves to cover the far post and central area.
GK must be in position before the opposition approaches the ball.
SCENARIO 2 — CORNER KICK (10 minutes):
GK positions centrally between posts, 2 steps off the line.
Before delivery: GK calls each defenders name and their zone responsibility.
During delivery: KEEPER (coming) or AWAY (not coming). Called when the ball is struck.
After the clearance: AWAY AWAY, then organise quickly before the second phase.
SCENARIO 3 — INDIRECT FREE KICK (7 minutes):
GK organises wall as normal but adds the instruction CHARGE ON MY CALL.
At the moment the first touch is played, GK calls CHARGE and the wall sprints forward.
coachingPoints: WALL DIRECTION FROM THE GK PERSPECTIVE: the wall must cover the near post as the GK sees it, not as the wall players see it. The GK moves the wall by calling LEFT or RIGHT from their own perspective — this is the opposite of the wall players left and right.
WALL DISTANCE: 9.15m from the ball. The GK must be aware of this rule and ensure the wall does not creep forward before the kick, which earns the opposition a retake.
SPEED OF ORGANISATION: in a match, the opposition will try to take free kicks quickly before the wall is set. The GK must direct the wall in under 8 seconds.

CORNER PRE-DELIVERY ORGANISATION: every defender should know their responsibility before the corner is taken. The GK should call each defenders name and their zone or marker 15 seconds before the delivery.

CORNER CALL TIMING: KEEPER or AWAY must be called as the ball is struck, not after it has already been delivered. Pre-delivery calls confuse defenders because the flight of the ball has not yet been seen.
AUTHORITY UNDER PRESSURE: set pieces in a match happen quickly and under crowd and opposition pressure. The GK must maintain their authority and clarity in this environment.
POST-DELIVERY ORGANISATION: after every set piece delivery (cleared or claimed), the GK must immediately call AWAY and begin organising for the second phase before the opposition has recovered.
CHARGE CALL TIMING (INDIRECT): the CHARGE must be called at the exact moment the first player touches the ball. Too early and the charge is illegal. Too late and the shot is already on its way.
commonMistakes: Wall organised too slowly, giving the opposition time to take a quick free kick before the wall is set
Wall direction calls from the GKs perspective being confused (calling LEFT when they mean the walls left)
No pre-corner organisation, leaving defenders uncertain about their responsibilities
KEEPER or AWAY call coming after the ball is already in the flight, making it useless to defenders
CHARGE call too late on indirect free kicks, allowing the shot to be taken before the wall moves
corrections: Wall too slow: time from the whistle to WALL SET called by the GK. Must be under 8 seconds. Track improvement across sessions
Direction confusion: use coloured cones (yellow left post, red right post) during practice to make the GKs directional reference concrete
No pre-corner organisation: require the GK to specifically name each defender and their responsibility in a pre-delivery call before any corner is taken
Late KEEPER/AWAY: in Phase 2 the coach calls BALL STRUCK as a cue training the GK to time the call to ball contact rather than ball flight
Late CHARGE: use a ball drop from the coach at the exact moment a player touches it. GK must call CHARGE at the drop, training the timing precisely
progressions: Add an opposition player allowed to take a quick free kick if the wall is not set within 8 seconds — creates urgency
Full 11v11 set-piece session where all set pieces (corners, free kicks, goal kicks) are covered in a match format with the GK leading all organisation
Introduce a second free kick taker who dummies the kick before the real taker shoots, testing whether the wall and GK maintain composure
regressions: Use a passive opposition (no movement, no shooting) while the GK practices the organisation sequence alone for each scenario
Allow extended time for each organisation phase (15 seconds) before reducing to match pace (8 seconds)
Practice each scenario separately for a full session before combining all three in the same session
matchApplication: Set-piece organisation is one of the most specifically learnable and impactful areas of GK performance. Research shows that set pieces account for approximately 30 percent of all goals scored in professional football, and the quality of the defensive organisation — particularly the GKs communication — is the biggest factor in whether set pieces are defended successfully. A GK who can quickly and confidently organise a wall, direct a corner defensive unit, and communicate throughout the delivery is protecting their team at exactly the moments of greatest danger.
coachTips: Allow extended time for the GK to practice each set-piece scenario in Phase 1 without any time pressure before introducing urgency.
The wall direction perspective issue (GKs left vs walls left) needs to be specifically addressed and practiced before live set pieces begin.

The authority coaching point is subtle but important: a GK who sounds uncertain in their communication creates uncertainty in their defenders. Practice calling with confidence even when the GK is still learning the system.
Record and review set-piece communication from a match — the gap between what the GK thinks they are communicating and what defenders are actually hearing is often significant.
This drill is most effective when run with the same defensive unit that plays together in matches — the communication relationship is specific to those players.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. SCENARIO 1 (Free kick): free kick position shown 20m from goal. GK shown at near post directing wall. Wall of 3 defenders shown with direction adjustment arrows (LEFT from GK perspective). GK final position shown covering far post and centre. 8-second timer shown. SCENARIO 2 (Corner): corner flag shown. GK shown centrally 2 steps off line. Pre-delivery call shown with defender names and zones. KEEPER call shown at moment of delivery. SCENARIO 3 (Indirect): CHARGE call shown at moment of first touch with wall sprint arrows forward.

title: GK Positioning for Through Balls
objective: Develop the GK positioning and decision-making required when a through ball is played behind the defensive line.
learningOutcomes: GK can judge whether to come for a through ball or hold the line
GK develops the sprint technique for covering through balls at pace
GK can deal with a through ball at different distances using correct technique
GK builds the defensive line awareness that allows correct through ball positioning
players: GK plus a coach or server plus 2-3 attackers
organisation: Full pitch defensive third. Servers play through balls at different distances and angles behind a simulated defensive line. GK must read the through ball and decide whether to come or hold based on whether they can win the ball cleanly.
instructions: Mark a defensive line with cones 15m from goal (representing the back 4).
GK positions 3-5m off the goal line, in a position where they can see over the defensive line.
Server plays through balls of different lengths: short (just behind the defensive line, 12m from goal), medium (20m from goal), long (25m+ from goal).
SHORT THROUGH BALL (12m from goal): GK can come. Sprint at maximum pace to reach the ball before an attacker running from the defensive line.
MEDIUM THROUGH BALL (20m from goal): it depends. GK must read the ball speed and attacker distance before deciding.
LONG THROUGH BALL (25m+): GK comes only if they are confident of reaching the ball comfortably. If in doubt, hold the line and organise defenders.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): no attacker. GK reads through balls and makes the come or hold decision at different distances. Coach confirms whether each decision was correct.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): attacker added, running from the defensive line simultaneously with the through ball. GK must win the race or hold if the attacker will get there first.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): GK makes all decisions independently. Coach evaluates but does not intervene.
coachingPoints: HIGH STARTING POSITION IS ESSENTIAL: a GK who starts on or very close to the goal line cannot intercept any through ball. The high starting position (3-5m off the line) is what makes through ball interceptions possible.

READ THE BALL FLIGHT EARLY: the moment a through ball is played, the GK must read its direction and speed instantly. Waiting to see where the ball is going by the time it reaches the defensive line is too late.
SPRINT AT FULL PACE IMMEDIATELY: there is no room for a tentative first step on a through ball. The first movement must be a full sprint in the correct direction.

WIN THE BALL CLEANLY OR DO NOT COME: if the GK comes for a through ball and cannot win it cleanly, they have committed to a position where they can easily be chipped. Only come if confident of a clean win.
USE THE HANDS WHEN POSSIBLE: if coming for a through ball and reaching it before the attacker, collect with the hands rather than the feet for the safest outcome.
FEET WHEN HANDS ARE NOT POSSIBLE: if the through ball is too flat for a clean hand collection, use the feet to play the ball back to a defender or into touch. Never play backwards toward the goal.
COMMUNICATION WHEN HOLDING: when the GK decides to hold the line and not come, they must immediately communicate to the defender who needs to track the attacker: NAME, TRACK or GOAL SIDE.

THE HIGH LINE REQUIRES EXCELLENT POSITIONING: teams that defend with a high line expose their GK to more through balls. A GK with a team defending high must be especially alert and in a high starting position.
commonMistakes: Starting too close to the goal line, making through ball interceptions impossible
Not reading the through ball early enough, hesitating before the first step and losing the race
Coming for a through ball that the attacker will clearly reach first
Using the feet on a through ball that could be collected cleanly with the hands
No communication to defenders when holding the line on a through ball the GK cannot reach
corrections: Too close to goal line: place a cone at the minimum starting position (3m off the line). GK must always be beyond this cone
Not reading early: in phase 1, the coach delays the through ball by 1 second from when they would naturally play it, giving the GK an artificial window to read the intention before it is played
Coming when attacker wins: introduce a colour code — server plays ball with a specific colour flag indicating whether GK should come (green) or hold (red)
Feet when hands possible: in phase 1 only, require hands collection on every through ball that the GK reaches comfortably
No communication when holding: make a TRACK call mandatory when the GK holds — no hold decision counts unless the GK also calls the defenders name
progressions: Moving defensive line to 20m from goal, exposing more space behind for through balls to exploit
Attacker starts 5m ahead of the defensive line, reducing the GKs window significantly and increasing the difficulty of the decision
Full match with the defensive team playing a high line, creating a realistic through ball frequency
regressions: Use no attacker for the full session and focus purely on the GK reading and collecting through balls at different distances
Slow all through balls by 50 percent to give the GK more time to read and make the decision
Start with only short through balls (12m) where the decision is always COME before introducing medium and long balls
matchApplication: Through ball awareness and interception is one of the defining qualities of a modern GK operating behind a high defensive line. GKs like Alisson, Ederson, and Manuel Neuer are famous for their ability to read through balls early and intercept them before attackers can reach them, turning a potential goal-scoring opportunity into a GK collection. The high starting position, the early reading, and the decisive sprint are the three technical elements that make this possible, and all three are directly trainable through this drill.
coachTips: The high starting position is the most important habit to establish first — a GK who starts too close to the line simply cannot execute the other elements regardless of how good their decision-making is.
Stand level with the defensive line during the drill to evaluate the GKs starting position relative to it from the attackers perspective.
The decision-making framework (short: come, long: hold, medium: depends) gives GKs a usable heuristic that covers most real match situations.
Film the drill from the side to show the GK their starting position and sprint path in relation to the ball flight.
Connect to professional examples: discuss specific through ball interceptions by elite GKs and identify the key decisions they made.
diagramDescription: Full pitch defensive third. Simulated defensive line shown 15m from goal with cones. GK shown in high starting position 3-5m off goal line (above the defensive line marker). Three through ball trajectories shown: short (12m from goal, COME label), medium (20m, DECISION label), long (25m+, HOLD label). GK sprint arrow shown going toward the short through ball. Attacker running arrow shown from the defensive line. Communication arrow shown from GK to a defender when holding: TRACK call.

title: GK Positioning — Narrowing Angles and Depth
objective: Teach the basics of GK positioning: where to stand for different shooting angles, why coming off the line reduces the goal the attacker can see, and how to use the body to fill the goal.
learningOutcomes: GK understands why position off the goal line is more effective than standing on it
GK can identify the correct position for central shots and wide-angle shots
GK develops the habit of adjusting position as the ball moves
GK builds the foundational positioning knowledge that all other GK skills build on
players: 1-2 GKs plus a coach and 3-4 servers
organisation: Full-size goal (or junior goal for younger players). Coach uses string from each post to show the shooting angle for different ball positions. GK physically sees how the angle reduces as they come off the line, making the correct position instantly visible.
instructions: SETUP: the coach holds two pieces of string from each goal post, meeting at the ball position. This creates a V shape showing exactly how much goal the attacker can see from that position.

DEMONSTRATION 1 (3 minutes): ball placed 15m from goal, centrally. GK stands on the goal line. String shows the full V angle. GK then steps forward 3m. String now shows a narrower V. GK steps forward 3 more metres. String shows an even narrower V. Players can see physically why position off the line is better.

DEMONSTRATION 2 (3 minutes): ball placed at a wide angle (left side, 12m from goal). String shows a wide angle. GK steps toward the ball and adjusts to the bisector. String shows the angle is now covered more evenly.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): coach places ball at 5 different positions. GK moves to what they think is the correct position for each one. Coach evaluates using the string.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): servers move around the area with the ball. GK must adjust position constantly to stay on the correct angle for wherever the ball is.
coachingPoints: COME OFF THE LINE: the goal line is where the GK starts, not where they defend. Coming off the line 3-5m for central shots reduces the visible goal significantly.
THE BISECTOR IS YOUR FRIEND: always stand on the line that cuts the angle in half (the bisector). This means you are covering both sides of the goal equally.
MOVE AS THE BALL MOVES: whenever the ball moves, the GK must move. A GK who stands still while the ball moves leaves one side of the goal exposed.
FOR WIDE ANGLES: come slightly toward the near post when the ball is wide because there is no far post angle. The attacker must shoot across goal which means the near post is most at risk.
SMALL STEPS, CONSTANT ADJUSTMENT: good GK positioning is not one big move and then standing still. It is constant small adjustments as the ball moves.
LOOK LIKE YOU FILL THE GOAL: when positioned correctly and the body is upright and slightly forward-leaning, a well-positioned GK fills a significant proportion of the visible goal area.
NEVER STAND FLAT-FOOTED: always on the balls of the feet. Flat-footed GKs cannot move quickly from a good position.
THE STRING TEST: any time you are unsure of your position, ask yourself: if I drew lines from both posts to where the ball is, am I standing where those lines meet or very close to it?
commonMistakes: Standing on or very close to the goal line for all shots regardless of position
Not adjusting when the ball moves, staying in one position while the attacker changes angle
Positioning to the left or right of the bisector without realising, exposing one post
Not coming far enough off the line for close central positions
Moving in one direction when the ball is wide, not following the bisector to the correct position
corrections: On goal line: string demonstration makes this instantly visible — GK can see their own exposed goal area from the string angle
Not adjusting: place a ball on a cone and ask the GK to call when they see it move, training the constant awareness
Off bisector: use the string from both posts as a permanent reference tool throughout phase 1
Not coming far enough: place a minimum depth cone 3m off the line for central positions. GK must reach this cone
Wrong direction for wide ball: use the string to show that for a wide ball, the near post is more exposed and the GK should lean toward it
progressions: Moving target: the server dribbles continuously around the area while the GK tracks and adjusts position constantly
Add a shot: after the string positioning work, the server shoots from where they are standing and the GK tests whether their position was correct
Full game with positioning as the only coaching focus: evaluate and discuss the GKs position after every shot attempt in the game
regressions: Start with the string demonstration only (no live practice) until the concept of angle reduction is clearly understood
Use a junior goal if available to make the positioning angles more manageable
Reduce to just one type of position (central only) before adding wide angle positions
matchApplication: GK positioning is the foundation upon which every other GK skill is built. A technically brilliant GK in the wrong position makes harder saves than an average GK in the correct position. The string angle demonstration in this drill makes an abstract concept instantly concrete — players can see with their own eyes how their position off the line and on the bisector changes how much goal the attacker can see. This foundational understanding, established at U11, supports every other positioning concept the GK will be taught throughout their development.
coachTips: The string demonstration is the most valuable 3 minutes in this entire drill. Do not rush it.
Give the GK players opportunities to hold the string themselves and to adjust their own position — physical involvement reinforces the learning.
Film the string demonstration from behind the goal to show the GK exactly what the attacker sees from different GK positions.
Use positive, encouraging language throughout — GK positioning can feel overwhelming at first and confidence is essential.
Connect to their favourite professional GK and discuss specifically where that GK stands for different types of shots.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal. String shown from each post meeting at a central ball position 15m from goal, creating a V angle. GK shown in 3 positions: on the goal line (full V shown), 3m off (narrower V), 6m off (narrowest V). Bisector line shown from the ball through the V to where the GK should stand. Wide ball position shown with string: near post more exposed, bisector shown shifting toward near post. GK shown at correct position for each scenario.

title: Shot Stopping — Low and High Diving Saves
objective: Develop the diving technique for both low and high saves, building the correct body mechanics, landing position, and recovery.
learningOutcomes: GK can execute a correct low dive with a safe landing position
GK can execute a high dive using the correct take-off and extension technique
GK develops the recovery movement after both dive types
GK builds the confidence and physical courage for committed diving saves
organisation: Full-size goal. Server stands at 12m from goal and fires shots to different zones: bottom corners (low dive), top corners (high dive). GK practices each dive type in structured phases before combining both in a live shooting sequence.
instructions: PHASE 1 - LOW DIVE TECHNIQUE (6 minutes):
Server fires low shots to the bottom left and right corners at medium pace.
LOW DIVE: push off the near foot (closest to the shot direction). Body dives parallel to the ground. Lead with the bottom hand (closest to the ground) — both hands behind the ball.
Contact the ball with both hands. Pull the ball into the chest immediately after the catch.
Landing: land on the hip and lower body, never the elbow or wrist. Roll slightly to absorb the impact.
After landing: get up immediately and reset to the set position.
PHASE 2 - HIGH DIVE TECHNIQUE (7 minutes):
Server fires shots to the top corners at varying pace.
HIGH DIVE: take off from the near foot. Drive the opposite knee upward for elevation. Extend both arms toward the ball at full stretch.
Contact: fingertips or full palms depending on the height. For saves above the bar: tip over using one hand.
Landing: land on the shoulder and upper arm, not flat on the back.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): server fires shots alternating low and high without pattern. GK must read the shot and apply the correct technique.
coachingPoints: LOW DIVE — LEAD WITH THE BOTTOM HAND: the hand closest to the ground must lead the dive. If the top hand leads, the ball will go under it. Think of placing the bottom hand behind the ball first.
HIGH DIVE — TAKE OFF FROM ONE FOOT: taking off from two feet is much slower than from one. Push explosively from the near foot and use the knee drive for elevation.
DO NOT CROSS THE FEET: during the take-off for a dive, the feet must not cross. Cross feet create a stumble. Push off the near foot and keep the feet parallel during the drive.
SAFE LANDING POSITION: every dive must end in a safe landing position. Low: hip and side. High: shoulder and upper arm. Never: elbow, wrist, or flat on the back.
BOTH HANDS BEHIND THE BALL: wherever possible, both hands should be positioned behind the ball at the moment of contact. One hand is a reflex save. Two hands is a controlled save.
RECOVERY SPEED: the first second after any dive must be spent getting back to the feet. A GK who stays on the ground after a save cannot deal with a rebound.
COMMIT FULLY: a diving save attempted at 80 percent commitment will fail. Full commitment is required. Once the decision to dive is made, execute it completely.

THE TIP OVER: for shots heading just over the bar, the correct technique is a one-handed tip over the bar rather than a full dive. This keeps the ball in play over the bar rather than back into the danger area.
commonMistakes: Leading with the top hand on a low dive, allowing the ball to go under the bottom hand
Taking off from two feet on a high dive, significantly reducing elevation and speed
Landing on the elbow or wrist rather than the hip or shoulder, creating injury risk
Not recovering to the set position after the save, remaining on the ground
Committing to a dive direction too early, making dummy saves from the attacker easy
corrections: Top hand leading on low dive: tape the bottom hand to mark it as the leading hand. Bottom hand must make contact first on every low dive
Two-foot take-off: practice the one-foot explosive take-off against a stationary ball 10 times before any live shots
Dangerous landing: use GK mats to practice the landing technique in isolation before any diving attempts at full pace
Not recovering: time the recovery after each save. GK must be upright within 2 seconds of completing the save
Committing too early: require the GK to stay in the set position until the ball has left the server foot before diving — practice the hold
progressions: Increase shot pace to create more urgency in the diving decision and technique
Add a rebound: after the GK makes a save and is on the ground, the server immediately fires a second shot requiring immediate recovery
Multiple angles: server moves to different positions around the penalty area and fires from each, requiring the GK to adjust position and dive technique for different angles
regressions: Start with the coach holding the ball and placing it gently into the corner while the GK practices the low dive motion against zero resistance
Use soft training balls at reduced pace in Phase 1 and 2 to build confidence in the diving motion
Allow the GK to take up to 3 seconds to set before the server fires, giving more time to prepare the correct dive preparation
matchApplication: Diving saves are the most visually dramatic element of goalkeeping but also one of the most technically demanding. The specific mechanics of the dive — which foot to push off, which hand leads, how to land safely — are learnable and improvable with deliberate practice. GKs who dive incorrectly (wrong foot take-off, wrong landing position) both save fewer shots and risk injury. The correct technique, drilled into automatic responses through high volumes of repetition, makes every dive more effective and completely safe.
coachTips: Safety is the highest priority in this drill. Ensure safe landing technique is established before any pace or commitment is added.
GK mats are very useful if available for the early phases of learning the landing position.
Celebrate committed dives even when the ball is not saved — the technical commitment is the achievement to build first.
Film dives from the side to show the GK their own take-off foot, hand position, and landing position.
This drill works best in dedicated GK training sessions rather than full team sessions, as it requires specific individual feedback that cannot be given effectively in a group context.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal. GK shown in set position centrally. LOW DIVE sequence: push-off arrow from near foot, body parallel to ground, bottom hand leading, both hands behind ball in bottom corner, hip landing shown. HIGH DIVE sequence: one-foot take-off arrow, knee drive upward, both arms fully extended to top corner, shoulder landing shown. RECOVERY arrow shown from landing position back to feet. Server shown at 12m with shot arrows to all 4 corners. Phase 3 shown with alternating shot arrows to low and high corners.

title: GK Kicking — Goal Kicks and Long Distribution
objective: Develop the goal kick technique and long kicking distribution that allows the GK to start attacks from dead ball situations.
learningOutcomes: GK can execute a driven goal kick that carries accurately to a target player
GK understands the tactical options available from a goal kick
GK develops the technique for kicking off the ground with accuracy and distance
GK builds the confidence to use kicking as a primary distribution method alongside throwing
organisation: Full pitch. GK takes goal kicks from the 6-yard box. Target players positioned at different distances and positions: short (edge of penalty area), medium (midfield), long (opposition half). GK practices the correct technique and decision-making for each distance.
instructions: TARGET ZONES set up across the pitch: Zone A (edge of penalty area, 25m), Zone B (central midfield, 40m), Zone C (wide midfield, 40m), Zone D (opposition half, 55m+).

GOAL KICK TECHNIQUE: non-kicking foot placed 10-15cm behind and to the side of the ball. Approach from 4-5 steps at a slight angle. Strike through the bottom centre of the ball with the laces. Head down, lean slightly forward, follow through toward the target.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): stationary targets. GK takes 3 kicks to each zone and receives feedback on accuracy and technique.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): moving targets. Players in each zone jog slowly. GK must lead the pass to where they will be.

PHASE 3 (7 minutes): game scenario. 4 defenders press from the opposition. GK reads the press and chooses which zone to kick to. If the press covers Zone B and C, kick to Zone D (over the press). If Zone D is covered by a high defensive line, kick to Zone A (short).
coachingPoints: TECHNIQUE FOR A DRIVEN GOAL KICK: this is not a punt — the ball is struck from the ground with the laces. The non-kicking foot position is the most important technical factor: too close and the kick goes up steeply, too far away and the kick goes flat and low.
HEAD DOWN THROUGHOUT: the head must stay down through the entire kicking motion. Lifting the head to watch the target is the most common cause of a mishit goal kick.
LEAN FORWARD NOT BACK: leaning backward when striking the ball causes it to go up steeply and fail to reach the intended distance. Lean slightly forward and drive through.
ACCURACY BEFORE DISTANCE: a goal kick that reaches Zone C accurately is more valuable than one that reaches Zone D but arrives in the wrong area. Build accuracy first, distance second.
READ THE PRESS BEFORE THE KICK: the 4 seconds while the ball is being placed gives the GK time to look up and read where the opposition press is. Kick to where the press is not.
SHORT OPTION IS OFTEN THE BEST: a short, accurate kick to Zone A that starts a passing sequence is often more valuable than a 50m kick that creates a 50-50 aerial contest.
DISGUISE THE DIRECTION: look at one target zone and kick to another. Elite GKs disguise their kick direction by looking at the Zone B player before kicking to Zone C.
PRACTICE BOTH FEET: goal kicks can be taken with either foot. A GK who can kick accurately with both feet has an additional tactical option that forces the opposition to cover both sides.
commonMistakes: Non-kicking foot too close to the ball, causing the kick to rise steeply and not reach the target
Head lifting before contact, causing a mishit that goes low or to the wrong side
Leaning backward and sending the kick high and short of the target
Reading the press too late (after the kick rather than before), making the wrong kick selection
Always kicking to the same zone regardless of the opposition press, becoming completely predictable
corrections: Foot too close: draw a foot marker on the ground at the correct 10-15cm beside and behind position. GK must land their non-kicking foot on the marker
Head lifting: place a sticker on the ball at the contact point. GK must still be able to describe the sticker position after the kick (forces head down through contact)
Leaning backward: place a forward-lean cone 15cm in front of the ball. GK must lean over the cone at contact
Late press reading: require the GK to verbally call which zone they are kicking to before they begin the approach
Always same zone: require a different zone from the previous kick on every rep until all zones have been used
progressions: Add a competitive element: the GK earns a point for every kick that reaches the correct target zone and a bonus point for kicks that are playable (at a comfortable height and pace for the receiver)
Two-touch rule for receivers: if the receiver needs more than 2 touches to control the kick, the kick was not good enough quality
Full match with the GK using only kicking (no throwing) for all distribution during a 10-minute period
regressions: Start with Zone A only (short goal kicks to the edge of the penalty area) before adding longer distances
Allow the GK to place and kick from a stationary approach rather than a full run-up
Use a smaller target area for Zone B and C so any kick reaching those areas counts, reducing the accuracy pressure
matchApplication: Accurate goal kick distribution is one of the most significant factors in a teams ability to maintain possession from dead ball situations. GKs who can play accurately to different zones, read the opposition press, and disguise their kick direction give their team a significant advantage in the 10-15 goal kicks they take per match. This is a genuinely coachable technical skill that improves dramatically with deliberate practice and proper technique — many GKs who struggle with goal kicks are simply using incorrect technique that can be corrected in a few sessions.
coachTips: The technique coaching is the priority in Phase 1. Be very specific about non-kicking foot position and head position — these two points correct most goal kick problems.
Mark the non-kicking foot position on the ground in Phase 1 so the GK gets immediate visual feedback on every kick.
The tactical coaching (reading the press) in Phase 3 is sophisticated. Walk through the press scenarios explicitly before introducing live opposition.
Film goal kicks from the side to show the GK their own non-kicking foot position, lean angle, and head position throughout the kick.
Connect to professional GKs who are specifically known for their distribution (Alisson, Ederson) and discuss how their kicking distribution directly benefits their teams attacking play.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. GK shown in 6-yard box with ball on the ground. Non-kicking foot marker shown 10-15cm beside and behind ball. Approach angle shown (4-5 steps at slight angle). Contact diagram showing laces through bottom centre of ball with forward lean indicated. Four target zones shown across the pitch: Zone A (edge of penalty area, 25m), Zone B (central midfield, 40m), Zone C (wide midfield, 40m), Zone D (opposition half, 55m+). Target players shown in each zone. Press scenario shown in Phase 3 with press arrows and kick selection decision.

title: Single Leg Balance and Football Movements
objective: Develop single-leg balance and stability as the foundation for all football movements including kicking, turning, and jumping.
learningOutcomes: Players can balance on one leg for 10 seconds with eyes open and 5 seconds with eyes closed
Players develop the ankle and knee stability that prevents common football injuries
Players connect balance training to specific football movements
Players build the proprioceptive awareness that underpins all athletic movement
equipment: Optional: balance boards or foam pads
pitchSize: Each player in a 2x2m personal space
organisation: Individual stations. Players work through a series of single-leg balance exercises in sequence, increasing difficulty across the session. No equipment needed beyond cones for personal spaces.
instructions: EXERCISE 1 (2 minutes): Stand on right leg. Hold for 10 seconds. Swap to left leg. 10 seconds. Repeat. Eyes open throughout.
EXERCISE 2 (2 minutes): Same but eyes closed. Dramatically harder. 5 seconds per leg is excellent.
EXERCISE 3 (2 minutes): Stand on one leg. Draw circles in the air with the raised knee. Clockwise then anticlockwise. 30 seconds each direction each leg.
EXERCISE 4 (2 minutes): Stand on one leg. Reach the other foot forward, sideways, and behind without touching the ground (the star excursion reach test). 5 reaches in each direction each leg.
EXERCISE 5 (2 minutes): Stand on one leg. Mini-squat down on that leg (single leg squat). Control the descent. Do not let the knee collapse inward. 8 reps each leg.
EXERCISE 6 (3 minutes): with a ball. Balance on one leg and juggle with the other foot. Any touches on the same leg (the standing leg side) count double.
FINAL 2 minutes: free balance challenge. Players create their own balance challenge and demonstrate it to a partner.
coachingPoints: BALANCE IS THE FOUNDATION OF ALL FOOTBALL: every time you kick, turn, jump, or change direction, you are balancing on one leg for a fraction of a second. Players with better single-leg stability are faster, more agile, and less likely to be injured.
THE ANKLE IS THE KEY: most balance control comes from the ankle. Consciously try to feel the ankle working during each exercise. Tiny adjustments in the ankle are what keep you balanced.

EYES CLOSED CHANGES EVERYTHING: with eyes closed, the body must use proprioception (joint position sense) rather than visual reference to balance. This is a more direct training of the nervous system quality that determines balance.
KNEE ALIGNMENT IS CRUCIAL: in the single-leg squat, the knee must track directly over the second toe. A knee that collapses inward (knee valgus) is a primary risk factor for knee injury in football.
SOFT SURFACE INCREASES THE CHALLENGE: if balance boards or foam pads are available, use them in Exercise 1 and 2. Even a folded towel under the foot significantly increases the proprioceptive demand.
ARMS FOR BALANCE: use the arms freely to help maintain balance. Extended arms are not cheating — they are part of natural human balance mechanics.
PROGRESS IS MEASURABLE: timing how long each player can balance with eyes closed gives a concrete measurement of proprioceptive ability. Track improvement across sessions.
BOTH LEGS EQUALLY: most players have a noticeably better balance leg. The weaker balance leg needs more attention and more repetitions.
BREATHE NATURALLY: holding the breath during balance exercises creates tension that actually makes balance worse. Breathe naturally throughout.
commonMistakes: Feet touching the ground repeatedly during single-leg exercises due to insufficient ankle stability
Knee collapsing inward during the single-leg squat, reinforcing a poor movement pattern
Not using the arms to help balance, making the exercises unnecessarily difficult
Only practicing on the dominant leg and ignoring the weaker leg
Rushing through exercises without genuinely challenging the balance system
corrections: Feet touching: reduce the exercise time to a target of 5 seconds with eyes open instead of 10, building gradually
Knee collapsing: place a resistance band just above both knees. The band provides feedback when the knee collapses inward
Not using arms: specifically demonstrate the arm extension and encourage its use as a tool
Only dominant leg: require equal time on each leg and specifically count the weaker leg reps
Rushing: time each balance hold aloud so players know exactly how long they are aiming for
progressions: Add a ball to every exercise: juggle on one leg, receive a throw on one leg, catch and throw on one leg
Reaction balance: player balances on one leg. Coach calls LEFT, RIGHT, FORWARD, BACK. Player reaches in the called direction and returns to balance.
Balance competition: who can hold eyes-closed single-leg balance longest? Track and celebrate improvement across sessions
regressions: Use wall support for Exercise 1 and 2 for players who cannot yet hold single-leg balance without it
Reduce single-leg squat depth to a very shallow mini-squat for players not yet strong enough for the full version
Remove eyes-closed exercises until eyes-open balance is established confidently
matchApplication: Single-leg balance and stability are directly associated with both performance and injury prevention in football. Research consistently shows that players with better proprioceptive ability (the nervous system quality trained by balance exercises) experience significantly fewer ankle and knee injuries, change direction faster, and maintain technical quality better under physical fatigue. These qualities are especially important for young players whose bodies are still developing — balance training at U11 builds the neuromuscular foundations that support athletic performance throughout their career.
coachTips: Balance training is most effective at the beginning of a session when the nervous system is fresh.
The eyes-closed exercises provoke laughter and surprise — use this positive energy to keep engagement high.
Demonstrate each exercise yourself before players attempt it, including common errors to watch for.
The single-leg squat is the most technically important exercise in this sequence. Spend extra time on the knee alignment coaching point.
Return to these exercises as part of the warm-up routine monthly and track measurable improvement in balance times.
diagramDescription: Individual 2x2m spaces. Six exercise stations shown in sequence: 1 (single-leg stand, eyes open timer), 2 (eyes closed version with wobble lines), 3 (knee circle diagram showing clockwise direction), 4 (star excursion reach showing 3 directions), 5 (single-leg squat with knee alignment line showing correct tracking over second toe), 6 (ball juggle on one leg). Arm extension shown as a balance aid. Weak leg emphasis marker shown.

title: Dynamic Balance — Change of Direction
objective: Develop dynamic balance through rapid change of direction movements that replicate the demands of match play.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain balance and control through sharp directional changes at increasing speeds
Players develop the deceleration technique that allows safe and effective direction changes
Players build the lateral stability needed for the side-to-side movements of match play
Players connect dynamic balance to specific match movements such as turning, cutting, and evading
equipment: Cones for agility course (8 cones per player)
pitchSize: Each player has a 15x5m agility lane
organisation: Agility lane per player: 8 cones arranged in a zigzag pattern across 15m. Players run through the course at increasing speed, making sharp directional changes at each cone. The course simulates the rapid direction changes of match play.
instructions: Set up 8 cones in a zigzag pattern: cone 1 at 0m left, cone 2 at 2m right, cone 3 at 4m left, cone 4 at 6m right, continuing to 14m.
The gap between left and right cones is 2m, creating a genuine lateral direction change at each.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): jog pace. Focus on technique at each direction change: plant the outside foot, push off explosively, drive the inside arm.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): 80 percent pace. Maintain technique. Time each run. Target: under 5 seconds for the full course.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): maximum speed. Full commitment to every direction change.
TIMING: time every run in Phases 2 and 3. Track personal best and challenge players to improve by 0.3 seconds across the session.
FINAL 3 minutes: with a ball. Dribble through the course at maximum speed with the ball. Ball must not go outside the 2m gap at each cone.
coachingPoints: PLANT FOOT OUTSIDE THE CONE: the foot closest to the change of direction must plant on the outside of the cone, creating the pivot point for the change. Planting inside the cone reduces the change angle and slows the movement.

DECELERATE INTO THE CHANGE, ACCELERATE OUT: do not try to maintain full speed through the direction change. Brief deceleration into the plant foot, explosive acceleration out. This is faster overall than attempting a high-speed turn.
DRIVE THE INSIDE ARM: the arm on the side you are turning toward drives powerfully forward and across the body. This arm action directly powers the directional change.
KEEP THE CENTRE OF GRAVITY LOW: bend the knees slightly before each direction change. A high centre of gravity makes direction changes slower and less stable.

SHORT, QUICK STEPS APPROACHING THE CONE: long strides approaching the direction change point make it much harder to plant and change direction safely. Shorten the steps in the last 2m before the cone.
EYES UP THROUGHOUT: look ahead to the next cone, not at the ground or the current cone. This allows earlier preparation for the next direction change.
THE EXPLOSIVE PUSH IS EVERYTHING: after planting, the push off the plant foot into the new direction must be explosive. This is the highest-value moment in the entire sequence.
CONSISTENT TECHNIQUE AT ALL SPEEDS: the same technique should apply at jogging pace and maximum speed. If technique breaks down at speed, slow back down and rebuild.
commonMistakes: Planting the inside foot at the cone rather than the outside foot, reducing the leverage for the change
Trying to maintain speed through the direction change rather than briefly decelerating
Not driving the inside arm, losing the rotational power that comes from arm drive
High centre of gravity making the changes slow and uncontrolled
Long strides into the cone, making the plant and push impossible to execute cleanly
corrections: Inside foot plant: place a marker 30cm outside each cone showing where the plant foot should land
Not decelerating: use a slower approach speed in Phase 1 until the decel-accel pattern is established
No arm drive: require players to call DRIVE with each direction change as a reminder to actively use the arm
High centre of gravity: ask players to touch the top of each cone as they pass it (forces lower body position)
Long strides: place 3 small markers 50cm apart before each cone requiring short steps in the approach
progressions: Reduce cone spacing to 1.5m, increasing the sharpness of the required direction change
Add a reactive element: coach calls LEFT or RIGHT at each cone and player must change to the called side (may not match the course direction)
Transfer to a 1v1 dribbling situation where the course-trained direction change skills are used to evade a defender
regressions: Increase cone spacing to 3m to make the direction changes gentler and more manageable at initial speeds
Walk through the plant-and-push technique at each cone before any live running
Reduce to 4 cones (half the course) until the technique is established before extending to the full 8
matchApplication: Dynamic balance and direction-changing ability are among the most performance-relevant physical qualities in football. Analysis of match data shows that players make 700-1000 direction changes per match, many at high speeds. The ability to decelerate efficiently, plant with stability, and accelerate explosively in the new direction directly determines how quickly a player can beat an opponent, create space, or recover defensively. The specific plant-foot technique and arm drive coaching points in this drill train the exact biomechanics that maximise the efficiency and speed of these direction changes.
coachTips: Timing is the most motivating element of this drill for U14 players. Make personal bests visible and celebrated.
Film players from above during Phase 3 if possible — the foot plant position is much clearer from above than from the side.
The decel-accel coaching point is counterintuitive. Demonstrate explicitly that slowing slightly before the cone results in a faster overall time than trying to maintain speed through it.
Connect to specific match movements: cutting inside a defender, recovering laterally to cover a wide run, sprinting to close down a ball.
Run this drill monthly and track improvement. Players who train direction changing specifically improve by 10-15 percent over a season.
diagramDescription: 15x5m agility lane per player. 8 cones shown in zigzag pattern: alternating left and right every 2m across 14m. Running path arrows shown zigzagging through the cones. At each cone: plant foot marker shown outside the cone, push-off arrow shown in new direction, inside arm drive shown. Deceleration zone shown 2m before each cone. Acceleration zone shown 2m after each cone. Timer shown above the lane with personal best marker.

title: Reaction Speed and First Step Quickness
objective: Develop the reaction time and first-step quickness that determine whether a player wins tackles, beats opponents, and reaches the ball first.
learningOutcomes: Players develop a faster reaction time from a standing position to movement
Players build the first-step quickness that is more important than top speed in most match situations
Players develop the athletic ready position that maximises reaction speed
Players connect reaction training to specific football situations requiring quick first steps
equipment: Coloured bibs or cards for signals
organisation: Individual 10m sprint lanes. Players start in a ready position. Coach gives a variety of signals (visual, audio, touch). Players react to the correct signal and sprint 10m. The key variable is reaction time, not running speed.
instructions: READY POSITION: feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, weight on balls of feet, slight forward lean. This is the optimal starting position for maximum reaction speed.
PHASE 1 - AUDIO REACTION (4 minutes): coach calls GO. Player sprints 10m. Rest 20 seconds. 8 reps. Focus: how quickly does the first step leave the ground?

PHASE 2 - VISUAL REACTION (4 minutes): coach holds up coloured cards. LEFT card: player sprints left. RIGHT card: player sprints right. CENTRE card: player sprints straight ahead. 8 reps. Rest 20 seconds between reps.

PHASE 3 - TOUCH REACTION (3 minutes): player stands with eyes closed. Coach touches right shoulder: player turns right and sprints 5m. Left shoulder: turns left and sprints 5m. Back: turns and sprints forward 5m.

PHASE 4 - BALL DROP REACTION (4 minutes): coach holds a ball at shoulder height. Drops it. Player must catch it before it bounces twice. Measures pure hand-eye reaction time. Increase the challenge by dropping from higher.
COMPETITION: pair players and race over 10m on a signal. Track who wins each race. Leaderboard format keeps energy high.
FINAL 3 minutes: players choose their preferred reaction signal for a final competitive phase.
coachingPoints: THE READY POSITION IS EVERYTHING: a player in the correct ready position (weight on balls of feet, knees bent, slight lean) will always react faster than a flat-footed, upright player. The position pre-loads the muscles for explosive movement.

THE FIRST STEP IS THE RACE: in most football situations (a loose ball, a tackle, getting to a cross) the player who takes the fastest first step wins regardless of top speed. The first 5m is where most football races are decided.

DO NOT ANTICIPATE: reacting to the actual signal is harder than anticipating when the signal will come. Resist the temptation to lean in a direction before the signal. False starts mean the player has stopped reading and started guessing.
EXPLOSIVE FIRST STEP: the first step should be short and powerful, driving directly into the ground backward. A long first step reaches forward and actually slows the initial acceleration.
KEEP THE HEAD STILL: during the reaction and first step, the head and eyes must focus on the signal source (the coach). Head movement before the signal delays reaction time.

RELAXATION BETWEEN REPS: the nervous system needs brief recovery between maximum-effort reaction reps. 20 seconds of relaxed standing between each rep is important for maintaining the quality of reaction.
BOTH DIRECTIONS EQUALLY: reaction speed to the left and right can be very different. If a player is significantly faster to one side, more practice to the weaker side is needed.
COMPETITION SHARPENS REACTION: racing against a partner produces faster reaction times than solo practice because competitive motivation activates the nervous system more fully.
commonMistakes: Standing flat-footed with an upright posture, eliminating the quick-reaction advantage of the ready position
Anticipating the signal and beginning to move before it is given
Long first step reaching forward rather than a short explosive push backward
Head moving toward the expected direction before the signal
Insufficient rest between reps, fatiguing the nervous system and reducing reaction quality in later reps
corrections: Flat-footed stance: place paper under each heel. The paper must not be crushed during the ready position — weight on balls of feet only
Anticipating signal: introduce random delays between reps (2 seconds, 5 seconds, 8 seconds) so the timing cannot be predicted
Long first step: place a cone 40cm ahead of the starting position. First step must not pass the cone
Head moving: require eye contact with the coach throughout the ready position — any head movement before the signal is a false start
Insufficient rest: enforce the 20-second rest period explicitly, counting aloud between every rep
progressions: Drop the rest period to 10 seconds between reps for players with excellent nervous system recovery ability
Add a direction instruction after the signal: the player reacts to GO then must change direction at a second cone based on a new signal
Combine with a football action: after the 10m sprint, player receives a pass and must immediately make a decision (shoot, pass left, or pass right based on a visual signal)
regressions: Use audio-only signals for the entire session before adding visual and touch signals
Increase rest periods to 30 seconds for players who find the physical demand too high
Start with a 5m sprint distance rather than 10m to reduce the physical demand while maintaining the reaction training focus
matchApplication: Reaction speed and first-step quickness are among the most important physical qualities in football. Research shows that elite footballers have significantly faster reaction times than amateur players, and that this quality is directly trainable. Most decisive football moments (reaching a loose ball, winning a tackle, closing down an attacker) are determined in the first 0.5 seconds after the decision trigger — the player with the faster reaction time consistently arrives first regardless of their top running speed. Training reaction time and first-step quality systematically from U12 produces players who are sharper, faster to the ball, and more effective in every contest.
coachTips: Keep the competitive and energetic atmosphere high throughout this session — the nervous system responds to arousal and motivation.
The ball drop reaction test is particularly engaging and provides immediate, visible feedback on reaction time.
Pair players of similar reaction speed for the competitive phase to keep the races competitive and motivating.
This drill works best at the start of a session before any fatigue accumulates — the nervous system must be fresh.
Track individual reaction times using a stopwatch for Phase 1 across sessions. Most players show 10-20 percent improvement in reaction time with consistent training.
diagramDescription: 10m sprint lanes side by side. Player shown in correct ready position: weight on balls of feet (paper under heels shown), knees bent, forward lean, eyes on coach. Four signal types shown: AUDIO (coach with whistle/clap), VISUAL (coloured cards: LEFT, RIGHT, CENTRE), TOUCH (shoulder tap), BALL DROP (ball held at shoulder height above the player). First step shown as a short powerful push backward into the ground. 20-second rest period shown between reps. Competition format shown with two players racing.

title: Reaction Saves and Catch — GK Reaction Training
objective: Develop basic reaction speed in young players through fun catching, dodging, and response games that build the neural foundations for quick movement.
learningOutcomes: Players develop basic reaction speed through enjoyable challenge games
Players build the hand-eye coordination that underpins reaction to a moving ball
Players develop the body awareness and readiness position for quick responses
Players experience the joy of getting faster through focused practice
organisation: Fun reaction games played in pairs and small groups. No formal athletic positions required. The focus is on making reaction training feel like a game rather than an exercise for U10 players.
instructions: GAME 1 - DROP CATCH (3 minutes): Player A holds a ball at chest height. Player B stands 1m away with hands at their sides. Player A drops the ball without warning. Player B must catch it before it hits the ground. Swap roles every 60 seconds.

GAME 2 - COLOUR FREEZE (3 minutes): all players move freely around a zone. Coach calls a colour. Every player wearing that colour must freeze instantly. All others must tag a frozen player before they unfreeze. Develops reaction to auditory signals and immediate body control.

GAME 3 - MIRROR HANDS (3 minutes): pairs stand facing each other 1m apart. Player A moves their hands anywhere in front of their body. Player B must mirror the movement as quickly as possible. Swap leader every 60 seconds.

GAME 4 - BALL CHASE (3 minutes): Player A holds a ball behind their back. Player B stands 2m directly in front. Player A rolls the ball in any direction without warning. Player B must run to reach it before it rolls 5m. Swap every 5 reps.
GAME 5 (3 minutes): free reaction game. Players create their own reaction challenge with a partner and play it.
