coachingPoints: REACTION IS A SKILL: getting faster reactions is not about being born with them. Like any skill, reaction speed improves with practice. Every game in this session is making you faster.

STAY READY BETWEEN GAMES: the body should never fully relax between each game rep. Stay on the balls of your feet and alert between each attempt. Preparation before the trigger is as important as the reaction to it.
WATCH THE SIGNAL SOURCE: in every game, identify the signal source (the ball, the hands, the voice) and keep full attention on it. Divided attention slows reaction.
DO NOT PREDICT: trying to guess what the signal will be actually slows reaction time. Wait for it genuinely and your reaction will be faster.
CELEBRATE FAST REACTIONS: every time a player catches the drop, tags a frozen player, or mirrors perfectly, celebrate it. These are achievements worth recognising.
BOTH SIDES EQUALLY: in Mirror Hands and Drop Catch, use both left and right hands equally. Reaction speed can be different on each side.
HAVE FUN: the most important instruction is to enjoy this session. Players who enjoy what they are doing put in more effort, which means better training and faster improvement.
CONNECT TO FOOTBALL: in a match you need fast reactions to save shots (GK), reach a loose ball, intercept a pass, or respond to a teammates movement. Every game today is training exactly that.
commonMistakes: Dropping attention between game reps and being caught off guard by the signal
Predicting the signal direction and moving before it is given
Only using the dominant hand in Mirror Hands and Drop Catch
Losing concentration and focus during the free games
Becoming competitive in a way that removes the fun from the session
corrections: Dropping attention: remind players between every rep to stay in the ready (alert) position
Predicting: introduce random pauses of different lengths before each signal to prevent rhythm prediction
Only dominant hand: specifically call out LEFT HAND ONLY or RIGHT HAND ONLY for alternate reps
Losing focus: switch games more frequently (every 90 seconds) to maintain novelty and engagement
Overly competitive: remove scores and rankings for a phase and play purely for fun, reintroducing competition after the enjoyment is restored
progressions: Reduce Drop Catch to 50cm height: player must react to a ball dropped from waist height
Add a direction call to Ball Chase: Player A calls LEFT, RIGHT, or FORWARD as they roll the ball to give Player B a tiny advantage
Colour Freeze tournament format: last player to freeze in each round is eliminated. Final player wins the round
regressions: Increase the drop height to 1.2m for players who find the standard Drop Catch too easy
Allow Player B to face sideways in Drop Catch, giving a slightly wider peripheral vision to detect the drop
Slow Mirror Hands to 50 percent pace for players who find the mirroring too difficult
matchApplication: The fun reaction games in this drill are specifically designed for U10 players because at this age, enjoyment and intrinsic motivation are the most powerful drivers of physical development. The games are not simplified versions of serious athletic training — they are genuinely effective reaction speed training methods that happen to be enjoyable. The Drop Catch specifically trains the hand-eye coordination and visual reaction time that is directly relevant to every ball-contact moment in football. The Colour Freeze trains auditory reaction and body control. Together they build the neural foundations that support faster, more responsive physical performance throughout a players development.
coachTips: Energy and enthusiasm from the coach are essential in a session with U10 players. Match their energy level.
The free reaction game in Game 5 is the most valuable part of the session for creativity and ownership — give players full freedom to invent.
Watch for players who are not in the ready position between reps and gently correct this as the most important habit to build.
Connect the games explicitly to football situations: that catch is exactly what a goalkeeper does when they react to a deflected shot.
This session can be run as a warm-up game for any training session — 10-15 minutes of reaction games is an excellent session opener.
diagramDescription: Open area with 3x3m personal spaces. GAME 1 shown: Player A holding ball at chest height, Player B with hands at sides, drop catch arrow shown with catch position below. GAME 2 shown: players spread across zone with coloured bib players frozen and others shown running to tag them. GAME 3 shown: pairs facing each other with mirrored hand positions. GAME 4 shown: ball rolling away from Player A with Player B sprinting to reach it. GAME 5 shown: open creative game with various different reaction challenges.

title: Speed Endurance — Repeated Sprint Ability
objective: Develop the ability to repeat maximum-effort sprints with short recovery periods, replicating the intermittent sprint demands of football.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain sprint quality across multiple repeated efforts with incomplete recovery
Players develop the physiological adaptations that improve recovery between sprints
Players understand the difference between speed training and speed endurance training
Players build the match-specific fitness to sprint at full intensity in the final minutes of a match
organisation: 40m sprint corridors marked on the pitch. Players sprint 40m, walk back (20 seconds recovery), and sprint again. The key variable is maintaining sprint quality across all repetitions despite the short, incomplete recovery.
instructions: WARM-UP (3 minutes): 2 easy jog laps of the area, 5 dynamic stretches each leg.
BLOCK 1 — BASELINE (4 minutes): 4 x 40m sprints with 60 seconds complete rest between each. Time every sprint. This establishes the baseline speed — the time the player can achieve when fully rested.
BLOCK 2 — SPEED ENDURANCE (12 minutes): 8 x 40m sprints with only 20-second walk-back recovery between each. Time every sprint.
The aim: how close can the player keep their Block 2 sprint times to their Block 1 baseline?
A player who maintains within 0.3 seconds of their baseline across all 8 reps has excellent speed endurance.
A player whose times drop by more than 0.8 seconds by rep 6-8 needs more speed endurance training.
BLOCK 3 — RECOVERY RUNS (3 minutes): 4 easy jogs of 40m. Active recovery.
Record every time. Discuss with players the relationship between their baseline speed and their speed endurance performance.
coachingPoints: SPEED ENDURANCE IS DIFFERENT FROM SPEED: speed training uses full rest to maximise each sprint quality. Speed endurance deliberately uses incomplete rest to train the recovery between sprints. Both are needed but they are separate qualities.

MAINTAIN FORM WHEN TIRED: as fatigue accumulates across Block 2, the temptation is to let the running form deteriorate (arms crossing, head dropping, stride shortening). Resist this. Maintaining form when tired is a skill in itself.
THE FIRST 2 REPS OF BLOCK 2 ARE EASY: the real training begins from rep 4-5 when the genuine fatigue from incomplete recovery starts to accumulate. The final 4 reps are where the adaptation happens.

MENTAL DISCIPLINE OVER PHYSICAL CAPACITY: in the later reps of Block 2, most athletes are more limited by mental willingness to hurt than by physical capacity. Practising the mental discipline to maintain pace when uncomfortable is a genuine training adaptation.
TIME EVERY REP: without timing, this session is just a series of runs. The times are the feedback that shows whether the speed endurance is improving. Take them seriously.
COMPARE BASELINE TO ENDURANCE PERFORMANCE: the gap between Block 1 and the later reps of Block 2 is the most important measurement. Reducing this gap is the goal of the session.
ACTIVE RECOVERY IN BLOCK 3: the easy jogs in Block 3 are not rest — they actively clear lactate from the muscles faster than standing still. Do not skip them.

CONSISTENT TECHNIQUE: the 40m distance and the specific timing protocol in this drill allow for direct comparison across sessions. Keep the distance and recovery time identical each time to make tracking meaningful.
commonMistakes: Sprint intensity dropping to 80-85 percent in Block 2 to make the times easier to maintain, defeating the purpose of the session
Running form deteriorating significantly across Block 2 reps due to fatigue
Not timing the reps, removing the performance data that gives the session its training value
Not walking (using running recovery) during the 20-second rest periods, getting more recovery than the protocol intends
Completing fewer reps than prescribed because the later reps feel too difficult
corrections: Intensity dropping: pair players of similar speed and introduce a minimum time standard — any rep run slower than 120 percent of baseline time is flagged as not genuine effort
Form deterioration: identify the one form element most likely to deteriorate (usually arm crossing) and assign a partner to watch for it in every rep
Not timing: use a clear visual timing system (coach reads times immediately after each rep aloud for all players to hear)
Running during recovery: place clear walk-only zones between the sprint cones. Any running during recovery means the rep does not count
Stopping early: use the pair-support format where both players in a pair must complete all 8 reps to earn any points
progressions: Reduce recovery to 15 seconds between Block 2 reps for players with excellent speed endurance already
Increase Block 2 to 10 reps for more advanced fitness levels
Add direction changes: sprint 20m, change direction at a cone, sprint 20m back (total 40m with a direction change)
regressions: Increase recovery to 30 seconds between Block 2 reps for players not yet conditioned for 20-second recovery
Reduce Block 2 to 6 reps and Block 1 to 3 baseline reps for younger or less fit players
Use a 25m distance rather than 40m for players who find 40m too physically demanding in the early sessions
matchApplication: Speed endurance — the ability to repeat maximum-effort sprints with short recovery — is one of the most match-relevant physical qualities in football. A player who can sprint at full intensity in the 85th minute of a match as fast as in the 5th minute has a massive physical advantage. This quality is physiologically trainable and responds quickly to consistent specific training. The measurement system in this drill (comparing Block 1 baseline times to Block 2 rep times) gives both the coach and the player direct, objective feedback about the quality of their speed endurance, making improvement visible and motivating.
coachTips: The timing system is the most important element of this drill. Invest in making it accurate and prominent.
Discuss the Block 1 vs Block 2 comparison explicitly with players after the session — most players are surprised by how much their times drop under repeated-sprint conditions.
This session is physically demanding. Ensure adequate warm-up and that players are well-hydrated before starting.
Run this session fortnightly rather than weekly — the physiological adaptations require adequate recovery between sessions.
Track Block 1 baselines and the Block 2 time drop across a full season. Players who train speed endurance consistently show measurable improvement in both raw speed and speed maintenance.
diagramDescription: 40m sprint corridor. BLOCK 1 shown: 4 sprint arrows with 60-second rest bars between each. Times shown above each: 5.8s, 5.8s, 5.9s, 5.8s (baseline established). BLOCK 2 shown: 8 sprint arrows with 20-second walk-back recovery bars between each. Times shown: 5.9s, 6.0s, 6.1s, 6.2s, 6.3s, 6.5s, 6.6s, 6.7s (time drop illustrated). Gap comparison shown: Baseline 5.8s vs Rep 8: 6.7s (gap = 0.9s). BLOCK 3 shown as easy jog arrows. Target gap of less than 0.3s shown as the excellence standard.

title: Agility With the Ball — Match-Specific Movement
objective: Develop agility in a ball-specific context, combining the footwork and directional change of agility training with the ball-control demands of football.
learningOutcomes: Players complete agility patterns at speed while maintaining close ball control
Players develop the coordination between footwork and ball handling under physical demand
Players build the sport-specific agility that transfers directly to match movements
Players connect agility training to specific football actions through ball-integrated exercises
organisation: Individual agility courses with a ball. Each course combines footwork stations with ball control stations to create a fully football-specific agility challenge. Multiple players work simultaneously.
instructions: COURSE SETUP: four stations across 15m.
STATION 1 (0-3m): agility ladder footwork (2 feet per box). Player dribbles the ball alongside the ladder (not through it) with one hand while doing the ladder footwork.
STATION 2 (3-7m): 4 cones in a diamond. Player dribbles through the diamond in a figure-of-8 pattern using alternate feet on each cone.
STATION 3 (7-11m): slalom through 4 cones spaced 1m apart. Use only the outside of the foot on each cone turn.
STATION 4 (11-15m): 3 quick direction changes across a 4m width. Player must cut sharply at each end using the foot movement from the dynamic balance drill.
Run the full course and time it. Walk back. Repeat.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): technique focus at 70 percent. Coach stations at each section giving specific feedback.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): competition pace. Time every run. Improve personal best.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): pressure round. Partner shouts GO randomly during the walk-back phase, meaning the next run must start within 3 seconds of the call (incomplete rest round).
coachingPoints: BALL CONTROL DOES NOT STOP DURING AGILITY: the ball is with you at every station, not put aside during the footwork and collected after. This is exactly what distinguishes football agility from track-and-field agility.
KEEP THE BALL CLOSE: a ball that runs away from the player during the agility course destroys the flow of the session and wastes time. Every touch keeps the ball within 1m.

OUTSIDE OF FOOT IN THE SLALOM: the outside of the foot is the most efficient surface for tight slalom turns because it allows the body to stay facing forward rather than turning sideways. Practice it specifically.
EYES AHEAD, NOT DOWN: during every station, the player should be looking ahead to the next station, not down at the ball. Peripheral vision handles the ball while the eyes plan ahead.
RHYTHM AND FLOW: the best agility runs are rhythmic. They have an almost musical quality. When the rhythm feels right, the ball feels easy and the movement is fast.
FOOTWORK AND BALL WORK ARE ONE ACTION: do not think of the footwork and the ball control as two separate things happening at the same time. They are one integrated movement pattern.
PERSONAL BEST IS THE COMPETITION: the time on the course is not compared between players of different sizes and speeds. Each player competes only with themselves.
REST IS PART OF THE SESSION: the walk-back recovery is not optional. Full-quality agility work requires genuine recovery between maximum-effort runs.
commonMistakes: Ball running too far away from the player during the agility course, disrupting the flow
Looking down at the ball during the footwork sections instead of ahead to the next station
Using only the inside of the foot in the slalom section instead of the outside of the foot
Running the course smoothly in a straight line but not genuinely making sharp directional changes at each station
Ball lost completely during the course and time wasted retrieving it
corrections: Ball too far away: reduce the course width and slow to 50 percent until the ball stays within 1m consistently
Looking down: place a cone at eye height on a stick at Station 2 — player must focus on the cone throughout Station 2
Only inside foot: place a colour marker on the outside of the boot. That surface must contact the slalom cones
Not sharp enough at direction changes: reduce the width of Station 4 to 2.5m, forcing sharper, more committed changes
Ball lost: use a slower ball (slightly deflated) that rolls less freely and is easier to control during the agility movements
progressions: Two balls: player uses one ball for Stations 1-2 and a second ball (placed at Station 3) for Stations 3-4, simulating a ball transition
Partner pressure: partner stands at Station 2 and adds slight shoulder-to-shoulder contact, replicating ball retention under physical pressure during agility
Course reversal: run the course in reverse order — different direction of turns challenges the less dominant side
regressions: Use walking pace for the full first 5 minutes before any timed attempts
Remove the ball from Station 1 (ladder footwork) to reduce the coordination demand at that station while the pattern is learned
Reduce to 2 stations only until the concept of ball-integrated agility is understood before adding all 4
matchApplication: Agility training with a ball directly transfers to match performance because it trains the integrated coordination of footwork, ball handling, and vision that characterises all football movement. Standard agility training without a ball develops the movement mechanics but fails to integrate them with the ball control demands of football. Players who consistently train ball-integrated agility develop the automatic coordination that allows them to change direction sharply and quickly while maintaining full control of the ball — a quality that is immediately visible in their match performance.
coachTips: Demonstrate the full course yourself at match speed before players attempt it — the integration of footwork and ball control is clearest when seen at full speed.
The timing element is essential for motivation. Make personal bests a genuine event in the session.
Give specific individual feedback at each station during Phase 1 — each station has a different technical demand.
Connect each station to a specific match movement: slalom = dribbling past defenders, direction changes = cutting away from a tackle.
Run this course monthly and track improvement. Players who integrate agility and ball work consistently show the most significant improvement in ball control quality under pressure.
diagramDescription: 15x5m agility course with 4 stations labeled. STATION 1 (0-3m): agility ladder with ball dribbling alongside, 2-feet-per-box footwork. STATION 2 (3-7m): diamond of 4 cones with figure-of-8 dribbling path. STATION 3 (7-11m): 4 slalom cones with outside-of-foot turn arrows at each. STATION 4 (11-15m): 4m-wide direction change zone with sharp cut arrows at each end. Ball shown at each station integrated into the movement. Timer shown above course. Personal best marker shown.

title: Core Strength for Football
objective: Develop football-specific core strength that improves balance, power transfer, and physical resilience during match play.
learningOutcomes: Players build core stability and anti-rotation strength relevant to football
Players develop the connection between core strength and ball-striking power
Players learn the correct breathing and bracing technique during core exercises
Players build injury resilience through targeted core training
pitchSize: Any flat outdoor or indoor area
organisation: 5-station core circuit. Players work 40 seconds per station, 20 seconds rest. 3 circuits total with 60 seconds between circuits. All exercises are bodyweight and require no equipment.
instructions: STATION 1 - DEAD BUG: lie on your back, arms pointing at the ceiling, knees at 90 degrees. Simultaneously lower your right arm to the ground behind your head and your left leg to straight, hovering 10cm off the ground. Return and swap sides. NEVER let your lower back lift off the ground.

STATION 2 - COPENHAGEN PLANK: lie on your side. Place your top foot on a low cone or a partners knee. Lift your hips so your body is in a straight line from head to foot. Hold. This specifically targets the inner thigh and lateral core — major injury prevention muscles.

STATION 3 - PALLOF PRESS (with resistance band): stand sideways to a fixed point with a resistance band. Hold the band at chest height. Press forward (arms straight) then return. The core must resist rotating. This is anti-rotation strength — essential for receiving physical challenges.

STATION 4 - SINGLE LEG DEADLIFT: stand on one leg. Hinge at the hip, reaching the opposite hand toward the ground while the free leg goes back. Return to standing. This trains the posterior chain and single-leg stability simultaneously.

STATION 5 - HOLLOW BODY HOLD: lie on your back. Press your lower back into the ground. Arms extend overhead, legs extend to 30 degrees. Hold the position. This is the foundation of all gymnastic core strength and transfers directly to heading power.
3 complete circuits. 60 seconds rest between circuits.
coachingPoints: THE CORE IS THE POWER TRANSFER POINT: every action in football — striking the ball, heading, shielding, changing direction — generates power in the legs and transfers it through the core. A weak core loses power at this transfer point.

BRACE THE CORE BEFORE EVERY REP: before beginning each repetition, take a breath and brace the abdominals as if expecting a punch. This creates intra-abdominal pressure that protects the spine and maximises power transfer.

LOWER BACK ON THE GROUND (DEAD BUG AND HOLLOW BODY): these are the most important technical points in this circuit. If the lower back lifts during these exercises, the exercise is not working and may cause injury. Keep it pressed flat.

ANTI-ROTATION IS UNDERTRAINABLE: most players train flexion (sit-ups, crunches) and extension but never anti-rotation. The Pallof Press is the most specific anti-rotation exercise for football — the resistance to twisting is exactly what allows a player to hold their position under physical challenge.
QUALITY OVER REPS: 8 perfect Dead Bug reps are worth more than 20 poor ones. Never sacrifice technique for the sake of a higher repetition count.

THE COPENHAGEN PLANK PREVENTS GROIN INJURIES: one of the most common and serious football injuries is the adductor (groin) strain. The Copenhagen Plank specifically strengthens the muscles that prevent this. It should be a weekly fixture.
BREATHE OUT ON THE EFFORT: exhale during the hardest part of each exercise (the press in the Pallof, the descent in the Single Leg Deadlift). This engages the core most effectively.

CONNECT TO HEADING POWER: the hollow body position in Station 5 is directly related to the arch-then-snap motion of a powerful header. A stronger hollow body hold produces a more powerful heading snap.
commonMistakes: Lower back lifting during Dead Bug and Hollow Body, defeating the purpose of the exercise and risking injury
Rotation of the hips during the Copenhagen Plank, reducing the lateral core challenge
Using the arms to push or pull during the Pallof Press rather than resisting the band with the core
Bent standing leg during the Single Leg Deadlift, reducing the posterior chain challenge
Not bracing the core before repetitions, losing the protective and power-enhancing benefit
corrections: Lower back lifting: place a hand under the lower back during the exercise. The hand must feel pressure from the back throughout. If the pressure releases, the back has lifted
Hip rotation in Copenhagen: place a ball between the knees. If it drops, the hips have rotated
Arms pushing in Pallof: the arms are fully extended before the press begins. Arms must maintain this extension throughout — any bending means the arms are pushing rather than the core resisting
Bent standing leg: place a straight-leg marker (small block) behind the standing knee as a contact reminder
No bracing: specifically call OUT BREATHE IN before each rep, giving players the cue to brace before beginning
progressions: Add a 5-second hold at the hardest point of each exercise (arm fully extended in Pallof, lowest point of Dead Bug) for an additional challenge
Introduce a loaded version of the Single Leg Deadlift using a light medicine ball or a filled water bottle
Add a 4th circuit for players who complete 3 circuits with excellent technique throughout
regressions: Reduce to 3 stations only for the first session before building to all 5
Allow 30-second holds on the easier exercises before building to the 40-second work period
Use longer rest periods (35 seconds between stations) for players new to core training
matchApplication: Core strength is one of the most evidence-backed areas of football fitness, with research consistently showing that targeted core training reduces injury rates (particularly groin and hamstring injuries), improves balance, and enhances the power transfer that generates kicking power and heading force. The specific exercises in this circuit were chosen because they directly target the anti-rotation, lateral stability, and posterior chain qualities most relevant to football movement. These are not generic gym exercises — they are the specific core training methods used by professional clubs specifically because of their football application.
coachTips: The Dead Bug is technically demanding and worth spending 2-3 minutes coaching in detail before the circuit begins.
The Copenhagen Plank is uncomfortable for most players who have never done it. Normalise this discomfort and encourage persistence — the benefit is significant.
Connect the Hollow Body to heading power explicitly and enthusiastically. Players who understand why an exercise matters do it better.
Run this circuit twice weekly as a complement to technical sessions. Core strength improvements are visible within 4-6 weeks of consistent practice.
Consider using this circuit as a shared warm-up experience for the whole squad — when core training is part of the team culture, compliance and quality are higher.
diagramDescription: 5 exercise stations in a circuit. STATION 1 (Dead Bug): person lying on back, arm reaching behind head and opposite leg extending to 10cm off ground, lower back pressed flat. STATION 2 (Copenhagen Plank): side plank with top foot on a cone, body straight line, hip raised. STATION 3 (Pallof Press): person standing sideways to band attachment, arms pressing forward. STATION 4 (Single Leg Deadlift): standing on one leg, hinge at hip, opposite hand reaching toward ground. STATION 5 (Hollow Body Hold): lying on back, arms overhead, legs 30 degrees, lower back flat. Circuit arrows showing the rotation between stations.

title: Aerobic Base — Continuous Run Programme
objective: Develop the aerobic base that allows players to sustain effort across a full 90-minute match through structured continuous running.
learningOutcomes: Players build the aerobic capacity needed to run effectively for 90 minutes
Players develop the correct running form for sustained effort
Players understand the connection between aerobic fitness and match performance quality
Players build the habit of structured aerobic training as part of their development
equipment: Cones to mark the running route
pitchSize: A marked running route (perimeter of the pitch or a specific course)
organisation: A clearly marked running route of approximately 400-500m (one full perimeter of a standard pitch). Players run the route continuously for 15 minutes at a prescribed pace (65-70 percent of maximum effort). After 15 minutes of continuous running, a 5-minute cooldown walk.
instructions: Mark the running route clearly: full perimeter of the pitch or a 400-500m course around the training area.
WARM-UP (3 minutes): walk the full route once, then 2 minutes of easy jogging.
SUSTAINED RUN (15 minutes): players run the route continuously at 65-70 percent effort. The correct pace is one at which a conversation is just possible (short sentences only).
The coach leads from the front at the correct pace for the first 5 minutes, then drops back to observe.
COACHES ROLE: walk the inside of the route and give encouragement. Call out any players running too fast (slowing down the aerobic adaptation) or too slow.
FORM COACHING during the run: head up, shoulders relaxed, arms at 90 degrees driving forward and back (not across the body), feet landing under the hips.
COOLDOWN (2 minutes): slow walk of the full route. Deep breathing.
RECORD: total distance covered in 15 minutes. Use this as the measure of aerobic improvement across sessions.
Aim: across a season, players should show measurable improvement in the distance covered in the same 15-minute window at the same perceived effort level.
coachingPoints: AEROBIC BASE IS THE FOUNDATION: all other physical qualities (speed, strength, power) are built on top of aerobic fitness. A player who runs out of energy in the 60th minute cannot use their speed or technical skills in the final 30 minutes when matches are often decided.

65-70 PERCENT IS THE MAGIC ZONE: too slow and the aerobic system is not challenged enough to improve. Too fast and the body switches to anaerobic energy production and the aerobic base benefit is reduced. The talk test (short sentences only) is the best guide.

FORM DURING SUSTAINED RUNNING MATTERS: poor running form (crossing arms, heel striking, forward head) wastes energy and reduces the aerobic training effect. Good form allows you to cover more distance at the same effort level.
BREATHING THROUGH THE NOSE: try to breathe through the nose during the first 5 minutes. If nose breathing becomes impossible, the pace is too high. This is a simple and effective intensity guide.

CONSISTENT PACE IS BETTER THAN VARIABLE PACE: starting fast and slowing down (positive split running) is less effective for aerobic development than starting controlled and maintaining the same pace throughout. Even pacing produces better aerobic adaptation.

THE BENEFIT IS CUMULATIVE: one aerobic run session produces no measurable improvement. Consistent aerobic running across a season produces significant improvement in aerobic capacity. Frequency and consistency matter more than any single session.

CONNECTION TO MATCH PERFORMANCE: players with a higher aerobic base maintain their technical quality better in the final 20 minutes of a match. The player who is still sharp at 85 minutes is almost always the one with the best aerobic fitness.

MENTAL TOUGHNESS ASPECT: sustained running at controlled effort requires mental discipline to maintain the pace when the body wants to slow. This mental discipline transfers directly to match situations.
commonMistakes: Running too fast in the first few minutes and being forced to slow significantly before the 15 minutes is up
Running too slowly at 50 percent or less, not challenging the aerobic system enough to produce adaptation
Arm crossing (arms swinging across the body rather than forward and back) wasting energy
Heel striking (landing on the heel rather than the midfoot) which is less efficient and increases injury risk
Stopping to walk before the 15 minutes is complete
corrections: Too fast at start: for the first 3 minutes enforce a mandatory slow pace by running at the front and requiring everyone to stay behind the coach
Too slow: check pace using the nose-breathing test. If players can breathe easily through the nose throughout, they are below 65 percent effort
Arm crossing: give the specific arm instruction (imagine you are pushing against two walls in front of you) and check every player during the first lap
Heel striking: ask players to think about landing under their hips rather than in front of them. This naturally encourages midfoot striking
Stopping to walk: pair players of similar fitness so the social element (not wanting to let a partner down) encourages continuation
progressions: Increase to 20 minutes of continuous running as aerobic fitness improves across the season
Add a fartlek element in the final 5 minutes: coach calls FAST and players accelerate for 20 seconds before returning to base pace
Progressive overload: add 1 minute per session every 3 sessions until players can comfortably run for 25 minutes at base pace
regressions: Reduce to 10 minutes of continuous running and allow 1 minute of walking recovery if needed
Reduce the route to 300m for smaller fields or to reduce the pace challenge
Allow players to run in pairs for the first session to reduce the psychological challenge of sustained individual effort
matchApplication: Aerobic base fitness is the single most important physical quality for sustained football performance across a full match. Research shows that professional players cover 10-13km per match and that technical performance in the final 15 minutes of a match is directly correlated with aerobic fitness levels. Young players who develop a good aerobic base at U13-U14 enter their senior development phase with a physical advantage that compounds over time. This simple, consistent continuous running programme is the most direct method of developing that base, and its effectiveness has been demonstrated repeatedly across all levels of football.
coachTips: The coach running at the front for the first 5 minutes and setting the correct pace is the most important coaching action in this session.
Make the distance tracking visible and celebrate improvement across sessions — seeing that you covered 200m more in the same time 4 weeks later is powerfully motivating.
Connect aerobic fitness explicitly to match performance quality in the final 20 minutes. Players who understand why they are doing this work harder during it.
Pair this session with the speed endurance drill. The aerobic base supports recovery between sprints. Players with better aerobic bases also have better repeated sprint ability.

Run this session every 2 weeks throughout the season and track improvement. The aerobic fitness improvement across a full season is one of the most measurable and motivating physical developments in youth football.
diagramDescription: 400-500m running route shown as the full perimeter of a standard pitch. Coach shown at the front leading at the correct pace. Players shown running behind in a group. Pace indicator shown: 65-70 percent effort (talk test: short sentences only). Form coaching points shown at relevant positions: head up label, arm drive forward and back (not across body), midfoot landing. Distance tracker shown: total distance covered in 15 minutes. Nose breathing guide shown as a pace check tool.

title: Corner Kick — Short Corner and Variation
objective: Develop the short corner routine as a tactical variation that creates different attacking angles and catches defences off guard.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a short corner combination to create a new crossing angle
Players understand when to use a short corner versus a direct delivery
Players develop the movement patterns that make short corners dangerous
Teams build a second corner variation to complement direct deliveries
players: 10-12 players (corner taker, 1 short option, 3-4 attackers in the box, defenders, GK)
pitchSize: Full penalty area plus corner area
organisation: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Corner routine practiced from both sides. Two variations alternated: direct delivery (already established) and short corner combination.
instructions: SHORT CORNER SETUP: the corner taker stands at the flag. Player B stands 5m away from the taker inside the pitch.
TRIGGER: the corner taker passes short to Player B.

Player B has two options based on what the defence does: Option 1 — play back to the corner taker who has moved to a new angle and now crosses. Option 2 — drive at the penalty area themselves if space opens.
DEFENSIVE READ: if defenders come out to press the short corner (2 defenders leave the box to prevent it), the box becomes less crowded and the subsequent cross is more dangerous.
If defenders hold (stay in the box to defend the area), the short corner creates a new crossing angle from 8-12m further inside the pitch.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): walk-through both variations. No opposition. Strikers rehearse movement adjustments for the delayed delivery.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): passive defenders. Taker chooses short or direct based on a hand signal from the coach.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live. Defenders choose whether to press or hold. Taker reads their decision and selects the correct option.
coachingPoints: READ THE DEFENSIVE RESPONSE IMMEDIATELY: the moment the short pass is played to B, both the taker and B must immediately read whether defenders are pressing or holding. This read takes 0.5 seconds and determines which option is correct.
PLAYER B MUST CONTROL QUICKLY: if B takes too long to control the short pass, the pressing defenders arrive before the next action can happen. One touch and release is the target.

THE NEW CROSSING ANGLE IS OFTEN BETTER: a cross from 10m inside the pitch creates a flatter, more dangerous delivery than a flag-level cross because it allows the ball to be played across the face of the goal.

STRIKERS MUST RESET THEIR RUNS: when a short corner is played, strikers in the box should briefly hold their runs and restart them as the new delivery angle is established. Premature runs on a short corner waste the movement.
COMMUNICATION IS THE MECHANISM: the corner taker should signal short or direct to Player B (and ideally to the box) before approaching the flag so all players are prepared.
DISGUISE IS A WEAPON: approaching the flag as if taking a direct corner before laying it short deceives the opposition. They press late and cannot recover.
PLAYER B MUST OFFER A MEANINGFUL SECOND OPTION: if B stands too close to the flag, they are easy to press. Standing 5m away forces the defence to send a player further out of the box.

VARY THE ROUTINE UNPREDICTABLY: alternating between short and direct corners in a match creates uncertainty for the defending team. A team that only ever does direct corners is easy to organise against.
commonMistakes: Player B standing too close to the flag, making the short option easy to press and cut off
Player B taking too long to control, allowing the pressing defender to arrive
Strikers not adjusting their runs when the short corner is played, wasting the delayed delivery
The corner taker not moving to a new angle after playing the short pass — staying at the flag
No signal or communication between the taker and Player B about which variation is being used
corrections: B too close: place a cone 5m from the flag. B must stand at the cone before the corner is taken
B slow to control: enforce a 1-touch rule for B in phase 1 to build the habit
Strikers not resetting: specifically coach the strikers to hold for 1 second when the short pass is played, then restart their runs
Taker not moving: the taker must begin moving toward the new crossing position the moment the short pass leaves their foot
No communication: require a clear hand signal (closed fist = short, open hand = direct) before every corner
progressions: Add a third option: B plays directly into the box after receiving the short pass without returning to the taker
Two short options: two players offer short, and the taker decides who to pass to based on which is less pressured
Full match corner practice including defensive corners (own team defends the corners taken against them)
regressions: Walk through the short corner combination at slow motion with no defensive pressure for the full first phase
Allow B to take 2 touches before the next action until the 1-touch habit is established
Use passive defenders throughout before introducing the live pressing decision
matchApplication: The short corner is one of the most effective tactical variations in football because it specifically exploits the difficulty defending teams have in deciding whether to press the short option (weakening the box) or hold (allowing the new crossing angle). Teams that master both the direct corner and the short corner variation are genuinely difficult to set up against because each option punishes a different defensive response. At U15 players are tactically mature enough to both understand and execute this decision-making routine.
coachTips: The live phase (phase 3) is the most important part because it trains the actual read of the defensive response that determines which option to use.
The signal system (closed fist vs open hand) must be established and practiced until it is automatic before any live phase.
Film phase 3 from above if possible to show teams the space created by the short corner in the box.
Connect to professional examples: many professional teams use short corner routines specifically to manipulate the defensive shape.
Rotate the corner taker role across multiple players so the team has more than one option for the short corner combination.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Corner flag shown. Player B shown 5m from flag. Short corner pass arrow from taker to B. TWO OPTIONS shown: Option 1 (if defenders press) — pass back to taker who moves to a new angle (arrow showing new crossing position), then cross arrow into box. Option 2 (if defenders hold) — B drives at the box. Defenders shown with two possible responses: PRESS (2 defenders leaving box, labeled) or HOLD (staying in box, labeled). Box movement shown with strikers resetting runs after the short pass is played.

title: Corner Kick — Attacking Movement Patterns
objective: Teach young players their first corner kick movement patterns, establishing clear roles and runs for attacking corners.
learningOutcomes: Players understand their individual role in an attacking corner routine
Players develop the timing of the run in relation to the corner delivery
Players learn the near post, far post, and penalty spot roles
Players build confidence participating in organised set piece routines
players: 8-10 players (corner taker, 3 movement players, defenders, GK)
organisation: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Corner practiced from one side (right). Three movement players assigned clear roles. Defenders added progressively. Focus on the players in the box learning their role before adding defensive pressure.
instructions: Assign roles before the drill begins: Player 1 (near post run), Player 2 (far post run), Player 3 (penalty spot hold).
PLAYER 1 — NEAR POST: starts 5m inside the area. Drives toward the near post as the corner is struck. Times the run to arrive as the ball does. Objective: flick on or direct shot.

PLAYER 2 — FAR POST: starts on the opposite side of the area. Drives toward the far post late. Times to arrive just after the ball passes the near post area. Objective: head or volley the ball back across goal.
PLAYER 3 — PENALTY SPOT: stands at the penalty spot and holds their position. Reacts to any flick-on from Player 1 or any clearance that drops to them. Objective: first-time shot.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): no defenders. All three players walk through their runs slowly to establish the pattern.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): corner taker delivers at match pace. Players time their runs. No defenders. GK in goal.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): add 2 defenders (one marking Player 1, one marking Player 2). Players must lose their marker before making their run.
coachingPoints: KNOW YOUR ROLE BEFORE THE CORNER IS TAKEN: every player in the box should know exactly what they are doing before the corner taker approaches the flag. Confusion during the delivery creates missed opportunities.

TIMING IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SKILL: the run must arrive at the target zone at the same time as the ball. Too early and the defender can track and mark. Too late and the ball has already passed. The timing takes practice.
NEAR POST RUN — LATE AND FAST: the near post run should start later than instinct suggests. Starting the run when the taker begins their approach is usually correct.
FAR POST RUN — HOLD AND ARRIVE: the far post player must hold their starting position longer than the near post player. They should not move until the ball is about to cross the near post area.
PENALTY SPOT PLAYER — STAY CALM: the penalty spot role requires patience. This player must resist the urge to chase the ball and instead remain in position to receive any flick-on or clearance.
MAKE THE RUN LOOK REAL: a half-hearted run at the near post does not commit the defender. The run must look like a genuine attempt to score — because it is.
VARY YOUR STARTING POSITION: do not always start from the same spot in the box. Varying the starting position makes it harder for defenders to pre-position to mark.
CELEBRATE THE RUN AS WELL AS THE GOAL: a perfectly timed run that does not result in a goal is still a success. The coach should celebrate the run quality as well as the outcome.
commonMistakes: All three players making the same run toward the near post, leaving the far post and penalty spot uncovered
Runs starting too early before the corner is struck, allowing defenders to track
Player 3 at the penalty spot chasing the ball rather than staying in their position
No communication between players about who has which role before the corner is taken
Players not losing their marker before making their run in Phase 3
corrections: All to near post: assign roles with coloured bibs in Phase 1 and 2. Player 1 wears a specific colour and must go near post. No debate.
Too early: place a starting cone for each player. They must not leave the cone until the taker begins their approach run
Player 3 chasing: use a specific instruction before every corner: Player 3, stay on the spot, wait for the ball to come to you
No communication: require each player to state their role aloud (near post, far post, spot) before every corner
Not losing marker: introduce the Phase 3 defenders in positions that make losing them achievable (standing beside rather than on the player)
progressions: Add a fourth movement player who holds the edge of the area for the second ball
Introduce corners from both sides, requiring players to adjust their runs for left and right corners
Short corner option: give the corner taker the choice of playing short to a fourth player or delivering direct
regressions: Use a junior goal to reduce the size of the target and make the delivery distances more manageable for U12
Walk through all three runs at slow motion with no ball before any delivery practice
Allow 10 minutes of technical delivery practice for the corner taker before adding moving players in the box
matchApplication: Corner kick routines are the gateway to set piece organisation for young players. Establishing clear individual roles, timing, and movement at U12 builds the foundational set piece understanding that develops into sophisticated routines at senior level. The three-role system (near post, far post, penalty spot) is the simplest complete corner framework and covers the three most dangerous areas of the goal. Teams that practice corners with clear individual roles convert a significantly higher proportion of corner situations into genuine chances.
coachTips: Keep roles simple and consistent at U12. Rotating roles can come later. For now, every player should own one specific role.
Celebrate the runs as loudly as goals. The run that creates the space is as important as the touch that scores.
Use coloured bibs to make roles visually clear throughout phases 1 and 2.
Connect to professional corners: watch a professional corner together and identify which player is playing near post, far post, and penalty spot.
Once the routine is established, practice it at the start of every training session as a 5-minute warm-up. Consistent repetition makes the routine automatic under match pressure.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Corner flag shown (right side). Player 1 (near post) shown with run arrow starting late, driving toward near post. Player 2 (far post) shown holding then driving late toward far post. Player 3 shown stationary at penalty spot. Corner delivery arc shown. Timing sequence shown: taker begins approach, Player 1 starts run, Player 2 holds, Player 2 starts run as ball passes near post area. Two defenders shown in Phase 3 being lost before the players make their runs.

title: Free Kick — Indirect Combinations Around the Box
objective: Develop indirect free kick routines that use combination play to create shooting opportunities from free kicks just outside the penalty area.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a pull-back and shoot routine from an indirect free kick
Players develop dummy run variations that deceive the defensive wall
Players understand when to use a combination versus a direct free kick routine
Teams build 2-3 reliable indirect free kick routines for different positions
players: 8-10 players (2 free kick takers plus attackers and defenders)
pitchSize: Full penalty area approach zone (18-25m from goal)
organisation: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Free kicks practiced from positions 18-22m from goal where direct shots are unlikely to trouble quality GKs, making combination routines more effective.
instructions: ROUTINE 1 — PULL-BACK AND SHOOT:
Player A stands over the ball. Player B stands 3m behind and to the side.
A plays the ball sideways or backward to B. B shoots first time at goal.
The defensive wall (set for A) is now in the wrong position for B shooting.
ROUTINE 2 — DUMMY AND SHOOT:
Player A runs over the ball without touching it (dummy). Player B, who was moving alongside A, strikes the ball.
The wall and GK both shift slightly in anticipation of A striking the ball. B strikes as they shift.
ROUTINE 3 — LAYOFF AND DRIVE:
Player A plays the ball to Player C who has moved into a shooting position from depth.
C shoots from a position and angle the wall does not cover.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): walk-through all three routines with no opposition. Establish the signals and triggers for each.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): passive wall and GK. Routines practiced at match pace.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): live wall and GK. Team chooses which routine based on the wall position.
coachingPoints: THE ROUTINE MUST LOOK THE SAME UNTIL THE LAST MOMENT: both the dummy and pull-back routines work because the defence sets up expecting one thing and something different happens. Any early signal that a combination is coming allows them to prepare.
FIRST TOUCH OF THE SECOND PLAYER MUST BE A SHOT: in the pull-back routine, B must shoot first time. A controlling touch removes the advantage created by the wall being out of position.
THE DUMMY MUST BE CONVINCING: Player A running over the ball must look identical to their normal approach to strike. Body lean, arm position, run-up — everything must match the real thing.

WALL POSITION DETERMINES WHICH ROUTINE: the coach or team captain reads the wall position before choosing the routine. If the wall leaves the right side open, the pull-back shoots to the right. If the GK is poorly positioned, the dummy might create a direct shot.
COMMUNICATION BEFORE THE FREE KICK: the two takers must agree on the routine with a signal (hand gesture, word) before approaching the ball. Last-minute confusion ruins the routine.
SHOOT LOW: combination free kick shots must go low. A high shot gives the GK time to react. A low, placed shot to the corner gives them almost none.

PLACEMENT OVER POWER IN COMBINATION ROUTINES: the B player in most combination routines does not need power. The wall is already in the wrong position. Place the ball accurately into the unguarded area.
PRACTICE BOTH SIDES: most free kick combinations are practiced from one side. In a match, free kicks come from both sides. Practice all routines from left and right.
commonMistakes: The combination signal arriving too late, causing confusion between the two takers
Player B controlling the pull-back before shooting, giving the defence time to recover
The dummy not being convincing enough, failing to make the wall and GK shift
Shooting high on combination free kicks, giving the GK too much time to react
Practicing routines from only one side of the pitch
corrections: Late signal: the routine must be agreed and signalled before either player approaches the ball — not while approaching
B controlling before shooting: strict first-touch shot rule. Any controlled touch means the attempt does not count
Weak dummy: coach evaluates every dummy attempt and gives immediate feedback on whether the body shape would have been convincing in a match
Shooting high: place a horizontal string 1m above the ground across the near post area. Every shot must go under it
One side only: after every 3 reps from the right, move the free kick to the left and repeat the same routine
progressions: Add a GK who tries to set up for the expected routine, testing whether the combination is disguised well enough to fool them
Introduce a shooting time limit: the combination must result in a shot within 3 seconds of the first touch, preventing overelaboration
Full match with free kicks awarded naturally, requiring the team to select and execute the correct routine under match pressure
regressions: Walk through all three routines at slow motion with no wall present until the signals and movements are established
Use a passive wall that holds position throughout and does not react to the dummy
Allow the combination takers to practice the pull-back and shoot routine in isolation (just the two of them, no other players) until the timing is automatic
matchApplication: Indirect free kick combination routines are responsible for a significant number of goals at every level of football. Teams with 2-3 well-practiced routines convert free kick positions in dangerous areas into genuine goals because the combination disrupts the defensive wall setup that is specifically designed to prevent a direct shot. The disguise element of combination routines (making the routine look like a direct shot until the last moment) is the most technically demanding aspect and requires many repetitions to perfect.
coachTips: The disguise coaching point is the most important in this drill and the one most worth spending extra time on.
Use a video review of professional combination free kick goals to show teams what a well-executed routine looks like.
The signal system between the two takers must be simple, clear, and practiced until it is completely automatic.
Rotate the two taker roles so multiple players develop the ability to execute both the first and second touch roles.
Connect to match situations: discuss specific free kicks your team has had in recent matches and which routine might have been best applied.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area. Free kick position shown 20m from goal, slightly left of centre. ROUTINE 1 shown: Player A over ball, short arrow to Player B behind and right, B first-touch shot arrow to the right of the wall. ROUTINE 2 shown: Player A dummy run (dashed foot-over-ball arrow), Player B strikes as the wall shifts (wall shift arrow shown). ROUTINE 3 shown: A plays to C who has moved laterally, C shoots from a position the wall does not cover (coverage gap labeled). Defensive wall shown with GK behind it. Signal system shown between takers.

title: Free Kick — Dead Ball Delivery Technique
objective: Develop the advanced technical delivery of direct free kicks using curl, dip, and driven techniques for different positions around the box.
learningOutcomes: Players can select and execute the correct free kick delivery technique based on the position and wall distance
Players develop the curl technique for free kicks from wide angles
Players develop the dip technique for free kicks from central positions 25-30m out
Players understand the tactical decision between shooting directly and using a combination
equipment: Cones to mark free kick positions
organisation: Free kick specialists practice delivery from 5 positions. GK in goal with a passive wall of 3-4 players. Focus is purely on technique — the correct delivery for each specific position.
instructions: 5 POSITIONS marked with cones: P1 (central, 20m), P2 (central, 28m), P3 (right of centre, 22m, 15 degree angle), P4 (wide right, 25m, 30 degree angle), P5 (wide left, 25m, 30 degree angle).
TECHNIQUE FOR EACH POSITION:
P1 (central, 20m) — DRIVEN LOW: laces technique, low trajectory, aimed for the corners. The wall cannot jump high enough and the GK has minimal reaction time.
P2 (central, 28m) — DIP TECHNIQUE: strike through the top of the ball with laces, slight backspin. The ball dips sharply over the wall and down under the crossbar. Contact at 2 oclock on the ball.
P3 (slight angle, 22m) — CURL INSIDE: inside of the right foot, contact at 3 oclock. Ball curls around or over the wall into the far post.
P4 (wide right, 25m) — CURL AROUND WALL: inside of right foot, contact at 3 oclock, greater power. Ball curls around the far side of the wall into the near post region.
P5 (wide left, 25m) — CURL AROUND WALL (left foot or right foot curl-around): mirror of P4.
3 attempts from each position per player. Coach evaluates technique and outcome.
coachingPoints: THE CONTACT POINT DETERMINES EVERYTHING: for curl, contact the ball on the outside quarter of the ball (3 oclock for right-foot curl, 9 oclock for left-foot curl). For dip, contact the top of the ball (12 oclock). For driven, contact the centre (6 oclock). These precise contact points are the technical foundation of all free kick delivery.

THE DIP TECHNIQUE IS THE HARDEST: a dipping free kick requires the ball to travel fast enough to carry over the wall but with enough topspin to bring it down sharply under the bar. The laces strike through the top of the ball at a slight downward angle creates this effect.
ANKLE POSITION FOR CURL: for the curl technique, the ankle must be turned outward (inside of foot facing down) at the moment of contact. The foot position creates the spin, not the body movement.
APPROACH ANGLE FOR CURL: approach from a wider angle than for a driven shot. The wider approach angle naturally opens the hips and facilitates the inside-foot contact.

NON-KICKING FOOT PLACEMENT: the non-kicking foot must be placed slightly further back from the ball for free kicks than for regular shots, allowing the kicking foot to get through and under the ball for spin.

FOLLOW THROUGH DIRECTION: for curl, the follow through sweeps across the body in the direction of the spin. For dip, the follow through is downward after contact. For driven, the follow through is directly at the target.
READING THE WALL GAP: before striking, look at the wall and identify whether there is a gap. A wall with a gap allows a driven shot through the gap. A tight wall requires a curl around or a dip over.
CONSISTENT APPROACH: every delivery from the same position should use the same number of steps and the same approach angle. Inconsistency in the approach causes inconsistency in the delivery.
commonMistakes: Using the same technique from every position regardless of the angle and distance
Contact point drifting from the intended position (too central for curl, too low for dip)
Inconsistent approach: different step counts and approach angles from the same position
Driven free kicks going too high due to leaning back at contact
No assessment of the wall before striking — shooting on autopilot regardless of what the wall presents
corrections: Same technique everywhere: make the position label (P1-P5) and the required technique visible on a card at each position
Contact point drift: place a marker dot on each ball with tape at the correct contact point for that position — must make contact with the marker
Inconsistent approach: count steps aloud during the approach for every rep until the correct number becomes automatic
Driven shots too high: place a string 50cm above the wall height that all driven free kicks must go under
No wall assessment: require players to describe what they see in the wall (gap, tight, one side open) before striking each free kick
progressions: Add a GK who moves and dives, testing whether the delivery is accurate enough to beat a genuinely working GK
Wind compensation practice: if outdoor conditions are windy, explicitly discuss how to adjust the delivery direction and power
Live match free kick scenario: free kicks awarded naturally in a scrimmage, requiring the specialist to select and execute the appropriate technique under match conditions
regressions: Start with P3 only (slight angle, curl technique) until the curl technique is established before adding the other positions
Use a static wall (cones rather than players) to remove any wall movement variable while technique is being established
Allow unlimited time between reps to set up the approach perfectly before adding the pressure of a live GK and wall
matchApplication: Direct free kick delivery is one of the highest-value individual skills in football because it directly produces goals from dead ball situations without requiring any combination or movement from teammates. Players who have mastered the three core delivery techniques (driven, dip, curl) for specific positions around the box are a significant attacking weapon for any team. The specific contact point coaching in this drill is the most precise technical instruction in football — these are millimetre-level adjustments that produce dramatically different ball trajectories.
coachTips: This drill requires specialists. Not every player needs to master all techniques — identify the 2-3 players in your squad with the best delivery technique and invest specifically in them.
Demonstrate each technique very slowly, showing the contact point explicitly. Film the contact point from close range if possible.
The dip technique takes significantly longer to master than curl or driven. Be patient and focus on the contact point above all other coaching points.
Connect to professional free kick specialists: Ronaldo, Beckham, Juninho — discuss each players technique and which of the three delivery types they primarily used.
Practice free kick delivery as a specific individual skill in every session. Even 5 minutes of free kick work per session for one specialist produces measurable improvement within a month.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Defensive wall of 4 players shown. 5 free kick positions labeled P1-P5. For each position, the delivery technique is shown: P1 (central 20m) — straight driven arrow low to corner. P2 (central 28m) — dipping arrow over wall dropping sharply. P3 (slight angle) — curl arrow inside wall toward far post. P4 (wide right) — curl arrow around outside of wall toward near post. P5 (wide left) — mirror of P4. Contact point clocks shown for each technique: 6 oclock (driven), 12 oclock (dip), 3 oclock (curl right).

title: Goal Kick — Playing Out Under Pressure
objective: Develop the team organisation and individual technique required to play out from goal kicks under an opposition high press.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the positional shape needed to receive a goal kick under pressure
GK develops the decision-making for selecting the correct target from a goal kick
Players build the composure to receive goal kicks under pressing pressure
Teams develop 3 reliable goal kick receiving positions that beat common pressing patterns
organisation: Full pitch. GK takes goal kicks. Opposition team presses with 4-5 players positioned to intercept the most obvious goal kick targets. Defending team must use movement and positioning to create receiving options that are not covered by the press.
instructions: RECEIVING SHAPE: before every goal kick, the receiving team sets a specific shape:
2 defenders split wide (at the edge of the penalty area on each side).
1 central midfielder drops between the two CB positions (creating a 3-man receiving line in the defensive third).
1 midfielder moves to the edge of the centre circle as a longer option.
1 striker stays high as a long ball option if the press covers everything else.
PRESSING TEAM sets up trying to cover the most obvious receivers: 2 players press the wide CBs, 2 players press the central midfielder options.
GK must read which receivers are free after the press sets and play to them.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): pressing team passive (hold positions but do not sprint to intercept). GK reads the free player and delivers.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): pressing team active at 70 percent. GK must be quicker in the decision.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full intensity press. If no short option is free, GK plays long to the high striker.
coachingPoints: THE SHAPE CREATES THE OPTIONS: the receiving team creates the options through their positioning before the ball is kicked. A poorly positioned team gives the GK one option. A well-positioned team gives the GK four.
GK MUST READ THE PRESS AS THEY APPROACH THE BALL: the 4-5 seconds from when the goal kick is awarded to when the ball is kicked is the GK reading window. Use every second of it.

SHORT IS ALWAYS THE FIRST CHOICE: if a short receiver is free, the short goal kick is almost always better than a long one. A short goal kick starts possession. A long goal kick starts a 50-50 contest.

THE TRIGGER FOR THE LONG BALL: when the press successfully covers all short options, the long ball to the high striker is the correct option. A weak short pass into a pressed player simply gives the ball away.

RECEIVING PLAYERS MUST CREATE SEPARATION: receiving players should make movement (a step away from the pressing player before returning to the receiving position) to create the 1-2 steps of separation needed to receive cleanly.
FIRST TOUCH DIRECTION IS CRITICAL: the player receiving a goal kick under pressure must take their first touch away from the nearest pressing player, not into them.
COMMUNICATE THE FREE PLAYER: the outfield players who can see the press clearly should call to the GK which player is free. The GK cannot always see the full picture from ground level.
PRACTICE BOTH SHORT AND LONG: the team must be comfortable with both options. A team that can only play short gives the opposition a strategy (just press everyone short) to nullify the goal kick.
commonMistakes: The receiving shape not being set before the ball is kicked, forcing the GK to kick before players are in position
GK making the decision too late, kicking under pressure rather than reading before approaching the ball
Receiving players standing still rather than making movement to create separation from the pressing player
First touch going toward the pressing player rather than away from them
No communication from outfield players to help the GK identify the free receiver
corrections: Shape not set: require all receiving players to confirm their position (raise hand) before the GK approaches the ball
Late decision: the GK must point at their intended target before approaching the ball, committing to the decision early
Standing still: require every receiving player to make one movement (step away then return) before every goal kick
Wrong first touch direction: add a first touch direction cone for each receiver showing which way their first touch should go to escape the press
No communication: require each outfield player to call FREE or PRESSED indicating their own status before every goal kick
progressions: Increase to 6 pressing players, forcing the receiving team to find more creative ways to create free options
Remove the long ball option entirely, forcing the team to play short on every goal kick even under maximum press
Add a time pressure: if the GK takes more than 5 seconds to kick after the referee signals, possession is awarded to the opposition
regressions: Use a passive pressing team throughout, allowing the GK to practice reading and selecting without urgency
Walk through the receiving shape with all players standing in their positions before any ball is involved
Reduce the pressing team to 2 players, making it easier to find a free receiver while the habit is established
matchApplication: Playing out from goal kicks is a defining aspect of modern football. Teams that can build attacks from goal kicks maintain possession more effectively, reduce the number of 50-50 aerial contests they face, and control the rhythm of the match. The specific challenge in this drill — reading a pressing system and finding the free player before the press arrives — replicates exactly the decision-making demands that GKs and receiving players face in matches against teams with organised pressing systems.
coachTips: Walk through the receiving shape explicitly before any practice begins, physically placing players in their positions.
The GK reading coaching point is the most important. Give the GK a specific pre-kick routine: look left, look right, identify the free player, then approach.
Film from above during Phase 3 if possible. The press coverage and free player identification are clearest from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional examples: discuss how specific teams (Man City, Liverpool) organise their goal kick receiving shape to beat the press.
Run this drill regularly — the press-reading and receiving shape habits take many sessions to become automatic.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. GK shown in 6-yard box. Receiving shape shown: 2 defenders wide (at edge of penalty area), central midfielder between CB positions, midfielder at edge of centre circle, striker high. Pressing team shown in press positions covering the most obvious receivers: 2 pressing wide defenders, 2 pressing central options. FREE player highlighted where the press has not covered (in this case the midfielder at the centre circle). GK decision arrow shown going to the free player. Long ball arrow shown as the alternative to the high striker.

title: Goal Kick — Short Passing Routine for Young Players
objective: Teach young players how to play short from goal kicks, giving the team a reliable way to start attacks from dead ball situations.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the concept of playing short from a goal kick rather than always kicking long
GK develops basic confidence playing short with their feet from a goal kick
Defenders and midfielders learn how to position to receive short goal kicks
Players experience the satisfaction of building from the back as a team
organisation: Defensive third with goal. GK takes goal kicks and plays short to teammates who have moved to receive. No opposition in phase 1. Small number of passive opponents in phase 2.
instructions: SETUP: one defender stands 10m from the GK on the left. A second defender stands 10m from the GK on the right. One midfielder stands 20m from the GK centrally.

PHASE 1 (5 minutes): GK plays a short goal kick to either wide defender. Defender receives and passes to the central midfielder. The sequence is: GK kicks short, defender receives, midfielder receives. Simple build.
After 5 successful sequences, add a slightly longer option: midfielder can now play forward to a target player at 30m.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): add 1 passive opponent who stands in the middle and cannot move. GK must play away from the opponent.
COACH INSTRUCTION THROUGHOUT: the most important habit is that the player receiving the goal kick is calm and unhurried. Tell them TIME before every receipt to reinforce confidence.
CELEBRATION: celebrate every successful build from a goal kick as a team achievement.
coachingPoints: GOING SHORT IS BRAVE AND SMART: in youth football, most teams always kick long from goal kicks. Playing short is a sign of confidence and intelligence. It is a much harder skill than kicking long but it produces much better results.

THE GK MUST PLACE THE BALL CAREFULLY: a goal kick played short must be accurate. An inaccurate short goal kick into a covered area gives the ball away immediately. Accuracy is more important than pace.

RECEIVING PLAYERS MUST SHOW CLEARLY: the player receiving the short goal kick must give the GK a clear target by showing their body and moving toward the ball. A passive receiver standing still is hard to pass to.
FIRST TOUCH AWAY FROM PRESSURE: the first touch on a short goal kick must go away from any nearby opponents. Even if no opponents are near in phase 1, practice the habit of touching away.
RELAX AND ENJOY IT: playing short from goal kicks can feel risky but it is actually safer than kicking long if done correctly. Enjoy the feeling of building from the back.
BOTH SIDES EQUALLY: practice the short goal kick to both the left defender and the right defender equally. Both sides must be comfortable.
ONE VOICE: the GK should hear one clear instruction from the coach before each goal kick (WIDE LEFT, WIDE RIGHT, or CENTRAL). This gives the GK a direction to play.
EVERYONE MOVES: every player on the team should be moving before the goal kick is taken, creating options and making themselves available.
commonMistakes: GK playing the goal kick to a player who is standing still and not showing clearly
First touch of the receiving player going toward the centre rather than away from pressure
Players not moving before the goal kick is taken, limiting the GK options
Receiving player rushing and playing the next pass too quickly
GK kicking long out of habit even when short options are available
corrections: Standing still receiver: require the receiving player to take 2 steps toward the GK before the goal kick is taken
Wrong first touch direction: place a directional cone beside each receiving position showing which way the first touch should go
Not moving: the drill cannot start until every outfield player is moving — GK holds the ball until they see movement
Rushing next pass: require a 2-second hold after the first touch before the next pass is played
GK kicking long: the coach calls SHORT before every goal kick in phase 1 as a constant reminder
progressions: Add a third passing option (a wide midfielder 25m from goal) to give the build a more natural look
Increase passive opponents to 2 in phase 2 before removing the passive restriction entirely
Link to a shooting opportunity at the end of the build sequence to give the routine a clear attacking purpose
regressions: Allow the GK to roll the ball rather than kick it for receiving players very close (5m) until basic confidence is established
Use a junior goal to reduce the goalkeeper distance and make the short pass more accessible
Walk through the positions at slow pace with no ball before any goal kicks are taken
matchApplication: Playing short from goal kicks is one of the most effective team habits a young side can develop. It teaches composure, ball control under no pressure, and the building-from-the-back philosophy that the best teams in the world use at every level. Introducing it at U10 — when there is no opposition pressure in phase 1 — gives players the safe environment to learn and practice the habit before it is needed in competitive matches. Teams that play short from goal kicks consistently develop a more composed, possession-based style that serves them well throughout their development.
coachTips: This is fundamentally a confidence-building exercise at U10. Keep the environment positive and encouraging throughout.
The TIME call before every receipt is the most important coaching tool in this session.
Walk among the players during the drill giving individual encouragement to each receiving player.
Connect to professional teams: show a clip of a team building from a short goal kick and ask players to identify who moved where.
Celebrate the team achievement of a completed build sequence as enthusiastically as you would celebrate a goal.
diagramDescription: Defensive third with junior or full-size goal. GK shown in 6-yard box about to take goal kick. Short goal kick arrow shown going to the wide left defender (10m from goal). Defender shown with first touch arrow going away from centre. Pass arrow from defender to central midfielder (20m from goal). Midfielder shown receiving with pass arrow forward to a target player at 30m. Phase 2 shows a passive opponent in the centre zone. Both wide options shown (left and right) with equal emphasis.

title: Penalty Shoot-Out Simulation
objective: Develop the ability to perform in a penalty shoot-out through systematic pressure simulation that builds composure and routine under maximum stress.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain their pre-kick routine under genuine competitive and emotional pressure
Players build the specific composure needed for a penalty in a shoot-out format
Players experience the psychological demands of a shoot-out in a safe training environment
Teams develop a planned shoot-out order and a collective mental approach
equipment: Scoreboard or paper to track results
organisation: Full shoot-out simulation with squad divided into two teams. Each player takes a penalty in turn. Teams alternate. First team to 5 saves wins each round. Multiple rounds allow every player to experience both scoring and missing situations. Post-simulation debrief is as important as the practice itself.
instructions: SETUP: divide the squad into two teams of equal size. Designate a shooting order for each team.
SIMULATION FORMAT: alternating penalties as in a real shoot-out. Team 1 Player 1 shoots, Team 2 Player 1 shoots, etc.
GK ROTATION: the GK alternates between teams, facing 5 penalties per round.
PRESSURE LAYERS added across rounds:
ROUND 1: standard shoot-out. Both teams take 5 penalties each. Track the score.
ROUND 2: add a team consequence — the losing team completes 20 press-ups after the round.
ROUND 3: individual pressure — before each penalty, the player must perform 10 squat jumps (replicating physical fatigue), then walk to the spot.
ROUND 4: audience pressure — remaining squad members stand along the penalty area watching and encouraged to be vocal.
ROUND 5: sudden death format — one player per team until a winner is found.
After each round: 2-minute team debrief. What worked? What broke down? What will each player do differently next round?
coachingPoints: THE ROUTINE MUST SURVIVE PRESSURE: the pre-kick routine established in regular penalty training (place ball, steps back, deep breath, pick corner, commit) must work exactly the same in Round 5 of a shoot-out as in training. If the routine changes under pressure, the player has not yet internalised it.

SEPARATE THE THINKING FROM THE DOING: the decision (which corner) must be made before the player stands over the ball. Standing over the ball is not the time to think. The approach and strike are purely physical execution of a pre-made decision.

RESET COMPLETELY BETWEEN PENALTIES: whether the previous penalty was scored or missed, the mental state must be completely reset before the next one. Past penalties are irrelevant. Only the next one matters.
THE GK IS TRYING TO DISTRACT YOU: the GK moving, waving, and making noise before the kick is deliberate. Acknowledge it and ignore it. They cannot stop a well-struck penalty placed to the corner.

POSITIVE BODY LANGUAGE THROUGHOUT: how a player walks to the spot, how they place the ball, and how they hold themselves all affect their own psychology. Walk confidently, place the ball deliberately, stand tall.

TEAM PENALTIES ARE WON BEFORE THEY ARE TAKEN: the team with the better collective preparation, clearer individual routines, and stronger mental resilience almost always wins a shoot-out. Physical and technical quality differences between teams at this stage are minimal — mentality decides it.

THE MISSED PENALTY IS NOT THE PROBLEM: missing a penalty in a shoot-out is not a failure — it is a statistical event. The problem is allowing a miss to affect the next taker. Teams must protect each other from the contagion of a miss.
KNOW YOUR ORDER BEFORE IT HAPPENS: every player should know exactly when they will shoot in the order before a competitive situation arises. Uncertainty about order creates additional anxiety.
