commonMistakes: Routine changing significantly between training penalties and shoot-out penalties due to pressure
Decision about which corner changing at the last moment when the GK moves
Physical fatigue from the squat jumps affecting the approach and technique in round 3
Players showing negative body language after missing (head down, slumped shoulders) affecting subsequent takers
The team not having an agreed shoot-out order, causing confusion about who shoots when
corrections: Routine changing: require players to narrate their routine aloud before every round 2+ penalty, reinforcing the steps
Changing decision: require players to whisper their chosen corner to the coach before beginning their approach. Any change from that corner is a penalty that does not count
Physical fatigue affecting technique: specifically practice the squat jump then penalty sequence in isolated sessions before the simulation

Negative body language: introduce a team rule that after any missed penalty, the taker must walk back with head up and be greeted positively by their teammates. The entire team is responsible for this.
No agreed order: create a written shoot-out order at the start of the simulation and stick to it exactly throughout all rounds
progressions: Blind shoot-out: the GK is told which corner by the coach just before the kick (to save it consistently) forcing takers to deal with saves in every round
Media pressure simulation: add a camera (phone) recording and reviewing each taker as they approach, adding an additional awareness layer
Team change: the shoot-out order is revealed only 30 seconds before the round begins, preventing over-preparation
regressions: Remove all consequences (rounds 2-4 pressure elements) for the full first simulation until players have completed one full practice shoot-out in a neutral environment
Reduce to 3 penalties per team per round to make the format more manageable
Allow players to choose their own position in the order to reduce the anxiety associated with specific order positions
matchApplication: Penalty shoot-outs are one of the most psychologically demanding situations in football. Research consistently shows that teams that practice shoot-outs regularly under simulated pressure convert a significantly higher proportion than teams that only practice penalties in calm settings. The progressive pressure system in this drill (team consequence, physical fatigue, audience pressure, sudden death) systematically builds the composure architecture that allows players to execute their trained technique even when the stakes are highest. At U15, players are entering the age range where competitive shoot-outs become common — this preparation can directly determine results.
coachTips: The post-round debrief (2 minutes after each round) is as important as the shooting itself. Use it to reinforce the routine and the mental framework.
Create the team culture around missed penalties before the shoot-out simulation begins — missing is normal and the team response to it determines the outcome more than the miss itself.
Track conversion rates across all 5 rounds and across multiple sessions. Improvement in pressured penalty conversion rates is motivating and measurable.
Connect the routine explicitly: what did players do with their technique in rounds 4 and 5 that was different from round 1? Were they still following the same routine?
Film round 4 or 5 and watch it back together as a team — the body language patterns visible on film are often surprising to the players themselves.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. Two teams shown alternating (Team 1: circles, Team 2: triangles). Penalty spot shown with approaching player. Shooting order lists shown for both teams. Pressure layers labeled on the side: Round 1 (standard), Round 2 (team consequence), Round 3 (squat jumps before kick — player shown doing them), Round 4 (audience watching), Round 5 (sudden death). Scoreboard shown tracking results. Debrief moment shown between rounds with team huddle. Routine steps shown: place ball, steps back, breathe, decide, commit.

title: Throw-In — Quick Restart Routines
objective: Teach players how to take and receive throw-ins quickly to maintain momentum and catch opponents unprepared.
learningOutcomes: Players can take a correctly executed throw-in without fouling
Players develop the habit of looking for the quick throw before opponents organise
Players learn simple 2-player receiving patterns for throw-ins in different zones
Players build the awareness that a throw-in is an attacking opportunity not just a restart
players: Groups of 3-4 (multiple groups across both touchlines)
pitchSize: Both touchlines of the pitch across all thirds
organisation: Pairs and groups of 3 practice throw-in routines from different zones: defensive third, midfield, attacking third. Each zone has a different priority (safety, build-up, attack).
instructions: THROW-IN TECHNIQUE REVIEW (2 minutes): both feet on or behind the line, ball delivered from behind the head with both hands, continuous motion.

ZONE 1 — DEFENSIVE THIRD THROW-IN (4 minutes): priority is safety. Player A takes the throw. Player B offers short (3m, safe). Player C offers longer but potentially under pressure. The throw goes to whoever is clearly safer. In the defensive third, never throw into a crowded area.

ZONE 2 — MIDFIELD THROW-IN (5 minutes): priority is progression. Player A takes the throw. Player B moves away from the line to create space, then turns and comes short. The quick throw to B who immediately plays forward is the target sequence.

ZONE 3 — ATTACKING THIRD THROW-IN (4 minutes): priority is danger. Player A takes the throw. Player B stands at the near post. Player A delivers a flat throw aimed for B to flick on or shoot. This is a mini set piece with a clear attacking intent.
Throughout: emphasise the QUICK THROW concept. If the receiver is free and close, take the throw immediately before opponents organise.
coachingPoints: QUICK IS OFTEN BEST: the most dangerous throw-in is the one taken before the opposition can organise their defence. Look immediately and throw if a teammate is free.
ZONE DETERMINES THE PRIORITY: in your defensive third, safety first. In midfield, progress forward. In the attacking third, treat it like a mini set piece.
BOTH FEET MUST STAY ON OR BEHIND THE LINE: a foul throw gives possession to the opposition. Make sure both feet are in contact with the ground on or behind the line throughout the throw.
THE THROW MUST COME FROM BEHIND THE HEAD: both hands must release the ball from behind the head in a continuous motion. Releasing from in front of the head or from one side is a foul throw.
THE RECEIVER MUST MOVE: a stationary receiver is easier to mark. Even a small movement (2-3 steps away then back) creates enough separation to receive cleanly.
THROW TO WHERE THE PLAYER IS GOING, NOT WHERE THEY ARE: like any pass, the throw should lead the receiver into space rather than to where they currently are.
PROTECT THE THROW IN TIGHT AREAS: in the defensive third, throw to a player who is facing the throw (not with their back to it) so the first touch can be controlled away from pressure.
CELEBRATE THE QUICK THROW THAT CATCHES OPPONENTS: the quick throw that exploits a gap in the opposition before they organise is one of the most satisfying actions in football. Celebrate it.
commonMistakes: Both feet not grounded during the throw, causing a foul throw and giving possession away
Releasing the ball from in front of the head rather than from behind
Receiver standing still, making themselves easy to mark
Throwing into a crowded area in the defensive third, risking losing possession dangerously
Never attempting the quick throw, always waiting and allowing the opposition to organise
corrections: Feet off ground: place tape on the ground at the line. Players must keep both feet behind or on the tape throughout the throw
Front-of-head release: use a throw target (a cone on a pole at head height) that forces the player to keep the ball behind their head until past the target
Receiver standing still: require the receiver to complete 2 steps before stopping to receive every throw
Throwing into crowds defensively: make it a rule in Zone 1 that the throw cannot go to a player with an opponent within 2m of them
Never quick throw: celebrate and award a bonus point for every quick throw taken within 3 seconds of the ball going out of play
progressions: Combine the throw-in with the next action: receiver controls and immediately passes to a third player who shoots or plays forward
Long throw introduction: identify any player with natural throwing power and give them 5 minutes of specific long throw practice for Zone 3
Add a defender: one defender presses the throw-taker, forcing the quick decision of which receiver to use
regressions: Remove Zone 3 (attacking throw-in) and focus only on Zones 1 and 2 for the full session
Allow the receiver to stand completely still in the early phase before introducing the movement requirement
Walk through each zone separately (one zone per session) before combining all three
matchApplication: Throw-ins are the most common set piece in football — a team typically has 20-30 throw-ins per match — yet they are almost universally neglected in training. Teams that have clear, practiced throw-in principles for each zone of the pitch gain significant possession advantages over teams that simply get to the ball and throw it without thought. The quick throw concept in particular is one of the highest-percentage attacking moments in football: a free player close to the touchline who receives a quick throw before the opposition organises can often advance 10-15m with the ball before any defensive pressure arrives.
coachTips: The quick throw concept is the most important coaching point in this drill and worth the most emphasis.
Walk along the touchlines during the drill giving individual encouragement and specifically celebrating every quick throw.
Film some throw-ins from a match and watch them back with the team — the opportunities for quick throws that were missed will be immediately obvious.
The foul throw correction is important for U11 — it is a common error at this age. Be patient and correct it every time gently.
Connect to the match context: discuss how many throw-ins your team had in the last match and how many were taken quickly versus allowing the opposition to organise.
diagramDescription: Three zones along the touchline labeled DEFENSIVE THIRD, MIDFIELD, ATTACKING THIRD. For each zone: thrower (A) shown at the line in correct throw-in stance (both feet behind line, ball behind head). ZONE 1 shows two receivers (B near, C slightly further) with safety priority arrow to B. ZONE 2 shows receiver B making a movement (step away then show for ball) with progression arrow. ZONE 3 shows receiver B at near post with throw-to-attack arrow. QUICK THROW timer shown at each zone (3 seconds).

title: Heading Foundations — Neck Muscles and Forehead
objective: Introduce the basic heading technique to young players using soft balls and short distances, building confidence and correct contact point.
learningOutcomes: Players can make deliberate contact with the ball using the forehead
Players understand the difference between heading with the forehead versus the top of the head
Players develop the neck muscle engagement needed for a safe, controlled header
Players overcome any initial fear of heading the ball
equipment: 1 soft ball or size 3 football per pair
organisation: Pairs standing 2m apart. Player A holds the ball in both hands at head height and releases it toward Player B who heads it back. The short distance and hand-delivered ball removes fear and allows technique focus. Progress to thrown serves and then moving headers.
instructions: Player A holds the ball at head height and pushes it gently toward Player Bs forehead.
Player B keeps their eyes open, chin slightly tucked, and contacts the ball with the flat part of their forehead.
After 5 successful contacts from standing: A gently tosses the ball underarm and B heads it back.
After 5 successful tosses: B takes one step forward into the header (the beginning of attacking heading).
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): hand-pushed delivery. Focus purely on forehead contact and eyes open.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): gentle underarm toss from 2m. Player B heads back to A hands.
PHASE 3 (4 minutes): gentle toss from 3m. Player B heads to a cone target 2m behind A.
Throughout: use a soft training ball. No competition, no pressure. Every attempt is celebrated.
coachingPoints: THE FOREHEAD IS THE CORRECT CONTACT POINT: the flat part of the forehead between the hairline and the eyebrows. Not the top of the head (painful), not the nose (dangerous), not the temple (dangerous).
EYES OPEN THROUGH CONTACT: this is the hardest habit to build and the most important. Closing the eyes before contact is natural but must be resisted. Watch the ball all the way to the forehead.
NECK MUSCLES ENGAGED: tighten the neck muscles before the ball arrives. This stabilises the head and transfers the energy of the serve into the header rather than allowing the head to move backward.
CHIN SLIGHTLY TUCKED: a slightly tucked chin automatically puts the forehead in the correct position to be the contact point. Head tilted back means the top of the head becomes the contact point.
ATTACK THE BALL: even at U9, the habit of going toward the ball rather than letting it come to you should begin. A small step forward into the header reinforces this.
NO FEAR: heading a football with the forehead when the neck muscles are engaged is completely safe and painless. Build confidence through soft balls and gentle delivery.
BOTH SIDES OF THE FOREHEAD: most players automatically favour one side. Practice headers that require using both the left and right side of the forehead.
CELEBRATE EVERY ATTEMPT: at U9 any deliberate attempt to head the ball with the forehead is a success worth celebrating.
commonMistakes: Eyes closing before or at the moment of contact
Contacting with the top of the head rather than the forehead, causing discomfort
Neck muscles not engaged, causing the head to move backward on contact
Leaning backward away from the ball rather than meeting it
Fear or reluctance making attempts hesitant and technically weak
corrections: Eyes closing: ask the player to call out the colour of a spot marked on the ball at the moment of contact — forces eyes open
Top of head contact: place one finger on the correct contact spot (forehead) before each rep as a tactile target
Neck not engaged: ask player to say HMM through the contact (engaging the jaw and neck muscles together)
Leaning back: place a cone target 1m in front of the player — they must make the header go toward the cone (forward) not backward
Fear: reduce ball size to a light foam ball for 3 minutes, rebuild confidence, then return to the soft football
progressions: Increase serve distance to 4m then 5m as confidence builds
Add a direction element: A holds up left or right hand and B must head the ball to that side
Partner count challenge: how many consecutive headers can the pair complete without the ball hitting the ground
regressions: Use a balloon for the first 2 minutes for very hesitant players — the slow movement gives maximum time to build confidence
Allow the player to hold the ball themselves and simply push it gently off their own forehead first
Stand side by side (not facing) so the ball arrives from the side, which some players find less threatening
matchApplication: Heading technique introduced at the correct age with a soft ball and gentle delivery is completely safe and builds a foundational skill that becomes increasingly important in football from U11 upward. The forehead contact point, neck muscle engagement, and eyes-open habits established at U9 in a fun, positive environment create the technical base that is very difficult to correct later if wrong habits are established first. This drill prioritises confidence and correct technique above all else.
coachTips: Use a soft training ball throughout. Never use a full-size adult ball for heading work at U9.
Your own demeanour is the most important coaching tool here. Approach heading with enthusiasm and zero anxiety and players will mirror that.
Heading training for U9 should be brief (10-15 minutes maximum) and always positive.
Check with your club or association about any specific guidelines on heading for younger age groups.
Celebrate every single attempt, regardless of outcome. The willingness to try is the achievement at this age.
diagramDescription: Pairs 2m apart. Player A shown holding ball at head height. Player B shown with correct heading posture: eyes open, chin tucked, forehead as contact point (highlighted), neck muscles engaged. Contact arrow shows ball meeting forehead. Three phase progressions shown: push delivery, gentle toss, toss with direction target. Celebrate icon shown after every attempt.

title: Heading in Combination — Jump and Direct
objective: Develop the jumping header and directed heading technique used to win aerial balls and score from crosses.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a jumping header from a served cross with correct take-off and contact
Players can direct headers accurately to a target zone
Players develop the physical confidence to compete for aerial balls
Players understand the biomechanics of generating power in a jumping header
players: Groups of 3-4 (server, header, target plus optional marker)
organisation: Server stands 8m to the side and delivers lofted balls at head height. Header player attacks the served ball and directs it to a target zone. Optional defensive marker added in Phase 3.
instructions: Server delivers a lofted ball that peaks at 2-3m height, arriving at the heading position.
Header player starts 3m from the landing zone and attacks the ball.
JUMP TECHNIQUE: take off from one foot (the foot on the side the ball is arriving from). Drive the opposite knee upward for elevation. Strike the ball at the peak of the jump.
DIRECTION TECHNIQUE: to head the ball left, contact the right side of the forehead and twist the neck slightly left. To head right, contact the left side of the forehead.
ATTACK THE BALL: the header must move toward the ball rather than waiting for it. A passive header loses height and power.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): stationary position, no jump. Headers from a standing position to establish direction control.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): full jump technique. Server delivers, player attacks the ball and directs to one of two target zones.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): add a passive marker standing behind the header. Player must create separation before jumping.
coachingPoints: ONE-FOOT TAKE-OFF GENERATES MORE ELEVATION: taking off from one foot is significantly faster and produces more height than taking off from two feet. Drive off the foot closest to the ball.
KNEE DRIVE ADDS ELEVATION: the knee drive of the non-jumping leg adds substantial elevation to the jump. Drive the knee up forcefully at take-off.

ATTACK THE BALL AT THE PEAK OF YOUR JUMP: timing the jump to contact the ball at the highest point of the jump requires practice. Too early means the ball has not arrived. Too late means you are already descending.
NECK MUSCLES CREATE POWER: in a jumping header, the snapping action of the neck forward at the moment of contact generates significant additional power. Think of the head as a hammer.
EYES ON THE BALL FROM SERVE TO CONTACT: track the ball from the moment it leaves the server hands. Any break in focus makes timing the jump significantly harder.

THE HEADER ACROSS GOAL IS MOST DANGEROUS: heading the ball back toward where it came from is easy to defend. Heading across the face of the goal (from a left cross, head right and vice versa) is the most dangerous and most skillful heading direction.
LAND SAFELY: after a jumping header, land on both feet with bent knees to absorb the impact. Do not land stiff-legged.
BODY POSITION IN THE AIR: the body arches backward slightly before contact, then snaps forward at the moment of contact. This arch-and-snap creates power.
commonMistakes: Taking off from two feet, reducing elevation and speed of jump
Not driving the knee upward, missing the additional elevation it provides
Jumping too early, reaching the peak before the ball arrives and heading downward
Not directing the header, always heading directly forward regardless of the ball position
Landing stiff-legged after the header, creating unnecessary impact
corrections: Two-foot takeoff: place a foot marker on the ground. Only one foot may touch the marker before take-off
No knee drive: place a horizontal bar (a cone on a stick) at chest height. The jumping knee must clear the bar at take-off
Jumping too early: server calls NOW at the correct take-off moment, training the timing to the serve
No direction: add two target zones (left and right of goal) and require alternating direction on every header
Stiff landing: place a soft mat or folded jumpers at the landing spot. Players must land bent-kneed to land softly on the mat
progressions: Live defensive header: passive marker becomes active and can jump to compete for the ball
Competitive format: server scores a point for every header not directed on target. Header player scores for every accurate direction
Full crossing and heading session: wide player delivers, header attacks, GK defends
regressions: Remove the jump entirely for Phase 1 and 2: all headers from standing position until direction control is established
Reduce the serve height and distance so the header has more time to judge the delivery
Use a softer ball to remove any discomfort concern while the jumping technique is being established
matchApplication: The jumping header is one of the most physically demanding technical skills in football, requiring the coordination of a timed jump, correct take-off mechanics, aerial body control, and precise contact direction — all in under 1 second. Players who develop jumping header technique at U12 have a significant physical advantage in aerial contests that grows in importance as they progress to senior football where aerial duels are more frequent and more physically contested.
coachTips: Film the jumping header from the side to show players their own take-off mechanics and peak height.
The arch-and-snap coaching point is best demonstrated by the coach performing the motion without a ball first.
Be patient with the timing — it genuinely takes many repetitions before the jump-to-contact timing becomes reliable.
Connect to professional heading: show Cristiano Ronaldo, Tim Cahill, or Peter Crouch heading at their peak and identify the take-off foot, knee drive, and contact point.
This is physically tiring. Keep sets short (5-6 jumps) with adequate rest between.
diagramDescription: 15m area. Server shown 8m to the side delivering a lofted ball. Header player shown in jump sequence: take-off from near foot, knee drive upward, body arch, forehead contact at peak of jump, follow-through with neck snap. Two target zones shown: LEFT (across goal) and FAR RIGHT (wide target). Direction arrows from forehead contact point showing how neck position determines ball direction. Landing position shown: both feet, bent knees. Passive marker shown behind the header player in Phase 3.

title: Power and Accuracy Shooting — Senior Standard
objective: Develop the full range of shooting techniques required at senior level: driven, placed, volley, and first-time shooting from different positions.
learningOutcomes: Players can select and execute the correct shooting technique based on position and ball delivery
Players develop consistency in shooting technique under realistic pressure
Players build the mental composure required for shooting in high-stakes moments
Players understand the tactical decision between power and placement in different situations
players: Groups of 4-6 (shooter, server, GK, optional defender)
pitchSize: Full penalty area and approach zones
organisation: Full-size goal with GK. Multiple shooting positions and delivery types. Shooters rotate through positions and delivery types across the session. Emphasis on technique selection and execution quality rather than volume.
instructions: SHOOTING MATRIX — 5 positions and techniques:
POSITION 1 (central 20m, still ball): driven laces technique. Maximum power. Low trajectory. Test: must reach the back of the net from 20m in under 0.5 seconds.
POSITION 2 (central 22m, rolled delivery): first-time placed shot. Inside of foot. Low corner. GK must move to save.
POSITION 3 (right channel 18m, crossed ball): side volley. Hip rotation for power. Target: far post low.
POSITION 4 (edge of box, through ball): 1-touch finish into either corner based on GK position.
POSITION 5 (penalty spot, static): pick the corner and execute. No power required — placement is the only variable.
3 attempts from each position per shooter per round. 2 rounds minimum.
GK is fully live throughout — no passive GKs at senior level.
After every shot: immediate technical feedback from the coach on one specific element.
coachingPoints: TECHNIQUE SELECTION IS THE FIRST DECISION: before the ball arrives, the shooter should already know which technique they are using. Deciding during the shot causes hesitation and poor contact.

POWER AND PLACEMENT ARE NOT THE SAME DECISION: from Position 1 (still ball, 20m), power is the primary variable — the GK has minimal reaction time. From Position 2 (rolled delivery), placement is primary — a placed ball to the corner is more effective than a powerful central shot.

FIRST-TIME SHOOTING IS AN ELITE SKILL: taking a shot first time (Position 2 and 4) requires the body to be pre-positioned before the ball arrives. The non-kicking foot must be set and the body balanced before the ball reaches you.
SIDE VOLLEY HIP ROTATION: in the side volley (Position 3), all the power comes from hip rotation. The leg swing is just the delivery mechanism. Rotate explosively through the ball.

GK POSITION DETERMINES THE CORNER: in every shooting situation, a brief glance at the GK before or during the approach reveals their position. Shoot to the corner furthest from where they are standing.
COMPOSURE IS A TECHNICAL SKILL: the physical composure to slow down slightly, set the body correctly, and place the shot is as much a technique as the kicking motion itself. Practice it deliberately.
FOLLOW THROUGH MATTERS AT SENIOR LEVEL: at U17 and above, the follow-through direction controls the final shot direction more than the initial foot position. Follow through toward the target corner.
VARY YOUR SHOOTING: a GK who has seen you shoot 10 times from Position 2 knows your pattern. Vary the corner selection unpredictably.
commonMistakes: Using the same technique regardless of the shooting position and delivery type
Deciding too late which corner to shoot into, causing hesitation during the approach
Side volley contact too low, sending the shot over the bar
First-time shots not pre-positioning the body, requiring an adjustment touch
Shooting centrally under pressure rather than committing to a corner
corrections: Same technique everywhere: before each rep the coach calls the required technique. After 2 rounds of coached technique, players choose independently
Late corner decision: require players to call LEFT or RIGHT before each shot — any shot that does not match the call does not count
Side volley over the bar: place a string 1.5m above the ground across the goal area — all volleys must go under it
Not pre-positioning: freeze the drill at the moment the ball is played and check the shooters body position before the ball arrives
Shooting centrally: award zero points for any central shot (between the posts but not within 1m of either post)
progressions: Add a recovering defender who starts 3m behind the shooter — adds urgency and defensive pressure
Reduce the shooting time limit to 1 touch only across all positions
Competition format: 5-position shootout. Score tracked across both rounds. Coach evaluates technique points as well as goals
regressions: Remove the GK and practice technique against the open goal before adding the GK challenge
Slow the delivery pace for Positions 2 and 4 to give more time for pre-positioning
Allow 2 touches at Positions 2 and 4 before reducing to 1-touch
matchApplication: At U17 and senior level, shooting quality is often the difference between a good player and an excellent one. The ability to select the correct technique based on the position and delivery, execute it under pressure from a live GK, and do so consistently across a session is a high-level skill that develops over many years of specific practice. This drill specifically addresses the decision-making element of shooting (which technique, which corner, power or placement) alongside the technical execution, because at senior level both must be automatic.
coachTips: This session requires a high level of individual feedback. Keep group sizes small (4-6) so each player gets meaningful coaching on every rep.
Film every shooting session from behind the goal. The corner selection, contact point, and trajectory are all clearest from this angle.
Senior players respond well to data: track goals per attempt across the session and share the numbers. Competition within the group raises the quality of every rep.
Connect to specific match situations your squad has faced: discuss a recent shooting opportunity that was missed and apply the relevant coaching point from the session.
This drill should be a regular fixture in senior technical sessions — shooting quality declines without specific practice.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with GK. 5 shooting positions labeled P1-P5 with delivery types shown. P1 (central 20m, stationary ball with driven shot arrow). P2 (central 22m, ball rolled by server with 1-touch inside-foot arrow). P3 (right channel 18m, crossed ball with side volley hip rotation diagram). P4 (edge of box, through ball with 1-touch arrow to corner). P5 (penalty spot with corner selection arrows). GK shown fully active. 0.5-second speed indicator shown for P1. Corner placement arrows shown for each position.

title: Ball Striking for Adults — Precision Under Match Conditions
objective: Develop and maintain precise ball-striking technique in adult players through match-realistic scenarios that combine decision-making with technical execution.
learningOutcomes: Adult players maintain and sharpen their shooting technique across a range of match scenarios
Players develop the consistency of technique that comes from deliberate regular practice
Players address individual technical weaknesses identified through session assessment
Players build the mental composure to convert chances in adult competitive football
pitchSize: Full penalty area and surrounding areas
organisation: Full-size goal with live GK. Session structured around 3 phases: individual technique assessment, targeted improvement work, and match-realistic shooting scenarios.
instructions: PHASE 1 — ASSESSMENT (5 minutes): each player takes 5 shots from Position 1 (central 20m, driven) and 5 from Position 2 (right channel 16m, placed). Coach observes and identifies the primary technical issue for each player.
Common adult technical issues: over-hitting with poor accuracy, too much side-foot with insufficient power, approach angle too straight for driven shots, non-kicking foot too far from ball.
PHASE 2 — TARGETED WORK (10 minutes): players work on their individually identified issue. Coach circulates giving 1-2 specific coaching points to each player in rotation.
PHASE 3 — MATCH SCENARIOS (10 minutes): four match-realistic shooting scenarios run in rotation:
SCENARIO A: ball played from midfield, player controls and shoots within 2 touches from 18m.
SCENARIO B: wide cross delivered from right. Player meets it at the far post with a side-foot or volley.
SCENARIO C: through ball in behind. Player runs onto it and shoots first time from 15m.
SCENARIO D: 1v1 with GK from 12m. Player must beat the GK with a composed finish.
3 reps per player per scenario. Score tracked. GK live throughout.
coachingPoints: TECHNIQUE DOES NOT MAINTAIN ITSELF: adult players who do not specifically practice shooting technique see measurable decline over a season. This session exists to maintain and improve the technical quality that match play alone cannot sustain.

IDENTIFY YOUR DOMINANT ERROR: most adult players have one primary shooting error that accounts for the majority of their missed chances. Identifying it and specifically practicing the correction produces the fastest improvement.

MATCH SCENARIOS REQUIRE MATCH SPEED: Phase 3 must be performed at full match intensity. Slowing down to get the technique right in a match scenario defeats the purpose. If the technique does not hold up at match speed, slow back down in Phase 2 and rebuild.
THE GK LIVE IN PHASE 3 IS ESSENTIAL: a passive GK in Phase 3 does not create the decision-making pressure that exists in a match. Shoot against a live, motivated GK.

COMPOSURE IN SCENARIO D: the 1v1 with the GK (Scenario D) is the most frequently missed chance in adult football. Composure — slowing slightly, setting the body, placing the shot — is the correct technical response. Not power.

CONSISTENT APPROACH IS THE FOUNDATION: adult players who approach shooting positions inconsistently (different step counts, different angles) produce inconsistent results. Identify and practice your consistent approach for each scenario.

TECHNICAL FEEDBACK IS SPECIFIC: vague feedback (shoot better, more accurate) is not useful. Specific technical feedback (your non-kicking foot is 30cm too far from the ball, move it 15cm closer) is directly actionable.
QUALITY NOT QUANTITY: 5 shots with full technical attention produce more improvement than 20 shots without focus.
commonMistakes: Adult habit of using the same technique for every shooting situation regardless of position or delivery
Rushing shots in match scenarios without the technical pre-positioning required for accuracy
Not applying individually identified technical corrections during Phase 3 (returning to default habits)
GK not being fully competitive in Phase 3, removing the decision-making pressure that makes the scenario realistic
Insufficient rest between shooting reps, causing physical fatigue to reduce technical quality
corrections: Same technique everywhere: before every Phase 3 rep, the coach calls the required technique type so the player must consciously select
Rushing Phase 3: introduce a mandatory body-set moment (0.5 seconds where the player sets their non-kicking foot before shooting) in all Phase 3 scenarios
Returning to default habits: assign each player a partner who watches specifically for their identified error and calls it immediately if it appears
Passive GK: GK must call which corner they are covering before every Phase 3 shot (adds mental pressure for the shooter)
Insufficient rest: enforce minimum 30-second rest between every Phase 3 scenario rep
progressions: Add a second defender who closes from behind in Scenario A and C, increasing urgency
Reduce touch allowance to 1-touch only in Scenario A
Video analysis round: film 5 shots from each player across Phase 2 and 3, review the footage as a group identifying technique vs outcome
regressions: Allow 3 touches in Scenario A and B for adult players returning from injury or long absence
Remove the GK from Phase 1 assessment entirely to allow pure technique evaluation without outcome pressure
Reduce Scenario D (1v1) to a static GK standing to one side of the goal, creating an obvious target until composure is established
matchApplication: Adult players in grassroots football almost universally receive less specific technical coaching than they did as youth players, yet technical skills require deliberate practice to maintain and improve. The shooting ability that adult players show in matches is the result of technical habits — good and bad — that were established years ago and have reinforced themselves through repetition. This session specifically interrupts poor technical habits through identification, targeted correction, and match-realistic practice. The assessment-first approach is the most important element: knowing exactly what each player needs to work on produces far more improvement per session than generic shooting practice.
coachTips: The individual assessment in Phase 1 is what makes this session specific and valuable. Spend genuine time identifying each players primary error before moving to Phase 2.
Adult players respond very well to specific, technical coaching delivered with confidence. Be direct and specific.
The targeted work in Phase 2 is where the real improvement happens. Do not rush Phase 1 assessment to get to the scenarios faster.
Pair players with similar technical issues in Phase 2 so your coaching time is efficient.

Run this session monthly across a season and track the conversion rates in Phase 3 scenarios. Measurable improvement across a season is the most powerful evidence of the value of specific technical sessions.
diagramDescription: Full-size goal with live GK. PHASE 1: two assessment positions shown (P1 central 20m, P2 right channel 16m) with observation icon for coach. Common errors listed alongside. PHASE 2: individual work shown with coach giving specific feedback to each player. PHASE 3: four scenario positions labeled A-D. Scenario A (controlled shot 18m), B (far post cross), C (through ball first time), D (1v1 12m). GK shown live and competitive throughout. Score tracker shown.

title: Volley in Transition — Receive and Strike
objective: Develop the volley in a transitional context where the player receives a ball on the turn and volleys immediately without it touching the ground.
learningOutcomes: Players can receive a dropping ball on the half-turn and volley immediately in a new direction
Players develop the combination of first touch and volley as a single fluid movement
Players build the composure to attempt a volley in a transitional moment
Players understand when the volley in transition is the correct option
organisation: Server stands 12m behind the volleyer position. Volleyer stands 18m from goal with back to goal. Server lofts the ball over the volleyers head or to the side. Volleyer must turn and volley at goal without the ball touching the ground.
instructions: Volleyer starts with their back to the goal, 18m from goal.
Server delivers a lofted ball from behind so it arrives at volleyer at knee-to-hip height.
THE TRANSITION VOLLEY: as the ball drops past the volleyer, they turn (left or right depending on which side the ball arrives) and strike it on the volley before it touches the ground.
The volley direction is toward the goal which is now behind the volleyer after the turn.
SIDE THE BALL ARRIVES ON DETERMINES THE TURN: ball arriving on the right side — turn right, right-foot volley. Ball arriving on the left side — turn left, left-foot volley.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): gentle serves, slow drops. Volleyer focuses on reading the delivery and positioning the body for the turn.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): faster serves, more realistic dropping pace. Volleyer attempts the volley from both sides alternately.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): GK added. Live finishing. Server varies which side the ball arrives on without warning.
coachingPoints: READ THE DELIVERY IMMEDIATELY: the moment the ball leaves the server, the volleyer must read which side it will arrive on and begin rotating to that side. Waiting until the ball is close is too late.

THE BODY TURN AND THE VOLLEY ARE ONE MOVEMENT: the rotation of the body toward the ball and the striking motion of the leg should flow together as one movement, not as a turn followed separately by a volley.

CONTACT THE BALL BEFORE IT DROPS TOO LOW: the ideal contact height for a transitional volley is between the knee and hip. Below the knee the contact angle sends the ball upward. Above the hip the body position becomes awkward.
EYES ON THE BALL LONGER THAN FEELS COMFORTABLE: the urge to look at the goal before striking is strong. Resist it until the moment of contact.
NON-KICKING FOOT POSITION IN THE TURN: as the body rotates, the non-kicking foot must be planted firmly before the volley. A rotating plant foot produces an inconsistent strike.
THE GOAL IS BEHIND YOU — USE SPATIAL AWARENESS: the volleyer must develop the spatial awareness to know where the goal is without turning to look at it during the strike.
BOTH SIDES ARE EQUALLY IMPORTANT: in match situations, the ball will arrive on both sides unpredictably. Both the left and right transitional volley must be practiced equally.

COMPOSURE IN THE TRANSITION MOMENT: this skill appears in matches during counter-attacks and quick transitions. The mental composure to attempt a volley in this moment is as important as the technique.
commonMistakes: Reading the delivery too late, being positioned on the wrong side when the ball arrives
Trying to turn and then set up for a volley as two separate actions, losing the fluidity of the movement
Contacting the ball too low (below the knee), sending it high over the bar
Looking at the goal during the turn rather than watching the ball through contact
Non-kicking foot still rotating at the moment of contact, causing an inconsistent strike
corrections: Late read: require the volleyer to call LEFT or RIGHT as soon as the ball leaves the server — trains the early read
Two separate actions: coach calls FLOW before every rep, emphasising the fluid turn-and-strike as one movement
Contact too low: place a cone at knee height beside the volleyer position. Ball must be above the cone at the moment of contact
Looking at goal: place a small target (coloured cone) on the ball. Player must call the colour at the moment of contact, keeping eyes on ball
Rotating plant foot: place a foot marker on the ground showing where the non-kicking foot must land and hold before the volley
progressions: Server varies delivery height as well as side: volleyer must adjust contact height and technique accordingly
Add a recovering defender who starts 5m behind the volleyer and can intercept if the volley is too slow to execute
Full small-sided game with bonus points for transitional volleys that result in a shot on target
regressions: Slow all deliveries to 50 percent pace and keep at knee-to-hip height for the first 5 minutes
Allow 1 bounce (half volley) before attempting the full volley while the turn-and-strike movement is established
Use only one side (right side delivery, right-foot volley) until the movement is fluent before adding the left side
matchApplication: The transitional volley — turning and striking a dropping ball in one movement — is one of the most spectacular and effective skills in football. It appears regularly in goals scored on the counter-attack, in transition play, and when a player receives a clearance or long pass on the turn. At U14, players have the physical coordination and technical foundation to begin developing this skill. The key coaching emphasis on reading the delivery early and executing the turn-and-strike as one fluid movement is what separates a practiced transitional volley from a lucky one.
coachTips: The fluidity of the movement is everything. Demonstrate it yourself (or show footage) at full speed so players can see the turn and strike as one action.
Phase 3 with the GK is the most important phase. Connect the skill directly to finishing and encourage composure over power.
Both sides must receive equal practice. Track which side each player is more comfortable with and give extra reps on the weaker side.
Film Phase 3 from behind the goal to show players the contact height, body position, and accuracy of their transitional volley.
Connect to professional examples: overhead kicks, bicycle kicks, and transitional volleys scored in matches show the range of this skill at its most developed.
diagramDescription: 20m channel to goal. Volleyer shown with back to goal 18m from goal. Server shown 12m behind volleyer lofting ball over. Two scenarios shown: BALL RIGHT (ball drops on right side, right-foot volley after right-side turn), BALL LEFT (ball drops on left side, left-foot volley after left-side turn). Turn-and-strike motion shown as a single curved arrow. Contact height zone shown (knee to hip). GK shown at the goal. Phase 3 arrow shows ball arriving from server to volleyer without warning which side.

title: Volley Finishing — Advanced Techniques
objective: Develop advanced volleying techniques for adult players including overhead kicks, bicycle kicks, and controlled side volleys used in match finishing situations.
learningOutcomes: Adult players can execute a controlled side volley accurately from a crossed ball
Players develop the technique and confidence for an overhead or bicycle kick in appropriate situations
Players understand when attempting advanced volley techniques is the correct decision
Players maintain the discipline of technique quality over spectacular attempts
equipment: Crash mat for overhead kick practice
organisation: Full penalty area with live GK. Three volley techniques practiced in sequence: controlled side volley from crosses, overhead kick technique, and the scissor kick variation. Crash mat used for overhead kick safety.
instructions: TECHNIQUE 1 — CONTROLLED SIDE VOLLEY (8 minutes):
Wide server delivers crosses from the right at hip-to-shoulder height.
Player meets the cross with a side volley: body side-on, hip rotation drives the strike, contact through the centre of the ball, low follow-through.
Target: far post low. GK live.
TECHNIQUE 2 — OVERHEAD KICK (8 minutes):
Server lofts the ball 2m above the player from in front.
Player falls backward (controlled fall) while swinging both legs upward. The non-striking leg provides elevation. The striking leg contacts the ball at the highest point.
SAFETY: practice on a crash mat or soft surface. Always practice the falling technique before adding the ball.
Target: not necessarily the goal in early practice — correct technique is the target.
TECHNIQUE 3 — SCISSOR KICK (9 minutes):
Similar to overhead kick but executed while the ball is arriving from the side rather than from in front.
The scissor motion involves one leg sweeping forward (for balance and elevation) while the other contacts the ball.
The ball arrives from the side and the player swings their body across it while the legs scissor.
coachingPoints: THE SIDE VOLLEY IS USED IN MATCHES: the side volley from a cross is a regular match situation. Master it as a reliable technique before pursuing overhead or bicycle kicks.

THE OVERHEAD KICK IS A LAST RESORT: an overhead kick should only be attempted when the ball is above shoulder height and no other technique is available. A side volley or a headed touch is almost always preferable. The overhead is spectacular but low percentage.
FALL SAFELY: the backward fall in an overhead kick is the most dangerous element. The arms must go out to break the fall before the body contacts the ground. Practice this fall without a ball first.

TIMING THE OVERHEAD KICK: the kick must contact the ball at the highest point of the leg swing — which means the player must already be falling backward before the kick. The take-off and kick are rapid and simultaneous.
HIP ROTATION IN THE SIDE VOLLEY: all the power in a side volley comes from the rotation of the hips through the ball. The leg swing itself contributes relatively little power compared to the hip.

THE SCISSOR KICK TECHNIQUE: the scissor leg (the non-kicking leg) swings forward explosively before contact, elevating the body and creating the platform for the kicking leg to swing across the body to meet the ball.

DO NOT ATTEMPT OVERHEAD OR BICYCLE KICKS IN CROWDED AREAS: in matches, these techniques carry a risk of injuring other players. They should only be attempted when the player has clear space around them.

COMPOSURE OVER SPECTACLE: a composed, well-struck side volley is almost always a better chance than a spectacular overhead kick attempted from a difficult angle. Know which situation calls for which technique.
commonMistakes: Attempting overhead kicks from too far from goal, reducing the chance of scoring even with correct technique
Falling backward for the overhead kick without arms extended, risking back injury
Using the overhead kick when a side volley or header would create a cleaner chance
Side volley hip rotation insufficient, reducing power to a leg-only strike
Attempting scissors or overhead kicks in training at full intensity without adequate technique preparation
corrections: Overhead from too far: place a shooting zone cone — overhead kicks can only be attempted from inside this zone (12m from goal)
Falling without arms: specifically practice the fall 5 times without a ball, arms extended, before any overhead kick attempts with the ball
Poor technique decision: ask before every rep which technique they are selecting and why — develops the decision-making alongside the technique
Insufficient hip rotation in side volley: place a ball on the opposite hip to the kicking foot. Hip must push this ball away as the striking hip rotates through
Full intensity without preparation: overhead kick technique at reduced height and delivery pace for the full Phase 2 before increasing the height and difficulty
progressions: Combine all three techniques in a random delivery session: server delivers at different heights and players must select the correct technique
Add a goalkeeper who specifically goes for the overhead kick catch (makes the shot harder to score but more realistic)
Competition format: 3 side volleys, 2 overhead attempts, 1 scissor attempt. Score tracked. Technique points awarded alongside goals
regressions: Remove overhead and scissor kicks entirely and develop only the side volley until it is consistently effective
Use a soft ball for overhead kick practice to remove any pain concern while the falling technique is established
Allow the server to hold the ball higher for overhead kick practice to give more time to set up the fall
matchApplication: Advanced volley techniques at adult level exist on a spectrum from the highly practical (side volley from a cross — a regular match occurrence) to the spectacular and rare (bicycle kick). This session specifically addresses both ends of the spectrum because adult players need to have the controlled side volley as a reliable technical option and to understand the appropriate situations for attempting more advanced techniques. The emphasis on technique quality and decision-making (when to attempt which technique) is what distinguishes effective advanced technique coaching from purely spectacular practice.
coachTips: Safety must be the priority in overhead kick practice. Never skip the falling technique practice phase.
The side volley is the priority technique in this session. Most of the coaching time should be here.
Adult players often want to practice spectacular techniques. Channel this enthusiasm by connecting the fundamentals (side volley) to why the spectacular techniques work.
Film overhead kick attempts from the side. Players are often surprised by how different their technique looks from how it feels.

Connect to iconic examples: Ronaldo bicycle kick vs Juventus (2018 Champions League), Wayne Rooney bicycle kick vs Manchester City (2011). Use these to illustrate when the technique is appropriate and how good the execution must be for it to work.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with GK. TECHNIQUE 1 (side volley): wide server on right delivering cross, player shown side-on with hip rotation arrow, side volley contact arrow going to far post low. TECHNIQUE 2 (overhead kick): server in front lofting ball above player, player shown in backward fall with arms extended, striking leg at highest point of swing, crash mat shown below. TECHNIQUE 3 (scissor kick): ball arriving from side, scissor leg swinging forward for elevation, kicking leg crossing body to meet ball. Safety notes shown for Techniques 2 and 3.

title: Shielding and Turn — Beating the Press in Tight Space
objective: Develop the combination of shielding under pressure and then spinning away to beat the press as a single connected technical sequence.
learningOutcomes: Players can shield the ball under maximum physical pressure for 3-4 seconds
Players can execute a quick spin from the shielding position to escape a tight press
Players develop the read of when to release (pass) versus when to spin (create individually)
Players build the physical and mental composure for contested situations in tight spaces
organisation: 5x5m zones. Player A receives the ball in the zone with their back to the defender. Player B applies maximum physical pressure from behind. Player A must shield for 3 seconds then either spin and drive into space or release to a teammate (a third player or cone target outside the zone).
instructions: Player A receives a pass into the 5x5m zone with their back to Player B.
Player B immediately applies full physical pressure — legally using their body to try to win the ball.
Player A shields: body between ball and B, low and wide stance, arms out legally, ball under control.
TIMING RULE: Player A must shield for minimum 3 seconds before making a decision.
DECISION 1 — SPIN: if Player B commits their weight to one side during the shield, Player A spins away on the opposite side.
DECISION 2 — RELEASE: if Player B applies balanced pressure with no committed weight, Player A releases to a target player or cone outside the zone.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): Player B applies pressure from directly behind. A shields and spins. No release option.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): Player B applies pressure from different angles. A must read and decide spin or release.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): live with 3 players. A receives in zone with B pressing. C available outside the zone. A decides spin or release based on what B gives them.
coachingPoints: THE SHIELD MUST BE IMPENETRABLE FOR 3 SECONDS: wide stance, low centre of gravity, ball under the foot furthest from the defender, body as a barrier. Nothing about the shield should feel tentative.

READ THE DEFENDERS WEIGHT: the decision to spin or release is based entirely on what the defender gives you. If their weight is on their heels or committed to one side, spin. If they are balanced and patient, release.

THE SPIN IS A SINGLE EXPLOSIVE MOVEMENT: pivot on the standing foot, drag the ball with the other foot in the new direction, accelerate immediately. Any pause between the components is immediately exploited by the defender.
SPIN TO THE OPEN SIDE: always spin away from the direction the defender has committed their weight. Spinning into a committed defender is walking into their tackle.
THE RELEASE IS NOT FAILURE: releasing to a support player from a shielding position is an excellent play. It beats the defender without needing to spin. Both outcomes are correct.

USE LEGAL CONTACT TO YOUR ADVANTAGE: the shoulder and upper arm can be used to feel the defenders pressure and to create the slight separation needed for the spin. This is completely legal and should be practiced as a technical skill.
PROTECT THE BALL WITH THE FURTHEST FOOT: the ball should always be under the foot furthest from the defender. This extends the distance between the defender and the ball as much as possible.
EXPLOSIVE ACCELERATION AFTER THE SPIN: the spin creates approximately 1 second of space. That second is only valuable if used with an immediate explosive acceleration in the new direction.
commonMistakes: Shielding with the ball too close to the defender, making it easy to reach around and poke away
Not reading the defenders weight and spinning into their committed side (into the tackle)
The spin executed too slowly, allowing the defender to recover and intercept
No acceleration after the spin — stopping to look around after creating space
Using the arms illegally to push the defender away rather than using the body and shoulder
corrections: Ball too close to defender: add a minimum ball-distance rule — the ball must always be more than 0.5m from the defender. Use a rope measurement during Phase 1 to establish the sense of this distance
Spinning into committed side: freeze every incorrect spin decision and ask which way the defenders weight was committed — builds the reading habit
Slow spin: time the spin from pivot to acceleration. Target under 1 second for the complete spin
No acceleration: place a speed gate 2m away from the spin position. Player must reach it within 2 steps of completing the spin
Illegal arms: any clear arm push results in ball loss to the defender, creating a real consequence
progressions: Double defender: after spinning free of B, a second defender C starts from the edge of the zone and must be passed or dribbled past within 3 seconds
Extended shielding: increase the minimum shield time to 5 seconds, building the physical and mental endurance for longer contested situations
Full game with shielding bonus points: goals scored from a sequence including a successful shield-and-spin earn 3 points instead of 1
regressions: Reduce defender pressure to 50 percent while the spin technique is established
Remove the time requirement and allow A to spin at any moment they choose
Use a larger zone (7x7m) to give more space for the spin and acceleration
matchApplication: Shielding combined with the spin-away is one of the most physically and technically demanding individual skills in football. A player who can hold the ball under maximum pressure and then spin away creates individual moments of quality in tight areas that open up team attacks. At U16, players are physically mature enough to practice the full contact element of shielding and to develop the reading of defenders weight that determines the spin timing. This is a skill that continues to develop into the adult game and is a distinguishing quality of technically excellent senior players.
coachTips: Ensure players understand the legal use of the body in shielding before any full-contact practice begins.
The reading of the defenders weight is the most sophisticated element. Use many freeze-and-ask moments in Phase 2.
Film the spin from behind so players can see whether they spun to the correct side relative to the defenders committed weight.
Connect to professional examples: players like Roberto Firmino, Zlatan Ibrahimovic, and Son Heung-min all use the shield-and-spin regularly in their games.
This drill is physically demanding for both players. Rotate roles frequently and ensure adequate recovery.
diagramDescription: 5x5m zone. Player A shown receiving ball in zone with back to Player B (defender applying maximum pressure). Shield position shown: body between B and ball, low stance, arms out, ball under far foot. SPIN DECISION shown: B weight committed right (arrow), A pivots on left foot and drags ball left (spin arrow). RELEASE DECISION shown: B balanced (no committed weight), A plays ball to C outside zone (pass arrow). Phase 3 shown with C available outside zone as the release target.

title: Shielding — Winning Fouls and Holding Play
objective: Develop the adult-level shielding technique for winning fouls, holding up play for teammates to arrive, and protecting the ball in pressured defensive situations.
learningOutcomes: Adult players can shield effectively under maximum physical pressure for 5+ seconds
Players develop the technique and awareness to win fouls through shielding
Players build the holding-up-play ability that allows teammates time to arrive and support
Players understand the tactical value of shielding as a team play contribution
organisation: 6x6m zones. One attacker receives with their back to a physically pressing defender. Full-contact shielding practice at adult intensity. Focus on technique, decision-making, and the specific skill of winning a foul through legal shielding.
instructions: Player A receives in the zone with back to Player B (defender applying adult-level pressure).
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): Player B applies steady pressure from directly behind. Player A focuses purely on the shield position — 5-second minimum hold.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): Player B applies varied pressure: left side, right side, directly behind. Player A reads and adjusts the shield position to keep the body between B and ball.

PHASE 3 (10 minutes): 2v1 (A plus a support player vs B). A shields under pressure while the support player moves to receive the release pass. A must decide when to release and when to hold based on when the support player is available.

WINNING A FOUL: a defender who reaches around the shielding player illegally (using arms, clipping the ankles, pushing the back) commits a foul. The shielding player must stay in a legal position to make the foul inevitable rather than creating an illegal obstruction.
Throughout: referee role for coach — call legal versus illegal contact clearly to educate both players.
coachingPoints: THE SHIELD POSITION IS A DEFENSIVE ARCHITECTURE: wide base, low centre of gravity, ball under the furthest foot, back broad and firm. Every element must be in place simultaneously.

BODY CONTACT IS LEGAL AND USEFUL: legal shoulder and upper arm contact in shielding is an active part of the technique. The contact allows you to feel the defenders movement and to use their pressure against them.

WINNING A FOUL REQUIRES BEING LEGAL: the shielding player must not obstruct illegally (using arms to push, turning to face the defender, handling). If the shield is legally maintained and the defender fouls, the referee must award the free kick.

HOLDING FOR TEAMMATES: the most important application of shielding is not turning or spinning — it is simply holding the ball safely for 3-4 seconds while teammates arrive to support. This changes the tactical picture without any individual brilliance required.

THE RELEASE TIMING IS THE SKILL: holding too long (past the moment a teammate was available) is as much an error as releasing too early (before a teammate is available). Read when the support is ready.

ADJUST THE SHIELD TO THE PRESSURE ANGLE: a defender pressing from the right means the ball should be on the left foot. A defender pressing from the left means the ball should be on the right foot. Constantly adjust.

STRENGTH AND TECHNIQUE TOGETHER: at adult level, shielding requires both technical correctness and physical strength. A technically perfect shield with insufficient physical engagement is overcome by a strong defender.

THE SHIELDING ROLE IS A TEAM CONTRIBUTION: in senior football, a target forward who can receive, hold up play for 4 seconds, and release accurately to a supporting midfielder or wide player is enormously valuable even if they never score.
commonMistakes: Body position too upright, making the shield easily overcome by a strong pressing defender
Ball too close to the defender — not under the furthest foot — allowing easy poke-arounds
Illegal obstruction (turning to face the defender, using arms to push back) removing the foul-winning opportunity
Not releasing when the support player becomes available, holding too long under pressure
No communication with the support player about when they are available to receive
corrections: Too upright: require the shielding player to maintain a position where their knee is bent below 45 degrees throughout the shield
Ball too close: use the rope measurement — ball must always be more than 0.5m from the nearest defender foot
Illegal obstruction: any illegal action immediately awards the ball to the defender. Clear rules enforced immediately
Holding too long: support player must signal when they are available (hand raised). Any hold beyond 2 seconds after the signal is an error
No communication: make a verbal signal from the support player (NAME, READY) mandatory before any release pass can be played
progressions: Triple pressure: three defenders rotate around the shielding player (one pressing, two covering) while three support players try to get free
Extended hold time: increase to 8-second minimum hold before any release is allowed
Full match scenario: team A must play through a shielding sequence (receive, hold 4 seconds, release) before any shot is allowed
regressions: Reduce defender to 50 percent pressure throughout the session while adult technique is established
Allow the shielding player to face the defender (not back to goal) for Phase 1 before introducing the back-to-goal requirement
Use a wider zone (8x8m) to give more space for adjustments before tightening to 6x6m
matchApplication: Effective holding-up play through shielding is one of the most valuable and underappreciated skills in adult grassroots football. Every senior team needs players who can receive difficult balls under pressure, hold them safely for 3-4 seconds while teammates arrive, and release accurately to continue the attack. This contribution is often invisible in the match statistics but is immediately noticed when it is absent — the ball is lost, counter-attacks are launched, and attacks break down before they start. This drill develops the specific physical and technical skills that make a player a dependable outlet in pressured match situations.
coachTips: The referee role is important in this drill. Clear, consistent calling of legal versus illegal contact educates both players and creates the correct technical environment.
The holding-for-teammates coaching point is the most important tactical element. Make it explicit and discuss how this skill affects match play.
Adult players at grassroots level often do not receive physical contact training. This session may be the first time they have practiced contested shielding. Be patient.
Film Phase 3 from the side. The shield position, ball protection, and release timing are all visible from this angle.

Connect to the tactical system: discuss how a reliable shielding player in the front line changes the attacking options available to the team — deeper players can commit forward knowing the ball will be held.
diagramDescription: 6x6m zone. Player A shown receiving with back to Player B (adult-level defender pressing firmly from behind). Correct shield position shown: wide base, low centre of gravity, ball under far foot, back broad. THREE PRESSURE ANGLES shown in Phase 2: from left (ball moves to right foot), from right (ball moves to left foot), from behind (ball central). 2v1 PHASE shown with support player C making movement to get available and hand-signal shown (READY call). Release pass arrow shown from A to C at the correct moment.

title: Weak Foot Under Pressure — Match Intensity
objective: Develop the weak foot to a match-ready standard through high-intensity practice that replicates the pressure, speed, and decision-making of competitive football.
learningOutcomes: Players use their weak foot reliably in 1v1 situations, passing, and shooting under genuine pressure
Players develop the confidence to choose the weak foot when it is genuinely the better option
Players build the technical consistency that allows the weak foot to function at match speed
Players reduce the technical gap between their strong and weak foot to a competitive level
organisation: 20x15m zone per group. Small-sided game (3v3) with a strict weak foot only rule applying to all passes, dribbles, and shots. Strong foot can be used only for running and first touch control. Every deliberate ball action must use the weak foot.
instructions: PHASE 1 (5 minutes): individual weak foot warm-up. Each player does 2 minutes of wall passing (weak foot only) at increasing pace.

PHASE 2 (8 minutes): 3v3 game with WEAK FOOT ONLY for all deliberate ball actions. Strong foot allowed for controlling a bouncing ball or running. Any strong-foot pass or shot must be retaken. The referee role rotates among non-playing players.

PHASE 3 (9 minutes): weaker restriction removed for 2 minutes (free play) then reintroduced for 2 minutes. Alternating. This tests whether the weak foot habits transfer when the restriction is lifted.
Score normally. Any strong-foot deliberate action not corrected within 3 seconds = opposition earns 1 point.
Coach specifically notes: which actions trigger automatic reversion to the strong foot? These are the habits to address.
coachingPoints: THE WEAK FOOT IMPROVES FASTEST UNDER COMPETITIVE PRESSURE: isolated technical work develops the skill. Competitive match pressure with a consequence for using the strong foot develops the automatic response. Both are needed but this session provides the competitive pressure that drills alone cannot.

REVERSION TO STRONG FOOT IS AN AUTOMATIC HABIT: when a player reverts to their strong foot during the game, they are not being lazy — they are following an automatic movement pattern built over years. Replacing it requires many repetitions under pressure.

FIRST TOUCH CAN STILL BE THE STRONG FOOT: the rule allows first-touch control with the strong foot. This means the player can receive the ball, control it, and then must play it with the weak foot. This is a realistic match application.

THE WEAK FOOT UNDER PRESSURE IS DIFFERENT FROM THE WEAK FOOT IN ISOLATION: every player has a better weak foot in an empty training exercise than in a 3v3 game under pressure. This session specifically closes that gap.

QUALITY BEATS SAFETY AT U17: at this age, weak foot actions should be taken with the intention of playing a good ball, not just avoiding using the strong foot. A weak, safe weak-foot pass that goes to a nearby player but achieves nothing is not the goal.

IDENTIFY THE SPECIFIC FAILURE PATTERN: most players fail on the weak foot in one specific type of action (usually under pressure from a defender, or when facing their own goal). Identify this specific pattern and address it directly.

THE STRONG-FOOT OPTION ALWAYS FEELS EASIER: at U17, the technical gap between strong and weak foot is still significant. The discomfort of using the weak foot under pressure is expected and normal. Persisting through this discomfort is what produces improvement.
CELEBRATE QUALITY WEAK-FOOT ACTIONS: a well-struck weak-foot shot or a perfectly weighted weak-foot through ball deserves as much celebration as any strong-foot equivalent.
commonMistakes: Automatic reversion to the strong foot under pressure, particularly when a defender is close
Weak-foot passes going only to the nearest safe option rather than attempting ambitious actions
Frustration at weak-foot technical errors affecting confidence and leading to further errors
Not maintaining the weak-foot rule during Phase 3 free play, immediately reverting to strong foot
The opposition taking advantage of the restriction by pressing specifically the weak foot side
corrections: Automatic reversion: the penalty system (opposition point for strong-foot action) creates an immediate consequence that interrupts the automatic habit
Only safe options: add a forward-passing requirement — at least 1 in 3 weak-foot passes must go forward
Frustration affecting confidence: normalise errors explicitly: every missed weak-foot pass is training. Missing is correct use of the session
Not maintaining in free play: keep a watcher role (non-playing player) whose job is specifically to call strong-foot actions during Phase 3 free play
Opposition pressing the weak side: allow the player with the ball to request a 1-second hold (hold their hand up) before receiving during Phase 2, giving more time to set the weak foot
progressions: One-touch only with the weak foot: all actions must be played first time, removing the option of setting up the weak foot
Add a finishing element: mini-goals at each end, all shots must be weak foot. This creates the highest-pressure weak foot moment
Full match session with weak-foot only rule applied for the entire training session
regressions: Allow 2-touch with weak foot (control then play) before reducing to 1-touch in later phases
Remove the opposition points penalty for strong-foot use and replace with the player self-reporting each reversion for honest self-assessment
Reduce zone size to 12x10m to create more opportunities for weak-foot actions in a shorter time
matchApplication: At U17, players are approaching the end of the developmental window where fundamental technical changes are most achievable. A genuine weak foot that functions at match level is the technical goal for the remainder of a players development before senior football. The research on two-footedness consistently shows that players with a genuinely functional weak foot have significantly wider tactical options, are harder to defend against, and continue to improve technically for longer in their careers. This competitive session creates the specific match-pressure conditions that produce the fastest weak-foot improvement in a short time.
coachTips: The referee role is crucial. Rotate it carefully and make sure it is taken seriously — the weak-foot rule only works if enforcement is consistent.
Specifically observe and note which situations trigger the automatic reversion. This information is the most valuable coaching output of the session.
The alternating free/restricted format in Phase 3 is the most important element. Watch specifically what happens in the 2 minutes of free play immediately after the restriction.
Film Phase 2 and let players review their own footage. Seeing their own automatic reversion patterns is more convincing than being told about them.

Connect to the professional standard: discuss how players like Messi, Ronaldo, Mbappe, and Salah use both feet functionally in match situations and how this makes them impossible to defend against predictably.
diagramDescription: 20x15m zone. 3v3 game shown with circles vs triangles. WEAK FOOT ONLY label shown prominently. Player shown receiving with strong foot (allowed for control) then passing with weak foot (arrow labeled WEAK FOOT). Reversion moment shown: player about to use strong foot with penalty indicator. Strong foot allowed zones shown (running, first touch control only). Phase 3 alternating format shown: FREE (2 minutes) then RESTRICTED (2 minutes) timeline. Coach observation position shown with notation pad for tracking reversion patterns.

title: Keep Away — First Possession Game
objective: Introduce the concept of keeping the ball away from opponents through the simplest possible possession game.
learningOutcomes: Players understand that keeping the ball is a team goal, not just an individual one
Players develop the habit of looking for a teammate to pass to when under pressure
Players experience the joy of successful team possession
Players begin to understand the basic spatial concept of spreading out to help the team
organisation: Groups of 4-6 players. One player is the chaser. The rest keep the ball away from the chaser. No goals, no scoring — purely keep the ball away. When the chaser touches the ball, the player who passed last becomes the new chaser.
instructions: One player is chosen as the chaser and stands in the middle of the zone.
The other players spread around the zone with one ball between them.
The players with the ball pass it to each other to keep it away from the chaser.
If the chaser touches the ball: the player who made the last pass becomes the new chaser.
If the ball goes out of the zone: the player who played it out becomes the new chaser.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): 3 players keep ball from 1 chaser in a 10x10m zone. Maximum 3 touches.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): 4 players keep ball from 1 chaser. Maximum 2 touches.
PHASE 3 (3 minutes): 5 players keep ball from 2 chasers. Test of real possession under more pressure.
Coach focus: celebrate passes to teammates who are free, celebrate spreading out.
coachingPoints: PASS TO A FREE FRIEND: the most important idea in keep away is passing to the teammate who has the most space. Look around before the ball arrives and find who is free.
SPREAD OUT: when you do not have the ball, your job is to find a space where you are easy to pass to. Standing close to a teammate gives the chaser an easy job covering you both.
MOVE AFTER YOU PASS: once you have passed the ball, move to a new space. Do not stand still. Moving creates a new passing option for your teammate.
CALL FOR THE BALL: if you are free and want the ball, call your teammates name or call BALL. Help your teammate find you.
KEEP THE BALL MOVING: the best way to keep the ball away from the chaser is to move it quickly. Holding it too long lets the chaser get close.
DO NOT PANIC: if the chaser comes close, look for the nearest teammate and pass quickly. Panicking leads to losing the ball.
FOOTBALL IS A TEAM GAME: the person without the ball is just as important as the person with it. Find a good space and make yourself easy to pass to.
CELEBRATE TOGETHER: when the team keeps the ball for a long time without the chaser touching it, that is a team success worth celebrating together.
THIS IS HOW ALL TEAMS IN THE WORLD TRAIN: every professional football team in the world plays keep away in training because it builds every important skill at once.
commonMistakes: All players bunching near the ball rather than spreading around the zone
Holding the ball too long and being caught by the chaser
Not calling for the ball, leaving the ball-carrier with unclear options
Passing to a player who is closely marked rather than a free player
Giving up after losing the ball rather than immediately chasing as the new chaser
corrections: Bunching: call SPREAD every 20 seconds as a reminder during phase 1
Holding too long: add a 3-second count rule — if still holding after 3 counts, automatic chaser swap
Not calling: make it a rule that players must say a name before receiving (JAMES, PASS) to build communication
Passing to marked player: celebrate passes to free players with a specific call (GOOD CHOICE) to reinforce the decision
Giving up: make the chaser role energetic and fun — the chaser is the most active player, praise their effort too
progressions: Reduce touches to 1-touch: every player must pass first time, creating maximum speed and challenge
Add a second ball: two balls being kept away from one chaser simultaneously — extremely hard and very fun
Expand to a larger area (15x15m) with 8v2 to give the possession team a comfortable experience before making it harder
regressions: Use a larger zone (12x12m) and reduce to 3v1 to make possession more comfortable initially
Allow unlimited touches in Phase 1 before reducing to 3 touches
Use a softer ball or a beach ball to slow the game down if the speed of the ball is causing difficulty
matchApplication: Keep away is the oldest and simplest possession training game in football and is used by every elite club in the world as a fundamental training method. At U8, the concepts of passing to a free teammate, spreading out, and moving after the pass are genuinely new ideas that have an immediate impact on how the players see the game. Even one session of keep away changes how U8 players understand their role when their team has the ball and begins to build the team possession mentality that underlies all of the more sophisticated tactical concepts they will learn in later years.
coachTips: Make this a joyful, energetic session. Keep away should feel like the most fun game in training.
Celebrate team sequences (5 passes without the chaser touching) as genuinely exciting achievements.
Join in yourself occasionally. Being the chaser or in the possession group for a minute is the most effective coaching tool at this age.
Play music. Keep away with a good beat playing in the background is a wonderful U8 training experience.
