Use this as the opening activity of every session for a month. The tactical habits it builds compound rapidly.
diagramDescription: 10x10m zone. One chaser (triangle) in the centre. 4 possession players (circles) spread around the zone with the ball. Pass arrows shown moving between the spread players away from the chaser. SPREAD OUT label shown with spacing indicators. MOVE AFTER PASSING arrow shown from a player who just passed moving to a new space. CALL FOR BALL speech bubble shown from a free player.

title: Shapes and Triangles — First Tactical Shape
objective: Introduce the triangle as the most fundamental attacking shape in football, developing the positional understanding that underlies all team tactics.
learningOutcomes: Players understand why a triangle shape creates more passing options than a line
Players can form a triangle with two teammates in any area of the pitch
Players develop the habit of finding triangle positions when their team has the ball
Players begin to understand how the triangle creates space and passing angles
organisation: Groups of 3. One ball. Players practice moving in triangle shapes, maintaining the triangle as the ball moves between them. The coach uses the visual of a triangle drawn on the ground with cones to make the concept concrete.
instructions: INTRODUCTION (3 minutes): draw a triangle on the ground with 3 cones. Place one player at each cone. Pass the ball around the triangle. Show players that from every cone, there are always 2 options to pass to.
Now draw a line with 3 cones (straight line). Pass the ball along the line. Show that the player in the middle only has 2 options but the end players each only have 1.
Ask the players: which shape gives more options? The triangle. Always choose triangle positions.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): 3 players pass in a triangle in a 12x12m area. When the ball moves, all 3 players must adjust their positions to maintain a triangle shape relative to the ball.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): add a passive defender in the centre of the triangle. The triangle must move and adjust to keep the defender on the outside of the shape.
PHASE 3 (3 minutes): two groups of 3 play keep away (4v2 with one neutral in each group). Possession group must form triangles constantly.
Coach walks around praising triangle shapes and pointing out when the triangle is lost.
coachingPoints: THE TRIANGLE GIVES YOU TWO OPTIONS: from any corner of a triangle, you can always see two teammates to pass to. From a line, the middle player has options but the end players have only one. Triangles are always better than lines.

THE BALL IS THE TOP OF THE TRIANGLE: when your team has the ball, think of the ball-carrier as the top of the triangle. Your job is to be at one of the bottom corners. Find a position that completes the triangle.
TRIANGLES MOVE WITH THE BALL: when the ball moves to a new player, the triangle must adjust. The new ball-carrier becomes the new top. The other two players shift to new corner positions.
SPACE BETWEEN THE CORNERS: the triangle only works if there is enough space between each corner. A tiny triangle with players very close together is not useful. Open the triangle up.

YOU ARE ALWAYS EITHER PASSING OR SUPPORTING: in football, you are either the player with the ball (passing) or a player without the ball (supporting, forming the triangle). Both roles are always happening.
THE TRIANGLE CREATES ANGLES: by standing in a triangle rather than a line, the passing options go in different directions. The defender cannot cover two different directions at once.
TRIANGLES ARE IN EVERY GREAT TEAM: Barcelona, Manchester City, Spain — every great football team uses triangle movements constantly. This is the foundation of all their beautiful football.

FIND YOUR TRIANGLE POSITION BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: do not wait until the ball is passed to you before looking for where to go. Find your triangle position while the ball is still with your teammate.
commonMistakes: Players forming lines rather than triangles, reducing their passing options
The triangle collapsing as all three players move toward the ball when one player has it
Triangles too small (players too close together), reducing the space and the angle of the passes
Players not adjusting their position when the ball moves, leaving the triangle shape lost
The concept being abstract — players not understanding visually what a triangle shape looks like on the pitch
corrections: Lines instead of triangles: use the physical cone demonstration (line vs triangle) again and ask players to put themselves in one of the cone positions
All moving to ball: specifically call OUT the two players without the ball and remind them to hold their triangle corner positions
Too small: place cones showing minimum distances. Triangle corners must be at least 5m from each other
Not adjusting: call TRIANGLE after every pass as a reminder for all players to check their position
Abstract concept: draw triangles on the ground with chalk or cones and have players physically stand in them before removing the visual guide
progressions: 4v1 with triangles: 4 players must always maintain at least one triangle shape while keeping the ball from 1 defender
Double triangles: 6 players form two overlapping triangles in a larger area (the hexagon shape used by elite teams)
Triangle combination play: the triangle must complete 5 passes before any player can move out of the triangle shape
regressions: Use the cone triangle throughout the whole session as a physical reference rather than removing it
Reduce to just 2 players passing with the coach as the third triangle corner in Phase 1
Allow players to walk to their triangle position rather than running in Phase 1 to reduce the movement complexity
matchApplication: The triangle is the most fundamental unit of team tactics in football. Every possession-based system, from tiki-taka to the Spanish national team to modern high-press football, is built on the triangle as its basic structural unit. Introducing this concept at U9 — when players are beginning to understand the game as a team activity rather than purely as individual action — gives them a visual, physical framework for thinking about their positioning that remains relevant and valuable throughout their entire footballing life. The coach who teaches triangles at U9 is giving their players one of the most enduring tactical tools in football.
coachTips: The cone demonstration (line vs triangle) is worth spending 3 full minutes on. The visual comparison is immediately understood by U9 players.
Walk around the session constantly pointing at triangle shapes when they appear (TRIANGLE! YES!) and gently pointing out when they are lost.
Connect the triangle to their favourite team or a team they know. Show them that every team uses triangles.
This concept can become a shared language: coaches who use TRIANGLE as a shorthand coaching call across all sessions create a team that constantly self-organises into triangles.
The triangle concept is the foundation for everything in the Tactical category that comes later. This is one of the highest-value sessions in the library.
diagramDescription: Two visual comparisons shown: LINE (3 cones in a straight line with arrows showing limited options — end players have 1 option each) vs TRIANGLE (3 cones forming a triangle with arrows showing 2 options from every corner). 12x12m area shown with 3 players at triangle corners. Ball at top corner with pass arrows to both bottom corners. Ball movement shown: ball moves to bottom left corner, all 3 players shift to form a new triangle with the new ball position at the top. Passive defender shown in Phase 2 on the outside of the triangle.

title: Gates Game — Creating and Using Space
objective: Develop the basic concept of creating and using space through a simple gate-scoring game that rewards wide play.
learningOutcomes: Players understand that space exists on the pitch and it is valuable to find it
Players develop the habit of spreading out rather than clustering around the ball
Players experience how using the wide areas creates more scoring opportunities
Players begin to understand the connection between their movement and creating space for teammates
organisation: 25x20m area with 4-6 small gates (2 cones, 2m apart) placed around the perimeter and within the area. Teams score by dribbling through a gate, not by shooting at a goal. A gate can only be scored through once every 30 seconds (resets). This rewards the team that finds and uses space across the whole area.
instructions: Set up 4-6 gates in the area. Some near the edges, some centrally.
Teams of 3 or 4 play possession football. To score a point: dribble through any gate with the ball under control.
A gate is blocked for 30 seconds after it is scored through (place a cone on it to show it is blocked).
The team with the most points after 10 minutes wins.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): 4 gates only. Players explore and find available gates.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): 6 gates including 2 near the touchlines to reward wide play. Gates near the touchlines are worth 2 points.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): free game. No gate scoring. Teams play to mini-goals. Observe whether the wide habits built in Phases 1 and 2 transfer to a regular game.
Coach focus: celebrate movement to the wide gates and praise players who find space away from the cluster.
coachingPoints: SPACE IS VALUABLE: space on the pitch is like gold. When you find a space with no defenders near you, that is the most important place to be.
GO WHERE NOBODY IS: the best place to receive the ball is where nobody is standing. Look for empty areas of the pitch and move toward them.
WIDE GATES ARE HARDER TO DEFEND: when your team uses the width of the pitch, the defending team has to cover a bigger area. This makes it harder to close down every gate.

THE BALL GOES TO THE SPACE, NOT AWAY FROM IT: some players move away from the ball to find space. Others stay near the ball because they want to touch it. The team that moves to create space will always find more gates.

SPREAD LIKE A STAR: if you imagine your team as a star shape (one player in the centre, others spread in different directions), you will always find space. If you cluster together, you will never find space.
COMMUNICATE: if you see an open gate, tell your teammate. Call GATE, LEFT or GATE, RIGHT so they know where the space is.
LOOK BEFORE YOU RECEIVE: before the ball comes to you, look around the area. Where are the open gates? Where is the space? Know where you are going before the ball arrives.
USE THE WHOLE AREA: teams that only play in the middle of the area score fewer points. Teams that use the whole area, including the wide edges, find more open gates.
commonMistakes: All players clustering in the centre of the area and ignoring the edge gates
Not looking for open gates before receiving the ball
Teams defending all gates at once rather than prioritising the most valuable ones
Players dribbling through already-blocked gates (covered by the cone marker)
No communication about where the open gates are
corrections: Clustering: add a wide-gate bonus (2 points) from the start of Phase 1 to immediately incentivise movement to the edges
Not looking before receiving: add a rule that a player must point to a gate before receiving the ball — builds the pre-receive scan habit
Defending all gates: coaches can observe which team has the better defensive structure and discuss it briefly at the half-time break
Blocked gates: emphasise the blocked gate cone marker at the start. Players must check whether a cone is on the gate before attempting to score through it
No communication: require teams to call GATE before every dribble-through attempt so communication becomes habitual
progressions: Reduce gate size to 1.5m for advanced players: tighter dribble required to score
Add a passing rule: the player scoring through the gate must have received a pass to score (no solo runs from halfway)
Moving gates: players holding a cone can move their gate to a new position every 60 seconds, adding spatial awareness
regressions: Increase gate size to 3m for struggling players
Reduce to 3 gates only to simplify the spatial awareness demand
Allow the team to nominate one gate as their target gate before each possession sequence, reducing the decision-making to a single focus
matchApplication: The gates game is one of the most effective spatial awareness and wide play development tools in youth football because it creates a direct incentive for using space across the whole pitch. In a regular game, young players naturally cluster because the ball is where the action is. The gates format disrupts this habit by rewarding players who find and use space — often in the wide areas where defenders have not followed them. Players who play the gates game regularly develop a natural spatial awareness and a habit of using the width that transfers directly to match play.
coachTips: Move around the area during the game specifically celebrating wide gate scores with extra enthusiasm.
The Phase 3 free game observation is the most valuable part of the session. Note whether the wide spreading habit transfers without the gate incentive.
Connect the gates game to a real match situation: in a match, the goal is the gate. The wide space is where the goal can be reached most effectively.
Use the gates game as the opening activity for any session where you want to develop wide play concepts.
The game format makes the tactical lesson implicit rather than instructional — players discover the value of width by finding it produces more points, rather than being told about it.
diagramDescription: 25x20m area. 6 gates shown: 2 centrally (standard, 1 point), 2 on left/right edges (wide, 2 points each), 2 at the ends (standard). Teams shown as circles and triangles. Ball shown near one cluster. Dribble arrow shown to a wide gate (2 point label). Blocked gate shown with cone marker. Star spread diagram shown in corner illustrating the ideal player distribution. Score tracker shown.

title: First Defending — Delay and Cover
objective: Introduce the two most fundamental defensive principles — the first defender delays and the second defender covers — through a simple and progressive practice.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the role of the first defender (press and delay) and the second defender (cover and support)
Players can identify their defensive role based on their position relative to the ball and the attacker
Players develop the habit of dropping into a defensive shape as a team when the opposition has the ball
Players experience how organised defending is more effective than chasing individually
players: Groups of 4-6 (2 attackers vs 2 defenders plus GK)
organisation: 30x20m zone with a goal at one end. Two attackers face two defenders. The session specifically focuses on the defensive pair and their roles relative to the ball.
instructions: TWO ATTACKERS start at one end of the zone with the ball. TWO DEFENDERS start in the middle of the zone.

THE FIRST DEFENDER: the defender closest to the ball sprints toward the ball-carrier to press. Their job is NOT to win the ball immediately — their job is to slow the attacker down and show them away from goal.
THE SECOND DEFENDER: the second defender does NOT join the press. They drop back, position themselves behind and to the side of the first defender, covering the space the first defender has left.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): walkthrough. Coach places defenders in the correct positions at each moment. No pressure. Just positioning.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): live 2v2. First defender presses. Second defender covers. Attackers try to score.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): score system. Defenders earn 1 point for every 10 seconds they hold the two attackers without conceding. Attackers earn a point for scoring.
Coach pauses to point out whenever both defenders press simultaneously (leaving the space behind), showing why this fails.
coachingPoints: THE FIRST DEFENDER DELAYS, NOT DIVES: the first defender approaches the ball-carrier quickly but does not dive in. They get within 1-2m and force the attacker to make a decision — pass or dribble. This is called jockeying.

THE SECOND DEFENDER COVERS THE SPACE BEHIND: the second defender drops behind the first defender. If the ball-carrier beats the first defender, the second defender is there as the next line of defence.
NEVER BOTH PRESS AT THE SAME TIME: when both defenders press the ball together, they leave a large space behind them that the second attacker can exploit. One presses, one covers. Always.

THE SECOND DEFENDER ALSO WATCHES THE SECOND ATTACKER: while covering the space behind the first defender, the second defender must also be aware of where the second attacker is. If the ball is passed to them, the second defender becomes the new first defender.

COMMUNICATION BETWEEN THE DEFENDERS: the second defender can see more of the game than the first defender. They should call instructions: HOLD (keep jockeying), TACKLE (safe to try), STEP (ball-carrier is getting away, step in to help).
BE PATIENT: organised defending requires patience. The aim is not to win the ball in the first second but to delay long enough for other defenders to recover.

GOAL-SIDE ALWAYS: both defenders must always maintain a goal-side position (between the attacker and the goal). A defender who lets an attacker get behind them toward goal has lost their defensive function.
THE SHAPE IS MORE IMPORTANT THAN THE INDIVIDUAL ACTIONS: one defender who makes a perfect challenge but leaves a gap is less effective than two defenders who maintain their shape and positions.
commonMistakes: Both defenders pressing the ball at the same time, leaving the covering space empty
The first defender diving in immediately without first slowing the attacker down
The second defender moving too far away from the first defender, leaving a gap between them
No communication between the two defenders
First defender not maintaining goal-side position as the attacker moves laterally
corrections: Both pressing: immediately pause play and show the space that has been created. Move one defender back to the covering position before restarting
Diving in immediately: remove tackling from Phase 1 entirely. First defender can only shadow and delay, not tackle
Second defender too far: use a maximum distance rope (5m) between the two defenders. They cannot be more than 5m apart
No communication: require the second defender to call at least one instruction per possession sequence
Not goal-side: place a cone behind the attacker at goal level. First defender must always stay between the attacker and this cone
progressions: 2v2 with GK: add a goalkeeper so the second line of defence is clear and the defensive shape has a real endpoint
Outnumbered defence: 3v2 where the defending pair must delay long enough for a third defender to arrive from the starting line
Transition: after the defending pair wins the ball, they immediately become attackers and the attacking pair becomes the defending pair
regressions: Reduce to 1v1 with the second defender in a static covering position until the jockeying habit is established
Use a passive attacker (slow dribble only) in Phase 1 to give the defenders more time to find their positions
Reduce the zone to 20x15m to make the defensive distances easier to manage
matchApplication: The first-defender-delay and second-defender-cover principle is the foundational defensive concept in organised football. Everything else in team defending (mid-block, high press, low block, cover and balance) is built on this two-player defensive relationship. Introducing it at U11, when players are starting to think tactically and are physically capable of maintaining organised defensive positions under pressure, creates the defensive awareness that underpins all future defensive development. Teams that learn to defend in organised pairs from an early age are significantly harder to break down than teams that chase the ball individually.
coachTips: Walk through the positions at least once before any live practice — the concept of first and second defender is new and must be understood before it can be executed.
The most important moment to coach is when both defenders press simultaneously. Pause immediately, show the space, and reset. Do this consistently and the habit changes quickly.
Praise the communication between defenders specifically — calling HOLD or TACKLE is advanced defensive intelligence at U11.
Connect to a professional example: show two defenders defending correctly against two attackers in a match clip.
This concept, practised consistently at U11, transforms how the whole team defends. It is worth returning to regularly throughout the season.
diagramDescription: 30x20m zone with goal at one end. Two attackers (circles) shown with ball. Two defenders (triangles): FIRST DEFENDER shown pressing the ball-carrier to within 2m (press arrow, labeled DELAY NOT DIVE). SECOND DEFENDER shown in covering position behind and to the side (labeled COVER THE SPACE). Correct spacing shown between the two defenders. WRONG example shown in corner: both defenders pressing simultaneously with empty space behind labeled DANGER ZONE. Communication arrow shown from second defender to first (HOLD call).

title: Advanced Possession — Rondo With Transition
objective: Develop elite-level possession play that seamlessly transitions between keeping the ball and attacking when the opportunity arises.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain possession at high intensity with minimal touches under real defensive pressure
Players develop the instant transition from possession to attack the moment defensive pressure drops
Players build the cognitive speed that processes defensive positions while maintaining possession
Players experience the intensity of elite possession training
players: Groups of 8 (6 in possession vs 2 defenders, rotating)
organisation: 6v2 rondo in a 12x12m square. Standard rondo rules apply but with a transition element: if the 6 complete 10 consecutive passes, the 2 defenders must immediately retreat to a goal 15m away while the 6 attack. The transition window is 5 seconds.
instructions: 6 possession players in the square. 2 defenders inside pressing.
Standard 6v2 rondo: maximum 2 touches, defenders swap when they win the ball.
TRANSITION TRIGGER: when the possession team completes 10 consecutive passes, the coach calls BREAK.
On BREAK: both defenders immediately sprint to a mini-goal 15m away (they become defenders of this goal).
The 6 possession players have 5 seconds to attack the mini-goal with a maximum of 3 players going forward.
The remaining 3 possession players hold their positions (do not all attack) to maintain structure.
After the attack (goal, save, or 5 seconds elapsed): return to the rondo. Reset the pass count.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): 10-pass trigger. Players focus on the rondo quality and the transition speed.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): reduce trigger to 7 passes, increasing the frequency of transitions.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): random trigger (coach calls BREAK at any moment). Players must always be ready to transition instantly.
coachingPoints: THE RONDO AND THE TRANSITION ARE ONE CONNECTED ACTION: elite players do not think of possession and attack as two separate modes. The possession exists to create the moment to attack. The transition is the purpose of the possession.
KEEP THE PASS COUNT IN YOUR HEAD: every player in the possession group should be counting the passes. Losing track means being caught flat-footed when the BREAK is called.

THE FIRST TOUCH AFTER BREAK MUST GO FORWARD: when the BREAK is called, the player with the ball plays forward immediately. No recycling sideways. The transition window is 5 seconds — every touch must progress.
ONLY 3 PLAYERS ATTACK: the team that sends all 6 players forward leaves no defensive cover. Discipline in the transition means 3 attack and 3 hold. The 3 who hold are just as important.

TRANSITION SPEED IS EVERYTHING: the 5-second window closes fast. A team that transitions slowly gives the defenders time to organise. A team that transitions instantly finds defenders still retreating.
THE DEFENDERS SPRINT TO DEFEND: after the BREAK, the defenders must sprint to their defensive position. A defender who jogs concedes a goal every time.

POSSESSION QUALITY DETERMINES TRANSITION QUALITY: sloppy possession (heavy touches, inaccurate passes) leaves the possession team out of position when the transition comes. Clean possession means every player is in the correct position to transition explosively.
AFTER THE ATTACK, RESET MENTALLY: whether the attack produced a goal or not, the team must immediately reset their mental state to possession mode. The rondo continues.
commonMistakes: Pass count lost — team not ready for the transition when it comes
All 6 players attacking on the BREAK, leaving no defensive cover
First touch after BREAK going sideways or backward rather than forward
Defenders jogging rather than sprinting to their defensive positions after the BREAK
Possession quality dropping as players begin anticipating the transition rather than focusing on the rondo
corrections: Pass count lost: designate one player whose only job in Phase 1 is to count aloud after every 5 passes (FIVE... SIX... SEVEN...) so everyone knows the count
All 6 attacking: colour the 6 possession players in two groups (3 red, 3 yellow). Only red can attack on BREAK. Only yellow can hold. This enforces the structure from the start
First touch backward: strictly enforce the forward-touch rule. Any backward touch after BREAK means the attack is cancelled and possession resets
Defenders jogging: the defenders earn 1 point if they are in their defensive position before the attackers reach the halfway point to the mini-goal
Possession quality dropping: reduce the trigger to 12 passes and enforce the 2-touch maximum strictly to prevent the anticipation from affecting rondo quality
progressions: Reduce the transition window to 3 seconds, significantly increasing the urgency
Add a GK in the mini-goal who can come off their line to narrow the angle
Remove the pass trigger entirely and replace with the random BREAK in Phase 3 for the full session
regressions: Increase trigger to 15 passes to give more time for the transition habit to develop before the frequency increases
Allow 3 touches in the rondo while the transition concept is being learned before reducing to 2
Slow the defenders sprint requirement — allow them to jog to their defensive position — before adding the urgency of a sprint
matchApplication: The rondo with transition drill is the most sophisticated expression of the possession-to-attack philosophy that defines elite football. Barcelona trained this way daily under Guardiola. The seamless shift between maintaining possession and exploding into attack is not an instinct — it is a trained response built through thousands of repetitions of this specific pattern. At U17, players are physically and cognitively ready for the demands of this drill. The combination of rondo technical quality, pass-counting focus, and explosive transition creates the complete possession-and-attack intelligence that characterises the most sophisticated players.
coachTips: The pass-counting element is what separates this drill from a standard rondo. Enforce it from the very beginning.
The 3-hold-3-attack structure is the most important tactical discipline. Address it immediately if it breaks down.
Film the transition moment from above. The speed of transition and the 3-3 split are both visible from above but difficult to evaluate from the sideline.
Connect to Guardiola Barcelona: discuss how the rondo was used as both a technical training tool and as a tactical preparation for the transition moments in matches.
This drill can be used as the primary possession warm-up for the entire season. The quality improves measurably across a season of consistent practice.
diagramDescription: 12x12m square. 6 possession players (circles) in various positions. 2 defenders (triangles) pressing inside. Pass count shown: 7-8-9-10. BREAK call shown from coach at 10 passes. Mini-goal shown 15m from the square. Transition arrows shown: 2 defenders sprinting to defend the mini-goal, 3 possession players (red circles) attacking toward mini-goal, 3 possession players (yellow circles) holding their positions in and around the square. 5-second window timer shown.

title: High Press — Team Press as a Weapon
objective: Develop a coordinated high press as a primary attacking tactical weapon used to win the ball high up the pitch and create immediate goal-scoring opportunities.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a coordinated high press that wins the ball within the opposition 30m
Players understand the specific triggers and collective reactions that make a press successful
Players develop the physical conditioning to press repeatedly across a full match
Teams build the collective identity of a pressing team that uses defence as an attacking weapon
organisation: Full pitch, full teams. One team presses high throughout the session. The other builds from the back. The pressing team has specific press triggers, shapes, and recovery protocols. This is a full tactical session run at match intensity.
instructions: PRESS SHAPE: pressing team sets up in a 4-4-2 high press structure.
THE FRONT 2: press both centre-backs as soon as the GK plays the ball out. Their press angle forces the play to one side.
THE WIDE MIDFIELDER (ball side): sprints to press the full-back as soon as the CB receives.
THE CENTRAL MIDFIELDERS: step up to cover the opposition central midfielders, blocking the escape pass.
THE FAR WIDE MIDFIELDER: holds position to cut off the switch pass.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): walkthrough at 70 percent. Building team plays out slowly. Pressing team rehearses the shape and triggers.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): full intensity. Building team plays at match pace. Pressing team presses at 100 percent.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): transition element added. When the pressing team wins the ball in the press, they have 5 seconds to score. If they do not score in 5 seconds, possession resets.
Score: pressing team earns 3 points for winning the ball in the press and scoring within 5 seconds.
coachingPoints: THE PRESS IS A TEAM ACTION, NOT AN INDIVIDUAL ONE: one player pressing alone is just chasing. The value of the press comes from all 5-6 nearby players moving simultaneously so that every passing option for the opposition is covered.

THE FRONT 2 ANGLE IS EVERYTHING: the front 2 must press at angles that prevent the play going forward centrally AND prevent the easy switch. If their angles are wrong, the press can be bypassed with one pass.

THE TOUCHLINE IS YOUR TEAMMATE: the most effective pressing trap is when the ball-carrier has the touchline on one side and a pressing player on the other. Use the pitch boundaries as part of the press.
PRESS INTENSITY OR DO NOT PRESS: a half-intensity press is the worst possible outcome. It disrupts the defensive shape without winning the ball. 100 percent effort or hold the shape.
RECOVERY WHEN THE PRESS IS BEATEN: when the press is bypassed, every player drops immediately into a compact defensive shape. No individual chasing. Organised recovery.

THE PRESS CREATES TRANSITION OPPORTUNITIES: the highest-quality goal-scoring opportunities come from winning the ball high up the pitch from a press. The opposition is in an attacking shape and cannot defend quickly.

PHYSICAL CONDITIONING IS REQUIRED: at adult level, a full-match high press requires exceptional physical preparation. The press works in short 5-10 second bursts. Recovery between bursts is as important as the press itself.

WATCH THE TRIGGERS COLLECTIVELY: every player in the pressing team must recognise the same trigger at the same moment. A GK pass to a CB, a heavy touch, a backward pass — these must all trigger the same immediate collective response.
commonMistakes: Individual players pressing alone without the collective reaction of nearby teammates
Front 2 press angles allowing easy central passes that bypass the press
Half-intensity pressing that disrupts the shape without creating any opportunity
No recovery organisation when the press is beaten — individuals chasing
Physical fatigue causing the press to lose intensity as the session progresses
corrections: Individual pressing: immediately pause and show which nearby players should have moved simultaneously with the pressing player — the collective reaction must be visible
Wrong front 2 angles: use cone markers showing the correct press angle for each front 2 player relative to the CB positions
Half intensity: remove the option of half-intensity pressing entirely. If the trigger occurs, full press or the session resets to the start of that possession
No recovery: blow the whistle 3 seconds after the press is beaten. Every player must be in recovery position before the whistle or they lose a point for the team
Physical fatigue: build in 30-second micro-rests every 5 minutes where both teams walk and recover before resuming at full intensity
progressions: Add a 4-second press timer: if the ball is not won within 4 seconds of the trigger, the press must immediately transform into an organised recovery
Two-press teams: both teams press. The team that presses more successfully (most ball wins in the opposition 30m) wins the session
Full match with the press as the designated tactical instruction for one team throughout
regressions: Reduce to pressing only the GK (one consistent simple trigger) for the first phase before adding the full trigger system
Reduce to 7v7 to make the press coordination simpler
Use a slow build-out team (building team restricted to slow passes) to give the pressing team more time to coordinate before adding full match pace
matchApplication: The high press used as a systematic attacking weapon changed the nature of professional football in the 2010s. Jurgen Klopp at Dortmund and Liverpool, Pep Guardiola at Barcelona and Man City, Mauricio Pochettino at Spurs — all built their team identities around the press as a primary offensive tool. At adult level, teams with a well-organised and physically conditioned high press create goal-scoring opportunities directly from their defensive actions, turning ball-winning into immediate attacks. This session specifically develops the press as a complete team system rather than just individual pressing behaviour.
coachTips: This is the most physically demanding session in the tactical category. Build it into a point in the session where players are warmed up but not yet fatigued.
The front 2 angle coaching point is the most technically specific element. Spend 5 minutes specifically on angle of approach before any live press attempts.
Film the session from above if possible. The collective press shape and the moments when it succeeds or fails are only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of Liverpool or Dortmund pressing as a team and identify each players role and movement.
A team that presses well together has a collective identity that is enormously powerful for team culture as well as match performance.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Building team shown in GK build-out shape. PRESSING TEAM shown in 4-4-2 press formation: Front 2 (arrows showing angled press toward both CBs, trapping play to the right side). Ball-side wide midfielder sprinting to press the full-back. Central midfielders stepping up to cover opposition CMs. Far wide midfielder holding to cut off switch. TOUCHLINE TRAP shown: full-back trapped between the wide press and the touchline. 5-second transition window shown after ball is won. Score system shown.

title: Counter-Attacking — Organisation and Rehearsal
objective: Develop a systematic counter-attack organisation that the whole team understands and can execute automatically from different transition moments.
learningOutcomes: Teams develop a shared and rehearsed counter-attack pattern that every player understands their role in
Players build the automatic speed and decision-making of a rehearsed counter-attack
Teams understand the relationship between defensive compactness and effective counter-attacking
Players develop the clinical finishing ability required to convert counter-attack opportunities
organisation: Full pitch. Team A defends in a compact shape and counter-attacks when they win the ball. Team B attacks and presses. The focus is on Team A developing and rehearsing their specific counter-attack patterns from different areas of winning the ball.
instructions: COUNTER-ATTACK PATTERN 1 — WIN IN THE DEFENSIVE THIRD:
When Team A wins the ball in their defensive third, the striker (Player 9) immediately sprints in behind the opposition line.
The ball-winner plays directly to Player 9 in behind within 2 touches.
Player 9 attacks the GK 1v1 or holds until a wide player (Player 7 or 11) arrives in support.
COUNTER-ATTACK PATTERN 2 — WIN IN THE MIDFIELD:
When Team A wins the ball in midfield, a central midfielder (Player 8) carries the ball forward while wide players sprint into wide channels.
Player 8 either shoots or plays to a wide player in the channel for a cross.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): Team B attacks at 50 percent intensity. Team A rehearses both counter-attack patterns when they win the ball.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): full intensity. Team B attacks fully. Team A counter-attacks from every ball they win.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): 5 points for a goal from a counter-attack within 5 touches. 1 point for any other goal.
coachingPoints: THE COUNTER-ATTACK MUST BE PLANNED IN ADVANCE: the most effective counter-attacking teams have pre-planned, rehearsed patterns that every player knows. Improvised counter-attacks are less effective because they rely on individual decisions under time pressure.

THE STRIKER RUNS BEFORE THE BALL IS WON: in Pattern 1, Player 9 begins their run the moment they see the ball is about to be won — not after it is won. A half-second head start makes the difference between a counter-attack chance and an offside flag.

2-TOUCH MAXIMUM FROM THE BALL-WINNER IN PATTERN 1: the ball must leave the ball-winner within 2 touches. More touches and the defenders have organised. The ball-winner identifies Player 9 immediately on winning the ball.

WIDTH IN PATTERN 2: when the midfield wins the ball and carries forward, the wide players must be sprinting into the wide channels simultaneously. A narrow counter-attack is easy to defend. Width stretches the retreating defence.

CLINICAL FINISHING IS THE BOTTLENECK: most counter-attack opportunities are wasted not because of poor organisation but because of poor finishing when the chance arrives. The 5-point bonus for a counter-attack goal creates an explicit incentive to be clinical.

DEFENSIVE COMPACTNESS ENABLES THE COUNTER: Team A can only counter-attack from ball wins. Ball wins come from a compact defensive shape that forces errors. The defence and the counter-attack are connected.
KNOW YOUR ROLE IN ADVANCE: every player in Team A should know their specific role in each pattern before the session begins. The pattern must be automatic.

TRANSITION SPEED DETERMINES THE OUTCOME: the difference between a successful counter-attack and a failed one is often measured in half-seconds. The team that moves first, fastest, and most purposefully wins the transition.
commonMistakes: Player 9 not starting their run until after the ball is won, missing the crucial head start
Ball-winner taking more than 2 touches before playing to Player 9, allowing defenders to recover
Wide players not sprinting into channels in Pattern 2, keeping the counter-attack narrow and easier to defend
No finishing quality — counter-attacks reaching the final third but not converting chances
Teams not understanding their specific role in each pattern before the session begins
corrections: Late striker run: in Phase 1 specifically, coach calls BALL WON 1 second before Team A actually wins it, giving Player 9 a cue to begin their run early
More than 2 touches from ball-winner: enforce a 2-touch maximum strictly with immediate possession reset if exceeded
Wide players narrow: wide players must touch a wide channel cone (placed at the touchlines 30m from goal) before receiving any pass in Pattern 2
No finishing quality: add a dedicated 5-minute finishing phase where only counter-attack finish scenarios are practiced
Roles not understood: all players must be able to describe their specific role in both patterns before Phase 1 begins. Any player who cannot describe their role is walked through it individually
progressions: Reduce counter-attack window to 4 touches instead of 5, increasing the urgency of execution
Add a second counter-attack pattern from a defensive set piece (corner or free kick) when the ball is cleared
Full match with counter-attack as the explicit game plan for Team A: all goals must come from organised counter-attacks to score
regressions: Use a walking/jogging opposition for Phase 1 to allow Team A to rehearse the patterns at reduced intensity
Reduce to Pattern 1 only for the full session before adding Pattern 2
Allow Player 9 an unlimited run-up time before the ball-winner must play the forward pass, reducing the timing pressure
matchApplication: Counter-attacking as an organised team system is one of the most effective tactical approaches in football and is used by elite teams ranging from Atletico Madrid and Chelsea (under Mourinho) to Leicester City in their title-winning season. The key distinction between a systematic counter-attack and an ad-hoc one is that every player knows their role and executes it automatically — the striker runs before the ball is won, the wide players sprint into channels simultaneously, and the ball moves forward within 2 touches. This planning and rehearsal is what makes counter-attacks decisive rather than opportunistic.
coachTips: Both counter-attack patterns must be walked through in detail with all players before any live practice begins.
The connection between the defence and the counter-attack (compactness creates the opportunities) should be explained explicitly.
Film Phase 2 from the side to evaluate transition speed and pattern execution.
Connect to specific professional examples: Leicester City 2015-16 and Atletico Madrid under Simeone are the clearest examples of organised counter-attacking as a primary system.
Running this session monthly and specifically evaluating which parts of the pattern break down each time produces rapid improvement in counter-attack quality across a season.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. PATTERN 1 shown: Team A defending compactly in their own half. Ball won by a defender (star). Player 9 shown already running (run started before ball was won, arrow showing early run). 2-touch pass sequence shown from ball-winner to Player 9 in behind. Player 9 shown 1v1 with GK. PATTERN 2 shown: ball won in midfield by Player 8, Player 8 carrying forward (dribble arrow), wide players 7 and 11 shown sprinting into wide channels (sprint arrows). 5-point counter-attack bonus shown.

title: Building From the Back — Adults Under Senior Pressure
objective: Develop a complete and systematic build-out-from-the-back approach that functions under the high press and physical intensity of adult competitive football.
learningOutcomes: Teams develop a coherent system for playing out from the back under adult-level pressing intensity
Players build the composure and technical ability to maintain possession in their own defensive third
GK develops the decision-making and distribution quality required as a build-out starting point
Teams understand the positional requirements of each role in the build-out system
organisation: Full pitch. Team A builds from the back. Team B presses with 4-6 players. Team A must use a structured build-out system to progress from their defensive third into the attacking half. This is a full-team tactical session for adult players.
instructions: BUILD-OUT SYSTEM POSITIONS:
GK: 3-5m off the goal line, directs the build.
CENTRE-BACKS: split wide to the edges of the penalty area creating a 3-man base line with the GK.
DEFENSIVE MIDFIELDER: drops between the two CBs or slightly in front as the link to the midfield.
FULL-BACKS: push into midfield positions creating width and a higher passing option.
ATTACKING MIDFIELDERS: position between the lines to receive and turn.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): Team B presses with 3 players. Team A rehearses the system at 70 percent intensity.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): Team B presses with 5 players at full intensity. Team A must use the system to play out.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full game. Score system: 3 points for successfully playing from GK into the attacking half with 5 or fewer passes. 1 point for any other goal.
coachingPoints: THE GK IS THE FOUNDATION: a GK who is comfortable receiving the ball and distributing accurately to the correct target is the most important player in the build-out. Without a capable GK, the system cannot work.

THE BACK 3 CREATES THE PLATFORM: the GK and two split CBs create a stable back 3 that the opposition press must cover. Covering 3 players spread across the full penalty area width requires 3 pressing players — this already stretches the opposition.

THE DEFENSIVE MIDFIELDER IS THE LINK: the DM position between or just in front of the CBs is the most important midfield position in the build-out. They provide the link from the back 3 to the higher options.
FULL-BACKS PUSH WIDE AND HIGH: as the build progresses, the full-backs push into midfield positions, providing wider options that stretch the press further and create space centrally.

PATIENCE IS A SKILL: under intense adult pressure, the natural response is to play long quickly. Resist this unless genuinely necessary. Patient build-up that uses 4-5 passes to play through the press creates better attacking positions than a 50-50 long ball.
STAY CONNECTED: the distances between players in the build-out system must be maintained. Gaps between the back 3 and the midfield allow the pressing team to intercept the connecting pass.
SWITCH TO THE FAR SIDE: when the press overloads one side, the first clean switch to the other side usually results in a free player at the far side with time and space.

THE LONG BALL IS A TACTICAL OPTION, NOT A TECHNICAL FAILURE: when the press covers all short options, the long ball to a specific target is a correct decision. It should be planned and specific, not panicked and random.
commonMistakes: GK not comfortable receiving under pressure, causing panic and poor distribution decisions
CBs not splitting wide enough, clustering centrally and making the build-out too easy to press
DM not showing into the correct position to receive the link pass from the CBs
Patience breaking down under pressure — long balls played when short options were available
The build-out system breaking down completely when the press intensifies in Phase 2
corrections: GK discomfort: specifically practice GK receiving under pressure separately (a server and 2 pressers) before the full team session
CBs not splitting: use cone markers at the correct wide positions for the CBs. They must stand at the markers before the build-out begins
DM not showing: the DM must verbally call READY before any CB can pass to them — this triggers the correct showing movement
Patience breaking down: enforce a minimum of 3 passes before any long ball in Phase 1 to build the patience habit
System breakdown in Phase 2: reduce the pressing team back to 3 players for a reset period before reintroducing full pressure
progressions: Add an advanced pressing trigger: if Team B wins the ball within Team As defensive third, they score 5 points — creating maximum urgency for Team A to execute the system cleanly
Full game with the build-out system as the mandatory approach for 15 consecutive minutes before any other approach is allowed
Remove the DM position from the system and require the full-backs to fill that role, testing a more advanced build-out variation
regressions: Use a passive pressing team (holding positions but not sprinting to press) for the full first phase until the positional system is established
Reduce the pressing team to 2 players for Phase 1
Allow each player in the build-out to use unlimited touches until the positional habits are established before introducing touch restrictions
matchApplication: Building from the back at adult level under genuine pressing intensity is one of the most challenging and rewarding team tactical concepts to develop. Teams that can reliably build from the back maintain possession more effectively, create higher-quality attacking opportunities, and control the tempo of matches. The system requires the GK to function as a genuine outfield player, requires the CBs to be comfortable under pressure, and requires collective discipline across 8-9 players simultaneously. Developing this at adult level takes many sessions but produces a team that is qualitatively harder to play against and more satisfying to play in.
coachTips: Walk through the system positions explicitly before any live practice, physically placing every player in their role.
The GK is the player most worth investing specific coaching time in before this session.
Film the build-out from above to evaluate the spacings, the positions, and where the system breaks down under pressure.
Connect to the professional teams that have made building from the back their defining identity: Man City, Liverpool, Spain.
Run this session monthly across a season. The positional habits and collective confidence build gradually and are still improving 6 months in.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. BUILD-OUT SYSTEM shown: GK on edge of 6-yard box. Two CBs split wide to edges of penalty area (3-man base). DM shown in pocket between CBs and midfield. Full-backs shown pushing into wide midfield positions. Attacking midfielders shown between lines. PRESSING TEAM shown with 5 players in a press shape. SWITCH OPTION highlighted: press overloading left side, switch pass arrow going to right CB who is free. Long ball option shown as alternative when all short options covered. 5-pass maximum shown to reach attacking half.

title: Transition Moments — Full Team Drill
objective: Develop the complete transition moment — both to attack and to defence — as a coordinated team response that every player executes automatically.
learningOutcomes: Players execute an instant and organised transition to attack within 2 seconds of winning the ball
Players execute an instant and organised transition to defence within 2 seconds of losing the ball
Teams develop a shared understanding of their roles in both transitions
Players build the physical and mental intensity to execute transitions at match speed across a full session
organisation: Full pitch. Both teams play a high-intensity game where transitions are the primary coaching focus. Coach calls TRANSITION at every change of possession. Both teams must execute their transition (to attack or to defence) correctly and instantly. Everything else is secondary to the transition moment.
instructions: Both teams play a normal match on the full pitch.
AT EVERY CHANGE OF POSSESSION: the coach immediately calls TRANSITION.
TEAM THAT JUST WON THE BALL: must look forward first within 1 second. If a forward pass exists, it must be played. Wide players sprint forward immediately.

TEAM THAT JUST LOST THE BALL: must press the nearest player to the ball immediately OR drop into shape if the ball is far away. No individual decisions — the nearest player presses, all others compact.

SCORING SYSTEM: 3 points for a goal scored within 6 seconds of a transition moment (fast attack). 2 points for winning the ball back within 4 seconds of losing it (fast press). 1 point for any other goal.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): coach pauses after every transition and evaluates both teams responses. Freeze-frame coaching.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): continuous play. Coach calls TRANSITION but does not pause.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): no coach call. Players must recognise and react to transition moments independently.
coachingPoints: EVERY POSSESSION CHANGE IS A TRANSITION: in a 90-minute match there are 200+ possession changes. Every single one is a transition moment. A team that executes 200 transitions well is almost impossible to beat.

THE 2-SECOND WINDOW: research shows that the 2 seconds immediately after a possession change are when the most dangerous opportunities are created (in attack) and the most conceded (in defence). These 2 seconds must be automatic.
TEAM THAT WINS THE BALL — LOOK FORWARD FIRST: the most common error after winning the ball is playing safe sideways or backward. Look forward first. If the option exists, take it immediately.
TEAM THAT LOSES THE BALL — NEAREST PRESSES IMMEDIATELY: the player nearest the ball when it is lost must press immediately. Not after looking around. Not after reacting. Immediately.

TRANSITION AND FITNESS ARE CONNECTED: executing transitions correctly requires physical intensity. A team that is tired cannot press immediately after losing the ball. Physical conditioning and tactical organisation work together.
WIDE PLAYERS IN ATTACK TRANSITIONS: when the ball is won, wide players sprint forward immediately. They do not wait to see what the ball-winner does. They run.
CENTRAL PLAYERS IN DEFENCE TRANSITIONS: when the ball is lost, central players compact immediately. They do not press toward the ball. They compact to cover the most dangerous central areas.

THE TEAM THAT TRANSITIONS BETTER WINS MORE MATCHES: across a full season, the team with the fastest and most organised transitions will consistently win more matches than a team with better technical quality but slower transitions.
commonMistakes: Pausing after a possession change instead of transitioning immediately
Looking around to assess the situation instead of executing the learned transition response
Wide players waiting for the ball rather than sprinting forward in attack transitions
Multiple players pressing the ball carrier in defence transitions, leaving gaps elsewhere
Transition quality dropping in the final 10 minutes of the session due to physical fatigue
corrections: Pausing after possession change: the coach calls TRANSITION loudly and immediately after every change. The call is the trigger — any player who does not move within 1 second of the call is immediately coached
Looking around: emphasise repeatedly that the transition response must be automatic. Remove the need to assess by training the specific automatic response for each role
Wide players waiting: in Phase 1, specifically coach and celebrate every wide player sprint that begins before the ball reaches the next player
Multiple pressing: show the gap created when 2+ players press simultaneously. One presses, all others compact — this is the rule
Fatigue affecting transitions: reduce the session length or introduce 90-second water breaks every 8 minutes to maintain quality
progressions: Remove the TRANSITION call and require players to recognise all possession changes independently — tests whether the habit is now genuine
Add a counter-attack element specifically: any fast-transition goal (under 4 seconds) earns 5 points
Combine with the high-press session for a complete defensive identity session
regressions: Use the freeze-frame method throughout Phase 1 and into Phase 2 before allowing continuous play
Slow the game pace using only 3-touch football throughout to give players more time to identify and react to transitions
Reduce to 8v8 to decrease the number of transitions per minute while the concepts are established
matchApplication: Transition speed and organisation is consistently identified by elite coaches and analysts as one of the top two or three factors that determine match outcomes. The 2 seconds immediately after a possession change are when the most goals are scored and conceded in professional football, and teams that consistently outperform their opponents in these moments — pressing faster when they lose the ball, attacking faster when they win it — create a cumulative tactical advantage across 90 minutes that is decisive. This drill specifically trains these moments as a collective team habit at U17, where players are physically and tactically ready to execute them at the intensity required.
coachTips: The freeze-frame coaching in Phase 1 is the most valuable coaching tool in this session. Use it generously and specifically.
The scoring system (3 points for transitions within 6 seconds) is the most important game element. Make the points visible and celebrated.
Film Phase 2 from above. The transition movements of all players simultaneously are only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional transition data: share statistics on how many professional goals are scored within 5 seconds of a possession change.
This session is the most physically demanding in the tactical category. Build it into a point in the season when fitness levels are high.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. POSSESSION CHANGE MOMENT shown with a star. Two transition arrows: ATTACK TRANSITION (team just won ball — forward arrow from ball-winner, sprint arrows from wide players, look-forward priority shown) and DEFENCE TRANSITION (team just lost ball — nearest player press arrow, all others shown compacting centrally). 2-second window shown as a timer icon. SCORING SYSTEM: 3 points (goal under 6 seconds), 2 points (ball won back under 4 seconds), 1 point (other goal). Phase 3 shown with no coach call — players read and react independently.

title: Staying Goal-Side — First Defensive Concept
objective: Introduce the most fundamental defensive concept: always staying between the attacker and your own goal.
learningOutcomes: Players understand what goal-side means and why it is important
Players can identify and move to a goal-side position in simple 1v1 situations
Players develop the habit of getting between the attacker and the goal as their first defensive action
Players build the spatial awareness needed to understand where danger comes from
equipment: Cones for a simple zone and mini-goal
organisation: Pairs in a 15m channel. Attacker has the ball at one end. Defender starts beside them. On GO, the attacker dribbles toward a mini-goal at the far end. The defender must move to get between the attacker and the goal before the attacker reaches it.
instructions: Mark a 15m channel with a mini-goal at one end.
Attacker and defender start side by side at the opposite end.
On GO: attacker dribbles slowly toward the mini-goal. Defender moves to get between the attacker and the goal.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): attacker walks. Defender focuses purely on positioning goal-side. No tackling required.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): attacker jogs. Defender must move faster to get goal-side before the attacker gets too close to the goal.
PHASE 3 (4 minutes): attacker moves at 70 percent. Defender must get goal-side AND slow the attacker down by jockeying (staying close without diving in).
Swap roles every 4 minutes. Coach celebrates every time the defender successfully gets goal-side.
coachingPoints: GOAL-SIDE MEANS BETWEEN THEM AND THE GOAL: goal-side is the position between the attacker and your own goal. If you are goal-side, the attacker has to go through you to reach the goal. If you are not goal-side, they can run around you.
GET THERE QUICKLY: the sooner you get goal-side, the safer you are. Sprint to get between the attacker and the goal as fast as you can.
STAY BETWEEN THE BALL AND THE GOAL: as the attacker moves, you must move with them. Wherever the ball goes, you must be between it and the goal.
DO NOT DIVE IN STRAIGHT AWAY: once you are goal-side, do not immediately try to tackle. Stay close and make the attacker slow down. They cannot score easily while you are in their way.
SMALL STEPS WHEN CLOSE: when you are close to the attacker, use small shuffling steps to stay between them and the goal. Big steps make you slow to change direction.
WATCH THE BALL NOT THE PLAYER: experienced attackers will try to make you look at their eyes or their body to trick you. Always watch the ball. The ball tells you which direction to defend.

YOUR JOB IS TO PROTECT THE GOAL: your goal as a defender is not to win the ball — it is to protect the goal. If you can stop the attacker from scoring, you have done your job perfectly even if you never touch the ball.
DEFENDING IS AS IMPORTANT AS SCORING: every time you protect your goal by getting goal-side, you are as important to your team as the player who scores a goal. Defence wins games.
commonMistakes: Running alongside the attacker rather than between them and the goal
Diving in to tackle immediately rather than getting goal-side first
Losing the goal-side position as the attacker moves sideways
Watching the attackers body and being faked out rather than watching the ball
Giving up when the attacker gets past and not continuing to track back
corrections: Running alongside: physically walk the defender through the correct movement path — diagonally in front of the attacker toward the goal before adjusting sideways
Diving in immediately: remove tackling in Phase 1 completely. Touching the ball at all in Phase 1 is an error
Losing goal-side position: place a cone behind the attacker at the goal level. The defender must always be between the attacker and this cone
Watching the body: ask the defender to point at the ball throughout the rep — forces eyes on the ball
Giving up: specifically celebrate continued effort after being beaten. Chase and track is always the right response
progressions: Attacker can now change direction at will, testing whether the defender can maintain goal-side position through lateral movement
Add a passive second attacker who the defender must also be aware of
Full 1v1 with the defender allowed to tackle: tests whether the goal-side habit holds up when tackling is also an option
regressions: Attacker walks at all times in Phase 1 and 2 to give the defender maximum time to learn the correct positioning
Use a large mini-goal (4m wide) to make the goal-side concept more forgiving initially
Start with the defender already in the goal-side position and simply ask them to maintain it while the attacker walks sideways
matchApplication: Goal-side positioning is the single most important concept in individual defending because it determines whether the defender is a barrier between the attacker and the goal or simply a spectator. Every other defensive skill (jockeying, tackling, recovery runs) is secondary to the fundamental act of getting between the ball and the goal. Introducing this concept at U9 — when players are beginning to think about their defensive responsibilities — builds the spatial defensive awareness that everything else in defending builds on.
coachTips: This is a brand new concept for U9 players. Walk through it physically yourself first, showing what goal-side means and what not-goal-side means.
Use the specific language consistently: GOAL-SIDE every time a defender achieves it, NOT GOAL-SIDE to correct it.
The celebration of good goal-side positioning is the most important coaching tool here. Make it feel like an achievement.
Keep the session light and fun. U9 players are learning a concept, not a skill — the concept will develop into a skill over many repetitions.
Connect to their favourite professional defender. Ask them what a defender does. Build the defensive identity.
diagramDescription: 15m channel with mini-goal at far end. Attacker (circle) shown dribbling from left. Defender (triangle) shown in two positions: WRONG POSITION (alongside attacker, no protection of goal), CORRECT POSITION (between attacker and goal, diagonal positioning). Goal-side arrow showing the diagonal movement path the defender must take to get goal-side. BALL TRACKING label shown with eyes-on-ball indicator. Mini-goal shown with protection zone between defender and goal.

title: Chasing Back — Never Give Up
objective: Build the mentality and habit of chasing back to recover defensively after losing the ball, teaching young players that every defensive effort matters.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the habit of chasing back toward their goal when their team loses the ball
Players build the mentality that every sprint back is a valuable contribution
Players experience how chasing back can change the outcome of a defensive situation
Players connect the effort of chasing back to protecting their teammates and their goal
pitchSize: 25m channel with mini-goals at each end
organisation: 25m channel. Teams of 2-3 attack and defend. When the attacking team scores or loses the ball, they immediately must sprint back to defend their own goal as the other team immediately counter-attacks. The chase-back is the coaching focus.
instructions: Set up the 25m channel with a mini-goal at each end.
Team A attacks toward Goal B. Team B defends.
When Team A loses the ball or scores: Team B immediately attacks toward Goal A.
Team A must sprint back to defend Goal A.
THE CHASE-BACK RULE: every player from Team A must visibly sprint back toward Goal A the moment the ball changes. No walking. No standing still. Sprint.
COACH FOCUS: watch specifically for which players chase back immediately and which hesitate. Celebrate the chasers loudly.
After 5 minutes: swap roles.
Add the chase-back challenge: the coach counts how quickly all Team A players get back. Can they all be past the halfway line before Team B shoots? Time it.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): if a chasing-back player gets close enough to block a shot or intercept a pass, award their team a bonus point.
coachingPoints: CHASE BACK EVERY SINGLE TIME: it does not matter if you think your team will win the ball back quickly. It does not matter if you are tired. Chase back every time, every transition, without exception.
SPRINT, DO NOT JOG: jogging back is not chasing back. A jog back gives the opposition time to score before you arrive. Sprint with everything you have.
EVERY METRE MATTERS: even if you cannot catch the ball or the attacker, every metre you close reduces the space they have. Getting within 5m of an attacker changes what they can do.

THIS IS THE HARDEST THING IN FOOTBALL: chasing back when you have just attacked — when your legs are tired and you want to stop — is the hardest physical challenge in football. The players who do it every time are the most valuable players on any team.

YOUR TEAMMATES NEED YOU: when you do not chase back, your teammates are defending with one fewer player. Every player who does chase back makes the defending team more organised and more likely to stop the attack.
TURN AND GO IMMEDIATELY: the moment the ball changes hands, turn your body and start running. Do not watch what happens first. Turn and go.

CELEBRATE THE CHASE, NOT JUST THE TACKLE: a player who chases back 25m and arrives just as a teammate makes a tackle is just as important as the tackler. The chase forces the attacker to hurry and gives the teammate time to defend.
PROFESSIONALS DO THIS EVERY TIME: show players a professional who is famous for chasing back (Mohamed Salah, Sadio Mane) and explain that this is a defining quality of the best players in the world.
commonMistakes: Walking or jogging back instead of sprinting after losing the ball
Stopping to watch what happens before starting the chase-back
Players at the front of the attack not chasing back because they are furthest from their own goal
Giving up the chase when it seems like the opposition will score anyway
Not turning immediately when possession is lost
corrections: Walking back: the coach calls SPRINT every time possession changes until the sprint-back habit is automatic
Watching first: use a clapping signal — on the clap, every player immediately turns and runs. Repeat this 10 times before the game begins
Attackers not chasing: specifically name and celebrate front players who chase back (BRILLIANT WORK running back)
Giving up when behind: tell players specifically that every chase-back that makes the attacker hurry is a success even if the opposition scores
Not turning immediately: practice the turn separately — stand facing one way, coach calls TURN, players spin and sprint 5m. 10 reps before the game
progressions: Reduce the channel to 20m so the chase-back distance is smaller and more achievable for the youngest players
Add a specific chase-back target: a cone the chasing players must reach before the opposition takes their first shot
Full game with chase-back bonus points: any goal that is prevented directly by a chase-back earns the defending team 2 bonus points
regressions: Use a 15m channel so the chase-back distances are very short and immediately achievable
Start with a completely passive opposition who do not actually attack so players can practice the chase-back motion without any outcome pressure
Begin with all players already in their defensive positions and simply practice the habit of turning and sprinting from closer distances
matchApplication: The chase-back habit is one of the most important developmental habits in youth football because it is the foundation of the defensive work rate and mentality that determines how effective a team is when they do not have the ball. Players who develop the automatic chase-back response at U8 carry this habit into every level of football they subsequently play. Teams built on this mentality are characterised by a collective resilience and defensive effort that makes them significantly harder to break down — not because of superior tactical organisation but because of the simple physical reality that a team that chases back together is always harder to score against than one that does not.
coachTips: Your celebration of the chase-back is the most important coaching action in this session. Be loud and specific.
Name the players who chase back best and celebrate them as the heroes of the session.
Tell the professional connection story. Knowing that Mohamed Salah sprints back on every transition makes U8 players sprint back.
Film the chase-back transitions and watch them back with the team. Seeing themselves sprint back is genuinely motivating.
Make the chase-back a team challenge: can every single player be back past the halfway line before the opposition shoots? This collective target builds team identity around defensive effort.
diagramDescription: 25m channel with mini-goals at each end. Team A shown attacking toward Goal B at left end (circle arrows moving left). TRANSITION MOMENT shown with star — Team A loses ball. Chase-back arrows shown from all Team A players (circles) immediately sprinting back toward Goal A (right end). Team B shown attacking toward Goal A (triangle arrows moving right). Sprint speed indicators shown on Team A arrows. Bonus point marker shown for chase-back player arriving to help defend. Time indicator showing how quickly Team A must get back.

title: Body Position in Defence — Angles and Stance
objective: Develop the correct defensive body position (side-on, low, balanced) that allows effective jockeying and quick reaction in 1v1 defensive situations.
learningOutcomes: Players can adopt the correct defensive stance: side-on, knees bent, weight on balls of feet
Players understand why the side-on stance is more effective than facing the attacker directly
Players develop the jockeying movement that maintains the defensive stance while tracking a moving attacker
Players build the physical foundation for all future defensive work
organisation: Pairs in a 10m channel. Defender practices the defensive stance and jockeying movement. Attacker moves slowly and deliberately to give the defender time to adjust. Phase 3 introduces a live 1v1 where the defender uses the correct stance.
instructions: DEMONSTRATE THE DEFENSIVE STANCE first: feet shoulder-width apart, slight bend at the knees, weight on the balls of the feet, body slightly side-on (not square-on), hands out for balance.
Ask players: why side-on instead of square-on? Because side-on lets you move in any direction quickly. Square-on makes you slow to one side.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): defenders practice the stance in pairs. No ball. Partner gently pushes from different directions. Defender must stay balanced and recover immediately.

PHASE 2 (5 minutes): attacker walks slowly around a 10m zone with ball. Defender must stay in front of the attacker in the correct stance, shuffling sideways to track the movement without crossing their feet.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): live 1v1 in the 10m channel. Attacker tries to dribble through. Defender uses the correct stance and jockeys without diving in.
KEY JOCKEYING RULE: the defender cannot tackle in Phase 2. They can only jockey. This forces the habit of maintaining the stance.
coachingPoints: SIDE-ON STANCE IS FASTER: a square-on stance (facing the attacker directly) means one direction is always slower to move toward. A side-on stance keeps both directions equally accessible.
LOW CENTRE OF GRAVITY IS POWERFUL: bending the knees lowers the centre of gravity. A low centre of gravity means better balance and faster direction changes. A tall, upright defender is slow to move.
WEIGHT ON BALLS OF FEET — NEVER FLAT-FOOTED: a flat-footed defender cannot move quickly. The weight must be on the balls of the feet, ready to push off in any direction at any moment.
DO NOT CROSS YOUR FEET: when shuffling sideways in the jockeying position, never cross one foot over the other. Crossed feet mean a momentary loss of balance that the attacker can exploit.
STAY 1-2 METRES FROM THE ATTACKER: too close and the attacker can push the ball past you and run. Too far and you give them time to shoot. 1-2 metres is the jockeying distance.
HANDS OUT FOR BALANCE: hands held out to the sides (like a balance beam walker) naturally maintain the low, balanced defensive position. Hands at the sides tend to cause the body to become upright.
WATCH THE BALL NOT THE ATTACKER FEET OR EYES: attacker will try to fake with their eyes and their body. The ball cannot fake. Watch the ball.
BE PATIENT: jockeying is a patient skill. The aim is to force the attacker to make an error or wait for defensive support to arrive. Diving in too early is almost always wrong.
commonMistakes: Standing upright rather than in the low, bent-knee defensive stance
Facing the attacker square-on rather than in the side-on position
Crossing the feet when shuffling sideways, creating a moment of imbalance
Standing too far from the attacker (more than 2m), giving too much shooting space
Diving in to tackle in Phase 2 when jockeying is the required action
corrections: Upright stance: place a hand on the defenders head during the stance check — they should be noticeably lower than their natural height
Square-on: place a shoulder marker cone to the side showing the required side-on body angle
Crossing feet: add a small cone between the feet. If the cone is kicked, the feet have crossed
Too far away: use a rope or band 2m long between the attacker and defender. Defender must keep some tension on it
Diving in: remove tackling with a firm consistent rule and point out immediately every time a dive-in occurs
progressions: Attacker moves at 70 percent pace, genuinely testing whether the defensive stance holds under more pressure
Add a second attacker who the defender must be peripherally aware of while maintaining their stance against the first
Full competitive 1v1 with tackling allowed once the stance and jockeying habits are established
regressions: Use very slow attacker movement (walking pace only) throughout Phase 2 while the stance is learned
Practice the stance without any ball or attacker — purely balance and position drills — for 3 minutes before any live practice
Use a wide channel (15m) rather than 10m to give the defender more recovery space if they are moved out of position
matchApplication: The defensive stance is the physical foundation of all individual defending. Every other defensive skill — tackling, covering, recovering — is built on the platform of a correct body position. A defender in the wrong stance is slower, less balanced, and less effective in everything they do defensively. Establishing the correct stance at U10, when players are beginning to take individual defensive responsibility seriously, gives them a physical habit that serves them throughout their career. The side-on position, the low centre of gravity, and the weight on the balls of the feet are constants in effective defending from grassroots to professional football.
coachTips: Demonstrate the correct stance and the wrong stance side by side so players can see the difference before feeling it.
The push-from-different-directions activity in Phase 1 makes the balance benefit of the low stance immediately physical and tangible.
The no-cross-feet rule is the most specific and most commonly violated coaching point in this drill. Check for it constantly.
Connect to their favourite professional defender and show a photo or video clip of them in the jockeying defensive stance.
Return to defensive stance as a physical warm-up activity at the start of any defensive session across the season.
diagramDescription: 10m channel per pair. STANCE DIAGRAM shown: feet shoulder-width, knees bent (angle shown), weight on balls of feet (heels raised slightly), body side-on (angle shown), hands out for balance. WRONG STANCE shown beside it: upright, square-on, flat-footed. JOCKEYING MOVEMENT shown: defender shuffling sideways (no foot crossing), tracking attacker movement. 1-2 metre distance band shown between attacker and defender. Ball-watching indicator shown: eyes on ball, not on attacker body.

title: Senior Defending — Winning the Ball in Duels
objective: Develop the full range of adult-level tackling and ball-winning techniques used in physical senior duels.
learningOutcomes: Adult players can execute the block tackle, sliding tackle, and shoulder charge correctly and legally
Players develop the timing and commitment required to win physical duels in adult football
Players understand the legal versus illegal contact in adult defending
Players build the physical and mental confidence for full-contact defending at senior level
organisation: Three-phase session. Phase 1: block tackle technique against a cooperative partner. Phase 2: timing the tackle in a live 1v1. Phase 3: full-intensity physical duels in a competitive format.
instructions: PHASE 1 — TECHNIQUE (7 minutes):
Partner A holds the ball stationary. Partner B executes 10 block tackles from each side, focusing on the inside-of-foot contact, the drive through the ball, and the staying-on-feet completion.
Then: Partner A rolls the ball slowly. Partner B times the tackle for the moment of a slight heavy touch.
PHASE 2 — TIMING (8 minutes):
Live 1v1 in a 20m channel. Attacker has a 3m head start. Defender must recover, jockey, and wait for the correct moment to attempt the tackle.
Tackle only when: the ball goes slightly away from the attacker (heavy touch) OR the attacker is moving away from goal (reducing danger of a missed tackle).
PHASE 3 — COMPETITION (7 minutes):
Full intensity 1v1. No head start advantage for the attacker. Both players compete fully.
Shoulder charge practice: specifically practice the legal shoulder charge (shoulder to shoulder, both players upright and competing for the ball).
coachingPoints: THE TACKLE IS A LAST RESORT, NOT A FIRST OPTION: at adult level, jockeying and delaying to allow teammates to recover is almost always preferable to attempting a tackle. The tackle should only be attempted when the timing is correct.
