TIMING THE TACKLE TO THE HEAVY TOUCH: the correct moment to attempt a block tackle is when the ball is slightly away from the attacker feet. Attempting the tackle when the ball is under close control almost always results in a foul.
COMMIT FULLY OR DO NOT ATTEMPT: a half-committed tackle at adult level simply gives the attacker a free kick. Once the decision to tackle is made, drive through the ball with full force.
THE BLOCK TACKLE — DRIVE THROUGH THE BALL: the block tackle uses the inside of the foot to meet the ball and the body drives through it. The foot does not stab at the ball — it blocks and drives.

THE SLIDING TACKLE — DIRECTION OF THE SLIDE: at adult level the sliding tackle is a useful weapon but the direction of the slide matters enormously. Slide toward the ball, not across the body of the attacker. Slide from the side or from behind never from the front.

THE SHOULDER CHARGE IS LEGAL AND EFFECTIVE: shoulder-to-shoulder contact while competing for the ball is completely legal. The shoulder charge uses the upper arm and shoulder to make firm contact with the attackers shoulder while competing for the ball. This is not a push.
STAY ON YOUR FEET WHEN POSSIBLE: a defender who goes to ground has reduced their effectiveness for 2-3 seconds. Stay on your feet after any tackle attempt when physically possible.
ANTICIPATE THE ATTACKER PATTERN: in Phase 3, watch for the attackers preferred move across 3-4 reps and anticipate the next one. Senior defending involves reading opponents and adjusting.
commonMistakes: Attempting the tackle when the ball is under close control rather than waiting for the heavy touch
Half-committed tackling that fails to win the ball and creates a free kick
Confusion about the legality of the shoulder charge (treating all contact as illegal)
Going to ground unnecessarily on the sliding tackle when a standing tackle was available
Not recovering from a missed tackle — staying on the ground after the attempt
corrections: Wrong timing: use a clear trigger word (HEAVY TOUCH) called by the coach when the ball is correctly away from the attacker, training timing to the correct moment
Half-commitment: any tackle attempt that does not produce either a won ball or a clean foul (a real contact) is repeated until full commitment is demonstrated
Shoulder charge confusion: demonstrate the legal and illegal version explicitly — show the difference between a shoulder charge and a push using two players
Going to ground unnecessarily: add a point system where going to ground unnecessarily loses 1 point, incentivising the standing tackle option
Not recovering: time the recovery from every tackle attempt. Any recovery taking more than 2 seconds after a missed tackle is immediately coached
progressions: Add a second attacker who arrives 3 seconds after the first, requiring the defender to recover from the first duel before immediately defending the second
Move to 2v1 where the defender must delay and jockey until a second defender arrives
Full small-sided game with physical tackling contact allowed: tests whether technique holds up in a live game context
regressions: Slow attacker movement to 50 percent in Phase 2 while tackle timing is established
Remove the sliding tackle from Phase 1 and 2 entirely until block tackle technique is reliable
Use Phase 1 only (cooperative partner, stationary ball) for the first session before any live defending
matchApplication: Adult defending requires a complete range of ball-winning techniques because adult attackers use the full range of dribbling skills, pace variations, and physical duels that require a defender to be equipped with more than just jockeying. The block tackle, sliding tackle, and shoulder charge are all legal, effective, and valuable defensive tools at senior level. The key coaching emphasis in this drill — timing the tackle to the heavy touch and committing fully once the decision is made — is what separates effective senior defending from both the reckless early dive-in and the passive jockey that never produces a ball win.
coachTips: Safety and legality must be established clearly before any contact practice begins. Explain what is legal and what is not.
The shoulder charge clarification is particularly valuable at adult level where many players are uncertain about what contact is permitted.
Pair players of similar size and physical development for Phase 3 to ensure both players can develop confidence and technique.
Film Phase 3 from the side to evaluate the timing of tackle attempts relative to the attackers touch.
Connect to professional defending: show footage of elite defenders timing their tackles to heavy touches and executing shoulder charges legally.
diagramDescription: 20m channel. PHASE 1 shown: Partner A with stationary ball, Partner B executing block tackle (inside of foot, drive through ball, stay on feet). PHASE 2 shown: attacker with 3m head start, defender recovering, HEAVY TOUCH moment highlighted with a star showing correct tackle timing. PHASE 3 shown: full intensity 1v1. SHOULDER CHARGE diagram: two players shoulder-to-shoulder competing for ball, legal contact area shown (upper arm and shoulder), illegal contact areas shown (behind, arms extended). Tackle timing options shown: block tackle vs sliding tackle angles.

title: Cover and Balance — Defensive Unit Shape
objective: Develop the defensive unit organisation of cover and balance positions used in advanced team defending structures.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the cover and balance roles in a defensive unit
Players can maintain the correct cover position relative to both the ball and their teammates
Players develop the collective defensive shape that minimises space for the opposition to exploit
Players build the communication and organisation that maintains the defensive unit under sustained pressure
organisation: Full defensive half of the pitch. A full defensive unit (back 4 plus defensive midfielder) works together against 4-5 attackers. The specific coaching focus is on the cover and balance positions of the defenders not directly involved in pressuring the ball.
instructions: DEFENSIVE UNIT SETUP: 4 defenders (right back, 2 centre-backs, left back) plus a defensive midfielder.
ROLES: when the ball is on the RIGHT SIDE of the pitch:
RIGHT BACK: presses the ball (first defender).
RIGHT CENTRE-BACK: provides cover behind the right back (second defender). Takes a position that covers the space behind the right back if they are beaten.
LEFT CENTRE-BACK: provides balance — shifts slightly right but maintains the central defensive coverage.
LEFT BACK: holds position on the far side as the balance player, covering the switch ball.
DEFENSIVE MIDFIELDER: drops into the space between the two CBs providing a safety net.

PHASE 1 (7 minutes): coach moves a ball to different positions in the attacking half. Defensive unit must reorganise into the correct cover and balance positions for each ball position. Walkthrough pace.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): live opposition with 4 attackers. Defensive unit must maintain cover and balance positions under real pressure.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full 5v5+GK in the defensive half. Defensive unit applies cover and balance concepts throughout.
coachingPoints: COVER IS BEHIND THE FIRST DEFENDER: the cover player positions themselves behind and to the inside of the first defender. If the first defender is beaten, the cover player is the next line. Without cover, a beaten first defender means a direct run to goal.

BALANCE IS THE FAR SIDE PROTECTION: the balance player covers the far side of the pitch from where the ball is. They prevent the switch pass from creating a dangerous situation by being already in position to deal with it.
THE SHAPE MOVES AS ONE: when the ball moves, every player in the defensive unit moves simultaneously. The cover and balance positions shift to reflect the new ball position.
COMMUNICATION IS THE COORDINATOR: the goalkeeper and the defensive midfielder have the best view of the defensive unit shape. They should be calling cover and balance positions throughout.

COVER MUST BE AT THE CORRECT ANGLE: the cover player should be at approximately 45 degrees behind the first defender, not directly behind them. This angle allows them to see both the ball and any runner making a run in behind.

THE BALANCE PLAYER IS THE MOST DISCIPLINED ROLE: the balance player on the far side is furthest from the ball and must resist the temptation to move toward the ball. Their job is to cover the far side, which requires patience.

DEFENSIVE COMPACTNESS REDUCES THE COVER REQUIREMENT: when the defensive unit is compact (players close together), the cover distances are shorter and the covers are easier to provide. Spread defensive units require greater movement to cover.

SWITCH BALL IS THE TEST OF BALANCE: the most effective way to beat a well-organised defensive unit is to switch the ball to the balance side. The balance player must be in position to deal with this before the switch is made.
commonMistakes: Cover player positioning directly behind the first defender rather than at the 45-degree angle
Balance player drifting toward the ball, leaving the far side exposed to the switch
The defensive unit not shifting together when the ball moves, leaving one player out of position
No communication between players about who has cover and who has balance in each situation
Cover distance too large — cover player too far behind the first defender to prevent a run in behind
corrections: Direct cover rather than 45-degree angle: use a cone marker showing the correct cover position angle before Phase 1 begins
Balance player drifting: specifically coach and call out every time the balance player moves toward the ball rather than holding their far-side position
Not shifting together: freeze play when the ball moves and check every players position simultaneously before restarting
No communication: make the cover call and balance call mandatory — no first defender can press until the cover player has called COVER
Cover too far: measure the maximum cover distance (5m behind the first defender) with a rope during Phase 1
progressions: Add a striker making runs in behind, specifically testing the cover player positioning and response
4v4+GK full game in the defensive half with cover and balance as the only instruction. Evaluate after 10 minutes.
Combine with the pressing triggers drill: the defensive unit alternates between a high press and a mid-block cover-and-balance shape based on a coach signal
regressions: Reduce to just the two CBs covering and balancing (no full back or DM roles) while the basic concept is established
Use a walking opposition for the full Phase 1 and 2 to give the defensive unit more time to find their positions
Walk through every cover and balance position change with no ball before introducing any live attackers
matchApplication: Cover and balance as a defensive unit concept is what separates teams that defend compactly from teams that defend in disorganised individual clusters. At U17, players are ready for the full complexity of defensive unit organisation — understanding not just their own role but how it connects to every other players role simultaneously. The cover and balance system taught in this drill is used by professional teams at every level and creates the defensive coherence that makes well-organised teams significantly harder to break down than technically equivalent but tactically disorganised ones.
coachTips: The walkthrough phase is essential and should not be rushed. Cover and balance positions are a new concept even for experienced players.
The coach calling the cover and balance positions explicitly during Phase 1 (that is cover, that is balance) builds the vocabulary and the visual understanding quickly.
Film from above during Phase 2 and 3. The cover and balance positions are only visible from an elevated angle.
The balance player is the most difficult role to maintain. Give this player specific attention and specific praise when they hold their position correctly.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of a well-organised back 4 defending with cover and balance and identify each players role position.
diagramDescription: Full defensive half. BALL ON RIGHT SIDE shown. Right back shown pressing (FIRST DEFENDER label). Right centre-back shown at 45-degree angle behind right back (COVER label, 45-degree angle indicator). Left centre-back shown shifted slightly right but central (BALANCE CENTRE label). Left back shown holding far side (BALANCE label, far side position). Defensive midfielder shown dropping between CBs (SAFETY NET label). SWITCH BALL TEST shown: ball switched to left side, left back now becomes first defender, all other positions shift accordingly.

title: Score in Any Goal — First Attacking Awareness
objective: Develop basic attacking awareness through a free game where players discover that shooting at the right goal at the right time creates scoring opportunities.
learningOutcomes: Players understand which goal to attack and which to defend in a game situation
Players develop the basic instinct to shoot when close to the opposition goal
Players experience the joy of creating and scoring goals
Players begin to understand the attacking and defending roles within a game
equipment: Cones for goals at each end
pitchSize: Any available open area with improvised goals
organisation: Small-sided free game with improvised goals. No tactical instruction during the game. The only rule is which goal each team attacks. The coach watches, celebrates goals, and uses brief pauses only to reinforce which goal each team is attacking.
instructions: Set up two improvised goals (pairs of cones, 2m wide) at each end of a small area.
Split into two teams with bibs.
Explain the only rule: your team attacks the goal at the far end. You defend the goal at the near end.
Let them play completely freely for 5 minutes. No instruction during play.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): pure free play. Coach watches and celebrates every goal enthusiastically.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): brief pause every 2 minutes. Coach asks one question: which goal are you attacking? Points to the correct goal. Restarts the game.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): free play again. The coach now only celebrates and encourages. No tactical instruction.
After the game: celebrate everyone. Ask who scored a goal. Ask how they created the chance.
coachingPoints: LOOK UP TO FIND THE GOAL: before you shoot, look up to see where the goal is. The player who knows where the goal is always has an advantage over the one who is looking at the ball.
GET CLOSE BEFORE SHOOTING: the closer you are to the goal when you shoot, the more likely you are to score. Try to get as close as possible before shooting.
SHOOT WHEN YOU HAVE A CHANCE: when the goal is in front of you and nobody is blocking your way, shoot. Do not wait for a better moment. Shoot.
PASS TO A TEAMMATE WHO IS CLOSER: if your teammate is closer to the goal than you are, pass to them so they can shoot. Helping a teammate score is just as good as scoring yourself.
HAVE FUN: scoring goals is one of the best feelings in sport. Enjoy every shot, every chance, and every goal — yours and your teammates.
DEFENDING IS ALSO PART OF THE GAME: when the other team has the ball, try to get between them and your goal. Help your teammates defend.
EVERY PLAYER CAN SCORE: the best teams have goals from all their players, not just one or two. Be ready to shoot when the chance comes, wherever you are on the pitch.
THE BALL GOES IN DIFFERENT DIRECTIONS: passing and shooting do not always go where you want them to at first. That is completely normal. Keep trying and your aim will get better.
commonMistakes: Shooting at the wrong goal — not understanding which goal to attack
Standing still and watching rather than moving toward the opposition goal when your team has the ball
Only one or two players chasing the ball while others stand and watch
Giving up after missing a shot rather than immediately looking for another chance
Not celebrating teammates goals — missing the team connection
corrections: Wrong goal: when a player shoots at their own goal, gently and kindly pause and show them which goal is theirs with no embarrassment
Standing still: walk alongside a player who is standing still and say RUN WITH ME — move with them toward the opposition goal to show the natural movement
Only one or two chasing: briefly celebrate any player who is running toward the ball even if they do not reach it
Giving up after miss: immediately after a miss call AGAIN and encourage the next attempt
Not celebrating teammates: model the celebration yourself enthusiastically after every goal regardless of who scored
progressions: Add a simple offside line (halfway): players cannot stand in the opposition half when their team does not have the ball
Introduce one rule per game (such as must pass to a teammate before shooting) to gradually add tactical complexity
Increase to 5v5 in a bigger area to introduce more of the spatial concepts of a real game
regressions: Use a larger goal (4m wide) to make scoring easier and build confidence in shooting
Play 2v2 or 3v3 in a very small area (8x6m) to keep the action constant
Use a light beach ball or foam ball if players are finding the football heavy or intimidating
matchApplication: The small-sided free game is the most appropriate format for introducing attacking concepts at U8 because the game itself teaches through discovery rather than instruction. When a U8 player realises that shooting from close range scores more often than shooting from far away, they have learned one of the most important attacking principles in football through experience rather than explanation. This session prioritises joy, movement, and the natural development of game understanding over any specific tactical instruction — which is exactly the correct approach for this age group.
coachTips: Your role in this session is to create an environment of joy and to celebrate goals and effort. There is very little active coaching instruction needed.
Resist the temptation to teach during the game. Watching is your most valuable tool here.
Make every goal feel like a big event. Your enthusiasm for goals sets the cultural tone.
Connect to the players love of football. Ask them about their favourite team and which goal their favourite player scored.
This is the simplest session in the library and also one of the most important. The joy of scoring and creating in a free game is the foundation of a lifelong love of football.
diagramDescription: Small area with improvised cone goals at each end. Two teams of 3-4 shown in different bibs playing freely. Ball shown in various positions. Goal arrows showing both teams attacking their respective goals. GOAL CELEBRATION shown at each end. Coach shown watching from the side (not intervening). Team connection shown with teammates celebrating together after a goal. FREE PLAY label shown prominently — no tactical arrows or formations.

title: Combination Play for Young Players — Give and Go
objective: Introduce the give-and-go to young players through a simple and enjoyable format that teaches the most fundamental two-player attacking combination.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute a give-and-go combination correctly at slow pace in a structured format
Players understand that passing and then running is more effective than just dribbling
Players develop the habit of running after their pass rather than standing still
Players experience the joy of a successful two-player combination
players: Groups of 3 (16 total across multiple groups)
equipment: Cones for a simple target (3 cones wide, 10m away)
organisation: Groups of 3: Player A (runs with ball), Player B (the wall), a cone target at the far end. Player A dribbles toward the cone target, passes to Player B who returns it immediately, and Player A collects and dribbles to the target. Walk-through pace throughout. Focus on the fun of the combination working.
instructions: Player A starts with the ball 10m from the cone target.
Player B stands 4m to the side of Player A, between A and the target.
Player A dribbles toward the cone target.
3m from the target: Player A passes to Player B.
Player A KEEPS RUNNING toward the target.
Player B passes immediately back into the space ahead of Player A who is now running through.
Player A collects the return and touches the ball through the target.
Celebrate together. Swap roles. Player A becomes Player B, Player B becomes Player A.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): walking pace. Focus on the pattern and the return pass.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): jogging pace. Add gentle energy.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): full pace. Add a passive cone gate that represents a defender between A and the target.
coachingPoints: PASS AND KEEP RUNNING: this is the most important lesson of the give-and-go. After you pass the ball to Player B, you keep running. The ball will come back to you in the space ahead. Do not stop.
THE RETURN MUST GO AHEAD OF WHERE YOU ARE: Player B must return the ball into the space in front of where Player A is running, not to where Player A was when they passed. Pass ahead of the runner.
TIMING THE PASS AND THE RUN: the pass from A to B and the continued run from A happen at the same time. It is one connected action — pass-and-run, not pass-then-run.
PLAYER B PLAYS FIRST TOUCH: Player B should return the ball with their first touch if possible. Any extra touch gives the imaginary defender time to recover.

THIS BEATS A DEFENDER: the give-and-go works because when Player A passes to B, the imaginary defender follows the ball to B. Meanwhile, A has run past them into space. The ball comes back to A who is now free.
IT LOOKS LIKE MAGIC: when it works well, the give-and-go looks like the ball has gone around the defender all by itself. This is because A and B have worked together perfectly.
TWO PLAYERS ARE BETTER THAN ONE: the give-and-go is the proof that two players working together can beat a defender more easily than one player alone. Football is always better as a team.
CELEBRATE TOGETHER: when the give-and-go works, both Player A and Player B have done something brilliant. Celebrate it as a team success.
commonMistakes: Player A stopping after the pass rather than continuing to run
Player B taking too long before returning the ball, allowing the defender to recover
Player B returning the ball to where Player A was rather than into the space ahead
The return pass going too hard, running away from Player A
The combination working but players not recognising what they have done (no celebration)
corrections: A stopping: physically run alongside Player A in Phase 1, keeping them moving after the pass
B too slow to return: enforce a 1-touch return rule from the very beginning — the instant the ball arrives at B, it must leave
B returning to wrong position: draw an X on the ground 2m ahead of A starting position — the return pass must reach the X
Return too hard: reduce the return distance to 3m and require a soft, accurate return
No celebration: after every successful combination call BRILLIANT and ask both players to celebrate — build the culture
progressions: Add a passive defender who stands between A and the target. The give-and-go must go around them
Introduce a second combination where B keeps the ball and A must ask for it with movement (more advanced)
Full small game where give-and-go goals earn double points
regressions: Allow Player A to stop briefly after passing and then continue running — reducing the timing demand while the pattern is learned
Use a larger return target (4m wide gate instead of a cone) to reduce the accuracy pressure on the return pass
Walk through the combination at very slow pace with the coach as Player B for the first 5 minutes
matchApplication: The give-and-go is the gateway combination skill in football and represents one of the most important lessons a young player can learn: two players working together are more powerful than one player alone. The specific habits developed here — passing and running, returning first touch, leading the runner with the pass — are the exact same principles used by the greatest tiki-taka teams in history. Introducing them at U9 through an enjoyable, celebratory practice that feels like fun rather than instruction creates the technical and attitudinal foundation for everything that follows in combination play.
coachTips: Keep the atmosphere celebratory throughout. Every successful combination deserves genuine celebration.
Run alongside players in Phase 1 to physically model the pass-and-run movement.
The first-touch return requirement for Player B is the most important technical rule. Enforce it from the start.
Connect to the game: tell players this is the same combination that professional teams use every match. They are doing what professional players do.
Once the pattern is established in Phase 3, this can become a daily warm-up activity at the start of sessions. 5 minutes of give-and-go work is an excellent session opener for any age group.
diagramDescription: 10m channel per group. Player A shown with ball 10m from cone target. Player B shown 4m to the side between A and target. Pass arrow from A to B. A running arrow continuing toward the target (simultaneous with pass). Return arrow from B leading into the space ahead of A (not back to where A was). A shown collecting the return and touching through the target gate. DEFENDER PHASE 3 shown as a passive cone between A and target that the give-and-go goes around. Celebration icons shown after the successful combination.

title: Movement to Create Space — Runs and Checks
objective: Develop the basic movements used to create space for yourself and teammates in attacking play: the check-away run and the run in behind.
learningOutcomes: Players can perform a check-away run to create separation from a marker and receive in space
Players understand the timing of a run in behind relative to the ball-carrier
Players develop the habit of moving to help their team even when they do not have the ball
Players build the spatial awareness to read where the space will be before it appears
organisation: Groups of 3-4 in a 20x15m zone. One ball. Players practice two specific movements: the check-away (move away from the ball then come back to receive) and the run in behind (move toward goal to receive in space). Passive defenders added in Phase 3.
instructions: MOVEMENT 1 — CHECK-AWAY:
Player A has the ball. Player B is marked by a passive defender.
Player B makes a movement AWAY from the ball (2-3 steps away from A).
The defender follows Player B away from the ball.
Player B immediately turns and moves BACK toward the ball (the check).
The defender has been pulled away and Player B now has 1-2 steps of separation to receive.
Player A plays to Player B in the space created. Player B receives and turns.
MOVEMENT 2 — RUN IN BEHIND:
Player A has the ball. Player B starts 10m ahead.
When Player A is ready to play the forward pass (head up, body open), Player B makes a run in behind the defensive line.
Player A plays into the space ahead of Player B. Player B receives in behind.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): check-away movement only with no defenders.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): run in behind only with no defenders.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): defenders added. Players choose which movement to use based on the defensive position.
coachingPoints: THE CHECK-AWAY CREATES THE SPACE: by moving away first, you drag the defender away from where you want to receive. When you check back, you are in the space you have created for yourself.

THE RUN IN BEHIND USES THE SPACE THAT ALREADY EXISTS: the run in behind does not create space — it finds the space that is already behind the defensive line and gets there before the defender can react.

TIMING IS THE KEY TO BOTH MOVEMENTS: too early and the defender has time to recover. Too late and the space has already closed. The timing comes from reading the ball-carrier. When they are ready to pass, you make the movement.

ONE MOVEMENT OR THE OTHER — NOT BOTH AT ONCE: choose one movement based on where the defender is. If the defender is tight behind you, use the check-away. If the defender is between you and the ball (facing you), use the run in behind.
MOVE BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: both movements must be started before the ball is played. A movement that starts after the ball is played is always too late.
SIGNAL TO THE BALL-CARRIER: a brief eye contact or a quick open-hand gesture to the ball-carrier signals that you are making a movement and tells them where to play the ball.

YOUR MOVEMENT HELPS THE WHOLE TEAM: even if you do not receive the ball after making a check-away or a run in behind, the movement may have created space for a different teammate by drawing a defender away.
KEEP MOVING: players who are constantly in motion — checking away, checking in, offering runs — are always harder to defend against than players who stand still and wait for the ball.
commonMistakes: Starting the movement too early before the ball-carrier is ready to play, allowing the defender to recover
Making the check-away but not providing a strong enough movement away to pull the defender out of position
Running in behind before the ball-carrier is in a position to play the pass, resulting in offside or a missed connection
Stopping the movement if the ball does not immediately come, standing still and hoping
Making the same movement repeatedly so the defender can read and anticipate it
corrections: Too early: add a visual trigger (ball-carrier raises hand when ready). Player can only start their movement after seeing the signal
Weak check-away: require a minimum 3-step movement away before the check-back. A 1-step movement does not pull the defender sufficiently
Run in behind too early: use the same hand-raise trigger. Player must wait for the signal before beginning the run
Stopping if ball does not come: require every player to make another movement immediately after a first movement goes unused
Predictable movement: alternate required movements (check-away one rep, run in behind the next) to build the habit of varying movements
progressions: Add fully live defenders who can also tackle, making the timing of the movements genuinely important
Combine both movements: check-away to create space near the ball, immediately followed by a run in behind if the defender tries to recover
Full small game with movement bonus points: any goal scored from a check-away or run in behind combination earns 2 points
regressions: Use no defenders for the full session, focusing purely on the mechanics of both movements before adding defensive pressure
Allow the check-away movement to be at walking pace before building to jogging pace
Use a larger zone (25x20m) to give more space for the movements and more time for the connection with the ball-carrier
matchApplication: Movement without the ball is where most of the important work in attacking football happens. The players who are most dangerous in attack are not always the ones with the best technical ability — they are the ones who make the smartest, most well-timed movements to receive in space. The check-away and run-in-behind are the two most fundamental movements in attacking football and cover the majority of situations where a player needs to create space for themselves. Teaching them at U11, when players are beginning to understand their role away from the ball, builds the attacking movement intelligence that becomes increasingly valuable as opposition defending becomes more organised.
coachTips: The timing coaching point is the most important in both movements. Use many repetitions of the trigger signal to build the connection between the ball-carrier readiness and the movement start.
Watch and celebrate every movement attempt, regardless of whether the ball connection is made. The movement is the achievement.

Connect to professional players known for their movement: Harry Kane, Roberto Firmino, and Thierry Henry are excellent examples of players whose attacking quality is primarily based on intelligent movement.

Both movements work together: a player who knows both the check-away and the run in behind and can choose between them based on the defensive situation is significantly harder to defend against than one who only knows one.
Return to these two movements regularly across the season. The timing and reading quality develops with repeated practice.
diagramDescription: 20x15m zone. MOVEMENT 1 (Check-Away): Player A with ball. Player B with passive defender beside them. Step 1: Player B moves 3 steps away (AWAY arrow). Defender follows (defender arrow). Step 2: Player B checks back (CHECK arrow). Space created shown (open zone). Pass arrow from A to B in the space. MOVEMENT 2 (Run in Behind): Player A with ball. Defensive line shown. Player B starting 10m ahead. Trigger signal shown (A raises hand). Run in behind arrow from B beyond the defensive line. Lead pass arrow from A into the space ahead of B.

title: Attacking in the Final Third — Adults
objective: Develop the complete range of attacking actions required to consistently create and convert high-quality chances in the final third of adult competitive football.
learningOutcomes: Adult players develop reliable attacking patterns for different final-third entry positions
Players build the clinical finishing technique required to convert chances in adult football
Teams develop coordinated attacking patterns that exploit different defensive weaknesses
Players build the match-specific composure to perform in high-pressure goal-scoring situations
players: 10-14 players (attacking unit plus defenders and GK)
organisation: Final third of the pitch. Attacking unit of 5-6 players works against a defensive unit of 4-5 plus GK. Session structured around three specific final-third attacking scenarios most common in adult football.
instructions: SCENARIO 1 — WIDE ENTRY AND CROSS (8 minutes):
Ball played wide to an overlapping full-back or winger 25m from goal.
Wide player attacks the defender 1v1 and delivers a cross.
Two strikers make coordinated near-post and far-post runs.
Third player arrives at the edge of the area for cut-backs and rebounds.
SCENARIO 2 — CENTRAL COMBINATION AND SHOOT (8 minutes):
Ball enters the final third centrally. Two attacking midfielders combine with a 1-2 to create a shooting opportunity from 18-22m.
If the combination does not create a clear shot: play the ball wide and recycle.
Emphasis on the quality of the final shot — placement and composure over power.
SCENARIO 3 — SET-PIECE RESPONSE (9 minutes):
Practice dealing with the second phase from corners and free kicks: the clearance, the recovery of the second ball, and the finishing from a recycled set piece.
Both scenarios run with defenders and GK applying genuine pressure.
coachingPoints: KNOW WHICH SCENARIO YOU ARE IN: as the ball enters the final third, every attacker should immediately recognise the scenario (wide entry, central combination, or recycled set piece) and execute the correct role for that scenario.

THE WIDE ATTACK REQUIRES COMMITMENT: the wide attacker must commit to beating their defender and delivering the cross. A wide player who pulls back from the 1v1 opportunity wastes the build-up that has created it.

CROSSING QUALITY DETERMINES EVERYTHING: in Scenario 1, the quality of the cross is the limiting factor. Low and hard to the near post creates the most opportunities. Looped crosses into a crowded box create saves.

CLINICAL FINISHING IN SCENARIO 2: the combination is only valuable if the resulting shot is on target. A creative combination followed by a shot into the stands wasted the opportunity. Placement first.

SECOND-PHASE ALERTNESS IN SCENARIO 3: the most commonly missed attacking opportunity in adult football is the second phase from a set piece. The player who arrives at the edge of the area is often completely free. Be that player.

MOVEMENT BEFORE THE BALL ENTERS THE FINAL THIRD: all three scenarios are more effective if the attacking unit has made movement before the ball arrives in the final third. Static attackers give the defence time to organise.

VARY THE SCENARIO: a team that always attacks via the same scenario is easy to defend. Alternating between wide attack, central combination, and set-piece recycling makes defending significantly harder.

COMPOSURE IN ADULT FINAL THIRDS: the physical intensity of adult football puts finishing under greater pressure than any youth level. The composure techniques from the individual finishing drills must be applied here under real adult defensive pressure.
commonMistakes: Attacking unit executing the wrong role for the scenario being run, causing confusion
Wide attacker pulling back from the 1v1 opportunity and crossing from too far out
All attackers making the same run in Scenario 1 (all to near post or all to far post), concentrating in one area
Shot quality breaking down under adult defensive pressure
No second-phase presence in Scenario 3 — all players too focused on the first ball
corrections: Wrong scenario roles: brief team meeting before each scenario begins where every player states their role aloud
Pulling back from 1v1: add a rule in Scenario 1 that the wide attacker must get within 2m of the defender before crossing
All same run: assign near post and far post roles with coloured bibs before Phase 1 and maintain them throughout
Shot quality breakdown: add the chance-quality scoring system from the U15 creating chances drill
No second-phase presence: designate one player in Scenario 3 whose specific role is the edge-of-area second ball — they earn a team point every time they receive the second ball in this position
progressions: Add a pressing recovery defender in all scenarios who sprints from the halfway line to pressure the final action
Combine all three scenarios into a free game where the attacking team chooses the scenario based on how the ball enters the final third
Video analysis session: film 15 minutes of the team playing and identify which final-third scenarios arise most frequently and which are most successfully converted
regressions: Use passive defenders throughout Scenario 1 and 2 while the movement patterns are established
Allow the wide attacker a free 1v1 with a passive defender before introducing a live 1v1 in Scenario 1
Remove the GK from Scenarios 1 and 2 initially to allow pattern and finishing practice without the additional pressure variable
matchApplication: The final third is where football matches are decided, and the specific techniques and patterns for consistently creating and converting chances in that area are what separate teams that score regularly from teams that create chances but fail to convert. At adult level, the physical intensity, defensive organisation, and GK quality all increase the technical and mental demands on attackers. This session specifically addresses the three most common adult final-third scenarios — wide attack and cross, central combination and shoot, and set-piece second phase — and develops both the team coordination and the individual finishing quality required to perform in them consistently.
coachTips: Walk through each scenario role assignment before any live practice — adult players respond well to understanding their specific role clearly.
The shot quality coaching point is the most important in Scenario 2. Use the chance quality system from the U15 drill to evaluate it specifically.
Film Scenario 1 from behind the goal and Scenario 2 from the side. Discuss with the attacking unit after the session.
Connect to your own teams recent match data: which of the three scenarios does your team create most frequently? Which do they convert at the highest rate? Use this to prioritise session time.
This session is most effective when run regularly with the same attacking unit, as the coordination between players in each scenario improves significantly with repeated shared practice.
diagramDescription: Final third of the pitch with full-size goal and GK. SCENARIO 1 shown: wide attacker on right attacking a defender 1v1 (dribble arrow), cross delivery (arc arrow), two strikers shown making near-post and far-post runs, third player at edge of area. SCENARIO 2 shown: ball entering centrally, two midfielders making a 1-2 combination (give-and-go arrows), shot from 20m (arrow to corner). SCENARIO 3 shown: set piece position, first ball delivered, clearance, second-phase player at edge of area receiving the recycled ball and shooting. Defenders shown in all three scenarios.

title: The Playground Duel — 1v1 Skill Challenge
objective: Create a playground-style 1v1 skill duel environment where players are free to try any move and are celebrated for creativity above all else.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the courage to attempt new skill moves in a competitive but joyful environment
Players build a personal 1v1 skill repertoire through free experimentation
Players develop the competitive enjoyment of football as individual expression
Players experience the street football culture where skill is celebrated above winning
players: Pairs rotating in a challenge bracket (8-16 total)
equipment: A simple scoreboard or points tracker
pitchSize: 8x6m zone per pair — multiple zones running simultaneously
organisation: Multiple 8x6m zones running simultaneously. Pairs compete in 90-second 1v1 duels. Points scored not for goals but for skill moves successfully executed against the defender. A nutmeg earns 3 points. A successful step-over and beat earns 2 points. A skill move attempted but not completed earns 1 point. Goals earn 1 point. After 90 seconds, players rotate to a new partner.
instructions: Each zone has two players competing 1v1.
SCORING: nutmeg = 3 points, skill move that beats the defender = 2 points, skill move attempted = 1 point, goal = 1 point.
Players are encouraged to try skill moves they have never tried before. The attempt always scores.
After 90 seconds: rotate. Play against as many different partners as possible across the session.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): introduce the scoring system. First round of 90-second duels. Focus is on trying moves, not winning.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): second round of duels with different partners. Players now know the scoring system and can be more deliberate.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): final round. Each player must name one specific move they will attempt before entering the zone. They earn a bonus point if they attempt that named move at least once.
Coach moves around celebrating skill attempts enthusiastically. The player with the most skill attempt points at the end is the champion — not the player with the most goals.
coachingPoints: THE ATTEMPT IS ALWAYS WORTH MORE THAN THE RESULT: in this session, trying a nutmeg and failing earns you more than a simple goal. The culture celebrates the attempt.

STREET FOOTBALL WAS ALWAYS ABOUT INDIVIDUAL EXPRESSION: the greatest players in history — Maradona, Ronaldinho, Neymar — all developed their skill moves in exactly this type of unstructured, joyful duel environment.

TRY SOMETHING YOU HAVE NEVER TRIED: the most valuable thing you can do in this session is attempt a skill move you have never tried in a real game before. Whether it works or not is less important than the courage to try.
THE DEFENDER IS NOT AN ENEMY: the player defending in this session is providing the resistance that makes the skill valuable. Thank your partner for the challenge.
YOUR OWN SKILL VOCABULARY IS UNIQUE: every great player has moves that are particularly theirs. Discover yours in this session. What feels natural? What do you enjoy trying?
FAILURE IS THE FASTEST TEACHER: a skill move that does not work in the first attempt tells you exactly what you need to adjust. Try it again immediately with the adjustment.
WATCH YOUR OPPONENTS MOVES: when your partner beats you with a skill move, watch how they did it and try it yourself in the next duel. This is how street football has always spread new skill moves.
CELEBRATE LOUDLY: in street football, a nutmeg is celebrated as loudly as a goal. Bring that culture into this session. When a great skill move happens, everyone celebrates.
commonMistakes: Playing safely rather than attempting skill moves to avoid the risk of failure
Only attempting one familiar skill move rather than experimenting with new ones
Becoming overly competitive about the score rather than the skill content
Defenders being too passive, making the duels too easy
Not celebrating skill attempts from opponents — missing the cultural element
corrections: Playing safely: remind players at the start of every phase that the attempt scoring system means it is always better to try a skill move than to play safely
Only one move: the Phase 3 naming requirement forces players to commit to a specific new move before entering the zone
Overly competitive about score: briefly restate the scoring system — skill attempts can outscore goals — so the competitive focus shifts to skill
Passive defenders: require defenders to always apply active jockeying pressure, never standing still
No celebration of opponents: model the celebration yourself after every skill move by any player
progressions: Remove the goal option entirely — points can only be earned through skill moves, making it a pure skill expression session
Add a skill move showcase at the end: each player demonstrates their favourite move discovered in the session to the whole group
Tournament format: skills-only bracket where the player with the most skill attempt points across 5 duels is the champion
regressions: Remove the scoring system entirely and play purely for fun before introducing the points system
Reduce to 3-minute duels instead of 90 seconds so players have more time to attempt moves
Allow players to choose a single move to practice repeatedly rather than varying across the session
matchApplication: The playground duel format recreates the most important developmental environment in football history: the street game where individual skill was celebrated, where players were free to try anything, and where the culture rewarded expression over safety. Research into the development of the most technically gifted players consistently identifies this type of unstructured, skill-first environment as a critical factor. The specific innovation of scoring skill attempts (rather than just goals) changes the psychology of the practice from a competitive game to a creative performance — which is exactly the environment where skill development flourishes.
coachTips: Your enthusiasm for skill attempts is the engine of this session. Every single attempt deserves a response from you.

The Phase 3 naming requirement is the most powerful element for development. When a player names a move they will attempt and then tries it in front of everyone, they are committing to creative courage.
Move around the zones constantly. Your presence beside a zone immediately raises the quality and enthusiasm of the duel.
Play music. High-energy music makes the duel format feel like a performance rather than a practice.
End the session by asking every player to name the best skill move they saw from an opponent. This builds the mutual appreciation culture of street football.
diagramDescription: Multiple 8x6m zones shown running simultaneously. In each zone: two players in active 1v1 duel. SCORING SYSTEM shown prominently: NUTMEG = 3 pts (illustration of ball through legs), SKILL MOVE BEAT = 2 pts (player being beaten by a step-over), SKILL ATTEMPT = 1 pt (attempt with miss shown), GOAL = 1 pt. Coach shown moving between zones celebrating. Phase 3 shown with NAMED MOVE label beside each player before they enter the zone. Celebration icons throughout.

title: Tiki-Taka Small Sided Game — Pass Move Score
objective: Combine the tiki-taka philosophy with competitive small-sided play, rewarding both possession quality and creative attacking play in a game that celebrates the best of both street football and structured possession.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain high-quality possession using tiki-taka principles in a competitive game
Players develop the transition from possession to creative attacking play
Players build the collective confidence of a team that is comfortable keeping the ball
Players experience the joy of winning through beautiful football rather than just effective football
pitchSize: 30x25m area with goals at each end
organisation: Standard small-sided game with a modified scoring system that rewards both passing quality and creative play. Three ways to score: a normal goal (1 point), a goal scored after 8 consecutive passes (3 points), or a goal scored immediately after a successful skill move beat (2 points). The scoring system creates a game that naturally develops both tiki-taka patience and street football creativity.
instructions: Set up the 30x25m area with mini-goals or goal-posts at each end.
Teams play a normal small-sided game. The scoring rules are explained before kick-off.
STANDARD GOAL: 1 point.
TIKI-TAKA GOAL: any goal scored by the team that has completed 8 or more consecutive passes in the same possession earns 3 points. Both teams count their own passes aloud.
STREET GOAL: any goal scored within 3 passes of a successful skill move beat (one player beating a defender with a skill move, the ball staying in possession) earns 2 points.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): play with all three scoring options. Teams discover that patience in possession leads to the highest-value goals.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): one team must use only tiki-taka goals (8 passes minimum), the other only street goals (skill move before goal). After 4 minutes, swap.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): free game with all scoring options. Teams choose their approach.
coachingPoints: THE SCORING SYSTEM CHANGES WHAT FOOTBALL FEELS LIKE: in this game, shooting immediately after winning the ball earns only 1 point. Patience, creativity, and skill earn 2-3 times more. This is how tiki-taka teams think about the game.

THE TIKI-TAKA GOAL REQUIRES COLLECTIVE PATIENCE: to earn 3 points, the whole team must want to pass. One player who shoots early at 7 passes resets the count. The collective discipline to reach 8 is real tiki-taka.

THE STREET GOAL REWARDS INDIVIDUAL COURAGE: attempting a skill move in a game where failure means losing possession takes courage. The 2-point reward for a successful skill move goal says to every player — your creativity is valued.
COUNT PASSES TOGETHER: the pass count must be shared aloud by the whole team. This builds the collective awareness and communication that is the foundation of tiki-taka.

POSSESSION IS NOT THE GOAL — THE GOAL IS THE GOAL: tiki-taka is not about keeping the ball for its own sake. It is about keeping the ball until the right moment to attack. When 8 passes have been completed, attack with purpose.
THE SKILL MOVE CREATES THE MOMENT: in the street goal route, the skill move is not the end — it is the beginning. After the beat, the team must still play the ball quickly and finish within 3 passes.
BOTH STREETS AND TIKI-TAKA ARE IN THIS GAME: a team that can both keep the ball patiently for 8 passes AND produce a skill move goal is playing the most complete and beautiful version of football.

CELEBRATE EVERY HIGH-VALUE GOAL AS A TEAM: a 3-point tiki-taka goal or a 2-point street goal should be celebrated significantly more than a standard goal. Make the value of beautiful football tangible.
commonMistakes: Teams shooting immediately after winning the ball, never building toward high-value goals
Pass count lost because only one player is counting rather than the whole team
Skill moves attempted but not in genuine 1v1 situations where they could create a goal opportunity
Players keeping the ball for possession well past 8 passes without transitioning to attack
Teams ignoring one of the two high-value scoring routes and defaulting to simple goals
corrections: Shooting immediately: allow only 1-point goals for a 3-minute phase, then introduce the higher-value options so teams feel the difference in ambition
Only one player counting: require the whole team to count aloud together — any missed number resets the count to zero
Skill moves not creating opportunities: require the skill move beat to happen within 15m of the goal to count
Not transitioning after 8 passes: introduce a maximum of 12 consecutive passes before a shot must be attempted
Ignoring one route: in Phase 2, force both routes alternately so teams develop comfort with both approaches
progressions: Increase the tiki-taka goal threshold to 12 consecutive passes for more advanced groups
Add a third scoring premium: a goal scored using the weaker foot from inside the box earns 4 points
Full match using the scoring system for an entire training session, creating a season-long culture of beautiful football
regressions: Reduce the tiki-taka threshold to 5 passes for groups less experienced with possession play
Allow 2-point goals for skill move beats regardless of whether they lead directly to a goal (just beat + possession maintained)
Remove the pass count entirely in Phase 1 and focus only on the skill move goal before introducing tiki-taka scoring
matchApplication: The Pass Move Score game is the fullest expression of The Streets Meet Tiki-Taka philosophy in a competitive format. It creates a game where patience and creativity are both rewarded above simple efficiency, where the player who keeps the ball for 8 passes and scores earns the same recognition as the player who beats a defender with a skill move and scores, and where the team that can do both is playing the game at its most beautiful. This is the philosophical core of the category: the belief that football is most exciting, most joyful, and most effective when it combines the collective intelligence of tiki-taka with the individual expression of street football.
coachTips: The scoring system explanation before kick-off is the most important moment of the session. Take 2-3 minutes to make it completely clear.
The collective pass-counting is both a coaching tool and a team-building exercise. Celebrate the first time the whole team counts to 8 together.
Phase 2 (where teams are assigned to one route each) is the most revealing phase. Watch which route each team finds more natural and use that information in future sessions.
Play music throughout. The combination of tiki-taka possession and street skill moves has a rhythm that music enhances.
End each session by discussing which goal was the most beautiful. This builds the shared aesthetic of what great football looks and feels like.
diagramDescription: 30x25m area with mini-goals at each end. Three scoring scenarios illustrated: STANDARD GOAL (1pt) shown with direct shot. TIKI-TAKA GOAL (3pts) shown with 8-pass sequence counting label and final shot. STREET GOAL (2pts) shown with skill move beat (step-over illustration), then 2 quick passes and a goal. Pass count shown as a shared team count bubble. Phase 2 shown with two teams assigned different routes. Celebration icons showing different sizes for 1pt, 2pt, and 3pt goals.

title: The Elastico Challenge
objective: Develop the elastico (flip-flap) skill move through progressive technical practice and competitive application, celebrating one of the most iconic street football moves.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the elastico technique: outside-of-foot push followed by an inside-of-foot pull-back
Players develop the rapid foot movement required for the elastico to be effective
Players build the confidence to attempt the elastico in a live 1v1 situation
Players connect to the Brazilian street football heritage of the elastico
equipment: Open flat area — cones for practice channels
pitchSize: Individual 3x3m spaces progressing to pairs in 8x6m channels
organisation: Three-phase session. Phase 1: individual elastico technique practice against a stationary cone. Phase 2: slow 1v1 application with a passive partner. Phase 3: live competitive 1v1 application with a specific bonus for successful elastico beats.
instructions: THE ELASTICO TECHNIQUE:
Step 1: dribble toward a defender at medium pace, ball on your dominant foot.
Step 2: use the OUTSIDE of your dominant foot to push the ball to the OUTSIDE (to the right if using the right foot).
Step 3: IMMEDIATELY use the INSIDE of the SAME foot to pull the ball back across to the LEFT (opposite direction).
Step 4: accelerate in the direction the inside-foot pull-back has sent the ball.
The key: the two touches happen in very rapid succession so they look like one movement to the defender.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): stationary practice against a cone. 20 reps right foot, 10 reps left foot. Count aloud.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): passive partner stands as a defender. Execute the elastico at slow pace. Partner provides feedback on whether the fake was convincing.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): live 1v1 in an 8x6m channel. Mini-goal at the far end. Any goal scored using the elastico earns 3 points. Any other goal earns 1 point.
coachingPoints: THE TWO TOUCHES ARE ONE MOVEMENT: the outside-of-foot push and the inside-of-foot pull must happen so close together in time that the defender perceives them as a single unpredictable movement, not as two separate touches.

THE OUTSIDE TOUCH IS THE FAKE: the purpose of the outside-of-foot push is entirely deceptive. It makes the defender think the ball is going to the outside. The inside-foot pull-back then sends it the opposite way.

THE INSIDE TOUCH IS WHERE THE MOVE IS: the inside-of-foot pull-back is the actual direction change. This touch must be decisive and directed. A weak inside touch produces a slow, unconvincing elastico.
SPEED OF FOOT NOT SPEED OF BODY: the elastico is a foot-speed move, not a body-speed move. The body stays relatively still while the foot executes both touches in rapid succession.

THE MOVE WORKS BEST WHEN THE DEFENDER IS CLOSE: if the defender is too far away, the fake does not register. The elastico is most effective at 0.5-1m from the defender, when the fake creates an immediate reaction.

COMMIT TO THE ACCELERATION AFTER: the elastico creates a window of approximately 0.5 seconds while the defender recovers their balance. That window must be used with an immediate explosive acceleration.

RONALDINHO MADE THIS MOVE FAMOUS: Ronaldinho used the elastico as one of his signature moves and performed it at the highest level against the best defenders in the world. Every player who practices this move is connecting to his legacy.
FAIL FORWARD: the elastico is difficult. Most first attempts are slow and unconvincing. Each attempt that fails tells you specifically what to adjust. Keep trying.
commonMistakes: Outside-of-foot push going too far away from the body, making the inside-foot pull-back very difficult to execute
The two touches separated by too much time, making the fake obvious and easy to read
Not accelerating after the pull-back, wasting the 0.5-second window created
Attempting the elastico from too far away from the defender, where the fake has no effect
Only practicing the right-foot version and never developing the left-foot elastico
corrections: Outside push too far: keep the outside push within 20cm of the standing foot — a small controlled push, not a long one
Too much time between touches: count aloud TWO in the time the two touches happen. Both touches must complete before TWO is said
Not accelerating: place a speed gate 2m past the move position. Player must reach it within 2 steps of completing the pull-back
Too far from defender: in Phase 2, require the player to execute the elastico within 1m of the partner cone/player
Only right foot: add a left-foot only round in Phase 1 — 10 reps left foot before the 1v1 phases begin
progressions: Speed up: the outside push must happen within 0.3 seconds and the pull-back within 0.3 seconds of that — use a rapid clap-clap signal to calibrate the timing
Combine with acceleration: after the elastico, player must reach a second cone 5m away in under 2 seconds
Competitive elastico tournament: round-robin format where the only move allowed is the elastico. Player with the most successful beats is the champion
regressions: Slow the required foot speed by 50 percent and focus purely on the two-foot-contact pattern before any timing pressure
Use a cone as the target instead of a live partner in Phase 2 to remove the social pressure
Allow 3 steps between the two touches in Phase 1 to establish the foot pattern before compressing the timing
matchApplication: The elastico is one of the most celebrated skill moves in football history precisely because it requires both technical precision and physical courage to execute against a live defender. Ronaldinho used it to beat defenders at the very highest level. Neymar has made it part of his personal skill vocabulary. Every player who learns the elastico is connecting to a lineage of Brazilian street footballers who invented, refined, and shared this move as part of the living culture of football creativity. The move is difficult, which is exactly what makes it worth learning.
coachTips: Tell the Ronaldinho story before the session. Knowing they are learning a move made famous by one of the greatest players ever is enormous motivation for U13 players.
Demonstrate the move yourself at slow motion (showing the two touches clearly) and then at full speed. The contrast is instructive.
The Phase 3 bonus scoring (3 points for an elastico goal) is the most important motivational element. Track and celebrate every successful elastico.
Film players in Phase 3 and show them their own elastico attempts. Seeing the timing gap between touches is more correctable than hearing about it.
Run this drill as a monthly specialist session. The elastico improves significantly with regular practice and becomes a genuine match tool within a season.
diagramDescription: Open area progressing to 8x6m channels. ELASTICO TECHNIQUE diagram: foot sequence shown in 3 steps: 1 (dribbling approach), 2 (outside-of-foot push RIGHT — fake direction arrow), 3 (inside-of-foot pull-back LEFT — actual direction arrow). TIMING shown: steps 2 and 3 compressed into a rapid double-arrow. Defender shown reacting to step 2 (fake) while step 3 has already redirected the ball. Phase 3 shown as a live 1v1 with bonus scoring: elastico goal = 3pts shown prominently. Ronaldinho inspiration reference shown.

title: Movement Freestyle — Create Your Own Drill
objective: Give players complete creative ownership over their own training by designing and teaching a drill of their own invention to their teammates.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the deep understanding that comes from teaching a skill or drill to others
Players build creative ownership over their own development through self-directed practice
Players experience the satisfaction of coaching peers and having their ideas valued
Players develop the meta-skill of analysing what makes a drill effective
equipment: Any available equipment (cones, poles, goals, bibs)
Paper and pen for design phase
organisation: Groups of 3-4 players. Each group has 5 minutes to design a drill, 5 minutes to practice it themselves, and 5 minutes to teach it to another group. The session ends with each group performing their drill for the whole team and receiving feedback.
instructions: DESIGN PHASE (5 minutes): each group of 3-4 is given paper and pen and asked to design a drill. The only requirements: it must involve a ball, it must be something all of them can do, and it must have a clear purpose (what skill does it develop?).
Coach circulates during the design phase asking questions: what skill will this develop? How will you know if it is working? What makes it fun?
PRACTICE PHASE (5 minutes): each group practices their own drill and refines it. They can make changes based on what works.
TEACHING PHASE (5 minutes): each group finds another group and teaches them their drill. The teaching group must explain the purpose, demonstrate the drill, and give feedback during practice.
SHOWCASE PHASE (10 minutes): each group performs their drill for the whole team. Coach asks two questions after each showcase: what skill does this develop? Would you use this drill again?
Finally: vote on the best drill. The group who designed it is celebrated as drill designers.
coachingPoints: TEACHING SHOWS YOU REALLY UNDERSTAND: when you try to explain a drill to someone else, you discover very quickly whether you really understand it. Teaching is the deepest form of learning.
YOUR IDEAS ARE VALID: there are no wrong drills in this session. Every drill designed by a player contains a valid insight about how football can be practiced. Trust your ideas.

THE BEST DRILLS ARE SIMPLE AND PURPOSEFUL: the great coaches throughout football history have designed the most effective drills. They are almost always simple (easy to understand), purposeful (develop a specific skill), and enjoyable (players want to do them). Aim for these three qualities.

CREATIVITY IN COACHING IS JUST LIKE CREATIVITY IN PLAYING: a coach who can invent a drill is using exactly the same creative skill as a player who invents a skill move. Creativity in football is everywhere.

FEEDBACK MAKES DRILLS BETTER: when you teach your drill and see how other players interact with it, you will see things about it you did not see before. This is how professional coaches improve their drills — through watching them be practiced.

YOUR TEAMMATES ARE YOUR BEST COACHES: the best feedback often comes from the players who practice the drill, not from the professional coach. Listen to what your teammates say about what works and what does not.
THERE IS ALWAYS A NEXT VERSION: every drill designed in this session has a next version that is slightly better. Think about how you would change it based on what you saw during the teaching phase.

YOU HAVE LEARNED FROM EVERY DRILL IN THIS LIBRARY: every drill in this session has come from the same creative process you are going through now. Professional coaches, just like you in this session, asked: what skill do I want to develop? How can I make practicing it fun?
commonMistakes: Groups designing drills that are too complex to teach in 5 minutes
Groups designing drills that have no clear connection to a football skill
Players not engaging seriously with the design phase, treating it as a rest period
Dismissing a groups drill as silly without identifying what is valid about it
Groups not adapting their drill during the practice phase when something does not work
corrections: Too complex: during the circulating design phase, ask each group to reduce their drill to its simplest version first
No football connection: ask the question what football skill does this develop and require an answer before the practice phase begins
Not engaging with design: ask each player to contribute at least one specific idea to the design. Record each players contribution on the paper
Dismissing others: frame the showcase feedback as first something good, then a question. No purely negative feedback allowed
Not adapting: specifically ask during the practice phase has anything surprised you about your drill and how might you change it
progressions: Full coach role reversal: one player leads the entire warm-up using their own drill design, with the coach as a participant
Drill design competition: groups design drills for a specific category (e.g. passing activation) and the group whose drill is most used across the season wins
Season-long drill book: collect every drill designed in these sessions in a team drill book. Build a library owned by the players
regressions: Provide a drill design template (simple diagram with name, purpose, instructions) to support groups who find the open design brief overwhelming
Reduce groups to pairs to simplify the collaboration
Give a specific starting point (the drill must involve a pass and a run) to narrow the open brief while still allowing creativity
matchApplication: Movement Freestyle is the most advanced session in The Streets Meet Tiki-Taka category because it asks players to step into the role of the coach — to think about football not as something that happens to them but as something they can design and shape. This is the logical extension of the creative philosophy of the entire category: if we ask players to be creative with the ball, we should also ask them to be creative about the game itself. Players who have experienced designing, teaching, and refining their own drills develop a depth of football understanding that purely passive learners never achieve.
coachTips: Your role in this session is almost entirely facilitative. Ask questions, listen, and celebrate. Do not correct or direct.
The showcase phase is the emotional highlight of the session. Make it feel genuinely special — a performance, not an assessment.
Take photos of the drill designs on the paper and share them with the team after the session.
The best drill designed in this session is worth actually using in a future training session. When you do, tell the team it was designed by their teammate.
This session is worth running once per month. Over a season, the creative and coaching intelligence it develops in players is remarkable.
diagramDescription: Open area. DESIGN PHASE shown with 3-4 players around paper drawing a drill diagram. PRACTICE PHASE shown with the same group testing their drill. TEACHING PHASE shown with one group demonstrating to another group. SHOWCASE PHASE shown with the whole team watching one group perform their drill. Coach shown in facilitative position — asking questions, not directing. Celebration and voting icons shown at the end. The Drill Designers label shown celebrating the winning group.

title: Futsal 2v2 — Rapid Fire Scoring
objective: Experience the rapid, high-touch intensity of futsal through continuous 2v2 games with rapid rotation, maximising every players ball contact time.
learningOutcomes: Players experience the maximum-touch environment of futsal small-sided play
Players develop the quick passing and movement of futsal within a simple 2v2 format
Players build the energy, intensity and joy of rapid-fire competitive football
Players discover the tactical benefits of a 2v2 partnership in tight space
players: Groups of 4 (2v2) rotating across multiple courts
pitchSize: 10x7m court per 2v2 game — multiple courts simultaneously
organisation: Multiple 10x7m futsal courts running simultaneously. 2v2 on each court. 90-second games. After every 90 seconds, both teams rotate to the next court and face a new opposition pair. This creates a rapid-fire tournament format where every player is almost always either playing or about to play.
instructions: Set up multiple 10x7m courts with mini-goals at each end.
Split players into pairs. Each pair plays on one court.
2v2 game starts. After 90 seconds the coach signals and all pairs rotate clockwise to the next court.
Track wins with a simple score (pair with most court wins after 10 minutes wins).
RULES: normal football rules within the small court. Ball out of play: restart from the nearest mini-goal (GK restart).
KEY TOUCH RULE: before any shot is allowed, both players on the attacking pair must have touched the ball in that possession. This forces maximum partner involvement.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): standard 2v2 with the two-touch rule. Rapid rotation every 90 seconds.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): add a FUTSAL TRAP rule. If the defending pair traps the ball in the corner, they earn a free restart from their mini-goal (rewards active defence).
Score and celebrate after each 90-second game.
coachingPoints: MORE TOUCHES THAN ANY OTHER FORMAT: in a 2v2 on a tiny court, every player touches the ball many times per minute. There is no hiding, no standing on the wing waiting. You are always involved.
YOUR PARTNER IS EVERYTHING: in a 2v2 you have only one partner and you must work with them constantly. Communicate, call for the ball, move to create space for them after every pass.
QUICK FEET BEAT PACE IN FUTSAL: in a 10x7m court there is no room to sprint. Players who use quick touches, close control, and clever movement beat faster players every time.
BOTH PLAYERS MUST TOUCH BEFORE SHOOTING: the two-touch rule forces the most important habit in this format. No individual runs from your own half to the goal. Always involve your partner.
THE MINI-GOAL IS ALWAYS CLOSE: in futsal, you are always close to the goal. Every time you have the ball you should be thinking about whether a shot is available right now.

USE THE WALLS IF AVAILABLE: in indoor futsal, the walls are part of the game. A pass off the wall to yourself or to a teammate bypasses a defender without any contact. If playing indoors, use this every game.
CELEBRATE EVERY 90 SECONDS: when the timer sounds, celebrate the game immediately regardless of who won. The next game is already starting. The energy must always be high.

THE BEST FUTSAL PLAYERS ARE THE BEST FOOTBALL PLAYERS: Ronaldo, Messi, Neymar, and virtually every great player grew up playing futsal. The quick feet, close control, and rapid decision-making of futsal are the same qualities that make great footballers.
commonMistakes: Only one player touching the ball (violating the two-touch rule)
Losing energy and intensity after losing a 90-second game
Not communicating with the partner about runs and passes
Playing too slowly in the tight space — needing too many touches to make decisions
Ignoring the mini-goal when the opportunity exists, circulating the ball unnecessarily
corrections: Only one player touching: immediately call the two-touch violation and restart possession for the defending pair
Losing energy after a loss: remind players that the next game starts in 5 seconds. The loss is already irrelevant
Not communicating: require the player without the ball to call NAME before every pass so partners must communicate on every action
Too slow in tight space: introduce a 3-touch maximum per player for Phase 2 to force quicker decision-making
Ignoring mini-goal: award a bonus point for any shot taken within 2 seconds of receiving the ball when in a shooting position
progressions: 1v1 futsal: remove the partner requirement and play 1v1 on even smaller courts (8x5m) for pure individual technique
Add walls: if an indoor space is available, use the walls as part of play for the full futsal experience
Speed challenge: how many goals can a pair score across 5 consecutive 90-second games? Their total is the pair score
regressions: Increase court size to 14x10m to give less experienced players more space and time with the ball
Allow unlimited touches for both players before the shot requirement
Extend games to 2 minutes with no rotation to give pairs more time to develop their partnership before a new opponent arrives
matchApplication: Futsal developed many of the greatest technical players in history specifically because the small space, constant movement, and rapid decision-making of the format train the exact qualities that make great footballers. The 2v2 format takes these principles to their most concentrated expression: two players in maximum partnership, in maximum space compression, with maximum ball contact frequency. Every 90-second game produces more developmental value than much longer large-sided games because every player is central to every action, every moment.
coachTips: The rotation format is what makes this session special. Keep it fast and energetic — 90 seconds, celebrate, rotate, play again.
Count touches aloud with players occasionally to show them how many more touches they are getting than in a larger game.
The pair dynamic is worth coaching: ask pairs between games what worked and what they want to try next game.
Play music throughout. The rapid-fire competitive format with music creates an extraordinary energy.
Connect to futsal professionals: show a clip of futsal if possible. Brazilian futsal in particular shows the quick-foot technical quality that transfers to football.
diagramDescription: Multiple 10x7m courts shown side by side. Each court has 2v2 teams (circles vs triangles). Mini-goals at each end. 90-SECOND TIMER shown prominently. ROTATION ARROWS shown: all teams rotating clockwise to the next court after the timer. TWO-TOUCH RULE label shown: both players must touch before shot. Score tracker shown per court. FUTSAL TRAP rule shown in Phase 2: defending pair shown cornering ball with a FREE RESTART reward. Energy and celebration icons throughout.

title: Rondo With Walls — Futsal Intelligence
objective: Develop the wall-pass and rebound intelligence of futsal by combining the rondo possession format with wall passes that cannot be intercepted.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the futsal-specific skill of using the wall as a passing option
Players build the spatial intelligence to identify when a wall pass creates a better outcome than a direct pass
Players develop the quick combination play of futsal in a possession rondo format
Players experience the unique tactical dimension that walls add to football creativity
equipment: Cones for an 8x8m square near a wall or fence
pitchSize: 8x8m square adjacent to a wall or fence
organisation: Standard 5v2 rondo in an 8x8m square. The unique element: one side of the square is against a wall or fence. Possession players can use the wall as a passing option — the wall always returns the ball to the sender or into a new position. Defenders cannot intercept wall passes.
instructions: Set up the 8x8m square with one side against a wall.
5 possession players, 2 defenders inside.

WALL PASS RULE: any player can play the ball against the wall at any time. The ball rebounds off the wall and can be collected by themselves or played to a teammate. Defenders cannot intercept a ball while it is in contact with the wall.
This creates a unique new passing option: instead of only passing to one of the other 4 teammates, any player also has the wall as an automatic return.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): standard 5v2 rondo with the wall available. Players discover the wall pass naturally.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): coach calls WALL at any moment. The player with the ball must use the wall within 2 touches or possession resets.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): defenders can now position themselves to cut off the wall pass (standing near the wall side). Possession players must decide when the wall is available and when it is not.
Count: how many successful wall passes does each team use across the session?
coachingPoints: THE WALL IS YOUR FASTEST PASSING OPTION: a pass to the wall and back happens faster than a pass to a teammate and a return. When defenders are pressing hard, the wall pass is often the quickest and safest option.

THE WALL PASS BYPASSES THE DEFENDER: a ball played into the wall and rebounding behind a pressing defender is uncatchable by that defender. This is exactly how futsal players use walls to escape pressure.

ANGLE OF THE WALL PASS DETERMINES WHERE IT RETURNS: a pass hit straight at the wall returns straight back. A pass hit at an angle returns at the opposite angle. Use this to play the wall as a pass to a specific position.
THE WALL PASS CREATES A 2v0: when you play the wall and collect your own rebound, you have effectively made a 1-2 with the wall with zero risk of interception during the wall contact.
WHEN NOT TO USE THE WALL: if a defender positions close to the wall side, the wall pass may rebound to a defensive position. Read whether the wall is safe before using it.
COMBINE WALL AND TEAMMATE PASSES: the most dangerous possession play uses both the wall and teammates unpredictably so defenders cannot focus on one option.
