On SWITCH: the ball must travel in maximum 2 passes from the left side to a wide right player who receives in space.
The wide right player has 3 seconds to deliver a cross or shoot before the right-side defenders can close.
SWITCHING PASS TECHNIQUE: the switching pass must be accurate and driven. A lofted switch gives the defence time to reorganise. A driven flat pass switches faster than defences can shift.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): coach calls SWITCH every 20 seconds. Attackers practice the two-pass switch sequence.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): attackers choose when to switch based on reading the defensive shape. Switch when the right side has fewer defenders.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): full game. Points scored for switching and then delivering a cross within 3 seconds.
coachingPoints: READ THE FAR SIDE BEFORE THE SWITCH: the player making the switching pass must already know where the far-side wide player is positioned before playing the switch. Looking across before the switch is essential.
THE SWITCH MUST BE QUICK: a slow switch gives the defence time to shift across. The switch pass and the movement of the wide player must happen simultaneously.

FAR-SIDE WIDE PLAYER MUST STAY WIDE: the player waiting for the switch must hold their wide position rather than drifting central. A wide player who drifts central removes the space the switch is designed to exploit.
TWO PASSES MAXIMUM TO SWITCH: direct ball to the wide player in one pass is ideal. Two passes is the maximum. More passes and the defence has reorganised before the ball arrives.
ATTACK IMMEDIATELY AFTER THE SWITCH: the wide player who receives the switch has a brief window before defenders recover. Attack immediately — do not hold or wait.

THE SWITCH CREATES OVERLOADS: when the ball switches from one side where 6 opponents are concentrated to the other side where only 3 are positioned, the wide receiver has a 2v1 or 3v2 advantage for approximately 3 seconds.
USE THE SWITCH UNPREDICTABLY: switching play too often becomes predictable. Use it when the opposition have fully committed to one side, creating the maximum width advantage.
BOTH DIRECTIONS: practice switching from left to right and from right to left equally. A team that can only switch in one direction is easier to defend against.
commonMistakes: Looking for the switch too early before the defence has committed to one side
Switch pass too slow (lofted rather than driven), giving the defence time to reorganise
Far-side wide player drifting central and removing the space the switch is designed to exploit
Attacking team not exploiting the advantage immediately after the switch
Switch being used predictably (every possession) rather than selectively when the situation is correct
corrections: Too early: add a minimum of 3 passes before any switch is allowed, ensuring possession is built before the switch
Slow switch pass: require the switch pass to arrive at the wide player within 2 seconds of leaving the passers foot
Wide player drifting: place a wide channel cone showing the minimum width the far-side player must maintain
Not exploiting immediately: the 3-second shooting or crossing window is strictly enforced with a whistle at 3 seconds
Predictable switching: coach calls NO SWITCH for a 2-minute phase, forcing the team to recognise that the switch is not always available
progressions: Reduce switch window to 2 seconds before the defensive side can close, increasing the urgency
Add a second switch in the same possession: left to right then immediately right to left
Full match with a switch bonus: any goal scored within 3 passes of a switch earns 3 points
regressions: Allow the switch to take 3 passes and extend the attacking window to 5 seconds while the pattern is established
Use passive defenders on the far side who hold their position rather than pressing immediately
Walk through the switch sequence step by step at slow pace before any live practice
matchApplication: Switching play is one of the most effective attacking tools in football because it exploits the natural tendency of defenders to compress toward the ball. A team that can circulate the ball on one side to attract the defensive block and then switch quickly to the opposite flank finds the most dangerous attacking position in football: a wide player with space in front of them, time to deliver a cross, and defenders who are still recovering across the pitch. Teaching this concept at U12 gives players one of the most useful attacking principles they will use at every level.
coachTips: The two-pass maximum rule is the most important constraint. Strictly enforce it and celebrate every fast switch.
The far-side wide player discipline is the hardest element to maintain. Check their positioning constantly during Phase 1.
Film from above during Phase 3 if possible — the defensive shape shift and the space created by the switch is only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional examples: every great team uses switching play as a core tactical weapon. Show a clip from a professional match.
Once established, this principle can be used as a constraint in any possession session.
diagramDescription: 40x30m area. Attacking team (circles) shown building possession on the left side. Most defensive triangles shown compressed on the left. Far-side wide player (circle) shown holding position on the right edge. SWITCH moment shown: driven pass arrow crossing the full width to the right wide player. 3-second attacking window shown as a timer. Defenders shown still on the left side trying to shift across. Far-side 2v1 advantage highlighted.

title: Creating Depth — Runs Beyond the Line
objective: Develop the runs and movements that create vertical depth in attack, stretching the defensive line and creating space between and behind defenders.
learningOutcomes: Players understand how diagonal and vertical runs stretch the defensive line
Players develop the timing of runs in behind to complement short combination play
Players learn when to make a penetrating run versus when to stay short
Teams develop the habit of always having a run in behind available as a threat
organisation: 35x25m zone. 4 attackers work against 3 defenders. The specific focus is on creating depth — at least one attacker must always be making or threatening a run in behind the defensive line. A goal only counts if it follows either a run in behind or a pass that exploits the space created by such a run.
instructions: 4 attackers begin possession at one end of the zone. 3 defenders hold a compact line.
DEPTH RULE: at all times, at least one attacker must be either making a run in behind the defensive line OR positioned beyond the defensive line as a depth threat.
If the depth player drops short, they must immediately be replaced by another attacker making a run in behind.
SCORING: a goal from a penetrating pass in behind earns 3 points. A goal from the space created by a run in behind (defender pulled out of position) earns 2 points. Any other goal earns 1 point.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): coach points to the depth player and calls DEPTH whenever the depth run is missing. Attackers learn to always maintain the threat.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): coach no longer calls DEPTH. Attackers must self-organise to maintain the threat.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): full game. The scoring system applies. Defenders specifically try to deal with both the short possession and the run in behind simultaneously.
coachingPoints: THE DEPTH RUN MAKES THE SHORT PASS BETTER: when a defender must track a run in behind, they cannot simultaneously press the ball. The depth run creates space for the short pass even when the ball is not played in behind.

THE THREAT IS AS VALUABLE AS THE PASS: a run in behind that is never played to still creates space for teammates by occupying a defender. The depth threat is valuable whether or not the ball is played.

TIMING TO THE BALL-CARRIER READINESS: the run in behind must begin when the ball-carrier is ready to play the pass — when their head is up and they can see the run. Too early and the run is wasted before the ball can be played.
VARY THE ANGLE OF THE DEPTH RUN: diagonal runs in behind are often more effective than straight runs because they are harder for a single defender to track and they cover more dangerous space.

THE DEPTH PLAYER MUST STAY ONSIDE: runs in behind are only valuable if they are onside. The depth player must watch the defensive line and time the run to stay level with or behind the last defender until the ball is played.
ROTATE THE DEPTH ROLE: all 4 attackers should take turns in the depth position. A team where only one player makes runs in behind is much easier to defend because only one defender needs to track it.
COMMUNICATE THE RUN: a brief signal (eye contact, hand gesture, or verbal call) from the depth runner to the ball-carrier tells the ball-carrier that the run is coming and when to play it.

DEPTH AND WIDTH TOGETHER: the most effective attacks combine width (wide players stretching the defensive line horizontally) with depth (runners stretching it vertically). Both together create the most space.
commonMistakes: Only one player ever making the depth run while others always stay short
Depth runs starting too early before the ball-carrier is ready to play the penetrating pass
Runs in behind going straight rather than diagonally, reducing the space covered and making them easier to defend
Depth player going offside repeatedly by starting the run too early
No communication between depth runner and ball-carrier about the timing of the run
corrections: Only one depth runner: designate a rotation requirement. After any depth run (whether or not the ball is played), a different attacker must make the next depth run
Too early: use the hand-raise trigger from the movement off the ball drill. Ball-carrier raises hand when ready. Depth runner starts run on the signal
Straight runs: place diagonal target cones showing where runs in behind should go. Runs must go toward these diagonal target positions
Offside: use a clear offside line (a line of cones) and specifically coach the depth player to stay behind it until the ball is about to be played
No communication: require a verbal call (NAME or GO) from the depth runner before any penetrating pass is played
progressions: Two depth runners simultaneously: both create the split run, forcing the central defender to choose which to track
Time the ball to the depth runner: the pass in behind must arrive before a defender tracking from the defensive line can intercept
Full game where the only valid goal is scored from a run in behind — forces the team to develop the depth habit
regressions: Remove the offside line for Phase 1 to allow unlimited depth runs without the timing pressure
Allow the depth player to receive and return to play (not forced to go to goal) while the movement pattern is established
Use passive defenders who hold their line rather than actively tracking, making the depth runs easier to execute
matchApplication: Creating depth through runs in behind is one of the fundamental attacking concepts that all great teams use to stretch and disorganise defensive lines. A defence that is compact and well-organised horizontally can be stretched vertically by runners who threaten the space behind. Once the defensive line drops to deal with this threat, space opens up between the lines for the short combination players. The interaction between depth and short play is what makes the most effective attacking football genuinely difficult to defend against.
coachTips: The DEPTH RULE is what makes this drill unique and valuable. Enforce it consistently and celebrate every time the depth threat is maintained.
Phase 2 (self-organisation without coach prompting) is the most important development phase. The quality of the self-organisation tells you how well the concept has been internalised.
Connect to the teams own match patterns: in your last match, how many times did an attacker make a run in behind that created space for a teammate? Discuss specific moments.
The scoring system incentivises both the run and the space it creates. Both outcomes are valuable and both should be celebrated.
Run this drill regularly across the season. The depth habit develops gradually and is one of the most visible tactical improvements when it becomes consistent.
diagramDescription: 35x25m zone. 4 attackers (circles) shown. DEPTH PLAYER shown making a diagonal run beyond the defensive line (dashed run arrow going diagonally in behind). 3 defenders shown: 2 compressing toward the ball, 1 tracking the depth run (pulled out of compact line). Space created between defenders highlighted. Ball-carrier shown with two options: pass in behind to depth runner (3 points) or pass into the space vacated by the tracking defender (2 points). Rotation arrow showing next attacker becoming the depth player.

title: Low Block Defending — Compact Shape and Counter
objective: Develop the discipline and organisation of a low defensive block that absorbs opposition attacks and transitions to counter-attacks.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain a compact defensive shape in a low block for extended periods
Players develop the specific individual roles within the low block structure
Players build the transition from low block to counter-attack that maximises the effectiveness of the defensive system
Teams experience how a well-organised low block limits the quality of opposition chances
organisation: Full pitch. Defending team of 6-7 sets up in a low block (defensive and midfield lines in their own half). Attacking team of 6 attacks continuously. When the defending team wins the ball they counter-attack with a maximum of 4 touches to score.
instructions: DEFENSIVE SHAPE: low block uses two compact lines in the defending teams own half.
DEFENSIVE LINE: 4 defenders across the width of the penalty area, 10-15m from goal.
MIDFIELD LINE: 3-4 midfielders 20-25m from goal, creating a compact band between the two lines.
KEY PRINCIPLE: the space between the two lines (between 10m and 20m from goal) must be minimal. No more than 10m between the defensive and midfield lines.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): attacking team possesses the ball freely around the defending teams half. Defending team holds the low block shape. Coach evaluates compactness using WIDTH and DEPTH checks.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): attacking team can enter the penalty area. Defending team must maintain shape, track runners, and deal with crosses while staying compact.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full competitive game. Defending team earns 3 points for a counter-attack goal within 4 touches of winning the ball. Attacking team earns 1 point for any goal.
coachingPoints: COMPACTNESS IS THE PRIMARY WEAPON: the low block works by denying space between and behind the defensive lines. If the space is too large, the attacking team plays through it. If it is compact, they are forced to attempt low-percentage long shots or crosses.

THE MIDFIELD LINE IS THE MOST IMPORTANT: the midfield line of the low block does the most work. They must press the ball when it arrives at the edge of their block, track any runners from midfield, and prevent passes between the lines.

DO NOT CHASE — HOLD THE SHAPE: the natural tendency under pressure is to chase the ball. In a low block, chasing the ball breaks the shape and creates the gaps the attacking team wants. Hold the shape and let the ball come to you.
TRACK RUNNERS FROM THE DEFENSIVE LINE: when an attacker makes a run into the penalty area, the relevant defensive-line player must track them. The cover player fills the gap left by the tracker.

THE COUNTER-ATTACK IS THE REWARD: the low block is not a passive defensive system — it is an active attacking plan. Winning the ball in the low block creates a transition opportunity. 4 touches maximum to score means the counter must be executed instantly.
COMMUNICATION IS CONSTANT: the players in the two lines must talk continuously. WIDTH checks, runner alerts, and cover calls must happen on every opposition possession.

PATIENCE UNDER PRESSURE IS THE MENTAL CHALLENGE: maintaining a low block shape for 10-15 minutes under sustained pressure is mentally and physically demanding. The team that maintains concentration and discipline for longer wins the defensive battle.

RECOGNISE WHEN TO PRESS: even in a low block, specific triggers (a back pass, a heavy touch, a poorly positioned attacker) should prompt a press from the nearest player while the rest of the block compacts inward.
commonMistakes: Midfield line dropping too deep and merging with the defensive line, removing the two-line structure
Individual players chasing the ball and breaking the compact shape
No counter-attack urgency when the ball is won — holding the ball rather than attacking immediately
Defensive line tracking runners without a cover player filling the gap
Communication stopping as the session progresses and fatigue increases
corrections: Midfield line dropping too deep: place cone markers at 20-25m from goal showing the minimum midfield line position. The midfield players cannot drop below this marker
Chasing ball: immediately call SHAPE when any player breaks from the block. Stop play and reset the positions before restarting
No counter-attack urgency: strictly enforce the 4-touch maximum. Any counter-attack possession that reaches 5 touches is turned over to the attacking team
No cover for tracking: require the nearest non-tracking defensive player to explicitly call COVER as they fill the gap whenever a tracker leaves position
Communication stopping: require a minimum of one verbal call per player per 30 seconds of defending. Silence is treated as a shape error
progressions: Reduce the counter-attack window to 3 touches to increase the urgency of the transition
Add a specific counter-attack target: the counter-attack must involve the player who won the ball in the transition
Remove the counter-attack element entirely and use only a point system for consecutive minutes of correct compact shape — rewards pure defensive discipline
regressions: Use a completely passive attacking team for Phase 1 (ball circulation only, no shooting or runs) while the shape is established
Extend the counter-attack window to 6 touches during initial sessions
Reduce to a 5v4 defending advantage to make maintaining the block less demanding while the positional habits are built
matchApplication: The low block is one of the most widely used and most effective defensive systems at every level of football, used by teams ranging from Atletico Madrid to countless grassroots clubs as their primary tactical approach. The combination of compactness, patience, and clinical counter-attacking that defines the low block requires specific training to develop — the shape, the communication, and the counter-attack mentality all need deliberate practice. Teams that execute a well-organised low block are significantly harder to break down than teams that defend in a disorganised reactive way.
coachTips: The WIDTH and DEPTH check system from the compactness drill is directly applicable here. Use it regularly throughout Phase 1.
The counter-attack urgency is the most important element for team motivation. Celebrate every successful 4-touch counter-attack goal enthusiastically.
Film from above during Phase 2. The two-line compactness and any gaps that appear between them are only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to professional examples: Atletico Madrid under Simeone is the best modern example of an elite low block. Show footage and identify the two lines.
This system requires consistent practice across many sessions before the shape and communication become automatic. Return to it regularly.
diagramDescription: Full pitch with emphasis on defending half. DEFENSIVE SHAPE shown: DEFENSIVE LINE (4 defenders, 10-15m from goal). MIDFIELD LINE (3-4 midfielders, 20-25m from goal). COMPACT SPACE between lines shown as a maximum 10m gap (labeled). Attacking team shown circulating the ball around the outside of the block. CHASING BALL shown as WRONG with an arrow showing a defender breaking shape. HOLDING SHAPE shown as CORRECT. COUNTER-ATTACK shown: ball won by defender, 4-touch sequence arrows going directly toward opposition goal within 4 touches.

title: Transition to Defence — Immediate Press After Losing Ball
objective: Develop the immediate collective pressing response in the 5 seconds after losing possession — the defensive transition moment that prevents opponents from exploiting the space left by the attacking shape.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the automatic pressing response within 2 seconds of losing the ball
Players understand their individual role in the defensive transition based on their position when possession is lost
Teams build the collective habit of immediate pressing that prevents transition goals
Players develop the physical intensity required for repeated pressing efforts across a full match
organisation: Full pitch competitive game. The session has one specific rule that makes every possession change a coaching moment: when any team loses the ball, the coach immediately calls PRESS and the team that just lost it must win it back within 5 seconds. If they do not, they concede a point to the opposition. Successful presses earn a point.
instructions: Both teams play a normal competitive match on the full pitch.
AT EVERY POSSESSION CHANGE: coach calls PRESS immediately.
The team that just lost possession must apply immediate collective pressure.
PRESSING RULES: the nearest player to the ball presses immediately. All other players within 10m press the most dangerous receivers. Players further away compact.
If the ball is won back within 5 seconds: pressing team earns 1 point.
If possession is not won back within 5 seconds: the team that won possession earns 1 point.
Goals also earn points (2 points each).
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): coach pauses play after every PRESS and evaluates: who pressed? Who compacted? Who did not react?
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): continuous play with the PRESS call but no pauses.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): no PRESS call from the coach. Players must recognise possession changes independently and press automatically.
coachingPoints: THE 5 SECONDS AFTER LOSING THE BALL ARE THE MOST IMPORTANT: in the 5 seconds after losing the ball, the team that just conceded possession is in an attacking shape and cannot defend effectively. The team that wins the ball is often in a transition position with space to attack. Winning the ball back in this window is the highest-value defensive action in football.

THE NEAREST PLAYER MUST PRESS IMMEDIATELY: there is no decision to be made for the nearest player when possession is lost. They press immediately. The decision about whether to press is made before the ball is lost, not after.
DO NOT WATCH THE BALL — PRESS IT: the most common error in defensive transition is players watching what happens rather than immediately reacting. The reaction must be automatic.

COMPACT THE DANGEROUS RECEIVERS: all players within 10m of the ball press either the ball-carrier or the most dangerous player they can receive a pass. This cuts off the simple escape passes that would beat the press.

PRESS WITH ENERGY OR NOT AT ALL: a low-intensity press is worse than no press. It uses energy without winning the ball, gives the opposition a false sense of pressure, and disorganises the defensive shape. Press at maximum intensity or hold a defensive shape.

THE FIRST PRESS OFTEN FAILS — THE SECOND MUST NOT: the press rarely wins the ball from the first press attempt alone. The second and third press attempts are what ultimately win the ball. Sustain the intensity for 5 full seconds.
PHASE 3 SHOWS THE REAL LEVEL: when the coach no longer calls PRESS, the quality of the automatic pressing response reveals the true level of the defensive transition habit.

WINNING THE BALL IN TRANSITION LEADS TO THE BEST CHANCES: statistically, goals scored from winning the ball in transition are the highest-quality chances in football because the opposition is in an attacking shape and cannot defend immediately.
commonMistakes: Players watching the possession change instead of immediately pressing
Only the nearest player pressing while others hold their positions or walk
Low-intensity press that uses energy without genuine ball-winning threat
No compacting of dangerous receivers — all pressure goes to the ball
Pressing habit failing to appear in Phase 3 without the coach call
corrections: Watching instead of pressing: the coach calls PRESS as loudly and urgently as possible to jolt players into the immediate response. Gradually reduce the volume across phases.
Only nearest player pressing: specifically coach the 2-3 players nearest the ball that ALL of them press — the nearest presses the ball, the others cut off the nearest passing options
Low intensity: make the 5-second window visible by counting aloud (ONE TWO THREE...). Players can hear the time pressure and increase their intensity
No compacting dangerous receivers: freeze play immediately after any press fails due to an easy escape pass and identify which dangerous receiver should have been covered
No automatic response in Phase 3: return to Phase 1 (paused evaluation) for one more session before attempting Phase 3 again
progressions: Reduce the press window to 3 seconds to increase the urgency and difficulty
Add a bonus: if the pressing team wins the ball and scores directly from the transition, they earn 5 points
Specific scenario practice: the coach deliberately creates 5-second transition moments by deliberately passing the ball to the other team and immediately calling PRESS
regressions: Use a walking match pace in Phase 1 to allow players more time to understand and react to the PRESS call
Reduce to 8v8 to lower the number of players involved and simplify the pressing responsibilities
Extend the press window to 8 seconds while the automatic pressing response is being developed
matchApplication: The 5 seconds after losing possession is consistently identified by elite coaches as the most critical defensive moment in football. Teams that press immediately and collectively in this window prevent the counter-attacks and transition goals that are the most dangerous in modern football. Jurgen Klopp built the entire Liverpool and Dortmund defensive identity around this principle — gegenpressing, or counter-pressing, the immediate collective press after losing the ball. This drill develops exactly that collective habit, making the immediate press an automatic response that requires no individual decision-making.
coachTips: Your PRESS call must be immediate — any delay communicates that the moment is not as urgent as it is.
Phase 3 is the real test and the most important phase. Schedule it for the last session of a 3-session block on this topic.
The point system makes every press meaningful. Keep the score prominent and discuss it between phases.
Connect to Klopp and Liverpool: the most famous and successful pressing team in recent history used exactly this principle as their primary defensive tactic.
Film Phase 3 from above. The pressing reaction time and the coverage of dangerous receivers are both visible from an elevated angle.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. POSSESSION CHANGE MOMENT shown with a star and PRESS call. Team that just lost the ball (circles) shown with immediate reaction: nearest circle pressing the ball (press arrow), 2 more circles pressing nearest dangerous receivers (coverage arrows), remaining circles shown compacting. 5-SECOND COUNTDOWN shown. SUCCESSFUL PRESS shown: ball won back within 5 seconds. FAILED PRESS shown: easy escape pass to uncovered receiver (dangerous receiver not covered highlighted). PHASE 3 shown with no coach call — players reacting automatically.

title: Overloads — Creating and Using 2v1 Advantages
objective: Teach young players how to recognise and exploit a 2v1 numerical advantage in attack — the most fundamental overload in football.
learningOutcomes: Players can identify when a 2v1 advantage exists in their attacking situation
Players learn the two options in a 2v1: pass to the free player or draw the defender and pass
Players develop the habit of creating 2v1 situations through movement and support
Players experience how a 2v1 advantage reliably creates goal-scoring opportunities
players: Groups of 3 (2 attackers vs 1 defender) rotating
organisation: Groups of 3 (Player A, Player B attacking vs Player D defending) in a 15x10m zone with a mini-goal at the far end. The session teaches the two correct decisions in a 2v1 and gives players many repetitions of both.
instructions: Player A starts with the ball at one end of the zone. Player B starts wide alongside A. Player D starts at the midpoint as the lone defender.
Player A dribbles toward Player D. Player B moves wide and ahead to provide the 2v1 support.
TWO DECISIONS for Player A:
DECISION 1 — DEFENDER COMES TO A: Player D steps toward A to press. A passes to B who is free. B attacks the mini-goal.
DECISION 2 — DEFENDER STAYS: Player D holds their position between A and B. A dribbles past D and attacks the mini-goal, or passes to B who has moved inside.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): Player D is passive (holds position). A and B practice recognising D position and making the correct decision. Coach narrates each decision.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): Player D is semi-active — can move toward either A or B but cannot tackle. A must read D movement and make the correct decision.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): full live 2v1. D can tackle. A and B must beat D and score.
Rotate the defender role every 3 minutes.
coachingPoints: THE 2V1 MEANS SOMEONE IS ALWAYS FREE: in a 2v1, the defender cannot cover both attackers simultaneously. The player the defender is NOT covering is always free. Find the free player.

WAIT FOR THE DEFENDER TO COMMIT: the correct moment to pass in a 2v1 is when the defender has committed to one player. If they are still between both players, dribble forward to force them to make a decision.
DRIBBLE TOWARD THE DEFENDER TO DRAW THEM: Player A should dribble directly at the defender to force a decision. If A stops or slows down, the defender does not need to choose.
THE PASS GOES TO WHERE THE FREE PLAYER IS GOING: the pass should lead the free player into the space they are running into, not to where they currently are.
PLAYER B MUST STAY WIDE: if both attackers are in the same area, the defender can cover both. Player B must maintain their wide position to keep the 2v1 genuine.

THIS IS THE MOST BASIC OVERLOAD IN FOOTBALL: the 2v1 is the foundation of all attacking overloads. Teams create 2v1s constantly through movement, overlaps, and combination play. Recognising and exploiting them is one of the most important attacking skills.
CELEBRATE THE CORRECT DECISION: even if the shot misses after a correct 2v1 decision, celebrate the decision. The decision was right. The finishing can be improved separately.

CREATE 2V1S FOR YOUR TEAMMATES: the most valuable player in an overload situation is the one who creates the overload through their movement — the player who makes the run to support that creates the 2v1 in the first place.
commonMistakes: Both attackers moving to the same side, allowing the defender to cover both
Player A not dribbling forward to force the defender to commit
Player B not staying wide, reducing the width of the 2v1
Pass going to where Player B is rather than where they are running into
Not recognising when the 2v1 exists and treating it as a 1v1
corrections: Both to same side: require A and B to stay on opposite sides of the cone that marks the centre of the zone
A not moving forward: place a progress cone 5m from A starting position. A must reach this cone before passing
B not staying wide: place a wide channel marker and require B to stay in the wide channel at all times
Pass to feet not space: draw an X on the ground 3m ahead of where B typically receives. The pass must reach or go past the X
Not recognising the 2v1: before every rep, coach asks Is this a 2v1? How do you know? Building the awareness through questioning
progressions: Add a third attacker (3v1) in Phase 3 and ask players which situation (2v1 or 3v1) is easier to exploit and why
Full small game where goals can only be scored after a 2v1 combination — forces teams to create and exploit overloads
Increase the zone length to 20m to give the 2v1 more time to develop before the mini-goal
regressions: Reduce to a completely passive defender who holds a fixed position throughout all three phases while the decision-making is established
Walk through the two decision scenarios at slow motion with the coach playing Player D before any live practice
Allow A to choose their starting side (left or right) to give them more control in the early stages of learning
matchApplication: The 2v1 is the most common and most fundamental attacking overload in football. It appears dozens of times in every match — through overlaps, combination play, counter-attacks, and transition moments. Teams and players who reliably exploit 2v1 advantages score more goals, create more chances, and are significantly more difficult to defend against than those who do not recognise and use the advantage. Teaching this concept at U11 — through clear instruction on the two decisions, progressive practice, and many repetitions — builds one of the most valuable attacking habits in football.
coachTips: Coach the two decisions explicitly and by name before any live practice. Decision 1 and Decision 2 should be the shared language of the session.
Use the question Is the defender coming or staying? as a constant coaching prompt in Phase 1 and 2 to build the reading habit.
Celebrate correct decisions loudly and specifically. Whether it results in a goal is secondary.
Connect to specific match situations: discuss a moment from your teams last match where a 2v1 existed and ask what the correct decision would have been.
Once this concept is established, reference it whenever a 2v1 appears in any session or match. DECISION 1 or DECISION 2 becomes a shared coaching language.
diagramDescription: 15x10m zone with mini-goal. Player A (circle) shown with ball. Player B (circle) shown wide right. Player D (triangle) shown centrally between A and B. TWO DECISION SCENARIOS shown: DECISION 1 — D steps toward A (press arrow), A pass arrow to wide B, B attack arrow toward mini-goal. DECISION 2 — D holds central (no movement), A dribble arrow forward and past D, or pass arrow to B who has moved inside. WIDE CHANNEL marker shown with B staying in wide position. Phase 3 shown as fully live with D able to tackle.

title: Combination Play — Third Man and Overlaps
objective: Develop advanced two and three-player combination patterns used in the final third to create shooting opportunities through coordinated movement and passing.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute the third-man combination at match speed with and without opposition
Players develop the overlap as an automatic attacking combination with a wide player
Players understand when to use a combination versus when to play direct
Teams develop 2-3 reliable combination sequences that every player knows
organisation: Full-size goal with GK. Three specific combination patterns are practiced in sequence, each for 7 minutes. Players rotate through different roles in each pattern. Defenders added progressively.
instructions: COMBINATION 1 — THIRD MAN RUN (7 minutes): Player A passes to Player B. Player C makes a run in behind as the third man. Player B plays first touch to Player C in behind. Player C finishes. After 5 minutes: add a passive defender marking Player C.

COMBINATION 2 — OVERLAP (7 minutes): Player A has the ball wide. Player B is inside. Player A passes to B and overlaps around the outside. Player B draws the defender and plays into Player A overlapping. Player A crosses or finishes. After 5 minutes: add a passive wide defender.

COMBINATION 3 — DOUBLE OVERLAP AND CUTBACK (8 minutes): Player A attacks wide. Player B overlaps as the first option. Player C runs beyond B as the second overlap (double overlap). Player A plays to whichever runner is free. The runner crosses or cuts back. Central player finishes the cutback.
Throughout: GK is fully live. Defenders added progressively from passive to semi-active.
coachingPoints: THE THIRD MAN IS ALREADY MOVING WHEN THE FIRST PASS IS PLAYED: the C player in Combination 1 must begin their run when A plays to B, not after B receives. By the time B controls, C must already be in position to receive.

THE OVERLAP ONLY WORKS IF THE DEFENDER TRACKS THE OVERLAPPER: the overlap creates space because the defender must decide between following the overlap or staying with the ball. If the defender does not track the overlap, the overlapper is free. If they do, the ball-carrier is free.

PLAYER B IN THE OVERLAP MUST HOLD UNTIL THE OVERRUN IS COMPLETE: the most common overlap error is Player B passing too early before the overlap is complete. Wait until Player A has overlapped fully before playing the pass.

THE DOUBLE OVERLAP EXPLOITS THE EXPECTED SINGLE OVERLAP: the single overlap has become familiar to defenders. The second overlapping runner (Player C) arrives as the defender tracks the first overlapper, creating a second free option that was not expected.

THE CUTBACK IS THE MOST DANGEROUS DELIVERY FROM A WIDE POSITION: a ball cut back from the byline to a central runner at the penalty spot is one of the statistically highest-quality chances in football. Prioritise the cutback finish over a driven cross.

COMBINATIONS MUST HAVE A CLEAR TRIGGER AND TIMING: every player in the combination must know exactly when to start their movement. The trigger is the first pass in each combination — when A plays to B, C starts moving. Every time. Without exception.

FINISH EVERY COMBINATION CLINICALLY: the whole purpose of the combination is to create a high-quality chance. A technically perfect combination followed by a poor finish wastes the investment. Finish with composure.

VARY WHICH COMBINATION IS USED: a team that always uses the same combination becomes predictable. Varying between the third man, the overlap, and the double overlap keeps defences uncertain about which is coming.
commonMistakes: Third man starting the run too late (after B receives rather than when A passes to B)
Player B in the overlap passing before the overlap is fully completed
Double overlap player C starting too early and arriving at the same time as B rather than later
No cutback option — all deliveries are crosses rather than cutbacks
Combinations practiced against no opposition and never tested with defensive pressure
corrections: Late third man run: use the trigger language explicitly. When A touches the ball to pass to B, count ONE. C must be running by TWO. Any run that starts after TWO is too late.
Passing before overlap complete: place a progress cone showing where Player A must reach before Player B can pass. The pass cannot be played until Player A passes the cone.
C starting too early: add a 2-second delay between A passing to B and C starting their run, ensuring the timing gap between B and C is maintained
No cutbacks: add a cutback bonus point specifically. Any finish from a cutback earns 2 points regardless of whether it goes in
No defensive pressure: immediately progress to passive defenders after the first 3 reps of each combination
progressions: Full semi-live 3v2 using all three combinations in sequence: attackers choose which combination based on the defensive shape
Match scenario: the team must use all three combinations in a single 10-minute period with a defender tracking each possible option
Defensive challenge: defenders specifically try to anticipate and intercept the most common combination, forcing attackers to vary their choice
regressions: Walk through each combination at slow motion with no opposition for the full first 7-minute phase before adding even passive defenders
Allow unlimited time for each combination sequence to develop in Phase 1 before introducing time pressure
Remove Combination 3 (double overlap) for initial sessions and establish Combinations 1 and 2 first
matchApplication: Advanced combination play in the final third is what separates technically good teams from tactically sophisticated ones. The third man combination, the overlap, and the double overlap are used consistently by elite attacking teams precisely because they create high-quality opportunities that isolated individual play cannot. All three combinations share the same underlying principle: two or more players coordinate their movement to force a defender to make an impossible choice, creating a free player in a dangerous position. Teaching these patterns at U15, when players have the technical foundation to execute them at match speed, gives teams attacking weapons that remain effective at every higher level.
coachTips: Walk through the trigger language explicitly before any live practice. FIRST PASS IS THE TRIGGER must become the automatic frame for all three combinations.
The cutback bonus point is worth implementing permanently in any session involving wide play. It shifts the cultural preference from crosses to the more dangerous cutback.
Film all three combinations from behind the goal during Phase 2 — the quality of the third man run, the overlap timing, and the cutback opportunity are all clearest from this angle.
Connect to professional examples: show footage of all three combinations used by the same team in the same match to illustrate how variety keeps defences uncertain.
Once all three combinations are established, use COMBINATION 1, COMBINATION 2, or COMBINATION 3 as shorthand tactical instructions in any match or training session.
diagramDescription: Final third with GK. THREE COMBINATION DIAGRAMS shown side by side. COMBINATION 1 (Third Man): A to B pass, C run in behind arrow starting at moment of A-B pass, B first-touch pass to C in behind, C finishes. COMBINATION 2 (Overlap): A wide with ball, pass to B inside, A overlap arrow around outside, B drawing defender arrow, pass to overlapping A, A crosses or finishes. COMBINATION 3 (Double Overlap): A wide, B first overlap arrow, C second overlap arrow arriving after B, A choosing between B or C based on which is free, cutback delivery shown. Cutback bonus point shown.

title: Attacking Transition — Win It and Score
objective: Develop the complete counter-attacking sequence from winning the ball to scoring, combining the physical intensity of the press with the clinical quality of the finish.
learningOutcomes: Players execute the full counter-attack sequence from ball-winning to finish at match speed
Players develop the specific physical and mental intensity of the transition from defence to attack
Players build the clinical finishing required to convert counter-attack opportunities
Teams rehearse specific counter-attack patterns from different areas of the pitch
organisation: Full pitch. Team A defends in a compact shape and executes counter-attacks when they win the ball. Team B attacks and attempts to score. The scoring system heavily rewards successful counter-attacks to make them the primary objective.
instructions: SETUP: Team A in a compact mid-block shape. Team B attacks with 6 players.

COUNTER-ATTACK SEQUENCE: Team A wins the ball. Designated striker (Player 9) has already started their run. Ball travels to Player 9 within 2 touches from the ball-winner. Player 9 attacks the GK 1v1 or combines with a wide player who has made a supporting run.
SCORING: Counter-attack goal within 6 seconds and 4 touches: 5 points. Counter-attack goal in 5-8 touches: 3 points. Any other goal: 1 point. Team B goal: 2 points.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): Team B attacks at 70 percent. Team A rehearses the counter-attack sequence with emphasis on Player 9 starting the run before the ball is won.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): full intensity. Team B attacks at 100 percent. Team A must win the ball and execute the counter under real pressure.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): specific scenario practice. Coach places the ball in 3 specific areas (defensive third, midfield, attacking third) and Team A practices the counter-attack from each position.
coachingPoints: PLAYER 9 RUNS BEFORE THE BALL IS WON: the striker must anticipate the ball win and begin their run 0.5-1 second before possession changes. This head start is the difference between a counter-attack chance and a recovered defensive situation.

2-TOUCH MAXIMUM FROM THE BALL-WINNER: the ball must leave the ball-winner within 2 touches. More touches and the counter-attack window closes. The ball-winner identifies their target while the ball is still in flight toward them.

LOOK FOR THE PASS OVER THE TOP FIRST: the most dangerous counter-attack delivery is a pass in behind the defensive line to a running striker. Look for this option first before considering alternatives.

WIDE PLAYERS SPRINT THE FULL WIDTH: when the counter is triggered, wide players must sprint immediately into wide channels even if they do not receive the ball. Their run occupies a defender and creates space for the central counter-attack.

FINISH AT THE FIRST OPPORTUNITY: in a counter-attack, time is the only resource. The longer the counter-attack takes, the more defenders have recovered. Shoot or cross at the first genuine opportunity.

THE COUNTER-ATTACK MINDSET: in the phase before winning the ball, every player in Team A is already thinking about what they will do when possession is won. The mental transition to attack happens before the physical one.

5 POINTS IS THE TARGET: the 5-point bonus for a counter within 6 seconds and 4 touches should be the primary objective. Every player should be aware of the counter-attack clock every time Team A wins the ball.

PRACTICE MAKES THE COUNTER AUTOMATIC: rehearsed counter-attack patterns that every player knows require no decisions under pressure. The sequence runs automatically because it has been practiced until it is instinctive.
commonMistakes: Player 9 starting their run after the ball is won rather than before it
Ball-winner taking more than 2 touches before releasing
Wide players not sprinting into channels when the counter is triggered
Finishing opportunities missed because the counter-attack is not completed at the first genuine chance
No counter-attack awareness — Team A simply builds from the back as normal after winning the ball
corrections: Late Player 9 run: add a visual trigger. When Team B plays a ball back toward their own GK, Player 9 immediately starts their run — this back pass is the trigger that a ball win is imminent
More than 2 touches: strictly enforce the 2-touch maximum with immediate possession reset on violation
Wide players not running: designate the wide players in Phase 1 and call their names the moment the ball is won as a specific trigger for their run
Missing first opportunity: add a 6-second shot clock that begins the moment Team A wins the ball. All 5-point opportunities must be taken within this window
No counter-attack awareness: before Phase 2, ask every player to state their specific role in the counter-attack sequence. No player can begin Phase 2 without knowing their role
progressions: Reduce the counter-attack window to 4 seconds and 3 touches to create maximum urgency
Add a GK who comes off their line aggressively on every counter-attack, testing the finishing composure under a challenging GK
Combine with defensive pressing: Team A must press for 30 seconds before the counter opportunity occurs, adding the physical fatigue of pressing to the counter-attack execution
regressions: Use a slow-walking Team B in Phase 1 to give Team A maximum time to rehearse the counter sequence
Allow Player 9 unlimited run-up time before the counter begins while the timing habit is established
Reduce to Phase 1 only for the first session, walking through the complete counter sequence without any defensive pressure
matchApplication: The counter-attack is one of the most effective and efficient attacking strategies in football, used by elite teams including Real Madrid, Atletico Madrid, and Liverpool as a primary source of goal-scoring opportunities. The specific qualities required — anticipating the ball win, releasing quickly, clinical finishing — are all trainable and improve rapidly with specific rehearsal. At U17, players are physically and tactically ready for the full demands of a rehearsed counter-attacking system, and the 5-point scoring system creates the competitive intensity that makes this drill replicate the urgency of real match counter-attacks.
coachTips: The Player 9 run before the ball is won is the most important coaching point and the one that makes the biggest difference. Enforce it from the very first rep.
The 5-point scoring system is the session engine. Keep the score visible and updated after every counter-attack attempt.
Film Phase 2 from the side. The timing of the Player 9 run relative to the ball win is clearly visible from the side.
Connect to specific match situations: discuss a match where your team won the ball in a dangerous position but did not counter-attack quickly enough. What happened?
Once the pattern is established, the counter-attack sequence should become a non-negotiable tactical principle that carries into every match.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Team A shown in compact mid-block. Team B attacking with 6 players. COUNTER-ATTACK MOMENT shown with a star. Player 9 shown already running (run started BEFORE ball is won — pre-run arrow). Ball-winner shown with 2-touch maximum indicator. Pass arrow going in behind to the running Player 9. Wide players shown sprinting into channels simultaneously (sprint arrows). Player 9 shown 1v1 with GK finishing. 5-POINT SCORING shown prominently: within 6 seconds and 4 touches. Counter-attack clock shown.

title: Penalty Kick Fundamentals
objective: Introduce the penalty kick to young players through a fun, progressive format that builds technique, confidence, and a simple pre-kick routine.
learningOutcomes: Players can place the ball correctly on the penalty spot and approach it consistently
Players develop a simple, repeatable penalty technique (placement over power)
Players build the mental composure to approach and take a penalty confidently
Players experience a penalty shoot-out format in a fun, positive environment
organisation: Simple, progressive penalty practice. Each player takes 3 penalties from the spot. GK stands on the line. Focus is purely on technique and fun — no pressure, pure enjoyment.
instructions: PENALTY TECHNIQUE for U9: approach from 3 steps behind the ball at a slight angle. Non-kicking foot placed beside the ball. Strike through the ball with the inside of the foot. Aim for a corner. Do not try to kick it hard — place it.
SIMPLE PRE-KICK ROUTINE: decide the corner before you start the run-up. Do not change your mind. Take a breath. Look at the corner. Run up. Kick.
PHASE 1 (3 minutes): each player takes 1 penalty against a stationary GK (GK stands in the centre and does not dive). Focus purely on the technique and the corner placement.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): GK can now move and try to save. Each player takes 1 penalty.
PHASE 3 (3 minutes): mini shoot-out. Teams of 3 take penalties alternately. First team to 2 saves wins the round.
After every penalty — scored or missed — celebrate the attempt and give one specific technical note.
coachingPoints: DECIDE THE CORNER BEFORE YOU WALK UP: once you decide which corner you are aiming for, do not change your mind. Changing your mind at the last moment is the most common reason for a missed penalty.
PLACEMENT BEATS POWER: a penalty placed accurately to the corner at medium pace is more likely to score than a powerful kick aimed generally at the goal. You do not need to kick hard.
THE INSIDE OF THE FOOT IS YOUR FRIEND: the inside of the foot gives you the most control and the most accuracy. Use it for every penalty until you are very confident and experienced.
3 STEPS AND A SLIGHT ANGLE: a simple consistent approach of 3 steps at a slight angle gives you the best body position to strike the ball accurately with the inside of the foot.
BREATHE BEFORE YOU RUN: one breath before the run-up helps the body relax and the foot move naturally. Tension in the body causes tension in the foot which causes poor contact.
THE GK CANNOT SAVE A WELL-PLACED PENALTY: a penalty placed accurately in the bottom corner at medium pace is almost impossible for any GK at any level to save. Trust the placement.
CELEBRATE EVERY PENALTY REGARDLESS OF OUTCOME: every player who walks up and takes a penalty at any level is doing something that requires courage. Celebrate every attempt.
THIS IS HOW PROFESSIONAL PLAYERS PRACTICE: every professional footballer practices penalties regularly. The players who are best at them are the ones who have practiced the routine the most.
commonMistakes: Changing the chosen corner during the run-up
Using maximum power rather than placing the ball
Rushing the run-up without a breath or composure moment
Not deciding a corner before beginning the approach
Becoming very upset after a missed penalty
corrections: Changing corner: require players to whisper their chosen corner to the coach before walking up. Any shot not to that corner is retaken
Too much power: require every penalty to arrive at the GK at or below waist height (no full-power shots over the bar)
Rushing: add a mandatory 2-second pause before the run-up begins after placing the ball
No corner decision: ask every player which corner they chose after every penalty — if they cannot say, they had not decided
Upset after miss: immediately and warmly remind the player that every professional misses penalties and ask them to walk back with their head up
progressions: Progressive shoot-out: add a consequence for the losing team (10 star jumps) to build the pressure element
Introduce a run-up variation: some players approach from 4 steps, some from 2. Find what works best for each individual
Add specific GK coaching: brief the GK on when to dive and when to wait, creating a genuine save challenge
regressions: Allow players to stand only 5m from the goal rather than the full penalty spot distance
Use a wider goal for Phase 1 before narrowing to the standard goal
Allow 2 attempts before the GK can move — builds confidence in the technique before adding the GK challenge
matchApplication: Introducing penalties at U9 as a fun, skill-building exercise rather than a high-stakes competition creates positive associations with one of the most psychologically demanding situations in football. Players who develop a consistent, simple penalty routine early in their development carry that routine throughout their career, and the composure habits built through repeated positive practice in a supportive environment are exactly what allows players to perform in real penalty situations later. The mini shoot-out format in Phase 3 introduces the competitive element while keeping the overall atmosphere joyful.
coachTips: Maintain an entirely positive atmosphere throughout. No criticism of missed penalties. Only encouragement and one technical note.
Model the correct technique and routine yourself before any player attempts their first penalty.
The corner-deciding requirement is the single most important habit to build. Enforce it warmly and consistently.
Connect to the players favourite professional player: do they know who is famous for penalties? What is their routine?
End the session with a celebratory shoot-out moment regardless of who scores. The session should end with energy and joy.
diagramDescription: Penalty spot to full-size or junior goal. GK shown on goal line centrally. TECHNIQUE shown: 3-step approach at slight angle, non-kicking foot beside ball, inside-of-foot contact, corner placement arrow to bottom corner. PRE-KICK ROUTINE shown as 4 steps: 1 (place ball), 2 (decide corner — thought bubble showing chosen corner), 3 (breath icon), 4 (run up and kick). Phase 3 mini shoot-out shown with two teams alternating and a score tracker.

title: Set Pieces for Adults — Dead Ball Strategy
objective: Develop a complete adult-level set piece system covering direct free kicks, indirect free kicks, and corners with multiple variations that cannot be easily scouted.
learningOutcomes: Teams develop 2 direct free kick routines for different positions around the box
Teams develop a corner routine with a clear primary and alternative option
Players understand the strategic value of unpredictable set piece variations
GK and defensive unit develop set piece organisation as a counter to opposition set pieces
organisation: Full session dedicated to adult-level set piece strategy. Three areas covered: attacking direct free kicks, attacking corners, and defending corners (linking to the defending set pieces drill). Multiple variations practiced for each.
instructions: SECTION 1 — DIRECT FREE KICKS (8 minutes):

ROUTINE A (central position 22m): one player stands over the ball. Second player makes a dummy run over the ball. Third player shoots after the dummy. The dummy player must be convincing — same approach as a shooter.
ROUTINE B (wide right position 25m): curved ball to the far post. Wall set by two players. Shooter curls around or over the wall. Third player positioned at the far post as a header option.
3 reps of each routine from each position. GK live throughout.
SECTION 2 — CORNERS (10 minutes):
ROUTINE A (direct delivery): near-post flick-on option and far-post arrival established. Two runners plus an edge player.
ROUTINE B (short corner into cross): as established in the corner set piece drill. Used when ROUTINE A is defended well.
SIGNAL SYSTEM: corner taker signals which routine with a hand gesture before approaching.
4 reps each routine from both corners.
SECTION 3 — DEFENDING OPPONENT SET PIECES (7 minutes):
Defensive team sets wall for 3 different free kick positions. GK leads and times the wall organisation. Then defends 4 corners using the zonal and man-marking system.
coachingPoints: SET PIECES ACCOUNT FOR APPROXIMATELY 30 PERCENT OF GOALS AT ALL LEVELS: investing specifically in set piece quality is one of the highest-return tactical investments a team can make. Every goal from a well-executed set piece is a goal that required minimal general play quality to create.

UNPREDICTABILITY IS THE GREATEST SET PIECE WEAPON: a team with 2 reliable routines for each set piece situation is significantly harder to defend than a team with 1. The opposition cannot pre-organise when they cannot predict which routine is coming.

THE SIGNAL SYSTEM IS THE MOST IMPORTANT ELEMENT: all set piece routines require every player to know which routine is being used before the ball is delivered. The signal system — a hand gesture before the approach — is what makes multiple routines possible.

MOVEMENT TIMING IS THE TECHNICAL CHALLENGE: the runners in both free kick and corner routines must start their movement at the correct moment relative to the delivery. Too early and the movement is tracked. At the right moment, the movement cannot be tracked.

THE DUMMY RUN MUST BE CONVINCING: in ROUTINE A for direct free kicks, the dummy player must approach the ball with the same body shape, speed, and intent as a genuine shooter. Any reduction in commitment makes the dummy ineffective.

PRACTICE AT MATCH INTENSITY: set pieces practiced at half intensity produce results at half intensity. Every delivery must be at full match pace and every defensive organisation must be at maximum urgency.

REVIEW YOUR LAST MATCH SET PIECES: before practicing, discuss the last match. How many set piece opportunities did the team have? Which were converted? Which failed and why? This makes the session immediately relevant.

OWN YOUR SET PIECES: a team that owns their set piece routines — that practices them consistently, that knows exactly what each player does in each routine — carries a collective confidence into matches that teams without set piece preparation do not have.
commonMistakes: Signal system not established before the session begins, causing confusion during delivery
Dummy run in ROUTINE A not convincing, making the routine ineffective
Runners in corners starting too early, allowing the defence to track them
Not practicing from both sides (left and right) equally
Set piece defending done at low intensity, not replicating the urgency of match set pieces
corrections: No signal system: establish and practice the signal system separately for 3 minutes before any live set piece practice begins
Weak dummy: require the dummy player to wear a different bib colour from the actual shooter — the evaluating coach watches the dummy and rates their convincingness on a 1-5 scale
Runners starting early: use the trigger rule — runners cannot start movement until the delivery player begins their approach run
Only one side: alternate left and right corners after every 2 reps. Track whether conversion rate differs between sides and address the weaker side
Low defensive intensity: add a consequence for the defending team (5 press-ups per goal conceded) to ensure maximum defensive concentration
progressions: Add a third routine for corners (the near-post run specialist) to create a three-option unpredictability
Introduce a short free kick to a designated target who shoots first time — a fourth free kick option for positions further from goal
Film all set piece attempts and review with the team: what did the defence adjust to? Which routine was hardest to defend?
regressions: Reduce to one routine per set piece situation for the first session before adding the second routine
Use passive defenders for the first 2 reps of each routine to establish the movement timing before adding defensive pressure
Remove Section 3 (defending set pieces) and dedicate the full session to attacking set pieces while patterns are established
matchApplication: At adult level, where set pieces account for a disproportionately high number of goals compared to youth football, a coherent and practiced set piece system is one of the most direct ways to improve match results. Teams that win leagues and cups at grassroots level consistently have better set piece organisation than their opponents — not because their players are more talented but because they have practiced and rehearsed their routines until they are automatic. This session develops that specific quality through multiple variations, clear signal systems, and match-intensity practice.
coachTips: Review the last match set pieces before this session begins. The more directly you connect the session to real match situations, the more engaged players will be.
The signal system explanation is worth 5 minutes of thorough coaching before any live practice. Clarity here makes everything else in the session more effective.
Every delivery must be at match pace and maximum intent. Half-pace set piece practice produces no adaptation for match intensity.
Film the session and review with the team. Seeing their own set piece routines from the perspective of a defending team is immediately instructive.
Establish a set piece review process after every match: how many set pieces did we have? How many did we convert? What do we practice next session?
diagramDescription: Full pitch. SECTION 1 shown: free kick position 22m central. ROUTINE A: dummy player shown running over ball (convincing approach arrow), shooter shown striking after the dummy. ROUTINE B: wide position, curved delivery arc, far-post runner. SIGNAL SYSTEM shown: hand gestures labeled ROUTINE A and ROUTINE B. SECTION 2 shown: corner flag. ROUTINE A (direct) with near-post and far-post runners. ROUTINE B (short corner) with pass-and-cross sequence. SECTION 3 shown: defensive wall set for 3 positions, GK directing.

title: Senior Futsal — Possession Cage
objective: Bring the futsal philosophy to adult players through a high-intensity possession cage game that develops close control, quick thinking, and the joy of small-space football.
learningOutcomes: Adult players rediscover the joy of high-touch football in a small-space competitive format
Players develop the quick feet and decision-making of futsal in a competitive adult context
Players experience how futsal principles of touch and movement improve their general football
Players connect to the global tradition of futsal as the foundation of technical excellence
organisation: 20x15m cage with mini-goals at each end. Two full games running simultaneously. 4v4 or 5v5 with standard futsal rules. The emphasis is on close control, quick feet, and maximum ball involvement for every player.
instructions: Set up the cage with mini-goals at each end.
Standard futsal rules: all boundaries active (no throw-ins — ball played off the walls or restart from the nearest goal if out of play).
TOUCH LIMIT: maximum 3 touches per player. This is reduced to 2 touches after 5 minutes.
MANDATORY INVOLVEMENT RULE: every player on the attacking team must touch the ball before a shot. Encourages team play and maximum individual involvement.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): 3-touch maximum. Players focus on quick control and movement after the pass.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): 2-touch maximum. Decision-making must be faster.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): free play (no touch limit). Players express what they have developed.
Between phases: 60-second discussion about what is working tactically.
coachingPoints: FUTSAL MADE THE GREATEST PLAYERS: Messi, Ronaldo, Neymar, and virtually every technically exceptional footballer grew up playing futsal. The close control, quick feet, and rapid decision-making of futsal are the same qualities that make great footballers at any level.

THE CAGE REMOVES WASTED TIME: in full-size football, a significant portion of time is spent on throw-ins and restarts. In the cage, the ball is almost always in play and almost always involves you. Maximum football, minimum downtime.

QUICK FEET BEAT PACE IN A CAGE: there is no space to accelerate in a 20x15m area. The player who wins in a cage is the one with the quickest feet, not the fastest runner. This levels the playing field.

EVERY PLAYER IS ALWAYS INVOLVED: in full-size football, a winger can go 10 minutes without touching the ball. In the cage, 10 seconds without a touch means something has gone wrong. This is the most important feature of the format.
USE THE WALLS OR BOUNDARIES: if playing in a walled space, use the boundaries as additional passing options. A ball played against the wall and returned is uncatchable for a defender.

CLOSE CONTROL IS THE LUXURY OF THE CAGE: the tight space requires close control. Players who practice in the cage develop the ball-holding ability that allows them to control in tight spaces in full-size matches.

ENJOY IT: adult players who play in the cage almost universally enjoy it more than standard full-size training formats. The touch volume, the constant involvement, and the skill required make it genuinely fun.

THE 3-MINUTE DISCUSSION BETWEEN PHASES IS VALUABLE: ask players what they notice about their own game. What decisions are they making? What is working? Adult players develop faster when they reflect on their own performance.
commonMistakes: Only one or two players dominating the ball rather than distributing to all teammates
Not moving after passing, standing still and waiting for the ball to return
Touch limit causing panicked play rather than quick composed play
Mandatory involvement rule being circumvented by slow movement from other players
Game becoming physical rather than technical due to the tight space
corrections: One player dominating: the mandatory involvement rule enforces distribution — any shot before all players have touched is disallowed and possession resets
Not moving after passing: make movement after every pass a strict rule. A player who stands still after passing cannot receive the ball again in that possession
Panicked play from touch limit: reduce to 4 touches for the first 3 minutes before the standard 3-touch limit is applied
Slow movement from others: the 3-touch rule applies to everyone — slow movement from teammates makes it very hard for the ball-carrier to reach 3 touches, creating natural incentive for movement

Game becoming physical: immediately and firmly apply futsal rules. Any deliberate physical challenge results in a free kick from the offending players goal. Zero tolerance for physical play in a skill environment
progressions: 1-touch only for Phase 3 free play: the most challenging possible format, requiring instant decision-making on every touch
Introduce walls: if an indoor space is available, play with full boundary walls for the complete futsal experience
Senior skills challenge: the player who creates the most successful 1-2 combinations across both phases wins a skills point
regressions: Increase to 4 touches per player for the first session
Increase the cage size to 25x20m to give more space while players adapt to the close control requirements
Remove the mandatory involvement rule for the first session and introduce it once the basic cage format is comfortable
matchApplication: The futsal cage brings adult players back to the fundamental joy of football: touching the ball, making quick decisions, and working closely with teammates in a way that full-size football rarely produces. The touch volumes in a cage session are 3-5 times higher than in standard full-size training, the decision-making demands are higher, and the enjoyment level for most adult players is significantly greater. Adult teams that regularly include cage sessions in their training develop noticeably better close control, quicker passing rhythm, and more confident technical play in tight spaces in full-size matches.
coachTips: The cage format almost always produces genuine enjoyment among adult players. Lean into this — celebrate skill, celebrate quick combinations, celebrate clever use of space.
The between-phase discussion is worth investing in with adult groups. Adults respond well to reflection and tactical conversation.
Film one phase from above. Show the footage to the group between phases. Seeing the pattern of movement and involvement from above is instructive and engaging.
Connect to the Brazilian culture of futsal as a direct explanation for why Brazilian players tend to have superior close control and quick feet.
Once established, the cage session can become a monthly fixture that adult players genuinely look forward to.
diagramDescription: 20x15m cage with mini-goals at each end. Two 4v4 or 5v5 games shown running simultaneously. MANDATORY INVOLVEMENT shown: all 4 or 5 attacking players with touch indicators before a shot is allowed. TOUCH LIMIT shown: 3 touches (Phase 1) then 2 touches (Phase 2). Movement arrows shown from every player after each pass. Wall/boundary pass shown if applicable. Between-phase discussion moment shown with team huddle. 60-second timer between phases.

title: Defending Crosses — Zonal Marking System
objective: Develop a zonal marking system for defending crosses where defenders own areas rather than tracking individual players.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the zonal principle for defending crosses
Players develop the correct zonal position for each delivery type
Players build the alertness to attack the ball in their zone rather than waiting
Teams build a reliable zonal cross-defence system that reduces confusion
organisation: Full penalty area with goal and GK. Wide server delivers crosses. 4 defenders each own a specific zone of the penalty area. Attackers try to find gaps between zones. Defenders must attack the ball in their zone rather than tracking individual runners.
instructions: ZONE SETUP: divide the penalty area into 4 zones. Zone 1 — near post (GK post to 6-yard box). Zone 2 — central near (6-yard box to penalty spot, near side). Zone 3 — central far (penalty spot to far side). Zone 4 — far post area.
Each defender owns one zone throughout the drill.
When a cross is delivered: every defender attacks any ball that enters their zone.
If no ball enters their zone: defenders hold their zonal position and remain alert for the second ball.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): wide server delivers crosses from the right. Passive attackers stand in zones. Defenders practice attacking balls in their zone.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): attackers move freely and try to arrive in zones ahead of the defenders. Defenders must read and arrive first.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): fully live. Attackers and defenders compete for every cross. GK communicates KEEPER or AWAY on every delivery.
coachingPoints: ATTACK THE BALL IN YOUR ZONE: in a zonal system, the defenders job is to attack any ball that enters their zone. They do not track the attacker — they track the ball.
ARRIVE BEFORE THE ATTACKER: the zonal defender must time their movement to arrive at the ball in their zone before the attacker. This requires reading the cross early.
EYES ON THE BALL FROM DELIVERY: the moment the wide player begins their crossing motion, every defender watches the ball — not the attacker in their zone.
THE ZONE IS NOT A FIXED SPOT: the zonal position adjusts slightly based on where the cross is delivered from. A near-post cross pulls Zone 1 closer. A lofted far-post cross shifts Zone 4 back.
GK COMMUNICATION DECIDES WHO TAKES CENTRAL BALLS: any ball in the central zones that the GK can reach is the GKs ball if they call KEEPER. Without a call, the zonal defender attacks.
SECOND BALL ALERTNESS: after any clearance or missed cross, every defender immediately returns to their zone and prepares for the second ball.
COMMUNICATION BETWEEN ZONES: adjacent defenders must communicate constantly about balls that fall between zones. The nearest defender attacks the ball when it falls in a boundary area.

ZONAL DEFENDING REQUIRES CONCENTRATION: the zonal defender who switches off for one delivery and is caught flat-footed is the weakness in the system. Concentration for every single cross is non-negotiable.
commonMistakes: Defenders tracking individual attackers rather than their zone, abandoning the zonal system
Defenders standing flat-footed in their zone rather than attacking balls that enter it
No GK communication, causing confusion between GK and Zone 2 and 3 defenders
Defenders not returning to their zone after dealing with a ball, leaving zones empty for second balls
Zones too rigid — defenders not adjusting position for different cross types and delivery positions
corrections: Tracking individuals: add a rule that any defender who moves more than 2m from their zone boundary loses a team point
Flat-footed: require every defender to make a forward movement toward the ball the instant a cross is struck
No GK communication: make GK KEEPER or AWAY call mandatory. Any delivery without a call is a training error
Not returning to zone: time the return to zone after each ball — all defenders must be back in zone within 3 seconds of the ball being dealt with
Zones too rigid: coach calls the cross position (NEAR or FAR) before delivery in Phase 1 so defenders can adjust their zone position
progressions: Add a second wide server alternating between left and right crosses, requiring the defensive unit to shift zones quickly
Reduce to 4v4 plus GK in a live game where all crosses must be defended zonally
Combine zonal and man-marking: Zones 1 and 4 are zonal, Zones 2 and 3 are man-marked — tests the hybrid system
regressions: Use only one type of cross (low near-post) throughout Phase 1 before varying delivery types
Make attackers passive throughout Phase 1 and Phase 2 while the zonal positions are established
Reduce to 3 zones (remove Zone 4) for the first session to simplify the system
matchApplication: Zonal cross-defending is used by many professional teams as an alternative to man-marking because it gives each defender clear spatial responsibility and removes the problem of being pulled out of position by decoy runners. A well-organised zonal system requires defenders who read the cross early, attack the ball in their area aggressively, and communicate constantly with adjacent defenders about boundary balls.
coachTips: Walk through the four zones physically with cones before any cross is delivered.
The most important coaching moment is when a defender tracks a runner rather than staying in their zone — pause immediately and reset.
Connect to the man-marking system the players already know and explain why the zonal system eliminates the problems of being pulled wide.
Film from behind the goal — the zone coverage and any gaps between zones are clearly visible from this angle.
Run both the zonal and man-marking corner defending drills in the same session so players understand both systems.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with GK. Four zones marked with cones: Zone 1 (near post to 6-yard box), Zone 2 (central near), Zone 3 (central far), Zone 4 (far post). One defender shown in each zone. Wide server delivering from the right. Ball entering Zone 2 — Zone 2 defender shown attacking the ball (forward movement arrow). Zone 1, 3, and 4 defenders shown holding their zone positions. GK shown centrally with KEEPER call. Attackers shown in zones unable to get ahead of the defenders.

title: Counter Attack — 3v2 Speed Game
objective: Develop the execution of a fast 3v2 counter-attack through a rapid-fire drill that maximises repetitions and builds the instinctive decision-making of the counter.
learningOutcomes: Players can identify and execute the correct decision in a 3v2 counter-attack situation
Players develop the pace and directness of the counter-attack transition
Players build the clinical finishing required to convert counter-attack chances
Players experience the joy and intensity of fast, direct attacking play
players: Groups of 5 (3 attackers vs 2 defenders) rotating
organisation: 30m channel from halfway to goal. 3 attackers start at the halfway line. 2 defenders start at the edge of the penalty area. On GO, attackers sprint forward and try to score. Defenders can only move once the attackers pass the first cone (10m from start). Multiple groups rotate every 90 seconds.
instructions: Mark the starting positions: attackers at the halfway point (30m from goal). Defenders at the edge of the penalty area (18m from goal).
On GO: all 3 attackers sprint forward with the ball.
Defenders can only move when the attackers reach the 10m mark (a cone 10m from the start).
The attacking trio has a clear numerical advantage. They must use it within 6 seconds.
KEY DECISION for the central attacker (ball-carrier): if both defenders drop to cover the middle, pass wide immediately. If one defender comes to press, play around them.
WIDE ATTACKERS: sprint into wide positions immediately and stay wide until the ball is played to them. Do not drift central.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): slow counter (attackers jog, defenders walk). Focus on the decision and the wide positioning.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): full pace. Genuine 3v2 at match speed.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): defenders active from the start (no delayed reaction). Full competitive 3v2.
coachingPoints: SPRINT FORWARD IMMEDIATELY: the moment the counter is triggered, all 3 attackers sprint forward without hesitation. Any pause gives the defenders time to organise.
WIDE ATTACKERS OWN THE WIDTH: both wide attackers must sprint to positions outside the two defenders. If they drift central, the counter becomes a 1v2 not a 3v2.
THE CENTRAL ATTACKER READS THE DEFENDERS: the player with the ball reads what the two defenders are doing. Both dropping centrally: pass wide. One pressing: play around them or pass early.
