PLAY FORWARD FIRST: in a counter-attack, the first pass should always be forward if available. A backwards or square pass in a counter-attack wastes the advantage.
FINISH AT THE FIRST OPPORTUNITY: do not over-elaborate. When a clear shooting chance appears, shoot. Counter-attacks that take too many touches give defenders time to recover.
THE PASS TO THE WIDE PLAYER SHOULD LEAD THEM: pass into the space ahead of the wide runner, not to their feet. A well-weighted lead pass allows them to shoot in stride.
COMMUNICATION BETWEEN THE THREE: brief calls (LEFT, RIGHT, SHOOT) between the three attackers make the counter-attack decision collective rather than the central players burden alone.
CELEBRATE THE CLINICAL COUNTER: a counter-attack goal scored in under 4 touches is one of the most satisfying moments in football. Celebrate every one loudly.
commonMistakes: All three attackers converging centrally, removing the width that creates the 3v2 advantage
Central attacker holding the ball too long waiting for a perfect moment rather than playing early
Wide attackers receiving but not shooting — passing back to the centre instead of finishing
No communication between the three attackers about who is free and where the space is
Counter-attacks taking too long and being caught by recovering defenders
corrections: All central: at the start of Phase 1 physically place the wide attackers in wide positions and insist they hold that width for the first 10 reps before allowing free movement
Holding too long: add a 4-second shot clock from the moment attackers cross the 10m mark
Wide attackers not shooting: make the rule that any wide attacker who receives inside the penalty area must shoot first time
No communication: require the central attacker to call LEFT or RIGHT before every pass to build the communication habit
Too slow: add a 6-second counter clock and require the shot to be taken before it expires
progressions: Reduce defender advantage: allow defenders to start at 15m from the goal rather than 18m, making the defensive recovery more realistic
Score system: 3 points for a goal in 3 touches or fewer, 2 points for 4 touches, 1 point for 5 or more
2v1 version: reduce to 2 attackers vs 1 defender for younger or less experienced groups before building to 3v2
regressions: Allow attackers unlimited time with no shot clock during Phase 1
Use passive defenders (cannot tackle, only position) throughout Phase 1
Reduce the channel to 20m to give attackers less distance to cover before the shot
matchApplication: The 3v2 counter-attack drill maximises repetitions of the most common and most effective transition attacking scenario in football. Each 90-second rotation gives every attacking player multiple opportunities to experience the decision-making, wide positioning, and clinical finishing that makes counter-attacks successful. The rotation format also means no player waits more than 90 seconds before their next counter-attack experience, keeping energy and engagement consistently high.
coachTips: The width discipline of the wide attackers is the most important coaching point. Check it on every single rep.
The 4-second shot clock is the most effective tool for building urgency. Use it from Phase 2 onward.
Pair the strongest dribbler as the central attacker in Phase 1 so the wide positioning can be the coaching focus rather than ball control.
Connect to the teams own match situations: discuss a recent match where a counter-attack was available but not taken quickly enough.
This drill works brilliantly as an energetic session opener. 18 minutes of 3v2 counter-attacks at the start of a session activates every player immediately.
diagramDescription: 30m channel. Attackers (3 circles) shown at the starting line. Defenders (2 triangles) shown at the edge of the penalty area. TRIGGER shown with GO and sprint arrows from all 3 attackers. Wide attackers shown sprinting into wide positions (outside the two defenders). Central attacker shown reading the defenders: BOTH DROP (defenders staying central) — pass arrow to wide attacker; ONE PRESSES — pass around the pressing defender. Shot clock shown: 4 seconds from the 10m mark. Goal shown with celebration icon.

title: Young GK — First Steps in Goal
objective: Give young players their very first experience of playing in goal through fun, confidence-building activities that make goalkeeping feel exciting and achievable.
learningOutcomes: Young players experience the goalkeeping position positively for the first time
Players develop the basic stance and ready position through fun activities
Players discover the satisfaction of making a save
Players build the first instinct of stepping forward to meet the ball rather than staying on the line
equipment: Junior goal or cones for a small goal (3m wide)
organisation: Small 3m goal using cones or a junior goal. Players rotate as GK after every 3 shots. Coach delivers gentle, fun shots that give the GK realistic save opportunities. The atmosphere is entirely positive and celebratory.
instructions: Player 1 stands in the 3m goal as the first GK.
Coach stands 5m away and rolls or gently kicks balls toward the goal at easy heights and directions.
STARTING POSITION: GK stands 1 step off the goal line, feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Tell them this is the power position — ready to jump anywhere.
SAVE 1 (2 minutes): coach rolls balls along the ground to each side. GK steps and stops them. Celebrate every save.
SAVE 2 (2 minutes): coach throws balls at hip height to each side. GK steps and catches. Celebrate every catch.
SAVE 3 (2 minutes): coach throws gently overhead at low height. GK jumps and catches above their head. Big celebration for every one.
SHOOTOUT (6 minutes): players take turns shooting from 6m. GK tries to save. After every 3 shots the GK rotates. Count total saves. Celebrate the GK as a hero regardless of score.
Throughout: the coach narrates with energy — WHAT A SAVE, UNBELIEVABLE, YOU ARE A GOALKEEPER!
coachingPoints: THE GK IS A HERO: from the very first session, establish the culture that the goalkeeper is one of the most important and most celebrated positions in the team.
STEP FORWARD TO MEET THE BALL: the most important habit is stepping toward the ball rather than staying glued to the line. Even 1 step forward makes the goal feel smaller to the shooter.
WATCH THE BALL ALL THE WAY INTO YOUR HANDS: keep your eyes on the ball every moment from when it is struck until it is in your hands.
THE POWER POSITION IS YOUR HOME: every time a new shot is about to be taken, reset to the power position — feet wide, knees bent, hands up at waist height, weight on balls of feet.
BRAVE IS BRILLIANT: stopping a ball with your hands, your feet, or any part of your body is brave and brilliant. There is no wrong way to make a save.
BOTH SIDES EQUALLY: practice stepping left and practice stepping right in equal amounts. A GK who can only go to one side is easier to beat.
EVERY SAVE IS AN ACHIEVEMENT: it does not matter whether it goes in or not. A GK who dives for a ball they cannot reach is showing courage and that is more important than the result.
GOALKEEPERS ARE DIFFERENT AND SPECIAL: explain to young players that the GK is the only player who can use their hands, which makes them the most unique and special player on the pitch.
commonMistakes: GK standing completely still on the goal line and not stepping to meet any ball
Closing eyes before the ball arrives, making saves very difficult
GK becoming upset or embarrassed when balls go in
Only moving to one side (dominant side) for every save
Not returning to the power position between shots
corrections: Standing on line: place a cone 1m in front of the GK. They must step past the cone to meet every ball
Closing eyes: ask the GK to call out the colour of the ball or a number you hold up just before it arrives — forces eyes open
Upset when ball goes in: immediately and warmly celebrate the attempt with equal enthusiasm to a save. Make going in irrelevant.
Only one side: alternate which side you roll the ball to and gently say OTHER SIDE when the GK starts toward the wrong side
Not resetting: call POWER POSITION before every shot so the GK has a verbal cue to reset
progressions: Increase shot pace gradually as the GK becomes more confident
Add a second player as a passive striker who approaches the goal before shooting — closer to a real match situation
Introduce a small side game with the GK as the real goalkeeper in their team
regressions: Use a balloon or beach ball for the very first session if any player is nervous about a football hitting them
Stand only 3m away for the first 2 minutes before stepping back to 5m
Use only rolling ground balls for the entire first session before adding aerial balls
matchApplication: First experiences in goal are enormously influential on whether a young player develops a love for goalkeeping or avoids the position. A positive, exciting, celebratory first GK session where every save is treated as a spectacular achievement creates the enthusiasm that produces dedicated young goalkeepers. The habits established here — the power position, the forward step, the eyes-on-ball — are the same foundations that professional GKs use in every match.
coachTips: Your performance as the enthusiastic narrator is the most important coaching tool in this session. Be loud, be positive, be dramatic about every save.
Rotate every player through the GK position. Some of your best future GKs may not know it yet.
Never allow any negative comments about the GK from other players. The GK role must be celebrated and respected from the very first session.
Take a photo of every player in the GK position during the shootout phase. Share it with parents afterward.
End every session by asking the GK of the final round: how did it feel to be the goalkeeper? Build the positive identity around the position.
diagramDescription: Small 3m cone goal. Young GK shown in power position: 1 step off line, feet wide, knees bent, hands at waist. Coach shown 5m away in three positions: rolling ground ball, throwing hip-height ball, throwing overhead ball. STEP FORWARD arrow shown from GK toward each ball type. CELEBRATE icon shown after every save. Rotation arrow showing next player becoming GK after 3 shots. Hero celebration shown around the GK position.

title: GK for Beginners — Catching and Claiming
objective: Develop the basic catching technique and the habit of claiming the ball confidently at U9.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the W-grip catching technique for balls at different heights
Players build the habit of claiming the ball by moving toward it and calling KEEPER
Players develop confidence in handling the ball under gentle pressure
Players connect GK technique to the specific match situations they will face
players: 1-2 GKs plus a coach and 2-3 servers
organisation: Junior or full-size goal. Coach and servers deliver balls at different heights. GK practices the catching technique for each height and develops the calling habit.
instructions: THE W-GRIP EXPLAINED: when catching a ball at chest height or above, the thumbs point toward each other behind the ball forming a W shape. The fingers spread wide. This creates the most secure grip.
THE SCOOP GRIP: when catching a ball below the waist, the fingers point downward and the hands scoop under the ball.
EXERCISE 1 — CHEST HEIGHT CATCHING (4 minutes): coach throws chest-high balls from 4m. GK catches with the W-grip, pulls the ball into the chest, and calls MINE after every catch.
EXERCISE 2 — LOW CATCHING (4 minutes): coach rolls or throws low balls. GK bends knees, gets low, and catches with the scoop grip. Body gets behind the ball as a second barrier.
EXERCISE 3 — VARIED HEIGHT (4 minutes): coach varies the height of each throw without warning. GK must read the height and select the correct grip automatically.
EXERCISE 4 — CALLING (3 minutes): coach throws balls into the goal area while 2 passive players stand nearby. GK must call KEEPER loudly before coming to claim every ball.
Celebrate every clean catch and every confident KEEPER call equally.
coachingPoints: THE W-GRIP GIVES THE SECUREST CATCH: the thumbs behind the ball in a W shape create a cage around the ball. If the ball hits the palm with no W, it can bounce out. The W grips it.
BODY BEHIND THE BALL: for every low ball, get the body directly behind the ball as a second barrier. If the hands miss, the body stops it.
STEP TOWARD THE BALL: do not wait for the ball to arrive at your hands. Step toward it. This gives you momentum into the catch and reduces the chance of the ball bouncing out.
CALL KEEPER LOUDLY: the KEEPER call tells everyone around you that you are coming for the ball. It must be loud enough to be heard over noise. Practice at full volume every time.
SOFT HANDS ON IMPACT: the hands cushion the ball slightly on impact by giving very slightly backward at the moment of contact. This absorbs the pace and keeps the ball secure.
BOTH HANDS ALWAYS: catching with one hand is a dangerous habit. Both hands must always be used for every catch.
WATCH THE BALL INTO YOUR HANDS: follow the ball all the way until it is in your hands. Looking away a split second early is a common cause of dropped catches.
HOLD IT TIGHT AND THEN DISTRIBUTE: after every catch, hold the ball tight for 1 second before distributing. This builds the habit of securing possession before releasing it.
commonMistakes: One-handed catching rather than using both hands for every ball
Not getting the body behind low balls, relying only on the hands
Not calling KEEPER, leading to confusion with nearby players
The ball bouncing out of the hands because the fingers are not spread wide enough
Not stepping toward the ball, letting it arrive at stationary hands
corrections: One hand: put a sticker on both hands. Both stickers must touch the ball on every catch
No body behind low ball: place a cone behind the GK at the same position as their feet. They must move the cone with their foot to get it behind the ball
Not calling: make the catch invalid unless it is preceded by a KEEPER call. No call means the rep does not count
Ball bouncing out: practice the W-grip against a wall — press both hands flat against the wall and slide the thumbs toward each other to feel the W position
Not stepping to ball: throw the ball 1m to the side so the GK must step toward it — cannot be caught from a stationary position
progressions: Add gentle competitive pressure: a passive player stands next to the GK as competition for high balls, requiring the GK to call and move with more urgency
Vary the pace of the throws — some very gentle, some slightly harder — building the soft-hands absorption habit
Combine with the young GK footwork session: after every catch the GK distributes to a target, linking catching and distribution
regressions: Use a beach ball or foam ball for Exercise 1 for players who are nervous about the grip with a harder ball
Stand only 2m away for all exercises in the first session
Remove the passive players from Exercise 4 until the KEEPER call is established confidently
matchApplication: Catching technique is the most fundamental GK skill because a ball that is caught is possession secured. The W-grip, the body-behind-the-ball habit, and the KEEPER call are the three technical foundations of all GK catching work. Establishing these correctly at U9, in a supportive and celebratory environment, creates the technical base that all future GK development builds on.
coachTips: Demonstrate the W-grip explicitly and let every player feel it with their own hands before any exercise begins.
The KEEPER call is as important as any technical element. A GK who calls confidently commands their area. Build this from the very first session.
Celebrate confident calling as loudly as clean catches.
Allow every outfield player to spend 5 minutes in goal during this session — building appreciation for the position across the squad.
Connect to professional GKs the players admire: their catching technique, their commanding of the area, their KEEPER calls.
diagramDescription: Goal area. GK shown in three catching positions: W-GRIP (ball at chest height, thumbs pointing toward each other behind ball, fingers spread — W shape labeled), SCOOP (ball below waist, fingers pointing down, scooping under ball, body behind it), VARIED HEIGHT (GK reading incoming ball at eye level and selecting grip). KEEPER CALL shown with speech bubble and volume indicator. Step-toward-ball arrow shown on every catch. Body-behind-ball indicator shown for low catches. W-grip close-up diagram shown.

title: Fun Fitness — Games That Build Fitness
objective: Develop fundamental movement skills and physical literacy through enjoyable games that build coordination, agility, and physical confidence without any structured fitness training.
learningOutcomes: Players develop basic coordination through fun movement games
Players build physical confidence and enjoyment of movement
Players develop fundamental movement skills: running, jumping, turning, balancing
Players experience physical activity as joyful rather than effortful
organisation: Pure fun physical games. No structured fitness. No counting. No effort targets. Just games that happen to develop coordination, agility, and movement quality while feeling like play.
instructions: GAME 1 — STUCK IN THE MUD (4 minutes): 2 players are IT and try to tag others. If tagged, you freeze in a star shape with legs apart and arms out. A free player crawls through your legs to unfreeze you. Coach joins in as IT occasionally.

GAME 2 — TAIL TAG (4 minutes): every player tucks a bib into the back of their shorts as a tail. Players try to steal each others tails while protecting their own. Last player with their tail wins each round.

GAME 3 — TRAFFIC LIGHTS (4 minutes): coach calls RED (freeze), AMBER (jog on the spot), GREEN (sprint freely around the area). Variations: BLUE (jump), YELLOW (balance on one leg). Increase the speed of the calls.
GAME 4 — COPY CAT (3 minutes): players pair up. Player A moves freely around the area performing any movement. Player B copies exactly. Swap after 90 seconds. The more creative the better.
Coach energy and enthusiasm is the engine of every game.
coachingPoints: MOVEMENT IS JOY: at U8 the most important outcome of any physical session is that players leave wanting to come back and do it again. If they enjoyed moving today, they will move more tomorrow.

EVERY GAME DEVELOPS REAL SKILLS: Stuck in the Mud develops agility and spatial awareness. Tail Tag develops quickness and direction changes. Traffic Lights develops reaction speed. Copy Cat develops coordination and creativity. These are real physical skills developed through play.
RUN IN ALL DIRECTIONS: most children naturally run forward. These games require running backward, sideways, and in curves. This develops the full range of movement patterns.
BALANCE AND CONTROL: stopping quickly (RED) and balancing on one leg (YELLOW) develop the body control that underpins all athletic movement.
COMPETE JOYFULLY: the games have a competitive element but it should always feel light and fun. Winning and losing are part of the experience but never the point.

PHYSICAL CONFIDENCE COMES FROM POSITIVE EXPERIENCES: children who have positive physical experiences become more physically active adults. Every fun physical session at U8 is an investment in a healthy future.
JOIN IN: the coach who plays the games with the children creates the best physical environment. Your participation gives permission for full commitment.

THE COOLDOWN IS IMPORTANT: after fast movement games, 2 minutes of slow walking and gentle shaking of the arms and legs helps the heart rate return to normal and teaches children to listen to their body.
commonMistakes: Players becoming too competitive and the games losing their joyful quality
One or two dominant players winning every round and others becoming discouraged
Players not using the full area, clustering in one part
Physical activity level too low because games become too structured or slow
Players copying the same movements in Copy Cat rather than being creative
corrections: Too competitive: remove scores and winners entirely for one round and focus purely on the enjoyment of the movement
Dominant players: pair dominant and less dominant players together in Tail Tag so the competition is balanced
Not using full area: mark the boundaries clearly and add a rule that players cannot stand still in one spot for more than 5 seconds
Low activity: increase the energy of the coach. Your energy level sets the group energy level. Be louder and more enthusiastic.
No creativity in Copy Cat: demonstrate creative movements yourself — rolling, spinning, hopping, skipping — to show the range of what is possible
progressions: Add a ball to every game: Tail Tag with players dribbling a ball while protecting their tail
Create player-invented games: after the 4 games, give players 3 minutes to invent their own physical game with whatever is available
Increase the difficulty of Traffic Lights: add 8 colours each with a different movement
regressions: Slow the pace of Traffic Lights to give players more time to respond to each colour
Reduce Tail Tag to 3v3 in a small area to make the competition simpler
Allow Copy Cat to use only one movement at a time rather than continuous flowing movement
matchApplication: Physical literacy — the ability to move with competence and confidence in a variety of physical situations — is one of the most important developmental outcomes of early sports participation. The games in this session develop the fundamental movement skills (FMS) that research consistently identifies as the foundation of lifelong physical activity and sports performance. Children who develop good FMS at U8 participate more in sports, perform better across multiple sports, and maintain higher physical activity levels throughout their lives.
coachTips: Your own enjoyment of the games is the most important coaching input. Children who see their coach genuinely having fun move more freely and with more confidence.
Run these games at the start of every session for U8 players. 15 minutes of fun movement before any football-specific work raises the quality of everything that follows.
Connect the games to football: after Tail Tag ask players what made them good at protecting their tail. The answers describe the same skills used in dribbling.
Take photos of the games and share with parents. Seeing children genuinely enjoying movement is enormously positive for family engagement.
This session pairs with the Streets Meet Tiki-Taka free play sessions as the bookends of a U8 training programme focused on joy, movement, and love of the game.
diagramDescription: Open area with players spread out. GAME 1 (Stuck in Mud): IT player shown chasing, frozen player in star shape, rescuing player shown crawling through legs. GAME 2 (Tail Tag): players shown with bibs as tails, chasing and protecting simultaneously. GAME 3 (Traffic Lights): RED (everyone frozen), GREEN (everyone sprinting in different directions), YELLOW (everyone balanced on one leg). GAME 4 (Copy Cat): pairs shown with Leader making a movement and Partner exactly mirroring it. Coach shown participating enthusiastically in each game.

title: Athletic Development for Young Players
objective: Develop basic speed, agility, and coordination in U9 players through fun athletic challenges that build the physical foundations for football performance.
learningOutcomes: Players develop basic sprinting technique through enjoyable racing games
Players build agility and direction-change ability through fun courses
Players experience healthy competition in a positive and encouraging environment
Players develop the physical confidence that comes from improving their own speed
organisation: A series of fun athletic challenges in a large open area. Racing, dodging, and agility games that feel like play but develop genuine speed and movement quality.
instructions: CHALLENGE 1 — STRAIGHT SPRINT RACE (3 minutes): 20m sprint races in pairs. Players line up side by side, coach says GO, first to the cone wins. Run 6 races each, swapping partners each time. Focus: run as fast as you can.
CHALLENGE 2 — OBSTACLE SPRINT (4 minutes): a simple course with 4 cones placed randomly that players must run around (not through). First to finish wins. Race in pairs.
CHALLENGE 3 — REACTION RACE (4 minutes): players start lying face down, sitting, or with their back to the course. On GO they must get up and sprint 10m. Tests reaction time and explosive first step.

CHALLENGE 4 — MIRROR RACE (4 minutes): two players face each other over a line 2m apart. Player A moves left or right randomly. Player B must mirror and stay facing them. On GO from the coach, both sprint 5m to a cone on their right side. Who gets there first?
Between challenges: 30 seconds of celebration and rest. Count who won each race but keep the atmosphere light and fun.
coachingPoints: RUN FAST WHEN YOU RUN FAST: when it is time to race, run at your absolute maximum. Half-speed racing does not make you faster. Full-speed racing does.
LEAN FORWARD WHEN YOU SPRINT: a slight forward lean when sprinting makes you faster because you are falling into your speed. Upright or backward lean slows you down.
ARMS DRIVE YOUR LEGS: when your arms pump forward and backward powerfully, your legs automatically go faster. Try pumping your arms as fast as you can and notice what happens to your feet.
LOOK AHEAD NOT DOWN: always look forward when sprinting. Looking down at your feet slows you and makes direction changes harder.
LOSING IS LEARNING: when you lose a race, something about your running was slightly different from the winner. Watching what they do and trying it next time is how you get faster.
BOTH SIDES EQUALLY: run courses that go both left and right equally. Speed in both directions is what makes a great football player.
YOUR SPEED IS YOUR OWN: do not compare your speed to others. Compare your speed today to your speed last week. Getting faster than yourself is the goal.
CELEBRATE EFFORT AS MUCH AS WINNING: the player who runs their absolute hardest and finishes third deserves as much celebration as the winner. Effort is always within your control.
commonMistakes: Players not sprinting at maximum effort during races
Players who lose becoming upset and disengaging
Only racing against the same opponents rather than mixing up the pairs
Reaction races starting before the GO signal (anticipating)
Direction changes in the obstacle course becoming sloppy as players rush
corrections: Not sprinting hard: pair players and ask them to sprint together rather than race first, establishing the sensation of full-speed running before competition
Upset at losing: immediately celebrate the effort of the losing player specifically and warmly before the winner is acknowledged
Same opponents: use a random pairing system (count off 1-2-1-2 before each challenge)
Anticipating GO: vary the delay between ready and GO (1 second, 3 seconds, 5 seconds) so the timing cannot be predicted
Sloppy direction changes: slow the obstacle course to 70 percent and specifically coach the foot placement around cones before full speed
progressions: Add a ball to Challenge 2: players dribble around the obstacle course instead of running freely
Time Challenge 1: record each players best time and compare to next session
Create a combined score: total wins across all 4 challenges and track across sessions
regressions: Reduce Challenge 4 to a simpler version where Player A just points left or right and Player B reacts before adding the race element
Increase the distance between cones in the obstacle course to make direction changes more gradual
Allow players to choose their starting position in Challenge 3 (standing, not lying down) before building to the more challenging starting positions
matchApplication: Basic speed and agility development at U9 sets the physical foundation for football performance across the following decade. Players who are exposed to a variety of running, reacting, and direction-changing challenges at this age develop the neuromuscular patterns that underpin both speed and coordination. The fun, competitive, but low-pressure format of this session ensures maximum effort from every player while maintaining the joyful atmosphere that U9 players need to continue developing their love of physical activity.
coachTips: Your own enthusiasm for the races is essential. Commentate the races dramatically, celebrate winners, and immediately find something to praise in every player.
The mirror race (Challenge 4) is particularly engaging and often produces genuine excitement — lean into the energy it creates.
Keep all four challenges moving quickly. U9 players who wait too long between runs lose enthusiasm.
Connect to football: after Challenge 3 (reaction race), ask players when in a football match they need to react quickly and sprint. Build the transfer to the game.
Track something measurable across sessions — even just counting total race wins — so players can see their progress over time.
diagramDescription: Open area. CHALLENGE 1 shown: two players racing side by side over 20m with finish line. CHALLENGE 2 shown: obstacle course with 4 cones, players weaving around them in a race. CHALLENGE 3 shown: player lying face down, GO signal, explosive get-up and sprint. CHALLENGE 4 shown: mirror race — two players facing each other, A moves left, B mirrors, GO signal, both sprint right to a cone. Celebration icons after each challenge. Lean-forward sprint technique diagram shown.

title: First Experience of Set Pieces — U8 Restarts
objective: Give young players their first positive experience of restart situations through simple and enjoyable practices that teach the basic rules and movements of goal kicks and throw-ins.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the basic rule of when a goal kick or throw-in is awarded
Players can take a basic throw-in with both feet on the ground
Players experience a goal kick restart in a fun, low-pressure format
Players develop the habit of positioning themselves to receive restarts
organisation: Simple restart practice in a fun format. No pressure, no correction overload. One concept per restart type, practiced through enjoyment.
instructions: THROW-IN INTRODUCTION (5 minutes):
Explain the rule: when the ball goes out over the side line, the team that did not touch it last takes a throw-in.
THROW-IN TECHNIQUE for U8: stand behind the line, hold the ball with both hands, bring it behind the head, throw forward. Both feet must stay on the ground.
Practice: players stand 3m apart in pairs and throw-in to each other. Count how many the pair can do in 30 seconds.
Coach checks one thing only: are both feet on the ground?
GOAL KICK INTRODUCTION (7 minutes):
Explain the rule: when the ball goes out over the end line last touched by an attacker, the defending team gets a goal kick.
For U8: the GK (or any player) places the ball anywhere in the 6-yard box and kicks it.
Practice: 5 players spread across the pitch. One player takes the goal kick. The others try to be in a good position to receive it.
After 5 kicks: rotate the kick-taker. Every player takes 2 goal kicks.
Celebrate every restart that leads to a team keeping the ball.
coachingPoints: RESTARTS ARE OPPORTUNITIES: every throw-in and goal kick is a chance for your team to have the ball. Treat them as a gift, not an interruption.
BOTH FEET ON THE GROUND FOR A THROW-IN: this is the only rule that matters at this stage. Both feet. On the ground. Behind the line.
GET INTO A GOOD POSITION WHEN YOUR TEAM HAS A RESTART: when your team has a throw-in or goal kick, your job is to find a space where you are easy to receive the ball.
THE GOAL KICK CAN GO ANYWHERE: your team can kick the ball to anyone in any direction from a goal kick. You do not have to kick it long — a short kick to a teammate who is free is often better.
CALL FOR THE BALL: if you are in a good position to receive a throw-in or goal kick, call for it. Help your teammate find you.
RESTARTS HAPPEN A LOT: in every match there are 20-30 throw-ins and several goal kicks. Getting these right gives your team lots of extra chances with the ball.
PRACTICE MAKES RESTARTS EASY: the first time you take a throw-in it might feel awkward. After practicing it a few times it becomes completely natural.
YOUR TEAM NEEDS YOU DURING RESTARTS: every restart is a team moment. Everyone has a role — the thrower or kicker, and all the receivers who find good positions.
commonMistakes: Both feet not on the ground during the throw-in — this is the most common error at U8
Ball not going behind the head before the throw — releasing from the front
No movement from teammates to help the throw-taker or kick-taker find a receiver
The ball going too long on the goal kick — overhitting
Only one player trying to receive the goal kick while others stand and watch
corrections: Feet off ground: place two foot markers on the ground at the line. Feet must stay on the markers throughout the throw
Ball not behind head: hold the ball above your own head first and show the player the behind-the-head position before the throw
No movement from teammates: call FIND A SPACE before every throw or kick as a reminder to all non-kickers
Ball going too long: for U8 goal kicks, allow a softer approach run and coach a gentle contact rather than a full kick
Only one receiver: assign 2-3 specific players as the target receivers before each goal kick so everyone has a role
progressions: Add a defender who tries to intercept the throw-in, introducing the competitive element of restarting quickly
Practice throw-ins from different positions along the touchline (near goal, midfield, attacking third) so players experience how the throw-in feels different in each area
Link to the next phase of play: after the goal kick is received, the receiving player does something with it (pass, dribble, shoot)
regressions: Use a very short throw-in distance (1m) for the first few reps so the technique can be established without distance pressure
Allow the goal kick to be taken with a gentle roll rather than a full kick for the most hesitant players
Walk through both restart types at walking pace before any real attempt
matchApplication: Restart situations account for a significant proportion of all ball-in-play moments in football and are often the moments when young players feel most confused and under pressure. A positive, simple, fun introduction to throw-ins and goal kicks at U8 removes this confusion and replaces it with a basic competence and a habit of positioning that serves players well immediately. At U8, the only technical requirement is both feet on the ground for the throw-in — everything else is positioning and enjoyment.
coachTips: One concept per restart. Do not try to coach both throw-in technique and goal kick technique perfectly in the same session.
The counting game (how many throw-ins in 30 seconds) is very engaging for U8 players and naturally motivates good technique.
Walk among players during the practice and give individual warm encouragement to each player.
Parents watching U8 sessions love seeing their children do something recognisable from real football. Restarts look like real football and parents respond positively to them.
Connect to a real match: after the session ask players if they have ever taken a throw-in in a match. Discuss what happened.
diagramDescription: Open pitch area. THROW-IN shown: player at touchline with both feet on the ground behind the line, ball held with both hands, brought behind the head, thrown forward to a receiver 3m away. Foot marker shown (both feet must stay on markers). GOAL KICK shown: ball in 6-yard box, kicker approaching, 5 receivers spread across the pitch in different positions. FIND A SPACE call shown from the kick-taker. Celebration shown after the receiver gets the ball. Simple rule labels shown for each restart type.

title: Direct Free Kick — Advanced Technique and Variations
objective: Develop advanced direct free kick delivery using curl, dip, power, and deceptive variations from multiple positions around the penalty area.
learningOutcomes: Players can deliver a curling free kick accurately from central and wide positions
Players develop the dipping free kick from distance that beats GKs at full stretch
Players understand the tactical decision-making of when to shoot versus when to use a combination
Players build the mental composure for a high-pressure free kick situation
organisation: Five specific free kick positions around the penalty area. GK in goal with a live 3-player wall. Each specialist takes 4 attempts from each position across two rounds. Emphasis on delivery quality and tactical decision-making.
instructions: 5 POSITIONS labeled with cones: P1 (central 20m), P2 (central 26m), P3 (right channel 22m, 20-degree angle), P4 (left channel 22m, 20-degree angle), P5 (wide right 28m, 35-degree angle).
WALL SETUP: 3-player wall set by the GK for each position. GK positions to cover the far post and central area.
DELIVERY OPTIONS for each position:
P1 (central 20m): POWER LOW — driven laces through the centre of the ball, low trajectory, power over placement.
P2 (central 26m): DIP — laces through the top of the ball, topspin, ball dips over the wall and under the bar.
P3 and P4 (slight angles): CURL INSIDE — inside of the kicking foot, contact at the outside quarter of the ball, ball curves around or through the wall.
P5 (wide): CURL AROUND — inside of the foot with greater power, ball curves around the far side of the wall.
PHASE 1 (10 minutes): 2 reps from each position. Technique focus. GK passive.
PHASE 2 (10 minutes): 2 reps from each position. GK fully live. Wall active.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): random position called by coach. Specialist must set up and deliver within 10 seconds.
coachingPoints: CONTACT POINT IS THE TECHNICAL FOUNDATION: for curl, contact the outside quarter of the ball (3 oclock for right foot). For dip, contact the top of the ball (12 oclock). For power, contact the centre (6 oclock). These three contact points produce three entirely different ball flights.

THE DIP REQUIRES TOP-SPIN: a dipping free kick has top-spin applied by the laces brushing across the top of the ball in a downward direction. The ball rises, then drops rapidly as the top-spin pulls it down.
APPROACH ANGLE AFFECTS CURL AMOUNT: a wider approach angle produces more curl because it opens the hip further and allows a more across-the-ball contact. A narrow approach produces less curl.

NON-KICKING FOOT POSITION CONTROLS HEIGHT: placing the non-kicking foot level with the ball produces a flat trajectory. Placing it slightly behind the ball produces elevation. Placing it in front produces a downward trajectory.

THE WALL POSITION DETERMINES WHICH OPTION TO USE: before delivering, the specialist looks at the wall and the GK position to decide which delivery is most likely to score. A wall that covers only one side may leave the other exposed for a curl.
DISGUISE THE APPROACH: vary the run-up slightly between delivery types so the wall and GK cannot read which option is coming from the approach.

COMPOSURE IS THE MENTAL SKILL: at U17, a free kick in a match is watched by the whole crowd and both teams. Developing the composure to execute the correct technique under this pressure requires practicing the routine at full intensity in training.

PRACTICE CREATES THE AUTOMATIC RESPONSE: the specialist who has taken 200 free kicks from P3 in training does not think about technique when a P3 free kick is awarded in a match. They simply execute. Volume of practice creates automaticity.
commonMistakes: Contact point drifting from the intended position across delivery attempts
Approach angle varying inconsistently, producing different amounts of curl
Non-kicking foot too far from the ball, reducing power transfer
Not reading the wall and GK position before delivering — choosing delivery type before seeing what is available
Composure breaking down under Phase 2 live GK pressure
corrections: Contact point drift: mark a spot on the ball with chalk at the correct contact position for each delivery type. Player must hit the spot.
Inconsistent angle: mark the approach run path with cones and require the player to follow the marked path exactly
Non-kicking foot too far: place a tape marker 15cm from the ball showing where the non-kicking foot must land
Not reading before delivering: require a 2-second pause after the whistle during which the player looks at the wall and GK before beginning the run-up
Composure breakdown: increase the pressure gradually. Phase 2 is only introduced after Phase 1 quality is established
progressions: Add a teammate who makes a run at the wall as a distraction option — the specialist can play short if the delivery is not on
Timer: 8 seconds from the whistle to the ball being struck — replicates the match pace at which referees expect free kicks to be taken
Full match scenario: free kicks awarded naturally in a training game, requiring the specialist to set up and deliver in a real match context
regressions: Remove the wall entirely and use Phase 1 only (no GK pressure) for the full first session
Allow unlimited prep time between reps during Phase 1 while delivery technique is established
Reduce to 3 positions only (P1, P3, P4) removing the most technically demanding positions initially
matchApplication: At U17, direct free kick delivery is a high-value individual technical skill that translates directly to match results. A player who can reliably deliver a dangerous free kick from multiple positions around the box is a significant attacking weapon for any team. The five positions in this drill cover the full range of free kick situations most commonly encountered in matches, and the three delivery techniques (power, dip, curl) give the specialist the tools to adapt their delivery to whatever the wall and GK position presents.
coachTips: This session is most valuable as a specialist session with the 2-3 players in the squad who have the best natural technique for free kicks.
The contact point marking (chalk on ball) is the single most effective technical tool in this session.
Film all deliveries from behind the goal to show the curve trajectory, the dip, and the power direction clearly.
Connect to professional free kick specialists the players admire and discuss their specific techniques.
Track conversion rates (goals per attempt by position) across sessions. Improvement in specific position conversion rates is measurable and motivating.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with 5 positions marked. P1 (central 20m) with POWER LOW trajectory shown as a straight flat arrow. P2 (central 26m) with DIP trajectory shown as an arc that rises then drops sharply under the bar. P3 and P4 (slight angles) with CURL INSIDE trajectory shown curving around or through the wall. P5 (wide) with CURL AROUND trajectory going around the far side. 3-player wall shown in each scenario. GK positioning shown. Contact point clocks shown: 6 oclock (power), 12 oclock (dip), 3 oclock (curl right foot). 10-second Phase 3 timer shown.

title: Senior Street Skills — Feints and Body Deception
objective: Develop the full range of body feints and deception moves used in senior street football to beat defenders in 1v1 situations.
learningOutcomes: Players can execute at least 3 different body feints convincingly at match speed
Players understand the mechanics of deception — what makes a feint work
Players develop the confidence to attempt feints against live opponents
Players build the creative 1v1 arsenal that characterises the most entertaining and effective attacking players
organisation: Three-phase session. Phase 1: isolated technique for three feints. Phase 2: feint selection in a live 1v1. Phase 3: open creative 1v1 where all feints are available.
instructions: THE THREE FEINTS:

FEINT 1 — THE STEP OVER: right foot steps over the ball from outside to inside (clockwise motion). Ball stays on the left side. Immediately accelerate left with the outside of the left foot. Can be done multiple times (double or triple step-over).

FEINT 2 — THE SHOULDER DROP: dribbling toward a defender, drop the right shoulder sharply toward the right as if about to go right. Plant the right foot. Push off to the LEFT with the left foot. The shoulder drop sells the fake direction.

FEINT 3 — THE CRUYFF TURN: fake to cross or pass with the right foot. Instead of following through, drag the ball behind the standing left leg with the inside of the right foot. Body turns 180 degrees. Accelerate in the new direction.
PHASE 1 (8 minutes): individual practice of all 3 feints against a cone. 10 reps each. Coach demonstrates first.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): passive defender. Attacker approaches and chooses one feint. Defender reacts. 6 reps then swap.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): fully live 1v1 in the channel. Mini-goal at the far end. All feints available.
coachingPoints: THE FEINT WORKS BECAUSE THE DEFENDER REACTS TO FALSE INFORMATION: the step-over works because the foot going over the ball looks like a shot or a cross. The shoulder drop works because the body language says one direction. The Cruyff turn works because the body shape says cross or pass. All three trick the defender with information that is not true.
COMMITMENT IS WHAT MAKES THE FEINT WORK: a half-hearted step-over fools no one. The feinting movement must be committed enough that the defender genuinely reacts to it.
TIMING THE ACCELERATION TO THE DEFENDER REACTION: the acceleration must happen the instant the defender shifts their weight in the fake direction. One step too late and they have recovered.
SPEED INTO THE FEINT HELPS: approaching the defender at genuine dribbling pace makes the feint more credible. A slow approach gives the defender too much time to read.
FEINT TO YOUR STRONGER SIDE: the Cruyff turn and step-over work in both directions but are easier in one. Know which direction is your natural acceleration direction and feint toward it.
DO NOT TELEGRAPH THE FEINT: players who always use the same feint from the same position become predictable. Vary the feint, the timing, and the position to keep defenders uncertain.
COMBINE FEINTS: a step-over followed immediately by a shoulder drop is extremely difficult to defend because the defender has been deceived twice in rapid succession.
REAL GAMES REWARD BRAVE ATTEMPTS: a feint attempted in a match and defended correctly is still courageous. A feint attempted and converted is one of the best moments in football.
commonMistakes: Step-over going in the wrong direction (clockwise when it should go counterclockwise for a rightward fake)
Shoulder drop not dropping far enough to convince the defender
Cruyff turn body position incorrect — facing the wrong way after the drag-back
Not accelerating immediately after the feint, allowing the defender to recover
Practicing feints but never attempting them in Phase 3 live situations
corrections: Wrong step-over direction: mark a clockwise arrow on the ground at the practice cone. Right foot must travel clockwise around the ball
Insufficient shoulder drop: record and show the player their own shoulder drop — seeing that it is barely noticeable usually immediately corrects it
Wrong Cruyff turn body position: walk through the turn at slow motion emphasising the 180-degree body rotation before any pace is added
No acceleration: place a speed gate 2m past the cone — player must reach it within 2 steps of completing the feint
Not attempting in Phase 3: award 2 bonus points for any attempted feint regardless of outcome
progressions: Double feint: combine step-over and shoulder drop in one movement — the defender is deceived twice
Speed challenge: time from the start of the feint to crossing the defender. Target under 1.5 seconds for the complete beat
Transfer to game: a small game where feint goals earn 3 points and all other goals earn 1 point
regressions: Remove the live defender from Phase 2 and use only a passive cone for the full Phase 2 before introducing passive defender
Slow Phase 1 to walking pace and narrate each feint step by step before any tempo is added
Focus on one feint only (step-over) for the full session before introducing the other two
matchApplication: Body feints and deception moves are the most distinctly human element of football — they require imagination, coordination, and the courage to attempt something creative in front of an opponent. The greatest dribblers in history (Maradona, Ronaldinho, Messi, Neymar) have all developed extensive feint repertoires that make them genuinely unpredictable in 1v1 situations. At U16, players have the physical coordination and competitive context to develop these moves into genuine match weapons. The street football culture celebrates these moves as art forms — which is exactly the spirit in which they should be practiced.
coachTips: Demonstrate each feint yourself before players attempt it. The visual model is the most effective instruction.
The Phase 3 bonus points for feint attempts is the most important motivational tool. Use it consistently.
Film Phase 3 and show players their own successful feints. Seeing yourself beat a defender with a Cruyff turn is enormously motivating.
Connect to professional examples for each feint: Ronaldinho for the step-over, Thierry Henry for the shoulder drop, Johan Cruyff for his eponymous turn.
Make feint practice a regular element of 1v1 sessions throughout the season. The repertoire builds gradually across many sessions.
diagramDescription: 12x6m channel. THREE FEINT DIAGRAMS shown: STEP OVER — right foot clockwise motion over ball (arrow), ball stays left, left foot push right with outside of foot (acceleration arrow). SHOULDER DROP — dribble toward defender, right shoulder drop (arrow), plant right foot, push left with left foot (acceleration arrow). CRUYFF TURN — fake cross body shape shown, inside of right foot dragging ball behind standing leg (arrow), 180-degree body rotation, acceleration in new direction. Phase 3 live 1v1 shown with all three feints available. Bonus point label for feint attempts.

title: The Streets Meets Senior Football — Adults
objective: Bring the street football philosophy into adult training through a session that values creative expression, flair, and individual skill alongside the tactical demands of senior football.
learningOutcomes: Adult players rediscover the creative freedom of street football in a competitive training environment
Players develop confidence to attempt skill moves and creative play in adult competitive situations
Players experience the joy that originally motivated their love of football
Teams build a culture where creativity and expression are valued alongside tactical discipline
organisation: A complete session structured around the street football philosophy applied to adult football. Three phases: individual skill expression, small-sided creative game, and a full game with a flair bonus system.
instructions: PHASE 1 — SKILL EXPRESSION WARM-UP (8 minutes): every player has a ball in their own space. 3 minutes of free touch work (any moves, any techniques, anything they enjoy). Then 5 minutes of pair challenges: each pair shares a ball and takes turns attempting one skill move at a time. Partner calls GOOD, ALMOST, or AGAIN as feedback.

PHASE 2 — CREATIVE SMALL-SIDED GAME (10 minutes): 4v4 or 5v5 with the flair scoring system. Standard goal = 1 point. Skill move that beats a defender = 1 bonus point (call SKILL immediately). Goal following a skill move = 3 points total. Nutmeg = 2 bonus points regardless of what follows.

PHASE 3 — FULL GAME (7 minutes): free game with the scoring system from Phase 2. Every skill move attempt (whether successful or not) earns 0.5 points. Success earns the full bonus. This rewards courage over caution.
Between phases: 30-second discussion about what creative moments stood out. Name specific players.
coachingPoints: ADULTS FORGET THEY ARE ALLOWED TO ENJOY FOOTBALL: somewhere between the ages of 14 and adulthood, many players lose the freedom and joy they had when they first played. This session gives explicit permission to have fun, be creative, and play with the freedom of the street.

SKILL MOVES WORK IN ADULT FOOTBALL: a Cruyff turn, a nutmeg, or a step-over beats a defender in an adult competitive match as effectively as at any other level. The skills are not less relevant at senior level.

THE SCORING SYSTEM CHANGES THE CULTURE: when the scoring system explicitly rewards skill attempts, the culture of the session shifts. Players who would never attempt a feint in training suddenly try them because the environment says it is valued.

CREATIVITY AND DISCIPLINE ARE NOT OPPOSITES: the greatest adult players (Iniesta, Scholes, De Bruyne) are both technically creative and tactically disciplined. This session develops the creative side without abandoning the disciplined side.

THE 0.5 POINT FOR ATTEMPTS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT ELEMENT: in street football, attempting a difficult skill move is celebrated regardless of outcome. This scoring rule brings that culture into adult training.
NAME THE CREATIVE MOMENTS: when a skill move happens, naming the player who attempted it (whether they succeeded or not) builds the culture of celebrating individual expression.

CONNECT TO WHY YOU STARTED PLAYING: ask players why they first fell in love with football. Almost always the answer involves playing freely without pressure — in the garden, in the street, with friends. This session recreates that.
CARRY IT INTO MATCHES: the freedom developed in this session should carry into match performance. A player who knows their coach values creative attempts will try more things in matches.
commonMistakes: Experienced players being unwilling to attempt skill moves due to fear of looking foolish
Phase 1 becoming too passive with players doing minimal touches rather than genuinely creative work
The scoring system not being taken seriously by adult players who are result-focused
Skill attempts becoming reckless and losing the ball in positions that hurt the team
The session losing energy in Phase 3 when the free game removes the explicit flair incentives
corrections: Unwilling to attempt moves: the coach must attempt skill moves themselves in Phase 1 — a coach who does the Cruyff turn and makes it look fun gives everyone permission to try
Passive Phase 1: pair challenges immediately after the individual time ensure everyone has a specific task. The GOOD, ALMOST, AGAIN system gives passive players something to contribute
Scoring not taken seriously: keep the score visible and updated loudly after every skill moment. The coach enthusiasm for skill scoring is what makes it culturally real
Reckless attempts: the 0.5 points for attempts only applies when the attempt is in a reasonable skill position — not when the team is defending their own box
Energy drop in Phase 3: the coach keeps commentating skill moments in Phase 3 the same way as Phase 2, maintaining the creative culture even without the explicit incentive
progressions: Senior skill challenge: a 5-minute dedicated skill competition where players perform their best move in front of the group and are scored on creativity and execution
Team street football tournament: two teams compete in a full street football session using all Streets Meet Tiki-Taka scoring principles
Monthly creative session: make this session a permanent monthly fixture and track which players develop the most creative confidence across a season
regressions: Reduce Phase 2 to 3v3 in a smaller area to give everyone more space and time with the ball
Remove the full-size goal from Phase 3 and use mini-goals to create more scoring opportunities and more skill opportunities
Allow 4 touches per player in Phase 2 before reducing to 2 touches in subsequent sessions
matchApplication: Adult football in grassroots settings too often becomes purely functional — a sequence of tactical instructions, set pieces, and fitness work that removes the joy that originally brought players to the game. The Streets Meets Senior Football session deliberately recaptures that original joy and brings it into adult training. Research on player development consistently shows that joy, intrinsic motivation, and creative freedom are as important for adult performance as technical and tactical development. A team that enjoys training trains harder, competes harder, and performs better. This session exists to ensure that adult players in your programme remember why they started playing.
coachTips: Your own willingness to attempt skill moves in Phase 1 is the single most important factor in the success of this session. If the coach does it, everyone does it.
Name specific creative moments loudly and specifically throughout the session. BRILLIANT STEP-OVER from Michael in the 5th minute — this is how you build the creative culture.
Take this session seriously as a performance development tool, not just a fun add-on. The creative freedom developed here transfers directly to match performance.
Ask players at the end of Phase 1 to name one skill move they attempted that they had not tried in training before. Celebrate every answer.
Make the Streets Meets Senior Football session a monthly cultural fixture. It will become one of the most anticipated sessions in the training calendar.
diagramDescription: Full area. PHASE 1 shown: individual player with ball in own space doing free creative movement, then pairs with GOOD/ALMOST/AGAIN feedback system. PHASE 2 shown: 4v4 small-sided game with flair scoring: SKILL MOVE label (1 bonus point), NUTMEG label (2 bonus points), SKILL THEN GOAL (3 total points), STANDARD GOAL (1 point), ATTEMPT label (0.5 points). Score shown updating after each flair moment. PHASE 3 shown: free game with coach commentating creative moments. Monthly fixture badge shown.

title: Passing Activation — Senior Speed Rondo
objective: Push passing activation to its highest intensity through a senior-level speed rondo that combines tiki-taka possession quality with maximum physical and cognitive demand.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain possession at maximum intensity under 2-defender pressure in a tight space
Players develop the physical and cognitive speed that characterises elite possession play
Players build the automatic 1-touch and 2-touch responses needed at senior level
Players experience the concentration and rhythm of elite possession training
organisation: High-intensity 5v2 rondo with a specific intensity protocol: 2-minute blocks at maximum effort with 30-second recovery between blocks. 6 blocks total. The specific rules push the technical and physical demand beyond a standard rondo.
instructions: 5 possession players in an 8x8m square. 2 defenders inside pressing.
TECHNICAL RULES for this senior rondo: maximum 2 touches (no 3-touch option), first-time play actively encouraged with a bonus point for any sequence of 3 consecutive first-time passes.
PHYSICAL RULE: defenders must press at 100 percent for the full 2 minutes. No passive pressing. Any defender who drops below full intensity is replaced immediately.
SCORING: 10 consecutive passes = 1 point. 3 consecutive first-time passes = 1 bonus point. Defenders earn 1 point for every turnover.
After every 2-minute block: 30-second recovery (walking only). Swap one defender.
6 blocks total = 12 minutes of work plus 3 minutes of recovery = 15 minutes.
PHASE 1 (blocks 1-2): establish quality. Coach evaluates touch quality and first-time pass frequency.
PHASE 2 (blocks 3-4): increase intensity. Defenders swap more frequently to maintain maximum pressing energy.
PHASE 3 (blocks 5-6): maximum intensity. No coaching interruptions. Count total points for each team.
coachingPoints: 2-TOUCH MAXIMUM AT SENIOR LEVEL IS THE BASELINE: at U16 and above, 2-touch maximum in a rondo is the standard. Players who need a third touch to play are technically behind the level. The first touch must always set up an immediate second touch.

FIRST-TIME PASSING IS THE ELITE STANDARD: the bonus point for 3 consecutive first-time passes creates explicit incentive for the highest technical standard. First-time passing in a tight rondo under 2 defender pressure is extremely demanding.

READ THE NEXT PASS BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: elite rondo players know where they are passing before the ball reaches their foot. The first touch is directed by a decision made before contact. This requires constant scanning and reading.

THE DEFENDERS PACE DETERMINES QUALITY: a high-intensity defending pair forces higher-quality possession play than a passive pair. If the defenders are not genuinely pressing at 100 percent, the training stimulus for the possession team is reduced.

RHYTHM IS TECHNICAL QUALITY: a senior rondo that has a rhythm — pass, pass, pass at even tempo — is technically better than one that is stop-start. Rhythm indicates automatic decision-making rather than deliberate processing.
THE 30-SECOND RECOVERY IS DELIBERATE: working in 2-minute maximum-effort blocks with 30-second recovery replicates the physical pattern of high-intensity possession sequences in senior matches.
COUNT THE PASSES TOGETHER: the whole group counts consecutive passes aloud. The collective count builds shared focus and makes every pass feel meaningful.
CELEBRATE THE FIRST-TIME SEQUENCES: when 3 consecutive first-time passes happen (rare, extremely difficult), it should be celebrated by the whole group immediately and specifically.
commonMistakes: Technical quality dropping in later blocks as physical fatigue accumulates
First-time passing attempted but inaccurate, causing more turnovers than the 2-touch option would produce
Defenders dropping intensity in later blocks, removing the training stimulus for the possession team
No collective counting, losing the shared focus that makes the pass targets meaningful
Recovery period becoming too long as players chat rather than returning to play
corrections: Technical quality dropping: in blocks 5-6, reduce to 3v1 briefly to give the possession team more space and allow recovery of technical quality before returning to 5v2

Inaccurate first-time passing: make first-time passes optional (bonus only) rather than attempting them when accuracy is not there yet. The 2-touch option should always produce the higher-quality pass
Defenders dropping intensity: swap defenders after every 90 seconds rather than every 2 minutes to keep fresh legs in the defending role
No collective counting: make counting mandatory from block 1. Any pass not counted by the group resets to zero
Recovery too long: the coach starts the 30-second recovery countdown audibly. When the 30 seconds is up, the next block starts immediately
progressions: Reduce to 6x6m square for blocks 5 and 6 to increase the difficulty without adding fatigue-based errors
Add a rule for blocks 5-6: the ball must change direction (left-right or right-left) at least once every 5 passes
Full team rondo: 8v3 in a larger 12x12m space, replicating team possession under game-realistic pressure
regressions: Use 3 touches maximum for blocks 1-2 while players adapt to the senior rondo intensity before reducing to 2 touches
Use only 1 defender for the first block to establish the rhythm and touch quality before adding the second
Extend recovery to 60 seconds between blocks for players who find 30 seconds insufficient recovery
matchApplication: The senior speed rondo is the most technically demanding passing activation drill in the library and represents the peak of the tiki-taka possession training philosophy. It combines maximum technical demand (2-touch maximum, first-time bonus), maximum physical intensity (100 percent pressing), and maximum cognitive demand (constant scanning and reading) in a format that is simultaneously competitive, collaborative, and joyful. Elite clubs worldwide use exactly this type of high-intensity rondo as a core session component precisely because it develops all three qualities simultaneously.
coachTips: The defender intensity is the most important variable to monitor. Walk the perimeter during each block specifically evaluating defender effort.
The first-time bonus point celebration is the cultural highlight of the session. Make it feel special every time it happens.
Film block 3 or 4 from above. The shape of the possession group, the first-touch directions, and the defender positions relative to the passing options are all visible from above.
Connect to the elite clubs who use this format: Barcelona La Masia, Liverpool Academy, Ajax — all use high-intensity rondos as a daily training fixture.
Track the highest consecutive pass count across blocks and across sessions. Groups that practice this regularly show significant improvement in their maximum count within 4-6 weeks.
diagramDescription: 8x8m square. 5 possession players (circles) spread around the square. 2 defenders (triangles) in the centre pressing at maximum intensity (press arrows shown intensely). SCORING shown: 10 passes = 1 point, 3 first-time passes = 1 bonus point (labeled FIRST TIME BONUS), defender turnover = 1 point. 2-MINUTE BLOCK / 30-SECOND RECOVERY timeline shown. Collective counting shown with pass count speech bubble (1...2...3...). First-time sequence shown: three rapid pass arrows with FIRST TIME BONUS star. Defender swap shown after each block.

title: Street Skill Showcase — U17 Expression
objective: Create the highest-level expression environment for senior players — a session entirely dedicated to individual skill, creativity, and the joy of football at its most expressive.
learningOutcomes: Senior players develop and perform their highest-level individual skill moves
Players build the competitive confidence to attempt creative moves in senior football
Players discover that creative expression and competitive success are complementary
Players experience the culture of mutual appreciation for skill that defines street football
organisation: Three-phase session entirely dedicated to senior expression. No tactical instruction. No fitness elements. Pure skill, creativity, and individual expression.
instructions: PHASE 1 — SOLO MASTERY (6 minutes): every player has a ball in their own space. Free practice of any skill, move, or technique they choose. Coach moves around specifically asking each player what they are working on and celebrating the answer.

PHASE 2 — SKILL BATTLES (8 minutes): pairs compete in 90-second skill battles in small zones. Points for: successful skill move beat (2 pts), nutmeg (3 pts), elastico executed (3 pts), rainbow flick (5 pts), goal (1 pt). Coach judges and celebrates every attempt.
PHASE 3 — SHOWCASE (6 minutes): each player performs their best move for the group. Group responds with genuine appreciation. Coach names and celebrates every player by something specific they saw.
End: vote for the most creative player of the session. Celebrate them specifically.
coachingPoints: SENIOR PLAYERS NEED PERMISSION TO BE CREATIVE: at senior level, the pressure to be tactically correct and physically effective can suppress the individual creativity that made players love football in the first place. This session explicitly grants permission to be creative.
YOUR BEST SKILL MOVE IS YOUR SIGNATURE: every player has a move that is most natural to them. This session is about discovering or rediscovering that signature move.
STREET FOOTBALL HAS NO AGE LIMIT: the philosophy of The Streets Meet Tiki-Taka applies as much to senior players as to youth players. The joy of individual expression does not have an expiry date.

THE SHOWCASE BUILDS MUTUAL RESPECT: when players perform for each other and the group responds with genuine appreciation, the team culture shifts. Creative attempts become valued and respected rather than risky and embarrassing.
FIVE POINTS FOR THE RAINBOW FLICK: the highest bonus in the scoring system is for the most spectacular move. This communicates that the most ambitious creative attempts are the most valued.

CREATIVITY IN TRAINING TRANSFERS TO MATCHES: players who attempt creative moves in a safe training environment develop the confidence to attempt them in matches. The session directly builds match creativity.
EVERY PLAYER HAS SOMETHING UNIQUE: in Phase 3, every player performs something. The coach must find and celebrate something genuinely specific and unique about every performance.
THIS IS WHY WE PLAY: ask players at the end of the session why they play football. The answers — joy, expression, connection, excitement — are exactly what this session provides.
commonMistakes: Senior players being too self-conscious to genuinely attempt creative moves
The scoring system not being taken seriously, reducing it to a casual kickabout
Phase 3 showcase becoming rushed or insufficiently celebrated
The session feeling like a break from real training rather than a valued session
Only one or two extrovert players dominating the energy
corrections: Self-consciousness: coach attempts a skill move themselves in Phase 1 and celebrates their own attempt publicly
Not taking scoring seriously: keep a visible scoreboard and update it loudly after every skill moment
Phase 3 rushed: allocate a minimum of 30 seconds per player in the showcase, not less
Feeling like a break: frame the session explicitly as a performance development session before it begins
One or two dominating: in Phase 2 specifically pair confident players with less confident ones so the battles are balanced
progressions: Add a team skill competition: total team points across all Phase 2 battles — the team collectively celebrating skill
Introduce a new move each session: coach teaches one new skill move at the start of Phase 1 that nobody has tried before
Film the entire session and share highlights with the squad afterward
regressions: Make Phase 3 optional (players can watch instead of perform) for the first session while the culture is established
Reduce Phase 2 to 60-second battles to keep the energy high and reduce self-consciousness
Allow pairs to collaborate on a skill move together rather than competing against each other
matchApplication: The U17 expression session is the fullest realisation of The Streets Meet Tiki-Taka philosophy for senior players. It acknowledges that creative individual expression is not just a youth player quality — it is the quality that makes football beautiful at every level. Senior teams that have a culture of appreciating and celebrating skill play more creative football, enjoy training more, and perform more confidently in matches. This session builds that culture.
coachTips: Frame this session as elite performance development, not as a fun extra. It is.
Your own creative participation is essential. A coach who attempts a Cruyff turn and laughs at themselves creates the culture of joyful expression.
The specific appreciation in Phase 3 is the most important moment. Name something specific about every player.
Return to this session monthly. The skill levels and confidence grow visibly across a season.
Share video highlights with players and families. Pride in creative skill is one of the most powerful motivators in youth and senior football.
diagramDescription: Open area. PHASE 1: individual players in spaces with balls, each doing different creative moves. PHASE 2: multiple paired 90-second battles with skill scoring: nutmeg (3pts), elastico (3pts), rainbow (5pts), skill beat (2pts), goal (1pt). Visible scoreboard shown. PHASE 3: player performing solo showcase for the group, group shown watching with appreciation. Coach shown naming specific players. MOST CREATIVE award shown at the end.

title: Pressing in Pairs — Hunting the Ball
objective: Develop the coordinated two-player press that traps the ball-carrier against a touchline or in a corner with no escape.
learningOutcomes: Players understand how two defenders work together to trap a ball-carrier
Players develop the press angles that cut off all passing options simultaneously
Players build the physical and mental intensity of the coordinated press
Players connect pair pressing to the team pressing concepts at a higher level
players: Groups of 4 (2 pressers vs 1 attacker with a server)
pitchSize: 15x15m zone with a corner trap area
organisation: 15x15m zone. Two defenders work together to trap a single ball-carrier in a corner or against a touchline. A server passes the ball to the attacker at the start of each rep. The defenders must coordinate their press to leave the attacker with no good option.
instructions: Server plays ball to the attacker who starts centrally in the zone.
The two defenders immediately begin their coordinated press.
PRESS ANGLES: Defender 1 (D1) approaches the ball-carrier from the front, showing them toward the corner. Defender 2 (D2) moves to cut off the escape route to the other side.
The goal: both defenders arrive simultaneously so the attacker is trapped with: the touchline on one side, D1 in front, D2 cutting off the other side.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): attacker is passive (holds the ball and does not try to escape). Defenders practice the coordinated press angles.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): attacker can dribble freely. Defenders must coordinate to trap.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): fully live. If defenders win the ball in 5 seconds, they earn a point. If attacker escapes, they earn a point.
coachingPoints: D1 SHOWS, D2 COVERS: D1 approaches from a slight angle that shows the attacker toward the corner (not directly at them). D2 moves to cover the escape side. This two-player coordination is what makes the press a trap.
ARRIVE SIMULTANEOUSLY: a press where D1 arrives 2 seconds before D2 is not a trap — D2 has not yet cut off the escape. Both must arrive at the same moment.
D1 DOES NOT TACKLE UNTIL D2 IS IN POSITION: D1 jockeys and delays while D2 moves into the covering position. When D2 calls READY, D1 can attempt the tackle.
USE THE TOUCHLINE AS A THIRD DEFENDER: the touchline or corner boundary reduces the space the attacker can use to escape. Show them toward it deliberately.
COMMUNICATION IS THE MECHANISM: D2 must call READY when in position. D1 must call BALL to confirm they are pressing. Both calls happen on every rep.
PRESS AT FULL PACE: a press at 80 percent gives the attacker time to play a pass before the trap is complete. Press at 100 percent from the first step.
IF THE PRESS FAILS: when the attacker escapes, both defenders immediately reorganise and try again. No individual chasing. Reset and re-press.
THIS IS THE FOUNDATION OF TEAM PRESSING: every team pressing system is built on individual pairs pressing together. Learning to press in pairs is the prerequisite for pressing as a team.
commonMistakes: D1 pressing directly at the attacker rather than showing them toward the corner
D2 arriving too late, allowing the attacker to escape before the trap is complete
No communication between D1 and D2 about when D2 is in position
D1 attempting the tackle before D2 is in position, leaving the escape route open
Both defenders pressing the ball simultaneously rather than D1 pressing and D2 covering
corrections: D1 not showing: place a cone showing D1s press angle — the approach must be from outside the cone, not through it
D2 too late: D2 must start their movement the instant D1 begins pressing, not after D1 has arrived
No communication: READY and BALL calls are mandatory. Any rep without both calls does not count
D1 tackling too early: enforce the rule that D1 cannot tackle until they hear the READY call from D2
Both pressing: immediately pause and reset when both press simultaneously — show the empty space created
progressions: Extend to 2v2: a second attacker provides a passing option for the ball-carrier, making the press significantly harder
Reduce the zone to 10x10m to make the trap faster and more achievable before extending back to 15x15m
Apply to a full game: any time a team wins the ball from a coordinated pair press in the game they earn a bonus point
regressions: Use a completely passive attacker throughout Phase 1 and 2 while the coordination is established
Slow both defenders to 70 percent pace in Phase 1 to allow time for the communication to develop
Walk through the press angles step by step at slow motion before any live practice
matchApplication: Coordinated pair pressing is one of the most effective and most trainable defensive skills in football. When two defenders work together with correct press angles and timing, they can trap even skilled attackers consistently. This two-player coordination is the building block of all team pressing systems and represents the bridge between individual defending and team defending.
coachTips: Walk the press angles very explicitly before Phase 1. Use cones on the ground to show D1 and D2 positions.
The READY call from D2 is the most important communication element. Enforce it from the first rep.
Celebrate successful traps with genuine enthusiasm — a coordinated press that wins the ball is a tactical achievement worth celebrating.
Connect to the team pressing session at U16/17 level: explain that everything the team does in the high press starts with this two-player coordination.
Film Phase 3 from above — the angles of approach and the trap formation are clearly visible from above.
diagramDescription: 15x15m zone with corner trap area marked. Server shown passing to central attacker. D1 shown approaching from angle (showing attacker toward corner, approach angle arrow). D2 shown cutting off escape route on the other side. TRAP shown: attacker in corner with touchline on right, D1 in front, D2 cutting off left. COMMUNICATION shown: D2 calling READY, D1 calling BALL. TACKLE moment shown: D1 attempting tackle after READY call. Phase 3 5-second clock shown.

title: Mid Block — Defensive Shape in Transition
objective: Develop the mid-block defensive shape as a transition system used when the team has just lost the ball in the attacking half and must immediately organise defensively.
learningOutcomes: Players understand the mid-block as a defensive shape used in specific match situations
Players can drop from an attacking position into a mid-block shape quickly and collectively
Players develop the communication and organisation of the mid-block from a transition moment
Teams experience how a well-timed defensive drop prevents dangerous transitions
organisation: Full pitch. Team A attacks with possession. On the coaches whistle, Team A immediately loses the ball and must drop into a mid-block shape in their own half. Team B attacks from where they receive the ball. Team A must organise the mid-block before Team B can exploit the transition.
instructions: Team A attacks with 5-6 players in Team Bs half.
On WHISTLE: Team A loses possession. Team B immediately has the ball.
Team A must drop into a mid-block defensive shape: two lines at 30-35m from their own goal.
MIDFIELD LINE of Team A: 3-4 players across the width at 30-35m from goal.
DEFENSIVE LINE of Team A: 3-4 players across the width at 18-20m from goal.
Maximum 5 seconds for Team A to establish the mid-block shape before Team B can play.
Team B attacks against the organising Team A.
SCORING: Team A earns 1 point for every 10 seconds of maintaining the mid-block correctly. Team B earns 2 points for a goal.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): 5-second organisation time before Team B can play. Focus on the drop and organisation.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): 3-second organisation time. Harder transition.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): no organisation time. Team B attacks immediately.
