coachingPoints: THE MID-BLOCK IS A TRANSITION DEFENSIVE SHAPE: the mid-block is used specifically when the team has just lost the ball in the attacking half and must quickly organise in their own half. It is not a starting defensive shape — it is a reactive one.
DROP TOGETHER, NOT INDIVIDUALLY: when the whistle goes, all players in Team A drop simultaneously. Individual players who do not drop break the forming shape and create dangerous gaps.

MIDFIELD LINE SETS FIRST: the midfield line drops to the 30-35m position and holds. The defensive line sets behind them. The midfield line is the first barrier and must be established before the defensive line.

THE SHAPE CREATES THE COMPACTNESS: two compact lines with less than 10m between them deny the space between the lines that would allow easy penetration. Players must monitor the distance between the lines constantly.
COMMUNICATE THE SHAPE AS IT FORMS: the most experienced or vocal player in the dropping group must call the organisation as it forms — LINE, COMPACT, HOLD.
THE 5 SECONDS IS THE COACHING EMPHASIS: how quickly Team A can establish the correct shape is the most important measurement. 5 seconds is achievable with practice. 3 seconds is excellent.
HOLD POSITION UNDER PRESSURE: once the mid-block is formed, players must resist the temptation to press individually. Hold the shape and let the ball come to the block.

THE MID-BLOCK CREATES COUNTER-ATTACK OPPORTUNITIES: when Team B is forced to play around the mid-block and eventually makes a mistake, Team A immediately transitions to attack from a compact and organised position.
commonMistakes: Individual players dropping at different speeds, creating gaps in the forming shape
Midfield line dropping too deep and merging with the defensive line, removing the two-line structure
No communication during the drop, leaving players uncertain of where to go
Players holding position too far up the pitch, not dropping far enough to form the mid-block
The defensive shape breaking immediately when Team B applies pressure, before it has been held for any time
corrections: Different drop speeds: on WHISTLE, all players drop together — any player not moving immediately is specifically called and corrected
Midfield line too deep: place cones at 30-35m showing the minimum midfield line position. Players cannot drop below these cones
No communication: designate a specific player as the organiser who calls the shape on every transition
Holding position too high: place cones at 30-35m and 18-20m showing where the two lines must form. Players must reach these positions before the shape is considered established
Shape breaking under pressure: enforce the hold rule — no individual pressing from the mid-block during Phase 1. Players must hold regardless of how close Team B gets
progressions: Reduce organisation time to 2 seconds in Phase 3 with Team B attacking immediately
Add a goalkeeper who communicates from behind the defensive line to support the organisation
Full match with the mid-block as Team As explicit tactical instruction for the entire session
regressions: Extend organisation time to 8 seconds for the first session while the drop pattern is established
Walk through the drop movement at slow motion before any live practice
Reduce to 8v6 giving Team A a numbers advantage while the organisational habits are built
matchApplication: The defensive transition from attack to mid-block is one of the most specific and most impactful tactical moments in football. Teams that can quickly and collectively organise a defensive shape after losing the ball prevent the transition goals that are among the most frequent and most dangerous in the game. The mid-block specifically is valuable because it creates a compact platform both for sustained defending and for the counter-attack that can follow when the ball is won.
coachTips: The speed of drop is the primary measurement. Use a stopwatch to time how quickly the two lines are established and celebrate improvements.
The communication organiser role is worth assigning to a specific player permanently so they develop the habit across many sessions.
Film from above during Phase 3 — the two-line shape and any gaps within it are only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to the low-block drill: the mid-block uses the same two-line principle as the low block but at a higher position on the pitch.
Run this drill in the same session as the high press drill to show players the full range of defensive shapes available to the team.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Team A shown with players advanced in attacking positions. WHISTLE shown. ALL TEAM A PLAYERS shown with simultaneous drop arrows. MIDFIELD LINE established at 30-35m (4 players across width). DEFENSIVE LINE established at 18-20m (4 players across width). Space between lines shown as less than 10m (COMPACT label). Team B shown with the ball beginning their attack. 5-second organisation timer shown. Shape scoring shown: 1 point per 10 seconds of correct shape maintained.

title: Pressing From the Front — Striker Pressing Triggers
objective: Develop the specific pressing triggers and angles used by forwards when pressing the opposition defensive line and goalkeeper.
learningOutcomes: Players identify the correct pressing trigger that begins the high press from a forward position
Players develop the press angle that cuts off the most dangerous passing options
Players understand the cover shadow principle in pressing — blocking passing lanes with body position
Players build the physical intensity and mental readiness to press at full pace from a forward position
pitchSize: Full pitch (defensive and midfield thirds of opposition)
organisation: Full pitch. 3-4 opposition defenders plus GK build out from the back. 2-3 forwards press the build-out. The session specifically coaches the triggers (what starts the press), the angles (how to approach to cut off passes), and the cover shadow (how body position blocks passing lanes).
instructions: PRESS TRIGGERS: the forward(s) begin pressing only on specific triggers, not continuously.
TRIGGER 1 — GK HAS BALL: forward presses the nearest CB as the GK tries to distribute.
TRIGGER 2 — BACK PASS: when a defender plays back to the GK, the forward immediately presses the GK.
TRIGGER 3 — HEAVY TOUCH: if a defender receives the ball and it bounces away from their foot, press immediately.

THE PRESS ANGLE: the pressing forward does not run directly at the ball-carrier. They approach from an angle that: (1) prevents the easy forward pass, (2) shows the ball-carrier toward the touchline or away from danger.

COVER SHADOW: the pressing forwards body position should block one passing option without touching the ball. Standing slightly to one side means the player behind the forward is in the forwards cover shadow.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): static walk-through. Opposition defenders hold positions. Forwards practice trigger recognition and press angles without defensive movement.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): opposition defenders move and pass slowly. Forwards press on triggers.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): full live press. Opposition builds at match pace. Forwards press at 100 percent.
coachingPoints: WAIT FOR THE TRIGGER: pressing before the trigger is ineffective because the ball-carrier has time and space to release. Wait for the specific trigger that commits the ball to a challenging position.

THE PRESS ANGLE CUTS OFF THE EASY PASS: the forward does not run at the CB — they approach from the side that prevents the easy forward pass. A forward who runs directly at the CB allows the CB to simply play around them.

COVER SHADOW IS A PASSIVE DEFENSIVE TOOL: the forwards body position can block a passing lane without running toward the player in that lane. Standing so that the midfielder behind them cannot easily receive is cover shadow.
PRESS AT FULL PACE OR HOLD: a 70-percent-pace press uses energy without winning the ball and gives the opposition a false sense of pressure. Full pace or hold the position.

THE SECOND FORWARD COVERS THE OTHER CB: when the first forward presses the right CB, the second forward immediately covers the left CB to prevent the easy switch. If there is only one forward pressing, they must cover the most dangerous short option.
READ THE GK AND CB BODY SHAPE BEFORE PRESSING: before beginning the press, the forward reads which direction the GK or CB is likely to play. Press to cut off the most likely pass.
PRESS IN WAVES NOT CONTINUOUSLY: the press happens in short 5-10 second waves. After a press fails (ball is played away), reset and wait for the next trigger rather than continuously chasing.

THE FORWARD PRESS CREATES SPACE FOR TEAMMATES: when the CB is forced wide by the forward press, the space between the lines opens. The forwards pressing creates the opportunity for the midfielders to press higher.
commonMistakes: Pressing before a trigger has occurred — wasting energy and position
Running directly at the CB rather than at an angle that cuts off the forward pass
No cover shadow awareness — body position not blocking any passing lane
Pressing at 70 percent rather than at 100 percent when a trigger occurs
Both forwards pressing the same CB, leaving the other CB completely free
corrections: Too early pressing: use a specific verbal trigger call from the coach (BACK PASS or HEAVY TOUCH) in Phase 1 so forwards practice waiting for and reacting to the trigger word
Straight press angle: place two cones showing the angle the forward must approach from. One cone in front of the forward (correct approach position), one showing the passing lane to block
No cover shadow: add a passive midfielder who receives the ball if no cover shadow is applied — immediately shows the consequence of poor body position
70 percent pressing: require a minimum approach speed (forwards must reach the CB before a count of 3 from the trigger)
Both pressing same CB: specifically designate which CB each forward covers before Phase 2 begins and maintain the assignments throughout
progressions: Add a real high press transition: when the press wins the ball, immediately counter-attack for 5 seconds
Introduce a second trigger line: forwards must hold below the midfield line until a trigger occurs, replicating the positional discipline of a structured press
Full match with the forward press as the teams primary defensive strategy
regressions: Use only Trigger 2 (back pass to GK) for the full session while the pressing habit is established
Make opposition defenders pass at walking pace in Phase 1 and Phase 2
Remove the cover shadow element for initial sessions and add it only once the trigger and angle habits are established
matchApplication: Forward pressing is the starting point of any high-press defensive system, but it requires specific technical knowledge (the triggers, the angles, the cover shadow) that many players have never been explicitly taught. Forwards who understand these mechanics are significantly more effective pressers than those who simply run at the ball, because their approach cuts off passing options and forces the opposition into difficult positions. This drill develops exactly that specific forward pressing knowledge.
coachTips: Walk through each trigger explicitly before any live practice and ask players to identify the triggers in real match footage.
The cover shadow concept is the most sophisticated element and worth spending 3-4 minutes on specifically in Phase 1.
Film Phase 3 from above — the press angles and cover shadows are only visible from an elevated angle.
Connect to elite pressing teams: discuss how Liverpool forwards under Klopp specifically used trigger-based pressing rather than continuous pressure.
Once the habits are established in this drill, they should be reinforced whenever pressing is discussed in any other session.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Opposition defensive line shown: GK, 2 CBs, 2 FBs. TWO PRESS TRIGGERS shown: BACK PASS (defender plays back to GK, forward immediately presses GK with press angle arrow), HEAVY TOUCH (CB receives, ball bounces away, forward immediately presses from an angle). PRESS ANGLE shown: forward approaches from the side that blocks the forward pass (angle indicator), ball-carrier shown with limited options. COVER SHADOW shown: forwards body position blocking the passing lane to a midfielder behind them (shadow zone labeled). TWO-FORWARD COORDINATION shown: Forward 1 pressing right CB, Forward 2 covering left CB.

title: Penetration — Dribble at Speed in Behind
objective: Develop the ability to receive a pass in behind a defensive line and carry the ball at speed toward goal — the most direct form of penetration in football.
learningOutcomes: Players can accelerate with the ball after receiving a pass in behind without losing control
Players develop the composure to finish after a fast dribble from deep
Players build the habit of looking up after receiving in behind rather than just running
Players connect the run in behind (from the movement drills) to the technical dribble that follows
organisation: A 30m channel with a goal at the far end. Server at the start plays a ball in behind a simulated defensive line (a cone placed at 15m). Player makes a run, receives the ball, dribbles at speed to goal, and finishes. Multiple groups run simultaneously.
instructions: Mark a defensive line with a cone at 15m from the start.
Player A starts at the beginning of the channel. Server stands to the side at the 15m mark.
On GO: Player A sprints toward and beyond the cone.
As Player A passes the cone: server plays a ball in behind (into the space between the cone and the goal).
Player A receives the ball in behind, controls it on the run, and dribbles at speed to the goal to finish.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): no goalkeeper. Player focuses on receiving in stride and dribbling at pace to goal.
PHASE 2 (5 minutes): GK added. Player must read the GK position on the approach and choose the correct finish.
PHASE 3 (5 minutes): add a recovering defender who starts 5m behind the player at the moment the ball is played — creates urgency.
Rotate server and runner roles every 5 minutes.
coachingPoints: RECEIVE IN STRIDE: the first touch after receiving in behind must keep the ball moving forward. A touch that stops the ball kills the momentum and allows recovering defenders to catch up.

LOOK UP BEFORE THE FINISH: after receiving and controlling, take one look up to find the goal and the GK position before the final touch. This brief look takes 0.5 seconds and dramatically improves finish quality.
KEEP THE BALL CLOSE BUT NOT TOO CLOSE: when dribbling at speed toward goal, the ball should be 1-2 touches ahead of you — close enough to control but not so close it slows you down.
ANGLE YOUR RUN TO CREATE A SHOOTING LANE: do not run directly at the GK. Run at a slight angle so you have a clear shooting lane to one side of the GK.
RECOVER THE BALL FIRST, THEN FINISH: if the first touch is heavy and the ball runs slightly too far, recover control before shooting rather than rushing a touch-and-hope shot.

THE NEAR POST IS OFTEN OPEN WHEN RUNNING FROM WIDE: a player running from a wide position and angling toward goal often has the near post available — the GK is covering the far post expecting a cross.
ACCELERATION AFTER RECEIVING IS THE KEY SKILL: the moment the ball is received in behind, the player must accelerate immediately. Any hesitation gives the recovering defender time to catch up.
CELEBRATE THE FINISH WHATEVER THE OUTCOME: any shot on target after a run in behind at speed is a success. The technical sequence (run, receive, control, dribble, finish) is the achievement.
commonMistakes: First touch too heavy, causing the ball to run away and the player to lose control
Looking only at the ball and never at the goal or GK during the approach
Running directly at the GK rather than at an angle that creates a shooting lane
Hesitating after receiving, allowing the recovering defender to catch up
Poor finish because no look-up before the final shot
corrections: Heavy first touch: reduce server pace by 30 percent so the ball arrives more slowly and a softer first touch is easier
Never looking at goal: add a mandatory look-up checkpoint (a cone at 10m from goal) where the player must call LEFT or RIGHT indicating which side of the GK they will finish
Running at GK: place a cone 3m to the right of the goal centre as the target for the approach run — player must reach this cone position before shooting
Hesitating: add the recovering defender from Phase 3 immediately in Phase 2 to create urgency from the start
Poor finish from no look: connect the look-up checkpoint to the finish — only when the player has done the look-up at 10m can they attempt the shot
progressions: Double penetration: player receives in behind, takes the ball to the byline, and delivers a cutback to a second arriving player
Combine with the movement drill: player makes the actual run in behind from a tactical set-up before receiving the ball and dribbling at speed
Reduce recovering defender start position to 2m behind — maximum urgency and the player must take 2 touches maximum before shooting
regressions: Remove the recovering defender entirely for Phase 1 and 2
Increase the serve to a rolling ball along the ground (easier to receive in stride than a lofted ball)
Start the player only 15m from goal to reduce the dribbling distance before adding the full 30m
matchApplication: Receiving a pass in behind and carrying it at speed toward goal is one of the most direct and most effective forms of penetration in football. It requires the combination of sprinting athleticism, a good first touch in stride, the composure to look up, and the technical finishing quality to convert. Developing this complete sequence at U11, when the defensive line is still forming and runs in behind are highly effective, gives players a direct attacking weapon that remains valuable at every level.
coachTips: The first touch in stride is the most technically demanding element. Be patient with it and celebrate every rep that achieves a clean in-stride receive.
The look-up checkpoint is the most important coaching habit to build. Enforce it from Phase 1.
Connect to the movement off the ball drill: the run in behind is the same movement. This drill is what happens after the pass is played.
Track conversion rates across phases (how many shots on target per run). Improvement across a session is measurable.
This drill pairs perfectly with the 1v1 to goal drill — alternate between the two across the session for high-intensity finishing volume.
diagramDescription: 30m channel with goal at far end. Defensive line cone shown at 15m. Player A shown starting at the beginning. Server shown at the side at 15m. Run arrow from A past the cone (beyond the defensive line). Ball played in behind arrow from server to the space at 18-20m. Player A shown receiving in stride (first touch forward). Dribble arrows at speed toward goal. LOOK UP checkpoint cone shown at 10m from goal. GK shown in goal. Finish arrows shown going to corners. Phase 3 shown with recovering defender 5m behind.

title: Creating Width — The Wide Overload
objective: Develop the tactical principle of creating a deliberate numerical overload in wide areas to produce high-quality crossing opportunities.
learningOutcomes: Players understand how to create a 2v1 or 3v2 advantage in wide areas through overlapping and underlapping runs
Players develop the decision-making between crossing early, dribbling inside, or combining
Players build the wide overload as a reliable attacking system from different starting positions
Teams develop coordinated wide attacking patterns that consistently create quality crossing opportunities
organisation: Full pitch. Attacking team creates width overloads from both flanks. 3-4 attackers combine in wide areas against 2 defenders to create crossing opportunities. The wide overload can come from an overlapping full-back, an underlapping midfielder, or a combination of both.
instructions: WIDE OVERLOAD SETUP (left flank example): Winger (W) has ball on the left, 25m from goal. Left back (LB) overlaps outside W. Central midfielder (CM) takes a third-man run inside W.
W has three options: cross early (if first-time delivery is available), play to overlapping LB who crosses, or play to underlapping CM who drives inside for a shot.
The choice is made by reading the defensive position: both defenders tracking W means LB is free for the overlap. One defender tracking LB means CM is free inside.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): right flank only. Walk-through of all three options. No defenders.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): 2 defenders on the flank. W must read the defensive positions and choose the correct option.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): both flanks active simultaneously. Defenders on both sides. Team coordinates wide overloads from both flanks within the same possession.
coachingPoints: THE WIDE OVERLOAD CREATES AN IMPOSSIBLE DEFENSIVE CHOICE: 3 attackers against 2 defenders in a wide area means one attacker is always free. The question is which one — and reading the answer before the ball arrives is the skill.

THE OVERLAP AND UNDERLAP WORK TOGETHER: the overlapping LB pulls one defender wide. The underlapping CM exploits the space created centrally. Both must run simultaneously so the defender cannot cover both.
CROSS EARLY IF THE LANE IS OPEN: the most dangerous moment to cross is the first available moment. A cross played before the defence has organised is much more dangerous than one played after.

THE WIDE PLAYER IS THE DECISION-MAKER: W reads both defenders. If both track the wide options (LB and W themselves), W drives inside with the ball. If defenders split, W plays to whichever option is free.
TIMING OF THE SUPPORTING RUNS: LB and CM must begin their runs before W has the ball, not after. Their movement creates the space that W reads. Late runs give defenders time to position.

QUALITY OF THE DELIVERY DETERMINES THE CHANCE: all the creative work in creating the overload is wasted if the delivery is poor. The player who crosses must deliver with accuracy and at a height that suits the runners.

WIDE OVERLOADS WORK BETTER FROM WIDE POSITIONS: the overload is most effective when W starts genuinely wide, stretching the defensive line. A winger who drifts central removes the space for the overlap.
COMMUNICATE THE CHOSEN OPTION: W should call OVERLAP (LB gets it) or INSIDE (CM gets it) to let teammates know which option is being used.
commonMistakes: W not reading which defender is tracking which runner, choosing randomly rather than reading
LB and CM starting their runs after W has the ball rather than before
W crossing too late after holding the ball waiting for a perfect moment
W, LB, and CM all going to the same area, removing the overload advantage
No call from W to let LB and CM know which option is being selected
corrections: Not reading defenders: freeze Phase 2 after W receives the ball and ask W to describe what each defender is doing. This builds the reading habit
Late runs: require LB and CM to start their runs when W receives the ball from the build-up pass, not when W is ready to cross
Crossing too late: add a 3-second cross rule — W must deliver within 3 seconds of receiving in the wide area
All going same area: specifically assign LB (outside) and CM (inside) before Phase 2 and enforce the separation
No call: make the call mandatory before Phase 2 begins. Any option taken without a call is penalised
progressions: Add a second defender pair to make it 3v3 in the wide area, requiring higher quality decision-making
Combine both flanks in Phase 3 with a midfielder distributing between the two wide areas based on which overload is better positioned
Add a clinical finishing requirement: the crossed ball must be converted by a specific runner (near post, far post, edge of box) for the overload to score full points
regressions: Reduce to 2 attackers vs 1 defender (simple 2v1 overload) while the coordination between W and one runner is established
Use passive defenders in Phase 2 who hold their position without pressing until Phase 3
Remove the decision-making element (always play to the same option) for Phase 1 while the pattern is established
matchApplication: The wide overload is one of the most consistent methods for creating high-quality crossing opportunities because it deliberately creates a numerical advantage in the area where the cross is delivered from. Teams that consistently produce wide overloads through overlapping and underlapping runs create more crosses in less-pressured positions, leading to higher-quality deliveries and better scoring opportunities from crosses.
coachTips: Walk through the three options explicitly before Phase 1 with all attackers understanding each role.
The reading skill is the most sophisticated element — use freeze-and-ask frequently in Phase 2.
Film Phase 3 from above to see whether the overload is being created correctly on both flanks.
Connect to professional teams with high cross volumes — explain that their crossing quality comes from deliberately creating wide overloads rather than simply having a wide player cross.
This drill pairs naturally with the defending crosses drills — run both in the same session.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. Left flank shown: Winger (W) shown with ball 25m from goal. LB shown making overlap run outside W (outside arrow). CM shown making underlap run inside W (inside arrow). 2 DEFENDERS shown: BOTH TRACKING W/LB (W plays to CM inside — inside arrow highlighted). ONE TRACKING LB (W plays to LB overlap — overlap arrow highlighted). CROSS EARLY option shown if defensive shape is open immediately. Call options shown: OVERLAP and INSIDE. Phase 3 shown with both flanks active simultaneously.

title: Transition to Attack — Quick Release Patterns
objective: Develop the quick release patterns used in the first 3 seconds after winning the ball to maximise the counter-attack opportunity before the defence can reorganise.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the automatic habit of looking forward immediately after winning the ball
Players learn two specific quick-release patterns for different areas of the pitch
Players build the team coordination needed to support the ball-winner instantly
Players connect the winning of the ball to the counter-attack as one continuous action
organisation: Full pitch. Team A defends in small numbers and wins balls in different areas. The moment they win the ball, they execute one of two quick-release patterns based on where the ball was won. Team B applies pressure to create urgency.
instructions: TWO QUICK-RELEASE PATTERNS:

PATTERN 1 — WIN IN DEFENSIVE THIRD: ball-winner immediately plays forward to the highest attacker. The attacker holds up or drives at goal. Two supporting players sprint into wide channels. Maximum 2 touches from the ball-winner.

PATTERN 2 — WIN IN MIDFIELD: ball-winner drives forward 5m carrying the ball. Two attackers sprint in behind the opposition line simultaneously. Ball-winner plays in behind to whichever attacker is in the better position.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): Team B is passive. Team A practices both patterns from marked starting positions for each pattern.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): Team B applies pressure at 70 percent. Team A must win the ball (from a loose pass by Team B) and execute the correct pattern.
PHASE 3 (6 minutes): fully live. Team B attacks. Team A defends and counter-attacks on every ball they win.
SCORING: goal from Pattern 1 = 3 points. Goal from Pattern 2 = 3 points. Goal from unplanned play = 1 point.
coachingPoints: LOOK FORWARD FIRST — ALWAYS: the most common error after winning the ball is looking sideways or backward. The first look must always be forward. Is there a forward option? Play it immediately.

THE PATTERN IS DECIDED BY WHERE THE BALL IS WON: Pattern 1 (long ball in behind) when won deep. Pattern 2 (carry and play in behind) when won in midfield. Knowing this before the ball is won means no decision is needed after.

2 TOUCHES MAXIMUM FROM THE BALL-WINNER IN PATTERN 1: the forward pass must leave the ball-winner within 2 touches. More touches and the space that existed when the ball was won has already been closed.

SUPPORTING RUNNERS START BEFORE THE BALL IS WON: the wide runners in Pattern 1 and the in-behind runners in Pattern 2 must begin their movement the instant they see the ball about to be won. Starting after the ball is won is too late.
COMMIT TO THE PATTERN: a ball-winner who hesitates between Pattern 1 and Pattern 2 often ends up using neither. Know which pattern to use before the ball is won and commit to it instantly.

THE FIRST 3 SECONDS ARE THE MOST VALUABLE: in the 3 seconds after the ball is won, the opposition is in their attacking shape and cannot defend. After 3 seconds they begin recovering. Use the 3 seconds.

THE SUPPORT RUNNER MUST MAKE THE PASS EASY: the supporting players in both patterns must position themselves so the ball-winner has an obvious, easy forward pass. Poor support positioning forces the ball-winner to recycle.
CELEBRATE EVERY SUCCESSFUL PATTERN: a goal scored from an executed quick-release pattern is a tactical achievement worth celebrating specifically and loudly.
commonMistakes: Ball-winner looking sideways or backward as the first action after winning the ball
Wrong pattern used for the area of the pitch where the ball was won
Supporting runners starting their movement after the ball is won rather than before
Ball-winner taking more than 2 touches in Pattern 1, allowing the defence to recover
No urgency — treating the ball win as normal possession rather than a counter-attack opportunity
corrections: Wrong first look: require a verbal FORWARD call from every ball-winner as their first action — this forces the forward look
Wrong pattern: coach calls the area (DEEP or MIDFIELD) immediately before the ball is won so the pattern is confirmed before the action
Late runners: add a running start point — runners must be already moving when the coach calls the area in Phase 1
More than 2 touches: enforce strictly with possession reset to Team B if 3 or more touches are used by the ball-winner in Pattern 1
No urgency: score only goals from the patterns (3 points) and give only 1 point for any other goal — makes the pattern execution economically important
progressions: Reduce reaction time to 2 seconds: the forward pass must leave the ball-winner within 2 seconds of winning the ball
Add a third pattern (won wide: drive to the byline and deliver a first-time cross) for advanced groups
Full match with both patterns as the teams explicit counter-attack system
regressions: Use Pattern 1 only for the full session before introducing Pattern 2
Allow the ball-winner 4 touches in Pattern 1 while the forward-look habit is established
Remove the live ball-winning element and start Phase 1 with the ball already in the ball-winners hands to focus purely on the pattern execution
matchApplication: Quick-release patterns after winning the ball are the difference between a team that consistently converts defensive actions into attacking opportunities and a team that wins the ball and then loses momentum by playing safely. The specific patterns in this drill are simple enough to execute automatically under pressure but sophisticated enough to create genuine goal-scoring opportunities. At U12, when players are beginning to understand tactical roles, these two patterns give the team a shared language for what to do immediately after winning the ball.
coachTips: The pattern naming (Pattern 1 and Pattern 2) gives the team a shared tactical language that can be referenced throughout the season.
The first look coaching point must be connected to a physical habit — the player physically turning their head forward the instant they win the ball.
Film Phase 3 from the side. The timing of the forward pass from the ball-winner and the supporting runners is clearly visible from the side.
Score the patterns specifically and loudly. When a Pattern 1 goal is scored, say PATTERN 1 — THREE POINTS before the celebration.
Once established, these patterns should be referenced every time a ball is won in training or matches.
diagramDescription: Full pitch. TWO PATTERNS shown: PATTERN 1 (win deep) — ball-winner shown receiving loose ball in defensive third, 2-touch pass shown going forward to highest attacker (long pass arrow), two wide runners sprinting into channels (width arrows), attacker shown holding up or driving at goal. PATTERN 2 (win midfield) — ball-winner receiving in midfield, 5m carry arrow, two in-behind runners shown (dashed arrows behind defensive line), ball-winner shown choosing the better-positioned runner. 3-SECOND WINDOW shown as timer on both patterns. Scoring shown: 3 points per pattern goal.

title: GK Communication — Organising Under Pressure
objective: Develop the GKs ability to communicate effectively during active defending situations — not just at set pieces but throughout the run of play.
learningOutcomes: GK communicates effectively to defenders during open-play defensive situations
GK develops the vocabulary for in-play communication beyond set piece calls
GK builds the confidence to direct experienced defenders during the game
GK learns when communication is most critical and what information to prioritise
organisation: Full defensive third. Attacking team attacks continuously. GK must communicate with defenders throughout every attacking sequence. Specific in-play communication vocabulary is introduced and practiced.
instructions: IN-PLAY COMMUNICATION VOCABULARY:
MAN ON: a defender on the ball is about to be pressed from behind. Call early — 0.5 seconds before the press arrives.
TIME: a defender on the ball has space and time. Gives them confidence to look up and play forward.
PUSH UP: the defensive line should step up to compress space or catch an attacker offside.
DROP: the defensive line should retreat deeper.
SWITCH: tell a defender to move the ball to the other side immediately.
NAME and TRACK: call a defenders name followed by TRACK to alert them to a runner they may not have seen.
PHASE 1 (6 minutes): attackers move at walking pace. GK practices the vocabulary specifically — one call per action. Coach evaluates accuracy and timing.
PHASE 2 (7 minutes): attackers at 70 percent. GK must communicate continuously throughout each attack.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): fully live. GK communicates throughout. Coach counts the number of correct GK calls in each attacking sequence.
coachingPoints: THE GK CAN SEE EVERYTHING: the GK is the only player who can see the entire game in front of them. This unique position makes them the most valuable communicator on the pitch — if they use the information they have.

EARLY IS EVERYTHING: a MAN ON call that comes 1 second before the press arrives is enormously valuable. A MAN ON call that comes as the press arrives is too late. The GK must call before the problem, not as it happens.
ONE CLEAR CALL: calling three things at once communicates nothing. Choose the single most important piece of information and call it clearly. PUSH UP is clear. PUSH UP, TIME, MAN ON is noise.
VOLUME CARRIES OVER MATCH NOISE: in a quiet training session, a quiet call is sufficient. In a match, the call must carry over the crowd, the players, and the wind. Practice at match volume.
NAME THE PLAYER: PUSH UP is useful. JAMES, PUSH UP is much more useful. Using the defenders name ensures the right player responds.

TIME GIVES CONFIDENCE: the TIME call is underused by most GKs. Telling a defender they have time gives them the confidence to look up and play well. This is one of the most positive contributions a GK can make.

COMMUNICATE BETWEEN ATTACKS TOO: in the 5 seconds between defending one attack and organising for the next, the GK should communicate the defensive shape adjustment needed before the next attack begins.

BUILD TRUST THROUGH CONSISTENCY: defenders learn to trust a GKs communication only when it is consistently accurate. A GK who calls MAN ON accurately every time earns the trust that makes their communication decisive.
commonMistakes: Calling too late — information arriving after the situation has already developed
Calling too many things at once, producing noise rather than communication
Not using defenders names, making calls uncertain about who should respond
Communication stopping when the game is moving quickly — exactly when it is most needed
GK leaving their line during communication rather than maintaining position and calling from it
corrections: Too late: practice the MAN ON call specifically — coach approaches a defender from behind in slow motion and GK must call MAN ON before the coach is within 2m
Too many calls: limit the GK to one call per 5-second period in Phase 1. Quality over quantity.
No names: require every call to include a name. MAN ON without a name does not count in Phase 1
Communication stopping when busy: coach specifically counts calls in Phase 2 — the count creates an explicit motivation to keep communicating
Leaving line while calling: GK is penalised 1 point whenever they move more than 3m from their line while communicating
progressions: Add an opposition pressing trigger: whenever the attacker presses a defender, the GK must choose between MAN ON (warn the defender) or SWITCH (redirect the ball away from the press). Tests decision-making.
Full match with communication scoring: GK earns 1 team point for every accurate in-play call. Track total across the match.
Double GK: two GKs rotate every 5 minutes, comparing their communication frequency and accuracy
regressions: Reduce attacking pressure to passive (attackers move but do not press) while the vocabulary is established
Reduce vocabulary to MAN ON and TIME only for the first session before adding the others
Allow GK extended time to call by slowing the attackers to 50 percent throughout Phase 2
matchApplication: In-play GK communication is one of the most underdeveloped areas of youth goalkeeping. Most GKs are coached to communicate at set pieces but not during open play, where the communication is equally valuable. A GK who communicates consistently and accurately throughout every defensive sequence organises their defensive unit more effectively than a GK who stays silent, and this organisation translates directly to fewer goals conceded. This drill develops the specific in-play vocabulary and habits that make communication automatic.
coachTips: The communication count in Phase 2 (how many correct calls per attacking sequence) is the most important measurement in this session.
Connect the TIME call to the defenders experience: ask a defender after the session whether hearing TIME changed how they played the ball. The answer is almost always yes.
Film from behind the goal — the GKs body position, the timing of calls, and the defenders responses are all visible from this angle.
Build a vocabulary chart and display it where defenders can see it before and during sessions.
Once established, evaluate the GKs in-play communication after every training match across the season.
diagramDescription: Full defensive third with GK in goal. SIX COMMUNICATION CALLS shown with situations: MAN ON (attacker approaching defender from behind, GK shown calling before the press arrives), TIME (defender with space, GK calling TIME), PUSH UP (defensive line stepping forward, GK calling with arm sweep), DROP (defensive line retreating, GK calling with arm sweep back), SWITCH (ball played wide, GK calling SWITCH with arm pointing), NAME + TRACK (defender named, runner highlighted, GK pointing). Communication count shown in Phase 3. Volume indicator showing match-level volume required.

title: Balance Under Pressure — Contact Stability
objective: Develop the balance and stability required to maintain technical quality when receiving physical contact from opponents during match play.
learningOutcomes: Players maintain balance and control when receiving shoulder-to-shoulder contact
Players develop the core stability that allows technical actions under physical challenge
Players build the confidence to compete physically while maintaining technical quality
Players connect balance training to the specific contested situations they face in matches
organisation: Pairs work in small contact zones. One player has the ball and performs technical actions while the partner provides legal shoulder-to-shoulder contact. The ball-carrier must complete the technical action despite the contact.
instructions: EXERCISE 1 — PASSING UNDER CONTACT (4 minutes): Player A dribbles in the zone. Player B applies legal shoulder contact from the side. Player A must pass accurately to a target cone despite the contact. Swap every 60 seconds.

EXERCISE 2 — RECEIVING UNDER CONTACT (4 minutes): server plays balls to Player A. Player B applies shoulder contact as the ball arrives. Player A must control and turn despite the contact. Swap every 60 seconds.

EXERCISE 3 — SHIELDING AND BALANCE (5 minutes): Player A shields the ball against Player B who tries to win it using legal shoulder contact and body pressure. Player A must maintain shielding position and balance for 5 seconds before releasing.

EXERCISE 4 — CONTESTED HEADING (5 minutes): server delivers balls at head height. Both players compete for the header with legal shoulder contact. The player who wins the header must direct it accurately to a target zone.
Throughout: legal contact only. No pushing from behind, no arm contact, no tripping. Shoulder and upper arm contact only.
coachingPoints: BALANCE IS A SKILL UNDER PRESSURE: balance in a static training exercise and balance under physical contact are different skills. The contact destabilises the centre of gravity and requires specific neuromuscular responses that must be practiced.

THE WIDE BASE IS YOUR FOUNDATION: when contact is expected, widen the foot stance to lower the centre of gravity. A narrow stance is easily destabilised. A wide stance is much more resistant to shoulder contact.

LEAN INTO THE CONTACT SLIGHTLY: a player who leans slightly toward the contact meets it with their whole body rather than being knocked sideways by it. Leaning away from contact transfers the force directly to the off-balance position.

TECHNICAL QUALITY MUST SURVIVE CONTACT: the key outcome of this drill is that passes are accurate, first touches are controlled, and headers are directed — all despite contact. If contact reduces technical quality to zero, the player needs more contact stability training.
LEGAL CONTACT IS NOT ASSAULT: shoulder-to-shoulder contact is completely legal in football. Building confidence and comfort with legal contact is an important part of physical development.

BREATHE THROUGH CONTACT: holding the breath tenses the muscles and makes balance harder. Continuing to breathe normally during physical contact maintains the muscular relaxation needed for good balance.

ANTICIPATE THE CONTACT: players who see contact coming and prepare for it are much more stable than players who are surprised by it. Learning to read when contact is about to arrive is a contact-stability skill.
CONNECT TO MATCH SITUATIONS: every contested header, every shoulder duel, every shielding action in a match requires exactly the balance and stability practiced in this drill.
commonMistakes: Technical quality completely breaking down under contact — passes going wide, first touches heavy
Not widening the stance when contact is expected, remaining in an easily destabilised position
Leaning away from contact rather than meeting it, making the destabilisation worse
Becoming tense under contact, gripping the ball too tightly or stiffening the joints
Illegal contact from the pressuring player, turning the balance drill into a wrestling match
corrections: Technical breakdown: reduce contact to 50 percent intensity while technical quality is rebuilt before gradually increasing
Narrow stance: place foot markers showing minimum foot width. Both feet must stay outside the markers throughout
Leaning away: specifically coach the lean-toward technique in isolation (partner pushes gently, player leans into it) before adding technical demands
Tensing: ask the player to breathe out loudly at the moment of contact — this forces relaxation and prevents tensing
Illegal contact: immediately and firmly correct any contact from behind, arms, or hands. Legal contact only is non-negotiable
progressions: Add a competitive element to Exercise 3: the player who wins more 5-second shields in a 3-minute period wins the round
Increase contact intensity gradually across sessions as contact stability improves
Add a passing target: shielded player must release to a specific target cone after 5 seconds — completing a technical action from the shield position
regressions: Reduce contact to light touch (partner barely touches shoulder) rather than genuine contact for the first session
Remove Exercise 4 (competitive heading) while heading-under-contact confidence is established
Use Exercise 1 only for the first session before adding the more complex exercises
matchApplication: Balance under physical contact is one of the most match-specific physical qualities in football and one of the most neglected in training. Technical quality that exists in isolation often disappears under physical contact in matches because the neuromuscular stability required to perform technical actions while being physically challenged has never been specifically trained. This drill bridges the gap between technical training and match conditions by introducing controlled, legal contact as a training variable.
coachTips: Safety and legality must be established completely clearly before any contact exercise begins.
The contact intensity should increase gradually across the session. Start at 30 percent and build to 80 percent.
Pair players of similar size and strength for all exercises to ensure both players can develop confidence.
Connect to specific match situations the players have faced: discuss a time they lost the ball under physical challenge and identify what balance technique might have helped.
This drill works best as a monthly fixture. The contact stability improvements across a season are significant and directly visible in match performance.
diagramDescription: 5x5m contact zone. EXERCISE 1 shown: Player A dribbling with ball, Player B applying shoulder contact from side (legal contact area labeled: shoulder and upper arm only). Pass arrow from A to target cone despite contact. EXERCISE 2 shown: server pass to A, B applying contact as ball arrives, A controlling and turning. EXERCISE 3 shown: A shielding ball from B, low wide stance shown, lean-toward-contact arrow, 5-second timer. EXERCISE 4 shown: heading competition with server lofting ball, both players competing with shoulder contact, direction arrow to target zone.

title: Reaction and Recovery — Multi-Direction Circuits
objective: Develop reaction speed combined with direction-change recovery through circuits that replicate the stop-start multi-direction demands of match play.
learningOutcomes: Players develop faster reaction to directional signals in multiple directions
Players build the recovery speed between directional changes that football requires
Players connect reaction training to the specific multi-direction movements of match play
Players improve their ability to change direction rapidly without losing balance or momentum
equipment: Coloured bibs or cards for signals
pitchSize: Star circuit (5 cones in a star shape per player)
organisation: Individual star circuits. 5 cones placed in a star shape: one central cone, four outer cones at North, South, East, West (3m from centre). Player starts at the central cone. Coach calls a direction. Player sprints to that cone, touches it, and sprints back to centre. Repeat on every call.
instructions: Set up a star circuit for each player: central cone plus 4 outer cones at N, S, E, W (3m from centre).
Player stands on the central cone in the ready position.
Coach calls a direction (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST) at random.
Player sprints to the called cone, touches it with the foot, and sprints back to the central cone.
Coach calls the next direction before the player has fully returned to centre.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): coach calls slowly (3-second gaps). Player focuses on first-step quickness.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): coach calls faster (1.5-second gaps). Player must react while still moving.
PHASE 3 (4 minutes): coach calls OPPOSITE as a special instruction. Player must sprint to the OPPOSITE cone to the one called (North called = sprint South). Tests cognitive speed.
PHASE 4 (6 minutes): pairs compete on adjacent circuits. Same calls. First to touch each cone wins the rep. Track scores.
coachingPoints: THE READY POSITION IS THE RESET POSITION: every time the player returns to the central cone they must touch it and reset to the ready position before the next call. A player who is still moving when the next call comes cannot react as quickly as one who is set.
FIRST STEP IN THE CALLED DIRECTION: the first step must be directly toward the called direction, not a sideways adjustment first. Direct first step to the target.

TOUCH THE CONE WITH THE FOOT NOT THE HAND: touching with the foot requires the player to get low (bending the knee) which is the correct body position for the direction change. Hand touching allows an upright approach.

PHASE 3 OPPOSITE CALL IS COGNITIVE TRAINING: the OPPOSITE call trains the brain to override the automatic motor response and produce a different response. This cognitive training directly improves decision-making speed in matches.
COMPETITION SHARPENS REACTION: racing against a partner in Phase 4 produces faster reaction times than solo practice because competitive motivation activates the nervous system more fully.

EVEN SPACING BETWEEN CONES IS IMPORTANT: if the cones are at different distances, the circuit does not train equal speed in all directions. Check that all 4 outer cones are exactly 3m from the centre.
MAINTAIN QUALITY IN LATER PHASES: as the calls get faster in Phase 2, the temptation is to cut corners or touch cones less precisely. Maintain full touches throughout.
BREATHE BETWEEN CALLS: the star circuit is physically demanding. Learning to breathe quickly and efficiently between each sprint is a physical skill that improves across sessions.
commonMistakes: Players moving from the central cone before the call is made (anticipating)
Touching the cone with the hand rather than the foot, removing the low body position requirement
Not returning fully to the central cone before the next call, making the circuit smaller than designed
Cognitive freezing on the OPPOSITE call — knowing the rule but not being able to execute it under fatigue
Competition in Phase 4 becoming negative when one player consistently wins
corrections: Anticipating: add a penalty for false starts — any movement before the call resets the score to zero
Hand touch: enforce foot-only touching from Phase 1. Any hand touch means the rep is repeated
Not returning to centre: place a physical trigger at the central cone (a small disc that the player must pick up and put down) making the central return explicit
Cognitive freezing on OPPOSITE: slow the call pace in Phase 3 to 3 seconds until the opposite response becomes reliable before speeding up
Competitive imbalance: change partners frequently and track total wins rather than consecutive wins to keep the competition balanced
progressions: Add a colour system: NORTH is always blue, SOUTH is red, EAST is yellow, WEST is green. Coach holds up a colour rather than calling a direction — adds a visual reaction element
Increase outer cone distance to 5m for more advanced players seeking greater sprint distance
Three-direction calls: coach calls two directions in sequence (NORTH then EAST) and player must touch both before returning
regressions: Slow Phase 1 call pace to 4-second gaps for players who need more time to establish the ready position habit
Reduce to 3-point circuit (N, S, Central) before building to the full 5-point star
Remove Phase 3 (OPPOSITE) for the first session while the basic circuit is established
matchApplication: The star circuit trains reaction speed in all four directions simultaneously, replicating the multi-directional nature of match-play reactions more accurately than linear sprint training alone. The OPPOSITE phase specifically trains cognitive reaction speed — the ability to override an automatic motor response and produce a different response — which is directly relevant to the decision-making under pressure that football requires.
coachTips: The ready position return to centre is the most important habit to establish from the very first rep.
The OPPOSITE phase is genuinely difficult and often produces laughter — embrace this energy as a positive sign of cognitive challenge.
Film Phase 4 from above to show players their own reaction time and direction-change quality in the competition format.
Connect to specific match situations: receiving a pass and immediately having to sprint a different direction because a defender appeared.
Track Phase 4 competition scores across sessions. Improvement in reaction competition wins is measurable.
diagramDescription: Star circuit shown: central cone with 4 outer cones at N, S, E, W (3m from centre each). Player shown in ready position at central cone. NORTH CALL shown: first step directly north, sprint arrow to north cone, touch with foot (low body position shown), sprint back to centre. SOUTH CALL shown: same pattern. PHASE 3 OPPOSITE shown: NORTH called (arrow pointing north), player sprinting SOUTH (arrow pointing south) — cognitive override illustrated. PHASE 4 shown: two adjacent circuits with two players racing to the same called cone.

title: Overloads — 3v1 Possession Triangle
objective: Develop the 3v1 positional triangle that gives young players their first experience of numerical advantage in possession.
learningOutcomes: Players experience keeping the ball with a numerical advantage
Players develop the triangle shape that creates three passing options from any position
Players build the habit of supporting the ball-carrier from multiple angles
Players understand that being in a triangle gives the ball-carrier options
organisation: Groups of 4. 3 possession players keep the ball from 1 defender in a 10x10m zone. The triangle of 3 players rotates around the zone. After 2 minutes the defender swaps.
instructions: 3 players form a triangle in the zone. 1 defender in the middle.
The 3 players pass to keep the ball from the defender.
TRIANGLE RULE: the 3 players must always form a triangle — no two players standing in a line.
After 2 minutes: the player who lost the ball (or the most recent to lose it) becomes the new defender.
PHASE 1 (4 minutes): maximum 3 touches. Focus on the triangle shape.
PHASE 2 (4 minutes): maximum 2 touches. Quicker.
PHASE 3 (4 minutes): defender can now tap any player to freeze them for 3 seconds, adding a fun element.
coachingPoints: TRIANGLE GIVES YOU TWO OPTIONS: from any point of the triangle you can pass to either of the other two. You always have a choice.
MOVE TO MAINTAIN THE TRIANGLE: when the ball moves to another player, you must move to maintain the triangle shape around the ball. Stay connected.
THE DEFENDER CANNOT COVER ALL THREE: the defender can only go toward one player at a time. The player furthest from the defender is almost always free.
QUICK PASSES BEAT THE DEFENDER: a defender who is still moving toward one player cannot recover if the ball is already moving to another. Pass before they arrive.
SUPPORT CLOSE BUT NOT TOO CLOSE: triangle players should be 4-5m from the ball, close enough to receive easily but far enough that the defender cannot cover both the ball and the pass.
FACE THE BALL ALWAYS: every player must always face the ball. Turning your back on the ball removes your ability to receive or respond.
CELEBRATE LONG PASSING SEQUENCES: count consecutive passes. 10 passes without the defender touching the ball is brilliant. 20 is world class.
THIS IS HOW ALL GREAT TEAMS PLAY: every great football team uses triangles constantly. Barcelona, Brazil, Spain — all built on exactly this three-player triangle possession.
commonMistakes: Two players standing in a line rather than maintaining the triangle shape
Passes going directly to the defender because the player with the ball panics
Not moving to maintain the triangle when the ball moves
Holding the ball too long, giving the defender time to arrive
Becoming frustrated and stopping when the defender wins the ball
corrections: Line not triangle: physically place the three players in correct triangle positions before every restart
Panic passes to defender: reduce to Phase 1 (3 touches) and slow the session pace until composure is established
Not moving to maintain triangle: call TRIANGLE every time the shape is lost as a reminder to adjust
Holding too long: enforce the 3-touch maximum strictly from Phase 1
Frustration: celebrate every sequence restart with the same energy as a long sequence
progressions: Reduce zone to 8x8m to make the triangle more compact and the passes shorter
Add a second ball: two balls in the zone simultaneously, both being kept from the same defender
Extend to 4v2: a larger triangle of 4 players keeping from 2 defenders in a 12x12m zone
regressions: Increase zone to 14x14m to give more space for longer sequences
Allow 4 touches to reduce the speed demand while the triangle habit is established
Use a passive defender who walks rather than runs for the first 2 minutes
matchApplication: The 3v1 triangle is the most fundamental overload situation in football and the entry point for understanding numerical advantage in possession. Every more sophisticated tactical concept (rondos, tiki-taka, overload situations) builds on the simple experience of keeping the ball from a single defender using a triangle of three players.
coachTips: Keep the atmosphere joyful. Count sequences aloud with the players.
Walk around the zones and celebrate specific correct triangle positioning.
Connect to professional players: show a rondo from a professional pre-training warm-up.
Run this as the opening possession activity for any session — 12 minutes of 3v1 triangles is the perfect activation.
Once established, this becomes a daily warm-up that the players can run themselves without coaching.
diagramDescription: 10x10m zone. 3 possession players (circles) shown in correct triangle formation. 1 defender (triangle) shown in the centre. Two pass option arrows shown from the ball-carrier to both other possession players. TRIANGLE SHAPE label shown. WRONG FORMATION shown in corner (two players in a line). Ball movement arrow with all 3 players adjusting their triangle positions to stay connected to the ball. Pass count shown rising.

title: Recovery Run — Tracking Runners
objective: Develop the specific recovery run used when tracking a striker who has received the ball in behind the defensive line.
learningOutcomes: Players can maintain a recovery run alongside a ball-carrying attacker without fouling
Players develop the angle and pace of recovery to stay goal-side
Players build the physical intensity and persistence of a tracking recovery run
Players understand when to recover and when to attempt a tackle during a tracking run
organisation: Pairs in a 30m channel with goal at the far end. Attacker starts at the top of the channel with ball. Defender starts 3m behind. Both sprint 30m — attacker toward goal, defender tracking alongside. The defender must get goal-side and must not foul.
instructions: Attacker starts at the 0m mark with ball.
Defender starts at the 3m mark (3m behind the attacker).
On GO: both sprint. Attacker drives toward goal (30m away). Defender must track alongside and get goal-side.
The defender must stay within 1m of the attacker throughout the run.
GOAL-SIDE is achieved when the defender is between the attacker and the goal centre.
At 20m from goal: defender may attempt a legal challenge if they are goal-side.
PHASE 1 (5 minutes): attacker jogs at 70 percent. Defender focuses on getting alongside and goal-side.
PHASE 2 (6 minutes): attacker sprints at full pace. Genuine race.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): attacker can change direction at 15m (left or right). Defender must stay alongside.
coachingPoints: THE GOAL-SIDE POSITION IS THE TARGET OF THE RUN: the recovery run is not about catching the attacker — it is about getting between the attacker and the centre of the goal. Even a recovery run that does not result in a tackle is successful if it achieves the goal-side position.

SPRINT THE SHORTEST PATH TO GOAL-SIDE: the defender should not sprint directly alongside the attacker if the goal-side position is available by a shorter diagonal route. Sprint diagonally to cut off the path to goal.
STAY WITHIN 1M: a defender more than 1m away from the attacker cannot affect the shot or tackle effectively. Within 1m, the presence alone changes the attackers options.

DO NOT DIVE IN UNTIL GOAL-SIDE: a tackle attempted while the defender is not yet goal-side puts the ball behind them, making the attacker more dangerous. Wait until goal-side before any tackle attempt.
USE LEGAL ARM CONTACT: during the sprint, slight shoulder contact (not pushing) is legal and can slow the attacker slightly while helping the defender maintain proximity.
TRACK THE BALL NOT THE BODY: if the attacker uses a change of direction at 15m, the defender must track the ball, not the attacker body. The body fakes but the ball does not.
COMMUNICATE WITH THE GK: during the tracking run, call to the GK whether you are getting goal-side (COVERING) or need help (KEEPER). This coordination prevents confusion.
EVERY METRE MATTERS: even a defender who cannot fully catch an attacker changes the finish quality by being close. Get as close as possible and maintain it.
commonMistakes: Sprinting alongside rather than diagonally to cut off the goal-side path
Diving in before reaching the goal-side position, pushing the ball behind them
Losing the tracking run completely by 15m and giving up
Not communicating with the GK about whether coverage is established
Being caught by the change of direction in Phase 3 because watching the body not the ball
corrections: Running alongside rather than diagonal: place a goal-side target cone showing where the defender should aim. Reach the target cone first.
Diving in before goal-side: enforce the no-tackle rule until the defender passes a goal-side marker at 20m from goal
Giving up: make the tracking run the only scoring metric. Any defender who stays within 1m of the attacker for the full run earns a point regardless of the tackle
No GK communication: require a verbal COVERING or KEEPER call at the 20m mark of every run
Change of direction: practice specifically against the Phase 3 change — attacker changes direction at 15m only and defender practices the ball-tracking adjustment for 3 minutes before Phase 3 begins
progressions: Attacker starts level with defender (no head start) for the most advanced groups
Add a crossing option: at 20m the attacker can choose to shoot or lay off to a wide player, requiring the defender to track the ball to the secondary option
Full game with recovery run bonus: any tackle made by a recovering defender earns 3 points for the defending team
regressions: Increase head start to 6m and reduce to Phase 1 only for the first session
Use walking pace for the first 3 minutes before any jogging pace
Remove Phase 3 (direction change) for the first two sessions
matchApplication: The tracking recovery run is one of the most physically and technically demanding individual defensive actions in football. It requires maximum sprint speed, correct diagonal angle to achieve the goal-side position, and the discipline not to dive in until the position is correct. Defenders who master the tracking recovery run prevent goals that would otherwise be certain, making it one of the highest-value individual defensive skills to develop.
coachTips: The goal-side angle rather than the parallel run is the most important technical correction in this drill.
Film Phase 2 from the side — the relative position of defender and attacker and the moment goal-side is achieved are clearly visible from the side.
Connect to specific match situations: discuss a recent goal where a recovery run either saved a certain goal or arrived too late.
Pair players of similar sprint speed so both get genuine challenge from Phase 2.
This drill is physically demanding. Adequate recovery between reps (30-45 seconds minimum) is essential for quality.
diagramDescription: 30m channel with goal at far end. Attacker (circle) shown at 0m with ball. Defender (triangle) shown at 3m behind. Sprint arrows shown: ATTACKER sprinting toward goal, DEFENDER shown sprinting diagonally (not alongside) to cut off the goal-side path. Goal-side target shown: defender between attacker and goal centre. 1m proximity marker shown. Phase 3 change of direction shown: attacker changing left at 15m, defender tracking the BALL (not the body) to follow. COVERING or KEEPER call shown at 20m from goal.

title: Set Pieces U17 Attacking Corner — Runner Patterns
objective: Develop coordinated U17-level attacking corner routines with runner timing, signal systems, and multiple variations.
learningOutcomes: Players execute a primary corner routine with correct runner timing
Players develop a secondary variation that exploits teams defending the primary routine
Players develop the signal system that communicates which routine is in use
GK and defenders develop counter-responses to the team own corner analysis
organisation: Full penalty area. Two corner routines practiced from both corners. Primary and secondary variations. Full defensive unit provides opposition. Signal system established.
instructions: ROUTINE A (PRIMARY): near-post flick-on target runs from the far side of the box. Far-post runner arrives late. Edge player at 14m. Signal: corner taker waves arm before approaching.

ROUTINE B (SECONDARY — exploits teams who press near post): winger delivers to a dummy runner who checks away from the near post, and the far post runner attacks the space the dummy movement created. Signal: corner taker bounces ball once before approaching.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): both routines from the right corner with passive defenders.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): both routines from both corners with active defenders.
PHASE 3 (7 minutes): corner taker chooses routine based on how the defensive unit has set up. Defenders try to recognise which routine is coming from the signal.
GK communicates throughout all phases.
coachingPoints: SIGNAL BEFORE APPROACH: the signal must be clearly visible to all runners before the corner taker begins their approach run. Signal, then approach, then delivery.
SECONDARY ROUTINE EXPLOITS THE DEFENCE OF THE PRIMARY: the secondary routine only works if teams have learned to defend the primary. Introduce the secondary after the primary is established.
RUNNER TIMING IS ALWAYS THE SAME WITHIN EACH ROUTINE: the consistency of runner timing is what makes routines reliable. Every rep of Routine A has identical runner timing.
THE DUMMY MOVEMENT MUST BE CONVINCING: in Routine B, the near-post check away must look identical to the Routine A near-post run until the last moment.

CORNER TAKER READS THE DEFENSIVE SHAPE IN PHASE 3: in Phase 3, the taker sees the defensive shape and selects the routine that exploits it. Both routines should be chosen roughly equally across Phase 3.
GK COMMUNICATION IS NON-NEGOTIABLE THROUGHOUT: KEEPER or AWAY on every delivery. No exceptions.
PRACTICE FROM BOTH CORNERS EQUALLY: routines look different from the left and right corners. Both must be practiced to equal standard.
FIRST-TIME CLEARANCE IS THE DEFENSIVE TARGET: defenders must practice winning and clearing on the first contact. A controlled clearance to the edge player is a failed defensive set piece.
commonMistakes: Signal not visible to all runners before the approach
Dummy movement in Routine B not convincing
Runner timing inconsistent between reps of the same routine
Corner taker not reading the defensive shape in Phase 3
Only practicing Routine A and never establishing Routine B
corrections: Signal not visible: require all runners to confirm they have seen the signal by raising a hand before the taker approaches
Weak dummy: video the dummy movement and compare it to the near-post run in Routine A — they must look identical
Inconsistent timing: assign a count (taker takes first step is count 1, runners begin at count 2) so timing is standardised
Not reading shape in Phase 3: pause before every Phase 3 corner and ask the taker what they see in the defensive shape and which routine it favours
Only Routine A: introduce Routine B before Phase 2 and require at least 4 reps of Routine B in Phase 2
progressions: Add Routine C: a short corner that combines with Routines A and B as a third option
Full match with corners awarded naturally, requiring the signal system and routine selection to work under match conditions
Analyse the opposition defensive corner shape from match footage and design a routine that exploits their specific weakness
regressions: Use passive defenders only throughout Phase 1 and Phase 2 while both routines are established
Increase signal visibility by using hand signals and calling the signal verbally simultaneously
Practice one routine from one corner only for the first session before adding the second routine and second corner
matchApplication: Advanced corner routines at U17 develop the set piece sophistication that directly translates to goals scored and matches won. Two coordinated routines with a signal system, practiced to a standard where runners are consistent and the delivery is accurate, give any team a genuine attacking weapon that cannot be easily defended because the opposition does not know which routine is coming.
coachTips: Walk through both routines explicitly with all players before any live practice begins.
The signal system is the most important element to establish cleanly. Take 5 minutes exclusively on signals before any delivery.
Film from behind the goal in Phase 3 to show the team their own corner delivery and runner patterns from the defenders perspective.
Connect to specific corner opportunities from your teams recent matches.
Run this session monthly. Routine quality and runner timing consistency improve significantly with regular practice.
diagramDescription: Full penalty area with GK. ROUTINE A signal shown (arm wave). Runner pattern: near-post runner from far side of box (late arrival run), far-post runner arriving at far post, edge player at 14m. ROUTINE B signal shown (ball bounce). Dummy runner shown making near-post movement then checking away (dummy arrow), far-post runner attacking the vacated near-post space. Both signal gestures shown clearly. Phase 3 corner taker shown reading defensive shape with two thought-bubble options (A or B). GK communication shown.

title: Tiki-Taka Warm-Up — The Daily Ritual
objective: Establish the tiki-taka passing warm-up as a daily ritual that begins every training session with high-quality ball movement, player connection, and the joy of passing football.
learningOutcomes: Players develop the automatic tiki-taka passing habits through daily repetition
Players build the team connection and rhythm that comes from shared passing ritual
Players develop the discipline of maximum 2 touches and constant movement through consistent practice
Players experience how a daily passing ritual elevates the quality of everything that follows in the session
organisation: A deliberately simple and consistent 12-minute warm-up that is run the same way every single training session. The consistency is the point. The ritual of doing this every session builds the collective habits that make tiki-taka automatic.
instructions: SAME STRUCTURE EVERY SESSION:
MINUTE 1-3: 4-touch maximum. Players pass freely within the 10x10m square with no pressure. Focus: soft first touch, head up before passing, move after every pass.
MINUTE 4-6: 3-touch maximum. Slightly faster. Players begin to find the rhythm.
MINUTE 7-9: 2-touch maximum. Match pace. Any player who takes 3 touches does 5 toe-taps immediately and continues.
MINUTE 10-12: 1v1 rondo introduced — one player in the centre presses while the others keep possession on 2 touches. Rotate every 90 seconds.
CONSTANT THROUGHOUT: every player must be moving before every pass. Nobody stands still. The square must always be occupied in a diamond or triangle shape.
The coach says nothing during this warm-up except for touch count violations. The players run it themselves.
coachingPoints: THE RITUAL IS THE COACHING: the value of this warm-up is not in any single session — it is in the cumulative effect of doing it the same way every day. After 20 sessions, the habits are automatic. After 50, they are part of the team identity.

MOVE BEFORE THE BALL ARRIVES: every player must already be moving to their next position before the ball leaves the passers foot. Waiting for the ball to arrive and then deciding where to go is too slow.

SOFT FIRST TOUCH IS THE FOUNDATION: the first touch must always control the ball and set up an immediate second touch. A first touch that requires an extra controlling action has already used up a touch.
HEAD UP BEFORE THE PASS: look at where you are passing before the ball is at your feet. The pass decision is made before the ball arrives. Receiving and then looking takes too long.

THE DIAMOND IS BETTER THAN THE SQUARE: the best possession shape in a 10x10m square is a diamond — one player at each point — because it gives every ball-carrier two clear passing options at different angles.
2-TOUCH IS THE STANDARD: at U14, 2-touch should feel natural and comfortable. If it feels rushed, the first touch is not good enough. Fix the first touch.
SILENCE FROM THE COACH IS INTENTIONAL: the players run this warm-up. The coach watches, evaluates, and speaks only to correct touch count violations. This develops player ownership.
THIS IS WHAT BARCELONA DO EVERY DAY: the daily rondo warm-up was a cornerstone of Barcelona training under Guardiola. Every great passing team has a daily possession ritual. This is yours.
commonMistakes: Players stopping moving after passing and waiting for the ball to return
First touch taking the ball out of playing range, requiring an extra touch
No passing options available because players have not moved to create angles
Touch count violations increasing as the 2-touch phase begins
The warm-up losing energy because it feels routine rather than purposeful
corrections: Stopping after passing: any player who stands still for more than 3 seconds is given 5 toe-taps immediately by the coach
Heavy first touch: the 3-touch maximum phase exists to address this — if the first touch is poor the second sets it up and the third passes
No passing options: the coach calls DIAMOND once to remind the group of the shape. After that, silence.
Touch violations increasing: slow back to 3-touch briefly for 30 seconds before returning to 2-touch
Losing energy: the coach raises their own energy during the 2-touch and rondo phases — voice tone and enthusiasm from the coach sets the group energy
progressions: Introduce a weekly record: how many consecutive passes in the 2-touch phase without a violation? Track and try to beat it weekly
Add a second ball at minute 7 — two balls in the 10x10m square simultaneously on 2-touch. Ultimate concentration required
End-of-session comparison: how does the quality at the end of the session (after 75 minutes of training) compare to the start? This shows the physical and mental demand of maintaining quality.
regressions: Use 4 touches for the full first 3 sessions before reducing to 3
Remove the 1v1 rondo (minute 10-12) for the first 5 sessions while the group possession habit is established
Increase to a 12x12m square to give more space during the establishment phase
matchApplication: The daily tiki-taka ritual is the single highest-value practice in this entire library when applied consistently across a season. It is not impressive in any individual session — it is transformative across 40 sessions. Teams that begin every session with the same high-quality possession warm-up develop collective habits, team rhythm, and passing quality that teams who do different warm-ups every session never achieve. This is the coaching philosophy embodied in The Streets Meet Tiki-Taka: consistency, quality, joy, and collective rhythm.
coachTips: Commit to running this warm-up the same way every single session for the full season. Resist the temptation to change it.
Your silence during the warm-up (except touch corrections) is the most powerful signal that the players own this. Resist the urge to coach.
Track the record for consecutive passes in the 2-touch phase and make it visible. Watch players push each other to beat it.

Connect to Barcelona: tell the players that the rondo at the end of this warm-up is the same activity that Messi, Xavi, and Iniesta started every training session with. They are doing what those players did.
After 3 months, watch a video from your first session and compare the passing quality. The difference will be extraordinary.
diagramDescription: 10x10m square. MINUTE 1-3 shown: 4 players moving freely, 4-touch label. MINUTE 4-6: same with 3-touch label. MINUTE 7-9: 2-touch label, toe-tap correction shown (player doing toe-taps after 3-touch violation). MINUTE 10-12: 1v1 rondo shown — one player in centre pressing, others circulating on 2-touch. DIAMOND SHAPE shown as optimal possession shape with 4 players at diamond points. Move-before-ball-arrives arrows shown from every player as the pass leaves the foot. SAME EVERY SESSION label shown prominently. Consecutive pass record tracker shown.

title: Compactness — Defending as a Back Four
objective: Develop the back four as a compact, coordinated defensive unit that maintains correct horizontal and vertical shape throughout a match.
learningOutcomes: Back four maintain correct horizontal spacing throughout all phases of attack
Back four step up and drop as a coordinated unit based on ball position
Players in the back four develop the communication that maintains the compact shape
Back four develop the stepping and covering principles when one member is beaten
organisation: Full pitch defensive half. Back four work as a coordinated unit against 4-5 attackers. Specific coaching of horizontal compactness (correct spacing between the four), vertical coordination (stepping up and dropping together), and stepping-and-covering when one defender is beaten.
instructions: BACK FOUR POSITIONS: Right Back (RB), Right Centre Back (RCB), Left Centre Back (LCB), Left Back (LB).
HORIZONTAL SPACING: the four should be evenly spaced across the width of the penalty area. Maximum gap between any adjacent pair: 8m. Check spacing regularly.
STEP UP TOGETHER: when the ball is played back to the opposition GK or midfield, all four step up simultaneously by 5m. Nobody steps without the others.
DROP TOGETHER: when the ball is played over the top or into the forward line, all four drop simultaneously. Nobody stays high.
COVER WHEN BEATEN: when RB is beaten by a winger, RCB covers the space left by RB moving wide. LCB shifts right to cover RCB space. LB shifts right to cover LCB space. The whole line shifts.
PHASE 1 (7 minutes): passive attackers move the ball around. Back four practice STEP and DROP together on coach signals.
PHASE 2 (8 minutes): active attackers try to exploit gaps. Back four maintain compactness.
